topics.title: Distributed System Models and Enabling Technologies: The Age of Internet Computing - High Performance and High Throughput Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Distributed System Models and Enabling Technologies: The Age of Internet Computing - High Performance and High Throughput Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Age of Internet Computing

The **Age of Internet Computing** refers to the current paradigm in distributed systems where computing resources, services, and data are delivered over the internet as **utility-like commodities** (similar to electricity or water). In this era, the internet itself acts as the *operating system* and *communication fabric* that binds together heterogeneous, geographically dispersed resources into a unified, scalable, and on-demand computing platform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition**: *Internet Computing* is a computing paradigm in which globally distributed, loosely coupled, autonomous systems cooperate over the internet to deliver computing services, information, and knowledge to end-users on demand, treating computing as a **utility (computing utility)** rather than a product.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Electrical Grid

Think of Internet Computing the same way you think of the **electrical grid**:
- You do not own the power plant; you just plug into the grid.
- You pay only for what you consume (pay-per-use).
- The provider scales capacity dynamically based on demand.
- Multiple sources (solar, wind, hydro) feed into one common delivery network.

In the same way, **cloud platforms (AWS, Azure, GCP)** and **grid computing systems** deliver compute, storage, and software as pluggable, metered, on-demand services. The "Internet" is the wire, and "Computing" is the current flowing through it.

## 1.2 Distributed System Models — A Quick Taxonomy

A **Distributed System** is a collection of independent computers (nodes) that appears to its users as a single coherent system. The KTU syllabus identifies several key models that evolved into the *Age of Internet Computing*:

| Model | Core Idea | Best-Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Cluster Computing** | Homogeneous nodes tightly coupled via LAN | HPC workloads |
| **Grid Computing** | Geographically distributed, heterogeneous, virtual organization | Scientific collaborations (e.g., CERN) |
| **Cloud Computing** | Elastic, pay-per-use, internet-scaled | Web services, SaaS, IaaS, PaaS |
| **P2P Computing** | All nodes are equal peers, no central server | File sharing, blockchain |
| **Edge/Fog Computing** | Computation pushed to the network edge | IoT, real-time analytics |

## 1.3 High Performance Computing (HPC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HPC Definition (KTU Syllabus)**: *High Performance Computing* refers to the use of **aggregated parallel processing power** of multiple compute nodes, supercomputers, or clusters to solve **single, complex, compute-intensive problems** that require massive amounts of arithmetic in the shortest possible wall-clock time.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Sprint Race (100m Final)

Imagine a math problem that takes **one** super-genius **1 year** to solve alone. With HPC, you **divide the problem** into 365 smaller sub-problems and assign each day's work to a different genius, who all run in parallel and finish in **1 day**. The goal is **minimum latency** for **one giant problem**.

### Key Performance Metric for HPC
- **FLOP/s (Floating-Point Operations per Second)** — measured in **Giga (10⁹)**, **Tera (10¹²)**, **Peta (10¹⁵)**, or **Exa (10¹⁸)** FLOP/s.
- **Speedup (S)** — ratio of sequential execution time to parallel execution time.
- **Parallel Efficiency (E)** — fraction of theoretical maximum speedup actually achieved.

## 1.4 High Throughput Computing (HTC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HTC Definition (KTU Syllabus)**: *High Throughput Computing* refers to the use of distributed, often idle, loosely coupled compute resources to execute a **very large number of loosely related, independent tasks** over **long periods of time**, maximizing the **number of jobs completed per unit time** (throughput) rather than minimizing the latency of a single job.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Post Office

Think of a post office that processes **millions of letters per day**. Each letter is independent; sorting and delivering them in parallel across many branches maximizes the **total letters delivered per day (throughput)**. No single letter is a "giant problem" — they are many small, independent jobs. If one branch is slow or fails, the rest keep working. The goal is **maximum volume**, not **minimum time per letter**.

### Key Performance Metric for HTC
- **Throughput (jobs/hour or tasks/second)** — total tasks completed per unit time.
- **Job Completion Rate** — average rate at which independent jobs are finished.
- **Makespan** — total wall-clock time to complete an entire job set.

## 1.5 HPC vs HTC — The Fundamental Philosophical Divide

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Many students confuse HPC and HTC. The difference is **NOT** about hardware. It is about the **nature of the workload**:
> - **HPC = "One big problem, fast"** → tightly coupled, low-latency interconnect (InfiniBand, shared memory).
> - **HTC = "Many small problems, many of them"** → loosely coupled, high-latency tolerant (Ethernet, internet).

## 1.6 Enabling Technologies of the Age of Internet Computing

The Age of Internet Computing is built upon several enabling layers:

1. **Network Infrastructure** — fiber optics, high-speed switches, **InfiniBand (up to 200 Gbps)**, **5G/6G wireless**.
2. **Virtualization** — **Hypervisors (Type-1: VMware ESXi, Xen; Type-2: VirtualBox)** and **containers (Docker, Kubernetes)**.
3. **Middleware** — CORBA, RMI, RPC, gRPC, message queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ).
4. **Web Services & Standards** — **REST, SOAP, gRPC** over **HTTP/HTTPS**, **JSON/XML**.
5. **Cluster & Grid Toolkits** — **MPI, OpenMP, Condor, BOINC, Hadoop, Spark**.
6. **Data Center Technologies** — modular servers, liquid cooling, software-defined networking (SDN).
7. **Open Standards & Protocols** — TCP/IP, DNS, TLS/SSL, OAuth, OpenID.
8. **Autonomic & Utility Computing** — self-healing, self-scaling, pay-per-use.

> [!NOTE]
> These enabling technologies collectively **abstract away** the underlying physical complexity, allowing end-users to view a *vast, distributed, heterogeneous* infrastructure as **a single, virtual, elastic computer**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Tiers of Internet Computing (Layered Architecture)

The Age of Internet Computing rests on **three architectural layers**:

### Tier 1 — Infrastructure (Hardware Fabric)
- **Compute**: Multi-core CPUs, GPUs, TPUs, FPGAs, ASICs, quantum accelerators.
- **Storage**: SAN, NAS, Object storage (S3), distributed file systems (HDFS, Ceph).
- **Network**: 10/40/100/200/400 Gbps Ethernet, InfiniBand, optical fiber, software-defined networks.
- **Power & Cooling**: Redundant PSU, UPS, CRAC units, liquid cooling.

### Tier 2 — Middleware (The "Glue" Software)
- **Resource Managers**: SLURM, Torque, Mesos, Kubernetes.
- **Communication Fabrics**: MPI, OpenMP, RDMA, gRPC.
- **Job Schedulers**: Condor, BOINC, HTCondor, Spark Scheduler.
- **Virtualization Layer**: KVM, Xen, Docker, Podman, containerd.
- **Service Registries**: ZooKeeper, etcd, Consul.

### Tier 3 — Applications & Services (User-Facing)
- **SaaS**: Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce.
- **PaaS**: Google App Engine, Heroku.
- **IaaS**: EC2, Azure VMs, GCE.
- **Scientific Gateways**: Galaxy, nanoHUB.

> [!NOTE]
> The **middleware layer is the soul of distributed computing** — it hides heterogeneity, provides transparency (location, failure, access, concurrency, replication), and orchestrates resources.

## 2.2 Detailed Breakdown — High Performance Computing (HPC)

### Why HPC?
- Scientific simulations: climate modeling, molecular dynamics (Folding@home), nuclear simulations.
- Real-time analytics: financial trading, weather forecasting.
- Engineering: CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics), FEA, crash simulation.
- AI/ML training: deep learning models (GPT, BERT, AlphaFold).

### Core Components of an HPC System
1. **Compute Nodes** — many multi-core servers with large RAM.
2. **Interconnect Network** — low-latency, high-bandwidth (InfiniBand, Omni-Path, Slingshot).
3. **Parallel File System** — Lustre, GPFS, BeeGFS, PVFS.
4. **Job Scheduler** — SLURM, PBS, LSF, Torque.
5. **Programming Model** — MPI, OpenMP, CUDA, OpenCL, OpenACC, HPF.
6. **Monitoring** — Ganglia, Nagios, Prometheus, Grafana.

### HPC Parallelism Types
- **Bit-level parallelism** — wider data paths (64-bit vs 32-bit).
- **Instruction-level parallelism (ILP)** — pipelining, superscalar, out-of-order execution.
- **Data parallelism** — SIMD, vectorization, GPU kernels.
- **Task parallelism** — MPI ranks, OpenMP threads, distributed processes.

### Performance Metrics
- **Latency (L)** — time to complete **one** task (lower is better).
- **Peak Performance (R_peak)** — theoretical maximum FLOP/s = cores × clock × FLOPs/cycle.
- **Sustained Performance (R_max)** — actual measured on benchmark (HPL, LINPACK).
- **Efficiency** — `η = R_max / R_peak`.

## 2.3 Detailed Breakdown — High Throughput Computing (HTC)

### Why HTC?
- Drug discovery: screening millions of compounds.
- Cryptography: brute-force key search.
- Particle physics: analyzing petabytes of collision data (CERN LHC).
- Bioinformatics: BLAST searches over genomic databases.
- Volunteer computing: SETI@home, Folding@home, Einstein@home.

### Core Components of an HTC System
1. **Worker Pool** — thousands of heterogeneous, often volunteer, machines.
2. **Job Broker/Scheduler** — Condor, BOINC server, HTCondor.
3. **Task Queue** — Bag-of-Tasks model.
4. **Checkpoint/Restart** — for long jobs on unreliable workers.
5. **Result Collector** — gathers, validates, and stores outputs.

### The "Bag-of-Tasks" (BoT) Model
A workload is decomposed into a **large set of independent tasks**. There is little to no inter-task communication. This is the **defining property** of HTC.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists **every formula you must memorize** for KTU exams on this topic. Pay special attention to units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = \dfrac{T_s}{T_p}$ | Speedup of parallel over sequential | Amdahl's Law problems |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{N}}$ | Amdahl's Law (fixed problem size) | Maximum achievable speedup |
| 3 | $S = f + (1-f) \cdot N$ | Gustafson's Law (scaled problem size) | HPC scalability analysis |
| 4 | $E = \dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{T_s}{N \cdot T_p}$ | Parallel efficiency | Cluster utilization |
| 5 | $R_{peak} = C \times f \times F$ | Peak FLOP/s (C=cores, f=Hz, F=FLOP/cycle) | HPC performance estimation |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{R_{max}}{R_{peak}} \times 100\%$ | System efficiency % | Benchmark reporting |
| 7 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Total Tasks}}{\text{Total Time}}$ | Jobs completed per unit time | HTC metrics |
| 8 | $T_p = T_s \times f + \dfrac{T_s \times (1-f)}{N}$ | Parallel execution time derivation | Proof of Amdahl's Law |
| 9 | $S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Theoretical max speedup as $N \to \infty$ | Amdahl's bound |
| 10 | $\text{Cost} = N \times T_p$ | Total processor-time product | Cost-optimal parallelization |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Convention Used in KTU Exams**:
> - $T_s$ = sequential execution time
> - $T_p$ = parallel execution time on $N$ processors
> - $f$ = **serial fraction** (the part that **cannot** be parallelized), $0 \le f \le 1$
> - $(1-f)$ = parallelizable fraction
> - $N$ = number of processors/nodes

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where These Systems Are Used in Industry

| Domain | HPC Use | HTC Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Pharma** | Molecular dynamics simulation | Drug candidate screening |
| **Finance** | Monte Carlo risk analysis | Portfolio rebalancing across thousands of accounts |
| **Climate** | Global circulation models | Processing satellite imagery tiles |
| **Astronomy** | N-body simulation | Sky survey image processing |
| **Defense** | Cryptanalysis, ballistics | Log file analysis, packet inspection |
| **Web/SaaS** | Not typical | **MapReduce** batch processing, log analytics |

> [!NOTE]
> The cloud-native world (AWS Batch, Azure Batch, Google Cloud Dataflow) is essentially **HTC delivered as a service** in the Age of Internet Computing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Amdahl's Law

**Amdahl's Law** is the single most important equation for understanding the **limits of HPC parallelization**. The KTU board frequently asks for its derivation and application.

### Problem Statement
A program requires a total sequential execution time of $T_s$. Let $f$ be the fraction of the program that **must run serially** (cannot be parallelized). The remaining $(1-f)$ fraction can be perfectly parallelized across $N$ identical processors. Find the maximum achievable speedup $S$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Partition the total sequential time into serial and parallel parts:

$$
T_s = T_{serial} + T_{parallel}
$$

**Step 2** — Express each part in terms of the serial fraction $f$:

$$
T_{serial} = f \cdot T_s \qquad T_{parallel} = (1-f) \cdot T_s
$$

**Step 3** — Run the serial part on **one** processor (no speedup possible):

$$
T_{serial}^{parallel\,run} = f \cdot T_s
$$

**Step 4** — Distribute the parallel part across $N$ processors evenly. Assuming perfect load balancing, each processor handles a fraction $\dfrac{(1-f)}{N}$ of the original work:

$$
T_{parallel}^{parallel\,run} = \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{N}
$$

**Step 5** — Add the two parts to get total parallel execution time $T_p$:

$$
T_p = f \cdot T_s + \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_s}{N}
$$

**Step 6** — Factor out $T_s$:

$$
T_p = T_s \left[ f + \frac{1-f}{N} \right]
$$

**Step 7** — Apply the speedup definition $S = \dfrac{T_s}{T_p}$:

$$
S = \frac{T_s}{T_s \left[ f + \dfrac{1-f}{N} \right]}
$$

**Step 8** — Cancel $T_s$:

$$
\boxed{\,S = \frac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{N}}\,}
$$

This is **Amdahl's Law**.

### Step 9 — Derive the Theoretical Upper Bound

Let $N \to \infty$ (infinite processors):

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S = \frac{1}{f + 0} = \frac{1}{f}
$$

**Conclusion**: Even with infinite processors, the speedup is bounded by $\dfrac{1}{f}$. If just 5% of your program is serial ($f=0.05$), the **maximum possible speedup is 20×**, no matter how many processors you add.

### Step 10 — Sanity Check

If $f = 0$ (fully parallelizable):

$$
S = \frac{1}{0 + \frac{1-0}{N}} = \frac{1}{1/N} = N \quad \checkmark
$$

If $N = 1$ (single processor):

$$
S = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{1}} = \frac{1}{f + 1 - f} = \frac{1}{1} = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Parallel Efficiency

Starting from efficiency definition $E = \dfrac{S}{N}$:

$$
E = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{1}{N \left[ f + \dfrac{1-f}{N} \right]}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $N$:

$$
E = \frac{1}{Nf + (1-f)} = \frac{1}{1 + (N-1)f}
$$

This shows efficiency drops as $N$ grows if $f > 0$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Examples

### Example 1: Amdahl's Law Speedup Calculation

**Question**: A program takes 100 seconds on a single processor. 20% of it is inherently serial. Find the speedup on 8 processors.

**Solution**:

Given: $T_s = 100\,s$, $f = 0.20$, $N = 8$.

Apply Amdahl's Law:

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{1 - 0.20}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.10} = \frac{1}{0.30}
$$

$$
\boxed{S \approx 3.33}
$$

Parallel execution time:

$$
T_p = \frac{T_s}{S} = \frac{100}{3.33} \approx 30\,s
$$

Efficiency:

$$
E = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{3.33}{8} \approx 0.416 = 41.6\%
$$

### Example 2: Finding Maximum Processors for Target Speedup

**Question**: Serial fraction $f = 0.10$. How many processors are needed to achieve $S = 5$?

**Solution**:

Starting from Amdahl's Law, solve for $N$:

$$
5 = \frac{1}{0.10 + \dfrac{0.90}{N}}
$$

Take reciprocal of both sides:

$$
0.20 = 0.10 + \frac{0.90}{N}
$$

Subtract $0.10$:

$$
0.10 = \frac{0.90}{N}
$$

Solve for $N$:

$$
N = \frac{0.90}{0.10} = 9
$$

**Result**: 9 processors suffice; adding more gives diminishing returns.

### Example 3: HTC Throughput Calculation

**Question**: A grid system processes 50,000 independent protein-folding jobs in 24 hours. A new algorithm doubles the per-node throughput. What is the new makespan if the cluster has 200 nodes, each originally handling $X$ jobs/hour?

**Solution**:

Original total rate:

$$
R_{old} = 200 \times X \text{ jobs/hour}
$$

Original makespan:

$$
T_{old} = \frac{50{,}000}{200X} = \frac{250}{X} \text{ hours}
$$

New rate (doubled):

$$
R_{new} = 400X \text{ jobs/hour}
$$

New makespan:

$$
T_{new} = \frac{50{,}000}{400X} = \frac{125}{X} \text{ hours} = 12 \text{ hours}
$$

**Result**: Throughput doubled → makespan halved.

### Example 4: Peak FLOP/s Calculation

**Question**: A supercomputer has 4096 nodes. Each node has 2 sockets of 16-core CPUs at 2.4 GHz, and each core can execute 16 FLOPs per cycle (AVX-512). Find the **R_peak**.

**Solution**:

Total cores:

$$
C = 4096 \times 2 \times 16 = 131{,}072 \text{ cores}
$$

Peak FLOP/s:

$$
R_{peak} = C \times f \times F = 131{,}072 \times 2.4 \times 10^9 \times 16
$$

$$
R_{peak} = 5.03 \times 10^{15} \text{ FLOP/s} \approx 5.03\, \text{PFLOPS}
$$

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — HPC vs HTC

| Dimension | HPC | HTC |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Minimum latency on one big problem | Maximum number of jobs per unit time |
| **Workload** | Tightly coupled, parallelizable | Loosely coupled, independent (BoT) |
| **Communication** | Frequent, low-latency (MPI) | Rare, high-latency tolerant |
| **Hardware** | Homogeneous, dedicated, fast interconnect | Heterogeneous, opportunistic, often idle |
| **Job Duration** | Hours to weeks | Seconds to hours |
| **Failure handling** | Checkpoint/restart + redundancy | Re-submit failed job to another worker |
| **Scheduler** | SLURM, PBS, LSF (gang scheduling) | Condor, BOINC (pull-based) |
| **Programming model** | MPI, OpenMP, CUDA, OpenCL | Master-worker, MapReduce, batch scripts |
| **Performance metric** | FLOP/s, speedup, efficiency | Throughput, makespan, jobs/sec |
| **Examples** | Climate modeling, CFD, AI training | BLAST search, SETI@home, log mining |
| **Suitability for cloud** | Limited (latency-sensitive) | Excellent (elastic, fault-tolerant) |

## 3.5 Karp's Flops (Killer Microseconds) — The HPC Latency Challenge

In HPC, even tiny communication latencies (microseconds) matter. **Karp's Flops** model the cost of a single floating-point operation in terms of network latency. If a processor can do $R$ FLOP/s and the latency to send a message is $\tau$ seconds, the **Karp number** is:

$$
K = R \cdot \tau
$$

Interpretation: if $K > 1$, the network is too slow — the processor wastes cycles waiting. Modern systems target $K \le 0.01$.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **HPC uses InfiniBand or Cray Slingshot** (latency $\sim 1 \mu s$) instead of standard Ethernet ($\sim 50 \mu s$).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]
**"Differentiate between High Performance Computing (HPC) and High Throughput Computing (HTC)."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer**:
- **HPC** aims at **minimizing the wall-clock time** of a **single complex problem** by using tightly coupled parallel systems with low-latency interconnects. Example: climate modeling.
- **HTC** aims at **maximizing the number of independent jobs** completed per unit time using loosely coupled, often opportunistic, resources. Example: SETI@home.
- HPC uses **MPI/OpenMP**; HTC uses **master-worker / bag-of-tasks** models.
- HPC demands **homogeneous hardware**; HTC tolerates **heterogeneous** volunteer machines.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]
**"What is the Age of Internet Computing? List any three of its enabling technologies."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer**:
The Age of Internet Computing is the current paradigm where computing, storage, and software are delivered as **on-demand, internet-scaled utilities** to users worldwide, much like electricity. Three enabling technologies:
1. **Virtualization** (hypervisors, containers).
2. **High-speed networks** (InfiniBand, SDN, 5G).
3. **Middleware** (MPI, RMI, gRPC, message queues).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **architecture of the Age of Internet Computing**, with a neat diagram of its three layers. List **four enabling technologies** in each layer. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A parallel program has a serial fraction of $f = 0.15$. It is executed on **16 processors**.
   (i) Compute the **speedup** using Amdahl's Law. (3 Marks)
   (ii) Compute the **parallel efficiency**. (2 Marks)
   (iii) What is the **theoretical maximum speedup** if infinite processors were available? (2 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a)

The Age of Internet Computing has three architectural tiers:

1. **Infrastructure Tier** — physical compute (CPUs, GPUs, TPUs), storage (HDFS, S3, Lustre), and network (InfiniBand, 5G, SDN). Four enabling technologies: multi-core CPUs, GPUs, InfiniBand, HDFS.

2. **Middleware Tier** — software that glues resources. Four enabling technologies: MPI, gRPC, Kubernetes, HTCondor.

3. **Application & Service Tier** — user-facing. Four enabling technologies: REST APIs, SaaS portals, scientific gateways, mobile clients.

*(See diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

#### Model Solution for (b)

Given: $f = 0.15$, $N = 16$.

**(i) Speedup**:
Apply Amdahl's Law:

$$
S = \frac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{N}} = \frac{1}{0.15 + \dfrac{0.85}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.15 + 0.053125}
$$

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.203125} \approx 4.92
$$

**(ii) Parallel Efficiency**:

$$
E = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{4.92}{16} \approx 0.3075 = 30.75\%
$$

**(iii) Maximum Speedup (N → ∞)**:

$$
S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{0.15} \approx 6.67
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b)**:
> - Many students write $S = N \times (1-f)$, which is **wrong** — that is a sloppy approximation, not Amdahl's Law.
> - Forgetting to **invert the denominator** is a very common mark-loser.
> - Reporting $E$ as a percentage without multiplying by 100 loses 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe the **High Throughput Computing (HTC) model** in detail. Explain the **Bag-of-Tasks (BoT) model** with a diagram. Give **two real-world examples**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A supercomputer has **2048 nodes**, each with **2 sockets of 18-core CPUs** at **2.6 GHz**, and each core executes **32 FLOPs per cycle** (AVX-512 + AVX-512 FP32).
   (i) Calculate the **peak performance (R_peak)** in PFLOPS. (4 Marks)
   (ii) If the sustained performance (R_max) on HPL benchmark is **70%** of R_peak, what is R_max in PFLOPS? (2 Marks)
   (iii) Comment on the **significance of efficiency** in HPC. (1 Mark)

#### Model Solution for (a)

HTC is a distributed computing model whose primary objective is to **maximize throughput** — the number of jobs completed per unit time. It typically:

- Uses **opportunistic, often idle, heterogeneous resources** (e.g., volunteer desktops, idle servers).
- Treats workloads as a **Bag-of-Tasks (BoT)** — a large collection of **independent jobs** with little or no inter-task communication.
- Uses **master-worker scheduling** — a central broker dispatches tasks; workers pull, compute, and return results.
- Tolerates **failures** by **re-submission** of failed tasks to other workers.
- Uses **checkpointing** for long-running jobs on unreliable nodes.

**Bag-of-Tasks Diagram** — already provided in Section 4.4.

**Two real-world examples**:
1. **SETI@home** — millions of volunteers analyzing radio-telescope data.
2. **BLAST** searches in bioinformatics — millions of independent sequence comparisons.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**(i) Peak Performance**:

Total cores:

$$
C = 2048 \times 2 \times 18 = 73{,}728 \text{ cores}
$$

Peak FLOP/s:

$$
R_{peak} = C \times f \times F = 73{,}728 \times 2.6 \times 10^9 \times 32
$$

$$
R_{peak} = 6.13 \times 10^{15} \text{ FLOP/s} = 6.13 \text{ PFLOPS}
$$

**(ii) Sustained Performance (R_max)**:

$$
R_{max} = 0.70 \times 6.13 = 4.29 \text{ PFLOPS}
$$

**(iii) Significance of Efficiency**:
Efficiency quantifies **how much of the theoretical peak is actually useful**. Low efficiency indicates memory bottlenecks, communication overhead, or poor load balancing. Modern HPC systems achieve only **60–80%** efficiency even when well-tuned.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b)**:
> - Forgetting to **convert GHz to Hz** (multiply by $10^9$) is a 1-mark deduction.
> - Confusing **PFLOP/s ($10^{15}$)** with **TFLOP/s ($10^{12}$)** is a fatal unit error.
> - Cores per node × sockets × nodes must be done **in the correct order** to avoid off-by-factor-of-2 errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam.**

- **Age of Internet Computing** = computing delivered as an internet-scaled utility (cloud, grid, edge).
- **Three Tiers** = Infrastructure → Middleware → Applications/Services.
- **HPC** = tightly coupled, single big problem, **minimize latency**, FLOP/s metric.
- **HTC** = loosely coupled, many small independent jobs, **maximize throughput**, jobs/sec metric.
- **Bag-of-Tasks (BoT)** is the canonical HTC workload model — independent tasks, no inter-task dependency.
- **Amdahl's Law**: $S = \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{N}}$ — **derived from first principles** in Section 3.1.
- **Maximum speedup** with infinite processors = $1/f$.
- **Parallel Efficiency** = $S/N = \dfrac{1}{1 + (N-1)f}$.
- **Gustafson's Law** (for KTU full-marks): $S = f + (1-f)N$ — applies when problem **size grows with N**.
- **Peak FLOP/s** = cores × clock × FLOPs/cycle.
- **Enabling technologies** (memorize 6): Virtualization, Middleware (MPI/RMI), High-speed networks (InfiniBand), Web services (REST), Data centers, Autonomic computing.
- **HPC programming models**: MPI, OpenMP, CUDA, OpenCL.
- **HTC programming models**: Master-worker, MapReduce, HTCondor DAGMan.
- **Cluster vs Grid vs Cloud**:
  - Cluster = LAN, homogeneous, single org.
  - Grid = WAN, heterogeneous, multi-org.
  - Cloud = internet, elastic, pay-per-use.
- **Karp's Flops** = $K = R \times \tau$ — measures if network can keep up with CPU.
- **Speedup is bounded** even for tiny $f > 0$; this is Amdahl's curse and motivates **algorithm redesign** to reduce $f$.
- **HTC tolerates failure** via **re-submission**; HPC tries to **avoid failure** via **redundancy and checkpointing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip**: When asked to "differentiate" in a 3-mark question, always give **at least 3 distinguishing points** plus one **example** for each. For 14-mark Amdahl's Law questions, **always show the full derivation** — partial credit is awarded for each correct step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centralized Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Centralized Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Centralized Computing — Module 1 Foundations

## 1. Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centralized Computing** is a computing paradigm in which all computational resources — including processing power (CPU), memory (RAM), storage, and I/O devices — are consolidated within a **single central system** (typically a mainframe or a powerful server) that serves multiple remote users or client terminals. Under this model, the central host retains exclusive authority over data, application logic, and execution control, while user terminals act as thin display/input devices.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCST602)**, centralized computing represents the *historical and conceptual baseline* against which modern paradigms such as **Distributed Computing**, **Parallel Computing**, **Cluster Computing**, **Grid Computing**, and **Cloud Computing** are benchmarked. It is a foundational stepping stone required to understand *why* the industry shifted away from monolithic architectures toward decentralized models.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terms (KTU Syllabus-Aligned Vocabulary):**
> - **Host Computer / Central Server:** The master machine that performs all processing.
> - **Dumb Terminal / Thin Client:** A peripheral device with minimal local processing capability.
> - **Time-Sharing:** A scheduling technique that interleaves CPU service among many concurrent users.
> - **Mainframe:** A class of high-performance, high-reliability centralized computers designed for bulk data processing.

---

## 2. Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Think of centralized computing like a **single huge public library** in a small town.

- Every book (data) is kept in **one central building** (the mainframe).
- Residents (users) don't keep personal copies of books at home. Instead, they walk/travel to the library, request a book, read it there (or borrow temporarily), and return it.
- A head librarian (operating system) enforces rules — who can read what, for how long, and in what order.
- If the library closes (the mainframe crashes), **no one in the town can access any book**.

This analogy captures the three defining behaviors of centralized computing:
1. **Resource Concentration** — Everything valuable lives in one place.
2. **Client Passivity** — The user terminals are not "smart"; they wait for the host.
3. **Centralized Control** — Governance, security, and scheduling are all in the hands of the host OS.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Modern Example:** A company's internal **SAP ERP system** running on a single corporate data-center server, accessed by employees worldwide via remote desktop, is a contemporary instance of centralized computing — even though it is web-fronted today.

---

## 3. Core Characteristics of Centralized Computing

The following properties are universally cited across KTU-recommended texts:

| # | Characteristic | Description |
|---|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Resource Centralization** | All CPU, RAM, disk, and I/O are physically located in one site. |
| 2 | **Single Point of Control** | One OS and one administrator govern the entire system. |
| 3 | **Centralized Data** | A single database instance serves all concurrent requests. |
| 4 | **Time-Sharing / Multiplexing** | CPU cycles are divided among users via schedulers. |
| 5 | **Uniform Security Policy** | Authentication, authorization, and auditing happen in one place. |
| 6 | **High Initial Cost, Low Marginal Cost** | Expensive to buy/maintain; cheap to add more users. |
| 7 | **Limited Fault Tolerance** | Host failure ⇒ entire system halts. |
| 8 | **Network Dependency** | Remote terminals depend on reliable WAN/LAN links. |

---

## 4. Historical Evolution (Context for KTU Module 1)

Centralized computing is **not new** — it is the *original* model. The evolution in Module 1 follows this trajectory:

```
1960s: Mainframe Era (IBM System/360)        → Centralized
1970s: Minicomputers & Time-Sharing          → Centralized-lite
1980s: Personal Computers (PCs)              → Decentralization begins
1990s: Client-Server                         → Hybrid
2000s: Cluster & Grid Computing              → Distributed
2010s+: Cloud, Fog, Edge                     → Decentralized at scale
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Specific Exam Insight:** Module 1 typically begins with centralized computing precisely because understanding its **limitations** motivates the design of distributed and parallel systems that dominate the rest of the syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Architectural Anatomy of a Centralized System

A centralized computing system has a strictly **layered, asymmetric** architecture. Let us break it down step-by-step.

### Step A — The Hardware Layer
- A single, physically large machine houses one or more **high-clock-speed CPUs**, large banks of **RAM (often in GBs to TBs)**, high-throughput storage (HDD/SAN arrays), and networking interfaces.
- Reliability features such as **ECC memory**, **redundant power supplies**, and **hot-swappable disks** are standard.

### Step B — The Operating System Layer
- A multi-user, multi-tasking OS (e.g., IBM z/OS, VMS, early UNIX) runs the entire workload.
- It implements **time-sharing** to give the illusion of simultaneous execution to many remote users.

### Step C — The Application Layer
- Application logic resides entirely on the host.
- Users invoke programs via **terminal sessions** (character-based) or later via **graphical remote-display protocols**.

### Step D — The Terminal Layer
- **Dumb terminals** (VT100-style) display text and forward keystrokes. They hold *no application state*.
- The host transmits screen updates back to the terminal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Centralized" survived so long:** The economic argument was compelling — sharing one expensive machine among 100+ users cost far less than giving each user a dedicated computer.

---

## 2. The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Design Decision

| Design Decision | The "Why" | The "How" (Mechanism) |
|------------------|-----------|----------------------|
| Use a single big machine | Cheaper per-user cost; simpler administration | Buy one mainframe, multiplex across users |
| Time-share the CPU | A CPU is fast; human I/O is slow — so the CPU idles | Scheduler swaps processes in/out using **round-robin** or **priority queues** |
| Keep all data on host | Single source of truth; no sync issues | Central DBMS enforces **ACID** transactions |
| Use dumb terminals | Software licensing, maintenance, and security all live on one box | Terminals only run a **terminal emulator** |
| Enforce central auth | Easier to audit and secure one chokepoint | Host validates every login via **username/password** against a central directory |

---

## 3. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Because centralized computing is largely architectural (not numeric), the "formulas" here are **theoretical models and performance relations** the examiner loves to test.

| Symbol | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|--------|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| $n$ | Number of concurrent users | $n = \dfrac{T_{cpu}}{T_{slice}}$ | dimensionless |
| $U_{cpu}$ | CPU Utilization under time-sharing | $U_{cpu} = 1 - p_{idle}$ | $0 \le U_{cpu} \le 1$ |
| $\tau$ | Time-slice (quantum) duration | Typically **10–100 ms** | milliseconds |
| $\eta_{tput}$ | System Throughput | $\eta_{tput} = \dfrac{\text{Completed Jobs}}{T_{total}}$ | jobs/second |
| $R$ | Response Time | $R = T_{queue} + T_{service} + T_{net}$ | seconds |
| $A$ | Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | dimensionless, $0 \le A \le 1$ |
| $S$ | Scalability Limit | $S_{max} \approx \dfrac{C_{host}}{R_{min\_per\_user}}$ | users |
| $C_{total}$ | Total Cost of Ownership | $C_{total} = C_{hw} + C_{sw} + C_{op} + C_{net}$ | currency units |
| $\rho$ | Server Load Factor | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | $\rho < 1$ for stability |
| $T_{wait}$ | Average Wait (M/M/1 Queue) | $T_{wait} = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$ | seconds |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Rule (KTU Exam PDFs):** When copying this table into your answer script, never use the `|` symbol inside cell math (e.g., do NOT write `|x|`). Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead — otherwise the cell will break in renderers.

---

## 4. Performance Theory — The M/M/1 Queue Model

The most common theoretical lens used in KTU examinations to model a centralized server is the **M/M/1 queuing model** (Kendall's notation).

**Assumptions:**
- Inter-arrival times are **exponentially distributed** (M).
- Service times are **exponentially distributed** (M).
- There is **one** server (1) — our centralized host.

**Key Results (used in derivations below):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \\[4pt]
L &= \frac{\rho}{1 - \rho} \\[4pt]
L_q &= \frac{\rho^2}{1 - \rho} \\[4pt]
W &= \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} \\[4pt]
W_q &= \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\lambda$ = arrival rate (requests/sec)
- $\mu$ = service rate (requests/sec)
- $\rho$ = server utilization factor
- $L$ = average number of jobs in system
- $L_q$ = average number in queue
- $W$ = average time in system
- $W_q$ = average waiting time in queue

> [!TIP]
> **Why M/M/1 matters here:** It mathematically proves that as $\rho \to 1$, the wait time $W \to \infty$. This is the *fundamental scalability ceiling* of centralized computing — and a favorite KTU question.

---

## 5. Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Centralized Computing |
|-------|--------------------------------------|
| **Banking** | Core banking systems (e.g., older mainframe-based ATMs) |
| **Aviation** | Airline reservation systems (e.g., Amadeus, Sabre — historically centralized) |
| **Government** | Census data processing, tax record systems |
| **Healthcare** | Centralized Hospital Information Systems (HIS) |
| **Telecom** | Legacy Signaling System No. 7 (SS7) switching centers |
| **Defense** | Strategic command-and-control mainframes |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line** derivations of every key relation a KTU examiner can ask about centralized computing. No step is skipped.

---

## Derivation 1 — Maximum Number of Concurrent Users in Time-Sharing

**Problem Setup:** A centralized mainframe has a total CPU time budget of $T_{cpu}$ seconds per second of wall-clock time. Each user is allotted a time-slice $\tau$ seconds and performs $N_{io}$ blocking I/O operations during the slice. Find the maximum number of users $n$ the system can serve without starving the CPU.

**Step 1 — Effective CPU time consumed by one user per slice:**
The user's process runs for $\tau$ seconds of CPU time, but during that slice it may block on I/O. Let $f_{io}$ be the fraction of slice time spent in I/O wait.

$$
t_{effective} = \tau \cdot (1 - f_{io})
$$

**Step 2 — Number of slices possible per second:**
Since each slice consumes $\tau$ wall-clock seconds (from the scheduler's viewpoint),

$$
n_{slices/sec} = \frac{1}{\tau}
$$

**Step 3 — Fraction of slices actually consuming CPU:**
Not every dispatched process uses the full slice; some terminate early. Let $p_{yield}$ be the probability that a process voluntarily yields before its slice ends.

$$
\text{CPU load factor} = 1 - p_{yield}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine to get $n_{max}$:**
The total CPU demand from $n$ users must not exceed 1 (100%). Therefore:

$$
n \cdot \tau \cdot (1 - f_{io}) \cdot (1 - p_{yield}) \le 1
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $n$:**

$$
\boxed{\,n_{max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{1}{\tau \cdot (1 - f_{io}) \cdot (1 - p_{yield})} \right\rfloor\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Example (typical KTU problem):**
Given $\tau = 0.05$ s, $f_{io} = 0.6$, $p_{yield} = 0.1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{max} &= \frac{1}{0.05 \cdot (1 - 0.6) \cdot (1 - 0.1)} \\[4pt]
        &= \frac{1}{0.05 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.9} \\[4pt]
        &= \frac{1}{0.018} \\[4pt]
        &= 55.55...
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\therefore n_{max} = 55 \text{ concurrent users (floor)}
$$

---

## Derivation 2 — M/M/1 Queue Response Time for the Centralized Server

**Problem Setup:** Requests arrive at the centralized host at rate $\lambda = 4$ req/s, and the host services them at rate $\mu = 5$ req/s. Compute the average response time $W$, the average number of jobs in the system $L$, and the probability the system is stable.

**Step 1 — Compute the traffic intensity $\rho$:**

$$
\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8
$$

**Step 2 — Stability check:** System is stable iff $\rho < 1$. Here $0.8 < 1$ ✓

**Step 3 — Compute $W$ (average time in system):**

$$
W = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{5 - 4} = \frac{1}{1} = 1 \text{ second}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $W_q$ (average wait in queue):**

$$
W_q = \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{0.8}{5 - 4} = 0.8 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $L$ (average number in system) via Little's Law $L = \lambda W$:**

$$
L = 4 \cdot 1 = 4 \text{ jobs}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $L_q$ (average number in queue):**

$$
L_q = \lambda W_q = 4 \cdot 0.8 = 3.2 \text{ jobs}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:** With only 20% spare capacity, the queue is already substantial. As $\lambda \to 5$, $W \to \infty$ — the **centralized bottleneck**.

---

## Derivation 3 — Availability and Downtime of a Centralized System

**Problem Setup:** A mainframe has MTBF = 30,000 hours and MTTR = 6 hours. Find availability and annual downtime.

**Step 1 — Apply the availability formula:**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{30000}{30000 + 6} = \frac{30000}{30006}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$
A \approx 0.9998 = 99.98\%
$$

**Step 3 — Annual downtime in minutes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{year} &= 365 \cdot 24 = 8760 \text{ hours} \\[4pt]
D_{year} &= (1 - A) \cdot H_{year} \\[4pt]
        &= 0.0002 \cdot 8760 \\[4pt]
        &= 1.752 \text{ hours/year} \\[4pt]
        &= 105.12 \text{ minutes/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** A single failure brings the **entire** system down for those 1.75 hours. There is no redundancy by design — this is the *single point of failure* problem.

---

## Derivation 4 — Cost-Per-User Analysis

**Problem Setup:** A mainframe costs \$1,000,000 with \$200,000/year operating cost. It serves $N = 500$ users over a 5-year amortization. Find cost per user per year.

**Step 1 — Total 5-year cost:**

$$
C_{5yr} = 1{,}000{,}000 + (200{,}000 \cdot 5) = 2{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Cost per user per year:**

$$
C_{user/yr} = \frac{C_{5yr}}{N \cdot 5} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{500 \cdot 5} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{2500} = 800 \text{ dollars/user/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison trigger:** If $N$ drops to 100, $C_{user/yr}$ jumps to \$4000 — proving the **economies of scale** argument for centralization.

---

## Analytical Matrix — Centralized vs. Decentralized (Conceptual Comparison)

| Dimension | Centralized Computing | Decentralized / Distributed |
|-----------|-----------------------|------------------------------|
| Control | Single host | Multiple autonomous nodes |
| Fault Tolerance | Low (SPOF) | High (replication) |
| Scalability | Vertical only (bigger box) | Horizontal (add nodes) |
| Latency | High for distant users | Low (locality) |
| Cost Structure | High CapEx, low OpEx | Low CapEx, moderate OpEx |
| Administration | Simple (one box) | Complex (many boxes) |
| Data Consistency | Trivial (one DB) | Hard (CAP theorem) |
| Security Perimeter | One perimeter to defend | Many attack surfaces |
| Example | IBM Mainframe, SAP Central | Hadoop, Cassandra, Kubernetes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagrams follow the **KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10** strict safety rules: only alphanumeric node IDs (prefixed with letters), no markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels, and reserved keywords avoided.

---

## Diagram 1 — Top-Level Architecture of a Centralized Computing System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[User Terminal Alpha] --> N1[Communication Network]
    A2[User Terminal Beta] --> N1
    A3[User Terminal Gamma] --> N1
    A4[User Terminal Delta] --> N1
    N1 --> H1[Central Mainframe Host]
    H1 --> H2[Central Processing Unit]
    H1 --> H3[Central Memory RAM]
    H1 --> H4[Central Storage Disk Array]
    H1 --> H5[Operating System Kernel]
    H1 --> H6[Central Database]
    H1 --> H7[Application Server]
```

---

## Diagram 2 — Request Flow (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Terminal Input] --> B2[Network Protocol Stack]
    B2 --> B3[Host OS Scheduler]
    B3 --> B4[Process Queue]
    B4 --> B5[CPU Dispatcher]
    B5 --> B6[Application Logic Execution]
    B6 --> B7[Central Database Query]
    B7 --> B6
    B6 --> B8[Output Buffer]
    B8 --> B9[Network Transmit]
    B9 --> B10[Terminal Display]
```

---

## Diagram 3 — Time-Sharing Scheduling (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    C1[Incoming User Request] --> C2[Authentication Module]
    C2 --> C3[Ready Queue Insertion]
    C3 --> C4[Round Robin Scheduler]
    C4 --> C5{Time Slice Expired?}
    C5 -- Yes --> C6[Preempt and Requeue]
    C5 -- No --> C7{Process Complete?}
    C7 -- Yes --> C8[Release Output to Terminal]
    C7 -- No --> C5
    C6 --> C3
    C4 --> C9[CPU Execution]
    C9 --> C5
```

---

## Diagram 4 — Failure Domain (Single Point of Failure Visualization)

```mermaid
graph TD
    D1[Central Host Failure] --> D2[All User Sessions Drop]
    D2 --> D3[All I/O Requests Stall]
    D3 --> D4[Data Access Lost Globally]
    D4 --> D5[Total System Downtime Event]
    D1 --> D6[No Redundancy Layer]
    D6 --> D5
```

---

## Diagram 5 — Evolution Roadmap from Centralized to Decentralized

```mermaid
graph LR
    E1[Centralized Mainframe] --> E2[Minicomputer Era]
    E2 --> E3[Client Server Model]
    E3 --> E4[Cluster Computing]
    E4 --> E5[Grid Computing]
    E5 --> E6[Cloud Computing]
    E6 --> E7[Fog and Edge Computing]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Note:** Topics requiring complex physical drawings (e.g., a wiring schematic of a mainframe backplane) have been intentionally replaced by **functional block diagrams** above, as recommended by the KTU 2024 Scheme answer-script validation policy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled after the **KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. The mark distribution strictly follows KTU Module-1 weightage: **Part A = 3 marks each, Part B = 14 marks each (internal choice)**.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define centralized computing. List any four of its defining characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

Centralized computing is a computing paradigm in which all processing, storage, and data management functions reside within a single, central computer system, and remote users access its resources via terminal or network connections.

**Four defining characteristics:**
1. **Resource Centralization** — All hardware resources (CPU, memory, storage) are co-located in one host.
2. **Single Point of Control** — One OS governs the entire workload.
3. **Centralized Data Management** — A single database instance serves all users, ensuring uniform data state.
4. **Time-Sharing Multiplexing** — CPU cycles are interleaved across multiple concurrent users via a scheduler.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award **1 mark** for a clean definition and **0.5 mark** per characteristic. Use bullet points — never write paragraphs in Part A.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a dumb terminal and a smart terminal in the context of centralized computing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Dumb Terminal | Smart Terminal |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| Local Processing | None — purely I/O device | Has local CPU and OS |
| Application State | Held entirely on host | Can run local apps |
| Examples | VT100, IBM 3270 | Modern PC with terminal emulator software |
| Cost | Very low | Moderate to high |
| Failure Impact | Only the user's screen dies | Local autonomy retained |

The dumb terminal is the **classical KTU-referenced** terminal type for centralized systems because it pushes all intelligence to the host.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Module-1 mandates that each 14-mark question offer an **internal choice**. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the architecture of a centralized computing system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the operating system in supporting multi-user time-sharing.**

**Model Answer:**

The architecture of a centralized computing system comprises four tightly-coupled layers: (i) the central hardware host, (ii) the operating system, (iii) the application and database layer, and (iv) the remote terminal layer.

**1. Central Hardware Host:** Houses one or more high-speed CPUs, large-capacity RAM, disk arrays, and high-throughput I/O channels. Reliability is ensured via ECC memory, redundant power, and hot-swappable disks.

**2. Operating System:** Runs a multi-user, multi-tasking kernel (e.g., z/OS, UNIX). It performs:
- **Process Scheduling** via round-robin or multilevel feedback queues.
- **Memory Management** with virtual memory paging.
- **File System Management** for shared storage.
- **Access Control** via centralized authentication (e.g., RACF on IBM mainframes).
- **Interrupt Handling** to multiplex terminal I/O.

**3. Application & Database Layer:** Hosts business logic (e.g., banking transactions) and the central DBMS. All queries funnel to one database engine, guaranteeing ACID consistency.

**4. Remote Terminal Layer:** Users interact via character-based terminals (3270, VT100) or graphical emulators. Each terminal maintains only a screen buffer and a keystroke buffer — no local computation.

**Role of OS in Multi-User Time-Sharing:**

The OS creates the illusion of simultaneous execution by:
- **Time-Slicing:** Allocating a quantum $\tau$ (e.g., 50 ms) to each runnable process.
- **Context Switching:** Saving and restoring CPU registers on every switch (cost $\approx 1$–$10\ \mu s$).
- **Priority Aging:** Preventing starvation of low-priority but long-waiting jobs.
- **Synchronization:** Ensuring that two users editing the same record see consistent data via locking primitives.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A centralized server receives requests at $\lambda = 8$ req/s and services them at $\mu = 10$ req/s. Model the system as M/M/1 and compute: (i) traffic intensity $\rho$, (ii) average number of jobs in system $L$, (iii) average waiting time in queue $W_q$, and (iv) probability of having more than 5 jobs in the system.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Traffic Intensity:**

$$
\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8
$$

Since $\rho < 1$, the system is **stable**.

**(ii) Average Number of Jobs in System $L$:**

$$
L = \frac{\rho}{1 - \rho} = \frac{0.8}{1 - 0.8} = \frac{0.8}{0.2} = 4 \text{ jobs}
$$

**(iii) Average Waiting Time in Queue $W_q$:**

$$
W_q = \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{0.8}{10 - 8} = \frac{0.8}{2} = 0.4 \text{ seconds}
$$

**(iv) Probability of More Than 5 Jobs in the System:**

For an M/M/1 queue, the steady-state probability of having $k$ jobs is:

$$
P_k = (1 - \rho) \cdot \rho^k
$$

$$
P(k > 5) = \sum_{k=6}^{\infty} (1 - \rho) \cdot \rho^k = \rho^6
$$

$$
P(k > 5) = (0.8)^6 = 0.262144 \approx 26.21\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often forget to **square the formula correctly** in part (iv) or confuse $P(k \ge 5)$ with $P(k > 5)$. Re-read carefully: the question asks for **strictly greater than 5**, so the summation starts at $k = 6$. A common error costs 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of centralized computing. How does the "single point of failure" (SPOF) problem limit its modern applicability?**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Simplified Administration** — Only one system to patch, monitor, and back up.
2. **Strong Security Perimeter** — A single firewall/DMZ guards all data.
3. **Data Consistency** — One DBMS instance guarantees ACID semantics without distributed transactions.
4. **Economies of Scale** — Per-user cost drops as user count rises.
5. **Easier Licensing and Auditing** — Software is installed in one place.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** — Host crash ⇒ total outage.
2. **Scalability Ceiling** — Limited by the physical capacity of one machine.
3. **Network Latency** — Distant users suffer high round-trip times.
4. **High Upfront Cost** — Mainframes cost millions of dollars.
5. **Underutilization Risk** — During off-peak hours, the expensive host idles.

**The SPOF Problem in Detail:**

A centralized system has **no built-in redundancy**. When the host fails:
- All in-flight transactions are lost (unless logged).
- All users are simultaneously disconnected.
- Recovery requires manual intervention (MTTR typically hours).

Modern mission-critical systems demand **five-9's availability (99.999%)**, which corresponds to only **5.26 minutes of downtime per year**. A single-mainframe centralized architecture cannot meet this without costly active-active clustering — at which point it has effectively *become* a distributed system.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare centralized computing, distributed computing, and client-server computing along any seven dimensions.**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form Expected by Examiners):**

| Dimension | Centralized | Distributed | Client-Server |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Number of Computers** | One | Many (autonomous) | Two tiers (clients + server) |
| **Resource Location** | Single site | Multiple, networked sites | Server in one place, clients distributed |
| **Failure Impact** | Catastrophic (total) | Graceful degradation | Server crash halts all clients |
| **Data Location** | One central DB | Replicated/partitioned | Centralized on server |
| **Scalability** | Vertical only | Horizontal | Moderate |
| **Communication** | Time-sharing on host | Message passing / RPC | Request-response over network |
| **Cost Model** | High CapEx | Moderate CapEx + OpEx | Low CapEx |
| **Example** | IBM Mainframe | Hadoop cluster | Web browser → Apache server |
| **Heterogeneity** | Homogeneous | Highly heterogeneous | Moderate |
| **Transparency** | N/A | Location, replication, failure transparency | Limited |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For part (b), KTU examiners award **1 mark per meaningful comparison point across all three columns**. Writing only "Centralized is one, Distributed is many" earns just 0.5 marks — be specific (give examples, mention protocols, mention redundancy). Always use a **table**, not paragraphs, for comparison questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1 / Centralized Computing**

- **Definition:** Single host owns all compute, storage, data, and control; users are thin terminals.
- **Origin:** Mainframe era (1960s) — IBM System/360, UNIVAC.
- **Key Tech:** Time-sharing, multiplexing, virtual memory, central DBMS.
- **Architecture Layers:** Terminal → Network → OS Scheduler → CPU/RAM/Disk → Central DB → Output.
- **Advantages (remember 5):** Simple admin, single security perimeter, data consistency, economies of scale, easy licensing.
- **Disadvantages (remember 5):** SPOF, scalability ceiling, network latency, high CapEx, idle wastage.
- **SPOF** is the **#1 KTU-favorite exam term** — always define it and propose mitigation (clustering, replication).
- **M/M/1 Model:** $\rho = \lambda / \mu$, $L = \rho/(1-\rho)$, $W = 1/(\mu-\lambda)$ — memorize cold.
- **Stability Condition:** Always check $\rho < 1$ *before* computing any other metric.
- **Little's Law:** $L = \lambda W$ — used in nearly every queueing derivation.
- **Time-Share Quantum $\tau$:** Typically 10–100 ms; smaller $\tau$ improves fairness but raises context-switch overhead.
- **Cost Argument:** Centralization wins for **high user counts with predictable load** (banking, reservations); loses for **bursty, global-scale workloads** (social media, video streaming).
- **Evolution Path (Module 1 Roadmap):** Mainframe → Minicomputer → Client-Server → Cluster → Grid → Cloud → Fog/Edge.
- **Modern Relevance:** Centralized models persist in **private cloud** and **hyperconverged infrastructure (HCI)** — but with built-in redundancy.
- **Valuation Pearls:** Use bullet points and tables in Part A; use diagrams and step-by-step math in Part B; never use `|` inside table math — use `\vert` or `\mid`.
- **Common Pitfall:** Confusing *centralized data* with *centralized computing* — a system can have centralized data but distributed processing (e.g., a data warehouse with multiple query engines).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "CROPS-SF":** **C**entralized = **C**oncentrated, **R**emote terminals, **O**ne OS, **P**redictable load, **S**ingle DB, **S**POF-prone, **F**ast response (local LAN) but **F**ar users suffer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parallel Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parallel Computing** is a computational paradigm in which multiple processing elements (cores, processors, or computers) work cooperatively to solve a single, large problem by dividing the workload into discrete sub-tasks that can be executed simultaneously. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, it is the simultaneous use of **multiple compute resources** to solve a computational problem, characterized by the decomposition of a problem into independent or loosely-coupled parts, each of which can be executed concurrently on separate processing units.

In the context of **PCCST602 (Advanced Computing Systems)**, parallel computing is treated as the foundational layer upon which heterogeneous architectures, accelerators (GPUs/FPGAs), and distributed systems are built. It is governed by three fundamental properties:

- **Concurrency:** Multiple tasks are *in-flight* at the same logical instant.
- **Parallelism:** Multiple tasks are *physically executing* at the same instant on different hardware units.
- **Scalability:** The system must maintain (or gracefully degrade) performance as the number of processing elements increases.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Restaurant Kitchen" Analogy**
> - **Serial Computing (1 Chef):** A single chef prepares a 5-course meal. He chops, cooks, plates, and serves each course one after the other. The total time is the *sum* of all tasks.
> - **Parallel Computing (5 Chefs):** The head chef divides the work. Chef 1 makes soup, Chef 2 grills, Chef 3 plates desserts, Chef 4 prepares salads, and Chef 5 handles beverages. They all work *at the same time*. Total time drops dramatically.
> - **The catch (Amdahl's Insight):** Some tasks *cannot* be parallelized — only the head chef knows the customer's allergies. This unavoidable serial portion limits how fast the meal can be served, no matter how many extra chefs you hire.

## 1.3 The Core Engineering Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Any parallel computing problem in KTU papers revolves around **three metrics**. Memorize them in **bold**:
> - **Speedup ($S_p$ or $\Psi$):** Ratio of serial execution time to parallel execution time, $S_p = \frac{T_s}{T_p}$.
> - **Efficiency ($\eta$):** Per-processor utilization, $\eta = \frac{S_p}{N}$ (where $N$ is processor count). The theoretical maximum is **1.0 (100%)**.
> - **Throughput ($\Theta$):** Number of tasks completed per unit time. Unlike speedup, it allows for *pipelines* where one slow stage affects all others.

## 1.4 Why Parallel Computing? (The Memory Wall)

> [!NOTE]
> Since ~2005, single-core CPU clock speeds have plateaued (~3–5 GHz) due to **power density limits** (the end of Dennard Scaling). The industry responded by placing **multiple cores on a single die**. A KTU student must understand that "parallelism is no longer an option — it is a necessity driven by the memory wall and the power wall."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Flynn's Taxonomy (1966) — The Foundational Classification

Flynn categorized computer architectures by the *number of instruction streams* and *data streams* operating concurrently. This is **mandatory** for KTU exams.

| Class | Instruction Stream | Data Stream | Real-World Example | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SISD** | Single | Single | Traditional desktop PC | Baseline serial machine |
| **SIMD** | Single | Multiple | GPU kernels, vector units | Data-parallel workloads |
| **MISD** | Multiple | Single | Fault-tolerant pipelines (rare) | Historically theoretical |
| **MIMD** | Multiple | Multiple | Multi-core CPU clusters | Most general-purpose systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern HPC systems are hybrids: a cluster of MIMD nodes, where each node contains SIMD GPU accelerators. KTU 2024 papers frequently ask students to *classify a given architecture* into Flynn's buckets.

## 2.2 The Four Granularities of Parallelism

Parallelism is applied at *different levels* of the program hierarchy. A KTU 14-mark question often asks the student to "explain the levels of parallelism with examples."

1. **Bit-Level Parallelism:** Increasing word size (8-bit → 16-bit → 64-bit). Each operation handles more data per cycle. *Example:* A 64-bit adder completes a 64-bit sum in one cycle that a 32-bit adder needs two cycles for.
2. **Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP):** Executing multiple instructions from a single thread per cycle via *pipelining*, *out-of-order execution*, and *speculation*. Limited by data dependencies.
3. **Data Parallelism:** Same operation applied to many data elements simultaneously. *Example:* Adding two vectors `A[i] + B[i]` for all $i$ in parallel. Dominates GPU programming (CUDA, OpenCL).
4. **Task Parallelism:** Distinct *functions* run in parallel. *Example:* A web server where thread 1 handles HTTP parsing, thread 2 queries the database, and thread 3 renders HTML.

## 2.3 Memory Architecture Taxonomy

This is a **direct KTU syllabus topic** in Module 1.

- **Shared Memory (Uniform Memory Access — UMA):** All processors see one global memory with equal access latency. *Example:* Multicore CPUs using a single DDR5 bus. **Pros:** Easy programming. **Cons:** Scalability bottleneck (memory bus saturation).
- **Distributed Memory:** Each processor owns a *private* local memory; communication is via explicit message passing (MPI, RPC). *Example:* HPC clusters with InfiniBand.
- **Hybrid Memory (NUMA — Non-Uniform Memory Access):** Modern multi-socket servers. Each CPU has local memory (fast) and remote memory (slow, accessed via QPI/UPI). *KTU trap:* Access time to "near" vs. "far" memory differs.

## 2.4 The Master Equation of Parallel Computing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amdahl's Law (1967)** is the single most tested formula in KTU PCCST602 Module 1. It dictates the *theoretical upper bound* of parallel speedup.

$$S(N) = \frac{T_s}{T_p} = \frac{1}{s + \frac{1-s}{N}}$$

where:
- $T_s$ = Serial execution time on a single processor.
- $T_p$ = Parallel execution time on $N$ processors.
- $s$ = Fraction of the program that is *strictly serial* (cannot be parallelized), $0 \le s \le 1$.
- $(1-s)$ = Fraction that *can* be parallelized.
- $N$ = Number of processors.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Devastating Limit:** As $N \to \infty$, the speedup converges to a finite constant. **Even an infinitely parallel machine is bounded by its serial fraction.**

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{s}$$

## 2.5 Gustafson's Law (1988) — The Scaled Counter-Argument

Amdahl's Law assumes the *problem size is fixed* (strong scaling). Gustafson observed that in real engineering, when given more processors, scientists **solve bigger problems**.

$$S_{gustafson}(N) = N - s(N-1) = s + N(1-s)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $s = 0.05$ (5% serial) and $N = 100$ processors:
> - **Amdahl** (fixed problem): $S = 1 / 0.05 = 20\times$
> - **Gustafson** (scaled problem): $S = 0.05 + 100(0.95) = 95.05\times$
> This is why KTU examiners love this contrast — it is a guaranteed 14-mark question on weak vs. strong scaling.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | LaTeX Equation | Variables & Range | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speedup | $S_p = T_s \div T_p$ | $S_p \ge 1$ always | Measures how much faster parallel is. |
| Efficiency | $\eta = S_p \div N$ | $0 \lt \eta \le 1$ | Per-core utilization, target is 1.0. |
| Amdahl's Law | $S(N) = 1 \div (s + (1-s)/N)$ | $0 \le s \le 1$ | Hard limit on fixed-problem speedup. |
| Amdahl Limit | $S_{\max} = 1 \div s$ | As $N \to \infty$ | Theoretical maximum with infinite CPUs. |
| Gustafson's Law | $S_g = s + N(1-s)$ | Scaled workload | Real-world HPC scaling. |
| Karp-Flatt Metric | $e = (1/S_p - 1/N) \div (1 - 1/N)$ | $0 \le e \le 1$ | Detects *hidden* serial bottlenecks. |
| Throughput | $\Theta = W \div T_{total}$ | tasks per second | System-level output rate. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Amdahl's Law

We derive the speedup formula from first principles so that a KTU student can reproduce it under examination conditions.

### Step 1 — Decompose the Serial Runtime

> Let the total execution time on a single processor be $T_s$. Decompose it into a serial portion and a parallel portion.

$$T_s = T_{serial} + T_{parallel}$$

### Step 2 — Define the Serial Fraction

> The *serial fraction* $s$ is the proportion of $T_s$ that cannot be parallelized (I/O, synchronization, single-threaded setup).

$$s = \frac{T_{serial}}{T_s} \quad \text{so} \quad T_{serial} = s \cdot T_s$$

By complementarity, the parallelizable portion is:

$$T_{parallel} = (1 - s) \cdot T_s$$

### Step 3 — Apply $N$ Processors to the Parallel Fraction

> On a single processor, the parallel fraction takes time $(1-s) \cdot T_s$. Distributed ideally across $N$ identical processors, it takes:

$$T_{parallel,\,N} = \frac{(1-s) \cdot T_s}{N}$$

The serial fraction *cannot be divided*, so it remains $s \cdot T_s$ on any number of processors.

### Step 4 — Compute the New Total Parallel Runtime

$$T_p = T_{serial} + T_{parallel,\,N} = s \cdot T_s + \frac{(1-s) \cdot T_s}{N}$$

Factor out $T_s$:

$$T_p = T_s \left( s + \frac{1-s}{N} \right)$$

### Step 5 — Form the Speedup Ratio

> By definition, $S(N) = T_s / T_p$. Substitute the expression from Step 4:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(N) &= \frac{T_s}{T_s \left( s + \frac{1-s}{N} \right)} \\
&= \frac{1}{s + \frac{1-s}{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical KTU form. $\blacksquare$

### Step 6 — The Asymptotic Limit

> Take the limit as $N \to \infty$. The term $(1-s)/N \to 0$:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{s + 0} = \frac{1}{s}$$

Therefore, with $s = 0.02$ (2% serial), no machine on Earth can ever exceed a $50\times$ speedup on this fixed problem. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 7-Mark Sub-Question)

> **Problem:** A program has 80% parallelizable code and 20% strictly serial code. It runs in 100 seconds on one processor.
> **(a)** Find the speedup on 4 processors.
> **(b)** Find the maximum achievable speedup with infinite processors.
> **(c)** Find the efficiency on 4 processors.

### Part (a) — Speedup on 4 Processors

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(4) &= \frac{1}{s + \frac{1-s}{N}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.2 + \frac{0.8}{4}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.2 + 0.2} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.4} \\
&= 2.5\times
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (b) — Infinite-Processor Limit

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= \frac{1}{s} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5\times
\end{aligned}
$$

### Part (c) — Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{S_p}{N} = \frac{2.5}{4} = 0.625 = 62.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Even with infinite CPUs, the program runs only 5× faster. Doubling $N$ from 4 to 8 gives $S(8) = 1 / (0.2 + 0.1) = 3.33\times$ — a *diminishing return*. KTU examiners expect students to comment on this in the answer.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Paradigms

| Property | Amdahl (Strong) | Gustafson (Weak) | Karp-Flatt (Diagnostic) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Workload | Fixed | Scales with $N$ | Fixed |
| Key Equation | $1 \div (s + (1-s)/N)$ | $s + N(1-s)$ | $e = (1/S_p - 1/N) \div (1 - 1/N)$ |
| Best For | Predicting limits | Real HPC runs | Diagnosing hidden overheads |
| KTU Use | 7-mark derivations | 7-mark counter-examples | 3-mark concept question |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Parallel Computing Architecture Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Parallel Computing Paradigm] --> B[Shared Memory]
    A --> C[Distributed Memory]
    A --> D[Hybrid NUMA]

    B --> B1[UMA - Uniform Memory Access]
    B --> B2[Multicore CPUs]
    B --> B3[OpenMP Threads]

    C --> C1[MPI Message Passing]
    C --> C2[HPC Clusters]
    C --> C3[InfiniBand Network]

    D --> D1[Multi-Socket Servers]
    D --> D2[Cache Coherent NUMA ccNUMA]
    D --> D3[QPI UPI Interconnect]

    A --> E[Flynns Taxonomy]
    E --> E1[SISD]
    E --> E2[SIMD GPU]
    E --> E3[MISD]
    E --> E4[MIMD Clusters]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Amdahl's Speedup Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    P0[Single Processor Baseline] --> T1[Total Serial Time Ts]
    T1 --> S1[Serial Fraction s x Ts]
    T1 --> P1[Parallel Fraction 1-s x Ts]
    P1 --> D1[Distribute across N processors]
    D1 --> T2[Parallel time = sTs + 1-s x Ts divided by N]
    S1 --> T2
    T2 --> SU[Speedup S N = Ts divided by Tp]
    SU --> LIM[As N to infinity, S to 1 divided by s]
    SU --> EFF[Efficiency eta = S N divided by N]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Flow — Granularity of Parallelism

```mermaid
graph TD
    G1[Bit Level 8 to 64 bit words] --> G2[Instruction Level Pipelining OoO]
    G2 --> G3[Data Level Vector GPU]
    G3 --> G4[Task Level Threads MPI]
    G4 --> OUT[Full Application Parallelism]

    G1 -.applies to.-> HW[Hardware Gates]
    G2 -.applies to.-> ILP[CPU Pipeline]
    G3 -.applies to.-> GPU[SIMD Units]
    G4 -.applies to.-> SW[Software Threads]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Distributed vs. Shared Memory Layout

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SharedMemoryCluster
        CPU1A[Core 1] --- BUS[Shared Bus / DDR5]
        CPU2A[Core 2] --- BUS
        CPU3A[Core 3] --- BUS
        CPU4A[Core 4] --- BUS
        BUS --- MEM[Global Memory]
    end

    subgraph DistributedMemoryCluster
        CPU1B[Node 1 CPU] --- MEM1[Local Memory 1]
        CPU2B[Node 2 CPU] --- MEM2[Local Memory 2]
        CPU3B[Node 3 CPU] --- MEM3[Local Memory 3]
        MEM1 --- NET[Network MPI]
        MEM2 --- NET
        MEM3 --- NET
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Define parallel computing. Differentiate between *concurrency* and *parallelism* with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Parallel computing is the simultaneous use of multiple processing elements to solve a single problem by dividing it into independent sub-tasks executed concurrently.
- **Concurrency (1 mark):** Multiple tasks *progress* during overlapping time periods but may not be executing at the exact same instant (e.g., a single-core CPU time-slicing between threads).
- **Parallelism (1 mark):** Multiple tasks are *literally executed* at the same physical instant on distinct hardware units (e.g., a 4-core CPU running 4 threads simultaneously).
- *One-line KTU key:* Concurrency is a *software* property; parallelism is a *hardware* property.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q2.** State and explain Amdahl's Law. What is the maximum speedup possible if the serial fraction is 4%?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (1 mark):** Amdahl's Law states that the speedup of a parallel program is bounded by $S(N) = \frac{1}{s + (1-s)/N}$, where $s$ is the serial fraction.
- **Explanation (1 mark):** As the number of processors $N$ increases, the parallel fraction benefits, but the serial fraction remains a bottleneck.
- **Computation (1 mark):** With $s = 0.04$, the maximum speedup is $\frac{1}{0.04} = 25\times$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

### OPTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the different levels of parallelism (bit-level, instruction-level, data-level, and task-level) with real-world examples for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parallel application has a parallelizable fraction of 92% and a serial fraction of 8%. Compute:
- (i) The speedup when $N = 16$ processors are used.
- (ii) The maximum theoretical speedup.
- (iii) The efficiency at $N = 16$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

- **Bit-level (1.5 marks):** Doubling word width halves the cycles needed for arithmetic. Example: a 64-bit integer addition on a 32-bit ALU needs 2 cycles, on a 64-bit ALU needs 1.
- **Instruction-level (1.5 marks):** Achieved through pipelining, multiple-issue, and out-of-order execution. Example: Intel Hyper-Threading duplicates architectural state to issue from two threads.
- **Data-level (2 marks):** A single instruction operates on an array of data. Example: GPU shader adding two 4096-element float vectors element-wise in a single kernel launch.
- **Task-level (2 marks):** Distinct functions run in parallel. Example: MapReduce — the *Map* function runs on $N$ nodes in parallel, followed by a *Reduce* phase.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

- **Given:** $s = 0.08$, $(1-s) = 0.92$, $N = 16$.

**(i) Speedup at $N=16$ (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(16) &= \frac{1}{0.08 + \frac{0.92}{16}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.08 + 0.0575} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.1375} \\
&\approx 7.27\times
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Maximum speedup (2 marks):**

$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{0.08} = 12.5\times
$$

**(iii) Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\eta = \frac{7.27}{16} \approx 0.454 = 45.4\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Trap:**
> 1. Students often confuse the **serial fraction** $s$ with the **parallel fraction** $(1-s)$. In Amdahl's formula, the denominator is `s + (1-s)/N`, NOT `(1-s) + s/N`.
> 2. Efficiency must be reported as a *decimal or percentage*, not as a raw speedup.
> 3. In (a), students forget to give **concrete examples** — definition alone gets only 4/7 marks. KTU strictly requires examples for full marks.

### OPTION B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a)** Discuss Flynn's Classification of computer architectures with neat diagrams. Identify which class a modern multi-core GPU cluster belongs to. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A weather simulation program takes 1000 seconds on a single processor. Parallelization reveals that 95% of the code scales perfectly, but 5% remains serial. Calculate:
- (i) The expected runtime on 32 processors.
- (ii) The speedup obtained.
- (iii) Comment on whether doubling the processors to 64 is justified. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

- **SISD (1.5 marks):** Single Instruction, Single Data. Old desktops. Sequential von Neumann machine.
- **SIMD (1.5 marks):** Single Instruction, Multiple Data. GPUs running CUDA kernels where one warp executes the same `add` on 32 different pixels.
- **MISD (1 mark):** Multiple Instruction, Single Data. Rare; used in fault-tolerant spacecraft computers.
- **MIMD (1.5 marks):** Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data. The general case — each CPU core runs its own thread on its own data.
- **Identification (1.5 marks):** A modern GPU cluster is a **hybrid**: each cluster node is MIMD (multi-core CPU), while the GPUs inside the node operate in **SIMD** mode (warps of 32 threads execute lockstep).

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $T_s = 1000$ s, $s = 0.05$, $N = 32$.

**(i) Runtime on 32 processors (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_p(32) &= T_s \left( 0.05 + \frac{0.95}{32} \right) \\
&= 1000 \times (0.05 + 0.0296875) \\
&= 1000 \times 0.0796875 \\
&= 79.6875 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Speedup (2 marks):**

$$
S(32) = \frac{1000}{79.6875} \approx 12.55\times
$$

**(iii) Justification of doubling to 64 (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(64) &= \frac{1}{0.05 + \frac{0.95}{64}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.01484} \approx 15.38\times \\
\Delta S &= 15.38 - 12.55 = 2.83\times \text{ gain}
\end{aligned}
$$

Doubling from 32 → 64 processors yields only a $2.83\times$ speedup gain (i.e., efficiency drops from 39% to 24%). It is **not economically justified** unless the simulation must be completed in a hard real-time deadline.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Trap:**
> 1. Many students report runtime in **milliseconds** while the question specifies **seconds** — instant 1-mark deduction.
> 2. For (a), students often draw a **table** instead of a **diagram** — KTU specifically asks for "neat diagrams." A box-and-arrow Mermaid-style block earns full credit.
> 3. The "comment" sub-part is mandatory. A numerical answer without justification loses 1 of the 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary (Memorize Before Exam)**

- **Definition:** Parallel computing = simultaneous execution of sub-tasks of a single problem on multiple processors.
- **Three Metrics:** Speedup $S_p = T_s/T_p$, Efficiency $\eta = S_p/N$, Throughput $\Theta = W/T$.
- **Flynn's Four:** SISD, SIMD, MISD, MIMD. Modern clusters = MIMD of SIMD nodes.
- **Granularity Hierarchy:** Bit $\to$ Instruction $\to$ Data $\to$ Task.
- **Memory Models:** Shared (UMA), Distributed (MPI), Hybrid (NUMA/ccNUMA).
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S(N) = 1 / (s + (1-s)/N)$. Limit is $1/s$.
- **Gustafson's Law:** $S_g = s + N(1-s)$. Used for weak/strong scaling distinction.
- **Karp-Flatt:** $e = (1/S_p - 1/N)/(1 - 1/N)$. Detects hidden serial portions.
- **The Memory Wall & Power Wall** are the *root cause* of multi-core adoption since 2005.
- **Pipelining ≠ Parallelism** — pipelining overlaps *stages*, not *tasks* (single instruction stream).
- **Efficiency $\le 1$ always** — if you compute $>1$, your formula has an error.
- **Always report units** — KTU deducts 0.5 marks for unit-less efficiency.
- **Amdahl vs. Gustafson:** Amdahl assumes fixed workload; Gustafson assumes workload scales with $N$. Real HPC follows Gustafson.
- **KTU 14-Mark Structure:** Expect 7 marks for *theory* (with diagrams) + 7 marks for *numerical computation* (with justification).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distributed and Cloud Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Distributed and Cloud Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Distributed Computing — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
