> **Distributed Computing** is a model in which components of a software system are shared among multiple, loosely coupled, geographically separated, and independently running computers (nodes) that coordinate their actions by passing messages over a communication network. The system is perceived by its users as a single, coherent computing platform, even though its operations span multiple autonomous machines.

The key emphasis here is **transparency** — the end user (or the application programmer) should not need to know that the system is split across many machines. This is formalized by the **ISO/ISO-ECMA Open Distributed Processing (ODP)** reference model and the seminal work of **Andrew S. Tanenbaum & Maarten van Steen**, the de-facto academic textbook references for distributed systems in B.Tech curricula.

### Core Properties of a Distributed System (The "Three Pillars")

| # | Property | Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|
| 1 | **Multiple Autonomous Computers** | Each node runs its own OS and clock. |
| 2 | **Connected by a Network** | Nodes communicate via message passing (TCP/IP, RDMA, etc.). |
| 3 | **Coordinated via Shared State** | Middleware, consensus algorithms, or distributed protocols ensure consistency. |

### Types of Distributed Systems (Taxonomy by Coupling)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect students to **classify** a given scenario into one of the four canonical types below. This is a **favourite 3-mark question** every semester.

1. **Distributed Computing Systems** — Cluster computing, Grid computing, Cloud computing. Goal: high performance / scalability. Example: SETI@home, Hadoop.
2. **Distributed Information Systems** — Transaction processing (OLTP) and enterprise application integration (EAI). Example: Online banking systems, E-commerce platforms.
3. **Distributed Pervasive/Embedded Systems** — Smart environments, IoT, sensor networks. Example: Smart homes, mobile ad-hoc networks (MANETs).
4. **Cloud-Based Distributed Systems** — Virtualized elastic resources delivered as services. Example: AWS EC2, Google Cloud Storage.

---

## 1.2 Cloud Computing — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **NIST SP 800-145 Definition (the GOLD standard for KTU boards):**
> **Cloud Computing** is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with **minimal management effort or service provider interaction**.

### The Five Essential Characteristics of Cloud (NIST)

1. **On-Demand Self-Service** — Provision resources automatically without human intervention at the provider.
2. **Broad Network Access** — Available over the network through standard mechanisms (HTTP, REST, gRPC) used by heterogeneous client platforms (mobile, laptop, IoT).
3. **Resource Pooling** — Physical and virtual resources are dynamically assigned and reassigned to multiple tenants (multi-tenancy).
4. **Rapid Elasticity** — Capacity scales out/in (horizontal scaling) or up/down (vertical scaling) to match demand in real time.
5. **Measured Service** — Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging metering at a level appropriate to the service (pay-per-use, utility computing).

### Service Models (The "Cloud Stack")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the **IaaS / PaaS / SaaS** pyramid — it appears in nearly every KTU exam paper either as a direct question or as a comparison table.

| Layer | Full Form | User Manages | Provider Manages | Example |
|-------|-----------|--------------|------------------|---------|
| **SaaS** | Software as a Service | Only data and access | Everything else | Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce |
| **PaaS** | Platform as a Service | Application + data | OS, runtime, middleware, servers | Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk |
| **IaaS** | Infrastructure as a Service | OS, runtime, app, data | Virtualization, servers, storage, networking | AWS EC2, Azure VM, Google Compute Engine |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick:** *“You can run any software (IaaS), build your own (PaaS), or just use the provider’s (SaaS).”*

### Deployment Models

1. **Public Cloud** — Open for general public use (AWS, Azure, GCP). Owned by a cloud provider.
2. **Private Cloud** — Used exclusively by a single organization. Higher control & security.
3. **Hybrid Cloud** — Composition of two or more clouds (public + private) bound by orchestration. Used for "cloud bursting".
4. **Community Cloud** — Shared by organizations with common concerns (e.g., government, healthcare).
5. **Multi-Cloud** — Strategy of using multiple public cloud providers to avoid vendor lock-in.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Electricity Grid for Computing"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy:**
> Think of cloud computing as the **electricity grid** for IT.
> - In the 1900s, factories had to **build their own power plants** (on-premise servers).
> - Today, they just **plug into the grid** and pay for the kilowatt-hours they consume.
> - **Cloud computing = Utility computing.** You pay only for the compute, storage, and bandwidth you consume. You don't buy, install, or maintain the "power plant" (servers, cooling, networking, datacenter).

**Distributed computing**, by contrast, is the **internal power distribution network** of a smart city: many substations (nodes) coordinating to keep the lights on even if one substation fails. The cloud IS a distributed system, but a distributed system is not necessarily a cloud (it could be a peer-to-peer file sharing network or a corporate intranet).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Distributed System Architecture Models

### 2.1.1 Logical Architecture Layers

A distributed system is organized into four logical layers, each with increasing abstraction:

1. **Hardware Layer** — Physical nodes, network interface cards, switches, routers, optical fiber.
2. **Operating System Layer** — Process scheduling, memory management, I/O handling on each node.
3. **Middleware Layer** — The "magic glue" that hides heterogeneity (CORBA, gRPC, RMI, Apache Thrift).
4. **Application Layer** — The end-user logic (banking app, social network, scientific simulation).

> [!NOTE]
> The **middleware layer is what differentiates a true distributed system from a mere network of independent machines.**

### 2.1.2 Architectural Styles (Master-Slave vs. Peer-to-Peer)

| Style | Description | Pros | Cons | Example |
|-------|-------------|------|------|---------|
| **Client–Server (2-tier)** | Server hosts services; clients consume them. | Simple, secure, easy to maintain. | Single point of failure, scalability bottleneck. | Web applications (HTTP) |
| **3-tier / n-tier** | Presentation, Business Logic, Data tiers separated. | Modularity, scalability, fault isolation. | More complex, latency between tiers. | E-commerce sites |
| **Master-Slave** | A coordinator (master) assigns work to workers (slaves). | Centralized control, simple task distribution. | Master is a single point of failure. | Hadoop MapReduce, MySQL Replication |
| **Peer-to-Peer (P2P)** | All nodes are equal; resources shared directly. | High fault tolerance, no single point of failure. | Hard to coordinate, security challenges. | BitTorrent, Blockchain, IPFS |
| **Microservices** | Application decomposed into independent, deployable services. | Polyglot, independently scalable, resilient. | Distributed transactions are hard, debugging complex. | Netflix, Uber, Amazon |
| **Serverless / FaaS** | Functions executed on-demand in stateless containers. | Zero ops, pay-per-execution, auto-scaling. | Cold start latency, vendor lock-in, complex debugging. | AWS Lambda, Azure Functions |

---

## 2.2 The CAP Theorem (Brewer's Theorem — *MANDATORY for KTU*)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 2000):** In any distributed data store, you can simultaneously deliver **at most TWO** of the following three guarantees:
> - **C** — **Consistency** (every read receives the most recent write or an error)
> - **A** — **Availability** (every request receives a response, even if it is stale)
> - **P** — **Partition Tolerance** (the system continues to operate despite arbitrary message loss between nodes)

### 2.2.1 Why P is Non-Negotiable

In any real distributed system, network partitions **will** occur (links fail, switches crash, packets drop). Therefore, partitions are **not optional** — they are a fact of life. So the real-world choice is between:

| Choice | Guarantee | Real Systems |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| **CP** | Consistency + Partition Tolerance (sacrifice availability) | HBase, MongoDB (default), Redis (with cluster), etcd, ZooKeeper |
| **AP** | Availability + Partition Tolerance (sacrifice strong consistency) | Cassandra, CouchDB, DynamoDB, Riak |
| **CA** | Consistency + Availability (impossible in real distributed systems with partitions) | Traditional RDBMS (single-node Oracle, PostgreSQL) |

### 2.2.2 Mathematical Formulation of CAP

CAP can be expressed as a constraint on the system state $S$ over a network partition $\mathcal{P}$:

$$
\forall \, \mathcal{P} \neq \emptyset \; : \quad \text{Guarantee}(C) \oplus \text{Guarantee}(A) \oplus \text{Guarantee}(P) \leq 2
$$

Where $\oplus$ denotes a logical OR over the three guarantees, bounded above by 2. Once $\mathcal{P} \neq \emptyset$, the system can satisfy at most two of $C$, $A$, $P$.

---

## 2.3 Cloud Computing — Deep Architecture

### 2.3.1 Cloud Reference Architecture (NIST SP 500-292)

The NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture identifies **five major actors**:

1. **Cloud Consumer** — Person/organization that uses cloud services.
2. **Cloud Provider** — Person/organization that makes services available (AWS, Azure).
3. **Cloud Auditor** — Conducts independent assessment of cloud services (security, performance).
4. **Cloud Broker** — Manages the use, performance, and delivery of cloud services; negotiates relationships between providers and consumers.
5. **Cloud Carrier** — The intermediary that provides connectivity and transport of cloud services (ISP, telecom).

### 2.3.2 Virtualization & Hypervisors — The Heart of the Cloud

**Virtualization** is the technology that enables cloud computing by abstracting physical hardware into logical resources.

- **Type 1 (Bare-Metal) Hypervisor** — Runs directly on hardware. Examples: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen, KVM.
- **Type 2 (Hosted) Hypervisor** — Runs as an application on a host OS. Examples: VirtualBox, VMware Workstation.

> [!NOTE]
> A **Virtual Machine (VM)** is an isolated, replicated copy of a physical machine, with its own virtual BIOS, virtual CPU, virtual RAM, and virtual disk. **Containers** (Docker, containerd) are a *lighter-weight* alternative that share the host OS kernel but isolate the user-space.

### 2.3.3 Cloud Performance Metrics (Mandatory Formulas)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the high-yield formulas for cloud/distributed system performance analysis. These appear in **14-mark Part B questions** as "calculate utilization/throughput/availability" problems.

#### A. **Availability (A)** — The "Uptime" Metric

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

Where:
- $\text{MTBF}$ = Mean Time Between Failures (hours)
- $\text{MTTR}$ = Mean Time To Repair (hours)

#### B. **Service Level Agreement (SLA) — "Number of Nines"**

$$
\text{Availability} = 1 - \left(\frac{\text{Downtime}}{\text{Period}}\right)
$$

| Nines | Availability | Annual Downtime | Use Case |
|-------|--------------|-----------------|----------|
| 1 Nine | 90% | 36.5 days | Personal blog |
| 2 Nines | 99% | 3.65 days | Internal tool |
| 3 Nines | 99.9% | 8.77 hours | SMB website |
| 4 Nines | 99.99% | 52.6 minutes | E-commerce, banking |
| 5 Nines | 99.999% | 5.26 minutes | Telecom core, medical |
| 6 Nines | 99.9999% | 31.5 seconds | Space mission control |

#### C. **Throughput (T)** — Operations per Unit Time

$$
T = \frac{N_{\text{ops}}}{t_{\text{total}}}
$$

#### D. **Speedup (S) — Amdahl's Law**

> [!NOTE]
> **Amdahl's Law** is the foundational equation for parallel/distributed performance. The *maximum* theoretical speedup when parallelizing a workload is bounded by the serial portion $s$ of the code.

$$
S(n) = \frac{1}{s + \frac{1-s}{n}}
$$

Where:
- $n$ = number of parallel processing nodes
- $s$ = fraction of execution time that is inherently serial (cannot be parallelized)
- $S(n)$ = speedup achieved with $n$ processors

The **theoretical maximum speedup** as $n \to \infty$ is:

$$
S_{\max} = \lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) = \frac{1}{s}
$$

This shows the fundamental limit: even infinite parallel hardware cannot speed up a task beyond $1/s$ if a fraction $s$ is serial.

#### E. **Scalability Efficiency**

$$
E = \frac{S(n)}{n} = \frac{1}{n \left(s + \frac{1-s}{n}\right)} = \frac{1}{1 + s(n-1)}
$$

#### F. **Cost-Performance Ratio (CPR)**

$$
\text{CPR} = \frac{\text{Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)}}{\text{Performance (throughput, latency, etc.)}}
$$

In cloud economics, the **pay-as-you-go** model aims to minimize CPR through:
- **Resource Right-Sizing** (matching instance type to workload).
- **Auto-Scaling** (scaling out during peak, scaling in during off-peak).
- **Spot/Preemptible Instances** (using spare capacity at 60–90% discount).

#### G. **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)**

$$
\text{BDP} = \text{Bandwidth} \times \text{Round-Trip Time (RTT)}
$$

This is critical for tuning TCP window sizes in high-latency distributed workloads.

---

## 2.4 Fault Tolerance, Replication & Consistency Models

### 2.4.1 Failure Models in Distributed Systems

| Failure Type | Description | Detection |
|--------------|-------------|-----------|
| **Crash Failure** | Node stops executing but its data is intact. | Heartbeat timeout. |
| **Omission Failure** | Node fails to send/receive messages. | Acknowledgment timeout. |
| **Timing Failure** | Node's response is outside the expected time interval. | Watchdog timers. |
| **Byzantine Failure** | Node behaves arbitrarily / maliciously (e.g., returns conflicting values to different observers). | Requires $3f+1$ replicas to tolerate $f$ byzantine faults. |
| **Network Partition** | Network splits into sub-networks that cannot communicate. | Link failure detection. |

### 2.4.2 Consistency Models

| Model | Read Returns | System Example |
|-------|--------------|----------------|
| **Strong (Linearizability)** | The most recent write across all nodes. | Google Spanner, ZooKeeper |
| **Sequential** | Some legal sequential order of all operations. | In-memory stores |
| **Causal** | Writes that are causally related are seen in order. | Cosmos DB (default) |
| **Eventual** | All replicas converge to the same value *given enough time* with no new writes. | Amazon S3 (eventual listing), DNS |
| **Read-your-writes** | A process always sees its own writes immediately. | Most cloud databases |

### 2.4.3 Replication Strategies

1. **Active Replication** — All replicas process every request. High availability, high resource cost.
2. **Passive Replication (Primary-Backup)** — One primary processes writes; backups receive log updates. Cheaper, but failover delay.
3. **Quorum-Based Replication** — Reads and writes succeed only when $R$ replicas respond for read and $W$ for write, with $R + W > N$ (N = total replicas). Example: DynamoDB's $W=2, R=2, N=3$ for fast reads.

---

## 2.5 Virtualization, Containers, and Serverless — Real-World Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cloud stack in 2024 is layered as: **Bare Metal → Hypervisor → Guest VMs → Container Runtime → Application Containers → Serverless Functions.**

### 2.5.1 Docker & Kubernetes (Container Orchestration)

- **Docker** packages an application + its dependencies into a single, portable **container image** (defined by a `Dockerfile`).
- **Kubernetes (K8s)** is the de-facto container orchestrator. It manages deployment, scaling (HPA = Horizontal Pod Autoscaler), and self-healing (restart on failure) of containers across a cluster.
- **Pods** are the smallest deployable units — they can contain 1+ tightly coupled containers sharing a network namespace.

### 2.5.2 Serverless (Function as a Service — FaaS)

- **AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions** execute code in stateless, event-driven containers.
- **Cold start latency** is a key challenge (typically 100ms–2s for first invocation).
- **Use cases**: image processing, IoT data pipelines, chatbots, scheduled jobs, glue logic between microservices.
- **Pricing model**: pay per **GB-second of compute** + number of invocations.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utilities

| Field | Use of Distributed + Cloud |
|-------|----------------------------|
| **Banking (e.g., SBI, Federal Bank)** | Distributed OLTP for global transactions; hybrid cloud for compliance. |
| **Healthcare** | Federated learning across hospitals without sharing patient data. |
| **Aerospace (ISRO, NASA)** | Distributed telemetry processing on ground stations; cloud ML for satellite image analysis. |
| **Smart Cities (Kerala KSITM)** | IoT sensor data ingested via edge → processed on cloud (Kerala Fibre Optic Network). |
| **E-Commerce (Flipkart Big Billion Days)** | Auto-scaling to handle 10x traffic; CDN edge caching (CloudFront, Akamai). |
| **Scientific Computing (LIGO, CERN)** | Volunteer computing (BOINC) + grid computing to analyze petabytes. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Amdahl's Law (Speedup Bound)

> [!NOTE]
> Amdahl's Law is the **most-tested equation** in the KTU Advanced Computing Systems module. Let us derive it from first principles so that you can reconstruct it in the exam hall.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Let a program take total time $T$ on a single processor. Partition this into two parts:
- **Serial part** that takes time $sT$ (where $0 \leq s \leq 1$).
- **Parallelizable part** that takes time $(1 - s)T$.

### 3.1.2 Single-Processor Baseline Time

$$
T_1 = sT + (1 - s)T = T
$$

(Trivially the whole task runs on one processor.)

### 3.1.3 Multi-Processor Execution

With $n$ identical processors, the **serial portion cannot be parallelized** — it still takes $sT$ time. The **parallel portion** is divided evenly among the $n$ processors, so it takes:

$$
T_{\text{parallel}}(n) = \frac{(1 - s)T}{n}
$$

### 3.1.4 Total Parallel Time

$$
T_n = sT + \frac{(1 - s)T}{n}
$$

### 3.1.5 Definition of Speedup

Speedup $S(n)$ is the ratio of the single-processor time to the $n$-processor time:

$$
S(n) = \frac{T_1}{T_n} = \frac{T}{sT + \frac{(1-s)T}{n}}
$$

Factor out $T$:

$$
S(n) = \frac{1}{s + \frac{1-s}{n}}
$$

This is **Amdahl's Law** — the canonical form. Q.E.D.

### 3.1.6 Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample KTU Problem:**
> "A distributed system executes a workload where 20% of the code is inherently serial and the remaining 80% is parallelizable. Find the speedup with 8 processors, and the maximum theoretical speedup with infinite processors."

**Solution:**

Given: $s = 0.20$, $n = 8$, $1 - s = 0.80$.

**Step 1 — Compute speedup with $n = 8$:**

$$
S(8) = \frac{1}{0.20 + \frac{0.80}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.10} = \frac{1}{0.30} = 3.33
$$

So 8 processors give only **3.33× speedup** — far less than the ideal 8×.

**Step 2 — Maximum speedup as $n \to \infty$:**

$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{s} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5
$$

**Interpretation:** Even with infinite processors, the workload can never run faster than **5×** because the 20% serial portion forms a hard ceiling.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Availability with Series and Parallel Redundancy

### 3.2.1 Series Configuration (No Redundancy)

If two components $C_1$ and $C_2$ are connected in **series**, the system fails if **either** component fails. The system's availability $A_{\text{series}}$ is the product of individual availabilities:

$$
A_{\text{series}} = A_1 \times A_2
$$

**Proof:** Both components must be available for the system to be available. The events are independent:

$$
A_{\text{series}} = P(\text{System Up}) = P(C_1 \text{ Up}) \cdot P(C_2 \text{ Up}) = A_1 \cdot A_2
$$

For $n$ components in series:

$$
A_{\text{series}}^{(n)} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i
$$

### 3.2.2 Parallel Configuration (Redundancy)

If two components are in **parallel**, the system fails only if **both** fail. Therefore:

$$
A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)
$$

**Proof:** The system is down only when both components are down. The "down" probability of each is $(1 - A_i)$. Independent events multiply:

$$
A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)
$$

For $n$ identical components in parallel with availability $A$ each:

$$
A_{\text{parallel}}^{(n)} = 1 - (1 - A)^n
$$

### 3.2.3 Worked Example — SLA Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample KTU Problem:**
> "A cloud datacenter has an individual server availability of 99%. If a service is replicated across 3 such servers in parallel, what is the new system availability? Comment on the SLA in terms of 'nines'."

**Solution:**

Given: $A_1 = A_2 = A_3 = 0.99$.

**Step 1 — Apply parallel formula:**

$$
A_{\text{system}} = 1 - (1 - 0.99)^3 = 1 - (0.01)^3 = 1 - 0.000001 = 0.999999
$$

**Step 2 — Express as percentage:**

$$
A_{\text{system}} = 99.9999\% \approx 6 \text{ Nines}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate annual downtime:**

A non-leap year has $365 \times 24 = 8760$ hours.

$$
\text{Downtime} = (1 - A) \times 8760 = 0.000001 \times 8760 = 0.00876 \text{ hours} = 31.5 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Interpretation:** By adding just 2 redundant servers in parallel, the availability jumped from 2 nines (99%) to **6 nines (99.9999%)**. This is the mathematical foundation of cloud SLAs like AWS's 99.99% EC2 SLA.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Quorum Intersection in Replicated Storage

### 3.3.1 Setup

We have $N$ replicas. A write succeeds when at least $W$ replicas acknowledge. A read succeeds when at least $R$ replicas respond.

**Goal:** Guarantee that the read set *intersects* with the latest write set, so the most recent write is always visible.

### 3.3.2 The Quorum Condition

For intersection to be guaranteed:

$$
R + W > N
$$

**Proof sketch (combinatorial):**

The write set has size $W$. The read set has size $R$. There are $N$ total replicas.

If $R + W \leq N$, it is possible to *disjointly* choose $R$ replicas for the read and $W$ other replicas for the write. In that case, the reader could see stale data. Hence, we require $R + W > N$ to **force** an overlap of at least:

$$
|R \cap W| \geq R + W - N
$$

For DynamoDB's default: $N = 3$, $W = 2$, $R = 2$. Then $R + W = 4 > 3$ ✅. The intersection has at least $4 - 3 = 1$ replica, ensuring the reader sees the latest write.

### 3.3.3 Common Configurations

| $N$ | $R$ | $W$ | Use Case |
|-----|-----|-----|----------|
| 3 | 1 | 1 | Fast reads, weak consistency (no quorum). |
| 3 | 2 | 2 | Balanced (DynamoDB default). |
| 3 | 3 | 1 | Fast writes, slow reads. |
| 5 | 3 | 3 | Strong consistency, high durability. |
| 5 | 2 | 3 | Write-heavy, slightly weaker reads. |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Distributed System Trade-offs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Compare X vs Y on parameters A, B, C, D."* Use these pre-built matrices in your exam.

### 3.4.1 Distributed System vs. Parallel System

| Parameter | Distributed System | Parallel System |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Node Coupling** | Loosely coupled, message passing. | Tightly coupled, shared memory/bus. |
| **Location** | Geographically distributed. | Physically co-located. |
| **Clock** | No global clock. | Often has global clock (e.g., HPC). |
| **Failure Model** | Independent failures, partitions. | Often assumed fail-stop. |
| **Communication** | Asynchronous, high latency. | Synchronous, low latency (μs). |
| **Examples** | Hadoop, Cassandra, Kubernetes. | Supercomputers, multi-core CPUs, GPUs. |

### 3.4.2 Cloud Computing vs. Grid Computing vs. Cluster Computing

| Parameter | Cluster | Grid | Cloud |
|-----------|---------|------|-------|
| **Geographical Spread** | Single room/datacenter. | Cross-organizational, multi-site. | Global (regions + zones). |
| **Coupling** | Tight (LAN, InfiniBand). | Loose (WAN). | Variable (LAN/WAN/Edge). |
| **Ownership** | Single org. | Multiple orgs federated. | Public: cloud provider; Private: in-house. |
| **Resource Type** | Homogeneous (same HW). | Heterogeneous. | Highly virtualized, elastic. |
| **Provisioning** | Static, slow. | Static, negotiated. | Dynamic, on-demand. |
| **Workload** | HPC, scientific. | Scientific, volunteer. | Web, enterprise, ML, IoT, SaaS. |
| **Middleware** | Message Passing Interface (MPI), LSF, SLURM. | Globus Toolkit, gLite. | Kubernetes, OpenStack, AWS APIs. |

### 3.4.3 IaaS vs. PaaS vs. SaaS — Engineering Trade-offs

| Parameter | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|-----------|------|------|------|
| **Control** | High (you manage OS). | Medium (you manage app). | None (use as-is). |
| **Flexibility** | Maximum. | High for the chosen platform. | Limited to provider features. |
| **Time to Deploy** | Minutes to hours. | Seconds to minutes. | Instant. |
| **Vendor Lock-in** | Low (migrate VMs). | Medium (depends on platform). | High (data + workflow tied). |
| **Best For** | Lift-and-shift migration, custom stacks. | API/microservice development. | Email, CRM, productivity. |
| **Cost** | Pay for VM-hours. | Pay for app-hours / requests. | Pay per user/month. |

### 3.4.4 Containers vs. Virtual Machines

| Parameter | Virtual Machine | Container (Docker) |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Boot Time** | 30s–2min. | < 1s. |
| **Size** | Gigabytes (full OS). | Megabytes (app + libs). |
| **OS Overhead** | Each VM runs its own kernel. | Containers share host kernel. |
| **Isolation** | Strong (HW-level). | Process-level (weaker). |
| **Density** | 10s of VMs per host. | 100s–1000s per host. |
| **Use Case** | Legacy apps, full OS control. | Microservices, CI/CD, serverless. |

## 4.3 CAP Theorem Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["DESIGNING A DISTRIBUTED DATASTORE"]
    q1{"IS NETWORK PARTITION TOLERANCE REQUIRED?"}
    a1["GO RDBMS. Single-node, no distribution."]
    q2{"NEED STRONG CONSISTENCY DESPITE PARTITIONS?"}
    cp["CHOOSE CP SYSTEM. HBase, MongoDB strict, etcd, ZooKeeper. Sacrifices availability during partitions."]
    ap["CHOOSE AP SYSTEM. Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB. Sacrifices strong consistency - read may be stale."]
    q3{"WHICH IS THE BUSINESS PRIORITY?"}
    cons["PRIORITIZE CONSISTENCY. Financial transactions, inventory."]
    avail["PRIORITIZE AVAILABILITY. Social media feeds, shopping carts, sensor data."]

    start --> q1
    q1 -->|No| a1
    q1 -->|Yes| q2
    q2 -->|Yes| cp
    q2 -->|No| q3
    q3 --> cons
    q3 --> avail
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam tip:** When asked "Which of CAP does system X prioritize?", look at how the system handles a partition:
> - If it **refuses writes** to maintain consistency → **CP**.
> - If it **accepts writes** on either side and reconciles later → **AP**.

## 4.4 Cloud Workload Distribution — Auto-Scaling Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    req["INCOMING USER REQUEST"] --> lb["CLOUD LOAD BALANCER"]
    lb --> hm["HEALTH MONITOR"]

    hm -->|Instance healthy| route["Route to least-loaded instance"]
    hm -->|Instance unhealthy| term["Terminate unhealthy VM"]
    route --> work["INSTANCE PROCESSES REQUEST"]

    term --> scale["AUTO SCALING GROUP TRIGGERS"]
    work --> metrics["EMIT METRICS to CloudWatch"]
    metrics --> eval{"SCALING RULE EVALUATED"}
    eval -->|CPU > 70 percent for 5 min| out["SCALE OUT - Launch 2 new VMs"]
    eval -->|CPU < 30 percent for 10 min| inn["SCALE IN - Terminate 1 VM"]
    eval -->|Normal| keep["KEEP CURRENT CAPACITY"]
    out --> lb
    inn --> lb
    keep --> lb
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** This is the **horizontal elasticity loop** that makes cloud computing economically superior to traditional hosting. Capacity adjusts to demand every few minutes — pay only for what you use.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    capp1["Container 1 - Node App"]
    capp2["Container 2 - Python App"]
    capp3["Container 3 - Java App"]
    crun["CONTAINER RUNTIME - containerd, runc"]
    hos["HOST OPERATING SYSTEM KERNEL"]
    hw2["BARE METAL HARDWARE"]

    capp1 --> crun
    capp2 --> crun
    capp3 --> crun
    crun --> hos
    hos --> hw2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison insight:** In the VM stack, each guest runs its **own kernel**, providing hardware-level isolation but at the cost of GBs per instance. In the container stack, all containers share the **host kernel** — no OS boot, just process isolation — yielding 10–100x higher density.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part A questions are direct, definitional, and test your ability to **recall and explain concisely**. Word count: 60–90 words. Use bullet points. No diagrams required (but a one-liner sketch helps).

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] — 3 Marks
**"Differentiate between distributed computing and cloud computing with suitable examples."**

**Model Answer:**

- **Distributed Computing** is a *general architectural paradigm* where multiple autonomous computers coordinate over a network to achieve a common goal. Example: SETI@home using volunteers' PCs, or Hadoop cluster processing big data.
- **Cloud Computing** is a *specific delivery model* (utility computing) where virtualized resources (compute, storage, services) are provisioned on-demand over the internet. Example: AWS EC2, Microsoft Azure.
- **Relationship:** All clouds are distributed systems, but not all distributed systems are clouds. A cloud is a *commercial, elastic, multi-tenant* distributed system.
- **Key difference:** Cloud adds **on-demand self-service, elasticity, and pay-per-use metering** on top of the distributed foundation.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 3 Marks
**"List and briefly explain the five essential characteristics of cloud computing as per the NIST definition."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **On-demand self-service** — Consumer provisions compute/storage automatically without human interaction with the provider.
2. **Broad network access** — Capabilities are available over the network and accessed through standard mechanisms (HTTP, REST) by diverse clients (mobile, laptop, IoT).
3. **Resource pooling** — Provider's physical and virtual resources are pooled to serve multiple consumers using a multi-tenant model with dynamic re-assignment.
4. **Rapid elasticity** — Capabilities can be scaled out or in, often automatically, to match demand. The consumer perceives the resource as unlimited.
5. **Measured service** — Cloud systems automatically meter resource usage (CPU hours, storage GB, bandwidth) and bill accordingly (pay-per-use).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions are **14 marks** with **internal choice** (you attempt one of two options). Each choice typically has **two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Sub-part (a) is usually a descriptive/comparison question, sub-part (b) is a derivation or numerical problem.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **CAP theorem** in detail. Discuss the trade-offs between Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance with one real-world system example for each combination (CP, AP, CA).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A parallel workload in a distributed system has 15% inherently serial code and 85% parallelizable code. **Calculate:**
1. The speedup achieved with 16 processors.
2. The maximum theoretical speedup with infinite processors.
3. The scalability efficiency with 16 processors.
4. Comment on what happens if the serial portion increases to 25%.

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — CAP Theorem

**Definition (2 marks):**
The CAP theorem, formulated by Eric Brewer in 2000 and proven by Gilbert and Lynch in 2002, states that in any distributed data store, you can simultaneously guarantee at most **two** of the following three properties:

- **C — Consistency:** Every read returns the most recent committed write, as if there were a single copy of the data.
- **A — Availability:** Every request (read or write) receives a non-error response, even if some nodes are down.
- **P — Partition Tolerance:** The system continues to operate despite arbitrary message loss between nodes due to network failures.

**Why Partition Tolerance is Non-Negotiable (2 marks):**
In a real distributed system spanning multiple machines, network failures (cable cuts, switch failures, congestion) are inevitable. Therefore, the system designer must always assume $P$ is true. The actual choice is between **CP** (consistency over availability) or **AP** (availability over consistency). A pure CA system is impossible in any realistic distributed deployment.

**Trade-offs with Examples (3 marks):**

| Combination | Trade-off | Real System | Reason |
|-------------|-----------|-------------|--------|
| **CP** | Sacrifices availability during partitions. | **HBase, MongoDB (strict mode), etcd, ZooKeeper** | These systems refuse writes on the minority side of a partition to maintain a single consistent view. Used in leader election, config stores. |
| **AP** | Sacrifices strong consistency; reads may be stale. | **Cassandra, Amazon DynamoDB, CouchDB, Riak** | These accept writes on both sides of a partition; conflict resolution happens later via vector clocks or last-write-wins. Used in shopping carts, social feeds, IoT. |
| **CA** | Sacrifices partition tolerance (effectively single-node). | **Traditional RDBMS** — PostgreSQL, MySQL (single instance), Oracle | In a single-node or in tightly coupled cluster without partitions, consistency + availability is possible. But once you add distribution, partitions become real. |

---

#### Part (b) — Amdahl's Law Calculation

**Given:**
- $s = 0.15$ (15% serial)
- $1 - s = 0.85$ (85% parallelizable)
- $n = 16$ processors

**Step 1 — Speedup with $n = 16$:** (2 marks)

$$
S(16) = \frac{1}{0.15 + \frac{0.85}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.15 + 0.053125} = \frac{1}{0.203125} = 4.923
$$

So $S(16) \approx \mathbf{4.92 \times}$.

**Step 2 — Maximum theoretical speedup as $n \to \infty$:** (2 marks)

$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{s} = \frac{1}{0.15} = 6.667 \approx \mathbf{6.67 \times}
$$

**Step 3 — Scalability efficiency with $n = 16$:** (2 marks)

$$
E(16) = \frac{S(16)}{n} = \frac{4.923}{16} = 0.308 = \mathbf{30.8\%}
$$

**Step 4 — When serial portion becomes $s' = 0.25$:** (1 mark)

$$
S_{\max}' = \frac{1}{0.25} = \mathbf{4.0 \times}
$$

$$
S(16) = \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.75/16} = \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.046875} = \frac{1}{0.296875} = 3.368 \approx \mathbf{3.37 \times}
$$

**Comment:** Increasing the serial portion from 15% to 25% **drastically reduces the maximum speedup from 6.67× to 4×**. This demonstrates Amdahl's sobering insight: **identifying and eliminating serial bottlenecks is far more impactful than adding more processors**. The lesson for distributed system designers: **profile your code, parallelize the serial parts, and only then scale out.**

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the three cloud service models — **IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS** — on the basis of (i) user responsibility, (ii) flexibility, (iii) examples, (iv) use cases, and (v) cost model. Draw a suitable diagram showing the layered cloud service stack.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cloud data center provides an individual server availability of **98%**. An e-commerce company deploys its application on **5 such servers arranged in parallel** for redundancy.

**Calculate:**
1. The availability of the parallel configuration.
2. The total annual downtime in hours.
3. The number of "nines" of availability.
4. Comment on what happens if only 2 of the 5 servers remain operational (i.e., the system tolerates up to 3 failures).

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Cloud Service Models Comparison

**Definition (1 mark):**
Cloud service models represent different levels of abstraction over computing resources, defining the boundary of responsibility between the cloud provider and the consumer.

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | **IaaS** | **PaaS** | **SaaS** |
|-----------|----------|----------|----------|
| **(i) User Responsibility** | OS, middleware, runtime, app, data. | Application + data only. | Only data and user access. |
| **(ii) Provider Responsibility** | Virtualization, servers, storage, networking. | OS, middleware, runtime, servers. | Entire stack including app. |
| **(iii) Flexibility** | Maximum (full control of OS). | Moderate (locked to platform). | Lowest (use as-is). |
| **(iv) Examples** | AWS EC2, Azure VM, GCP Compute Engine. | AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine, Heroku. | Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce, Slack. |
| **(v) Use Cases** | Lift-and-shift migration, custom enterprise apps, dev/test environments. | API development, microservices, ML model deployment. | Email, CRM, collaboration, productivity. |
| **(vi) Cost Model** | Pay per VM-hour (e.g., $0.04/hr for t3.micro). | Pay per app-hour or request. | Pay per user/month subscription. |

**Cloud Service Stack Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["APPLICATION LAYER - User writes App code"]
    L2["DATA LAYER - User manages data"]
    L3["RUNTIME / MIDDLEWARE - Tomcat, Node.js, Python"]
    L4["OPERATING SYSTEM - Linux, Windows"]
    L5["VIRTUALIZATION - Hypervisor, VMs"]
    L6["PHYSICAL SERVERS, STORAGE, NETWORK"]

    L1 --> L3
    L2 --> L1
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6
```

- **IaaS:** User controls L1, L2, L3, L4. Provider controls L5, L6.
- **PaaS:** User controls L1, L2. Provider controls L3, L4, L5, L6.
- **SaaS:** User controls only L2. Provider controls L1, L3, L4, L5, L6.

---

#### Part (b) — Availability with Parallel Redundancy

**Given:**
- Individual server availability $A_i = 0.98$
- Number of servers $n = 5$
- Configuration: **Parallel**

**Step 1 — System availability:** (3 marks)

$$
A_{\text{system}} = 1 - (1 - A_i)^n = 1 - (0.02)^5
$$

$$
(0.02)^5 = 0.02 \times 0.02 \times 0.02 \times 0.02 \times 0.02 = 3.2 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
A_{\text{system}} = 1 - 3.2 \times 10^{-9} \approx \mathbf{0.9999999968 \approx 99.99999968\%}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual downtime:** (2 marks)

Total hours in a non-leap year: $365 \times 24 = 8760$ hours.

$$
\text{Downtime} = (1 - A_{\text{system}}) \times 8760
$$

$$
\text{Downtime} = 3.2 \times 10^{-9} \times 8760 = 2.803 \times 10^{-5} \text{ hours}
$$

Converting to seconds:

$$
\text{Downtime} = 2.803 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600 = \mathbf{0.1009 \text{ seconds/year}}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of "nines":** (1 mark)

$99.99999968\% \approx$ between **8 and 9 nines** (specifically $\approx 8.5$ nines).

For comparison:
- 8 nines = 99.999999% → ~3.15 seconds/year downtime.
- 9 nines = 99.9999999% → ~0.315 seconds/year downtime.

Our system lies between these. Practically, the provider advertises **8-nines (99.999999%) SLA**.

**Step 4 — What if 3 of 5 servers fail?** (1 mark)

If 3 of 5 servers fail, only 2 remain. The system is effectively $n = 2$ parallel with $A = 0.98$:

$$
A_{\text{reduced}} = 1 - (0.02)^2 = 1 - 0.0004 = 0.9996 = 99.96\%
$$

This is only **~3.5 nines**, dropping from 8 nines — illustrating that **parallel redundancy is only effective as long as at least one server remains up**. In practice, a 5-server parallel cluster can tolerate up to 4 simultaneous failures and still serve requests (degraded).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **CAP confusion:** Many students write "CAP is C, A, P — you must choose TWO." The correct nuance is: "P is mandatory in distributed systems, so the REAL choice is between C and A." Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if this is missed.
> 2. **Amdahl's Law unit error:** Confusing the speedup ratio (dimensionless) with absolute time. The formula $S(n) = \frac{1}{s + (1-s)/n}$ is unitless. Always end with the value (e.g., 4.92×), not in seconds.
> 3. **Availability arithmetic mistake:** Forgetting to subtract from 1 in the parallel formula. The common wrong answer is $(0.02)^5 = 0.000000032$ (this is the *failure* probability, not availability). Availability = $1 -$ that.
> 4. **IaaS/PaaS/SaaS layer confusion:** Mixing up "user manages" vs "provider manages." A handy rule: in IaaS, you manage up to **OS + runtime**; in PaaS, you manage **app + data**; in SaaS, you manage **only data**. Drawing the stack diagram earns the bonus mark.
> 5. **Missing "nines" calculation:** Students compute availability but forget to map it to "number of nines." Always state both the percentage and the nines explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Print this section and revise 5 minutes before walking into the exam hall.

### 🔑 Key Definitions
- **Distributed System** — Multiple autonomous computers coordinating via message passing, appearing as a single coherent system.
- **Cloud Computing (NIST)** — Ubiquitous, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources.
- **5 Essential Cloud Characteristics** — On-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service.
- **3 Service Models** — IaaS, PaaS, SaaS.
- **4 Deployment Models** — Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.
- **CAP Theorem** — A distributed store can guarantee at most 2 of {C, A, P} simultaneously. P is mandatory, so choose between C and A.

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Formulas
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S(n) = \dfrac{1}{s + (1-s)/n}$; $\;S_{\max} = 1/s$
- **Availability (single):** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$
- **Availability (series):** $A_{\text{series}} = \prod_i A_i$
- **Availability (parallel):** $A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_i (1 - A_i)$
- **Quorum condition:** $R + W > N$
- **Bandwidth-Delay Product:** $\text{BDP} = \text{Bandwidth} \times \text{RTT}$
- **Annual Downtime** for $k$ nines: $8760 \times 10^{-k}$ hours

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Numbers
- 1 nine = 90% = 36.5 days/yr downtime.
- 3 nines = 99.9% = 8.77 hours/yr.
- 4 nines = 99.99% = 52.6 min/yr.
- 5 nines = 99.999% = 5.26 min/yr.
- 6 nines = 99.9999% = 31.5 sec/yr.

### 🔑 Must-Recognize Systems
- **CP systems:** HBase, MongoDB (strict), etcd, ZooKeeper, Spanner.
- **AP systems:** Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB, Riak, Cosmos DB.
- **CA (single-node):** Traditional RDBMS (PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle).
- **IaaS examples:** AWS EC2, Azure VM, GCP Compute Engine.
- **PaaS examples:** Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku.
- **SaaS examples:** Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce, Slack.

### 🔑 Common Architecture Buzzwords
- **API Gateway** — Single entry point for microservices (Kong, AWS API Gateway).
- **Service Mesh** — Sidecar proxies for service-to-service communication (Istio, Linkerd).
- **CDN** — Edge caching for static content (CloudFront, Akamai, Cloudflare).
- **Container Orchestrator** — Manages container lifecycle (Kubernetes, ECS, Nomad).
- **Serverless / FaaS** — Event-driven, stateless functions (AWS Lambda, Azure Functions).
- **Load Balancer** — Distributes traffic across instances (L4 vs L7 — NLB vs ALB).
- **Message Queue** — Asynchronous decoupling (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS).

### 🔑 Failure Models to Remember
- **Crash** — Node stops; data intact.
- **Omission** — Node fails to send/receive.
- **Byzantine** — Node acts arbitrarily/maliciously; requires $3f+1$ replicas to tolerate $f$ faults.
- **Network Partition** — Network splits; sub-networks can't communicate.

### 🔑 Replication & Consistency
- **Active Replication** — All replicas process every request.
- **Passive (Primary-Backup)** — One primary, async log shipping to backups.
- **Quorum** — $R + W > N$ for guaranteed read of latest write.
- **Consistency Spectrum:** Strong → Sequential → Causal → Read-your-writes → Eventual.

### 🔑 Real-World Engineering Insights
- **Netflix uses Cassandra** (AP) for streaming metadata — can tolerate inconsistency.
- **Google Spanner** is CP — uses atomic clocks + TrueTime for global consistency.
- **Amazon S3** is eventually consistent for listings (list may not show recently uploaded object for seconds).
- **Kubernetes** is the de-facto container orchestrator in 2024–2025.
- **Hybrid cloud** is the most adopted enterprise pattern: on-prem for sensitive data, public cloud for burst.

### 🎯 Top 3 Exam-Day Strategies
1. **Always draw diagrams** for service model comparisons, layered architecture, and CAP — these earn easy 1–2 marks.
2. **Show the formula, then substitute, then simplify** for any numerical problem. Examiners give partial credit for the correct formula even if the final arithmetic is wrong.
3. **State assumptions clearly** (e.g., "Assuming independent failures, identical components, steady-state operation") before applying the formulas. This signals maturity to the examiner.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Objectives of HPC and HTC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Objectives of HPC and HTC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Performance Computing (HPC)** refers to the aggregation of computing power delivered in a manner that delivers **substantially higher performance than a typical desktop computer or workstation** to solve large-scale, tightly-coupled, computation- and data-intensive problems in science, engineering, and business. HPC is fundamentally **latency-driven** and **speed-oriented**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Throughput Computing (HTC)** refers to the use of many independent, loosely-coupled computational tasks executed over long periods to maximize the **aggregate number of completed jobs per unit time**. HTC is fundamentally **throughput-driven** and **volume-oriented**.

The design objectives of these two paradigms, although both target the *efficient* use of distributed compute resources, are derived from fundamentally different optimization goals. The KTU 2024 syllabus for Advanced Computing Systems positions HPC and HTC as **complementary philosophies** of large-scale system design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Sports Car vs The Delivery Fleet**
> - **HPC is like a Formula 1 race car.** Every microsecond of latency matters. The car is engineered for a single mission — to finish a specific lap as fast as possible. It uses lightweight alloys, custom tires, and a powerful engine. If the engine fails, the entire mission fails. Each component is tightly integrated and must be in perfect synchronization.
> - **HTC is like a national postal delivery network.** Nobody cares how fast one letter arrives in isolation; what matters is that **millions of letters reach their destinations per day**. If one truck breaks down, the rest of the fleet still delivers. Tasks are *independent*, *loosely coupled*, and failures are *tolerable* because the system is statistically robust.

## 1.3 The Six Design Objectives (Syllabus Highlights)

For both HPC and HTC systems, the KTU 2024 curriculum prescribes a structured set of design objectives. These are not coincidental; they are the *engineering trade-off axes* along which every distributed system is tuned.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 6 Canonical Design Objectives of Large-Scale Computing Systems:**
> 1. **Performance** — Speed of execution (latency) for HPC; aggregate output (throughput) for HTC.
> 2. **Scalability** — Graceful performance retention as resources (nodes, cores, memory) are added.
> 3. **Reliability & Availability** — Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF); system uptime.
> 4. **Cost-Efficiency** — Performance per unit currency (e.g., GFLOPS per dollar).
> 5. **Energy Efficiency** — Performance per Watt (e.g., GFLOPS/W), measured by metrics like **PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness)**.
> 6. **Programmability & Usability** — Ease of expressing parallel/distributed workloads.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are the *de facto* standard units used across the HPC and HTC literature and the **TOP500** and **Green500** supercomputing lists:

- **FLOP/s** (Floating-Point Operations per Second) — Standard unit of computational throughput.
- **GFLOPS** = $10^9$ FLOP/s
- **TFLOPS** = $10^{12}$ FLOP/s
- **PFLOPS** = $10^{15}$ FLOP/s
- **EFLOPS** = $10^{18}$ FLOP/s
- **PUE** = Total Facility Power / IT Equipment Power (ideal = 1.0)
- **MIPS** = Million Instructions Per Second
- **MTBF** = Mean Time Between Failures (hours)

> [!NOTE]
> The **TOP500** list ranks supercomputers by their achieved **R_max (TFLOP/s)** on the **LINPACK** benchmark. The **Green500** ranks by **GFLOPS/Watt**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Design Objectives

Each of the six design objectives can be operationally broken down into measurable sub-goals. Understanding this decomposition is essential for KTU examination answers.

### 2.1.1 Performance
- **For HPC (Latency-driven):** Time-to-Solution $T_s$, Speedup $S_p$, Parallel Efficiency $E_p$.
- **For HTC (Throughput-driven):** Job Completion Rate $J_c$ (jobs/hour), Aggregate Throughput $\Theta$ (tasks/s).

### 2.1.2 Scalability
- **Strong Scaling** — Fixed problem size, increased processors. Tests *parallelizability*.
- **Weak Scaling** — Problem size grows proportionally with processors. Tests *problem-size growth*.
- Limited by **Amdahl's Law** (strong) and **Gustafson's Law** (weak).

### 2.1.3 Reliability
- **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures)
- **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair)
- **Availability** $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$
- HTC tolerates *task-level* failures; HPC tolerates *node-level* failures via **checkpoint-restart**.

### 2.1.4 Cost-Efficiency
- Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) = Acquisition + Power + Cooling + Maintenance.
- Performance per Rupee/Dollar = $\frac{\text{Peak FLOP/s}}{\text{TCO over lifetime}}$.

### 2.1.5 Energy Efficiency
- **GFLOPS/W** — Industry standard metric.
- **PUE** = Total Facility Power / IT Power.
- **EPI** (Energy Per Instruction) for embedded/edge HTC systems.

### 2.1.6 Programmability
- HPC uses **MPI (Message Passing Interface)**, **OpenMP**, **CUDA**, **OpenACC**.
- HTC uses **batch schedulers** (Slurm, PBS, HTCondor), **workflow engines** (Pegasus, Makeflow).
- Abstraction layers: APIs, DSLs (Domain-Specific Languages), and parallelizing compilers.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **the definitive formula sheet** for Module 1 problems. Memorize every cell.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Speedup | $S(p) = \frac{T(1)}{T(p)}$ | $T(1)$ = serial time, $T(p)$ = parallel time on $p$ processors | dimensionless |
| 2 | Amdahl's Law | $S(p) = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$ | $f$ = serial fraction, $p$ = processors | dimensionless |
| 3 | Asymptotic Speedup | $S_\infty = \frac{1}{f}$ | Limit as $p \to \infty$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Parallel Efficiency | $E(p) = \frac{S(p)}{p}$ | Fraction of ideal speedup retained | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | Gustafson's Law | $S(p) = p - f(p-1)$ | Scaled speedup under weak scaling | dimensionless |
| 6 | Karp-Flatt Metric | $f = \frac{\frac{1}{S(p)} - \frac{1}{p}}{1 - \frac{1}{p}}$ | Empirically extracted serial fraction | dimensionless |
| 7 | Throughput | $\Theta = \frac{N_{jobs}}{T_{window}}$ | Jobs completed in time window | jobs/s |
| 8 | System Availability | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Uptime fraction | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 9 | PUE | $\text{PUE} = \frac{P_{facility}}{P_{IT}}$ | Power Usage Effectiveness | dimensionless ($\ge 1$) |
| 10 | Compute Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{R_{max}}{R_{peak}}$ | HPL achieved / theoretical peak | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 11 | Cost per Job | $C_{job} = \frac{C_{node} \cdot t_{exec}}{N_{jobs} \cdot t_{window}}$ | Amortized cost of a job | currency/job |
| 12 | Energy per FLOP | $E_{FLOP} = \frac{P \cdot t}{N_{FLOP}}$ | Joules consumed per floating op | Joules |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HPC** powers climate modeling (CESM, GFS), molecular dynamics (GROMACS, NAMD), CFD (ANSYS Fluent), quantum chemistry (VASP), and AI training (frontier models).
- **HTC** powers cosmological surveys (LSST), bioinformatics pipelines (genome assembly), Monte Carlo simulations, web-crawl indexing, and SETI@home-style volunteer computing.
- The shared **design objective** of both is to **minimize wall-clock time per unit of useful work delivered** — HPC minimizes per-task time; HTC minimizes per-job cost across an ensemble.

> [!NOTE]
> Modern exascale systems (Frontier, Fugaku, LUMI) blur the HPC/HTC boundary by offering both tightly-coupled (MPI) and loosely-coupled (workflow) execution modes. This convergence is a key KTU 2024 syllabus discussion point.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No shortcuts. No truncation.

## 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law (Latency-Limited HPC Speedup)

**Step 1 — Partition the workload.**
A program is decomposed into a *serial* component and a *parallel* component.

Let $T_{total}$ be the total execution time on a single processor. Define the **serial fraction** $f$ as the fraction of $T_{total}$ that *cannot* be parallelized.

$$T_{serial} = f \cdot T_{total}$$

$$T_{parallel} = (1 - f) \cdot T_{total}$$

**Step 2 — Ideal parallel execution time.**
Assume the parallel portion divides *perfectly* across $p$ processors (no communication overhead, no contention, infinite memory bandwidth).

$$T_{parallel}(p) = \frac{(1 - f) \cdot T_{total}}{p}$$

**Step 3 — Total parallel time.**
The serial portion remains unaffected by adding processors.

$$T(p) = f \cdot T_{total} + \frac{(1 - f) \cdot T_{total}}{p}$$

**Step 4 — Define speedup.**
Speedup $S(p)$ is the ratio of serial time to parallel time.

$$S(p) = \frac{T(1)}{T(p)} = \frac{T_{total}}{f \cdot T_{total} + \frac{(1-f) \cdot T_{total}}{p}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify by canceling $T_{total}$.**

$$S(p) = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$$

This is **Amdahl's Law**, a cornerstone of HPC design. It tells us that even an infinitesimally small serial fraction $f$ imposes a hard ceiling on achievable speedup.

**Step 6 — Asymptotic speedup.**
As the number of processors $p \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{p \to \infty} S(p) = \lim_{p \to \infty} \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}} = \frac{1}{f + 0} = \frac{1}{f}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** If $f = 0.05$ (only 5% serial), the maximum achievable speedup is $S_\infty = 1/0.05 = 20\times$, regardless of how many processors are added. This is the *design constraint* that motivates HPC architects to minimize serial bottlenecks.

## 3.2 Derivation of Gustafson's Law (Weak Scaling for HTC)

**Step 1 — Restate the assumption.**
In *weak scaling*, the problem size grows linearly with the number of processors. Let the parallel work scale as $p - 1$ (i.e., the extra problem added per new processor).

**Step 2 — Total work on $p$ processors.**
Per the scaled workload assumption:

$$T(p) = f + (1 - f) \cdot p$$

(Here we set the serial time $T_{serial} = f$ as a normalized unit.)

**Step 3 — Scaled speedup.**

$$S_{scaled}(p) = \frac{T(p)}{T(1)} = \frac{f + (1 - f) \cdot p}{f + (1 - f)} = \frac{f + (1 - f) \cdot p}{1} = f + (1 - f) \cdot p$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange.**

$$S_{scaled}(p) = p - f \cdot p + f = p - f(p - 1)$$

This is **Gustafson's Law**. It is the *throughput-oriented* counterpart of Amdahl's Law and is the foundational equation justifying HTC-style "give each worker more work" design.

**Step 5 — Compare with Amdahl.**
- Amdahl: problem fixed, processors added $\Rightarrow$ speedup $\to 1/f$ (bounded).
- Gustafson: problem scales with processors $\Rightarrow$ speedup $\approx p$ (linear, unbounded).

## 3.3 Derivation of Parallel Efficiency

**Step 1 — Definition.**
Efficiency $E(p)$ is the fraction of *useful* work each processor performs, relative to its peak.

$$E(p) = \frac{S(p)}{p}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Amdahl's $S(p)$.**

$$E(p) = \frac{1}{p \left( f + \frac{1-f}{p} \right)} = \frac{1}{f \cdot p + (1 - f)}$$

**Step 3 — Limit behavior.**
- As $p \to 1$: $E(1) = \frac{1}{f + (1-f)} = 1$ (perfect efficiency, no parallelism).
- As $p \to \infty$: $E(\infty) = 0$ (efficiency collapses; this is why HPC has *practical* processor-count ceilings).

## 3.4 Derivation of System Availability

**Step 1 — Definition of failure and repair cycles.**
A system alternates between *operational* periods (mean duration MTBF) and *repair* periods (mean duration MTTR).

**Step 2 — Operational fraction.**

$$A = \frac{\text{Time operational}}{\text{Total time}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Step 3 — Example.**
If MTBF $= 1000$ hours and MTTR $= 5$ hours:

$$A = \frac{1000}{1005} \approx 0.9950 = 99.50\%$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: HPC vs HTC

| Design Objective | HPC Orientation | HTC Orientation | Conflict? |
|------------------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------|
| Primary metric | Latency / Time-to-Solution | Aggregate Throughput / Jobs/day | Yes |
| Workload type | Tightly-coupled (MPI, OpenMP) | Loosely-coupled (independent jobs) | Yes |
| Failure model | Catastrophic (checkpoint-restart) | Task-level (retry / discard) | Yes |
| Inter-node comm | High (low-latency interconnect) | Low (TCP/IP, file transfer) | Yes |
| Scheduler goal | Minimize queue + run time | Maximize utilization + fairness | Partial |
| Cost model | High TCO, low node count | Low TCO, many cheap nodes | Yes |
| Energy metric | GFLOPS/W (compute-bound) | Jobs/kWh (throughput-bound) | Yes |
| Scalability | Limited by Amdahl (strong) | Scales with Gustafson (weak) | Yes |
| Programmability | Parallel paradigms required | Sequential, batch paradigms | Yes |
| Example | Weather simulation | Genome alignment (BLAST grid) | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
