<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between High Performance Computing (HPC) and High Throughput Computing (HTC) with respect to design objectives and workload characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | HPC | HTC |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Primary design goal | Minimize **latency / time-to-solution** | Maximize **aggregate throughput / jobs per day** |
| Workload type | Tightly-coupled, parallel (MPI) | Loosely-coupled, independent jobs |
| Failure handling | Checkpoint-restart, catastrophic failure model | Task retry, statistical failure tolerance |
| Typical interconnect | Low-latency (InfiniBand, Omni-Path) | Commodity Ethernet |
| Example | Weather simulation, CFD | Genome pipelines, Monte Carlo ensembles |

*(Award full 3 marks for covering goal, coupling, and example.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State Amdahl's Law and explain why it imposes a hard ceiling on HPC speedup.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Amdahl's Law states that the speedup $S(p)$ achievable on $p$ processors for a program with serial fraction $f$ is given by

$$S(p) = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$$

As $p \to \infty$, the speedup approaches the asymptotic limit $S_\infty = 1/f$. This imposes a **hard ceiling** on HPC performance because *no matter how many processors are added*, the serial fraction $f$ (e.g., I/O, synchronization, single-threaded sections) dominates the total execution time. For example, with $f = 0.02$, the maximum achievable speedup is $50\times$, regardless of investment in additional compute nodes.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Amdahl's Law with Gustafson's Law.** Amdahl assumes *fixed* problem size; Gustafson assumes *scaled* problem size. Examiners *specifically* test this distinction.
> 2. **Always specify units** when reporting performance numbers. "Speedup = 20" with no context loses 1 mark; "Speedup = 20$\times$ on 32 processors" is full credit.
> 3. **Do not skip the definition of every symbol** in derivations. A formula sheet without a symbol legend is penalized under KTU 2024 marking schemes.
> 4. **PUE values less than 1 are physically impossible.** If you calculate PUE $< 1$, recheck.
> 5. **For HTC questions, never invoke latency metrics.** Examiners deduct marks for using HPC-style latency arguments in an HTC context.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **design objectives of HPC systems**. Discuss in detail the metrics used to evaluate each objective. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parallel program has a serial fraction of $f = 0.04$. Compute the speedup, efficiency, and asymptotic speedup for $p = 8, 32, 128$ processors. State two engineering strategies to mitigate the Amdahl bottleneck. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

The six design objectives of HPC are:

1. **Performance (3 sub-metrics):**
   - **Latency** — wall-clock time $T(p)$ to complete one execution.
   - **Speedup** $S(p) = T(1)/T(p)$ — multiplicative gain over serial baseline.
   - **Parallel Efficiency** $E(p) = S(p)/p$ — utilization factor.

2. **Scalability (2 sub-metrics):**
   - **Strong scaling** — fixed workload, varying $p$; characterized by Amdahl's Law.
   - **Weak scaling** — workload grows with $p$; characterized by Gustafson's Law.

3. **Reliability (2 sub-metrics):**
   - **MTBF** — Mean Time Between Failures (hours).
   - **Availability** $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$.
   - HPC uses **checkpoint-restart** to recover from node failures.

4. **Cost-Efficiency:**
   - TCO = Acquisition + Power + Cooling + Maintenance.
   - Metric: **GFLOPS per Rupee/Dollar** over system lifetime.

5. **Energy Efficiency:**
   - Metric: **GFLOPS/Watt**.
   - **PUE** = Facility Power / IT Power; target PUE $\approx 1.1$–$1.2$ for modern datacenters.

6. **Programmability:**
   - APIs: **MPI, OpenMP, CUDA, OpenACC**.
   - Trade-off: high-performance primitives vs developer productivity.

#### Model Solution for (b)

Using Amdahl's Law:

$$S(p) = \frac{1}{0.04 + \frac{0.96}{p}}, \quad E(p) = \frac{S(p)}{p}$$

| Processors $p$ | Speedup $S(p)$ | Efficiency $E(p)$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 8   | $\frac{1}{0.04 + 0.12} = \frac{1}{0.16} = 6.25$   | $6.25/8 = 0.781$  |
| 32  | $\frac{1}{0.04 + 0.03} = \frac{1}{0.07} \approx 14.29$ | $14.29/32 \approx 0.447$ |
| 128 | $\frac{1}{0.04 + 0.0075} = \frac{1}{0.0475} \approx 21.05$ | $21.05/128 \approx 0.164$ |

**Asymptotic speedup:**

$$S_\infty = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{0.04} = 25\times$$

**Two engineering strategies to mitigate Amdahl's bottleneck:**

1. **Minimize serial fraction $f$** — Refactor I/O, replace single-threaded libraries with parallel equivalents, use asynchronous communication to overlap with computation. Example: replacing a serial file read with parallel HDF5/MPI-IO.
2. **Asynchronous / Non-blocking communication** — Overlap MPI communication with computation using `MPI_Isend`/`MPI_Irecv` so that the serial fraction of the parallel code approaches zero.
3. *(Bonus)* **Load balancing** — Ensure even work distribution; otherwise the slowest node becomes the de-facto serial fraction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **design objectives of HTC systems**. Compare HTC scheduling strategies with HPC scheduling. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bioinformatics HTC cluster processes $N = 50{,}000$ independent genome-alignment jobs per day. The average job requires $t_{job} = 12$ minutes on a single worker node. The cluster has $W = 200$ worker nodes. Compute the aggregate throughput, the average cluster utilization, and recommend whether the cluster should be expanded by 50% if the target SLA is 60,000 jobs/day. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Design Objectives of HTC:**

1. **Aggregate Throughput** — Maximize jobs completed per unit time. Metric: $\Theta = N_{jobs}/T_{window}$.
2. **Scalability (Weak)** — Add nodes linearly to absorb more jobs; Gustafson-style scaling.
3. **Resource Utilization** — Keep nodes busy; minimize idle time.
4. **Fault Tolerance (Task-Level)** — Failed jobs are re-queued; no checkpointing.
5. **Cost Efficiency** — Use commodity hardware; minimize cost per job.
6. **Heterogeneity Tolerance** — Accept mixed-resource pools (volunteer computing, cloud spot instances).

**HPC vs HTC Scheduling Comparison:**

| Feature | HPC Scheduler | HTC Scheduler |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| Goal | Minimize makespan of a single job | Maximize throughput over many jobs |
| Workload | Tightly-coupled, gang-scheduled | Bag-of-tasks, independent |
| Allocation | All-or-nothing (full partition) | Incremental, opportunistic |
| Examples | Slurm (in MPI mode), PBS Pro | HTCondor, Grid Engine, BOINC |
| Preemption | Rare (costly to checkpoint) | Common (jobs are short) |
| Fair-share | Secondary priority | Primary policy (DRAA, priority) |
| Failure response | Abort and restart from checkpoint | Re-queue and continue |

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:** $N = 50{,}000$ jobs/day, $t_{job} = 12$ min, $W = 200$ nodes, $T_{window} = 24$ h = $1440$ min.

**Aggregate Throughput (jobs per day):**

$$\Theta = \frac{50{,}000}{1 \text{ day}} = 50{,}000 \text{ jobs/day}$$

**Total node-minutes available per day:**

$$C_{capacity} = W \cdot T_{window} = 200 \cdot 1440 = 288{,}000 \text{ node-minutes/day}$$

**Total node-minutes demanded per day:**

$$C_{demand} = N \cdot t_{job} = 50{,}000 \cdot 12 = 600{,}000 \text{ node-minutes/day}$$

**Cluster Utilization:**

$$\eta = \frac{C_{demand}}{C_{capacity}} = \frac{600{,}000}{288{,}000} \approx 2.083$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiners' trap:** Utilization $> 1$ means the cluster is **over-subscribed** — jobs are queueing. The cluster cannot physically keep up. A naive answer might say "we have spare capacity" by computing $\Theta$ alone.

**Effective (sustained) throughput with 200 nodes:**

$$\Theta_{sustained} = \frac{C_{capacity}}{t_{job}} = \frac{288{,}000}{12} = 24{,}000 \text{ jobs/day}$$

**SLA target:** 60,000 jobs/day.

**Required node-minutes for 60,000 jobs/day:**

$$C_{req} = 60{,}000 \cdot 12 = 720{,}000 \text{ node-minutes/day}$$

**Nodes required:**

$$W_{req} = \frac{C_{req}}{T_{window}} = \frac{720{,}000}{1440} = 500 \text{ nodes}$$

**Recommendation:**
A 50% expansion gives $W_{new} = 200 \cdot 1.5 = 300$ nodes, providing $300 \cdot 1440 = 432{,}000$ node-minutes, which still falls short of the 720,000 required. **A 50% expansion is insufficient.** The cluster must scale to **at least 500 nodes (a 150% expansion)** to meet the 60,000 jobs/day SLA. A combined strategy of expansion + job-duration optimization (e.g., parallelizing the alignment kernel) is recommended.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam.)**

- [ ] **HPC = latency-driven; HTC = throughput-driven.** This single sentence is worth 2 marks anywhere it appears.
- [ ] **Six design objectives:** Performance, Scalability, Reliability, Cost, Energy, Programmability. Know **two metrics per objective**.
- [ ] **Amdahl's Law:** $S(p) = 1/(f + (1-f)/p)$. Asymptote $= 1/f$. Strong scaling.
- [ ] **Gustafson's Law:** $S(p) = p - f(p-1)$. Weak scaling. Linear growth.
- [ ] **Karp-Flatt:** Empirically extracts $f$ from observed $S(p)$. Use when $f$ is unknown.
- [ ] **PUE** = Total Facility Power / IT Power. Ideal = 1.0. Never $< 1$.
- [ ] **Availability** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR). Express as a percentage.
- [ ] **HPC failure = catastrophic** → checkpoint-restart. **HTC failure = statistical** → re-queue.
- [ ] **TOP500** ranks by R_max on LINPACK. **Green500** ranks by GFLOPS/W.
- [ ] **Units to memorize:** GFLOPS ($10^9$), TFLOPS ($10^{12}$), PFLOPS ($10^{15}$), EFLOPS ($10^{18}$).
- [ ] **HPC interconnect:** InfiniBand, Omni-Path (low latency, high bandwidth).
- [ ] **HTC interconnect:** Commodity Ethernet (sufficient for file-based workflows).
- [ ] **HPC schedulers:** Slurm, PBS (in MPI mode). **HTC schedulers:** HTCondor, BOINC.
- [ ] **Parallel Efficiency** $E(p) = S(p)/p$. Always between 0 and 1 (ideal = 1 at $p=1$).
- [ ] **The KTU-favorite numerical trap:** Compute utilization as *demand / capacity*. If $> 1$, the system is over-subscribed.
- [ ] **HPC and HTC are converging** in modern exascale systems — be prepared to discuss this synthesis in 14-mark answers.
- [ ] **When asked "state the design objectives"**, always list *all six* — partial lists are penalized.
- [ ] **For derivation questions, never skip algebraic steps.** KTU examiners allocate 2–3 marks specifically to the simplification step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT and Cyber Physical Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** IoT and Cyber Physical Systems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Internet of Things (IoT) — Formal Definition According to the **ITU-T Recommendation Y.2060** (the international standard adopted by KTU syllabus), the **Internet of Things (IoT)** is defined as: > *"A global infrastructure for the Information Society, enabling advanced services by interconnecting (physical and virtual) things based on existing and evolving interoperable information and communication technologies."* In simpler KTU-board-exam language: > **IoT = A network of physical objects ("things") embedded with sensors, software, and communication technologies, with the goal of connecting and exchanging data with other devices and systems over the internet.** The "thing" in IoT can be: - A **person** (with a heart-rate monitor) - An **animal** (with an RFID livestock tag) - A **machine** (a car with built-in sensors) - An **inanimate object** (a smart refrigerator) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (CO1):** IoT is one of the three foundational pillars of Advanced Computing Systems, along with **Cloud Computing** and **Cyber Physical Systems (CPS)**. The KTU examiner often asks: *"How is IoT different from the traditional Internet?"* — The key answer is that IoT connects **devices/things**, not just computers. --- ## 1.2 Cyber Physical System (CPS) — Formal Definition According to **Edward A. Lee & Sanjit A. Seshia** (the seminal reference used in academic CPS literature), a **Cyber Physical System (CPS)** is: > *"A system featuring a tight combination of, and coordination between, the system's computational (cyber) and physical elements."* KTU board-friendly definition: > **CPS = A mechanism that is controlled or monitored by computer-based algorithms, tightly integrated with the internet and its users. It is a system where physical processes affect computations and vice-versa — forming a feedback loop.** > [!NOTE] > **Key distinction often asked in exams:** IoT focuses on **connectivity and data exchange** among things. CPS focuses on **real-time feedback control** of physical processes through computation. CPS = IoT + Real-time Control + Actuation. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition ### 🍳 Analogy 1 — IoT = "Smart Refrigerator with WiFi" Imagine your **ordinary refrigerator**. It cools food. That's it. Now imagine a **smart refrigerator** that: 1. Has sensors (temperature, humidity, door-open counter) 2. Is connected to WiFi 3. Sends you a notification: *"Milk is low. Order from BigBasket?"* 4. You press a button on your phone → milk gets ordered **This is IoT.** The fridge is a "thing" connected to the internet, **sensing and reporting** — but it does **not** control the world around it. It only **observes and informs**. ### 🚗 Analogy 2 — CPS = "Self-Driving Car Braking System" Now imagine a **Tesla on a highway**. - A **lidar sensor** measures distance to the car ahead (physical process) - The **onboard computer** processes this data in real time (cyber process) - Based on computation, the computer sends a signal to the **brake actuator** (physical action) - The car **slows down** to avoid collision - The new speed is again measured, the loop continues. **This is CPS.** A continuous **Sensor → Compute → Actuate → Sensor** feedback loop. Computation directly **controls the physical world in real time**. > [!TIP] > **Memory trick for KTU exam:** > - **IoT = "Eyes and Ears"** (sense + communicate) > - **CPS = "Eyes + Ears + Brain + Hands"** (sense + compute + actuate + feedback) > - **Cloud Computing = "Memory"** (store and process big data remotely) --- ## 1.4 IoT vs. Traditional Internet — The Paradigm Shift | Parameter | Traditional Internet | IoT | |---|---|---| | Endpoint | Computers, phones, servers | Sensors, actuators, RFID tags, embedded devices | | Data generator | Humans (typing, uploading) | Machines (auto-generated sensor data) | | Data volume | Petabytes | **Zettabytes (10²¹ bytes)** | | Data velocity | Variable | Real-time / streaming | | Communication | Mostly human-driven | Mostly machine-to-machine (M2M) | | Standard protocols | HTTP, FTP, SMTP | **MQTT, CoAP, AMQP, LwM2M** | | Power source | AC mains / charged batteries | Often **energy-harvested or battery-limited** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 4 V's of Big Data in IoT (Very common 6-mark question):** > - **Volume** — massive scale (billions of devices) > - **Velocity** — high-speed real-time streams > - **Variety** — heterogeneous data (text, video, sensor readings) > - **Veracity** — uncertainty, noise, trust issues # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Reference Architecture of IoT (5-Layer Model) The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically tests the **5-layer IoT architecture** (as defined by the IoT World Forum Reference Model and Cisco's framework). Each layer has a distinct role. ### **Layer 1 — Perception / Sensors Layer (Edge Hardware)** - **Role:** Physical sensing of environmental parameters. - **Components:** Temperature sensors (LM35, DHT11), humidity sensors, accelerometers (MPU6050), GPS modules (NEO-6M), RFID readers, cameras, gas sensors (MQ-2). - **Key metrics:** Sampling rate (Hz), resolution (bits), accuracy (%), power consumption (mW). - **Example:** A DHT11 sensor in a smart agriculture field measures soil temperature every 10 seconds. ### **Layer 2 — Network / Transport Layer (Connectivity)** - **Role:** Transmit the sensed data to a processing unit (gateway, cloud, or edge node). - **Technologies:** - **Short-range:** Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE), Zigbee, Z-Wave, NFC, Wi-Fi (802.11). - **Long-range:** LoRaWAN, Sigfox, NB-IoT, LTE-M. - **Wired (industrial):** RS-485, CAN bus, Modbus, Ethernet/IP. - **Key metrics:** Bandwidth (bps), latency (ms), range (m), packet loss rate (%). ### **Layer 3 — Middleware / Processing Layer (Edge / Fog)** - **Role:** Local preprocessing, filtering, aggregation, security enforcement, and protocol translation. - **Functions:** - **Data filtering** (remove noise / redundant readings) - **Data aggregation** (average, min, max over a window) - **Protocol translation** (e.g., MQTT ↔ CoAP) - **Local decision-making** (actuate if temperature > 80°C) - **Hardware:** Raspberry Pi, NVIDIA Jetson Nano, industrial PLCs. ### **Layer 4 — Application Layer (Business Logic)** - **Role:** Deliver the specific IoT service to the end user. - **Examples:** - **Smart home:** Mobile app controlling lights, locks, AC. - **Smart agriculture:** Dashboard showing soil moisture map. - **Healthcare:** Real-time patient monitoring with alert system. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Predictive maintenance dashboard. ### **Layer 5 — Business Layer (Management)** - **Role:** Manage the entire IoT system, build business models, monitor SLAs. - **Functions:** Profitability analysis, user analytics, application-layer security, regulatory compliance (GDPR, India's DPDP Act 2023). - **Output:** Strategic decisions — e.g., *"Deploy 10,000 more soil sensors in Region B based on yield data."* --- ## 2.2 CPS Architecture — The 3C Triad A CPS is structured around the **3C model** — **Communication, Computation, and Control**, with a closed feedback loop. ``` Physical World ⟷ Sensors ⟷ Cyber (Compute) ⟷ Actuators ⟷ Physical World ``` ### **The 3C Components in Detail** | Component | Function | Example in Autonomous Car | |---|---|---| | **Computation (Cyber)** | Runs algorithms (control laws, ML models) | Decision: brake vs steer | | **Communication** | Wired/wireless data exchange | CAN bus, V2X, 5G | | **Control** | Closes the loop with physical processes | Brake actuator (hydraulic) | ### **The CPS Feedback Loop — Step by Step** 1. **Physical Process** (the car is moving at 100 km/h) 2. **Sensing** (radar measures distance to obstacle = 30 m) 3. **Computation** (compute required deceleration: $a = \frac{v^2}{2d}$) 4. **Communication** (cyber decision sent to actuator via CAN bus) 5. **Actuation** (brake pads engage, hydraulic pressure applied) 6. **Physical Response** (car decelerates, new speed measured) 7. **Loop back to Step 1** — this is the **closed-loop control** that distinguishes CPS from IoT. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU board examiner's favorite question:** *"Differentiate between an open-loop and closed-loop CPS."* Answer: An **open-loop** CPS does not feed the output back to the controller (e.g., a washing machine timer). A **closed-loop** CPS continuously monitors the output and adjusts (e.g., a cruise control system in a car). --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following formulas are **must-know for KTU ESE and internal exams**. Each is mathematically derivable and verifiable. | # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Units | Where Used | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** — sampling frequency must be at least twice the highest signal frequency to avoid aliasing. | Hz | Sensor sampling design | | 2 | $D = f_s \times N \times b$ | **Data rate per sensor** = sampling freq × number of channels × bits per sample. | bits/sec (bps) | IoT bandwidth planning | | 3 | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | **Shannon-Hartley Theorem** — maximum channel capacity. | bits/sec | Network capacity analysis | | 4 | $T_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} = T_{sense} + T_{proc} + T_{comm} + T_{act}$ | **End-to-end CPS latency** — total delay from sensor to actuator. | seconds | Real-time CPS design | | 5 | $a = \dfrac{v^2}{2d}$ | **Required deceleration** for safe stop from velocity $v$ in distance $d$. | m/s² | Autonomous vehicles | | 6 | $E = P \times t$ | **Energy consumption** of a node over time $t$. | Joules (J) | Battery life of IoT nodes | | 7 | $PDR = \dfrac{P_{received}}{P_{delivered}}$ | **Packet Delivery Ratio** — quality of IoT link. | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Network reliability | | 8 | $E_b = \dfrac{P_{TX}}{R_b}$ | **Energy per bit** transmitted. | Joules/bit | Energy-efficient protocol design | | 9 | $B_{total} = N_s \times D$ | **Aggregate bandwidth** for $N_s$ sensors. | bps | Gateway uplink design | | 10 | $t_{jitter} = \vert t_{actual} - t_{expected} \vert$ | **Jitter** — deviation of packet timing. (Use \vert not pipes inside tables.) | seconds | Real-time CPS timing | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Do not use the vertical bar `|` for absolute value inside a markdown table — it breaks the table syntax. Use `\vert` or `\mid` as shown in row 10. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart Grid (CPS):** Real-time load balancing prevents blackouts. - **Medical CPS:** Insulin pumps automatically inject the right dose based on continuous glucose monitoring (CGM). - **Industrial CPS (Industry 4.0):** Robotic arms in car assembly lines adjust torque 1,000 times per second. - **Smart Agriculture (IoT):** Drip irrigation systems trigger only when soil moisture sensor reading falls below a threshold. - **Smart City:** Air quality sensors + traffic sensors + smart traffic lights form a city-scale CPS for pollution control. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides the **complete mathematical derivations** behind the formulas listed in Section 2. Every algebraic transition is shown step-by-step, so a KTU student can reproduce them in the answer script. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nyquist Sampling Theorem (Why $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$) **Premise:** A bandlimited analog signal $x(t)$ has its energy concentrated in the frequency range $[0, f_{max}]$ Hz. We want to sample it at rate $f_s$ samples/second such that the original signal can be **perfectly reconstructed** from samples alone. **Step 1 — Mathematical Statement.** Let $x(t)$ be a continuous-time signal with Fourier Transform $X(f) = 0$ for all $\vert f \vert > f_{max}$. Let the sampled signal be: $$ x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \cdot \delta(t - nT_s) $$ where $T_s = 1/f_s$ is the sampling period. **Step 2 — Fourier Transform of the Sampled Signal.** Using the **sampling property** of the impulse train in the frequency domain: $$ X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s) $$ This means the spectrum of $x(t)$ gets **replicated** every $f_s$ Hz, scaled by $f_s$. **Step 3 — Condition to Avoid Aliasing (Spectrum Overlap).** For perfect reconstruction using a low-pass filter, the shifted copies of $X(f)$ must **not overlap** in the frequency domain. The first non-zero copy of $X(f)$ is centered at $+f_s$. Its right edge reaches $f_s + f_{max}$. The left edge of the central copy is at $-f_{max}$. To prevent overlap: $$ f_s - f_{max} \geq f_{max} $$ **Step 4 — Solving the Inequality.** $$ \begin{aligned} f_s - f_{max} &\geq f_{max} \\ f_s &\geq 2 f_{max} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, the **minimum sampling frequency** (called the **Nyquist rate**) is: $$ \boxed{f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_{max}} $$ > **Real-world example:** Human voice has $f_{max} \approx 4 \text{ kHz}$, so telephony uses $f_s = 8 \text{ kHz}$. Music CDs use $f_s = 44.1 \text{ kHz}$ for $f_{max} \approx 20 \text{ kHz}$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — IoT Data Rate per Sensor **Problem:** A smart factory deploys a vibration sensor on each of 50 motors. Each sensor samples at 1 kHz, with 16-bit resolution, and has 2 channels (X and Y axis). Calculate the total uplink bandwidth required if all sensors transmit simultaneously. **Step 1 — Define the per-sensor data rate.** $$ D_{sensor} = f_s \times N \times b $$ where: - $f_s = 1000$ Hz (sampling frequency) - $N = 2$ (number of channels) - $b = 16$ bits/sample **Step 2 — Plug in values.** $$ \begin{aligned} D_{sensor} &= 1000 \times 2 \times 16 \\ D_{sensor} &= 32000 \text{ bits/sec} = 32 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute aggregate bandwidth for 50 sensors.** $$ \begin{aligned} B_{total} &= N_s \times D_{sensor} \\ B_{total} &= 50 \times 32\,000 \\ B_{total} &= 1\,600\,000 \text{ bps} = 1.6 \text{ Mbps} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Engineering Decision.** The factory gateway uplink must support at least **1.6 Mbps**. An Ethernet link (100 Mbps) easily handles this, but a LoRaWAN link (max 50 kbps) would **not work** for 50 sensors — so protocol choice matters. > **Note for KTU answer writing:** Always state the *assumption* (e.g., "assuming no protocol overhead, no compression") to score full marks. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shannon Channel Capacity (IoT Link Design) **Problem:** An IoT node uses an NB-IoT link with bandwidth $B = 180$ kHz and SNR $\frac{S}{N} = 15$ dB. Calculate the maximum achievable data rate. **Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear scale.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SNR}_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) \\ 15 &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) \\ \frac{S}{N} &= 10^{1.5} = 31.62 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Shannon-Hartley Theorem.** $$ C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Substitute values.** $$ \begin{aligned} C &= 180\,000 \times \log_2(1 + 31.62) \\ C &= 180\,000 \times \log_2(32.62) \\ C &= 180\,000 \times 5.027 \\ C &\approx 904\,886 \text{ bps} \approx 0.9 \text{ Mbps} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** The NB-IoT link can support up to **0.9 Mbps** in theory. In practice, achievable throughput is **30–50% of Shannon limit** due to coding overhead, fading, and protocol headers. So a realistic capacity is ~300–450 kbps. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — End-to-End CPS Latency (Safety-Critical System) **Problem:** A self-driving car CPS has the following component latencies: - $T_{sense}$ = 5 ms (radar sensing) - $T_{proc}$ = 20 ms (onboard decision) - $T_{comm}$ = 10 ms (CAN bus transmission) - $T_{act}$ = 15 ms (brake actuator response) Compute total latency, and determine if it satisfies the safety constraint of 100 ms. **Step 1 — Sum all components.** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} &= T_{sense} + T_{proc} + T_{comm} + T_{act} \\ T_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} &= 5 + 20 + 10 + 15 \\ T_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} &= 50 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compare with safety bound.** $$ T_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} = 50 \text{ ms} \;\ll\; T_{max} = 100 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 3 — Conclusion.** ✅ The CPS **meets** the 100 ms safety requirement with 50 ms of headroom. A vehicle traveling at 100 km/h covers only **~1.4 m in 50 ms**, which is acceptable for collision avoidance given a typical radar range of 150–200 m. > **Engineering takeaway:** Latency budget design is the #1 task in safety-critical CPS (automotive, aviation, medical). --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Battery Life of an IoT Node **Problem:** A wireless sensor node consumes $P = 50$ mW average power. It is powered by a coin cell battery of capacity $C = 600$ mAh at voltage $V = 3$ V. Estimate the operational lifetime in years. **Step 1 — Convert battery capacity to Joules.** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{batt} &= C \times V \times 3600 \\ E_{batt} &= 0.600 \text{ Ah} \times 3 \text{ V} \times 3600 \text{ s} \\ E_{batt} &= 6480 \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute lifetime in seconds.** $$ \begin{aligned} t &= \frac{E_{batt}}{P} \\ t &= \frac{6480 \text{ J}}{0.050 \text{ W}} \\ t &= 129\,600 \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to years.** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{years} &= \frac{129\,600}{60 \times 60 \times 24 \times 365} \\ t_{years} &\approx 0.0041 \text{ years} \\ t_{years} &\approx 1.5 \text{ days} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Conclusion.** The node lasts only **~1.5 days** with a 600 mAh battery! To extend it to years, we need **duty cycling** (sleep most of the time) and **energy harvesting** (solar, vibration, RF). --- ## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix | Derivation | Input Parameters | Output Quantity | KTU Marks Weightage | |---|---|---|---| | Nyquist Rate | $f_{max}$ | $f_s$ | 3–4 marks | | Per-sensor data rate | $f_s$, $N$, $b$ | $D$ | 3 marks | | Shannon capacity | $B$, SNR | $C$ | 4–5 marks | | CPS latency | $T_{sense}, T_{proc}, T_{comm}, T_{act}$ | $T_{end-to-end}$ | 4 marks | | Battery life | $P$, $C$, $V$ | $t$ | 3 marks | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics The diagrams below use **Mermaid** (with strict safeguards: alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown inside labels, no reserved keywords as node names). --- ## 4.1 IoT 5-Layer Reference Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB L5[LAYER 5: BUSINESS LAYER
Profit Models, Analytics, SLA] L4[LAYER 4: APPLICATION LAYER
Smart Home, Health, IIoT Apps] L3[LAYER 3: MIDDLEWARE LAYER
Filtering, Aggregation, Protocol Translation] L2[LAYER 2: NETWORK LAYER
WiFi, BLE, LoRa, NB-IoT, 5G] L1[LAYER 1: PERCEPTION LAYER
Sensors, Actuators, RFID Tags] L1 -->|Raw Sensor Data| L2 L2 -->|Network Packets| L3 L3 -->|Processed Data| L4 L4 -->|Service Output| L5 L5 -->|Policy Rules Down| L4 L4 -->|Control Commands| L3 L3 -->|Translated Commands| L2 L2 -->|Transmitted to Field| L1 ``` **Explanation:** - **Bottom-up flow** = data acquisition and processing. - **Top-down flow** = management commands and policy enforcement. - The **Middleware layer** is the most important for KTU — it is where **edge computing** happens. --- ## 4.2 Cyber Physical System Feedback Loop (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[PHYSICAL PROCESS
Vehicle Moving] S1[SENSOR SUBSYSTEM
Radar + Lidar] C1[CYBER COMPUTATION
Control Algorithm] A1[ACTUATOR SUBSYSTEM
Brake / Steering] F1[FEEDBACK CHANNEL
CAN Bus / 5G] P1 -->|Measurable Variables| S1 S1 -->|Digitized Data| C1 C1 -->|Computed Control Signal| A1 A1 -->|Physical Force / Torque| P1 P1 -.->|State Update| F1 F1 -.->|Continuous Monitoring| C1 ``` **Key insight:** Notice the **dashed feedback line** from `P1` to `C1` via `F1`. This represents the **continuous, real-time nature** of CPS. Compare this with IoT, which has **no actuator** and **no closed loop** in most cases. --- ## 4.3 IoT vs CPS — Functional Comparison Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IoTBlock[IoT SYSTEM] S2[Sensors] -->|Read| ENV2[Environment] S2 -->|Data| GW2[Gateway / Cloud] GW2 -->|Alerts| USR2[End User] USR2 -.->|Manual Action| ENV2 end subgraph CPSBlock[CPS SYSTEM] SENS3[Sensors] -->|Read| PHYS3[Physical Plant] SENS3 -->|Data| CTRL3[Controller] CTRL3 -->|Command| ACT3[Actuator] ACT3 -->|Force| PHYS3 PHYS3 -.->|Feedback| SENS3 end ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **IoT block** is **open-loop** at the cyber level — humans act on the data. - The **CPS block** is **closed-loop** at the cyber level — the controller acts on the data **automatically and continuously**. --- ## 4.4 IoT-CPS-Cloud Integration (End-to-End System View) ```mermaid flowchart LR DEV[FIELD DEVICES
Sensors and Actuators] EDGE[EDGE NODE
Raspberry Pi / Gateway] FOG[FOG LAYER
Local Server Cluster] CLOUD[CLOUD LAYER
AWS IoT / Azure] APP[APPLICATION LAYER
Mobile App / Dashboard] USER[END USER] DEV -->|MQTT or CoAP| EDGE EDGE -->|Aggregated Data| FOG FOG -->|Filtered Streams| CLOUD CLOUD -->|Analytics + ML Output| APP APP -->|Visualization and Alerts| USER USER -->|Commands via App| APP APP -->|Reverse Control| CLOUD CLOUD -->|Policy Push| FOG FOG -->|Action Trigger| EDGE EDGE -->|Actuator Command| DEV ``` **Why this matters for KTU exams:** A typical **14-mark question** asks you to *"Design an IoT architecture for a smart agriculture / smart city application."* This 4-tier diagram (Device → Edge → Fog → Cloud → App) is the **canonical answer template**. --- ## 4.5 Hardware Stack — IoT Node Anatomy ```mermaid flowchart TB PWR[POWER MODULE
Battery + Solar + Harvester] SENS[TRANSDUCER LAYER
DHT11, MQ2, MPU6050] MCU[MCU LAYER
ESP32, Arduino, STM32] COM[COMM MODULE
WiFi, BLE, LoRa] APP2[APPLICATION FIRMWARE
C / Python / RTOS] SEC[SECURITY MODULE
AES, TLS, Secure Boot] PWR --> SENS SENS --> MCU MCU --> COM COM --> APP2 SEC -.->|Protects| MCU SEC -.->|Encrypts| COM ``` > **Key takeaway:** Every IoT node has **6 functional blocks**. KTU may ask you to label a diagram or explain the role of each block. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap This section mirrors the **exact pattern of KTU End Semester Examination (ESE)** under the 2024 Scheme. Part A questions carry **3 marks each** and Part B questions carry **14 marks each** with **internal choice**. --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Define the Internet of Things (IoT) as per ITU-T Y.2060. List any four application domains of IoT.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** As per **ITU-T Recommendation Y.2060**, the Internet of Things is *"a global infrastructure for the Information Society, enabling advanced services by interconnecting physical and virtual things based on existing and evolving interoperable information and communication technologies."* **Four Application Domains (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):** 1. **Smart Home** — Connected lighting, security, HVAC. 2. **Smart Healthcare** — Remote patient monitoring, wearable ECG. 3. **Smart Agriculture** — Soil moisture-based drip irrigation. 4. **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — Predictive maintenance, asset tracking. 5. *(Optional 5th)* **Smart City** — Traffic, pollution, waste management. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]** **Differentiate between IoT and Cyber Physical System (CPS) with a suitable example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | IoT | CPS | |---|---|---| | Primary Focus | Sensing and communication | Real-time control and actuation | | Feedback Loop | Open-loop (human acts on data) | Closed-loop (controller acts automatically) | | Components | Sensors + Gateway + Cloud | Sensors + Controller + Actuators | | Time-criticality | Often non-real-time | **Strictly real-time** | | Example | Smart AC sending temperature to phone | **Adaptive cruise control** braking automatically | (1.5 marks for the table, 1.5 marks for the example and the **closed-loop distinction**.) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern) The KTU 2024 scheme mandates that **every Part B question has internal choice (a) or (b)** at the module level. Below, **Question A and Question B are two fully independent alternatives**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **5-layer reference architecture of IoT**. List the function of each layer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A temperature sensor in a cold storage unit samples at 2 kHz with 12-bit resolution. There are **4 such sensors** per room, and the cold storage has **10 rooms**. Calculate: (i) The per-sensor data rate. (ii) The aggregate bandwidth requirement at the central gateway. (iii) The required minimum sampling frequency if the highest expected thermal fluctuation frequency is 800 Hz. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **(a) 5-Layer IoT Architecture (7 marks):** Provide a labelled block diagram (as in Section 4.1 of these notes) showing the 5 layers vertically: **Perception → Network → Middleware → Application → Business**. For **full 7 marks**, write **2–3 lines per layer**: 1. **Perception Layer:** Physical sensors and actuators collect data (temperature, humidity, vibration). Hardware: DHT11, MPU6050, RFID. 2. **Network Layer:** Transmits data using Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, NB-IoT, 5G. Provides addressing, routing, reliability. 3. **Middleware Layer:** Performs data filtering, aggregation, protocol translation, security (authentication, encryption). Runs on edge/fog nodes. 4. **Application Layer:** Delivers domain-specific services (smart home app, patient monitoring dashboard). Interacts with end users. 5. **Business Layer:** Manages SLAs, profitability, regulatory compliance. Converts data into business decisions. Conclude with one sentence on **how data flows upward** and **commands flow downward**. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Per-sensor data rate (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} D_{sensor} &= f_s \times N \times b \\ &= 2000 \times 1 \times 12 \\ &= 24\,000 \text{ bps} = 24 \text{ kbps} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Aggregate bandwidth (2 marks):** Total sensors = 4 sensors/room × 10 rooms = 40 sensors. $$ \begin{aligned} B_{total} &= 40 \times 24\,000 \\ &= 960\,000 \text{ bps} = 960 \text{ kbps} \approx 1 \text{ Mbps} \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Minimum sampling frequency (3 marks):** By Nyquist Theorem: $$ \begin{aligned} f_s &\geq 2 f_{max} \\ f_s &\geq 2 \times 800 \\ f_s &\geq 1600 \text{ Hz} \end{aligned} $$ Since the current $f_s = 2000$ Hz $> 1600$ Hz, the system **meets the Nyquist criterion** with a 400 Hz safety margin. (1 mark for the conclusion.) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Predicted Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the **Cyber Physical System (CPS) architecture** with a block diagram. Discuss the **3C model** (Communication, Computation, Control) and the role of **closed-loop feedback** with an example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An IoT node uses an NB-IoT link with bandwidth 200 kHz and SNR of 20 dB. Calculate: (i) The Shannon channel capacity. (ii) The end-to-end CPS latency given $T_{sense}=2$ ms, $T_{proc}=15$ ms, $T_{comm}=8$ ms, $T_{act}=10$ ms. Does it meet a 50 ms hard real-time deadline? (iii) The energy consumption per day if the node draws 80 mW average. The battery is 1200 mAh at 3.7 V. Comment on the operational lifetime. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **(a) CPS Architecture (7 marks):** Draw the **closed-loop block diagram** (as in Section 4.2 of these notes) with: **Physical Process → Sensor → Cyber Controller → Actuator → Physical Process (with feedback).** Explain the **3C model** (3 marks): - **Computation:** Embedded processors / microcontrollers running control algorithms (PID, MPC, ML). - **Communication:** Wired (CAN, Ethernet) or wireless (5G, Wi-Fi) links between subsystems. - **Control:** Closes the loop — converts cyber decisions into physical actions via actuators. Explain **closed-loop feedback** with an example (3 marks): - **Example:** A smart thermostat in a building. Temperature sensor → microcontroller computes error = (setpoint − actual) → PID algorithm outputs heating/cooling command → HVAC actuator adjusts. - Without feedback, the system is open-loop and **drifts** from setpoint. With feedback, it **self-corrects continuously**. State the **real-time constraint** clearly: a CPS must respond within a **deterministic deadline** (1 mark). **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Shannon Capacity (2 marks):** Convert SNR: $20 \text{ dB} = 10^{2.0} = 100$ (linear). $$ \begin{aligned} C &= B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\ &= 200\,000 \times \log_2(101) \\ &= 200\,000 \times 6.658 \\ &\approx 1.33 \text{ Mbps} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) End-to-End Latency (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end} &= 2 + 15 + 8 + 10 = 35 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ Since $35 \text{ ms} < 50 \text{ ms}$, the deadline **is met** with 15 ms headroom. ✅ **(iii) Energy and Lifetime (3 marks):** Battery energy: $$ E_{batt} = 1.2 \text{ Ah} \times 3.7 \text{ V} \times 3600 = 15\,984 \text{ J} $$ Daily energy consumption: $$ E_{day} = 0.080 \text{ W} \times 86\,400 \text{ s} = 6912 \text{ J} $$ Lifetime: $$ t = \frac{15\,984}{6912} \approx 2.31 \text{ days} $$ **Comment (1 mark):** The node lasts only **~2.3 days**, which is impractical. **Solutions:** duty cycling (sleep mode 95% of the time), energy harvesting, or a larger battery. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where KTU students lose marks on this topic:** > 1. **Confusing IoT and CPS:** Many students write "IoT = CPS" — this costs 2–3 marks. Always emphasize the **closed-loop control** aspect of CPS. > 2. **Forgetting to state assumptions in numericals:** KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if you do not write "assuming no protocol overhead / no compression / no retransmission." > 3. **Writing "Nyquist rate" without units:** Always write "$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ in **Hz**" and explicitly say "sampling frequency must be at least **twice** the highest signal frequency." > 4. **Drawing the 5-layer IoT diagram without labels:** A diagram without labelled arrows (showing data flow direction) is marked incomplete. > 5. **Memorising IoT definitions verbatim:** KTU prefers **conceptual understanding**. Frame the definition in your own words and supplement with the standard ITU-T reference. > 6. **Missing the real-time angle in CPS:** Always mention **"hard real-time"** vs **"soft real-time"** deadlines — this is a 2-mark bonus differentiator. > 7. **Pipes inside tables breaking markdown:** Use `\vert` for absolute value (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) inside table cells, not the literal `|` character. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] **IoT definition** = ITU-T Y.2060 — *"global infrastructure interconnecting physical and virtual things."* - [ ] **CPS definition** = tight integration of **cyber (compute)** and **physical (process)** elements via **closed-loop feedback**. - [ ] **5 Layers of IoT** = Perception → Network → Middleware → Application → Business. - [ ] **3C of CPS** = Communication + Computation + Control. - [ ] **4 V's of Big Data** = Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity. - [ ] **Nyquist Theorem** = $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. - [ ] **Per-sensor data rate** = $D = f_s \times N \times b$. - [ ] **Shannon capacity** = $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$. - [ ] **CPS latency budget** = $T_{sense} + T_{proc} + T_{comm} + T_{act}$. - [ ] **Open-loop vs Closed-loop** — CPS is closed-loop; IoT is mostly open-loop. - [ ] **IoT protocols** — MQTT, CoAP, AMQP, LwM2M (NOT HTTP as primary). - [ ] **Battery lifetime formula** — $t = \frac{C \cdot V \cdot 3600}{P}$ seconds. - [ ] **Energy efficiency in IoT** is achieved by **duty cycling** and **energy harvesting**. - [ ] **Real-time constraint** in CPS is **non-negotiable** — missed deadlines can cause **physical harm**. - [ ] **Sample numerical problem** — always state units, assumptions, and a one-line conclusion. - [ ] **Common IoT sensors** — DHT11 (T+RH), MQ-2 (gas), MPU6050 (IMU), NEO-6M (GPS). - [ ] **Common IoT MCUs** — ESP32, Arduino Uno, STM32, Raspberry Pi Pico. - [ ] **Communication technologies** — BLE, Zigbee, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, Wi-Fi, 5G. - [ ] **Cloud platforms** — AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT. - [ ] **Security triad for IoT/CPS** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (CIA). > **Final Exam Tip:** For 14-mark design questions, always start with a **block diagram**, label every block, state **2–3 lines per block**, and end with **one numerical calculation** to demonstrate quantitative understanding. This 4-part structure (Diagram → Explanation → Numerics → Conclusion) guarantees 12+ marks out of 14 in KTU ESE.

topics.title: Technologies for Network-Based Systems: Multicore CPUs and Multithreading Technologies, GPU Computing, Virtual Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Technologies for Network-Based Systems: Multicore CPUs and Multithreading Technologies, GPU Computing, Virtual Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Technologies for Network-Based Systems — The Big Picture

A **Network-Based System** in the KTU 2024 syllabus refers to a distributed computing environment in which multiple compute resources (CPUs, GPUs, storage, memory) are interconnected through a high-speed network fabric to collaboratively solve a single problem or serve many clients. To make such systems efficient, four foundational hardware/software technologies are employed:

1. **Multicore CPUs** — Multiple processing cores on a single silicon die.
2. **Multithreading Technologies** — Hardware/software mechanisms allowing a single core to manage multiple threads of execution.
3. **GPU Computing** — Massively parallel accelerators originally built for graphics, now repurposed for general-purpose computation (GPGPU).
4. **Virtual Machines (VMs)** — Software-emulated computing environments providing hardware abstraction, isolation, and portability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** These four technologies are the *core building blocks* of modern data centers, cloud platforms (AWS, Azure, GCP), HPC clusters, and edge computing nodes. Every subsequent module in PCCST602 builds upon this foundation.

---

## 1.2 Multicore CPUs — Formal Definition

A **Multicore CPU** (also called a *chip multiprocessor*, CMP) is a single integrated circuit (IC) that contains **two or more independent processing cores**, each capable of fetching, decoding, executing, and retiring instructions in parallel. The cores typically share lower-level caches (L2/L3) and the system interconnect, while retaining private L1 caches and register files.

**Key metrics KTU expects you to know:**

- **Die (Silicon Wafer):** The physical semiconductor substrate on which cores are etched. Current manufacturing nodes are **3 nm, 5 nm, 7 nm**.
- **Clock Frequency ($f$):** Measured in **GHz** (e.g., 3.5 GHz = 3.5 × 10⁹ cycles/sec).
- **IPC (Instructions Per Cycle):** Average number of instructions a core completes per clock cycle.
- **Transistor Count ($N_t$):** Number of MOSFETs on the die (Modern CPUs: ~50–100 billion).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Kitchen Brigade:** Think of a single-core CPU as a chef in a kitchen. No matter how fast the chef is, only one dish is cooked at a time. A multicore CPU is like hiring 4, 8, 16, or even 128 chefs in the same kitchen, each with their own cutting board (registers/L1) but sharing the pantry (L3 cache) and the stove (memory bus). Tasks can now be cooked in *parallel*, dramatically reducing total meal-prep time.

---

## 1.3 Multithreading Technologies — Formal Definition

**Multithreading** is the ability of an operating system, runtime, or hardware to manage **multiple threads of execution** within a single process. Hardware multithreading (as opposed to software-only threading) allows a core to switch between threads in **nanoseconds** when the active thread stalls (e.g., on a cache miss, pipeline hazard, or I/O wait), thereby hiding latency and improving *throughput*.

**KTU-Recognized Categories of Hardware Multithreading:**

| Technique | Description | Typical Context |
|-----------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Coarse-Grained Multithreading (CGMT)** | Switches to a new thread only on a long stall (e.g., L2 cache miss). Single-thread performance ≈ single-threaded core. | Sun UltraSPARC T-series |
| **Fine-Grained Multithreading (FGMT)** | Switches threads every cycle in round-robin fashion. Hides pipeline stalls beautifully but reduces single-thread latency. | NVIDIA Denver, some research CPUs |
| **Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT)** | Issues instructions from *multiple threads in the same cycle* to keep the pipeline full. The most efficient form. | Intel Hyper-Threading (2 threads/core), AMD Zen (2 threads/core) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Multitasking Surgeon:** A coarse-grained surgeon operates on patient A, and if the anesthesia machine delays, only then she turns to patient B. A fine-grained surgeon alternates between A and B every single stitch. An SMT surgeon is even smarter — she has four hands; while the left hand sutures, the right hand can apply antiseptic, both in the *same moment*. SMT keeps every functional unit busy in a single clock cycle.

---

## 1.4 GPU Computing — Formal Definition

A **Graphics Processing Unit (GPU)** is a specialized processor originally designed to accelerate computer graphics rendering (rasterization, shading, texture mapping). Modern GPUs employ a **SIMT (Single-Instruction, Multiple-Thread)** execution model, in which thousands of lightweight threads execute the same instruction on different data, making them ideal for **data-parallel** workloads such as deep learning, scientific simulation, and cryptography.

**Key distinctions from CPUs:**

- **CPUs** optimize for *latency* — minimize the time of a single task.
- **GPUs** optimize for *throughput* — maximize the number of tasks per second.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Football Stadium vs. The Chess Club:** A CPU is a chess club with 8 grandmasters — they take 5 minutes to deliberate a single brilliant move. A GPU is a football stadium with 10,000 amateur players — no single player is brilliant, but together they can fill every seat in a stadium in the time the chess club finishes one move. For problems that look the same everywhere (multiply this matrix, render this pixel), the GPU wins by sheer mass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GPGPU (General-Purpose GPU Computing):** Refers to using GPUs for non-graphics tasks via frameworks like **NVIDIA CUDA**, **OpenCL**, **ROCm (AMD)**, and **SYCL/oneAPI (Intel)**.

---

## 1.5 Virtual Machines — Formal Definition

A **Virtual Machine (VM)** is a software-emulated, fully isolated computing environment that behaves exactly like physical hardware to the guest operating system and applications running inside it. VMs enable **hardware abstraction, resource partitioning, server consolidation, and cross-platform portability**.

**Two principal KTU-recognized types:**

1. **System Virtual Machine (Type 1 Hypervisor):** Runs directly on bare metal, partitioning a single physical server into multiple VMs. Examples: **VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen, KVM**.
2. **Process Virtual Machine (Type 2 Hypervisor / Runtime):** Runs as an application on a host OS, providing an abstract execution environment for a single program. Examples: **JVM (Java Virtual Machine), CLR (.NET runtime), Python interpreter**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Apartment Building vs. The Hotel Room:** A Type 1 hypervisor is the building itself — the concrete, steel, and elevator infrastructure. Each apartment is a VM: the Smiths in 3B, the Khans in 5A, each with their own door, lock, water supply, and electricity meter. The building manager (hypervisor) decides how much power and water each apartment gets. A Type 2 hypervisor is more like a hotel room — it's a temporary space inside someone else's building, with the same furniture layout but no permanent ownership.

---

## 1.6 Why These Four Technologies Together?

In modern data centers, **multicore CPUs + multithreading + GPUs + VMs** are *stacked*, not chosen. A single server might host:
- 2 physical CPUs × 32 cores × 2 SMT threads = **128 logical processors**
- 4–8 GPUs for AI training
- 50–100 VMs, each with virtualized vCPUs, vRAM, and vGPU

The *combined* design multiplies performance, isolation, and elasticity — the foundation of every cloud platform on earth.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Multicore CPU — Architectural Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 On-Chip Hierarchy

A modern multicore CPU is organized as a hierarchy of compute, cache, and interconnect:

1. **Core (per unit):** ALUs, FPUs, register file, branch predictor, L1 I-cache, L1 D-cache, TLB, out-of-order engine.
2. **Shared L2 / L3 Cache:** Coherent across cores via protocols like **MESI, MOESI**.
3. **Interconnect / NoC (Network-on-Chip):** A mesh or ring fabric (e.g., Intel Ring Bus, AMD Infinity Fabric, Apple AMX) that routes data between cores, caches, and I/O.
4. **Memory Controller (IMC):** Often integrated on-die; connects to DDR5/LPDDR5X DIMMs.
5. **I/O Hub (PCIe, CXL, USB):** Provides expansion to GPUs, NVMe SSDs, and network cards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept — Cache Coherence:** When two cores cache the same memory line and one writes to it, the coherence protocol (MESI = Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid) ensures the *other* core either updates or invalidates its copy within nanoseconds. Without this, multicore systems would produce nondeterministic, corrupted results.

### 2.1.2 Scaling Laws and the Multicore Trade-off

- **Dennard Scaling** (broken in 2005): Voltage could not scale with transistor size, leading to the **Power Wall**.
- **Moore's Law** (still partly alive): Transistor count doubles roughly every **18–24 months**.
- **The Multicore Response:** Manufacturers shifted from raising single-core frequency to adding more cores, keeping **Total Power ≈ Constant**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Memory Wall:** As CPU cores got faster, the speed gap between CPU and DRAM widened. Multicore worsens the memory wall because more cores compete for the same memory bandwidth. The metric **Bytes-per-Flop (AI/HPC)** becomes critical.

---

## 2.2 Multithreading — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.2.1 Why Hardware Multithreading is Necessary

A modern out-of-order CPU can issue 4–8 instructions per cycle, but **stalls** for hundreds of cycles on:
- **L1 miss (4–10 cycles)**
- **L2 miss (12–40 cycles)**
- **L3 miss (40–80 cycles)**
- **DRAM access (~200–400 cycles)**
- **Branch mispredict (~15–20 cycles)**

During these stalls, the pipeline is *idle*. Multithreading fills the bubble with work from another thread, raising **utilization** without raising frequency.

### 2.2.2 SMT vs. CGMT vs. FGMT — Comparative Analysis

| Property | CGMT | FGMT | SMT (Hyper-Threading) |
|----------|------|------|------------------------|
| **Switch Granularity** | On long stall (1000+ cycles) | Every cycle (1 cycle) | Same cycle (issues from multiple threads) |
| **Pipeline Slots Used Per Cycle** | 1 thread | 1 thread (round-robin) | Multiple threads, simultaneously |
| **Single-Thread Performance** | 100% (baseline) | Slightly reduced (context-switch overhead) | ~80–95% of baseline per thread |
| **Throughput Gain (typical)** | 1.1–1.3× | 1.5–2× | 1.2–1.4× per core |
| **Hardware Cost (area)** | Low | Medium | Medium (duplicate register file, rename tables) |
| **Used In** | Legacy SPARC | Research CPUs (Tera MTA) | **Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, IBM POWER10** |

---

## 2.3 GPU Computing — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.3.1 CPU vs. GPU Architectural Philosophy

| Attribute | CPU | GPU |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Optimization Goal** | Minimize latency of one task | Maximize throughput of many tasks |
| **Core Count** | 4–128 (heavyweight) | 1,000–24,000 (lightweight) |
| **Clock Speed** | 3–6 GHz | 1–3 GHz |
| **Cache Size** | Large (L3 ≈ 32–128 MB) | Small (~3–6 MB total) |
| **Memory Bandwidth** | ~50–100 GB/s (DDR5) | ~1,000–3,000 GB/s (HBM3) |
| **Control Logic** | Complex (OoO, speculation, branch prediction) | Simple (SIMT) |
| **Power Consumption** | 65–250 W per socket | 300–700 W per GPU |
| **Best For** | Sequential, branchy, I/O-heavy code | Data-parallel, math-heavy code |

### 2.3.2 SIMT Execution Model

In **SIMT**, the GPU groups 32 threads into a **Warp** (NVIDIA) or **Wavefront** (AMD). All 32 threads in a warp execute the **same instruction** in lockstep. If threads diverge (e.g., on a branch), the warp *serializes* the divergent paths, a phenomenon called **Warp Divergence**, which severely hurts performance.

### 2.3.3 GPU Memory Hierarchy

- **Registers** (per thread, fastest)
- **Shared Memory / L1 Cache** (per SM, ~128 KB on Hopper)
- **L2 Cache** (global, ~50 MB on H100)
- **HBM3 DRAM** (80 GB, ~3 TB/s)

---

## 2.4 Virtual Machines — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.4.1 Type 1 vs. Type 2 Hypervisor (KTU Favourite)

| Property | Type 1 (Bare-Metal) | Type 2 (Hosted) |
|----------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Runs On** | Hardware directly | Host OS |
| **Examples** | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen, KVM | Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels |
| **Performance** | Near-native | 5–15% overhead |
| **Use Case** | Data centers, cloud (AWS EC2) | Developers, students, testing |
| **Security** | Strong (hardware isolation) | Weaker (host OS is attack surface) |

### 2.4.2 VM Building Blocks

A VM is constructed by emulating four logical interfaces:

1. **CPU Virtualization:** Binary translation (old Intel VT-x) or hardware-assisted (Intel VT-x, AMD-V).
2. **Memory Virtualization:** Two-level page tables — guest virtual → guest physical → host physical (via Extended Page Tables, EPT).
3. **I/O Virtualization:** Emulated devices (slow), paravirtualized (Xen, KVM virtio), or **SR-IOV** (Single Root I/O Virtualization — direct PCIe passthrough).
4. **Network Virtualization:** Virtual NICs (vNIC), virtual switches (Open vSwitch).

### 2.4.3 Container vs. VM (KTU 2024 Distinction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Containers (Docker, Podman, containerd)** are *not* full VMs. They share the host kernel and isolate processes via Linux namespaces and cgroups, with no separate guest OS. Containers start in **~50 ms** and use **~50 MB**, vs. VMs which start in **~30 s** and use **~2–10 GB**. Containers are an OS-level abstraction; VMs are a hardware-level abstraction.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize these formulas — they appear in KTU 2024 ESE frequently.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | **Amdahl's Law (Speedup)** | $S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}$ | $n$ = parallel processors, $p$ = parallel fraction | $S \le \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ |
| 2 | **Theoretical Max Speedup** | $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | $p$ = parallel fraction | As $n \to \infty$ |
| 3 | **Gustafson's Law (Scaled Speedup)** | $S_{\text{scaled}}(n) = n - p(n-1)$ | $n$ = processors, $p$ = sequential fraction | For weak scaling |
| 4 | **Parallel Efficiency** | $E = \dfrac{S(n)}{n} = \dfrac{1}{n(1-p) + p}$ | $S(n)$ = speedup, $n$ = cores | $0 \le E \le 1$ |
| 5 | **CPU Time (Amdahl's Original)** | $T(n) = (1-p)T + \dfrac{pT}{n} = T\left[(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{n}\right]$ | $T$ = serial time | Identical to $S$ reciprocal |
| 6 | **GFLOPS Calculation** | $G = \text{cores} \times \text{clock (Hz)} \times \frac{\text{FLOPs/cycle}}{\text{10}^9}$ | — | For peak theoretical |
| 7 | **Memory Bandwidth** | $BW = \text{channels} \times \text{rate} \times \frac{\text{bus-width (bytes)}}{\text{cycle}}$ | DDR5-6400 dual = 2 × 6400 × 8 bytes/cycle | GB/s |
| 8 | **Roofline Model Bound** | $A_{\max} = \min\left(\pi, \beta \cdot I\right)$ | $\pi$ = peak FLOPs, $\beta$ = memory BW, $I$ = AI (FLOP/byte) | $A_{\max}$ = attainable GFLOPs |
| 9 | **Multi-core Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{T_{\text{parallel}}}{n \times T_{\text{serial}}}$ | — | $0 \le U \le 1$ |
| 10 | **Power (Dynamic CMOS)** | $P_d = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$ | $\alpha$ = switching activity, $C$ = capacitance, $V$ = voltage, $f$ = freq | Watts |
| 11 | **IPC (Instructions Per Cycle)** | $IPC = \dfrac{\text{Instructions Retired}}{\text{Clock Cycles}}$ | — | Higher is better |
| 12 | **Cache Hit Rate** | $H = \dfrac{H_{\text{hit}}}{H_{\text{hit}} + H_{\text{miss}}}$ | — | $0 \le H \le 1$ |
| 13 | **AMAT (Avg Mem Access Time)** | $AMAT = t_{L1} + MR_{L1}\left(t_{L2} + MR_{L2} \cdot t_{RAM}\right)$ | $t$ = latency, $MR$ = miss rate | ns |
| 14 | **VM Overhead** | $O_{VM} = \dfrac{T_{VM} - T_{native}}{T_{native}} \times 100\%$ | $T$ = execution time | % |
| 15 | **SMT Throughput Gain** | $G_{SMT} = \dfrac{IPC_{SMT}}{IPC_{ST}}$ | — | Typically 1.2–1.4 |

> **Engineering Utility of these formulas:**
> - **Amdahl's Law** tells architects *why* simply doubling cores does not double speed.
> - **Roofline Model** tells GPU programmers whether their kernel is **compute-bound** or **memory-bound**.
> - **AMAT** tells CPU architects *which* cache level to optimize for biggest speedup.
> - **VM overhead** tells cloud engineers when to use **SR-IOV passthrough** instead of emulated I/O for HPC.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law (The Heart of Parallel Computing Theory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amdahl's Law is a compulsory KTU 2024 ESE topic.** You must be able to derive and apply it to any multicore / multithreaded scenario.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Let the total execution time of a serial (single-threaded) program be $T$. The program is composed of two parts:
- A **sequential** part that *cannot* be parallelized, with time $(1-p) \cdot T$.
- A **parallel** part that *can* be perfectly distributed across $n$ processors, with time $p \cdot T$.

Here, $0 \le p \le 1$ is the **parallel fraction**.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

We seek the new total execution time $T(n)$ when $n$ processors are applied to the program.

**Step 1:** Write the total serial time as the sum of both fractions.

$$
T = (1-p) \cdot T + p \cdot T
$$

**Step 2:** The sequential portion is unaffected by adding more processors. It still takes $(1-p) \cdot T$ time.

**Step 3:** The parallel portion is divided evenly across $n$ processors. Assuming perfect load balancing, each processor handles a fraction $1/n$ of the parallel work, so the time becomes $\dfrac{p \cdot T}{n}$.

**Step 4:** Sum the two contributions to obtain the new total time.

$$
T(n) = (1-p) \cdot T + \dfrac{p \cdot T}{n}
$$

**Step 5:** Factor out $T$.

$$
T(n) = T \left[ (1-p) + \dfrac{p}{n} \right]
$$

**Step 6:** Define **Speedup** $S(n)$ as the ratio of serial time to parallel time.

$$
S(n) = \dfrac{T}{T(n)} = \dfrac{T}{T \left[ (1-p) + \dfrac{p}{n} \right]}
$$

**Step 7:** Cancel $T$ to obtain Amdahl's canonical equation.

$$
S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}
$$

**Step 8:** To find the theoretical upper bound, take the limit as $n \to \infty$.

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + 0} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}
$$

This is the **Maximum Theoretical Speedup**, the *hard ceiling* imposed by the sequential portion.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A program has a parallel fraction of $p = 0.9$. It is run on 8 cores. Find the speedup and the maximum theoretical speedup.

**Step 1:** Substitute $p = 0.9$ and $n = 8$ into Amdahl's Law.

$$
S(8) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.9) + \dfrac{0.9}{8}}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the parallel term.

$$
\dfrac{0.9}{8} = 0.1125
$$

**Step 3:** Add to the sequential term.

$$
(1 - 0.9) + 0.1125 = 0.1 + 0.1125 = 0.2125
$$

**Step 4:** Take the reciprocal.

$$
S(8) = \dfrac{1}{0.2125} \approx 4.706
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the maximum.

$$
S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.9} = \dfrac{1}{0.1} = 10
$$

**Conclusion:** Even with infinite cores, the program cannot exceed 10× speedup. With 8 cores, only ~4.7× is achieved. The lesson: *the sequential 10% dominates.*

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Gustafson's Law (Weak Scaling)

Amdahl's Law assumes a *fixed* problem size. Gustafson's Law is more realistic for HPC: the problem size scales with $n$.

**Step 1:** Express the parallel run time as a function of $n$ and serial fraction $s = 1 - p$.

$$
T(n) = s + (1-s) = 1 \quad \text{(normalized)}
$$

**Step 2:** The serial processor's time is therefore the same: $T = 1$.

**Step 3:** Define the scaled speedup as $n \cdot T(n) - s(n-1)$.

$$
S_{\text{scaled}}(n) = n - s(n-1) = n - (1-p)(n-1)
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.

$$
S_{\text{scaled}}(n) = n - (n-1) + p(n-1) = 1 + p(n-1)
$$

**Step 5:** As $n \to \infty$, $S_{\text{scaled}} \to p \cdot n$, which is **linear** in $n$. *This is why HPC can scale to millions of cores — problem size grows with them.*

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)

A memory access first checks L1, then L2, then DRAM. The expected access time is the latency-weighted sum.

**Step 1:** L1 hit time is $t_{L1}$ (a base cost always paid).

**Step 2:** On L1 miss (probability $MR_{L1}$), the access goes to L2, adding $t_{L2}$.

**Step 3:** On L2 miss (probability $MR_{L2}$), the access goes to DRAM, adding $t_{DRAM}$.

**Step 4:** Combine.

$$
AMAT = t_{L1} + MR_{L1} \cdot t_{L2} + MR_{L1} \cdot MR_{L2} \cdot t_{DRAM}
$$

**Step 5:** Factor the L2 cost.

$$
AMAT = t_{L1} + MR_{L1} \left( t_{L2} + MR_{L2} \cdot t_{DRAM} \right)
$$

**Worked example:** $t_{L1} = 1$ ns, $t_{L2} = 10$ ns, $t_{DRAM} = 200$ ns, $MR_{L1} = 0.05$, $MR_{L2} = 0.20$.

$$
AMAT = 1 + 0.05 \times (10 + 0.20 \times 200)
$$

$$
AMAT = 1 + 0.05 \times 50 = 1 + 2.5 = 3.5 \text{ ns}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Power in CMOS (Dynamic Power)

The dynamic power dissipated by a CMOS circuit (per transistor) is given by the activity-energy product.

**Step 1:** Energy per transition of a capacitor $C$ at voltage $V$ is $\dfrac{1}{2}CV^2$. Per cycle (charging + discharging) it is $CV^2$.

**Step 2:** Multiply by the activity factor $\alpha$ (0 to 1) and the clock frequency $f$ (transitions per second) to get energy per second, i.e., power.

$$
P_d = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^2 \cdot f
$$

**Step 3:** Note that reducing $V$ has a **quadratic** benefit. This is why low-power CPUs (mobile) use lower voltages. As $V$ decreased by 30%, power drops by ~50%.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Speedup vs. Parallel Fraction vs. Core Count

The table below (precomputed, useful for KTU ESE quick answers) shows the speedup $S(n)$ for various $(p, n)$ combinations:

| $p \backslash n$ | 2 | 4 | 8 | 16 | 32 | 64 | $\infty$ |
|------------------|-----|-----|-----|------|------|------|----------|
| **0.50** | 1.33 | 1.60 | 1.78 | 1.88 | 1.94 | 1.97 | 2.00 |
| **0.75** | 1.60 | 2.29 | 2.91 | 3.37 | 3.66 | 3.83 | 4.00 |
| **0.90** | 1.82 | 3.08 | 4.71 | 6.40 | 7.80 | 8.65 | 10.00 |
| **0.95** | 1.90 | 3.48 | 5.93 | 9.14 | 12.31 | 15.13 | 20.00 |
| **0.99** | 1.98 | 3.88 | 7.48 | 13.91 | 24.43 | 39.22 | 100.00 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the table:** Even at $p = 0.99$, the 64-core speedup is only 39× (out of a max of 100). This visually proves Amdahl's pessimism: *the sequential part is the bottleneck.*

---

## 3.6 Roofline Model Derivation (KTU 2024 Hot Topic)

The Roofline Model bounds the attainable performance $A$ of a kernel as:

$$
A = \min\left(\pi, \beta \cdot I\right)
$$

Where:
- $\pi$ = peak compute performance in **GFLOPs/s**
- $\beta$ = memory bandwidth in **GB/s**
- $I$ = arithmetic intensity in **FLOPs/byte**

**Derivation Step 1:** A kernel requires $F$ FLOPs and transfers $D$ bytes. By definition, $I = F/D$.

**Step 2:** The *time* to execute is at least $\max(F/\pi, D/\beta)$. The *performance* is $F/T$:

$$
A = \dfrac{F}{\max(F/\pi, D/\beta)} = \min\left(\pi, \dfrac{F}{D/\beta}\right) = \min(\pi, \beta \cdot I)
$$

**Worked example:** NVIDIA A100 has $\pi = 19.5$ TFLOPs/s and $\beta = 1.5$ TB/s. A matrix-multiply kernel has $I = 200$ FLOPs/byte.

$$
\beta \cdot I = 1500 \text{ GB/s} \times 200 \text{ FLOPs/byte} = 300{,}000 \text{ GFLOPs/s} = 300 \text{ TFLOPs/s}
$$

$$
A = \min(19.5, 300) = 19.5 \text{ TFLOPs/s}
$$

The kernel is **compute-bound** (saturates the GPU's compute ceiling).

---
