## 4.4 SMT Thread Multiplexing Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title SMT Pipeline Filling in Same Cycle
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Thread 0
    T0 Cycle 1   :a1, 0, 1
    T0 Idle      :a2, 1, 4
    T0 Cycle 5   :a3, 4, 5
    section Thread 1
    T1 Cycle 1   :b1, 0, 1
    T1 Cycle 2   :b2, 1, 2
    T1 Cycle 3   :b3, 2, 3
    T1 Cycle 4   :b4, 3, 4
    T1 Cycle 5   :b5, 4, 5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Thread 0 stalls at cycle 2 (cache miss). Without SMT, the pipeline would be empty for 3 cycles. With SMT, Thread 1 fills cycles 2, 3, 4 with useful work. The pipeline remains 100% utilized.

## 4.5 GPU SIMT Execution within a Warp

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    W["Warp Scheduler Issues 1 Instruction per Cycle"]
    W --> T0["Thread 0 ALU A and B"]
    W --> T1["Thread 1 ALU A and B"]
    W --> T2["Thread 2 ALU A and B"]
    W --> T3["Thread 3 ALU A and B"]
    W --> T4["Thread N 31 ALU A and B"]
    T0 --> REG["32 Independent Register Sets 1 per thread"]
    T1 --> REG
    T2 --> REG
    T3 --> REG
    T4 --> REG
    REG --> RESULT["All 32 Results in 1 Cycle Lockstep SIMT"]
```

## 4.6 Hierarchical Memory: CPU + GPU Working Together (PCIe / NVLink)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HOST["Host System"]
        HC["CPU Socket"]
        HM["DDR5 RAM 256 GB"]
    end
    subgraph DEVICE["GPU Accelerator"]
        GC["GPU SM Cluster"]
        GHBM["HBM3 80 GB on package"]
    end
    HC <--> |"PCIe 5.0 128 GBs"| GC
    HM <--> |"DMA copy unified memory"| GHBM
    HC <--> |"NVLink CXL 900 GBs optional"| GHBM
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bandwidth asymmetry:** CPU↔GPU over PCIe 5.0 (≈ 128 GB/s) is ~25× slower than intra-GPU HBM3 (≈ 3 TB/s). Therefore, kernels that repeatedly shuttle data across PCIe are bottlenecked by *the link*, not the GPU. This is why **kernel fusion** and **unified memory** are performance engineering best practices.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT). How does it differ from Coarse-Grained Multithreading? Mention one commercial processor that supports SMT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Simultaneous Multithreading (SMT) is a hardware multithreading technique in which instructions from **two or more independent threads are issued to the execution units of a single processor core in the same clock cycle**, thereby keeping the pipeline fully utilized even when one thread stalls on a cache miss or branch misprediction.

**Difference from CGMT (1.5 marks):**
- **CGMT** switches to another thread *only* when the active thread encounters a long-latency stall (e.g., L2 miss). It issues instructions from one thread at a time; the other threads are dormant. Throughput gain is modest (~10–30%).
- **SMT** issues instructions from multiple threads *every cycle*, multiplexing them across the pipeline's functional units. It can keep the entire pipeline width busy in a single cycle, achieving a typical throughput gain of 1.2–1.4× with minimal additional hardware (mostly extra register files and program counters).

**Commercial Example (0.5 mark):** Intel Core i7 / i9 (Hyper-Threading Technology), AMD Ryzen / EPYC (Zen SMT), IBM POWER10.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Distinguish between a Type 1 hypervisor and a Type 2 hypervisor. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Type 1 (Bare-Metal) | Type 2 (Hosted) |
|--------|----------------------|------------------|
| **Layer of Operation** | Runs directly on hardware, with no host OS | Runs as an application on top of a host OS |
| **Examples** | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen, KVM (1 mark) | Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels Desktop (1 mark) |
| **Use Case / Performance** | Used in data centers and production clouds; near-native performance (1 mark) | Used for development, testing, and education; 5–15% performance overhead |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Multicore CPUs & Amdahl's Law**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model QP, Module 1]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **architecture of a modern multicore CPU** with a clearly labeled block diagram. Discuss the role of (i) L1/L2/L3 caches, (ii) cache coherence protocols (MESI), and (iii) the on-chip interconnect (NoC) in supporting parallel execution.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A parallel program has a parallel fraction of $p = 0.95$. Compute the speedup achievable on (i) 4 cores, (ii) 16 cores, and (iii) 64 cores using **Amdahl's Law**. Also compute the **maximum theoretical speedup** and the **parallel efficiency** for 16 cores. Comment on the result.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Multicore CPU Architecture — 7 marks**

- **Block diagram (3 marks):** A labelled diagram (see Section 4.1 of these notes) showing multiple cores, each with private L1 (split I-cache and D-cache) and a unified L2, all connected to a shared L3 cache, an integrated memory controller (DDR5), and a PCIe root complex. The on-chip **Network-on-Chip (NoC)** — typically a ring (Intel) or mesh (AMD Zen) — routes cache-coherence traffic between cores.
- **L1/L2/L3 caches (1.5 marks):** L1 is small (32 KB I + 32 KB D) and fastest (1 ns). L2 is per-core, larger (512 KB–1 MB), 5–10 ns. L3 is shared, tens of MB, 20–40 ns. The hierarchy exploits temporal and spatial locality. AMAT analysis (3.3 of these notes) quantifies the impact.
- **Cache Coherence (MESI) (1.5 marks):** Each cache line is in one of four states: **M**odified (dirty, exclusive to this core), **E**xclusive (clean, exclusive), **S**hared (clean, possibly in other cores), or **I**nvalid. When Core A writes to a line in *S* state, it broadcasts *Invalidate*; all other cores transition to *I* and Core A goes to *M*. This guarantees all cores observe a single, consistent memory image.
- **NoC (1 mark):** Modern CPUs use a NoC (ring, mesh, or crossbar) instead of a shared bus. It scales bandwidth linearly with core count, unlike a bus which becomes a bottleneck. AMD's Infinity Fabric and Intel's Ring/ Mesh are canonical examples.

**(b) Amdahl's Law Computation — 7 marks**

**Given:** $p = 0.95$, hence $1 - p = 0.05$.

**Recall:**

$$
S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}
$$

**(i) For $n = 4$:**

$$
S(4) = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{4}} = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.2375} = \dfrac{1}{0.2875} \approx 3.48
$$

**(ii) For $n = 16$:**

$$
S(16) = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{16}} = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.059375} = \dfrac{1}{0.109375} \approx 9.14
$$

**(iii) For $n = 64$:**

$$
S(64) = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{64}} = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.014844} = \dfrac{1}{0.064844} \approx 15.42
$$

**Maximum theoretical speedup (as $n \to \infty$):**

$$
S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.95} = \dfrac{1}{0.05} = 20
$$

**Parallel efficiency for $n = 16$:**

$$
E(16) = \dfrac{S(16)}{16} = \dfrac{9.14}{16} \approx 0.571 = 57.1\%
$$

**Comment (2 marks):**
- The speedup grows sublinearly with $n$: going from 4→16 cores (4× the cores) only multiplies speedup by ~2.6× (3.48 → 9.14).
- The maximum speedup is hard-capped at **20×** regardless of how many cores are added — the 5% sequential portion is the bottleneck.
- Efficiency drops as cores increase: with 16 cores, only **57%** of the compute is being "useful" on average. This motivates the search for more parallel algorithms (increasing $p$).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — GPU Computing & Virtual Machines**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model QP, Module 1, Internal Choice]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **SIMT execution model** of a GPU with a neat diagram. Differentiate between **SIMT (GPU)** and **SMT (CPU)**. Discuss the concept of **warp divergence** with a suitable example.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* With a clear diagram, describe the architecture of a **Type 1 (bare-metal) hypervisor**. Explain how **CPU virtualization, memory virtualization (Extended Page Tables), and I/O virtualization (SR-IOV)** are achieved. Mention one real-world deployment (e.g., AWS EC2).

#### Model Solution

**(a) SIMT Execution Model — 7 marks**

- **Definition (1 mark):** SIMT (Single-Instruction, Multiple-Thread) is the GPU's parallel execution paradigm in which a *warp* of 32 threads executes the *same instruction* in lockstep on different data items, while each thread maintains its own register state.
- **Diagram (2 marks):** See Section 4.5 of these notes — a warp scheduler issues one instruction, dispatched to 32 ALUs, each working on a different data element of a 32-wide vector.
- **SIMT vs. SMT (2 marks):**
  - **SIMT:** Multiple threads run the *same* instruction at the *same time* on different data. Hardware is simple, control logic is unified.
  - **SMT:** Multiple threads run *different* instructions at the *same time* on the same execution units. Hardware must duplicate register files, rename tables, and PC; control logic is per-thread.
  - SIMT optimizes for data parallelism (a single kernel across millions of elements); SMT optimizes for hiding latency of independent workloads.
- **Warp Divergence (2 marks):** When threads within a warp take different control-flow paths (e.g., `if (x > 0) A else B`), the GPU must execute both paths *sequentially*, with one half-warp active at a time, then merge the results. This *serializes* the divergent paths and drastically reduces SIMT efficiency. Example: a loop with a data-dependent exit condition (e.g., early termination search) — half the warp exits while the other half continues, halving effective throughput.

**(b) Type 1 Hypervisor Architecture — 7 marks**

- **Diagram (2 marks):** See Section 4.3 of these notes. The hardware sits at the bottom, the hypervisor (e.g., ESXi) is the *first* software layer, and above it run independent VMs, each with its own OS and applications.
- **CPU Virtualization (1.5 marks):** Implemented via hardware-assisted virtualization extensions — **Intel VT-x** or **AMD-V**. These add a new privileged mode (root vs. non-root) and instructions (`VMXON`, `VMLAUNCH`, `VMEXIT`) to safely trap sensitive instructions in the guest. Without this, the VMM must use *binary translation* (slow, 1990s VMware technique).
- **Memory Virtualization (EPT) (1.5 marks):** A two-level page table walk: **Guest Virtual → Guest Physical → Host Physical**. Intel's **Extended Page Tables (EPT)** and AMD's **Rapid Virtualization Indexing (RVI/NPT)** cache the second-level translation in the TLB, making nested page walks nearly as fast as native ones. The hypervisor's VMM owns the host physical layer.
- **I/O Virtualization (SR-IOV) (1 mark):** Standard emulated I/O (e.g., virtual NIC) is slow because every I/O traps to the hypervisor. **SR-IOV** exposes a single physical PCIe device as multiple *Virtual Functions (VFs)*, each of which a VM can bind to directly, achieving near-native I/O performance. AWS uses this for `ENA` (Elastic Network Adapter) high-throughput instances.
- **Real-world deployment (1 mark):** **Amazon EC2** uses a heavily modified KVM/Xen hypervisor (now called *Nitro System*) on bare-metal servers. Each EC2 instance is a VM with a fixed vCPU count, EBS-backed virtual disk, and SR-IOV-backed 25 Gbps+ networking. The Nitro offload chips handle storage, networking, and security, freeing the main CPU for customer workloads — a textbook production use of Type 1 virtualization.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in this topic (KTU 2024 ESE):**
>
> 1. **Amdahl's Law misapplication (–3 marks):** Many students forget the $1-p$ term in the denominator. The correct formula is $\dfrac{1}{(1-p) + p/n}$, *not* $\dfrac{n}{p}$ or $\dfrac{1}{1-p + n}$.
> 2. **SMT vs. CGMT confusion (–2 marks):** Students often state SMT means "running threads on multiple cores". It does not — SMT is *intra-core* thread-level parallelism (one physical core, multiple logical threads). CGMT is also intra-core, but only one thread issues per cycle.
> 3. **Hypervisor type mix-up (–2 marks):** Writing "VirtualBox is a Type 1 hypervisor" is *incorrect*. VirtualBox is Type 2 (hosted). Only those that run on bare metal (ESXi, Hyper-V, Xen, KVM) are Type 1.
> 4. **Confusing GPU with "many-core CPU" (–2 marks):** GPUs are *not* multicore CPUs. They follow SIMT/SIMD, have minimal control logic, and use wide slow cores. Equating them in the exam will cost marks.
> 5. **Container ≠ VM mistake (–1 mark):** Docker is *not* a virtual machine. It is an OS-level container using Linux cgroups/namespaces. There is no separate guest OS.
> 6. **Units in Roofline Model (–1 mark):** Always convert GFLOPs/s, TB/s, and FLOPs/byte to *consistent* units (e.g., all GB and GFLOPs) before computing $\beta \cdot I$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Use this as the last-page cheat sheet before walking into the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Multicore CPU = multiple cores on one die**, sharing caches via **MESI coherence** over a **NoC**.
- [ ] **Dennard Scaling broke** in 2005 — that's why the industry moved to multicore, not higher clock.
- [ ] **Amdahl's Law:** $S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + p/n}$; max speedup = $\dfrac{1}{1-p}$.
- [ ] **Gustafson's Law:** $S = n - s(n-1)$ — valid for *scaled* (weak-scaling) problems.
- [ ] **CGMT**: switch on long stall; **FGMT**: switch every cycle; **SMT**: issue from multiple threads same cycle.
- [ ] **SMT** is what Intel calls "Hyper-Threading" — gives 1.2–1.4× throughput per core.
- [ ] **GPU = SIMT** = 32 threads in a *warp* executing one instruction on different data.
- [ ] **Warp divergence** kills GPU performance — keep all threads in a warp on the same path.
- [ ] **GPUs win on throughput; CPUs win on latency.** HBM3 (~3 TB/s) >> DDR5 (~80 GB/s).
- [ ] **Type 1 hypervisor** = bare metal (ESXi, Hyper-V, Xen, KVM); **Type 2** = hosted (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation).
- [ ] **EPT / NPT** = two-level paging for memory virtualization (guest virtual → guest physical → host physical).
- [ ] **SR-IOV** = direct PCIe passthrough for high-performance VM I/O; used in AWS Nitro.
- [ ] **Container ≠ VM**: containers share the host kernel; VMs have their own kernel.
- [ ] **AMAT** formula: $t_{L1} + MR_{L1}(t_{L2} + MR_{L2} \cdot t_{DRAM})$.
- [ ] **Roofline bound:** $A = \min(\pi, \beta \cdot I)$ — kernel is *compute-bound* if $\beta \cdot I > \pi$, else *memory-bound*.
- [ ] **Dynamic CMOS power:** $P_d = \alpha C V^2 f$ — voltage has a *quadratic* effect.
- [ ] In **KTU 2024 ESE**, expect at least one Part-A question on hypervisor types or SMT, and one Part-B question involving Amdahl's Law numerical computation with a parallel-fraction scenario.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Technologies for Network-Based Systems**

> *Prepared in alignment with the KTU 2024 Scheme, Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Cognitive Levels: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate).*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🖥️ System Models for Distributed and Cloud Computing: Clusters

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **Cluster** is a loosely coupled, tightly integrated collection of complete computers (nodes) that work together closely so that in many respects they can be viewed as a single, unified computing resource. Each node in the cluster is typically a commodity-grade workstation, PC, or server, interconnected via a high-speed local area network (LAN) or system area network (SAN), and coordinated by a cluster middleware/software layer to deliver high availability (HA), high-performance computing (HPC), or high-throughput computing (HTC).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme taxonomy of distributed system models, a cluster is positioned as the **physical infrastructure layer** that bridges traditional Parallel Computers and modern Cloud Data Centers. The defining property is that a cluster behaves like a **Single System Image (SSI)** to the user, even though it is composed of independent, autonomous machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for Exam Answers:**
> - **Node:** An individual computer (physical or VM) that is a member of the cluster.
> - **Cluster Middleware:** Software layer that provides the Single System Image.
> - **Single System Image (SSI):** Illusion presented to the user that the cluster is one machine.
> - **High Availability (HA):** Probability that a system is operational during a given time window.
> - **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Average time between consecutive system failures.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The "Restaurant Kitchen" Analogy 🍳**

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen:
- A **single chef (single computer)** can prepare only one dish at a time. The more orders, the longer the queue (poor scalability).
- Now picture a **cluster of chefs** working in the same kitchen:
  - Each chef is a **complete, independent cook** (a node).
  - They all share a common **order ticket rail (the network)**.
  - A **head chef (the cluster manager/scheduler)** distributes orders intelligently.
  - If one chef falls sick, another can take over his orders (fault tolerance).
  - To the customer, the kitchen appears as **one unified entity** producing food rapidly.

This is exactly what a cluster does — many complete, independent machines, working under a unified manager, presenting a single coherent interface to the outside world.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners love analogies in 2-mark and 3-mark questions. A well-placed one-line analogy in your answer can often secure full marks.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are the **industry-standard, board-expected numerical benchmarks** for cluster evaluation:

- **MPI (Message Passing Interface):** De-facto standard for HPC cluster communication — latency typically **1–10 μs** in modern InfiniBand fabrics.
- **Typical HPC Cluster Size:** **100–100,000 nodes** (e.g., Frontier supercomputer).
- **High-Availability Cluster Availability Target:** **99.999% ("Five Nines")** → ≈ 5.26 minutes downtime/year.
- **Single System Image Transparency Levels:** 1 (minimal) to 8 (full illusion).

---

## 1.4 Cluster vs. Grid vs. Cloud — Position in the Distributed Computing Hierarchy

| Property | Cluster | Grid | Cloud |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Geographic Scope** | Single room / data center | Geographically dispersed | Global |
| **Node Coupling** | Tightly coupled (LAN/SAN) | Loosely coupled (WAN) | Virtualised, abstracted |
| **Ownership** | Single organization | Multiple, federated | Third-party (provider) |
| **Primary Goal** | HPC / HA | Resource sharing | On-demand elastic service |
| **Connectivity** | Dedicated high-speed | Standard Internet | Internet / API |

> [!NOTE]
> Clusters are the **physical building blocks** upon which Grids and Clouds are constructed. A Cloud Data Center is essentially a **warehouse-scale cluster**.

---

## 1.5 Cluster Classification (KTU Syllabus Directive)

According to the KTU PCCST602 Module 1 syllabus, clusters are classified based on their **primary functional objective** into three canonical categories:

1. **High-Performance Computing (HPC) Clusters** — Optimised for raw compute throughput (e.g., Beowulf clusters, weather modelling).
2. **High-Availability (HA) Clusters** — Optimised for continuous uptime (e.g., database failover clusters, bank transaction servers).
3. **Load-Balancing Clusters** — Optimised for distributing user requests across many front-end nodes (e.g., web server farms).

Additionally, modern taxonomies (post-2020) include **High-Throughput Computing (HTC) Clusters** for batch/big-data workloads (e.g., Hadoop/YARN backends).

# 🧠 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Architecture of a Cluster

A cluster is not "just many computers plugged into a switch." It has a rigorously layered architecture. The KTU 2024 Scheme (per Tanenbaum & Van Stee's *Distributed Systems* model adopted in the syllabus) defines **three canonical layers**:

### Layer 1 — Hardware (Physical) Layer
- **Compute Nodes:** Independent commodity PCs/servers, each with its own CPU, RAM, disk, and I/O.
- **Network Interconnect:** Switched fabric — historically Fast/Gigabit Ethernet, now **InfiniBand**, **Omni-Path**, or **100/200 GbE**.
- **Storage:**
  - **DAS (Direct Attached Storage):** Local disks of each node.
  - **NAS (Network Attached Storage):** File-level access over LAN.
  - **SAN (Storage Area Network):** Block-level access over a dedicated high-speed fabric.

### Layer 2 — Cluster Middleware (Software) Layer
This is the **brain** of the cluster. It implements the Single System Image. KTU expects you to know these sub-components:

- **Resource Manager / Scheduler:** Allocates jobs to nodes (e.g., SLURM, PBS Pro, LSF, Kubernetes Scheduler).
- **Single System Image (SSI) Manager:** Hides the distributed nature.
- **Availability Infrastructure:** Heartbeat monitors, failover managers (e.g., Pacemaker/Corosync).
- **Parallel Programming Libraries:** MPI, OpenMP, OpenSHMEM.
- **Cluster File System:** Lustre, GPFS (IBM Spectrum Scale), BeeGFS.

### Layer 3 — Application (User) Layer
- Domain-specific parallel applications (scientific simulations, databases, web services).

> [!TIP]
> **Exam mnemonic — "H-M-A"**: **H**ardware, **M**iddleware, **A**pplication. The order from bottom to top.

---

## 2.2 Why Clusters? — The Underlying Engineering Rationale

| Limitation of Single Computer | Cluster Solution |
|---|---|
| Bounded by transistor & clock-speed limits | **Horizontal scaling** (add more nodes) |
| Single point of failure | **Redundancy** via replication & failover |
| Low utilization (peak vs. average workload) | **Consolidation** of workloads |
| Expensive proprietary parallel machines | **Commodity hardware** (Beowulf philosophy) |

The cluster model is a direct engineering response to the **end of Dennard Scaling** (~2006) — when individual CPU clock speeds stopped increasing, parallelism (and hence clustering) became the only path forward.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **frequently tested** in the KTU University Exam under "Part B" analytical questions. Memorise the formulae, units, and boundary conditions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{parallel}(n) &= T_{seq} / n + T_{overhead} \\
S(n) &= T_{seq} / T_{parallel} \\
E(n) &= S(n) / n = 1 / (1 + n \cdot T_{overhead} / T_{seq}) \\
A(t) &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \\
N_{eff} &= N_{total} \cdot (1 - F_{fract}) \\
L_{acc} &= S(n) \cdot C_{node} \cdot n + C_{network} + C_{storage} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_{seq}$ | Sequential execution time | seconds (s) | $T_{seq} > 0$ |
| $n$ | Number of cluster nodes | dimensionless integer | $n \geq 1$ |
| $T_{overhead}$ | Parallelization overhead (MPI, sync) | seconds (s) | Increases with $n$ |
| $S(n)$ | Speedup | dimensionless | $S(n) \leq n$ (Amdahl bound) |
| $E(n)$ | Parallel efficiency | ratio $\in [0,1]$ | $E(n) \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$ |
| $A(t)$ | System availability | ratio $\in [0,1]$ | Aims for 0.99999 (5-nines) |
| $MTBF$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours | $MTBF \gg MTTR$ |
| $MTTR$ | Mean Time To Repair | hours | Should be minimised |
| $N_{eff}$ | Effective usable nodes | count | $N_{eff} \leq N_{total}$ |
| $F_{fract}$ | Fraction of failed/unavailable nodes | ratio $\in [0,1]$ | $F_{fract} = 0.10$ for 90% utilisation |
| $C_{node}$ | Per-node capital cost | ₹ / USD | Commodity ~ low; HPC ~ high |
| $L_{acc}$ | Total cluster acquisition cost | ₹ / USD | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Never write $S(n) = n$ for a real cluster. Always include the overhead term, otherwise you will lose the 2 marks allotted for "discussing overhead and scalability."

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CERN Large Hadron Collider (LHC):** ~1 million cores, distributed globally using grid-on-cluster architecture, processing **petabytes/day**.
- **Google Search:** Operates on **warehouse-scale clusters** of 50,000–100,000 commodity servers per data center.
- **Weather Forecasting (ECMWF, IMD):** HPC clusters run the **WRF / GFS** numerical models.
- **Banking Core Systems:** HA clusters ensure **24/7 uptime** for ATM and UPI transaction processing — directly relevant to India's digital payment infrastructure.
- **AI/ML Training:** Modern LLM training is done on **GPU clusters** (e.g., NVIDIA DGX SuperPOD, AWS P5 instances).

---

## 2.5 Scalability — The Fundamental Cluster Challenge

**Horizontal Scalability** (scale-out, adding more nodes) vs. **Vertical Scalability** (scale-up, making one node bigger) — clusters excel at the former.

Three classical types of scalability:

1. **Size Scalability** — Ability to obtain performance gain by adding more nodes.
2. **Geographic Scalability** — Ability to maintain performance despite physical distance between nodes.
3. **Administrative Scalability** — Ability to manage an increasing number of nodes without administrative complexity explosion.

# 🔬 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Amdahl's Law Applied to a Cluster

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Amdahl's Law is the **single most important theoretical result** in cluster performance analysis. It defines the **hard upper bound** on speedup, regardless of how many nodes you add.

**Starting Premise:** Any parallel program consists of a **sequential fraction (f)** that cannot be parallelised, and a **parallel fraction (1 − f)** that can.

**Step 1 — Define the sequential execution time.**

Let the total sequential execution time on a single node be:

$$
T_{seq} = 1.0 \text{ (normalised unit)}
$$

**Step 2 — Partition the time into sequential and parallel parts.**

$$
T_{seq} = f \cdot T_{seq} + (1 - f) \cdot T_{seq}
$$

Here:
- $f \cdot T_{seq}$ = the **serial fraction** (inherently non-parallelisable).
- $(1 - f) \cdot T_{seq}$ = the **parallelisable fraction**.

**Step 3 — Express parallel execution time on n nodes.**

The serial fraction still takes $f \cdot T_{seq}$ (no node can help). The parallel fraction is now divided across $n$ nodes:

$$
T_{parallel}(n) = f \cdot T_{seq} + \frac{(1 - f) \cdot T_{seq}}{n}
$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $T_{seq}$ for clarity.**

$$
T_{parallel}(n) = T_{seq} \cdot \left[ f + \frac{1 - f}{n} \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the definition of speedup $S(n) = T_{seq} / T_{parallel}(n)$.**

$$
S(n) = \frac{T_{seq}}{T_{seq} \cdot \left[ f + \frac{1 - f}{n} \right]} = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1 - f}{n}}
$$

**Step 6 — Take the asymptotic limit as $n \to \infty$ (infinite cluster).**

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{f + \frac{1 - f}{n}} = \frac{1}{f + 0} = \frac{1}{f}
$$

**Conclusion — The Amdahl Bound:**

$$
\boxed{S_{max} = \frac{1}{f}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** If just 5% of a program is sequential ($f = 0.05$), the **maximum possible speedup is 20×** — *no matter how many nodes you add*. This is why clusters must be paired with **highly parallel algorithms**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Availability of an Active-Passive HA Cluster

**Setup:** A 2-node HA cluster runs in active-passive mode. The active node serves requests; the passive node is a hot-standby that takes over upon failure.

**Given Data:**

- $MTBF_{node} = 10{,}000$ hours
- $MTTR = 4$ hours
- $T_{failover\_detect} = 30$ seconds $\approx 0.00833$ hours

**Step 1 — Compute single-node availability.**

$$
A_{single} = \frac{MTBF_{node}}{MTBF_{node} + MTTR} = \frac{10000}{10000 + 4} = \frac{10000}{10004} \approx 0.9996002
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective MTTR of the cluster (downtime per failure).**

The cluster's downtime per failure includes detection time + actual failover time:

$$
MTTR_{cluster} \approx T_{failover\_detect} + MTTR \approx 0.00833 + 4 = 4.00833 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute cluster availability.**

The system fails only if *both* nodes are down. Assuming independence:

$$
A_{cluster} = 1 - (1 - A_{active}) \cdot (1 - A_{passive})
$$

For an active-passive pair with identical nodes and assuming the passive is **hot-standby** (i.e., it does not fail in standby):

$$
A_{cluster} \approx \frac{MTBF_{node}}{MTBF_{node} + MTTR_{cluster}} = \frac{10000}{10000 + 4.00833} \approx 0.9995992
$$

Wait — note that adding a passive node *by itself* does not improve the **failure rate** of a single active node. The actual cluster availability gain comes from eliminating the *repair window* (failover continues while the failed node is being repaired). The full derivation is:

$$
\boxed{A_{cluster} = 1 - \frac{MTTR_{cluster}}{MTBF_{node} + MTTR_{cluster}} \cdot \frac{MTTR_{node}}{MTBF_{node}}}
$$

A simplified practical formula used in industry:

$$
\boxed{A_{cluster} \approx 1 - \frac{MTTR^2}{MTBF^2}}
$$

Numerically:

$$
A_{cluster} \approx 1 - \frac{16}{10^8} = 1 - 1.6 \times 10^{-7} = 0.99999984
$$

This corresponds to the **"Five Nines" (99.999%)** target — the gold standard for bank/finance HA clusters.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cost-Optimal Cluster Sizing

**Problem:** A KTU research lab wants to run a workload with parallel fraction $f = 0.95$. Each compute node costs ₹ 2,00,000. What is the cost-optimal number of nodes to achieve 90% of the maximum possible speedup?

**Step 1 — Maximum possible speedup (Amdahl bound).**

$$
S_{max} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{0.95} \approx 1.0526
$$

**Step 2 — Target speedup (90% of $S_{max}$).**

$$
S_{target} = 0.9 \times 1.0526 \approx 0.9474
$$

**Step 3 — Solve Amdahl's equation for n.**

$$
0.9474 = \frac{1}{0.95 + \frac{0.05}{n}}
$$

Invert both sides:

$$
\frac{1}{0.9474} = 0.95 + \frac{0.05}{n}
$$

$$
1.0556 = 0.95 + \frac{0.05}{n}
$$

$$
\frac{0.05}{n} = 1.0556 - 0.95 = 0.1056
$$

$$
n = \frac{0.05}{0.1056} \approx 0.473
$$

Since $n$ must be $\geq 1$, the lab needs only **1 node** to capture 90% of the available speedup. **Conclusion:** The workload is inherently serial; buying a cluster is not cost-justified.

**Step 4 — Cost computation.**

$$
L_{acc} = 0.473 \times 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹ 94{,}684
$$

But since $n$ is rounded to 1, the actual cost is:

$$
L_{acc} = 1 \times 2{,}00{,}000 = ₹ 2{,}00{,}000
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Lesson:** Amdahl's Law quantitatively proves why **algorithm redesign** (reducing $f$) is often more cost-effective than buying more hardware.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Cluster Type

| Workload Characteristic | Recommended Cluster Type | Key Metric to Optimise | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tightly-coupled, synchronous MPI jobs | **HPC Cluster** | Flops/sec, interconnect latency | CFD simulation, climate model |
| Transactional, user-facing, latency-sensitive | **HA Cluster** | Availability $A(t)$, MTTR | UPI switch, airline reservation |
| Web-tier, request-oriented, stateless | **Load-Balancing Cluster** | Requests/sec, throughput | E-commerce, social media |
| Embarrassingly parallel, batch-oriented | **HTC Cluster** | Job throughput, job-hour efficiency | MapReduce, video transcoding |
| Mixed workload, modern cloud-native | **Cloud Cluster (K8s)** | Cost/performance ratio, elasticity | Microservices SaaS platforms |

> [!NOTE]
> The boundaries are blurring in 2024+: modern container orchestrators (Kubernetes) implement *all four* cluster types simultaneously on the same physical hardware.

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a cluster in the context of distributed computing. List the three primary categories of clusters.**

**Model Answer (Expected for 3 marks):**

> A cluster is a collection of independent, complete computers (nodes) interconnected by a high-speed network and coordinated by cluster middleware to function as a single, unified computing resource, presenting a **Single System Image (SSI)** to the user.
>
> The three primary categories are:
> 1. **High-Performance Computing (HPC) Clusters** — for compute-intensive parallel workloads.
> 2. **High-Availability (HA) Clusters** — for fault-tolerant, always-on services.
> 3. **Load-Balancing Clusters** — for distributing user requests across multiple front-end nodes.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is Single System Image (SSI)? Mention any four services it provides.**

**Model Answer (Expected for 3 marks):**

> **Single System Image (SSI)** is the illusion presented to the user and applications that the entire cluster is a single, unified computer, even though it is composed of many independent nodes.
>
> Four services provided by SSI:
> 1. **Single Entry Point** — One access point (e.g., a virtual IP or head node) for all users.
> 2. **Single File Hierarchy** — A unified view of the file system spanning all nodes.
> 3. **Single Job Management** — Users submit jobs to one queue, scheduler distributes them.
> 4. **Single Process Space** — Unique process IDs cluster-wide, with transparent migration.

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - In Amdahl's Law problems, students **almost always forget the sequential fraction $f$** and incorrectly write $S(n) = n$. This loses **2–3 marks instantly**.
> - Students confuse **MTBF with MTTR** — MTBF is *time between failures*, MTTR is *time to repair*. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> - Diagrams of cluster architecture carry **2 marks**. Always draw the **three layers** explicitly: hardware, middleware, application.
> - For HA cluster questions, the examiner expects you to **draw the topology** (active-passive or active-active), not just describe it verbally.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **(a) Explain the three-layer architecture of a cluster with a neat diagram. Why is the middleware layer considered the most critical?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Three Layers of Cluster Architecture:**

1. **Hardware Layer:** The physical infrastructure comprising compute nodes, network interconnect, and shared storage. Each node is an independent computer with its own CPU, memory, and I/O. They are connected via a high-speed switched network (InfiniBand, Gigabit Ethernet, etc.). Storage may be DAS, NAS, or SAN.

2. **Middleware Layer:** The software "glue" that aggregates the individual nodes into a cluster. It includes:
   - Job scheduler (e.g., SLURM, PBS)
   - Single System Image manager
   - Resource manager
   - High-availability infrastructure (heartbeat, failover)
   - Parallel programming libraries (MPI, OpenMP)
   - Cluster file system (Lustre, GPFS)

3. **Application Layer:** The user-facing programs — scientific simulations, web services, databases, analytics pipelines — that consume the unified cluster resources.

**Why the Middleware Layer is Most Critical:**

- Without middleware, the cluster is just a set of disconnected machines, no better than individual PCs.
- The middleware **implements the Single System Image**, which is the defining property of a cluster.
- It provides **fault tolerance, load distribution, security, and resource allocation** — all the features that distinguish a true cluster from a mere network of workstations.
- Performance, scalability, and availability of the entire cluster depend almost entirely on the quality of the middleware.

*(Include a labelled diagram of the three layers — refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

---

#### **(b) A parallel application has 8% sequential code. Calculate the maximum speedup achievable. If the workload runs in 100 seconds on 1 node, how much time is saved by moving to 16 nodes, assuming ideal zero-overhead scaling for the parallel fraction?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Sequential fraction: $f = 0.08$
- Parallel fraction: $1 - f = 0.92$
- $T_{seq} = 100$ s
- Number of nodes: $n = 16$

**Step 1 — Maximum speedup (Amdahl's bound).**

$$
S_{max} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{0.08} = 12.5
$$

**Step 2 — Sequential time partition.**

- Sequential part: $0.08 \times 100 = 8$ s (cannot be parallelised).
- Parallel part: $0.92 \times 100 = 92$ s.

**Step 3 — Parallel execution time on 16 nodes (ideal scaling for the parallel part).**

$$
T_{parallel}(16) = 8 + \frac{92}{16} = 8 + 5.75 = 13.75 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual speedup achieved.**

$$
S(16) = \frac{100}{13.75} \approx 7.27
$$

**Step 5 — Time saved.**

$$
\Delta T = 100 - 13.75 = 86.25 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

Even with 16 nodes, we get only $7.27/12.5 = 58.2\%$ of the maximum possible speedup. The sequential 8% is the bottleneck — **this is the key insight of Amdahl's Law**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **(a) Differentiate between Active-Passive and Active-Active HA cluster topologies. With a neat diagram, explain how failover works in an Active-Passive cluster.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Property | Active-Passive | Active-Active |
|---|---|---|
| Number of active nodes | 1 | 2 (or more) |
| Standby role | Passive node is idle / hot-standby | All nodes actively serve |
| Resource utilisation | Lower (50% idle) | Higher (100% utilised) |
| Failover behaviour | Passive takes over fully | Load shifts to remaining active nodes |
| Complexity | Simpler | More complex (state synchronisation) |
| Use case | Mission-critical but moderate load | High-traffic critical services |

**Failover Process in Active-Passive Cluster:**

1. **Heartbeat Mechanism:** The passive node continuously sends/receives heartbeat signals (ICMP or custom protocol) from the active node at regular intervals (e.g., every 1 second).

2. **Failure Detection:** If the active node misses 3 consecutive heartbeats (≈ 3 seconds), the passive node declares it failed.

3. **Resource Takeover:** The passive node:
   - Acquires the **virtual IP address** (using tools like `keepalived` or `Pacemaker`).
   - Mounts the **shared storage**.
   - Starts the **critical services** (database, application server).

4. **ARP Update:** The cluster broadcasts a gratuitous ARP to update the network switch's MAC table, redirecting incoming traffic to the new active node.

5. **Recovery (Optional):** When the original active node is repaired, it may either:
   - Remain passive (operator-driven failback), or
   - Automatically resume as active and force the current active to become passive (STONITH — Shoot The Other Node In The Head — is used to prevent split-brain).

*(Include a labelled diagram of the Active-Passive topology — refer to Section 4.3 of these notes.)*

---

#### **(b) An HA cluster has two identical nodes, each with $MTBF = 8760$ hours and $MTTR = 6$ hours. Compute the availability of (i) a single node, (ii) the HA cluster in active-passive configuration, and (iii) the annual downtime in minutes.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $MTBF = 8760$ hours (≈ 1 year)
- $MTTR = 6$ hours
- Active-Passive configuration

**Part (i) — Single Node Availability.**

$$
A_{single} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{8760}{8760 + 6} = \frac{8760}{8766} \approx 0.999315
$$

**Part (ii) — Cluster Availability (Active-Passive).**

The system fails only when both nodes are unavailable. With an active-passive topology, the passive node covers the active node's repair window:

$$
A_{cluster} = 1 - \frac{MTTR^2}{MTBF^2} = 1 - \frac{36}{8760^2}
$$

$$
A_{cluster} = 1 - \frac{36}{76{,}737{,}600} = 1 - 4.69 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.99999953
$$

**Part (iii) — Annual Downtime in Minutes.**

Total minutes in a year:

$$
T_{year} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}
$$

Annual downtime:

$$
D_{annual} = (1 - A_{cluster}) \times T_{year} = 4.69 \times 10^{-7} \times 525{,}600
$$

$$
D_{annual} \approx 0.2465 \text{ minutes} \approx 14.8 \text{ seconds per year}
$$

**Conclusion:** The HA cluster achieves the "**Five Nines**" availability standard (99.999%), reducing annual downtime from **31.6 minutes** (single node) to just **15 seconds** (cluster).

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (15 must-know points before the exam):**

1. **Cluster Definition:** A loosely coupled collection of complete computers working as a single unified resource.
2. **Single System Image (SSI):** The defining property — user sees one machine.
3. **Three Primary Types:** HPC, HA, Load-Balancing (plus HTC in modern taxonomy).
4. **Three-Layer Architecture:** Hardware → Middleware → Application.
5. **Middleware Components:** Scheduler (SLURM), HA manager (Pacemaker), cluster file system (Lustre), parallel libraries (MPI).
6. **Beowulf Cluster:** Commodity hardware + Linux + MPI = cost-effective HPC.
7. **Amdahl's Law:** $S_{max} = 1/f$ — sequential fraction $f$ is the ultimate bottleneck.
8. **Speedup formula:** $S(n) = T_{seq} / T_{parallel}(n)$.
9. **Efficiency formula:** $E(n) = S(n) / n$, with $E(n) \in [0,1]$.
10. **Availability formula:** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$.
11. **HA Topology:** Active-Passive (1 active + 1 standby) vs. Active-Active (both serve).
12. **STONITH:** "Shoot The Other Node In The Head" — prevents split-brain in failover.
13. **Heartbeat:** Periodic signal used to detect node failure; missed beats trigger failover.
14. **Cluster vs. Grid vs. Cloud:** Cluster = single site, Grid = federated multi-org, Cloud = virtualised elastic service.
15. **Warehouse-Scale Cluster:** Modern cloud data centers are massive clusters of 50,000–100,000 commodity servers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Hook for Amdahl's Law:** *"The 5% rule — even 5% sequential code caps your speedup at 20×."*
>
> **Memory Hook for HA:** *"MTBF is how long it works; MTTR is how long it takes to fix."*
>
> **Memory Hook for Architecture:** *"H-M-A — Hardware, Middleware, Application (bottom-up)."*

---

*End of Module 1 Notes — System Models for Distributed and Cloud Computing: Clusters*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Grids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grid Computing** is a distributed computing paradigm that coordinates **heterogeneous, geographically dispersed, and loosely-coupled resources** (compute servers, storage systems, instruments, and data repositories) across administrative domains to deliver common computational goals for solving large-scale, resource-intensive problems. The resources are pooled dynamically and presented to users as a single, unified **virtual resource** — often metaphorically called a *"Virtual Supercomputer."*

A **Grid** is therefore NOT a single machine. It is a *coordinated federation* of independent computing entities that:

- Belong to different administrative domains (different organizations, geographies, ownerships).
- Are shared selectively to form a **Virtual Organization (VO)**.
- Use **standard, open, general-purpose protocols** (unlike the proprietary, tightly-coupled nature of a Cluster).

The term was popularized by **Ian Foster and Carl Kesselman (1998)** in their seminal book *The Grid: Blueprint for a New Computing Infrastructure*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — 2-Mark Definition Anchor**
> A Grid is a hardware and software infrastructure that provides dependable, consistent, pervasive, and inexpensive access to high-end computational capabilities, orchestrated via standard open protocols.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Electrical Power Grid"

Think of a Grid computer exactly like the **electrical power grid** that lights your home:

| Electrical Grid Analogy | Grid Computing Mapping |
|---|---|
| Power stations (hydel, thermal, nuclear) | Geographically distributed compute servers / clusters |
| High-voltage transmission lines | High-speed WAN network (Internet backbone) |
| Step-down transformers at homes | Resource brokers / middleware adapters |
| Your switchboard | User's local machine submitting jobs |
| Electricity as a utility | Computing power as a utility (pay-as-you-use) |

**The intuition:** You do not care *which* power station generated your electricity. You simply *plug in* and consume power. Similarly, in Grid Computing, the user does not care *which* machine executed the job — they simply submit a task and the Grid middleware routes it to the most suitable available resource.

> [!TIP]
> If the analogy "power grid" → "compute grid" clicks, then you have fundamentally understood Grid Computing. The rest is engineering detail.

---

## 1.3 Why Grids Exist — The Driving Forces

1. **Underutilization of existing resources** — Idle desktop PCs, lab workstations, university clusters.
2. **Demand for massive compute** — Problems in bioinformatics (gene sequencing), climate modeling, particle physics (LHC at CERN) need **Petaflop / Exaflop** scale.
3. **Geographic collaboration** — International research consortia need to share data and compute.
4. **Heterogeneous ownership** — No single organization can afford the entire infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Philosophy (Ian Foster's Checklist)** — A true Grid system must satisfy **three** properties:
> 1. **Coordinates resources that are not subject to centralized control.**
> 2. **Uses standard, open, general-purpose protocols and interfaces.**
> 3. **Delivers nontrivial qualities of service** (throughput, response time, security).

---

## 1.4 Fundamental Characteristics of a Grid

- **Heterogeneity** — Mix of hardware (x86, ARM, GPUs), OS (Linux, Windows), and network (Ethernet, Infiniband).
- **Resource Sharing** — Selective, controlled sharing between VOs.
- **Virtualization** — Resources appear as a single logical pool.
- **Scalability** — Should gracefully handle growth from 10 to 10,000 nodes.
- **Adaptability / Fault Tolerance** — Must handle node failures without job loss.
- **Security** — Strong authentication and authorization across trust boundaries.
- **Standardization** — Adheres to OGSA (Open Grid Services Architecture) and WSRF standards.
- **Decentralized Control** — No single admin owns all nodes.

---

## 1.5 Types of Grids (Classification Anchor)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks this as a **3-mark question** in Part A.

| Type | Primary Resource Shared | Canonical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Compute Grid** | CPU cycles, processing power | Scientific simulations, Monte Carlo analysis |
| **Data Grid** | Storage capacity and large datasets | CERN's LHC data, genomic databases |
| **Knowledge Grid** | Knowledge artifacts, ontologies | AI reasoning, semantic search |
| **Utility Grid** | Everything as a metered service | Commercial on-demand computing (precursor to Cloud) |
| **Desktop Grid / Volunteer Computing** | Idle desktop PCs | SETI@home, Folding@home |
| **Sensor Grid** | Real-time sensor data streams | Environmental monitoring, IoT backbones |

---

## 1.6 Grid vs. Cluster vs. Cloud — The 60-Second Distinction

This is **the single most-asked comparison** in KTU exams for this module.

| Parameter | Cluster | Grid | Cloud |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coupling** | Tightly coupled (LAN) | Loosely coupled (WAN/Internet) | Loosely coupled (Internet) |
| **Ownership** | Single organization | Multiple organizations (VOs) | Single provider (multi-tenant) |
| **Heterogeneity** | Mostly homogeneous | Strongly heterogeneous | Abstracted / virtualized |
| **Resource Type** | Homogeneous compute | Heterogeneous + distributed | Virtualized pools |
| **Middleware** | Lightweight (e.g., MPI) | Heavy (Globus, gLite) | Lightweight (Hypervisor) |
| **Goal** | High throughput single app | Share across orgs | Elastic on-demand service |
| **Billing** | CapEx (one-time) | Quota / collaborative | OpEx (pay-per-use) |
| **Examples** | Beowulf, Sun Cluster | EGEE, TeraGrid | AWS EC2, Azure VM |
| **Scheduling** | Centralized scheduler | Distributed / decentralized | Provider-side orchestration |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic Anchor:** Cluster = *one* family. Grid = *neighborhoods cooperating*. Cloud = *rented apartment service*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Open Grid Services Architecture (OGSA) — The 5-Layer Stack

The **de facto** architectural reference model for Grid Computing, defined by the Global Grid Forum (GGF) and formalized in OGSA. KTU examiners expect students to draw AND explain this diagram.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — The Five Layers (Bottom to Top):**

### Layer 1 — FABRIC LAYER (Physical / Resource Layer)
- Contains the **actual physical resources**: compute nodes, storage arrays, network fabrics, scientific instruments, sensors.
- Provides **local resource managers** (e.g., local Linux `cgroups`, LSF, Torque).
- Exposes low-level capabilities upward via local interfaces.

### Layer 2 — CONNECTIVITY LAYER
- Defines the **core communication and security protocols** for Grid transactions.
- **Protocols:** TCP/IP, HTTP/S, SOAP, TLS/SSL.
- **Security foundation:** GSI (Grid Security Infrastructure) with X.509 certificates, mutual authentication, delegation.
- Establishes **trust** between distinct administrative domains.

### Layer 3 — RESOURCE LAYER
- Manages **individual resources** using the connectivity layer.
- Implements **GRID Resource Information Service (GRIS)** and **Grid Resource Registration Protocol (GRRP)** for resource discovery and monitoring.
- Implements **Grid Resource Access Management (GRAM)** protocol for job submission to local schedulers.
- Concerns: allocation, monitoring, accounting of *single* resources.

### Layer 4 — COLLECTIVE LAYER
- Coordinates **multiple resources** across the grid.
- Services here are *directory-level* (not single-resource): Resource discovery, brokering, monitoring, data replication, community scheduling.
- Implements **Meta-Schedulers** (e.g., Condor-G, PBS-Pro Grid).
- Provides **VO-level** services: certificate authorities, accounting portals, data catalogs.

### Layer 5 — APPLICATION LAYER
- Contains the **user-facing applications** that consume grid services.
- Each application calls APIs from the layers below using standard toolkits (Globus, gLite, Unicore).
- Examples: BLAST for bioinformatics, climate simulators, financial Monte Carlo.

---

## 2.2 Virtual Organization (VO) — The Heart of Grid

> [!NOTE]
> A **Virtual Organization** is a dynamic, cross-institutional collection of individuals, institutions, and resources that share a common objective and a common set of rules governing access and usage.

**Example:** The **LHC Computing Grid (LCG)** has thousands of physicists from 50+ countries, sharing compute and storage to analyze petabytes of particle collision data. They collectively form a single VO.

**VO characteristics:**
- Dynamic membership (join/leave).
- Distributed governance.
- Shared policy and trust fabric.
- Resource sharing governed by SLAs (Service Level Agreements).

---

## 2.3 Grid Middleware — The "Glue"

Middleware is the software layer that abstracts resource heterogeneity and presents a unified interface.

| Middleware | Origin | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Globus Toolkit** | Argonne National Lab / USC (USA) | Reference implementation of OGSA; GRAM, MDS, GSI services |
| **gLite** | CERN / EGEE project | Lightweight middleware for LHC data grid |
| **Unicore** | Germany | Java-based, workflow-driven |
| **BOINC** | UC Berkeley | Volunteer / desktop grid platform (SETI@home) |
| **Condor** | Univ. of Wisconsin | High-throughput scheduling, supports Grid via Condor-G |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These equations form the analytical spine of Grid performance analysis. Memorize the units and constraints.**

### 2.4.1 Speedup (Amdahl's Law for Grid Parallelism)

Let:
- $T_s$ = execution time on a single (uniprocessor) node
- $T_p$ = execution time on $p$ parallel grid nodes
- $f$ = fraction of code that **must remain sequential** (serial portion)

$$
S(p) \;=\; \frac{T_s}{T_p} \;=\; \frac{1}{f + \dfrac{(1-f)}{p}}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $S(p)$ | Speedup achieved using $p$ nodes | Dimensionless ratio |
| $f$ | Sequential fraction of workload | $0 \le f \le 1$ |
| $p$ | Number of grid nodes | Positive integer |

### 2.4.2 Maximum Theoretical Speedup (Limit as p → ∞)

$$
S_{\max} \;=\; \lim_{p \to \infty} S(p) \;=\; \frac{1}{f}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even with infinite nodes, speedup is bounded by $\frac{1}{f}$. The sequential bottleneck dominates.

### 2.4.3 Parallel Efficiency

$$
E(p) \;=\; \frac{S(p)}{p} \;=\; \frac{1}{p\,\!\left(f + \dfrac{1-f}{p}\right)}
$$

Efficiency approaches 1 (ideal) when $f$ is small and $p$ is moderate. Falls as $p$ grows due to communication overhead.

### 2.4.4 Grid Throughput (Jobs per Unit Time)

$$
\text{Throughput} \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{completed}}}{T_{\text{observation}}}
$$

Where $N_{\text{completed}}$ = number of jobs finished in observation window.

### 2.4.5 Total Job Execution Time on Grid

$$
T_{\text{total}} \;=\; T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{transfer}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{result\_return}}
$$

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $T_{\text{queue}}$ | Time spent waiting for resource allocation |
| $T_{\text{transfer}}$ | Time to move input data across the WAN |
| $T_{\text{compute}}$ | Actual execution time on the chosen resource |
| $T_{\text{result\_return}}$ | Time to return outputs to the user |

### 2.4.6 Bandwidth × Latency Cost (Data Grid)

$$
T_{\text{transfer}} \;=\; \frac{D}{B} + L
$$

Where $D$ = data size, $B$ = bandwidth, $L$ = network latency.

### 2.4.7 Cost-Efficiency Index (Custom KTU Metric)

$$
\eta_{\text{grid}} \;=\; \frac{S(p)}{C_{\text{grid}}}
$$

Where $C_{\text{grid}}$ = total cost of owning/renting the $p$ grid nodes. The grid configuration with **highest $\eta$** is operationally optimal.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Grid Application |
|---|---|
| **Bioinformatics** | BLAST gene-sequence search across distributed genome databases |
| **High Energy Physics** | CERN LHC — 1 PB/sec raw data, processed by 170+ grid sites |
| **Climate Science** | IPCC global climate simulation across international supercomputers |
| **Drug Discovery** | Molecular docking with millions of ligand candidates |
| **Finance** | Monte Carlo option pricing across risk-analytics grid |
| **Astronomy** | SETI@home, Einstein@home (volunteer grids) |
| **Earthquake Engineering** | Shake-table parallel simulations |

---

## 2.6 Grid Security Infrastructure (GSI) — Quick Reference

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **X.509 Certificates** | Identity for users/services (issued by CA) |
| **Proxy Certificates** | Short-lived delegated credentials (avoids password transmission) |
| **Mutual Authentication** | Both client & server authenticate each other |
| **GSI-Secure Conversation** | TLS-based session keys for confidentiality |
| **Community Authorization Service (CAS)** | VO-level fine-grained access control |
| **MyProxy** | Credential repository for delegated logins |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Speedup Bound of a Grid (Amdahl's Law)

> **Problem Context:** A scientific workload is deployed on a Grid of $p$ identical compute nodes. Performance engineers determine that **12%** of the workload code is inherently sequential (file IO, single-thread initialization, serial reduction). Find: (i) the speedup on $p = 8$ nodes, (ii) the asymptotic maximum speedup, (iii) the efficiency at $p = 8$.

### Step-by-Step

**Given:**
- Sequential fraction $f = 0.12$
- Parallelizable fraction = $1 - f = 0.88$
- Number of grid nodes $p = 8$

**Step 1 — Write Amdahl's Law.**

$$
S(p) \;=\; \frac{T_s}{T_p} \;=\; \frac{1}{f + \dfrac{(1-f)}{p}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values into the parallelizable portion.**

$$
\frac{1-f}{p} \;=\; \frac{0.88}{8} \;=\; 0.1100
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
f + \frac{1-f}{p} \;=\; 0.12 + 0.1100 \;=\; 0.2300
$$

**Step 4 — Invert to obtain speedup.**

$$
S(8) \;=\; \frac{1}{0.2300} \;=\; 4.3478
$$

**Step 5 — Asymptotic maximum (limit as $p \to \infty$).**

$$
S_{\max} \;=\; \lim_{p \to \infty} \frac{1}{0.12 + \dfrac{0.88}{p}} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.12 + 0} \;=\; \frac{1}{0.12} \;=\; 8.3333
$$

**Step 6 — Parallel Efficiency at $p = 8$.**

$$
E(8) \;=\; \frac{S(8)}{p} \;=\; \frac{4.3478}{8} \;=\; 0.5435 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 54.35\%
$$

**Result Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $S(8)$ | $\approx 4.35\times$ |
| $S_{\max}$ | $\approx 8.33\times$ |
| $E(8)$ | $\approx 54.35\%$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even with infinite nodes, this workload is capped at **8.33× speedup** because the 12% serial code is the bottleneck. The engineer should refactor the serial portion to unlock further speedup — *not* buy more nodes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Grid Job Latency for a Data-Intensive Task

> **Problem Context:** A bioinformatics job needs to analyze a 2.4 GB genomic dataset stored at a remote grid data center. The inter-site WAN bandwidth is 600 Mbps, the one-way network latency is 80 ms, the queue wait time is 2 minutes, compute time on the assigned node is 5.5 minutes, and result return is 200 KB. Find total user-perceived latency.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Compute data transfer time.**

$$
T_{\text{transfer}} \;=\; \frac{D}{B} + L
$$

Convert: $D = 2.4 \text{ GB} = 2.4 \times 8 = 19.2 \text{ Gb}$. Bandwidth $B = 600 \text{ Mbps}$.

$$
T_{\text{transfer}} \;=\; \frac{19.2 \text{ Gb}}{0.6 \text{ Gb/s}} + 0.08 \text{ s} \;=\; 32.0 \text{ s} + 0.08 \text{ s} \;=\; 32.08 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute result return time.**

Result $D_r = 200 \text{ KB} = 200 \times 8 = 1600 \text{ Kb} = 1.6 \text{ Mb}$.

$$
T_{\text{result}} \;=\; \frac{1.6 \text{ Mb}}{600 \text{ Mbps}} + 0.08 \text{ s} \;=\; 0.00267 \text{ s} + 0.08 \text{ s} \;\approx\; 0.083 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum all components.**

$$
T_{\text{total}} \;=\; T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{transfer}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{result}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} \;=\; 120 \text{ s} + 32.08 \text{ s} + 330 \text{ s} + 0.083 \text{ s} \;=\; 482.16 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} \;\approx\; 8 \text{ min } 2.16 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Bottleneck analysis.**

| Component | Duration | % of Total |
|---|---|---|
| Queue wait | 120 s | 24.9% |
| Data transfer | 32.08 s | 6.7% |
| Compute | 330 s | **68.4% (bottleneck)** |
| Result return | 0.083 s | 0.02% |

> [!TIP]
> **Optimization Recommendation:** Since compute dominates, upgrading the compute node yields the highest gain. The data transfer is acceptable; no bandwidth upgrade is urgent.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optimal Grid Node Count for Cost-Efficiency

> **Problem Context:** A grid node costs ₹$\,45,000$ per month to operate (power, cooling, amortized hardware). Each additional node adds a fixed **communication overhead** of 0.5% per node to the workload's effective sequential fraction. Find the optimal $p$ that **maximizes** $\eta_{\text{grid}} = \dfrac{S(p)}{C_{\text{grid}}}$.

**Step 1 — Express effective sequential fraction with overhead.**

$$
f_{\text{eff}}(p) \;=\; f_0 + 0.005 \cdot (p-1)
$$

where $f_0 = 0.10$ (baseline serial fraction).

**Step 2 — Express speedup.**

$$
S(p) \;=\; \frac{1}{f_0 + 0.005\,(p-1) + \dfrac{1 - f_0 - 0.005\,(p-1)}{p}}
$$

**Step 3 — Cost function.**

$$
C_{\text{grid}}(p) \;=\; 45{,}000 \cdot p \;\; \text{(₹ per month)}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\eta(p)$ for candidate $p$ values.**

| $p$ | $f_{\text{eff}}$ | $S(p)$ | $C_{\text{grid}}$ (₹) | $\eta$ (× per ₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.1000 | 1.000 | 45,000 | 0.0000222 |
| 2 | 0.1050 | 1.91 | 90,000 | 0.0000212 |
| 4 | 0.1150 | 3.61 | 180,000 | 0.0000201 |
| 8 | 0.1350 | 6.21 | 360,000 | 0.0000173 |
| 12 | 0.1550 | 7.87 | 540,000 | 0.0000146 |
| 16 | 0.1750 | 8.89 | 720,000 | 0.0000123 |
| 20 | 0.1950 | 9.55 | 900,000 | 0.0000106 |

**Step 5 — Identify the maximum.**

The maximum $\eta$ occurs at **$p = 1$** under the assumed cost model — but in real grid deployments, nodes are *shared*, so marginal cost is closer to zero. The model demonstrates the classic **diminishing returns of scale**: each additional node yields less speedup per rupee.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** This is the kind of analysis that earns full marks when a student explicitly identifies the bottleneck, draws the table, and concludes with engineering insight — *not* by merely writing the formula.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended Architecture | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Single-department tight-coupled HPC simulation | **Cluster** | Low latency LAN, homogeneous nodes |
| Multi-national research collaboration sharing petabytes of LHC data | **Data Grid** | Geographically distributed data, VOs |
| University sharing idle desktop PCs at night for SETI-style problems | **Desktop Grid / BOINC** | Best-effort, voluntary, fault-tolerant |
| On-demand burst compute for a startup with no hardware | **Cloud** (not Grid) | Elastic, metered, self-service |
| Real-time seismic sensor data fusion across continents | **Sensor Grid** | Stream-oriented, low-latency ingest |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — OGSA 5-Layer Grid Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    user1[USER APPLICATION LAYER L5]
    user2[Bioinformatics BLAST]
    user3[Climate Simulator]
    user4[Monte Carlo Engine]

    appL[APPLICATION LAYER L5]
    colL[COLLECTIVE LAYER L4]
    resL[RESOURCE LAYER L3]
    connL[CONNECTIVITY LAYER L2]
    fabL[FABRIC LAYER L1 PHYSICAL RESOURCES]

    resA[Compute Servers CPU GPU]
    resB[Storage Arrays SAN NAS]
    resC[Scientific Instruments]
    resD[Sensor Networks]
    resE[Network Backbone WAN]

    brok[Resource Broker Meta Scheduler]
    dirS[Directory Service MDS]
    repS[Data Replication Service]
    casS[Community Authorization CAS]

    gram[GRAM Protocol]
    gris[GRIS Information Service]
    grrp[GRRP Registration Protocol]

    gsi[GSI Security X509 TLS]
    tcpip[TCP IP HTTP SOAP]
    auth[Mutual Authentication]

    user1 --> appL
    user2 --> appL
    user3 --> appL
    user4 --> appL

    appL --> colL
    colL --> brok
    colL --> dirS
    colL --> repS
    colL --> casS

    colL --> resL
    resL --> gram
    resL --> gris
    resL --> grrp

    resL --> connL
    connL --> gsi
    connL --> tcpip
    connL --> auth

    connL --> fabL
    fabL --> resA
    fabL --> resB
    fabL --> resC
    fabL --> resD
    fabL --> resE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Data and control flow **top-down** for service usage, and **bottom-up** for resource advertisement. The user touches only the Application Layer; lower-layer details are abstracted by middleware.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid — Virtual Organization (VO) Resource Federation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    voA[VIRTUAL ORGANIZATION LHC PHYSICS COLLABORATION]

    org1[INSTITUTE A INDIA]
    org2[UNIVERSITY B GERMANY]
    org3[LAB C USA]
    org4[INSTITUTE D JAPAN]

    res1[Cluster 500 CPU]
    res2[Petabyte Storage]
    res3[GPU Farm 200 GPU]
    res4[Network 100 Gbps]

    user1[Researcher A]
    user2[Researcher B]
    user3[Researcher C]

    caA[Certificate Authority]
    ssoA[Single Sign On]
    casA[Authorization Server]

    user1 --> voA
    user2 --> voA
    user3 --> voA

    voA --> org1
    voA --> org2
    voA --> org3
    voA --> org4

    org1 --> res1
    org2 --> res2
    org3 --> res3
    org4 --> res4

    voA --> caA
    voA --> ssoA
    voA --> casA
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid — Grid Job Lifecycle (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[User Submits Job via Portal]
    authN[Authentication via GSI]
    authZ[Authorization via CAS]
    disc[Resource Discovery via MDS]
    brok[Meta Scheduler Selects Best Resource]
    queueNode[Job Queued at Local Scheduler]
    trans[Data Staging to Compute Node]
    exec[Job Execution on Resource]
    mon[Monitoring and Heartbeat]
    comp[Job Complete]
    ret[Result Staging Back to User]
    aud[Accounting and Logging]
    endNode[User Receives Result]

    startNode --> authN
    authN --> authZ
    authZ --> disc
    disc --> brok
    brok --> queueNode
    queueNode --> trans
    trans --> exec
    exec --> mon
    mon --> comp
    comp --> ret
    ret --> aud
    aud --> endNode
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Drawing Tip:** Examiners award 1–2 marks extra for clean, labeled arrows and a legend. Always include the **direction of data flow** and name the protocol in parentheses (e.g., "submit via GRAM").

---

## 4.4 Mermaid — Grid vs Cluster vs Cloud Decision Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{Need to share across multiple organizations?}
    q2{Real time elastic on demand required?}
    q3{All nodes owned by one admin on same LAN?}
    ans1[Use GRID COMPUTING]
    ans2[Use CLOUD COMPUTING]
    ans3[Use CLUSTER COMPUTING]

    q1 -->|Yes| ans1
    q1 -->|No| q2
    q2 -->|Yes| ans2
    q2 -->|No| q3
    q3 -->|Yes| ans3
    q3 -->|No| ans1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

### **Q1. Define Grid Computing. List any FOUR characteristics of a Grid.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Grid Computing** is a distributed computing paradigm that coordinates heterogeneous, geographically dispersed resources from multiple administrative domains to deliver a unified virtual computing infrastructure for solving large-scale problems.
>
> **Four Characteristics:**
> 1. **Heterogeneity** — Resources vary in hardware, OS, and network.
> 2. **Resource Sharing** — Selective sharing among Virtual Organizations.
> 3. **Scalability** — Graceful growth from tens to thousands of nodes.
> 4. **Decentralized Control** — No single administrative authority.
>
> *(Optional 4th can be: Fault Tolerance, Standardization, Security.)*

---

### **Q2. What is a Virtual Organization (VO)? Give one real-world example.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> A **Virtual Organization (VO)** is a dynamic, cross-institutional alliance of individuals, institutions, and computing resources that collaborate to achieve shared goals, governed by a common set of sharing rules.
>
> **Example:** The **LHC Computing Grid (LCG)** at CERN, where thousands of physicists from 50+ countries pool compute and storage to analyze particle collision data.

---

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End-Semester Exam — Module 1 — 14-Mark Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

#### **(a) Explain the five-layered Open Grid Services Architecture (OGSA) with a neat diagram.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

The **OGSA**, defined by the Global Grid Forum, provides a layered reference model for Grid Computing. The five layers, from bottom to top, are:

| Layer | Responsibility | Key Protocols / Services |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Fabric Layer** | Hosts physical resources (compute, storage, instruments, sensors). Provides local resource managers (LSF, Torque). | Local OS schedulers, SAN drivers |
| **2. Connectivity Layer** | Defines core communication and security protocols enabling Grid transactions. | TCP/IP, HTTP/S, SOAP, TLS/SSL, GSI |
| **3. Resource Layer** | Manages individual resources; defines protocols for resource access, monitoring, and information publishing. | GRAM (job submission), GRIS (information), GRRP (registration) |
| **4. Collective Layer** | Coordinates multiple resources; offers directory-level, VO-wide services. | Resource brokers, MDS directories, data replication, CAS |
| **5. Application Layer** | Hosts user-facing applications and toolkits that consume grid services. | Globus Toolkit, gLite, Unicore client APIs |

**Diagram (re-draw in exam):**

```
+-------------------------------------+
|   APPLICATION LAYER (L5)            |  ← BLAST, Climate Sim
+-------------------------------------+
|   COLLECTIVE LAYER (L4)             |  ← Brokers, MDS, CAS
+-------------------------------------+
|   RESOURCE LAYER (L3)               |  ← GRAM, GRIS, GRRP
+-------------------------------------+
|   CONNECTIVITY LAYER (L2)           |  ← TCP/IP, GSI, TLS
+-------------------------------------+
|   FABRIC LAYER (L1)                 |  ← CPU, Storage, Sensors
+-------------------------------------+
```

**Conclusion:** OGSA provides a clean separation of concerns, enabling independent evolution of protocols at each layer and interoperability across heterogeneous grid deployments.

---

#### **(b) Compare Grid Computing with Cluster and Cloud Computing across any SEVEN parameters.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Cluster | Grid | Cloud |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coupling** | Tightly coupled (LAN) | Loosely coupled (WAN) | Loosely coupled (Internet) |
| **Ownership** | Single organization | Multiple organizations (VO) | Single provider, multi-tenant |
| **Heterogeneity** | Mostly homogeneous | Strongly heterogeneous | Virtualized / abstracted |
| **Distance Between Nodes** | Within meters / building | Across cities / continents | Across data centers globally |
| **Resource Type** | Homogeneous compute | Heterogeneous + distributed | Virtualized compute / storage pools |
| **Middleware** | Lightweight (MPI, OpenMP) | Heavy (Globus, gLite) | Hypervisor + API (OpenStack) |
| **Goal** | High-throughput single app | Resource sharing across orgs | Elastic, on-demand service |
| **Billing** | CapEx (capital expense) | Cooperative / shared | OpEx (pay-per-use metered) |
| **Examples** | Beowulf, Sun Cluster | EGEE, TeraGrid, LCG | AWS EC2, Microsoft Azure |
| **Scheduling** | Centralized (single scheduler) | Distributed / decentralized | Provider-side orchestrator |

**Conclusion:** The three paradigms are *complementary*, not competitive. Clusters excel at single-org HPC; Grids excel at cross-org collaboration; Clouds excel at elastic commercial services.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

#### **(a) Explain the Grid Security Infrastructure (GSI) in detail. Highlight the role of X.509 certificates, proxy certificates, and Mutual Authentication.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

The **Grid Security Infrastructure (GSI)** is the security fabric of OGSA. It enables secure communication, single sign-on, and delegation across mutually distrustful administrative domains.

**1. X.509 Certificate-Based Identity**
- Every grid user and service holds a **digital certificate** issued by a trusted **Certificate Authority (CA)**.
- The certificate binds a **Distinguished Name (DN)** to a **public key**.
- The user's private key (kept secret) is used for digital signatures; the public key is used by others to verify.

**2. Proxy Certificates — Delegation**
- Users do **not transmit their private key** to remote services.
- They create a **short-lived proxy certificate** (typically valid 12 hours) signed by their long-lived credential.
- The proxy can further delegate to other services, enabling **single sign-on** across multiple grid sites.
- Compromised proxies expire automatically, limiting damage.

**3. Mutual Authentication**
- Both client **and** server present certificates and prove possession of their private keys.
- Achieved via the **TLS handshake** (GSI-Secure Conversation).
- Prevents man-in-the-middle and impersonation attacks.

**4. Authorization Layer — Community Authorization Service (CAS)**
- Certificates establish *identity*; CAS enforces *policy*.
- The VO administrator grants fine-grained permissions (e.g., "this user may run jobs of max 8 hours on this cluster").
- The local resource enforces the CAS-issued capability token.

**5. MyProxy — Credential Repository**
- Allows users to **store** long-lived credentials on a trusted MyProxy server.
- Users retrieve short-lived proxies as needed — enables web portal logins.

**Conclusion:** GSI solves the **cross-domain trust** problem that distinguishes Grid from Cluster. Without GSI, federated VOs would be operationally infeasible.

---

#### **(b) Discuss the major applications and challenges of Grid Computing.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

### Applications

1. **High Energy Physics** — CERN's LHC Computing Grid processes ~1 PB/sec of collision data across 170+ sites.
2. **Bioinformatics** — Distributed BLAST searches across genomic databases.
3. **Climate Modeling** — IPCC simulations of global warming across international supercomputers.
4. **Drug Discovery** — Virtual screening of millions of molecular compounds (molecular docking).
5. **Financial Modeling** — Monte Carlo simulations for option pricing and risk analytics.
6. **Earthquake Engineering** — Parallel finite-element simulations of structural dynamics.
7. **Astronomy & Citizen Science** — SETI@home, Folding@home (volunteer desktop grids).

### Challenges

1. **Heterogeneity** — Integrating diverse hardware, OS, and middleware stacks is engineering-intensive.
2. **Security** — Cross-domain trust, credential management, and policy enforcement remain complex.
3. **Scalability of Scheduling** — Centralized schedulers do not scale to thousands of nodes; decentralized schedulers introduce consistency issues.
4. **Network Latency** — WAN latency dwarfs LAN performance; data-intensive jobs suffer.
5. **Fault Tolerance** — Nodes can disappear (volunteer PCs); jobs must checkpoint and resume.
6. **Standardization** — Despite OGSA, multiple competing middleware stacks cause fragmentation.
7. **Quality of Service (QoS)** — Guaranteeing throughput, response time, and availability is harder than in a single data center.
8. **Interoperability with Cloud** — Many workloads have migrated to Cloud, raising the question of Grid's continued relevance.

**Conclusion:** Grid Computing remains the dominant paradigm for **cross-organizational, data-intensive, science-driven** workloads. Its future increasingly intersects with **Cloud Federation** and **Federated Learning** architectures.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing Grid with Cluster or Cloud** — Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if the student treats "Grid" and "Cluster" as synonyms. Always emphasize the **multi-organization / WAN / VO** nature of Grid.
> 2. **Skipping the diagram in the OGSA question** — A neat labeled 5-layer diagram is **mandatory** for full 7 marks. Without it, expect a 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Forgetting the 3 Ian Foster properties** — *"Not centralized control, standard protocols, nontrivial QoS."* Examiners award bonus marks for citing them.
> 4. **Writing "Cluster is faster than Grid"** as a blanket statement — Always qualify with context (single-app vs. multi-org).
> 5. **Omitting units in derivations** — Speedup is dimensionless, throughput is jobs/sec, transfer time is seconds. Missing units = −0.5 mark each.
> 6. **Wrong layer order in OGSA** — Fabric is **bottom**, Application is **top**. Reversed diagram = −1.5 marks.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Grid** = federation of heterogeneous, multi-org resources presenting a virtual supercomputer.
- [x] **Core Analogy:** Electrical power grid → compute grid.
- [x] **Ian Foster's 3 Grid criteria:** decentralized control + open protocols + nontrivial QoS.
- [x] **Six types of Grids:** Compute, Data, Knowledge, Utility, Desktop, Sensor.
- [x] **OGSA 5 Layers (bottom-up):** Fabric → Connectivity → Resource → Collective → Application.
- [x] **VO** = dynamic cross-institutional alliance with shared policies (e.g., LHC).
- [x] **Key Middleware:** Globus Toolkit, gLite, Unicore, BOINC, Condor.
- [x] **Amdahl's Law:** $S(p) = \dfrac{1}{f + (1-f)/p}$; max speedup = $1/f$.
- [x] **Efficiency:** $E(p) = S(p)/p$.
- [x] **Total Latency:** $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{queue}} + T_{\text{transfer}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{result}}$.
- [x] **GSI stack:** X.509 + Proxy Certs + Mutual Auth (TLS) + CAS + MyProxy.
- [x] **Grid ≠ Cluster ≠ Cloud:** WAN-based VO sharing vs. LAN-based single-org vs. elastic metered service.
- [x] **Real-world showcase:** CERN LHC Grid (170+ sites, petabytes/day).
- [x] **Biggest Challenges:** security, scheduling at scale, WAN latency, fault tolerance, fragmentation.
- [x] **Modern Relevance:** Grid ↔ Cloud federation, edge computing, federated learning.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: P2P Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** P2P Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition — Peer-to-Peer (P2P) System**
> A **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) System** is a *distributed application architecture* in which participating nodes (called **peers**) share and consume resources (CPU cycles, storage, bandwidth, content, services) directly with one another, **without the mandatory mediation of a central server**. Every node acts simultaneously as a **client** (resource consumer) and a **server** (resource provider), forming a self-organizing, symmetric overlay network over the underlying physical Internet.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (Course Outcome mapping for PCCST602), P2P systems are positioned as a foundational pillar of modern *distributed computing paradigms*, alongside **client–server**, **cluster**, and **cloud** models. They form the architectural backbone of systems such as BitTorrent, IPFS, Blockchain, Skype (legacy), and Bitcoin.

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight**
> As per Module 1 of PCCST602, students must be able to:
> 1. Differentiate P2P from traditional client–server architectures.
> 2. Classify P2P systems based on **structure** (structured / unstructured / hybrid) and **coordination** (centralized lookup / decentralized lookup).
> 3. Analyze overlay construction algorithms (Chord, Pastry, CAN, Tapestry).
> 4. Evaluate lookup complexity, fault tolerance, and scalability metrics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Village Library" Model

Imagine a village where there is **no central library building**. Instead, every household keeps some books on its own shelf.

- **Sharing**: If your neighbour wants a book you have, they borrow it directly from you.
- **Searching**: To find a book, you don't call a librarian — you ask a few neighbours, who ask their neighbours, until the book is found (this is *query flooding*).
- **Joining**: A new family moving into the village automatically becomes both a *lender* and a *borrower* — they don't need anyone's permission.
- **Resilience**: If one house burns down (peer failure), the books still exist in other houses.

This is exactly how a P2P system works. Each peer contributes storage/compute/bandwidth and benefits from the collective pool.

### 1.3 Core Properties of a P2P System

| # | Property | Description | KTU Exam Keyword |
|---|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Decentralization** | No single point of control or failure | "absence of central authority" |
| 2 | **Symmetry** | Every peer is both client and server | "peers are autonomous & equal" |
| 3 | **Self-organization** | Network forms without administrator | "ad-hoc overlay construction" |
