> 2. **Speedup ≠ Efficiency.** Speedup $S$ is absolute (×); efficiency $E = S/N$ is a ratio (0 to 1). Many students interchange these and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Asymptotic limit** is the speedup as $N \to \infty$. Do not compute it for finite $N$.
> 4. **In diagrams,** the head node must be *separate* from the compute nodes. Drawing them inside one box is a structural error and costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Communication overhead** in real clusters is rarely zero. If the question mentions "with communication cost," you *must* use the extended formula $S_{real}$ from §3.1, Step 5.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **cluster** = collection of COTS nodes + high-speed network + middleware scheduler + shared storage.
- ✅ The **five design objectives** are: Scalability, High Availability, Single System Image, Price/Performance, Flexibility.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ — speedup is **bounded** by $\dfrac{1}{1-P}$.
- ✅ **Gustafson's Law:** $S = N - (N-1)f_s$ — speedup is **linear** when problem size scales.
- ✅ **Availability formula:** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$; "five nines" = 99.999%.
- ✅ **SSI** has 4 layers: hardware, software, middleware, application.
- ✅ **Beowulf (1994)** is the historical origin of modern cluster computing.
- ✅ The **head node** is for login/submission; **compute nodes** do the work; **storage** is shared.
- ✅ The **interconnect** (InfiniBand / 100 GbE) is the *backbone* — bottleneck if not sized correctly.
- ✅ Real clusters must account for **communication overhead** $T_{comm} = t_s + t_w \cdot m$.
- ✅ Sequential portions **strangle** scalability — always profile and refactor.
- ✅ Modern GPU clusters (e.g., NVIDIA H100, AMD MI300) deliver **>50 PetaFLOPS** per rack.
- ✅ KTU's favorite exam trick: ask for **speedup + efficiency + asymptotic limit** in one problem — practice all three together.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Issues: Ensuring High Availability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design Issues: Ensuring High Availability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High Availability (HA)** is a quality-of-service engineering discipline and a set of architectural design principles that collectively ensure a computing system remains operationally accessible and functionally usable for a *contractually guaranteed* proportion of time, despite the occurrence of hardware faults, software bugs, network partitions, or planned maintenance events.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme — Advanced Computing Systems (PCCST602)**, high availability is treated as a *first-class non-functional design requirement* that must be engineered into distributed systems, cloud platforms, and large-scale service architectures from the earliest design phase (not retrofitted later).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024, Module 2):** The term *Design Issues* explicitly signals that students must reason about **trade-offs** — availability vs. cost, availability vs. consistency, availability vs. performance. Merely defining HA is insufficient; you must discuss the **issues** (problems/constraints) that arise when designing for HA.

### Key Terminology Box

> [!NOTE]
> - **Availability (A):** Probability that a system is operational at a random instant of time. Expressed as a decimal or "nines" percentage.
> - **MTBF — Mean Time Between Failures:** Average operational uptime between two consecutive failure events.
> - **MTTR — Mean Time To Repair:** Average time required to detect, isolate, and restore a failed component.
> - **MTTF — Mean Time To Failure:** Average expected lifetime of a non-repairable component.
> - **RTO — Recovery Time Objective:** Maximum acceptable duration of an outage after a failure.
> - **RPO — Recovery Point Objective:** Maximum acceptable amount of data loss measured in time.
> - **SPOF — Single Point of Failure:** Any component whose failure brings down the entire system.
> - **SLA — Service Level Agreement:** A contractual commitment specifying the guaranteed availability level.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Always-On Hospital" Model

Imagine a **24×7 Emergency Hospital**:

| Hospital Element | Computing Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patients arriving any second | Incoming user requests (HTTP/gRPC/API calls) |
| On-duty doctors | Active production servers |
| On-call doctors (sleeping at home) | Standby / passive replicas |
| Backup generators | Redundant power supplies & UPS |
| Parallel road routes to the hospital | Multi-homed network paths (multi-AZ deployment) |
| Triage desk & ambulance dispatch | Load balancer & health-check probe |
| Regular drills & maintenance windows | Planned failover tests, rolling updates |

**What does the hospital do to stay "highly available"?**

1. It never depends on a *single* doctor — multiple doctors are scheduled on rotation.
2. It has a *backup* generator that automatically kicks in if main power fails.
3. It runs *fire drills* (testing) so the failover is rehearsed, not improvised.
4. It accepts a *small acceptable delay* (e.g., 5 minutes) for switching to backup — this is its **RTO**.

Now map the same reasoning to a distributed system. A highly available system is one that has **eliminated all single points of failure**, **automated failover** between redundant components, and **tested** its recovery procedure rigorously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Lock-In:** *High Availability ≠ Fault Tolerance.* Fault tolerance means the system produces *zero* observable disruption during a failure. High availability means the system produces a *quantified, bounded* disruption (defined by the RTO) but is overall operational for the promised percentage of time. Fault tolerance is a *stronger* and *more expensive* property.

---

## 1.3 The "Nines" — Industry-Standard Availability Tiers

The most universally cited metric in the KTU syllabus is the **"Nines" rating** of availability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Availability Classes (must memorize for KTU exams):**
> - **99%** → "Two Nines" → acceptable for internal tools
> - **99.9%** → "Three Nines" → typical SaaS applications
> - **99.99%** → "Four Nines" → e-commerce, banking-grade
> - **99.999%** → "Five Nines" → telecom, emergency services (carrier-grade)
> - **99.9999%** → "Six Nines" → theoretical maximum, Google-class infrastructure

The word **"nines"** refers to the count of the digit **9** appearing consecutively in the decimal representation of the availability percentage. This is *the* standard vocabulary used by AWS, Azure, GCP, telecom carriers, and KTU examiners.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Availability Equation

The mathematical foundation of every high-availability design begins with a single equation that connects *reliability* (how rarely things fail) with *recoverability* (how quickly things are fixed):

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this equation matters for KTU:** The KTU 2024 examiner frequently asks students to compute availability from MTBF and MTTR values, or inversely to compute permissible MTTR given a target availability tier. This is a **3-mark to 7-mark** favorite.

### 2.1.1 Logic Breakdown of Each Term

- **MTBF (numerator):** The longer the system runs between failures, the higher the availability. *Design implication →* invest in reliable components, mature software, and stress testing.
- **MTTR (denominator):** The faster we recover, the higher the availability. *Design implication →* automate failover, use hot standbys, and pre-provision replacements.
- **Limiting cases:**
  - If $\text{MTTR} \to 0$ (instant recovery), then $A \to 1$ (100% available).
  - If $\text{MTBF} \to 0$ (constant failure), then $A \to 0$ (never available).

### 2.1.2 Equivalent Form Using MTTF

For a non-repairable system (one that is replaced, not fixed), the equation becomes:

$$
A(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad \text{where} \quad \lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTTF}}
$$

This is the **exponential reliability function** for a constant failure rate $\lambda$. The probability of survival beyond time $t$ decays exponentially.

---

## 2.2 Downtime Budget Per Availability Class

The most practical engineering derivation is converting the *availability percentage* into *permissible downtime per unit time*. This is what an SLA actually enforces.

$$
D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \quad \text{(in minutes)}
$$

Similarly for monthly:

$$
D_{\text{monthly}} = (1 - A) \times 30 \times 24 \times 60
$$

And daily:

$$
D_{\text{daily}} = (1 - A) \times 24 \times 60
$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master cheat sheet** for any KTU numerical or design question on this topic. Memorize it. In examinations, replacing `|` with `\vert` ensures the markdown renders correctly.

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variable Definitions | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steady-state Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | MTBF in hours, MTTR in hours | Compute overall system uptime fraction |
| Annual Downtime | $D_a = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ | Result in minutes per year | SLA enforcement & capacity planning |
| Series Availability | $A_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | $A_i$ = availability of $i$-th component | Components that all must work (weakest link) |
| Parallel Availability (1+1 redundant) | $A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ | $A_i$ = availability of $i$-th replica | Components where at least one must work |
| Reliability Function | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ = constant failure rate, $t$ = mission time | Probability of no failure up to time $t$ |
| Mean Time To Failure | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = constant failure rate | For non-repairable components |
| Effective MTTR with Hot Standby | $\text{MTTR}_{\text{eff}} = \text{MTTR}_{\text{detect}} + \text{MTTR}_{\text{switch}}$ | Detection time + switchover time | Designing automated failover |
| CAP Trade-off Bound | $A_{\text{system}} \leq A_{\text{weakest\_link}}$ | For serial systems | Floor of total availability |

---

## 2.4 Core Design Issues (Why HA is "Hard")

The KTU 2024 module title is **"Design Issues: Ensuring High Availability"** — emphasis on the word *issues*. A perfect answer must enumerate the design challenges, not just the solutions.

### 2.4.1 Issue 1 — Single Point of Failure (SPOF) Elimination

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A SPOF is any element whose failure causes the entire system to fail. Classic SPOFs in naive designs: a single database server, a single load balancer, a single power supply, a single network link.

**Design Response:** Introduce *redundancy* at every tier — the N+1, 2N, or 2N+1 redundancy patterns.

### 2.4.2 Issue 2 — Detection Latency vs. False Positives

How do you know a node has failed? Heartbeats. But if heartbeats are too aggressive, you get **false positives** (node marked dead when it is merely slow — the "split-brain" problem). If too slow, MTTR increases. This is a fundamental detection-vs-accuracy trade-off.

### 2.4.3 Issue 3 — Data Consistency under Replication

To remain available during a node failure, you must replicate data. But replication forces a choice:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 1999):** A distributed data store can simultaneously provide **only two** of the following three guarantees:
> - **C** — Consistency (every read receives the most recent write)
> - **A** — Availability (every request receives a response)
> **P** — Partition tolerance (the system continues despite network message loss)
>
> Since network partitions are *inevitable* in real distributed systems, the practical trade-off is between **C and A**. HA-favoring systems choose AP (e.g., Cassandra, DynamoDB). Consistency-favoring systems choose CP (e.g., ZooKeeper, etcd).

### 2.4.4 Issue 4 — Cost vs. Availability Diminishing Returns

Moving from 99% (two nines) to 99.9% (three nines) is engineering-feasible with reasonable cost. Moving from 99.999% (five nines) to 99.9999% (six nines) typically requires *10× the infrastructure spend* for the same proportional improvement. The **cost curve is non-linear and convex**.

### 2.4.5 Issue 6 — Planned Downtime vs. Unplanned Downtime

Most real-world outages in mature systems are **planned** (deployments, patches, hardware swaps). A robust HA design must treat *rolling updates* and *blue-green deployments* as first-class operations that consume the downtime budget.

### 2.4.7 Issue 7 — Geographic Distribution & Disaster Recovery

A single data center is a *regional* SPOF (earthquake, flood, power grid failure). True HA requires **multi-region** or **multi-AZ** (Availability Zone) deployment, which introduces latency and consistency complications.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Cloud Platforms:** AWS, Azure, GCP advertise *regional* availability SLAs of 99.99% and *multi-region* SLAs of 99.999%.
- **Telecommunications:** Five-nines availability is the *legacy standard* (only ~5.26 minutes of downtime per year).
- **Financial Trading Systems:** Sub-second RTOs with zero RPO are engineered using *synchronous replication* across geographically separated data centers.
- **Embedded & Safety-Critical Systems (Aerospace, Medical):** Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) with majority voting achieves fault tolerance that approaches HA behavior.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Availability Equation to the "Nines" Table

**Problem:** Show the relationship between availability percentage and the maximum permissible annual downtime.

**Step 1 — State the Steady-State Availability Equation:**

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the Unavailability Fraction (the complement of availability):**

$$
U = 1 - A = 1 - \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine fractions over a common denominator:**

$$
U = \frac{(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}) - \text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the numerator (MTBF and -MTBF cancel):**

$$
U = \frac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

This is a beautiful result: **unavailability is dominated by MTTR**, not by MTBF. The faster you recover, the closer you get to perfect availability.

**Step 5 — Compute Total Minutes in a Year:**

$$
T_{\text{year}} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply unavailability fraction by total minutes to get the permissible annual downtime:**

$$
D_{\text{annual}} = U \times T_{\text{year}} = \frac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 525{,}600
$$

**Step 7 — Tabulate for standard availability tiers:**

Let us evaluate the annual downtime $D_{\text{annual}}$ for each tier by substituting $A$ and computing $(1-A) \times 525{,}600$.

For **two nines** ($A = 0.99$):

$$
D_a = (1 - 0.99) \times 525{,}600 = 0.01 \times 525{,}600 = 5{,}256 \text{ minutes}
$$

For **three nines** ($A = 0.999$):

$$
D_a = 0.001 \times 525{,}600 = 525.6 \text{ minutes}
$$

For **four nines** ($A = 0.9999$):

$$
D_a = 0.0001 \times 525{,}600 = 52.56 \text{ minutes}
$$

For **five nines** ($A = 0.99999$):

$$
D_a = 0.00001 \times 525{,}600 = 5.256 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 8 — Convert to a more human-readable form for the five-nines case:**

$$
5.256 \text{ minutes} = 5 \text{ minutes} + 0.256 \times 60 \text{ seconds} \approx 5 \text{ minutes } 15 \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Five nines corresponds to roughly *5 minutes and 15 seconds of permissible downtime per year*. This is the entire *allowed* outage budget for an entire year — every second matters.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Series System Availability (The Weakest-Link Rule)

**Problem:** A web application consists of three serial components: a load balancer with availability $A_1 = 0.99$, an application server with $A_2 = 0.999$, and a database with $A_3 = 0.9999$. Find the overall system availability.

**Step 1 — Recognize the Series Dependency:** In a series system, *all* components must work for the system to work. If *any* component fails, the entire system fails.

**Step 2 — Write the Series Availability Product Formula:**

$$
A_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i = A_1 \times A_2 \times A_3
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the given values:**

$$
A_{\text{series}} = 0.99 \times 0.999 \times 0.9999
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the product step by step (left to right):**

$$
0.99 \times 0.999 = 0.98901
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by the third factor:**

$$
0.98901 \times 0.9999 = 0.98901 - 0.98901 \times 0.0001
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the subtracted term:**

$$
0.98901 \times 0.0001 = 0.000098901
$$

**Step 7 — Subtract to get the final answer:**

$$
A_{\text{series}} = 0.98901 - 0.000098901 = 0.988911099
$$

**Step 8 — Convert to a "nines" classification for context:**

$$
A_{\text{series}} \approx 0.9889 = 98.89\% \approx \text{"Two Nines"}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The *most reliable single component* (the database at four nines) was *dragged down* to two nines by the *least reliable component* (the load balancer at two nines). This is the **weakest-link effect**. In KTU exam answers, the punchline is: *"Improving a single weak component yields the highest availability ROI."*

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Parallel (1+1) Redundant System Availability

**Problem:** Two identical web servers, each with availability $A_i = 0.9$, are deployed in parallel with a load-balancer-based failover. Compute the combined availability.

**Step 1 — Identify the Redundancy Topology:** A parallel system remains operational as long as *at least one* component is working. Both must fail simultaneously for the system to go down.

**Step 2 — Compute the Unavailability of a Single Component:**

$$
U_i = 1 - A_i = 1 - 0.9 = 0.1
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Probability That Both Components Fail Simultaneously (Independence Assumption):**

$$
U_{\text{parallel}} = U_1 \times U_2 = 0.1 \times 0.1 = 0.01
$$

**Step 4 — Convert Back to Availability by Taking the Complement:**

$$
A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - U_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99
$$

**Step 5 — Generalize to the n-Component Parallel Formula:**

$$
A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - A_i)
$$

**Step 6 — Verify the Generalization:**

$$
A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)\cdots(1 - A_n)
$$

Substituting $A_1 = A_2 = 0.9$ and $n = 2$:

$$
A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (0.1)(0.1) = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99
$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the Result:**

> [!NOTE]
> By adding *one extra* redundant server of equal quality, we have improved availability from **90%** to **99%** — an order-of-magnitude improvement. This is the mathematical justification for why redundancy is the cornerstone of HA design.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Inverse Problem: MTTR Required for a Target Availability

**Problem:** A system has MTBF = 8760 hours (one year of operation). What is the maximum allowable MTTR to achieve four nines (99.99%) availability?

**Step 1 — Start from the Availability Equation:**

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve Algebraically for MTTR (the unknown). Rearrange:**

$$
A \times (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}) = \text{MTBF}
$$

**Step 3 — Distribute A on the left-hand side:**

$$
A \cdot \text{MTBF} + A \cdot \text{MTTR} = \text{MTBF}
$$

**Step 4 — Isolate the MTTR term by subtracting $A \cdot \text{MTBF}$ from both sides:**

$$
A \cdot \text{MTTR} = \text{MTBF} - A \cdot \text{MTBF}
$$

**Step 5 — Factor out MTBF on the right-hand side:**

$$
A \cdot \text{MTTR} = \text{MTBF} \cdot (1 - A)
$$

**Step 6 — Divide both sides by $A$ to solve for MTTR:**

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{MTBF} \cdot (1 - A)}{A}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the Numerical Values:**

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{8760 \times (1 - 0.9999)}{0.9999} = \frac{8760 \times 0.0001}{0.9999}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the Numerator:**

$$
8760 \times 0.0001 = 0.876 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 9 — Divide by the Denominator:**

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{0.876}{0.9999} \approx 0.8761 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 10 — Convert to More Intuitive Units:**

$$
0.8761 \text{ hours} \times 60 \text{ min/hr} \approx 52.56 \text{ minutes}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** For a system with one-year MTBF, achieving four nines requires the failure-recovery process to complete in **less than 53 minutes** — every single time. This is a tight operational constraint and is why real HA systems use *hot standbys* with sub-second automated failover.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — HA Design Patterns

| Design Pattern | Availability Impact | Cost Impact | Latency Impact | Consistency Impact | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Active-Passive (Hot Standby)** | High (sub-second failover) | 2× infrastructure | Negligible | Strong (synchronous) | Databases, stateful services |
| **Active-Active (Load Balanced)** | Very high (no failover delay) | $\geq$ 2× infrastructure | Negligible | Eventual (async replication) | Stateless web tier, CDNs |
| **N+1 Redundancy** | Moderate | $(N+1)/N$ cost multiplier | Low | Strong | General enterprise |
| **Geographic Multi-Region** | Highest (disaster-grade) | 3× to 5× cost | High (cross-region RTT) | Weak (async) | Mission-critical SaaS |
| **Triple Modular Redundancy + Voting** | Approaching fault tolerance | 3× cost + voter logic | Low | N/A | Aerospace, safety-critical |
| **Rolling Upgrade** | Preserves availability during deploys | No extra hardware | Low | Per-shard | Any production system |
| **Circuit Breaker Pattern** | Prevents cascade failures | No extra hardware | Adds monitoring overhead | N/A | Microservices chains |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Active-Passive (Hot Standby) HA Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ClientA[Client Request 1] --> LB1
    ClientB[Client Request 2] --> LB1
    ClientC[Client Request 3] --> LB1
    LB1[Load Balancer Tier] --> Node1
    LB1 --> Node2
    Node1[Active Server Node 1 - Primary] --> HeartBeat
    Node2[Passive Server Node 2 - Standby] --> HeartBeat
    HeartBeat[Heartbeat and Health Probe Channel] --> Node1
    HeartBeat --> Node2
    Node1 --> DataLayer
    Node2 --> DataLayer
    DataLayer[Shared or Replicated Data Layer] --> MonitorSvc
    MonitorSvc[Monitoring and Alerting Service] --> Admin
    Admin[On call SRE Engineer]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The load balancer dispatches traffic only to **Node 1** under normal conditions.
- **Node 2** remains in *hot standby* — its state is kept in sync via the heartbeat channel.
- The **monitoring service** continuously polls both nodes; on detecting Node 1 failure, it triggers a *promotion* of Node 2 to active.
- The **on-call SRE** receives alerts but is not in the critical failover path.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Active-Active (Stateless Service) HA Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Internet[Public Internet Traffic] --> EdgeLB
    EdgeLB[Global Edge Load Balancer Anycast or GeoDNS] --> RegionA
    EdgeLB --> RegionB
    RegionA[Region A Cluster] --> AppA1
    RegionA --> AppA2
    RegionA --> CacheA
    RegionB[Region B Cluster] --> AppB1
    RegionB --> AppB2
    RegionB --> CacheB
    AppA1[App Server Instance A1] --> AsyncQueue
    AppA2[App Server Instance A2] --> AsyncQueue
    AppB1[App Server Instance B1] --> AsyncQueue
    AppB2[App Server Instance B2] --> AsyncQueue
    AsyncQueue[Async Replication and Event Bus] --> CacheA
    AsyncQueue --> CacheB
    CacheA[Local Distributed Cache]
    CacheB[Local Distributed Cache]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- All four app-server instances accept traffic simultaneously.
- The **global edge load balancer** uses GeoDNS or anycast to direct users to the nearest healthy region.
- State is synchronized across regions through an **asynchronous event bus** — note the *eventual consistency* trade-off (CAP-AP choice).

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Failure Detection and Failover State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Healthy
    Healthy --> Suspect: Heartbeat timeout 1
    Suspect --> Healthy: Heartbeat recovered
    Suspect --> Degraded: Heartbeat timeout 2
    Degraded --> Failed: Quorum loss confirmed
    Failed --> Recovering: Standby promotion initiated
    Recovering --> Healthy: New primary verified by quorum
    Failed --> [*]: RTO exceeded, manual intervention
    Healthy --> PlannedMaintenance: Operator initiated
    PlannedMaintenance --> Healthy: Rolling upgrade complete
```

**Reading the state machine:**
- The system moves through **five operational states** based on heartbeat signals.
- A *single* missed heartbeat moves the node to "Suspect" (avoiding false positives).
- A *second consecutive* missed heartbeat moves it to "Degraded" (warning state).
- Only after *quorum confirmation* of failure does it transition to "Failed" and trigger standby promotion.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Hierarchical HA Stack (Defense-in-Depth)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Top[User Request Enters System] --> L1
    L1[Layer 1 - Geographic Redundancy Multi-Region] --> L2
    L2[Layer 2 - Network Path Diversity Multi ISP and Multi AZ] --> L3
    L3[Layer 3 - Edge Load Balancing DNS and Anycast] --> L4
    L4[Layer 4 - Application Tier Active Active Cluster] --> L5
    L5[Layer 5 - Stateful Service Tier Active Passive with Hot Standby] --> L6
    L6[Layer 6 - Data Tier Synchronous Replication with Quorum Write] --> L7
    L7[Layer 7 - Power and Physical Infrastructure UPS plus Generator plus Dual Utility Feeds]
    L1 -. Removes .-> R1
    R1[Region level disaster]
    L2 -. Removes .-> R2
    R2[ISP or single AZ failure]
    L3 -. Removes .-> R3
    R3[Load balancer failure]
    L4 -. Removes .-> R4
    R4[Application instance crash]
    L5 -. Removes .-> R5
    R5[Stateful service crash]
    L6 -. Removes .-> R6
    R6[Data corruption or single replica loss]
    L7 -. Removes .-> R7
    R7[Power outage or cooling failure]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each layer of the stack is designed to *remove a specific class of failure* (shown as dashed arrows to the right).
- This is the **defense-in-depth** principle: no single layer is trusted to handle all failure modes.
- A KTU answer that maps HA techniques to *specific failure categories* scores significantly higher than one that lists techniques without justification.
