# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q: Define High Availability in the context of distributed systems. Differentiate between Active-Active and Active-Passive redundancy models with one example use case for each.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** High Availability (HA) is a design discipline that ensures a computing system remains operationally accessible for a guaranteed percentage of time (e.g., 99.99%) by eliminating single points of failure through redundancy and automated failover.
>
> **Active-Passive (1 mark):** One node serves all traffic (active); the other is a *hot standby* that remains idle but state-synchronized. On active-node failure, the passive node is promoted. *Example:* Primary-replica database cluster (e.g., PostgreSQL streaming replication).
>
> **Active-Active (1 mark):** Both nodes serve traffic simultaneously, sharing the load. On one node's failure, the other absorbs its full load. *Example:* Stateless web servers behind a load balancer (e.g., Nginx + two Apache instances).

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q: What is the significance of MTBF and MTTR in high-availability design? A system has MTBF = 5000 hours and MTTR = 1 hour. Compute its availability and state the equivalent "nines" classification.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance (1 mark):** MTBF measures *reliability* (how rarely failures occur), while MTTR measures *recoverability* (how quickly service is restored). Together they determine the steady-state availability: $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$.
>
> **Computation (1 mark):** Substituting the values,
> $$
> A = \frac{5000}{5000 + 1} = \frac{5000}{5001} \approx 0.9998
> $$
>
> **Nines classification (1 mark):** $0.9998 = 99.98\%$, which lies between **three nines (99.9%)** and **four nines (99.99%)**, closer to the four-nines tier.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

> **Q (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the Active-Passive high availability architecture. Discuss the role of the heartbeat mechanism and the danger of *split-brain syndrome*. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** A distributed system consists of three serial components with individual availabilities $A_1 = 0.95$, $A_2 = 0.98$, and $A_3 = 0.99$. (i) Calculate the overall system availability. (ii) If the weakest component is replaced by a parallel pair of identical components (each with the same availability as the original weakest), recalculate the new overall system availability. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Active-Passive Architecture (2 marks):**

In an Active-Passive HA topology, one node (the *primary*) handles all incoming traffic, while a second node (the *standby* or *passive* replica) remains idle but continuously synchronized with the primary. A load balancer or virtual IP (VIP) abstraction directs all requests to the primary. The passive node only begins serving traffic if the active node fails or is administratively demoted.

**Step 2 — Role of Heartbeat (2 marks):**

A **heartbeat** is a periodic health-check signal exchanged between the active and passive nodes (typically every 1–10 seconds). Its functions are:
- Confirm the active node is alive and responsive at network, process, and application levels.
- Trigger a *failover* decision when heartbeats are missed consecutively beyond a configured threshold.
- Convey the *latest replicated state* checkpoint to ensure the passive node is promotion-ready.

**Step 3 — Split-Brain Syndrome (2 marks):**

> [!WARNING]
> **Split-brain** is a dangerous failure mode in which *both* nodes believe they are the active primary — typically caused by a network partition that prevents the nodes from exchanging heartbeats. Both nodes then accept writes, leading to **data divergence, corruption, and split ownership** of the virtual IP. The standard mitigation is a **quorum-based decision** (only the node that retains majority connectivity to witness nodes is allowed to remain primary), implemented via fencing tokens or STONITH (Shoot The Other Node In The Head).

**Step 4 — Diagram Reference (1 mark):**

Refer to the *Block Diagram 4.1 — Active-Passive HA Topology* in Section 4 of these notes.

---

#### Model Solution for Q (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify the Weakest Component (1 mark):**

Comparing the three availabilities: $A_1 = 0.95$, $A_2 = 0.98$, $A_3 = 0.99$. The *weakest link* is **$A_1 = 0.95$** (the lowest availability).

**Step 2 — Compute Original Series Availability (2 marks):**

$$
A_{\text{original}} = A_1 \times A_2 \times A_3 = 0.95 \times 0.98 \times 0.99
$$

Computing step by step:

$$
0.95 \times 0.98 = 0.931
$$

$$
0.931 \times 0.99 = 0.92169
$$

Therefore, $A_{\text{original}} = 0.92169$ (≈ 92.17%, slightly above two nines).

**Step 3 — Compute the Parallel Availability of the Replacement (1.5 marks):**

Replace the single weakest component (0.95) with two parallel components (each 0.95). The parallel availability formula gives:

$$
A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - 0.95)(1 - 0.95) = 1 - (0.05)(0.05) = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the New Series Availability (1.5 marks):**

$$
A_{\text{new}} = A_{\text{parallel}} \times A_2 \times A_3 = 0.9975 \times 0.98 \times 0.99
$$

Computing the rightmost product first:

$$
0.98 \times 0.99 = 0.9702
$$

Multiplying by 0.9975:

$$
0.9702 \times 0.9975 = 0.9702 - 0.9702 \times 0.0025
$$

$$
0.9702 \times 0.0025 = 0.0024255
$$

$$
A_{\text{new}} = 0.9702 - 0.0024255 = 0.9677745
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on the Result (1 mark):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comment:** Availability improved from **0.9217 (92.17%)** to **0.9678 (96.78%)** — a gain of approximately **4.6 percentage points** by redundantly duplicating only *one* of three components. This demonstrates the **diminishing-returns behavior** of redundancy when applied selectively, and the **strongest ROI** of removing the weakest link.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

> **Q (a)** Explain the CAP theorem with respect to high-availability design. Why is partition tolerance a non-negotiable property in real distributed systems, and what design choices does this force? Give one example each of a CP system and an AP system. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b)** A system must achieve an annual availability of 99.95% (between three and four nines). The current MTBF is measured at 30,000 hours. Calculate: (i) The maximum permissible annual downtime in minutes. (ii) The required MTTR to meet the SLA. (iii) If the team can reduce MTTR to half the calculated value, what is the new availability? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — CAP Theorem Statement (2 marks):**

The CAP theorem, formulated by Eric Brewer (1999) and later proven by Gilbert and Lynch (2002), states that a distributed data store can simultaneously provide **only two** out of the following three guarantees:
- **C** — **Consistency:** Every read receives the most recent committed write (linearizability).
- **A** — **Availability:** Every request (read or write) to a non-failing node receives a non-error response.
- **P** — **Partition tolerance:** The system continues to operate despite arbitrary network message loss between nodes.

**Step 2 — Why Partition Tolerance is Non-Negotiable (2 marks):**

In any real distributed system that spans multiple machines, network links, switches, or geographic regions, **network partitions are inevitable**. They occur due to cable cuts, switch failures, congestion, BGP route flaps, or transient routing loops. Since the network *will* partition, the designer cannot opt out of P. Therefore, the *real* design choice is between **C and A** during a partition — the famous "CP vs. AP" trade-off.

**Step 3 — CP System Example (1 mark):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Apache ZooKeeper (or etcd, or HBase):** During a network partition, ZooKeeper's leader-electing nodes will *stop accepting writes* if they lose quorum. They prefer returning an error (unavailable) over serving stale data. This preserves strong consistency at the cost of write availability.

**Step 4 — AP System Example (1 mark):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Amazon DynamoDB (or Cassandra, Riak):** During a partition, DynamoDB continues to accept both reads and writes on all reachable nodes, using vector clocks or last-writer-wins semantics. This preserves availability at the cost of returning potentially stale or conflicting data that must be resolved later (eventual consistency).

**Step 5 — HA Design Implication (1 mark):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An HA-focused designer therefore *chooses AP* by default — sacrificing strong consistency for continuous service. Consistency is later recovered through background anti-entropy, read repair, or application-level conflict resolution. This is the architectural foundation of all modern cloud-native HA databases.

---

#### Model Solution for Q (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute Maximum Permissible Annual Downtime (2 marks):**

Given target availability $A_{\text{target}} = 0.9995$:

$$
D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - A_{\text{target}}) \times 525{,}600
$$

$$
D_{\text{annual}} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 525{,}600 = 0.0005 \times 525{,}600
$$

$$
D_{\text{annual}} = 262.8 \text{ minutes per year}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Required MTTR (2.5 marks):**

Starting from the availability equation:

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

Rearrange to isolate MTTR:

$$
\text{MTTR} = \text{MTBF} \times \frac{1 - A}{A}
$$

Substitute $\text{MTBF} = 30{,}000$ hours and $A = 0.9995$:

$$
\text{MTTR} = 30{,}000 \times \frac{1 - 0.9995}{0.9995} = 30{,}000 \times \frac{0.0005}{0.9995}
$$

Compute the fraction:

$$
\frac{0.0005}{0.9995} \approx 0.00050025
$$

Multiply:

$$
\text{MTTR} = 30{,}000 \times 0.00050025 \approx 15.0075 \text{ hours}
$$

Convert to minutes for intuition:

$$
15.0075 \text{ hours} \times 60 \text{ min/hr} \approx 900.45 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute New Availability with Halved MTTR (2 marks):**

If the team reduces MTTR to half:

$$
\text{MTTR}_{\text{new}} = \frac{15.0075}{2} = 7.50375 \text{ hours}
$$

Substitute into the availability formula:

$$
A_{\text{new}} = \frac{30{,}000}{30{,}000 + 7.50375} = \frac{30{,}000}{30{,}007.50375}
$$

$$
A_{\text{new}} \approx 0.99975
$$

**Step 4 — Final Comment (0.5 mark):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Halving the MTTR from 15 hours to 7.5 hours improved availability from 99.95% to 99.975% — pushing the system from "between three and four nines" into the **four-nines tier**. This illustrates the **leverage of fast recovery** on the overall SLA.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in HA Questions:**
> 1. **Writing definitions without numbers:** A KTU answer on HA *must* include at least one numerical computation (MTBF/MTTR → availability, or downtime budget for a nines target). A purely qualitative answer caps your score at 60% of marks.
> 2. **Confusing MTTF with MTBF:** MTTF applies to *non-repairable* components; MTBF applies to *repairable* systems. Examiners test this distinction explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting the series "weakest link" effect:** A common mistake is to *add* component availabilities instead of *multiplying* them. Always use $A_{\text{series}} = \prod A_i$.
> 4. **Omitting the CAP trade-off:** When asked about HA design, students often list techniques (redundancy, replication, failover) but omit the **fundamental CAP trade-off** that constrains the design space. Always mention CAP if the question is worth 7+ marks.
> 5. **Ignoring cost:** A 14-mark answer that does not even mention the *cost vs. availability* trade-off will be marked incomplete by a strict KTU examiner.
> 6. **Not drawing a block diagram:** A neat, labeled block diagram is worth 2–3 marks by itself in Part B answers. Skipping it is a guaranteed deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this in the last 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **Core formula:** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ — memorize, derive, and know its inverse form.
- **Five standard tiers:** 99%, 99.9%, 99.99%, 99.999%, 99.9999% — with annual downtimes 5256 min, 525.6 min, 52.56 min, 5.26 min, 0.526 min respectively.
- **Series system:** $A_{\text{series}} = \prod A_i$ — total availability is *limited by the weakest link*.
- **Parallel system:** $A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod (1 - A_i)$ — redundancy improves availability multiplicatively (in log space).
- **CAP theorem:** Real systems must be either **CP** (consistent, may become unavailable) or **AP** (available, may return stale data). HA systems default to **AP**.
- **Redundancy patterns:** Active-Passive (hot standby, strong consistency, 2× cost) vs. Active-Active (no failover delay, eventual consistency, ≥ 2× cost).
- **Failure modes to defend against:** Hardware crash, software bug, network partition, data center disaster, planned maintenance, human operator error, security breach.
- **Defense-in-depth layers:** Geographic, network, load balancing, application, stateful service, data, physical infrastructure.
- **Split-brain prevention:** Quorum-based decisions + fencing tokens + STONITH mechanism.
- **RTO / RPO:** Recovery Time Objective (downtime tolerance) and Recovery Point Objective (data-loss tolerance) are the *user-facing* HA metrics.
- **Cost principle:** The cost of availability grows **non-linearly and convexly** — five nines costs roughly 10× what three nines costs.
- **CAP is the *meta-constraint*;** everything else (redundancy, replication, failover) is an *engineering response* to that constraint.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 — Design Issues: Ensuring High Availability.**
> A complete, well-structured answer in the KTU exam should: (1) define HA, (2) state the formula, (3) compute at least one numerical example, (4) draw a labeled block diagram, (5) discuss at least one design issue (CAP, cost, SPOF, split-brain, or planned downtime), and (6) conclude with the practical engineering trade-off. Six elements = full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cluster Families and Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cluster Families and Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Computer Cluster?

A **Computer Cluster** is a set of loosely or tightly connected computers (compute nodes) that work together so closely that, in many respects, they can be viewed as a **single integrated computing resource**. The connected computers execute tasks cooperatively and communicate using a high-speed network interconnect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> A cluster is a type of parallel/distributed system that consists of a collection of interconnected standalone computers working together as a single, integrated computing resource. It typically leverages commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) hardware, a high-throughput network, and specialized middleware to deliver high performance, high availability, or load balancing.

### 🔧 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **busy restaurant kitchen during a dinner rush**:
- A single chef (single server) is overwhelmed → service is slow.
- A **team of chefs** (cluster of nodes) is assigned different stations (compute tasks), but they all share a common **pass-through window** (the network interconnect) and a common **pantry** (shared storage).
- The **head chef** (cluster middleware / scheduler) coordinates who cooks what, ensuring no chef is idle while others are overworked (**load balancing**).
- If one chef faints, another immediately takes over the dish (**high availability/fault tolerance**).

This is exactly how a cluster works: **multiple independent machines coordinated by middleware to appear as one powerful, reliable system**.

## 1.2 Why Clusters? The Motivation

| Bottleneck in Single Systems | Cluster Solution |
|---|---|
| Limited CPU/Memory ceiling | Aggregate multiple nodes' resources |
| Single point of failure | Redundancy via HA mechanisms |
| Sequential processing limits | Parallel job distribution (e.g., MPI) |
| Cost of supercomputers | Cost-effective COTS components |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** Clusters dominate modern HPC (Top500 list shows > 80% of supercomputers are clusters) because they offer **supercomputer-class performance at workstation-class cost**.

## 1.3 Cluster Families — The Major Classifications

Cluster families are typically classified along **three axes**:

### A. By Function / Purpose
1. **High-Performance Computing (HPC) Clusters** — Maximize compute throughput for scientific simulations.
2. **High-Availability (HA) Clusters** — Maximize uptime via redundancy (e.g., banking servers).
3. **Load-Balancing Clusters** — Distribute incoming client requests evenly across nodes.
4. **Grid Clusters** — Geographically distributed, multi-organizational collaboration.

### B. By Node Configuration
- **Homogeneous Clusters** — All nodes identical (preferred for predictability).
- **Heterogeneous Clusters** — Nodes vary in CPU, memory, OS (useful for legacy integration).

### C. By Architectural Topology
- **Centralized Cluster** — Master/slave (or head/worker) model.
- **Decentralized/Distributed Cluster** — Peer-to-peer coordination.
- **Hybrid Cluster** — Combines both.

> [!TIP]
> **Remember the acronym "HHL": HPC, HA, Load-balancing** — these are the three families most tested in KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Architecture of a Cluster

Every cluster — regardless of family — is built from **five fundamental layers** (from bottom to top):

### Layer 1: Cluster Hardware
- **Compute Nodes** (2 to 10,000+ commodity PCs/workstations/servers)
- **Network Interface Cards (NICs)** for each node
- **Interconnect Switches** (Gigabit Ethernet, InfiniBand, Myrinet)
- **Cluster Storage** (NFS, SAN, parallel file systems like Lustre, GPFS)

### Layer 2: Cluster Operating Systems
- **Linux distributions** dominate (CentOS, Rocky Linux, Ubuntu Server)
- Each node runs its own copy; homogeneity simplifies management
- Some specialized cluster OSes: **MOSIX, OpenSSI, Kerrighed** (research-grade)

### Layer 3: Cluster Middleware (The "Glue")
This is the heart of the cluster. It performs three critical roles:
1. **Single System Image (SSI)** — Hides distribution from users
2. **System Management** — Configuration, monitoring, provisioning
3. **High-Availability Infrastructure** — Heartbeat, failover, checkpoint/restart

### Layer 4: Programming Environments
- **Message Passing Interface (MPI)** — De-facto standard (OpenMPI, MPICH)
- **Parallel Virtual Machine (PVM)** — Older, still used
- **OpenMP** — Shared-memory threading (within a node)
- **Job Schedulers** — SLURM, PBS, Torque, LSF

### Layer 5: Cluster Applications
- Scientific computing (CFD, Genomics, Weather)
- Big Data (Hadoop/Spark — a specialized cluster framework)
- Web serving, database replication, AI training

## 2.2 Detailed Cluster Family Comparison

| Feature | HPC Cluster | HA Cluster | Load-Balancing Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Goal** | Maximum FLOPS | Maximum uptime | Maximum throughput |
| **Typical Nodes** | 100s to 100,000s | 2 to 16 | 2 to 100s |
| **Interconnect** | InfiniBand / Omni-Path | Gigabit Ethernet | Gigabit / 10G Ethernet |
| **Middleware** | SLURM + MPI | Pacemaker + Corosync | HAProxy / LVS / NGINX |
| **Failover Behavior** | Job migration | Service migration | Request rerouting |
| **Redundancy Level** | N+1 / N+M | Active-Passive / Active-Active | Active-Active |
| **Cost** | Very High | Medium | Low–Medium |
| **Example** | Frontier (ORNL) | Bank DB servers | Google front-end |

## 2.3 The Beowulf Architecture (Most Tested Sub-Topic)

The **Beowulf cluster** is a specific, famous class of HPC cluster designed in **1994** at NASA (Sterling & Becker). It is essentially the **blueprint for modern commodity clusters**.

### Beowulf Structural Rules:
- **One Master Node** (controls the cluster, distributes jobs)
- **Multiple Slave/Worker Nodes** (do the actual computation)
- **Private Network** (typically a dedicated Ethernet or Myrinet switch)
- **COTS components only** — no specialized hardware
- **Open-source software** (Linux, MPI, PBS)
- The master node is **the only node visible to the outside world**; slaves are hidden

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** Beowulf = "COTS + Linux + MPI + dedicated private network + master/worker topology"

### Beowulf vs NOW (Network of Workstations)

| Aspect | Beowulf | NOW |
|---|---|---|
| **Ownership** | Dedicated to cluster | User desktops, idle cycles |
| **Availability** | Always available | Opportunistic (only when idle) |
| **Management** | Centralized | Decentralized |
| **Performance Predictability** | High | Low (unpredictable loads) |

## 2.4 Single System Image (SSI) — The Key Abstraction

SSI is what makes a cluster **look like one machine** to the user. It has four sub-components:

1. **Single Entry Point** — One access point to submit jobs
2. **Single File Hierarchy** — One root directory spanning nodes (e.g., via NFS)
3. **Single Process Space** — A global PID across nodes
4. **Single I/O Space** — Unified device and storage view

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **SSI** with a true **SMP (Symmetric Multiprocessor)**. SSI is a *software illusion* over distributed hardware; SMP is *true shared-memory hardware*. The cluster physically is **not** a single computer.

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Definition / Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Cluster Speedup (ideal)** | $$S_p = \frac{T_1}{T_p}$$ | Ratio of sequential time to parallel time on $p$ nodes |
| **Amdahl's Law** | $$S_p = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}$$ | $f$ = serial fraction, $p$ = number of nodes |
| **Efficiency** | $$E_p = \frac{S_p}{p} = \frac{T_1}{p \cdot T_p}$$ | Expressed as a decimal (0 ≤ E ≤ 1) |
| **Cluster Availability** | $$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$ | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair |
| **HPC Throughput** | $$R = \frac{N_{\text{tasks}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$$ | Tasks completed per unit time |
| **Network Bandwidth** | $$B = \frac{D}{T}$$ | $D$ = data transferred, $T$ = time, units = bits/s |
| **Communication Overhead** | $$T_{\text{comm}} = t_s + t_w \cdot m$$ | $t_s$ = startup time, $t_w$ = per-word time, $m$ = message size |
| **FLOPS** | $$\text{FLOPS} = N_{\text{cores}} \times f_{\text{CPU}} \times N_{\text{IPC}}$$ | Floating-point operations per second |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipe-Symbol Rule Applied:** I have used `\mid` and `\vert` for any absolute-value notation in table cells, per protocol. For example, $S_p = \frac{T_1}{T_p}$ is safe; if you need $\vert x \vert$ use $\vert x \vert$ not $\vert x \vert$ (raw).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Weather Forecasting (ECMWF):** Runs on HPC clusters predicting atmospheric behavior.
- **Genomic Sequencing (BLAST):** Distributes sequence alignment across HA clusters.
- **Aerospace Simulation:** Boeing uses clusters to model airflow over aircraft wings (CFD).
- **Banking:** Reserve Bank of India uses HA clusters for 24/7 transaction processing.
- **AI Training:** OpenAI, Google DeepMind train large language models on GPU clusters of 10,000+ nodes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Amdahl's Law Applied to a Cluster

This is a **high-yield KTU derivation** that appears frequently in Part B questions.

### Problem Statement:
A cluster runs a program. **5% of the program is inherently sequential** (must run on one node). The remaining **95% is perfectly parallelizable** across the cluster's nodes. Calculate the maximum speedup achievable on an 8-node cluster and the parallel efficiency.

### Given:
- Serial fraction: $f = 0.05$
- Parallel fraction: $1 - f = 0.95$
- Number of nodes: $p = 8$

### Step 1: Amdahl's Law Expression
The speedup formula is:

$$
S_p = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Values

$$
S_p = \frac{1}{0.05 + \frac{0.95}{8}}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Denominator

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{0.95}{8} &= 0.11875 \\
f + \frac{1-f}{p} &= 0.05 + 0.11875 = 0.16875
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Final Speedup Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_8 &= \frac{1}{0.16875} \\
S_8 &\approx 5.926
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Efficiency Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_8 &= \frac{S_8}{p} \\
E_8 &= \frac{5.926}{8} \\
E_8 &\approx 0.7408 \text{ or } 74.08\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Interpretation (for model answer)

Even with **8 nodes**, we only achieve a **5.93× speedup**, not the theoretical **8×**. This is because the sequential 5% portion becomes the bottleneck — it limits total speedup regardless of node count.

### Step 7: The Limiting Case (∞ nodes)

$$
S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20
$$

**So with infinite nodes, we cap at 20× speedup.** This is Amdahl's pessimistic law.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Cluster Families

| Criterion | HPC Cluster | HA Cluster | Load-Balancing Cluster | Grid Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Optimizes for** | Speed | Uptime | Throughput | Resource sharing |
| **Node coupling** | Tight (MPI) | Tight (heartbeat) | Loose (TCP/HTTP) | Very loose (Internet) |
| **Storage model** | Parallel FS (Lustre) | Replicated SAN | Shared nothing or NFS | Distributed (HDFS-like) |
| **Failure tolerance** | Job restart | Hot-standby failover | Automatic rerouting | Best-effort |
| **Geographic span** | Single datacenter | Single datacenter | Single datacenter | Multi-continent |
| **Programming model** | MPI/OpenMP | Active/passive scripts | Round-robin DNS | Globus toolkit / gLite |
| **Cost per node** | High (CPU/RAM) | Medium (replication) | Low (commodity) | Variable |
| **Sample software stack** | SLURM + OpenMPI | Pacemaker + DRBD | LVS + IPVS | Globus + Condor-G |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Availability of an HA Cluster

### Problem:
An HA cluster has **MTBF = 5000 hours** and **MTTR = 4 hours**.
- (a) Calculate the availability.
- (b) If we add a hot-standby node (redundancy), how does the availability change?
- (c) What is the maximum allowable MTTR to achieve 99.99% availability (Four Nines)?

### Solution:

**Part (a): Basic Availability**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \\
A &= \frac{5000}{5000 + 4} \\
A &= \frac{5000}{5004} \\
A &\approx 0.99920 \text{ or } 99.920\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b): With Hot Standby**
With redundant nodes, effective MTTR drops dramatically (failover takes ~5 seconds = 0.00139 hours). New availability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{HA}} &= \frac{5000}{5000 + 0.00139} \\
A_{\text{HA}} &\approx 0.9999997 \text{ or } 99.99997\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c): Solving for MTTR at 99.99%**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.9999 &= \frac{5000}{5000 + MTTR} \\
0.9999 \times (5000 + MTTR) &= 5000 \\
4999.5 + 0.9999 \cdot MTTR &= 5000 \\
0.9999 \cdot MTTR &= 0.5 \\
MTTR &= \frac{0.5}{0.9999} \\
MTTR &\approx 0.5001 \text{ hours} \approx 30 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** "Four Nines" (99.99%) corresponds to about **52 minutes of downtime per year**. This is a famous industry benchmark for HA systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📒 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Cluster Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client[Client User or Job Submitter]
    master[Master Node: Scheduler and Front End]
    sched[Job Scheduler: SLURM or PBS]
    ssi[Single System Image SSI Layer]
    fs[Shared File System: NFS or Lustre]
    net[High Speed Interconnect Switch]
    n1[Worker Node 1]
    n2[Worker Node 2]
    n3[Worker Node 3]
    n4[Worker Node N]
    storage[Cluster Storage: SAN or Parallel FS]
    mon[Monitoring and Heartbeat Service]

    client --> master
    master --> sched
    master --> ssi
    sched --> net
    ssi --> fs
    net --> n1
    net --> n2
    net --> n3
    net --> n4
    fs --> storage
    mon -.-> n1
    mon -.-> n2
    mon -.-> n3
    mon -.-> n4
    mon -.-> master
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram maps the **functional flow** of job submission → scheduling → distribution → execution → storage → monitoring. Note the dashed lines from "Monitoring" indicating **out-of-band heartbeats** (a critical HA mechanism).

## 4.2 Cluster Family Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clusters[Computer Cluster Families]

    clusters --> byfunc[By Function]
    clusters --> byarch[By Architecture]
    clusters --> bynode[By Node Type]

    byfunc --> hpc[HPC Cluster: Scientific Simulation]
    byfunc --> ha[HA Cluster: Banking and Telecom]
    byfunc --> lb[Load Balancing Cluster: Web Servers]
    byfunc --> grid[Grid Cluster: Multi Organization]

    byarch --> central[Centralized: Master and Worker]
    byarch --> decent[Decentralized: Peer to Peer]
    byarch --> hybrid[Hybrid: Hierarchical Centralization]

    bynode --> homo[Homogeneous: All Nodes Identical]
    bynode --> hetero[Heterogeneous: Mixed Hardware]

    hpc --> beo[Subtype: Beowulf]
    ha --> failover[Subtype: Active Passive or Active Active]
    lb --> lvs[Subtype: LVS Linux Virtual Server]
```

## 4.3 Beowulf Cluster — Master/Worker Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ext[External Network Internet]
    mn[Master Node: Head and File Server]
    pvt[Private Interconnect: Gigabit Ethernet or InfiniBand]
    s1[Slave Node 1]
    s2[Slave Node 2]
    s3[Slave Node 3]
    s4[Slave Node 4]
    sN[Slave Node N]
    ss[Shared Storage: NFS or Parallel FS]

    ext <--> mn
    mn <--> pvt
    pvt <--> s1
    pvt <--> s2
    pvt <--> s3
    pvt <--> s4
    pvt <--> sN
    mn <--> ss
    s1 -.-> ss
    s2 -.-> ss
    s3 -.-> ss
    s4 -.-> ss
    sN -.-> ss
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** The dashed lines (e.g., `s1 -.-> ss`) represent **read-only or batch access** to shared storage by worker nodes. The master node has **read/write control** (solid arrow) over the shared filesystem.

## 4.4 Cluster Job Execution Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant M as Master Node
    participant Q as Job Queue
    participant W as Worker Node
    participant FS as Shared Storage

    U->>M: Submit job via SSH or Web Portal
    M->>Q: Enqueue job with priority
    Q->>M: Dequeue next job
    M->>W: Dispatch job via MPI or SSH
    W->>FS: Read input data
    FS-->>W: Return input data
    W->>W: Execute parallel computation
    W->>FS: Write output results
    W->>M: Report job completion
    M->>U: Notify job done
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📕 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List and briefly explain the three major cluster families based on their primary function.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **High-Performance Computing (HPC) Clusters** (1 mark): These clusters are designed to maximize computational speed for parallel scientific workloads. Example: Beowulf clusters running MPI for CFD simulations.

2. **High-Availability (HA) Clusters** (1 mark): These clusters aim to provide uninterrupted service through redundancy and automatic failover. Example: Banking database servers using active-passive configurations.

3. **Load-Balancing Clusters** (1 mark): These clusters distribute client requests across multiple nodes to maximize request throughput. Example: A web server farm using HAProxy for request distribution.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is Single System Image (SSI) in the context of clusters? List any two of its components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Single System Image (SSI) is a **cluster middleware abstraction** that makes a collection of distributed computers appear to users, applications, and the network as a **single integrated computing resource** (2 marks).

**Two components of SSI** (1 mark):
- **Single File Hierarchy** — one unified root directory across all nodes.
- **Single Process Space** — a global unique PID across the entire cluster.

*(Other valid components: Single Entry Point, Single I/O Space, Single Network Interface.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **omit the network interconnect** when drawing cluster diagrams → **lose 2 marks**.
> - Confusing **MPI with PVM** → PVM is older and process-based; MPI is the modern standard. Examiners deduct marks if swapped.
> - Forgetting to mention the **Beowulf characteristic** "dedicated private network" → loses the "COTS + private network" mark.
> - Always **end Amdahl's Law problems with interpretation** — examiners reward application/commentary beyond just numbers.

---

### 🟦 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the **Beowulf cluster architecture** in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss its key design principles and advantages over traditional parallel supercomputers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cluster runs a parallel application. The inherently sequential portion is **8%** of the total execution time. The cluster has **16 compute nodes**.
- (i) Calculate the maximum speedup achievable using Amdahl's Law.
- (ii) Calculate the parallel efficiency.
- (iii) If the program is rewritten to reduce the sequential portion to **2%**, what is the new maximum speedup on the same 16 nodes? Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Beowulf is a **class of commodity HPC cluster** designed by Thomas Sterling and Donald Becker at NASA in 1994. It pioneered the use of COTS hardware, open-source Linux, and MPI for supercomputer-class computing.

**Key Design Principles (3 marks):**
1. **COTS Components:** Uses mass-produced PCs, Ethernet cards, and switches — drastically reduces cost.
2. **Dedicated Private Network:** Workers communicate over a private network, isolated from public traffic → low latency, high bandwidth.
3. **Open-Source Software Stack:** Linux OS, MPI (Message Passing Interface), and PBS job scheduler — no proprietary licenses.
4. **Master/Worker Topology:** A single head node manages the cluster; worker nodes are hidden from the external network.
5. **Scalable:** Adding more worker nodes linearly increases compute capacity (up to Amdahl's limit).

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
        External Network
              |
              v
        +-------------+
        |  MASTER     |
        |   NODE      |
        +------+------+
               |
        +------v------+
        |  Private    |
        |  Network    |
        +--+--+--+--+
           |  |  |  |
           v  v  v  v
        [W1][W2][W3][W4]   (Worker/Slave Nodes)
        [W5][W6][W7][W8]
```

**Advantages Over Traditional Supercomputers (1 mark):**
- **Cost:** 10–100× cheaper than proprietary supercomputers.
- **Scalability:** Incremental node addition without redesign.
- **Maintainability:** Standard components, easy repair/replacement.
- **Open Standards:** No vendor lock-in.

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Given:** $f = 0.08$, $1-f = 0.92$, $p = 16$

**(i) Maximum Speedup (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{16} &= \frac{1}{f + \frac{1-f}{p}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.08 + \frac{0.92}{16}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.08 + 0.0575} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.1375} \\
S_{16} &\approx 7.273
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Parallel Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{16} &= \frac{S_{16}}{p} \\
&= \frac{7.273}{16} \\
E_{16} &\approx 0.4546 \text{ or } 45.46\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) After optimization, new $f' = 0.02$, $1-f' = 0.98$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S'_{16} &= \frac{1}{0.02 + \frac{0.98}{16}} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.02 + 0.06125} \\
&= \frac{1}{0.08125} \\
S'_{16} &\approx 12.308
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
Reducing the sequential fraction from 8% to 2% (a 4× reduction) **increased speedup from 7.27× to 12.31×** — a 1.69× improvement. This demonstrates that **algorithmic optimization to reduce serial portions is far more cost-effective than simply adding more nodes** to a cluster. This is a key insight of Amdahl's Law.

---

### 🟩 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Past Paper]**
**(a)** Discuss the **three main cluster families** (HPC, HA, Load Balancing) with a focus on their architectural differences, typical middleware, and real-world application scenarios. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a generic cluster**, clearly labeling the **five layers** (hardware, OS, middleware, programming environment, applications). What is the role of the cluster middleware in enabling Single System Image (SSI)? **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**HPC Cluster (≈2.5 marks):**
- **Goal:** Maximum FLOPS for scientific problems.
- **Middleware:** SLURM / Torque scheduler + OpenMPI / MPICH.
- **Architecture:** Tightly coupled, low-latency interconnect (InfiniBand).
- **Real-world use:** Weather forecasting (ECMWF), genome sequencing, CFD in aerospace.

**HA Cluster (≈2.5 marks):**
- **Goal:** Continuous service availability (99.99%+).
- **Middleware:** Pacemaker + Corosync (heartbeat), DRBD (replication).
- **Architecture:** Active-Passive (or Active-Active) with shared/replicated storage.
- **Real-world use:** Banking transaction servers, telecom call processors, airline reservation systems.

**Load-Balancing Cluster (≈2 marks):**
- **Goal:** Distribute user requests evenly across many servers.
- **Middleware:** HAProxy, LVS (Linux Virtual Server), NGINX, Round-Robin DNS.
- **Architecture:** Front-end dispatcher routes requests to back-end pool.
- **Real-world use:** Google search front-end, e-commerce websites (Amazon, Flipkart), social media platforms.

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Five Layers of a Cluster (4 marks):**

```
+----------------------------------------+
| LAYER 5: Cluster Applications          |
| (Scientific Codes, Web Services, AI)   |
+----------------------------------------+
| LAYER 4: Programming Environments      |
| (MPI, PVM, OpenMP, Job Schedulers)     |
+----------------------------------------+
| LAYER 3: Cluster Middleware            |
| (SSI, HA, System Management)           |
+----------------------------------------+
| LAYER 2: Cluster Operating Systems     |
| (Linux on each node)                   |
+----------------------------------------+
| LAYER 1: Cluster Hardware              |
| (Nodes, NIC, Interconnect, Storage)    |
+----------------------------------------+
```

**Role of Middleware in SSI (3 marks):**
The middleware layer **abstracts the physical distribution** of the cluster nodes by providing a Single System Image. It:
1. **Hides complexity** — User submits a job to a single entry point; middleware distributes it.
2. **Provides global file namespace** — User sees one root directory (via NFS or parallel FS) instead of per-node filesystems.
3. **Unifies process management** — Global PID space, allowing one process to spawn children on remote nodes.
4. **Manages resources globally** — Centralized load balancing, scheduling, and monitoring.

Without middleware, the cluster would behave as a "network of independent computers" — defeating the purpose of integration.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Cluster Families & Architecture**

- ✅ **Cluster =** Collection of interconnected independent computers working as a single integrated resource.
- ✅ **Three Function-based Families:** **HPC** (speed), **HA** (uptime), **Load-Balancing** (throughput). *(Acronym: "HHL")*
- ✅ **Beowulf =** COTS + Linux + MPI + Master/Worker + Private Network (NASA, 1994, Sterling & Becker).
- ✅ **SSI = Single System Image:** Makes distributed cluster look like one machine (four sub-components: Entry, File, Process, I/O).
- ✅ **Five-Layer Architecture:** Hardware → OS → Middleware → Programming Environment → Applications.
- ✅ **Middleware is the "glue":** SSI + System Management + HA infrastructure.
- ✅ **Master Node:** Sole external-facing node; schedules jobs, holds shared storage; workers are hidden.
- ✅ **Amdahl's Law:** $S_p = \frac{1}{f + \frac{(1-f)}{p}}$; capped at $\frac{1}{f}$ as $p \to \infty$.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $E_p = \frac{S_p}{p}$; always ≤ 1.
- ✅ **Availability** $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$; "Four Nines" = 99.99% = ~52 min downtime/year.
- ✅ **MPI vs PVM:** MPI = standard, library-based; PVM = older, virtual machine framework.
- ✅ **Homogeneous > Heterogeneous** for cluster predictability and management.
- ✅ **Cost rule of thumb:** Cluster performance per dollar >> traditional supercomputer.
- ✅ **Schedulers to know:** SLURM (most popular in academia), PBS/Torque, LSF (industry).
- ✅ **Top interconnect types:** Gigabit Ethernet (cheap), InfiniBand (high performance), Myrinet (legacy HPC).
- ✅ **Common KTU diagram requirement:** Always show **Master, Workers, Network Switch, and Shared Storage** — missing any one loses marks.
- ✅ **Watch out for confusion:** Beowulf ≠ Grid; Beowulf is a single-org cluster, Grid is multi-org across the internet.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GPU Clusters Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** GPU Clusters Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **GPU Cluster (Per KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> A **GPU Cluster** is a collection of interconnected compute nodes, where each node is equipped with one or more **Graphics Processing Units (GPUs)** acting as hardware accelerators, working in tandem with multi-core CPUs over a **high-bandwidth, low-latency interconnect fabric**, coordinated by a distributed software stack (MPI + CUDA/OpenCL + Resource Manager) to deliver aggregate throughput, parallelism, and energy efficiency for data-parallel, compute-intensive workloads such as deep learning training, scientific simulation, and large-scale analytics.

In the context of the **2024 NEP-aligned PCCST602 syllabus**, a GPU cluster is studied as a **heterogeneous HPC architecture** where the components fall into the following layered taxonomy:

| Layer | Component Class | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **L0 – Hardware Fabric** | GPU, CPU, Memory, Storage, NIC | NVIDIA H100, Intel Xeon, NVMe SSD |
| **L1 – Physical Interconnect** | PCIe, NVLink, InfiniBand, Ethernet | NVIDIA NVLink 4.0, Mellanox HDR |
| **L2 – System Software** | CUDA Driver, NCCL, RDMA, OFED | CUDA Toolkit 12.x |
| **L3 – Cluster Middleware** | Resource Manager, Scheduler, MPI | Slurm, OpenMPI, UCX |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition: The "Mega-Factory of Math"**
> Imagine a city where every house (a **compute node**) has a few very smart general-purpose managers (**CPUs**) but also a giant workshop full of 10,000 tireless workers who are each *only* good at one tiny task — painting a single pixel, multiplying two numbers, or adding up a small list. Those 10,000 workers represent the **GPU's thousands of tiny cores**. 
> 
> Now imagine each house is connected by a **high-speed metro line** (the **interconnect**). A **postal service** (the **MPI runtime**) carries the problems and results between houses. A **city hall dispatcher** (the **job scheduler / Slurm**) decides which workshop gets which job and which workers are free.
> 
> A **GPU cluster**, then, is the city itself — multiple such houses linked by metro lines, all governed by a single dispatcher, churning through problems too big for any single workshop to handle.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following industry-standard metrics are used to characterize GPU cluster components:

- **Peak Performance**: measured in **TFLOPS** ($10^{12}$ floating-point operations/sec) or **PFLOPS** ($10^{15}$).
- **Memory Bandwidth**: measured in **GB/s** — typical HBM3 ≈ **3350 GB/s** (single stack).
- **Interconnect Bandwidth**: **GB/s** — NVLink 4.0 ≈ **900 GB/s**; InfiniBand HDR ≈ **200 Gbps (25 GB/s)**.
- **Network Latency**: measured in **microseconds (μs)** — typical MPI over IB ≈ **1–2 μs**.
- **Power Density**: measured in **kW per rack** — modern AI racks ≈ **40–120 kW**.
- **Speedup (Amdahl)**: dimensionless ratio $S = \frac{T_1}{T_p}$.
- **Scaling Efficiency**: $E = \frac{S}{p} \times 100\%$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Decomposition of a GPU Cluster

The architecture is best understood as **five interlocking subsystems**. Each is described below with its operational logic and the *why* behind it.

### 2.1.1 Compute Node (The Worker)
- **CPU Host** — typically 1–2 server-grade sockets (Intel Xeon / AMD EPYC, 32–128 cores). It runs the OS, prepares data, launches kernels, and handles control flow.
- **GPU Device(s)** — 1–8 accelerators per node (e.g., NVIDIA H100, A100, AMD MI300). GPUs host thousands of lightweight cores organized into **Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs)**.
- **Host Memory (DDR5)** — typically **256 GB – 2 TB** per node; feeds the GPU via PCIe or NVLink.
- **Device Memory (HBM2e/3)** — typically **40–80 GB** per GPU, providing the high bandwidth the cores need.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why both CPU and GPU?** The CPU excels at *latency-bound*, branching, irregular code (control plane). The GPU excels at *throughput-bound*, regular, data-parallel code (data plane). Heterogeneity exploits both.

### 2.1.2 GPU Internal Architecture
- **SM (Streaming Multiprocessor)** — execution unit containing hundreds of CUDA cores, tensor cores, register file, shared memory, and warp scheduler.
- **CUDA Cores / SIMT lanes** — execute one thread per lane, in lockstep (32 threads = 1 warp).
- **Tensor Cores** — specialized matrix-multiply–accumulate units (e.g., FP8, FP16, BF16, TF32) delivering hundreds of TFLOPS for AI.
- **L2 Cache + HBM** — HBM is built using stacked DRAM dies connected by **Through-Silicon Vias (TSVs)**, giving massive bandwidth at moderate capacity.

### 2.1.3 Interconnect Fabric (The Nervous System)
- **Intra-node** — **PCIe Gen5 (≈128 GB/s)** or **NVLink 4.0 (≈900 GB/s)** between CPU↔GPU and GPU↔GPU inside one box.
- **Inter-node** — **InfiniBand (HDR/NDR)**, **200/400 GbE**, or proprietary fabrics like **NVLink Network** for GPU-to-GPU across boxes.
- **Topology** — fat-tree, dragonfly+, or rail-optimized — chosen to minimize **bisection bandwidth bottlenecks**.

### 2.1.4 Storage Subsystem
- **Parallel File Systems** — Lustre, GPFS/Spectrum Scale, BeeGFS — aggregate throughput across thousands of clients.
- **Local NVMe** — provides scratch space and high-IOPS staging; typical 30–100 TB per node.
- **Object Stores** — S3-compatible tiers for cold/checkpoint data.

### 2.1.5 Software Stack
| Layer | Responsibility | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Application** | User code | PyTorch, TensorFlow, GROMACS |
| **Framework Runtime** | Parallelism abstraction | NCCL, horovod, DeepSpeed |
| **Communication Lib** | Message passing | OpenMPI, MPICH, NCCL |
| **Resource Manager** | Job scheduling | Slurm, PBS Pro, LSF |
| **System SW** | Drivers, kernel | NVIDIA Driver, CUDA Toolkit |
| **Firmware / BMC** | Hardware control | IPMI, Redfish |

## 2.2 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Equations for Module 2 — GPU Clusters**

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Amdahl's Law** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p}}$ | $S$ = speedup, $f$ = parallel fraction, $p$ = processors |
| **Parallel Efficiency** | $E = \dfrac{S}{p}$ | Often expressed as percent |
| **Gustafson's Law** | $S_{\text{scaled}} = p - f(p-1)$ | Speedup when problem size scales with $p$ |
| **Strong Scaling Time** | $T_p = T_s + T_p^{\text{comm}}$ | $T_s$ = serial, $T_p^{\text{comm}}$ = parallel+comm |
| **Roofline Bound** | $\text{Perf} = \min(P_{\text{peak}},\;I \times \beta)$ | $I$ = operational intensity (FLOP/byte), $\beta$ = HBM bandwidth |
| **Comm-to-Compute Ratio** | $r = \dfrac{T_{\text{comm}}}{T_{\text{compute}}}$ | If $r \gg 1$, cluster is communication-bound |
| **Bandwidth–Latency Time** | $T_{\text{net}} = L + \dfrac{M}{B}$ | $L$ = latency (s), $M$ = message size (bytes), $B$ = bandwidth (B/s) |
| **HBM Stack Bandwidth** | $B_{\text{stack}} = f_{\text{ck}} \times 2 \times N_{\text{channels}} \times W$ | $f_{\text{ck}}$ = memory clock, $N_{\text{channels}}$ = channel count |
| **Power Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Perf (GFLOPS)}}{\text{Power (W)}}$ | GFLOPS/W — green-computing metric |
| **Checkpoint Interval (Young)** | $I_c = \sqrt{2\,T_c\,M_{\text{MTBF}}}$ | Optimal checkpoint period, $T_c$ = checkpoint time, $M_{\text{MTBF}}$ = mean time between failures |
| **Cluster Availability** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | $A \to 1$ is desired |
| **Aggregate Throughput** | $P_{\text{agg}} = N_{\text{nodes}} \times N_{\text{GPU/node}} \times P_{\text{GPU}}$ | Total cluster peak |

> **LaTeX Notice:** All absolute-value / conditioning notation uses `\vert` or `\mid` to keep table structure intact.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

GPU clusters are the workhorse infrastructure behind:
- **LLM training** (GPT, LLaMA — thousands of H100s, ~ExaFLOP aggregate).
- **Climate modeling** (e.g., ICON, IFS on EuroHPC LUMI — 552 nodes, 11.5 PFLOPS).
- **Computational fluid dynamics**, **molecular dynamics (GROMACS)**, **genomics (BLAST, AlphaFold)**.
- **National HPC missions** — India's **PARAM** series, NSM, and AI supercomputers are all GPU-cluster architectures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Amdahl's Law and the Parallel Fraction

**Premise.** A program of total runtime $T_1$ on one node is split into a *serial* portion of time $T_s$ and a *parallel* portion of time $T_p^{\text{work}}$ distributed across $p$ workers.

**Step 1.** Define the serial fraction $f$ as the fraction of original runtime that is non-parallelizable:

$$f = \dfrac{T_s}{T_1}$$

Consequently, the parallelizable portion is $(1-f)$ of $T_1$.

**Step 2.** When $p$ workers are available, the serial part still takes $T_s$ and the parallel part is divided across $p$ workers (assuming perfectly balanced work) giving $T_p^{\text{work}}/p$.

$$T_p = T_s + \dfrac{(1-f)\,T_1}{p}$$

**Step 3.** Speedup $S = T_1 / T_p$:

$$S = \dfrac{T_1}{T_s + \dfrac{(1-f)\,T_1}{p}}$$

**Step 4.** Substitute $T_s = f\,T_1$ and cancel $T_1$:

$$S = \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{(1-f)}{p}}$$

**Final canonical form (Amdahl's Law):**

$$\boxed{\,S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p}}\,}$$

**Interpretation.** As $p \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{p \to \infty} S = \dfrac{1}{f}$$

Thus **the serial fraction is the asymptotic ceiling**. In GPU clusters, kernel launch overhead, stragglers, and MPI collectives inject a residual $f \neq 0$, capping $S$ well below $p$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Gustafson's Law (Weak Scaling)

**Premise.** Amdahl assumes a *fixed problem size*. Gustafson observes that on a real cluster, we usually **enlarge the problem** to exploit more hardware.

**Step 1.** Run time on $p$ nodes for the scaled problem:

$$T_p' = f\,T_1 + (1-f)\,p\,T_1' \cdot \dfrac{1}{p}$$

For *weak scaling*, $T_1'$ is chosen so per-node work is constant. The form simplifies to:

$$S_{\text{scaled}} = p - f\,(p-1)$$

**Step 2.** Expanding:

$$S_{\text{scaled}} = p - fp + f = p(1-f) + f$$

**Interpretation.** Even with $f = 0.05$, on $p = 1000$ nodes we still get $S \approx 950.05$ — weak scaling is far more forgiving and is the **operative model for AI training on GPU clusters**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Roofline Model — the Architectural Ceiling

**Step 1.** Operational intensity $I$ is FLOPs per byte of DRAM traffic:

$$I = \dfrac{\text{FLOPs}}{\text{Bytes}} \quad [\text{FLOP/byte}]$$

**Step 2.** The machine's *memory-bound* performance is the bandwidth-limited ridge:

$$P_{\text{mem}}(I) = I \times \beta_{\text{HBM}}$$

**Step 3.** The machine's *compute-bound* performance is the flat peak:

$$P_{\text{compute}} = P_{\text{peak}}$$

**Step 4.** The achievable performance is the minimum of the two:

$$\boxed{\,P(I) = \min\bigl(P_{\text{peak}},\; I \times \beta_{\text{HBM}}\bigr)\,}$$

The **ridge point** $I^*$ where the two curves meet is:

$$I^* = \dfrac{P_{\text{peak}}}{\beta_{\text{HBM}}}$$

For an NVIDIA H100, $P_{\text{peak}} \approx 989\,\text{TFLOPS}_{\text{FP16}}$ and $\beta_{\text{HBM}} \approx 3350\,\text{GB/s}$:

$$I^* = \dfrac{989 \times 10^{12}}{3350 \times 10^{9}} \approx 295\;\text{FLOP/byte}$$

Any kernel with $I > 295$ is compute-bound; below that, it is memory-bound.

## 3.4 Derivation: Network Time Model (LogP-style)

For a message of size $M$ bytes over an interconnect of bandwidth $B$ and one-way latency $L$:

$$T_{\text{net}} = L + \dfrac{M}{B}$$

**Example.** All-reduce of 1 GB gradient over 8 H100s connected by NVLink 4.0 (assume effective $B \approx 450$ GB/s aggregate), $L \approx 1\,\mu\text{s}$:

$$T_{\text{net}} = 1 \times 10^{-6} + \dfrac{1 \times 10^{9}}{450 \times 10^{9}} = 1 \times 10^{-6} + 2.22 \times 10^{-3} \approx 2.22\;\text{ms}$$

If the GPU compute for the same iteration is, say, 5 ms, then the comm-to-compute ratio is $r = 2.22/5 \approx 0.44$ — acceptable but non-trivial.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Component-Level Performance Levers

| Component | Knob | Effect on Performance | Bottleneck Symptom |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GPU SM count** | Increase cores | Higher peak FLOPS | Occupancy stalls |
| **HBM bandwidth** | Wider bus / faster stacks | Raise ridge point $I^*$ | Kernel time ∝ data size |
| **NVLink / NVSwitch** | Replace PCIe | Lower CPU↔GPU latency | Stalled H2D copies |
| **InfiniBand NDR** | Upgrade fabric | Lower all-reduce time | MPI wait dominates |
| **PCIe Gen5** | Lane count | Higher host↔device BW | DMA queue full |
| **NVMe scratch** | More parallel SSDs | Faster checkpointing | Slurm `ckpt` stalls |
| **Slurm config** | Topology-aware alloc | Fewer cross-rail hops | straggler nodes |
| **NCCL algorithm** | `Ring` vs `Tree` | Reduce comm volume | bandwidth saturation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level GPU Cluster Block Architecture

```mermaid

        subgraph NODE3["Compute Node 03"]
```

## 4.2 Software Stack Layered View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Application - PyTorch / GROMACS / CFD Solver]
    L1[Accelerated Libraries - cuBLAS, cuDNN, NCCL]
    L2[Programming Model - CUDA / OpenCL / SYCL]
    L3[Communication Runtime - OpenMPI / NCCL / UCX]
    L4[Resource Manager - Slurm / PBS / LSF]
    L5[OS Kernel - Linux + OFED + NVIDIA Driver]
    L6[BMC and Firmware - IPMI, Redfish, GPU VBIOS]
    L7[Physical Hardware - GPU, CPU, NIC, NVMe, Switches]

    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6
    L6 --> L7
```

## 4.3 Data-Flow Schematic for a Distributed Training Step

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant GPU0 as GPU Node 0
    participant GPU1 as GPU Node 1
    participant GPU2 as GPU Node 2
    participant GPU3 as GPU Node 3
    participant IB as InfiniBand Fabric
    participant FS as Parallel FS

    GPU0->>FS: Load Mini-Batch
    GPU1->>FS: Load Mini-Batch
    GPU2->>FS: Load Mini-Batch
    GPU3->>FS: Load Mini-Batch

    par Forward Pass
        GPU0->>GPU0: Local Forward on Sub-batch
        GPU1->>GPU1: Local Forward on Sub-batch
        GPU2->>GPU2: Local Forward on Sub-batch
        GPU3->>GPU3: Local Forward on Sub-batch
    end

    par Gradient All-Reduce (NCCL Ring)
        GPU0->>IB: Send Gradient Chunk
        IB->>GPU1: Forward Chunk
        GPU1->>IB: Send Gradient Chunk
        IB->>GPU2: Forward Chunk
        GPU2->>IB: Send Gradient Chunk
        IB->>GPU3: Forward Chunk
        GPU3->>IB: Send Gradient Chunk
        IB->>GPU0: Forward Chunk
    end

    par Optimizer Step
        GPU0->>GPU0: SGD/Adam Update
        GPU1->>GPU1: SGD/Adam Update
        GPU2->>GPU2: SGD/Adam Update
        GPU3->>GPU3: SGD/Adam Update
    end

    GPU0->>FS: Async Checkpoint
    GPU1->>FS: Async Checkpoint
    GPU2->>FS: Async Checkpoint
    GPU3->>FS: Async Checkpoint
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the major hardware components of a GPU cluster and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Compute Node** — server hosting multi-core CPU + 1–8 GPUs + DDR host memory + local NVMe storage. Performs the actual computation. *(1 mark)*
2. **High-Speed Interconnect** — InfiniBand / NVLink / 400 GbE providing low-latency, high-bandwidth GPU-to-GPU and node-to-node communication. *(1 mark)*
3. **Parallel Storage System** — Lustre / GPFS / BeeGFS with metadata + data servers delivering aggregate I/O bandwidth to all nodes. *(1 mark)*

*(Head node and software stack are bonus credit.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between CPU and GPU based on architecture and intended workload.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | CPU | GPU |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Count** | Few (8–128) powerful cores | Thousands (10k+) lightweight cores |
| **Design Goal** | Low latency, complex control | High throughput, data-parallel |
| **Cache** | Large L1/L2/L3 | Small L1, large HBM, modest L2 |
| **Workload** | Serial, branching, OS, control plane | SIMT kernels, AI, HPC, vector math |
| **Memory** | DDR with low bandwidth | HBM with very high bandwidth |

*(Any three contrasting points ⇒ 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the layered architecture of a GPU cluster. Describe the role of **compute node, interconnect, parallel storage, and software stack** in delivering aggregate throughput. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A GPU cluster has 64 nodes, each with 4 H100 GPUs. Each H100 delivers 989 TFLOPS at FP16. Compute (i) the aggregate peak performance of the cluster, (ii) the sustained TFLOPS if the parallel efficiency is 42 %, and (iii) the roofline ridge point $I^*$ given HBM3E bandwidth 3350 GB/s. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution for Question A

**Solution (a) — Layered Architecture (7 marks):**

A GPU cluster is organized as **four cooperative tiers**:

1. **Compute Tier (1.5 marks)** — Each node pairs a multi-core CPU (host) with 1–8 GPUs (device). The CPU handles serial code, OS, I/O orchestration, and CUDA stream management. The GPU executes massively parallel kernels. The two communicate through **PCIe Gen5** (≈128 GB/s) or, on premium nodes, **NVLink 4.0** (≈900 GB/s) for low-latency CPU↔GPU handoff.

2. **Interconnect Tier (2 marks)** — The **intra-node** fabric is NVLink/NVSwitch (GPU↔GPU within a server) and PCIe (CPU↔GPU). The **inter-node** fabric is **InfiniBand HDR/NDR** or **NVLink Network** for direct GPU-to-GPU across boxes. Topology is typically a **fat-tree** so that the **bisection bandwidth** scales with the cluster. RDMA (Remote Direct Memory Access) lets GPUs read remote memory without CPU involvement, which is essential for NCCL collectives.

3. **Storage Tier (1.5 marks)** — A **parallel file system** (Lustre, GPFS) splits data across many **Object Storage Servers (OSS)**, fronted by **Metadata Servers (MDS)**. Each node also has **local NVMe** for high-IOPS scratch and **checkpointing**. This avoids the I/O bottleneck that would otherwise starve thousands of GPUs.

4. **Software Tier (2 marks)** — From the bottom up: **BMC/firmware** → **Linux kernel + OFED** → **NVIDIA driver + CUDA runtime** → **resource manager (Slurm)** → **MPI/NCCL** → **framework (PyTorch, etc.)** → **user application**. The scheduler enforces topology-aware allocation so that an MPI rank is placed on a GPU physically close to its frequent communication partners.

*(Schematic block diagram in Section 4.1 is the visual aid — awarding full marks requires labelling, not just listing.)*

---

**Solution (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Aggregate peak performance (2 marks):**

$$P_{\text{agg}} = N_{\text{nodes}} \times N_{\text{GPU/node}} \times P_{\text{GPU}}$$

$$P_{\text{agg}} = 64 \times 4 \times 989\;\text{TFLOPS}$$

$$P_{\text{agg}} = 256 \times 989 = 253{,}184\;\text{TFLOPS}$$

$$P_{\text{agg}} = 253.18\;\text{PFLOPS}$$

**(ii) Sustained performance at $\eta = 0.42$ (2 marks):**

$$P_{\text{sust}} = \eta \times P_{\text{agg}} = 0.42 \times 253.18\;\text{PFLOPS}$$

$$P_{\text{sust}} \approx 106.34\;\text{PFLOPS}$$

**(iii) Roofline ridge point $I^*$ (3 marks):**

$$I^* = \dfrac{P_{\text{peak}}}{\beta_{\text{HBM}}}$$

$$I^* = \dfrac{989 \times 10^{12}\;\text{FLOP/s}}{3350 \times 10^{9}\;\text{byte/s}}$$

$$I^* = \dfrac{989{,}000}{3350} \approx 295.22\;\text{FLOP/byte}$$

A kernel with $I > 295$ is **compute-bound**; below it, the kernel is **memory-bound** and the cluster will be I/O starved regardless of how many GPUs are added.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question A):**
> 1. *Do not* forget the unit conversions: TFLOPS → PFLOPS requires $\div 10^3$.
> 2. *Do not* write TFLOPS and PFLOPS interchangeably inside the same line — examiners will deduct.
> 3. In part (a), simply *listing* components scores ≤ 3 marks; the diagram and the *role* description are mandatory for full 7 marks.
> 4. In part (b), the ridge-point formula is $P_{\text{peak}}/\beta$ — students often invert it. Memorize as: **peak divided by bandwidth**.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Internal Choice)

**(a)** Discuss the **internal architecture of a modern GPU** (SM, CUDA cores, Tensor cores, warp scheduler, register file, shared memory, L1/L2 cache, HBM). Explain why GPUs are well-suited to data-parallel workloads. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A distributed training job with parallel fraction $f = 0.04$ runs on $p = 256$ GPUs. (i) Compute the Amdahl speedup and the parallel efficiency. (ii) Comment on the gap between strong and weak scaling when the same job is reframed under **Gustafson's Law**. (iii) If the per-iteration GPU compute is 8 ms and the gradient all-reduce over the cluster takes 3.2 ms, compute the comm-to-compute ratio and recommend whether the bottleneck is in computation or communication. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution for Question B

**Solution (a) — GPU Microarchitecture (7 marks):**

A modern NVIDIA GPU (e.g., H100) is built as a hierarchy:

1. **Top-level die** — ~80 billion transistors housing **132 SMs** (Streaming Multiprocessors). *(0.5 mark)*

2. **Inside each SM** *(2 marks)*:
   - **128 FP32 CUDA cores** (SIMT lanes) executing threads in **warps of 32**.
   - **4 Tensor cores** (4th-gen) accelerating matrix-multiply-accumulate in FP8/FP16/BF16/TF32.
   - **Register file** — 65,536 × 32-bit registers per SM, supporting high occupancy.
   - **Shared memory + L1 cache** — 256 KB combined, configurable.
   - **Warp scheduler / dispatch unit** — issues 4 warps/cycle to the execution units.
   - **Special Function Units (SFUs)** — transcendentals, reciprocal, etc.

3. **Outside the SMs** *(2 marks)*:
   - **L2 cache** — ~50 MB shared across the die.
   - **HBM3 stacks** — 5 stacks × 16 GB ≈ 80 GB at ~3.35 TB/s, fed via a 5120-bit memory controller.

4. **PCIe Gen5 + NVLink 4.0 interfaces** for host and peer-to-peer links. *(0.5 mark)*

5. **Why GPUs excel at data-parallel work (2 marks):**
   - **Massive thread-level parallelism** (10,000s of resident warps) hides long memory latency.
   - **SIMT execution** amortizes control overhead across 32 threads.
   - **HBM bandwidth** is ~10× DDR5, satisfying the high FLOP/byte demand of linear algebra.
   - **Tensor cores** deliver 4–8× throughput on the matrix kernels that dominate AI/HPC.

---

**Solution (b) — Scaling & Comm-to-Compute Analysis (7 marks):**

**(i) Amdahl's Speedup and Efficiency (2.5 marks):**

$$S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p}} = \dfrac{1}{0.96 + \dfrac{0.04}{256}}$$

$$S = \dfrac{1}{0.96 + 0.00015625} = \dfrac{1}{0.96015625}$$

$$S \approx 1.0415$$

Parallel efficiency:

$$E = \dfrac{S}{p} = \dfrac{1.0415}{256} \approx 0.00407 = 0.407\,\%$$

This **vanishingly small efficiency** is the classic Amdahl trap: with a non-zero serial fraction, throwing hardware at a fixed-size problem wastes it.

**(ii) Gustafson Reframing (2 marks):**

$$S_{\text{scaled}} = p - f(p-1) = 256 - 0.04(255) = 256 - 10.2 = 245.8$$

$$E_{\text{weak}} = \dfrac{245.8}{256} \approx 96.0\,\%$$

By **scaling the problem with the hardware** (more data, bigger model), we recover near-linear weak-scaling efficiency — the regime in which modern AI training lives.

**(iii) Comm-to-Compute Ratio (2.5 marks):**

$$r = \dfrac{T_{\text{comm}}}{T_{\text{compute}}} = \dfrac{3.2\;\text{ms}}{8\;\text{ms}} = 0.40$$

Since $r < 1$, the workload is **compute-bound**. However, $r = 0.40$ is large enough that communication is non-negligible. Recommendations:
- **Overlap** all-reduce with the next forward pass using **CUDA streams**.
- Use **gradient compression** (e.g., FP8 grads) or **ZeRO/FSDP** to reduce the message size.
- Ensure the **Slurm allocation is topology-aware** so that all-reduce uses NVLink/NVSwitch where possible.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. In Amdahl, the **parallel fraction is $(1-f)$**, **not $f$**. Mixing these up yields $S \approx 25$ instead of $1.04$ — a 14-mark casualty.
> 2. For Gustafson, students frequently write $S = p(1-f) + f$ as the *strong* law — it is the *weak* law. State explicitly whether you are computing strong or weak scaling.
> 3. Always include units in the comm-to-compute ratio (`ms/ms` ⇒ dimensionless) and interpret the threshold ($r<1$ ⇒ compute-bound; $r \gtrsim 1$ ⇒ comm-bound).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — GPU Cluster Components**

- **Definition:** A GPU cluster = many compute nodes, each with CPU + GPU + memory + local storage, joined by a high-bandwidth interconnect and orchestrated by Slurm/MPI/NCCL.
- **Five Core Components:** (1) Compute Node (CPU + GPU + DDR + NVMe); (2) Interconnect (PCIe, NVLink, InfiniBand); (3) Parallel Storage (Lustre/GPFS + cold tier); (4) Resource Manager (Slurm); (5) Programming Model (CUDA + MPI + NCCL).
- **GPU Hierarchy:** Device → SMs → CUDA cores / Tensor cores → warp scheduler → HBM. Tens of thousands of threads, SIMT execution, hide latency by **occupancy**.
- **Interconnect Roles:** PCIe for CPU↔GPU; NVLink for GPU↔GPU inside a node; InfiniBand/NVLink-Network for inter-node; **fat-tree** preserves bisection bandwidth.
- **Storage Role:** Aggregate I/O via parallel file systems; local NVMe for scratch and checkpointing.
- **Software Stack Order:** User app → framework (PyTorch) → NCCL/cuDNN → CUDA runtime → driver → kernel + OFED → hardware.
- **Key Equations to Memorize:**
  - Amdahl: $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/p}$
  - Gustafson: $S_{\text{weak}} = p - f(p-1)$
  - Roofline: $P = \min(P_{\text{peak}}, I \cdot \beta)$
  - Ridge point: $I^* = P_{\text{peak}} / \beta$
  - Network time: $T_{\text{net}} = L + M/B$
  - Aggregate peak: $P_{\text{agg}} = N_{\text{nodes}} \times N_{\text{GPU}} \times P_{\text{GPU}}$
- **Common Bottlenecks:** (a) Memory-bound kernels ($I < I^*$); (b) Comm-bound collectives (large $r$); (c) Serial fraction leakage (Amdahl ceiling); (d) Stragglers in MPI ranks.
- **Mitigations:** Topology-aware scheduling, gradient compression, kernel fusion, overlap of compute and comm, mixed-precision (FP8/BF16), ZeRO/FSDP sharding.
- **Top Exam Traps:**
  - Confusing $f$ and $(1-f)$ in Amdahl.
  - Forgetting the $\div 10^3$ when converting TFLOPS → PFLOPS.
  - Treating PCIe and NVLink as interchangeable — they differ by ~7× bandwidth.
  - Stating "GPU = many CPUs" — wrong; GPUs are throughput-optimized, CPUs are latency-optimized.
- **Real-World Anchors:** NVIDIA DGX H100, DGX SuperPOD, Meta AI Research SuperCluster, India AI Compute Mission — all are GPU clusters at scale.
- **Sample Numeric to Remember:** 64 nodes × 4 H100 × 989 TFLOPS = **253.18 PFLOPS** peak; ridge point ≈ **295 FLOP/byte**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer Clusters Design Principles: Single System Image Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Computer Clusters Design Principles: Single System Image Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single System Image (SSI)** is a collection of hardware and software components working cooperatively to provide a unified, integrated computing environment that presents a single, transparent view of the cluster's aggregated resources (processors, memory, storage, I/O, and networking) to the user, applications, and the network.

In the context of **cluster computing**, SSI is one of the **four pillars of cluster design** (along with High Availability, Scalability, and Cluster Management). Without an SSI layer, a cluster is merely a collection of loosely-coupled independent nodes — with SSI, the cluster *behaves like a single powerful machine*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept (KTU Board Definition):** A cluster with SSI hides the heterogeneity and distribution of nodes, exposing only a single coherent interface. The end user should not need to know *which* node their job is running on.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **modern hospital with multiple specialists**:
- Patients see *one* reception desk (Single Entry Point).
- All patient records are stored in *one* central digital system (Single File System).
- The chief administrator manages *one* hospital, not a federation (Single Administration).
- The MRI machine, the pharmacy, the laboratory — all behave as part of *one* unified infrastructure (Single I/O, Single Memory).

Now, if the hospital had *no* shared system, each specialist would be an island. A patient would have to carry files between doctors, repeat tests, and know exactly which doctor handles what. That is precisely what happens in a cluster **without SSI** — users must manually SSH to specific nodes, move files, submit jobs per node, and track resources individually.

## 1.3 The Eight Pillars of SSI Features (KTU High-Yield)

The standard KTU reference classifies SSI into the following **eight features**, also known as the **"SSI Taxonomy"**:

| # | Feature | Role |
|---|---------|------|
| 1 | **Single Entry Point** | One login point for the entire cluster |
| 2 | **Single File System (SFS)** | Unified view of all local/disk storage |
| 3 | **Single Job Management System** | Centralized job submission & scheduling |
| 4 | **Single Memory Space** | Shared distributed shared memory |
| 5 | **Single Process Space** | Global process ID across the cluster |
| 6 | **Single Network** | One network namespace/address |
| 7 | **Single Administration Point** | Centralized config/user management |
| 8 | **Single I/O Space** | Unified access to all I/O devices |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exam:** *"Entry – File – Job – Memory – Process – Network – Admin – I/O"* → **E–F–J–M–P–N–A–I**

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Typical SSI middleware overhead:** **5%–15%** of raw node performance.
- **Scalability ceiling without SSI:** O(N²) coordination cost; with SSI middleware this can be reduced to O(N log N).
- **Transparency levels (ISO/OSI-inspired):** Access, Location, Migration, Concurrency, Replication, Failure, Performance, Scaling.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition of SSI

The SSI feature set is not a single monolithic layer — it is a **vertical stack** of cooperating middleware components. Let us break each pillar down:

### 2.1.1 Single Entry Point
- **How:** A virtual IP (VIP) is provided by a load-balancing front-end (e.g., a Linux Virtual Server – LVS, or a load-balancing cluster manager).
- **Why:** The user connects to *one* DNS name; the cluster transparently forwards the connection to any healthy node.
- **Failure Handling:** If Node A dies, the VIP migrates to Node B within seconds via protocols like **VRRP (Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol)** or **Keepalived**.

### 2.1.2 Single File System (SFS)
- **How:** Implemented via distributed file systems such as **NFS (Network File System)**, **Lustre**, **GPFS (General Parallel File System)**, or the cluster-aware **OCFS2 (Oracle Cluster File System)**.
- **Why:** A file path like `/home/user/data.txt` resolves to the same physical byte stream regardless of which node the user is currently logged into.
- **Three Implementation Levels:**
  1. **Global File System** — fully distributed metadata & data (e.g., Lustre).
  2. **Shared Disk File System** — SAN-backed, lock-coordinated (e.g., OCFS2, GFS2).
  3. **Network File System** — central server exports to clients (e.g., NFS).

### 2.1.3 Single Job Management System
- **How:** Implemented using **Job schedulers / resource managers** such as **SLURM (Simple Linux Utility for Resource Management)**, **PBS (Portable Batch System)**, **Torque**, or **Grid Engine**.
- **Why:** A user submits one job to the cluster; the scheduler decides *which* node(s) execute it based on load, priority, queue, and availability.
- **Components:**
  - **Job Submitter** (client)
  - **Job Controller** / Master
  - **Job Executor** / Slaves (compute nodes)

### 2.1.4 Single Memory Space (Distributed Shared Memory – DSM)
- **How:** Software-managed coherence layer above message passing; pages of memory are shared across nodes.
- **Examples:** **TreadMarks**, **IVY**, **Shmem** in OpenSHMEM.
- **Why:** Programs written using shared-memory parallelization (e.g., OpenMP-style) can be ported with minimal change.
- **Consistency Models:** *Sequential*, *Causal*, *Processor*, *Weak*, *Release*.

### 2.1.5 Single Process Space
- **How:** Global process IDs (GPIDs) are issued by a central daemon; local PIDs are mapped to global IDs.
- **Why:** `kill -9 12345` works the same on any node; processes can be checkpointed/restarted across nodes.

### 2.1.6 Single Network
- **How:** One virtual IP subnet, one virtual interface per cluster; node IPs are internal implementation details.
- **Why:** Simplifies application configuration — applications bind to the cluster's virtual address, not individual nodes.

### 2.1.7 Single Administration Point
- **How:** Centralized directory service — **LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol)**, **Active Directory**, or **NIS (Network Information Service)**.
- **Why:** Add a user once → available cluster-wide. User authentication, quotas, and policies are consistent.

### 2.1.8 Single I/O Space
- **How:** Device virtualization — any node can access any disk, tape, or specialized I/O device through a unified namespace.
- **Why:** Load-balances I/O bandwidth; provides high-availability access to critical storage.

## 2.2 The Eight Transparency Levels (ISO-aligned)

| Transparency | Meaning | SSI Feature Implementing It |
|--------------|---------|------------------------------|
| Access | Hide differences in data representation | Single File System |
| Location | Hide physical location of resource | Single Memory / Process Space |
| Migration | Hide resource movement | Job Management + Process Space |
| Concurrency | Hide shared-resource contention | Single Process + Memory Space |
| Replication | Hide existence of multiple copies | Single File System |
| Failure | Hide faults and recovery | Single Entry + Administration |
| Performance | Hide performance variations | Job Management |
| Scaling | Hide growth of the system | Administration + Network |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> SSI is qualitative, but cluster performance is governed by quantitative laws. The following formulas are **high-yield** for KTU numerical problems in this module.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Amdahl's Law (Speedup)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}$ | $S$: speedup, $p$: parallel fraction, $N$: nodes | Dimensionless; $S \le N$ |
| 2 | **Maximum Speedup (limit)** | $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | as $N \to \infty$ | Upper bound; never exceeded |
| 3 | **Cluster Efficiency** | $E = \dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{1}{N(1-p) + p}$ | $E$: efficiency | $0 \le E \le 1$ |
| 4 | **Cluster Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{J_{total}}{t_{end} - t_{start}}$ | $J_{total}$: total jobs, $t$: time | jobs/sec |
| 5 | **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)** | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$: node failure rate | hours |
| 6 | **Cluster Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR}$ | $MTTR$: mean time to repair | $0 \le A \le 1$ |
| 7 | **Network Bandwidth (BSP)** | $B_{eff} = \dfrac{M}{t_{c} + M \cdot t_{b}}$ | $M$: message size, $t_c$: comm. latency, $t_b$: per-byte time | bytes/sec |
| 8 | **Communication Latency** | $t = t_{s} + t_{w} \cdot M$ | $t_s$: start-up, $t_w$: per-word, $M$: words | seconds |
| 9 | **Memory Coherence Miss Rate** | $m = \dfrac{miss}{ref}$ | miss: page invalidations, ref: total references | Dimensionless |
| 10 | **Scalability Bound** | $N_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{C \cdot t_{s}}{t_{w}}}$ | $C$: no. of processors for best perf | nodes |

> [!WARNING]
> In all formulas above, the variable `N` denotes the **number of nodes** in the cluster, NOT the speedup itself. Many students mistakenly substitute $N = S$, losing marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **High-Performance Computing (HPC):** Weather forecasting (ECMWF), molecular dynamics (GROMACS), and computational fluid dynamics all rely on clusters with SSI to simplify developer access.
- **E-commerce:** Google, Amazon, and Flipkart front-end clusters expose a single URL but balance millions of users across thousands of backend nodes.
- **Scientific Research:** CERN's LHC data analysis clusters use SSI file systems (Ceph) and job schedulers (HTCondor) to let physicists submit jobs without knowing the underlying topology.
- **AI/ML Training:** Modern LLM training jobs (GPT, BERT) treat the GPU cluster as a single machine; SSI middleware (e.g., **Kubernetes + MPI + shared storage**) makes this transparent.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Speedup from Amdahl's Law (KTU Favourite)
