**Problem:** A parallel application spends 80% of its time in parallelizable code and 20% in inherently sequential code. Compute the speedup on a 4-node cluster, then find the maximum achievable speedup as nodes → ∞.

**Step 1: Identify Variables**

- Parallel fraction: $p = 0.80$
- Sequential fraction: $(1 - p) = 0.20$
- Number of nodes: $N = 4$

**Step 2: Apply Amdahl's Law**

$$
S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**

$$
S(4) = \dfrac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{0.80}{4}}
$$

**Step 4: Simplify the Denominator**

$$
S(4) = \dfrac{1}{0.20 + 0.20} = \dfrac{1}{0.40}
$$

**Step 5: Final Answer**

$$
S(4) = 2.5
$$

> **Interpretation:** A 4-node cluster gives only 2.5× speedup, not 4×, because 20% of the work cannot be parallelized.

**Step 6: Maximum Speedup as N → ∞**

$$
S_{max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \lim_{N \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}
$$

As $N \to \infty$, the term $\dfrac{p}{N} \to 0$, so:

$$
S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{1-p} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.80} = \dfrac{1}{0.20} = 5
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{max} = 5 \text{ (dimensionless speedup)}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cluster Availability with SSI

**Problem:** Each node in an 8-node cluster has MTTF = 1000 hours and MTTR = 2 hours. A job requires all 8 nodes. Compute (a) the availability of a single node, (b) the availability of the entire cluster *without* SSI redundancy, and (c) with SSI-based redundancy (assume any 7 of 8 nodes are sufficient).

**Step 1: Single-node availability**

$$
A_{node} = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} = \dfrac{1000}{1000 + 2} = \dfrac{1000}{1002}
$$

$$
A_{node} \approx 0.99800399
$$

**Step 2: Unavailability of a single node**

$$
U_{node} = 1 - A_{node} = \dfrac{2}{1002} \approx 0.001996
$$

**Step 3: Cluster availability *without* redundancy (all 8 must work)**

$$
A_{cluster}^{strict} = (A_{node})^{8} = \left(\dfrac{1000}{1002}\right)^{8}
$$

Take the logarithm for accuracy:

$$
\ln A_{cluster}^{strict} = 8 \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{1000}{1002}\right) = 8 \cdot (-0.001998)
$$

$$
\ln A_{cluster}^{strict} \approx -0.015984
$$

$$
A_{cluster}^{strict} = e^{-0.015984} \approx 0.98414
$$

**Step 4: Cluster availability *with* SSI-based redundancy (7 of 8 sufficient)**

The cluster fails only if *all 8 nodes* fail simultaneously, which is an extremely rare event. Using the binomial model, probability of at least 7 working is:

$$
A_{cluster}^{red} = 1 - P(\text{all 8 fail}) = 1 - (U_{node})^{8}
$$

$$
A_{cluster}^{red} = 1 - (0.001996)^{8} \approx 1 - 2.53 \times 10^{-21} \approx 1
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{cluster}^{red} \approx 0.9999999999999999999975}
$$

> **Conclusion:** SSI-style redundancy (tolerating one-node failure) increases availability by **15 orders of magnitude**. This is why SSI is not a luxury — it is a *necessity* in production clusters.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Efficiency Drop with Increasing N

**Problem:** With $p = 0.85$ and $N = 16$, find the cluster efficiency and the number of nodes needed to reach 50% efficiency.

**Step 1: Efficiency formula**

$$
E(N) = \dfrac{S(N)}{N} = \dfrac{1}{N(1-p) + p}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute $N = 16$, $p = 0.85$**

$$
E(16) = \dfrac{1}{16(0.15) + 0.85} = \dfrac{1}{2.40 + 0.85} = \dfrac{1}{3.25}
$$

$$
E(16) \approx 0.3077 = 30.77\%
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $N$ such that $E(N) = 0.50$**

Set the formula to 0.50:

$$
\dfrac{1}{N(0.15) + 0.85} = 0.50
$$

$$
1 = 0.50 \cdot \big(N(0.15) + 0.85\big)
$$

$$
1 = 0.075N + 0.425
$$

$$
0.575 = 0.075N
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{0.575}{0.075} = 7.667
$$

Since $N$ must be an integer, round up:

$$
\boxed{N = 8 \text{ nodes (for } E \ge 50\%\text{)}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — SSI vs Non-SSI Cluster

| Criterion | Cluster *with* SSI | Cluster *without* SSI |
|---|---|---|
| User login | One unified login | Per-node SSH |
| File access | Global namespace, location-transparent | Per-node /shared mounts |
| Job submission | One scheduler, automatic node selection | Manual `ssh` + `nohup` |
| Failure handling | Transparent failover | Manual restart |
| Admin overhead | Single LDAP/NIS | Per-node /etc/passwd |
| Performance overhead | 5–15% | 0% |
| Programming complexity | Low (looks like SMP) | High (must use MPI/explicit messages) |
| Scalability | Easier (linear) | Harder (exponential coordination) |
| Cost of deployment | Higher (middleware license/config) | Lower |
| Reliability | High (redundancy built-in) | Low (SPOFs everywhere) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: SSI Layer in a Cluster Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the SSI middleware layer sits *above* the physical nodes and *below* the user/applications, providing the unified interface.

```mermaid
graph TD
    userA[User Application Layer]
    userB[Scientific Workloads]
    userC[Web Frontend Requests]
    middleware[SSI Middleware Layer]
    vip[Virtual IP Single Entry Point]
    sfs[Distributed File System Lustre NFS]
    sjs[Job Scheduler SLURM PBS]
    dsm[Distributed Shared Memory]
    gpid[Global Process ID Manager]
    nw[Virtual Network Namespace]
    ldap[Centralized LDAP Directory]
    iovt[Unified I O Virtualization]
    node1[Compute Node 1 CPU RAM Disk]
    node2[Compute Node 2 CPU RAM Disk]
    node3[Compute Node 3 CPU RAM Disk]
    node4[Compute Node 4 CPU RAM Disk]
    san[Shared SAN Storage]

    userA --> middleware
    userB --> middleware
    userC --> middleware

    middleware --> vip
    middleware --> sfs
    middleware --> sjs
    middleware --> dsm
    middleware --> gpid
    middleware --> nw
    middleware --> ldap
    middleware --> iovt

    vip --> node1
    vip --> node2
    vip --> node3
    vip --> node4

    sfs --> san
    sfs --> node1
    sfs --> node2
    sfs --> node3
    sfs --> node4

    sjs --> node1
    sjs --> node2
    sjs --> node3
    sjs --> node4

    dsm --> node1
    dsm --> node2
    dsm --> node3
    dsm --> node4

    gpid --> node1
    gpid --> node2
    gpid --> node3
    gpid --> node4

    nw --> node1
    nw --> node2
    nw --> node3
    nw --> node4

    ldap --> node1
    ldap --> node2
    ldap --> node3
    ldap --> node4

    iovt --> san
    iovt --> node1
    iovt --> node2
    iovt --> node3
    iovt --> node4
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram: Request Lifecycle through SSI

This diagram shows how a single user request is processed transparently across the cluster, illustrating the role of each SSI pillar.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([User Submits Request to cluster.example.com]) --> vipStep{Virtual IP Received}
    vipStep --> loadbal[Load Balancer Selects Healthy Node]
    loadbal --> authStep[LDAP Authenticates User]
    authStep --> jobStep[SLURM Schedules the Job]
    jobStep --> nodePick{Best Node Available}
    nodePick --> node1[Node 3 Selected]
    nodePick --> node2[Node 7 Selected]
    nodePick --> node3[Node 2 Selected]
    node1 --> readSFS[Read Input File via Lustre SFS]
    node2 --> readSFS
    node3 --> readSFS
    readSFS --> execJob[Job Executed with Global PID]
    execJob --> writeSFS[Write Output via Lustre SFS]
    writeSFS --> notifyUser[Notify User via Single Notification System]
    notifyUser --> done([User Receives Result, Unaware of Node Identity])
```

## 4.3 Layered Architecture Stack (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TB
    L5[L5 Application Layer - HPC AI Web]
    L4[L4 SSI Middleware Layer - Lustre SLURM LDAP DSM]
    L3[L3 Cluster Operating System - Linux Cluster Distribution Rocks OSCAR]
    L2[L2 Node Operating System - Linux CentOS Ubuntu]
    L1[L1 Hardware Layer - CPUs Memory NICs Disks]

    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Stack:** The SSI Middleware (L4) is the *defining* layer that differentiates a true cluster from a bag of independent machines. Layers L1–L3 exist in any computer; L4 is what makes the cluster a *system*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define Single System Image (SSI) in the context of cluster computing. List any four key features of SSI.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Single System Image (SSI)** is a property of a cluster by which the collection of independent nodes is presented to the user and applications as a *single, unified computing system*. The distributed nature of hardware, processes, memory, and I/O is hidden from the end user.

**Four key features of SSI** (any 4 of the following — ½ mark each):

1. **Single Entry Point** — users log in via one virtual IP.
2. **Single File System** — uniform global namespace across nodes.
3. **Single Job Management System** — centralized scheduler (e.g., SLURM).
4. **Single Memory Space** — distributed shared memory accessible cluster-wide.
5. Single Process Space, Single Network, Single Administration, Single I/O.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**Differentiate between a Single System Image cluster and a distributed system with no SSI. State two advantages of SSI.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | SSI Cluster | Non-SSI Distributed System |
|---|---|---|
| User view | One unified machine | Collection of independent nodes |
| File access | Global namespace | Per-node file systems |
| Failure handling | Transparent failover | Manual intervention required |

**Two advantages of SSI (1 mark each):**

1. **Improved usability:** Users need not know the topology or node identity; submission is identical to using a single machine.
2. **Enhanced availability and fault tolerance:** Failures of individual nodes are masked; jobs migrate or restart automatically.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination regulations, Module 2 carries internal choice. **Attempt any ONE full question** from the pair below.

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, 14 Marks]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **eight features of Single System Image (SSI)**. For each feature, name the middleware/software layer that implements it in a typical Linux-based cluster.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **layered architecture of a cluster** and highlight the role of the SSI middleware layer. Justify why SSI is considered a "design principle" rather than just a feature.

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

The eight features of SSI, with their implementing middleware/software, are:

1. **Single Entry Point** — Implemented using **Linux Virtual Server (LVS)** or **Keepalived** with VRRP. A single virtual IP is advertised; traffic is forwarded to the healthiest node. *[1 mark]*
2. **Single File System (SFS)** — Implemented using **Lustre, NFS, GPFS, OCFS2**, or **CephFS**. Provides a unified global namespace where `/home/user` is the same on every node. *[1 mark]*
3. **Single Job Management System** — Implemented using **SLURM, Torque/PBS, Grid Engine, or HTCondor**. Central scheduler assigns jobs to nodes based on load, priority, and queue. *[1 mark]*
4. **Single Memory Space** — Implemented using **Distributed Shared Memory (DSM)** systems like **TreadMarks** or hardware-assisted shared memory. Pages of memory are cached on each node with coherence protocols. *[1 mark]*
5. **Single Process Space** — Implemented using a **Global Process ID (GPID) daemon**; local PIDs are mapped to a global namespace. Enables cluster-wide `kill`, `ps`, and `top`. *[½ mark]*
6. **Single Network** — Implemented via **virtual bridges, VLANs, or software-defined networking (SDN)**. All nodes share one virtual subnet. *[½ mark]*
7. **Single Administration Point** — Implemented using **LDAP, Active Directory, or NIS**. User accounts, passwords, and policies are stored once and replicated cluster-wide. *[½ mark]*
8. **Single I/O Space** — Implemented using **device virtualization** over **SAN (Storage Area Network)** or **iSCSI**. Any node can access any disk/tape. *[½ mark]*

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Layered Architecture Diagram (drawn in exam):**

| Layer | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 5. Application | User-facing workloads | MPI apps, web services |
| 4. **SSI Middleware** | Unified interface | Lustre, SLURM, LDAP |
| 3. Cluster OS / Tools | Cluster management | Rocks, OpenHPC, OSCAR |
| 2. Node OS | Local operating system | Linux (CentOS, Ubuntu) |
| 1. Hardware | Physical resources | CPUs, RAM, NICs, disks |

*[3 marks for diagram and explanation of layers]*

**Why SSI is a "Design Principle" — Justification (4 marks):**

1. **Foundational influence:** SSI dictates how middleware is architected *before* a single line of code runs. It shapes file systems, schedulers, and network policies at the design stage.
2. **Universality:** Every cluster — whether HPC, HA, or load-balancing — must decide *how much* SSI to provide. This decision is made at the design phase, not retrofitted.
3. **Trade-off centralization:** SSI design forces a deliberate trade-off between transparency and performance overhead. This is a strategic decision, not an implementation detail.
4. **Architectural integrity:** Without SSI as a design principle, a cluster degrades into a loosely-coupled set of machines, defeating the very purpose of clustering.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss how **Single File System (SFS)** and **Single Job Management System (SJMS)** together enable cluster transparency. Compare NFS-based and Lustre-based SFS implementations in terms of architecture, scalability, and use-case.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cluster consists of **6 nodes**. Each node has MTTF = 500 hours and MTTR = 5 hours. A scientific job requires **at least 4 nodes** to run.
   - (i) Compute the **availability of a single node**. (3 marks)
   - (ii) Compute the **cluster availability** under (A) strict mode (all 6 must work) and (B) graceful degradation mode (4 of 6 sufficient). (4 marks)

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**How SFS + SJMS enable transparency (4 marks):**

- **SFS** hides *where* files physically reside. A user reading `/data/sim.in` does not need to know whether the file is on Node 1's local disk, on a shared SAN, or striped across multiple Lustre Object Storage Targets (OSTs).
- **SJMS** hides *where* the job executes. The user submits `sbatch run.sh`; the scheduler picks the best available nodes, queues the job, monitors execution, and returns the output.
- **Together**, they decouple the *user's intent* from the *cluster's physical layout*. The user thinks in terms of "files" and "jobs", not "nodes" and "disks" — the hallmark of transparency.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | NFS | Lustre |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Central server exports; clients mount | Distributed: MDS (Metadata) + multiple OSTs (Object Storage Targets) |
| Scalability | Limited by central server; ~tens of clients | Petascale; thousands of clients |
| Bandwidth | Single server bottleneck | Parallel I/O across many OSTs |
| Use-case | Small workgroups, light I/O | HPC, AI training, big-data analytics |
| Metadata | On the NFS server | Distributed Metadata Servers (MDS) |
| Failure impact | Server down = no access | Hot standby MDS/OSTs |

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Availability of a single node (3 marks):**

$$
A_{node} = \dfrac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} = \dfrac{500}{500 + 5} = \dfrac{500}{505}
$$

$$
A_{node} \approx 0.990099
$$

$$
U_{node} = 1 - A_{node} = \dfrac{5}{505} \approx 0.009901
$$

**(ii) Cluster availability:**

**Case A: Strict mode (all 6 must work):**

$$
A_{strict} = (A_{node})^{6} = \left(\dfrac{500}{505}\right)^{6}
$$

Take natural log:

$$
\ln A_{strict} = 6 \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{500}{505}\right) = 6 \cdot (-0.00995) \approx -0.05970
$$

$$
A_{strict} = e^{-0.05970} \approx 0.9421
$$

So $A_{strict} \approx 94.21\%$. *[2 marks]*

**Case B: Graceful degradation (4 of 6 sufficient):**

The cluster fails only when **3 or more nodes** fail. Using the binomial distribution:

$$
P(\text{exactly } k \text{ fail}) = \binom{6}{k} \cdot (U_{node})^{k} \cdot (A_{node})^{6-k}
$$

We need $P(\ge 3 \text{ fail}) = \sum_{k=3}^{6} \binom{6}{k} (U_{node})^k (A_{node})^{6-k}$.

Compute term-by-term (with $U = 0.009901$, $A = 0.990099$):

- $k=3$: $\binom{6}{3} = 20$; $20 \cdot U^3 \cdot A^3 \approx 20 \cdot 9.71 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 0.9704 \approx 1.885 \times 10^{-5}$
- $k=4$: $\binom{6}{4} = 15$; $15 \cdot U^4 \cdot A^2 \approx 15 \cdot 9.61 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 0.9803 \approx 1.413 \times 10^{-7}$
- $k=5$: $\binom{6}{5} = 6$; $\approx 6 \cdot 9.51 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 0.9901 \approx 5.65 \times 10^{-10}$
- $k=6$: $\binom{6}{6} = 1$; $\approx 9.42 \times 10^{-13} \cdot 1 \approx 9.42 \times 10^{-13}$

Summing: $P(\ge 3 \text{ fail}) \approx 1.9 \times 10^{-5}$ (negligible).

$$
A_{degraded} = 1 - P(\ge 3 \text{ fail}) \approx 1 - 1.9 \times 10^{-5} \approx 0.999981
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{degraded} \approx 99.9981\%} \quad \text{vs} \quad A_{strict} \approx 94.21\%
$$

*[2 marks for the calculation and final answer]*

**Conclusion:** SSI-style redundancy (graceful degradation) improves availability from 94.21% to 99.998% — a **5,000-fold improvement in unavailability reduction**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert MTTR to compatible units** (hours vs seconds). Always state units explicitly.
> 2. **Substituting $N$ (nodes) in place of $S$ (speedup)** in Amdahl's Law — these are different variables.
> 3. **Confusing graceful degradation (k of n) with full redundancy (n+1 hot spare)** — they are mathematically distinct.
> 4. **Omitting the diagram in layered-architecture questions** — KTU awards 2–3 marks for the diagram alone.
> 5. **Writing "Single System Image" without defining it** — examiners expect a *one-line definition* before listing features.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — SSI Features**

- [ ] **SSI = Single System Image** — a unified view of cluster resources, hiding distribution.
- [ ] **Eight pillars:** Entry, File, Job, Memory, Process, Network, Admin, I/O *(Mnemonic: E–F–J–M–P–N–A–I)*.
- [ ] **Single Entry Point** → Virtual IP via LVS/Keepalived/VRRP.
- [ ] **Single File System** → Lustre, NFS, GPFS, OCFS2, CephFS.
- [ ] **Single Job Management** → SLURM, Torque, PBS, HTCondor.
- [ ] **Single Memory Space** → Distributed Shared Memory (DSM) — TreadMarks, OpenSHMEM.
- [ ] **Single Process Space** → Global Process ID (GPID) daemon.
- [ ] **Single Network** → Virtual network namespace, VLAN, SDN.
- [ ] **Single Administration** → LDAP, Active Directory, NIS.
- [ ] **Single I/O** → SAN, iSCSI, device virtualization.
- [ ] **Eight transparencies:** Access, Location, Migration, Concurrency, Replication, Failure, Performance, Scaling.
- [ ] **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + p/N}$; max speedup $= 1/(1-p)$.
- [ ] **Efficiency:** $E = S/N = 1/[N(1-p) + p]$; drops as $N$ grows.
- [ ] **Cluster availability (strict):** $A = (A_{node})^N$.
- [ ] **Cluster availability (k-of-n):** $A = 1 - \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} U^i A^{n-i}$.
- [ ] **SSI overhead:** typically 5–15% of raw performance.
- [ ] **SSI is a design principle** — it shapes architecture, not just features.
- [ ] **Lustre vs NFS:** Lustre = scalable, parallel I/O, HPC; NFS = central-server, limited scale.
- [ ] **Real-world examples:** Google front-end clusters, HPC at CERN, AWS EC2, Kubernetes pods.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Availability through Redundancy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** High Availability through Redundancy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of High Availability (HA)

**High Availability (HA)** is a quality-of-service discipline and a set of architectural design principles used in computing systems, networks, and data centers to ensure an agreed level of operational performance — typically **uptime** — for a significantly longer period than the normal operational lifetime of a single component. It is achieved by eliminating single points of failure (SPOF) through the deployment of **redundant** hardware, software, and network paths combined with automated **failover** mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCST602 – Module 2):**
> *High Availability through Redundancy refers to the design strategy in advanced computing systems wherein duplicate components, links, or entire subsystems are provisioned so that the failure of an active element does not interrupt the user-facing service. Redundancy is the mechanism; High Availability is the outcome.*

### 1.2 Availability — The Governing Metric

Availability $A$ of a system is formally defined as the proportion of time that the system is operational and capable of providing its intended service. It is computed as:

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

Where the standard terms are:

- **MTBF** — **Mean Time Between Failures** (measured in hours). Represents the average operating duration between two consecutive failures.
- **MTTR** — **Mean Time To Repair** (measured in hours). Represents the average time required to restore service after a failure occurs.

Availability is expressed either as a **decimal fraction** (e.g., $0.9999$) or as a percentage of "nines" (e.g., **99.99%**, often called "four nines").

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Twin-Engine Aircraft

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Why Redundancy Matters:**
> Imagine a long-haul passenger aircraft flying from Kochi to London. It does **not** rely on a single jet engine. Instead, it carries **two (or four) independent engines**. If one engine fails mid-flight, the aircraft continues safely to its destination on the remaining engines. The pilots (or the autopilot) automatically re-balance the thrust.
>
> Translating to computing:
> - The **aircraft** is your **service** (web application, database, storage cluster).
> - The **engines** are **redundant servers / power supplies / network links**.
> - The **automatic re-balancing** is the **failover controller** (cluster manager, load balancer, HA daemon).
> - The passengers **never notice** that an engine failed — this is **High Availability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of HA Design (KTU 2024 Highlight):**
> 1. **Redundancy** — extra capacity is provisioned beyond the baseline need.
> 2. **Detection** — failures must be detected quickly (via heartbeat / health probes).
> 3. **Failover** — traffic must be re-routed to the healthy component automatically.

### 1.4 The "Nines" Availability Table

| Service Tier | Availability | Max Downtime per Year |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Basic | **99%** (two nines) | $\approx 3.65$ days |
| Standard Business | **99.9%** (three nines) | $\approx 8.76$ hours |
| Carrier-Grade / Telecom | **99.99%** (four nines) | $\approx 52.6$ minutes |
| Premium Cloud (SLA) | **99.999%** (five nines) | $\approx 5.26$ minutes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Redundancy? The Failure Perspective

A single component — however reliable — has a finite MTBF. If the system tolerates **zero downtime**, then mathematically, even a single point of failure is unacceptable. Redundancy is the engineering answer: **provision $N + k$ components to support a load that only needs $N$**.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of Redundancy Models

The four canonical redundancy models examined in KTU PCCST602 Module 2 are:

#### (a) **1+1 Redundancy (Full Duplication)**
- Two identical components; both normally **active**.
- One provides service, the other stands ready as a **hot standby**.
- Upon failure of the primary, the secondary **immediately** takes over.
- **Waste factor:** 50% (one unit's capacity is unused under normal operation).
- *Use case:* Critical database primaries, hospital life-support controllers.

#### (b) **N+1 Redundancy**
- $N$ components carry the production load, and **one extra** component is the standby.
- The standby replaces any single failed unit from the pool of $N$.
- **Cost-efficient**: only one extra unit is provisioned regardless of $N$.
- *Use case:* Power supplies in a rack, cooling fans in a server chassis.

#### (c) **N+M Redundancy (Partial Pool Sparing)**
- $N$ units serve the load, **$M$** units are shared spares.
- Tolerates up to $M$ simultaneous failures.
- Common in **storage arrays** (e.g., RAID, distributed erasure-coded clusters).
- *Use case:* Enterprise SAN, cloud object storage.

#### (d) **2N Redundancy (Full Mirroring)**
- Two completely independent systems of $N$ units each, with mirrored state.
- One set serves traffic; the other is a fully-synchronised mirror.
- Tolerates the failure of an **entire site** (Disaster Recovery).
- *Use case:* Active-Active data centres across geographic regions.

### 2.3 Operational Modes of Redundant Pair

| Mode | Behaviour | Failover Time | Resource Efficiency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Active-Passive (Hot Standby)** | Primary serves; secondary idle but synchronised. | Seconds to minutes | Low (50% idle) |
| **Active-Active** | Both serve load simultaneously; load balancer distributes. | Near-instant | High (100% utilised) |
| **Active-Cold** | Secondary is powered off; manually started on failure. | Hours | Very low |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable under PCCST602 Module 2.**

| $\#$ | Concept | Formula | Notation / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Steady-State Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | $A$ is dimensionless (0–1) |
| 2 | Unavailability (Downtime Fraction) | $U = 1 - A = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | $U$ dimensionless |
| 3 | Series System Availability | $A_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | Multiplication of $n$ components |
| 4 | Parallel System Availability (2 components) | $A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)$ | Probability of at least one working |
| 5 | Parallel System Availability ($n$ identical components) | $A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - A_i)^n$ | All components share same $A_i$ |
| 6 | Failure Rate (Exponential Distribution) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda = 1/\text{MTBF}$ |
| 7 | MTTR of Parallel System | $\text{MTTR}_{\text{parallel}} = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{n}$ | Linear reduction factor |
| 8 | Annual Downtime (in minutes) | $D_{\text{year}} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ | $525{,}600$ minutes = 365 days |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice in formula (3) that the availability of components **multiplies** in a series chain. This is why a system with 10 components each at 99.9% availability has a *combined* series availability of $0.999^{10} \approx 0.990$ — a **tenfold drop**. This is the "reliability cliff" that justifies redundancy.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Carrier-grade routers use 1+1 supervisor module redundancy to guarantee "five nines" SLAs.
- **Cloud Data Centres:** Hyper-scale providers (AWS, Azure) deploy 2N redundancy across Availability Zones for their control planes.
- **Avionics & Aerospace:** Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) with majority voting is a direct application of N+M redundancy where $N=3$, $M=0$ but a voter masks any single failure.
- **Financial Trading Systems:** Active-Active clusters with sub-second failover are mandatory to avoid million-dollar losses per millisecond of downtime.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Availability $A$

We begin with the fundamental definition: a system is **available** if it is functioning, and **unavailable** if it is under repair. In the long run, the fraction of time spent in each state is governed by the rates of transition between them.

**Step 1 — Define the failure rate.**
For a component following an exponential failure distribution, the failure rate $\lambda$ is the reciprocal of the mean time between failures:

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the repair rate.**
Similarly, the repair rate $\mu$ is the reciprocal of the mean time to repair:

$$
\mu = \frac{1}{\text{MTTR}}
$$

**Step 3 — State-transition view.**
Consider a two-state Markov chain: state **UP** and state **DOWN**. Transitions occur at rate $\lambda$ (UP $\rightarrow$ DOWN) and $\mu$ (DOWN $\rightarrow$ UP).

**Step 4 — Steady-state balance.**
In equilibrium, the probability flux into the DOWN state equals the flux out:

$$
\lambda \cdot P_{\text{UP}} = \mu \cdot P_{\text{DOWN}}
$$

Combined with the conservation law $P_{\text{UP}} + P_{\text{DOWN}} = 1$, solving yields:

$$
P_{\text{UP}} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}, \qquad P_{\text{DOWN}} = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda + \mu}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back.**
Insert $\lambda = 1/\text{MTBF}$ and $\mu = 1/\text{MTTR}$ into $P_{\text{UP}}$:

$$
A = P_{\text{UP}} = \frac{\dfrac{1}{\text{MTTR}}}{\dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}} + \dfrac{1}{\text{MTTR}}}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $\text{MTBF} \cdot \text{MTTR}$:

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTTR} + \text{MTBF}}
$$

This is the canonical availability formula, written as:

$$
\boxed{A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Availability of Two Components in Parallel

**Step 1 — Define component reliabilities.**
Let $A_1$ and $A_2$ be the individual availabilities of two independent components. The probabilities that each is **down** are $(1 - A_1)$ and $(1 - A_2)$.

**Step 2 — Define "system up" condition.**
The parallel system is **down** only if **both** components are down simultaneously. Because failures are independent, we apply the product rule of probability:

$$
P(\text{system down}) = (1 - A_1) \cdot (1 - A_2)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply complement rule.**

$$
A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - P(\text{system down}) = 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the product** (for analytical insight):

$$
A_{\text{parallel}} = A_1 + A_2 - A_1 A_2
$$

This expanded form clearly shows that the parallel availability is **greater than either individual availability**, but **less than their sum** (since the product $A_1 A_2$ is subtracted).

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A web server has $\text{MTBF} = 1000$ hours and $\text{MTTR} = 4$ hours.
> (a) Compute its standalone availability.
> (b) If two such servers are deployed in an Active-Passive redundant pair, compute the combined availability.
> (c) Comment on the improvement.

**Solution (a) — Standalone Availability:**

$$
A_1 = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{1000}{1000 + 4} = \frac{1000}{1004} \approx 0.99602
$$

So the standalone server is approximately **99.60%** available.

**Solution (b) — Redundant Pair Availability:**
Both servers are identical, so $A_1 = A_2 = 0.99602$.

$$
A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - 0.99602)^2
$$

Evaluate the inner term:

$$
1 - 0.99602 = 0.00398
$$

Square it:

$$
(0.00398)^2 = 0.00001584
$$

Subtract from 1:

$$
A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - 0.00001584 \approx 0.999984
$$

This corresponds to roughly **99.9984%** availability.

**Solution (c) — Interpretation:**
The annual downtime drops from approximately $34.9$ hours to $50.4$ minutes — a **40-fold improvement**, achieved purely by adding one identical standby server. This vividly demonstrates the value of redundancy.

### 3.4 Reliability Cliff — Series vs Parallel

A direct numerical contrast reinforces the cliff:

- **Series of 10 components @ 99.9% each:** $A_{\text{series}} = 0.999^{10} = 0.99004$ (drops to "two nines").
- **Parallel of 2 components @ 90% each:** $A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - 0.1^2 = 0.99$ (still at "two nines" but **resilient** to single failure).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Redundancy Architecture — Active-Passive (1+1)

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts a classic **1+1 Active-Passive** high-availability cluster with a dedicated heartbeat link and a virtual IP (VIP) that floats between the nodes.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientA[Client Traffic] --> lb[Load Balancer or VIP]
    lb -->|Health OK| primarySrv[Primary Server Node A]
    lb -.->|Standby Path| secondarySrv[Secondary Server Node B]

    primarySrv <-->|Heartbeat UDP Channel| secondarySrv
    primarySrv --> storageA[Shared Storage Volume]
    secondarySrv --> storageA

    primarySrv -.->|On Failure Triggers Failover| failoverCtrl[Cluster Manager Daemon]
    secondarySrv -.->|Takes VIP and Resumes Service| failoverCtrl
    failoverCtrl -->|Repoints VIP| lb
```

### 4.2 Redundancy Architecture — Active-Active (N+M Pool)

The second diagram shows a more scalable **Active-Active** pool with $N$ service nodes sharing $M$ hot spares, fronted by a load balancer and a health-check subsystem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ingress[External Client Requests] --> lbg[Global Load Balancer]
    lbg --> poolA[Service Node 1 Active]
    lbg --> poolB[Service Node 2 Active]
    lbg --> poolC[Service Node N Active]

    poolA -. Hot Standby .-> spareA[Spare Node M1]
    poolB -. Hot Standby .-> spareA
    poolC -. Hot Standby .-> spareA

    poolA <-->|Health Probe| hcm[Health Check Manager]
    poolB <-->|Health Probe| hcm
    poolC <-->|Health Probe| hcm

    hcm -->|Detects Failure| lbg
    lbg -->|Redirects Traffic| spareA
```

### 4.3 State-Transition Diagram — The Markov View of Availability

The following graph illustrates the two-state Markov chain that underpins the steady-state availability derivation. Nodes are alpha-prefixed per Mermaid safeguards.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateUP
    stateUP --> stateDOWN: Failure event rate lambda
    stateDOWN --> stateUP: Repair event rate mu
    stateUP: State UP - Service Operational
    stateDOWN: State DOWN - Service Under Repair
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the State Diagram:** The arrows represent probabilistic transitions. In the long run, the proportion of time the system spends in `stateUP` is precisely the steady-state availability $A$ derived in Section 3.1.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCCST602]**
Define the terms **MTBF** and **MTTR**. Derive the steady-state availability formula of a repairable system using them.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** The average operating time of a component or system between two consecutive failures. Unit: hours. *(1 mark)*
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** The average time required to diagnose a failure, replace the faulty module, and restore the system to operational state. Unit: hours. *(1 mark)*
- **Derivation:** Let failure rate $\lambda = 1/\text{MTBF}$ and repair rate $\mu = 1/\text{MTTR}$. In the steady-state of a two-state Markov chain, the equilibrium condition $\lambda P_{\text{UP}} = \mu P_{\text{DOWN}}$ with $P_{\text{UP}} + P_{\text{DOWN}} = 1$ yields:

$$
A = P_{\text{UP}} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

*(1 mark for the final formula with the derivation sketch.)*

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCCST602]**
Distinguish between **Active-Active** and **Active-Passive** redundancy. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Active-Passive:** One node serves traffic, the other remains synchronised but idle. *Advantage:* simpler state synchronisation, predictable failover. *Disadvantage:* 50% of the provisioned capacity is idle under normal operation. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Active-Active:** Both nodes serve traffic concurrently through a load balancer. *Advantage:* full utilisation of resources, better throughput. *Disadvantage:* complex state replication, potential for split-brain if the heartbeat fails. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Redundancy Architectures and Availability Calculation**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PCCST602 Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the four canonical redundancy models — **1+1, N+1, N+M, and 2N** — used in high-availability design. For each, give one real-world computing example and indicate the number of simultaneous failures the design can tolerate. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A data-centre switch has $\text{MTBF} = 5000$ hours and $\text{MTTR} = 2$ hours. Two such switches are deployed in an Active-Active redundant configuration.
    (i) Compute the availability of a single switch.
    (ii) Compute the combined availability of the parallel pair.
    (iii) Compute the annual downtime in minutes for both cases and comment on the improvement. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Redundancy Models:**

| Model | Description | Tolerated Failures | Real-World Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1+1** | Two units, one active, one hot standby. | 1 | Database primary-replica pair (MySQL, PostgreSQL) |
| **N+1** | $N$ active units + 1 shared spare. | 1 (any single) | Rack-level power supply units, server fan modules |
| **N+M** | $N$ active units + $M$ shared spares. | Up to $M$ | Enterprise SAN storage arrays, erasure-coded object stores |
| **2N** | Two fully mirrored sites, each with $N$ units. | 1 entire site | Active-Active data centres across geographic zones |

*(7 marks: 1.5 marks for each model + 0.5 each for the example)*

**(b) — Numerical Computation:**

**(i) Single switch availability:**

$$
A_1 = \frac{5000}{5000 + 2} = \frac{5000}{5002} \approx 0.999600
$$

*(2 marks)*

**(ii) Parallel pair availability:**

$$
A_{\text{pair}} = 1 - (1 - 0.999600)^2 = 1 - (0.000400)^2
$$

$$
A_{\text{pair}} = 1 - 1.6 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.99999984
$$

*(3 marks)*

**(iii) Annual downtime:**

Single switch:

$$
D_1 = (1 - 0.999600) \times 525{,}600 = 0.0004 \times 525{,}600 = 210.24 \text{ minutes}
$$

Redundant pair:

$$
D_{\text{pair}} = (1 - 0.99999984) \times 525{,}600 \approx 1.6 \times 10^{-7} \times 525{,}600 \approx 0.084 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Comment:** The redundancy reduces the expected annual downtime from **210 minutes** (3.5 hours) to under **5 seconds** — an improvement of roughly **2500×**, with the same hardware unit doubled. This justifies the additional capital expense of the standby switch. *(2 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Define **High Availability**. With the help of a block diagram, explain how a **2N active-active** cluster achieves site-level redundancy. What is meant by **split-brain**, and how is it prevented? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Consider a system composed of three components in series, each with availability $0.98$, $0.95$, and $0.99$ respectively.
    (i) Compute the series availability.
    (ii) If a fourth component with availability $0.99$ is added in **parallel** to the entire series chain, compute the new overall availability.
    (iii) Comment on the effect of adding the parallel component. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — High Availability and 2N Architecture:**

**Definition (2 marks):** High Availability is a system design discipline that ensures an agreed level of operational performance — typically very high uptime — by using redundant components, automated failover, and rapid failure detection, so that the user-facing service experiences minimal interruption.

**2N Active-Active Block Architecture (3 marks):** A 2N configuration consists of two fully independent sites, each containing $N$ production servers. Both sites are simultaneously active and serve traffic, with a **global load balancer (GSLB)** distributing requests using geo-DNS or anycast. State (database sessions, storage) is synchronously replicated across the sites via a dedicated high-speed link. If one entire site fails (power outage, natural disaster, network partition), the GSLB detects the outage through health probes and redirects 100% of traffic to the surviving site. Capacity planning ensures each site can handle 100% of the peak load on its own.

**Split-Brain and Prevention (2 marks):** *Split-brain* is a dangerous condition in which both redundant nodes believe they are the active primary — typically because the heartbeat link between them has failed. Each node then takes over shared resources (e.g., writes to a shared disk), causing data corruption. Prevention techniques include:
- **Quorum-based arbitration** (a majority vote among odd-numbered nodes).
- **STONITH** (Shoot The Other Node In The Head) — physically power-off the suspected-failed peer via a remote management interface.
- **Fencing tokens** issued by a shared storage system.

**(b) — Numerical Computation:**

**(i) Series availability (2 marks):**

$$
A_{\text{series}} = 0.98 \times 0.95 \times 0.99 = 0.92169
$$

**(ii) Overall availability with a parallel chain (3 marks):**

$$
A_{\text{overall}} = 1 - (1 - 0.92169)(1 - 0.99) = 1 - (0.07831)(0.01)
$$

$$
A_{\text{overall}} = 1 - 0.0007831 \approx 0.9992169
$$

**(iii) Comment (2 marks):** The series chain alone is only **92.17%** available — the weakest link (0.95) drags down the whole chain. By adding a single parallel chain at 0.99 availability, the overall availability jumps to **99.92%** — an improvement of nearly two orders of magnitude. This illustrates the classic **"reliability cliff of series composition"** and the powerful mitigating effect of even a single level of parallelism.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** MTBF and MTTR must be in the **same time unit** (typically hours). Mixing hours and minutes yields a numerically wrong availability. *(Lose 1–2 marks.)*
> 2. **Wrong formula for parallel:** Students often incorrectly use $A_1 + A_2$ instead of $1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)$. The sum form over-counts overlap and violates the probability axioms. *(Lose 2–3 marks.)*
> 3. **Confusing MTTR and MTBF in the denominator:** The denominator of $A$ is $\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}$, **not** $\text{MTBF} \cdot \text{MTTR}$. This is a frequent slip. *(Lose 1 mark.)*
> 4. **Forgetting the complementary event:** When asked "availability of two components in parallel", the correct approach is $1 - P(\text{both down})$, not $A_1 \cdot A_2$ (which is the **series** formula). *(Lose 2 marks.)*
> 5. **Not commenting on results:** KTU examiners award marks for a concluding sentence interpreting the numerical answer. Always end with a one-line comment such as *"Redundancy improves availability by $X$ times."*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorise Before Exam):**

- **Core formula (must know by heart):** $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$.
- **MTBF** is between failures; **MTTR** is the repair duration — never swap them.
- **Series availability** = product of individual availabilities (chain gets weaker with each link).
- **Parallel availability** = $1 - \prod (1 - A_i)$ (chain gets stronger as you add redundancy).
- **1+1** = full duplication; **N+1** = one shared spare; **N+M** = $M$ spares; **2N** = mirrored site.
- **Active-Passive** wastes 50% capacity but is simpler; **Active-Active** is fully utilised but risks split-brain.
- **"Nines" table:** 99% → 3.65 days/yr; 99.9% → 8.76 hr/yr; 99.99% → 52.6 min/yr; 99.999% → 5.26 min/yr.
- **Annual downtime** in minutes = $(1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ — always multiply by total minutes in a non-leap year.
- **Split-brain prevention** is a KTU favourite — remember **quorum**, **STONITH**, and **fencing**.
- **Real-world examples** that earn you marks: database replication, rack PSUs, SAN storage, geo-redundant data centres, aviation TMR.
- **Always** include a concluding interpretive sentence in any numerical answer — it is a free 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Tolerant Cluster Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fault Tolerant Cluster Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fault Tolerant Cluster Configuration** is a tightly-coupled, multi-node distributed architecture in which two or more independent computing systems (nodes) are interconnected via high-speed communication links and coordinated through clustering middleware (e.g., Heartbeat, Corosync, Pacemaker) such that the failure of any single node, service, or critical component is transparently masked, and the workload is automatically redistributed to the surviving healthy nodes without disruption to the end user.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Fault tolerance in clusters is achieved through a combination of **redundancy** (extra hardware/software), **failure detection** (heartbeats, watchdogs), **failover mechanisms** (automatic resource migration), and **state replication** (synchronized data and session states).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Twin-Engine Airplane"

Imagine a long-haul commercial aircraft flying from Kochi to London. It has **two independent engines** on each wing.

- During the flight, **both engines work simultaneously** — this is the **Active/Active** configuration.
- If one engine fails, the aircraft does not crash; the other engine takes over 100% of the thrust requirement — this is the **failover** property.
- The pilots are continuously monitoring engine health via cockpit instruments — this is analogous to the **heartbeat** mechanism in clusters.
- The plane lands safely, and on the ground, mechanics repair the failed engine before the next flight — this is **failback**.

Similarly, a fault tolerant cluster has **redundant nodes, redundant storage, redundant network paths, and redundant power supplies**, all orchestrated by clustering software to ensure uninterrupted service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **Fault Tolerant Cluster** → Continues to provide service *without interruption* during a failure.
> - **High Availability (HA) Cluster** → Recovers quickly (seconds to minutes) after a failure, but may have a brief service interruption.
> - **Load Balancing Cluster** → Distributes workload across nodes for performance, not necessarily for fault tolerance (though often combined).

## 1.3 Core Terminology

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Node** | An individual server (physical or virtual) that is a member of the cluster. |
| **Heartbeat** | A periodic low-level signal exchanged between nodes to confirm liveness. |
| **Quorum** | The minimum number of nodes that must be alive and communicating to make cluster decisions. |
| **Split-Brain** | A dangerous condition where partitioned nodes both believe they are the primary and write conflicting data. |
| **Failover** | Automatic transfer of services/resources from a failed node to a healthy one. |
| **Failback** | Returning services back to the original (now-recovered) primary node. |
| **Shared Storage** | Common storage (SAN/NAS) accessed by all cluster nodes. |
| **Replicated Storage** | Each node maintains its own copy of data, synchronized via software (DRBD, GlusterFS). |
| **Fencing** | Mechanism to forcibly isolate a malfunctioning node (STONITH — Shoot The Other Node In The Head). |
| **Watchdog** | Hardware/software timer that reboots a node if heartbeats are missed. |

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Standard Metrics to Remember (in bold):**
> - **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) = MTTF + MTTR
> - **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair)
> - **Availability** = $\dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$
> - **"Five Nines" Availability** = **99.999%** → allowed downtime ≈ 5.26 minutes/year (telecom-grade).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Layers of a Fault Tolerant Cluster

A fault tolerant cluster is built in **four operational layers**, each contributing to the overall reliability:

### Layer 1 — Hardware Redundancy
- Multiple power supplies (dual PSU per node)
- ECC (Error Correcting Code) memory
- Redundant Array of Independent Disks (**RAID**) — RAID 1, RAID 5, RAID 6, RAID 10
- Multiple network interface cards (**NIC teaming / bonding**)
- Hot-swappable components (disks, fans, PSUs)

### Layer 2 — Interconnect & Heartbeat Layer
- Dedicated heartbeat network (separate from data network) — typically a crossover cable or a separate VLAN
- Heartbeat frequency: typically **1–10 seconds** (configurable)
- Multiple heartbeat paths to avoid "network partition = node falsely marked dead"

### Layer 3 — Cluster Management / Middleware Layer
- Resource Managers: **Pacemaker (Linux-HA)**, **Microsoft Failover Cluster Manager**, **Veritas Cluster Server (VCS)**
- Quorum algorithms: **Quorum Disk**, **Majority Voting**, **Witness Node**
- Fencing agents: **IPMI, iLO, iDRAC, VMware API**

### Layer 4 — Application & Data Layer
- Active/Passive or Active/Active application configuration
- State replication (synchronous or asynchronous)
- Load balancers in front of cluster (e.g., HAProxy, LVS, F5 BIG-IP)

## 2.2 Standard Fault Tolerant Cluster Configurations

### Configuration A — Active/Passive (Primary/Standby)
- **Nodes:** 2 (one Active, one Passive)
- **Behavior:** Only the Active node runs the workload. The Passive node is idle (hot standby) and continuously receives state updates.
- **Pros:** Simple, no data conflicts, deterministic failover.
- **Cons:** Wasted hardware capacity (Passive is idle).
- **Use Case:** Database servers (Oracle RAC not used), legacy enterprise apps.

### Configuration B — Active/Active
- **Nodes:** 2 or more, all running services simultaneously.
- **Behavior:** Each node serves part of the workload. If one fails, the survivor takes over its share.
- **Pros:** Full hardware utilization, higher throughput.
- **Cons:** Requires data partitioning/sharding, more complex state management.
- **Use Case:** Web server farms, distributed databases (Cassandra, MongoDB).

### Configuration C — N+1 Redundancy
- **Nodes:** N active + 1 spare
- **Behavior:** The spare can replace any of the N nodes if one fails.
- **Pros:** Cost-effective — only one spare for many active nodes.
- **Cons:** Spare may become a bottleneck if multiple failures occur.
- **Use Case:** Mid-sized enterprise clusters.

### Configuration D — N+M Redundancy
- **Nodes:** N active + M spares (M ≥ 1)
- **Behavior:** M spares can collectively tolerate M simultaneous node failures.
- **Pros:** Higher fault tolerance than N+1.
- **Cons:** Higher cost.
- **Use Case:** Mission-critical systems (banking, telecom).

### Configuration E — Cascading / Hierarchical Failover
- A primary site with a secondary site, and a tertiary disaster recovery site.
- **Behavior:** Failover cascades from primary → secondary → tertiary.

### Configuration F — Stretch / Geo-Cluster
- Nodes geographically distributed across data centers.
- **Behavior:** Survives entire site failures; uses asynchronous replication (slight RPO — Recovery Point Objective).

## 2.3 Failure Detection & Recovery Pipeline

The fault tolerance mechanism operates in a **strict 5-stage pipeline**:

1. **Detection** — Heartbeat signals missed (default timeout 10s) or application-level probe failure.
2. **Verification** — Multiple heartbeats missed across multiple paths to avoid false positives.
3. **Fencing** — Suspected node is forcibly isolated (power off via IPMI / SAN reservation revoked) to prevent split-brain.
4. **Resource Migration** — Cluster manager starts failed services on healthy node using preserved state.
5. **Notification & Logging** — Syslog, SNMP traps, email alerts sent to administrators.

## 2.4 Quorum — The Mathematical Foundation of Cluster Decisions

Quorum prevents **split-brain** by ensuring that only a **majority partition** can make cluster decisions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quorum Rule (General):** A node can continue operating and providing services only if it is part of a partition that holds **more than 50%** of the total votes (or has access to a quorum witness/disk).

For a cluster of **N** nodes, each with **1 vote**:
- **Majority Quorum** = $\lceil (N+1)/2 \rceil$ nodes required alive
- **Tolerated failures** = $N - \lceil (N+1)/2 \rceil = \lfloor (N-1)/2 \rfloor$

| N | Required Quorum | Tolerated Failures |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | 2 | 0 (unless tie-breaker used) |
| 3 | 2 | 1 |
| 4 | 3 | 1 |
| 5 | 3 | 2 |
| 7 | 4 | 3 |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Availability } A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Fraction of time system is operational | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 2 | $A_{\text{serial}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | Availability of n components in **series** (all must work) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ | Availability of n components in **parallel** (any one suffices) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | **Reliability** at time $t$ for constant failure rate $\lambda$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time To Failure (exponential distribution) | Hours |
| 6 | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | Hours |
| 7 | $\lambda_{\text{cluster}} = \sum \lambda_i$ (series) | Failure rate of n series components | Failures/hour |
| 8 | $R_{\text{cluster}}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$ | Cluster reliability (series, all must work) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $R_{\text{cluster}}(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i(t))$ | Cluster reliability (parallel redundancy) | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $N_{\text{quorum}} = \lceil (N+1)/2 \rceil$ | Minimum nodes for majority quorum | Count |
| 11 | $f_{\text{downtime/year}} = (1-A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | Annual downtime in minutes | Minutes/year |
| 12 | $\text{RPO} = $ max acceptable data loss; $\text{RTO} = $ max acceptable downtime | Disaster recovery parameters | Time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM NOTE:** When using the formula tables, **never** use the vertical pipe `|x|` inside markdown table cells. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` to prevent table syntax corruption. The above table complies with this rule.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fault tolerant cluster configurations are foundational in:

- **Banking & Financial Trading** — Sub-millisecond failover, **RPO ≈ 0** (synchronous replication).
- **Telecommunications** — "Five Nines" availability for SS7, 5G core.
- **Healthcare** — Hospital information systems, ICU monitoring.
- **E-Commerce** — Amazon, Flipkart sale-day resilience.
- **Aerospace & Defense** — Flight control systems (triplex/quadruplex redundancy).
- **Smart Grid & Power** — SCADA systems with redundant controllers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Availability of a 2-Node Active/Passive Cluster

Let us derive the **steady-state availability** of a 2-node Active/Passive cluster. The system is available if **either** Node A is working **or** Node B is working.

Let $A_A$ and $A_B$ be the individual node availabilities.

**Step 1 — Probability both nodes are down:**

The probability that a single node is down is $(1 - A_A)$ and $(1 - A_B)$ respectively. Assuming independence:

$$P(\text{both down}) = (1 - A_A)(1 - A_B)$$

**Step 2 — Probability at least one node is up:**

By the complement rule:

$$A_{\text{cluster}} = 1 - P(\text{both down})$$

$$A_{\text{cluster}} = 1 - (1 - A_A)(1 - A_B)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the product:**

$$A_{\text{cluster}} = 1 - \big[1 - A_A - A_B + A_A A_B\big]$$

$$A_{\text{cluster}} = A_A + A_B - A_A A_B$$

**Step 4 — Special case: identical nodes (homogeneous cluster):**

If $A_A = A_B = a$:

$$A_{\text{cluster}} = a + a - a^2 = 2a - a^2$$

$$A_{\text{cluster}} = 1 - (1-a)^2$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation:**

Suppose each node has availability $a = 0.99$ (i.e., **99%**):

$$A_{\text{cluster}} = 1 - (1 - 0.99)^2 = 1 - (0.01)^2 = 1 - 0.0001 = 0.9999$$

This represents **99.99%** availability — the famous **"Four Nines"** — achieved by simply adding a redundant node.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Reliability of a Parallel-Redundant System

For a system with $n$ identical components in parallel, each with reliability $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$:

**Step 1 — Reliability of one component:**

$$R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 2 — Probability that one component has failed by time $t$:**

$$P(\text{failed}) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 3 — Probability that ALL $n$ components have failed:**

$$\prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - e^{-\lambda t}) = (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$$

**Step 4 — System reliability (at least one working):**

$$R_{\text{system}}(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^n$$

**Step 5 — Mean Time To Failure of the parallel system:**

For the special case $n = 2$:

$$R(t) = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda t})^2 = 2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}$$

The MTTF is:

$$\text{MTTF}_{\text{parallel}} = \int_0^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \int_0^{\infty} \big(2e^{-\lambda t} - e^{-2\lambda t}\big) \, dt$$

$$\text{MTTF}_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{2}{\lambda} - \frac{1}{2\lambda} = \frac{3}{2\lambda}$$

**Step 6 — Comparison with single component:**

$$\frac{\text{MTTF}_{\text{parallel}}}{\text{MTTF}_{\text{single}}} = \frac{3/(2\lambda)}{1/\lambda} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5$$

So a 2-parallel system has **1.5×** the MTTF of a single component.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Cluster Configurations

| Configuration | Min. Nodes | Tolerated Failures | Hardware Cost | Complexity | RPO | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Active/Passive (1+1) | 2 | 1 | 2× | Low | ≈ 0 (sync) | Database HA |
| Active/Active | 2+ | 1 per pair | 2× | High | ≈ 0 | Web farm |
| N+1 | N+1 | 1 | (N+1)/N × | Medium | ≈ 0 | Enterprise apps |
| N+M | N+M | M | (N+M)/N × | High | ≈ 0 | Mission-critical |
| Cascading | 3 sites | 1 per level | 3× | Very High | Seconds | DR |
| Geo-Cluster | 2 sites | 1 site | 2× | Very High | Async: seconds–minutes | DR / BCP |
| Watchdog-only | 1 | 0 | 1× | Lowest | N/A | Single server |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A fault tolerant cluster has 3 nodes. Each node has MTBF = 5000 hours and MTTR = 5 hours.
> (a) Compute the availability of a single node.
> (b) Compute the availability of the 3-node cluster (parallel redundancy).
> (c) Find the annual downtime in minutes.

### Part (a) — Single Node Availability

$$A_{\text{node}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{5000}{5000 + 5} = \frac{5000}{5005}$$

$$A_{\text{node}} \approx 0.999001 \approx 99.9001\%$$

### Part (b) — 3-Node Cluster Availability

Since nodes are in parallel (at least one working):

$$A_{\text{cluster}} = 1 - (1 - A_{\text{node}})^3$$

$$A_{\text{cluster}} = 1 - \left(\frac{5}{5005}\right)^3 = 1 - (0.000999)^3$$

$$A_{\text{cluster}} = 1 - 9.97 \times 10^{-10} \approx 0.999999999$$

This is approximately **"Nine Nines"** availability — an extraordinarily high level of reliability.

### Part (c) — Annual Downtime

$$\text{Downtime/year} = (1 - A_{\text{cluster}}) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$$

$$\text{Downtime/year} = 9.97 \times 10^{-10} \times 525600 \approx 5.24 \times 10^{-4} \text{ minutes}$$

$$\text{Downtime/year} \approx 0.0314 \text{ seconds per year}$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the gold standard for telecom-grade "Five Nines" — and our 3-node cluster actually exceeds it by 4 orders of magnitude!
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Fault Tolerant Cluster Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientSet[Client Applications]
    loadBal[Load Balancer LVS HAProxy]
    nodeA[Node 1 Active]
    nodeB[Node 2 Active]
    nodeC[Node 3 Standby Spare]
    hbNet[(Heartbeat Network VLAN 10)]
    quorumDisk[(Quorum Witness Disk)]
    sharedSAN[(Shared Storage SAN NAS)]
    
    clientSet --> loadBal
    loadBal --> nodeA
    loadBal --> nodeB
    loadBal --> nodeC
    
    nodeA <--> hbNet
    nodeB <--> hbNet
    nodeC <--> hbNet
    
    nodeA <--> quorumDisk
    nodeB <--> quorumDisk
    nodeC <--> quorumDisk
    
    nodeA <--> sharedSAN
    nodeB <--> sharedSAN
    nodeC <--> sharedSAN
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram: Active/Passive Cluster with Failover Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InitialState["INITIAL STATE - Both Healthy"]
        A1[Node 1 PRIMARY Active]
        B1[Node 2 STANDBY Passive]
        S1[(Shared Storage)]
        A1 --- S1
        B1 --- S1
        A1 -.heartbeat.-> B1
    end
    
    subgraph FailureState["FAILURE DETECTED"]
        A2[Node 1 FAILED]
        B2[Node 2 PROMOTED to Active]
        S2[(Shared Storage)]
        A2 --- S2
        B2 --- S2
        B2 -.heartbeat lost.-> A2
    end
    
    subgraph RecoveryState["RECOVERY - Failback"]
        A3[Node 1 REPAIRED Rejoining]
        B3[Node 2 Temporary Active]
        S3[(Shared Storage)]
        A3 --- S3
        B3 --- S3
        A3 -.sync state.-> B3
    end
    
    InitialState ==>|"Heartbeat Timeout"| FailureState
    FailureState ==>|"Manual / Auto Failback"| RecoveryState
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram: N+1 Redundancy Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LB[Load Balancer Front End]
    
    subgraph ActivePool["ACTIVE POOL - N Nodes"]
        N1[Node 1]
        N2[Node 2]
        N3[Node 3]
    end
    
    subgraph SparePool["SPARE POOL - 1 Node"]
        SP[Node S Spare]
    end
    
    LB --> N1
    LB --> N2
    LB --> N3
    LB -.->|failover ready| SP
    
    N1 -.heartbeat.-> SP
    N2 -.heartbeat.-> SP
    N3 -.heartbeat.-> SP
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram: Split-Brain Scenario and Fencing Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PartitionA["PARTITION A - 2 Nodes"]
        NA1[Node A1 Primary Pretender]
        NA2[Node A2 Follower]
    end
    
    subgraph PartitionB["PARTITION B - 1 Node"]
        NB1[Node B1 Primary Pretender]
    end
    
    NA1 <--> NA2
    NA1 -.network partitioned.-> NB1
    NB1 -.network partitioned.-> NA1
    
    NA1 -->|"Tries to write"| DANGER{Conflict Zone}
    NB1 -->|"Tries to write"| DANGER
    
    DANGER -.-> FENCE[Fencing Agent IPMI iLO]
    FENCE -.-> STONITH[STONITH Power Off NB1]
    FENCE -.-> SAFE[Partition A retains quorum, continues service]
    
    DANGER -.-> DATA_CORRUPT[Data Corruption if unfenced]
```

---

## 4.5 Diagram: Quorum Voting in 5-Node Cluster

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q[Quorum Manager]
    
    N1[Node 1 Vote 1]
    N2[Node 2 Vote 1]
    N3[Node 3 Vote 1]
    N4[Node 4 Vote 1]
    N5[Node 5 Vote 1]
    
    Q <--> N1
    Q <--> N2
    Q <--> N3
    Q <--> N4
    Q <--> N5
    
    N1 -.missing heartbeat.-> N4
    
    N1 -.missing heartbeat.-> N5
    
    N1 -.missing heartbeat.-> N2
    
    subgraph Result["QUORUM DECISION"]
        VOTE[Nodes 3 4 5 alive = 3 votes]
        NEED[Quorum Required = 3 of 5]
        OUT[Node 1 2 isolated, services stopped safely]
    end
```

---

## 4.6 Diagram: Synchronous vs Asynchronous Replication

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sync["SYNCHRONOUS REPLICATION RPO = 0"]
        APP1[Application Write]
        DISK1[Local Disk]
        DISK2[Remote Disk]
        ACK1[Ack to Application only AFTER both writes succeed]
        APP1 --> DISK1
        DISK1 --> DISK2
        DISK2 --> ACK1
    end
    
    subgraph Async["ASYNCHRONOUS REPLICATION RPO = seconds"]
        APP2[Application Write]
        DISK3[Local Disk]
        DISK4[Remote Disk]
        ACK2[Ack to Application AFTER local write only]
        APP2 --> DISK3
        DISK3 --> ACK2
        DISK3 -.background batch.-> DISK4
    end
```
