# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Sem VI]`

> **Q: Define fault tolerant cluster configuration. List any two key characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

A **fault tolerant cluster configuration** is a multi-node computing architecture in which redundant nodes, storage, and network paths are coordinated by clustering middleware such that the failure of any single component does not interrupt service delivery to end users.

**Two key characteristics:**

1. **Redundancy** — Multiple instances of hardware (nodes, disks, NICs, power supplies) and software (services, applications) exist so that any single failure has a spare.
2. **Automatic Failover with Fencing** — Upon detection of failure (via heartbeat loss), the cluster middleware automatically migrates resources to a healthy node and **fences** (isolates) the failed node to prevent split-brain.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "load balancing" as a feature of fault tolerant clusters. Load balancing is a *performance* feature, not a fault tolerance feature. Use the term "**redundancy + automatic failover**" explicitly to score full marks.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Sem VI]`

> **Q: What is quorum in a cluster? Why is it essential to prevent split-brain?**

**Model Answer:**

**Quorum** is the minimum number of cluster nodes that must be alive and in communication with each other for the cluster to continue providing services and making configuration decisions. It is determined by a voting algorithm, typically requiring **strict majority** ($\lceil (N+1)/2 \rceil$ of N nodes).

**Why essential to prevent split-brain:**

When a network partition occurs, the cluster splits into two or more isolated groups. Without quorum, **both partitions** might independently try to become the primary, leading to **split-brain** — both partitions write to shared storage, causing **silent data corruption**. Quorum ensures that **only the majority partition** continues to serve requests, while the minority partition voluntarily steps down (stops its services), thereby preserving data integrity.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Model Paper]`

> **Q: Explain the different fault tolerant cluster configurations in detail. Compare Active/Passive and Active/Active configurations with suitable diagrams. Discuss the role of heartbeat, quorum, and fencing mechanisms.**

#### Part (a) — Cluster Configurations Explained (7 Marks)

The following are the major fault tolerant cluster configurations:

1. **Active/Passive (1+1):** One node actively serves workloads; the second node is a hot standby. The standby continuously receives state updates. On failure, services migrate to the standby. Pros: simple, no data conflicts. Cons: 50% hardware idle.

2. **Active/Active:** Both (or all) nodes run services concurrently, sharing the workload. Each can take over for the others. Pros: full utilization, higher throughput. Cons: requires data partitioning, complex state synchronization.

3. **N+1 Redundancy:** N active nodes share 1 spare. The spare replaces any failed active node. Cost-effective for medium-scale deployments.

4. **N+M Redundancy:** M spares can absorb M simultaneous failures. Used in mission-critical systems (banking, telecom).

5. **Cascading Failover:** Multi-site topology — primary → secondary → tertiary DR site.

6. **Geo-Cluster / Stretch Cluster:** Nodes in geographically separated data centers; tolerates entire site failures.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Active/Passive | Active/Active |
|---|---|---|
| Active nodes | 1 | All (≥2) |
| Hardware utilization | ~50% | ~100% |
| Failover time | Seconds | Near-instant |
| Data conflicts | None (single writer) | Possible (needs partitioning) |
| Complexity | Low | High |
| Example | Oracle DB HA | Apache web farm |

#### Part (b) — Heartbeat, Quorum, Fencing (7 Marks)

**Heartbeat Mechanism:**
Each node sends a periodic "I'm alive" signal (default every 1–2 seconds) over a dedicated network (often a crossover cable or separate VLAN). If a node misses **3–5 consecutive heartbeats** (configurable timeout), it is declared **dead**. Multiple heartbeat paths prevent false positives from a single link failure.

**Quorum Mechanism:**
Quorum uses **majority voting** to decide which partition of a split cluster may continue operating. With N nodes, at least $\lceil (N+1)/2 \rceil$ must be reachable. A **quorum witness** (extra node or shared disk) can break ties in 2-node clusters. Without quorum enforcement, both partitions could write conflicting data — causing **data divergence and corruption**.

**Fencing Mechanism:**
Fencing is the **forcible isolation** of a malfunctioning node. Two main types:
- **Power Fencing (STONITH):** The cluster sends an IPMI/iLO command to power off the suspect node.
- **Storage Fencing (SCSI Persistent Reservations):** The cluster revokes the suspect node's access to the shared LUN.

Fencing is **mandatory before failover** to guarantee that the failed node cannot simultaneously write to shared storage and corrupt data.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students confuse **quorum** (a *decision rule* for which partition survives) with **fencing** (a *physical action* to isolate a node). Both are required, but they serve different purposes. Quorum decides **who is in charge**; fencing **physically enforces** that decision. Writing only one in the answer will cost 2–3 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Sem VI]`

> **Q: With neat diagrams, explain the Active/Passive and Active/Active cluster configurations. A 2-node cluster has each node with MTBF = 4000 hours and MTTR = 8 hours. Calculate: (i) availability of a single node, (ii) availability of the cluster, and (iii) annual downtime in minutes.**

#### Part (a) — Configurations with Diagrams (7 Marks)

**Active/Passive Configuration:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Client1[Clients] --> VIP1[Virtual IP]
    VIP1 --> Active[Node 1 ACTIVE]
    Active <-->|Heartbeat| Passive[Node 2 PASSIVE]
    Active <-->|Read Write| SAN1[(Shared Storage)]
    Passive <-.->|State Sync| SAN1
```

- Only Node 1 actively runs services. Node 2 is a hot standby.
- Services are bound to a **Virtual IP (VIP)** that floats to the surviving node on failure.
- Storage is shared via SAN/NAS.

**Active/Active Configuration:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client2[Clients] --> VIP2[Virtual IP]
    VIP2 --> LB[Load Balancer]
    LB --> Node3[Node 1 - App A]
    LB --> Node4[Node 2 - App B]
    Node3 <-->|Heartbeat| Node4
    Node3 <-->|Read Write| SAN2[(Shared Storage)]
    Node4 <-->|Read Write| SAN2
```

- Both nodes run different applications (or partitioned workloads).
- On failure, the survivor takes over both applications.
- Requires careful application design to avoid resource contention.

**Key Differences:**

- Active/Passive → **single writer**, no data conflicts, simpler.
- Active/Active → **multiple writers**, needs data partitioning or distributed locking, more complex.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:** MTBF = 4000 hours, MTTR = 8 hours, 2-node cluster.

**(i) Single Node Availability:**

$$A_{\text{node}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{4000}{4000 + 8} = \frac{4000}{4008}$$

$$A_{\text{node}} \approx 0.998004 \approx 99.8004\%$$

**(ii) Cluster Availability (2 nodes in parallel):**

$$A_{\text{cluster}} = 1 - (1 - A_A)(1 - A_B) = 1 - \left(\frac{8}{4008}\right)^2$$

$$A_{\text{cluster}} = 1 - (0.001996)^2 = 1 - 3.984 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$A_{\text{cluster}} \approx 0.999996 = 99.9996\%$$

This corresponds to approximately **"Five Nines"** availability — telecom-grade!

**(iii) Annual Downtime:**

$$\text{Downtime/year} = (1 - A_{\text{cluster}}) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$$

$$\text{Downtime/year} = 3.984 \times 10^{-6} \times 525600$$

$$\text{Downtime/year} \approx 2.094 \text{ minutes/year}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** In numerical answers, examiners check **units** and **final rounding**. A common error is writing MTBF = 4000 hrs but using MTBF = 4000 *seconds* in the formula. Always write the unit explicitly: **MTBF = 4000 hours**. Another pitfall is forgetting to use the **2-node parallel formula** and computing only single-node availability — this loses 3 marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fault Tolerant Cluster Configurations**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Multi-node architecture with redundant components and automatic failover to mask failures transparently.

- ⭐ **Six Major Configurations:** Active/Passive, Active/Active, N+1, N+M, Cascading, Geo-Cluster.

- ⭐ **Operational Pipeline:** Detection → Verification → Fencing → Resource Migration → Notification.

- ⭐ **Heartbeat:** Periodic liveness signal (1–2 sec interval); 3–5 missed beats → node declared dead.

- ⭐ **Quorum Formula:** $N_{\text{quorum}} = \lceil (N+1)/2 \rceil$; minority partition voluntarily shuts down.

- ⭐ **Fencing:** STONITH (power off) or SCSI reservations — must happen *before* failover to prevent split-brain.

- ⭐ **Availability Formulas:**
  - Single: $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$
  - Series: $A = \prod A_i$
  - Parallel: $A = 1 - \prod (1 - A_i)$

- ⭐ **Reliability Formula:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, where $\lambda = 1/\text{MTTF}$.

- ⭐ **"Five Nines" = 99.999%** = 5.26 minutes downtime per year.

- ⭐ **2-Node Parallel Cluster** with each node at 99% availability → cluster reaches **99.99% (Four Nines)**.

- ⭐ **RPO** (Recovery Point Objective) = max acceptable data loss; **RTO** (Recovery Time Objective) = max acceptable downtime.

- ⭐ **Synchronous replication** → RPO = 0, higher latency; **Asynchronous replication** → RPO > 0, lower latency.

- ⭐ **Active/Passive** is used when data conflicts must be avoided (databases); **Active/Active** when full hardware utilization is desired (web servers).

- ⭐ **Split-brain** is the most dangerous failure mode — prevented by **quorum + fencing**, never by heartbeat alone.

- ⭐ **STONITH** = "Shoot The Other Node In The Head" — violent-sounding but absolutely essential acronym to remember for KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Checkpoint and Recovery Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Checkpoint and Recovery Techniques # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Checkpoint** is a fault-tolerance mechanism in which a process or distributed computation captures its complete execution **state** — including memory image, register context, program counter, and inter-process communication (IPC) message queues — and writes it to **stable storage** (non-volatile memory) at well-defined intervals known as *checkpoint intervals*. **Recovery** is the complementary mechanism in which, upon detecting a process or node failure, the system **rolls back** to the most recent valid checkpoint and resumes execution from that consistent state, thereby masking the failure from the application's perspective. In the context of **Advanced Computing Systems** (parallel, distributed, cloud, and high-performance computing), checkpoint-recovery is a non-negotiable reliability layer, classified under the broader umbrella of **Backward Error Recovery (BER)**. ### Key Terminology Used in KTU Syllabus - **Stable Storage** – Storage that survives processor crashes (often implemented using replication, e.g., RAID-1, mirrored disks, or NVRAM). - **Consistent Global State** – A snapshot such that for every received message, the corresponding send event is also recorded. - **Recovery Line** – The most recent consistent cut from which all processes can roll back. - **Domino Effect** – Cascading rollback that forces the system all the way back to its initial state. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 2 directly tests the student's ability to *differentiate* between coordinated, uncoordinated, and communication-induced checkpointing, and to *apply* Young's formula for optimal checkpoint interval computation. Memorize the symbol conventions: `δ` for checkpoint overhead, `M` for MTBF, and `T_r` for mean recovery time. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Video Game Save Point" Imagine you are playing an open-world video game (think *GTA V* or *Elden Ring*). - **Checkpoint ≈ Save Game**: Periodically, the game serializes your character's position, inventory, quest progress, and HP into a save file on your hard drive. - **Failure ≈ Game Crash / Death**: Your console overheats, or your character dies. - **Recovery ≈ Load Game**: Instead of replaying 20 hours from the beginning, the system reads the save file and restores your character *exactly* to where you were, plus small re-execution. - **Checkpoints Too Frequent**: You spend more time saving than playing (wasteful overhead). - **Checkpoints Too Rare**: If you crash, you lose 5 hours of progress (long rollback). - **Optimal Checkpoint Interval**: The "Goldilocks zone" — saves often enough that re-doing lost work is minimal, but not so often that you spend your life in the save menu. > [!NOTE] > **Parallelism Extension:** In single-player games there is *one* save file. In a **distributed multiplayer match** (think *PUBG* squads), you must coordinate save states across *multiple* players, otherwise a player joining late will see an inconsistent world (e.g., a dropped loot crate the squad has already picked up). This is exactly the *Consistent Global State* problem in distributed checkpointing. ## 1.3 Physical Constants, Metrics & Standard Values | Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Mean Time Between Failures | `M` (or MTBF) | 10² – 10⁶ | hours | | Checkpoint Overhead | `δ` | 1 – 60 | seconds | | Recovery Time | `T_r` | 1 – 300 | seconds | | Failure Rate | `λ = 1/M` | 10⁻⁶ – 10⁻² | per hour | | Stable Storage Write Bandwidth | `B_s` | 100 – 10000 | MB/s | ## 1.4 Why Checkpointing? — Motivation in Advanced Computing Modern HPC systems (e.g., Frontier, Fugaku) contain **millions of cores**. With so many components, the **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) shrinks dramatically**. A study by Cappello et al. (2014) shows that for exascale systems, faults are expected every **few minutes to hours** rather than weeks. Without checkpointing, a single fault would discard *days* of computation. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Checkpoint–Recovery Lifecycle (Structured Logic) The mechanism operates as a closed-loop finite state machine. Every process transitions through these states: 1. **Computation Phase**: Process executes its normal workload, modifying volatile state. 2. **Trigger Condition**: Either a *time interval* (e.g., every 60 minutes) or an *event* (e.g., every 1000 transactions) signals a checkpoint. 3. **Coordination (if any)**: For *coordinated* schemes, the initiator process broadcasts a `CHECKPOINT_REQUEST` and waits for ACKs. 4. **State Capture**: All in-flight messages in the channel buffers are flushed; the process's address space, registers, and PCB are serialized. 5. **Stable Write**: Serialized state is written to *stable storage* (typically via `fsync` or equivalent). 6. **Resume**: Process acknowledges completion (if coordinated) and resumes normal computation. 7. **Failure Detection**: Watchdog timers, heartbeats, or hardware interrupts detect a fault. 8. **Recovery Initiation**: Coordinator triggers a `RECOVERY` broadcast. 9. **Rollback**: Each failed process reads its last checkpoint, restores state, and re-executes the deterministic prefix. 10. **Re-synchronization**: Processes re-establish communication channels and resume. ## 2.2 Taxonomy of Checkpointing Techniques ### A. Independent Dimensions **Dimension 1 — Coordination Strategy:** - **Coordinated (Synchronous) Checkpointing**: All participating processes take checkpoints at the same logical time. Easier to maintain consistency; suffers from the *synchronization overhead* and the *stop-the-world* latency. - **Uncoordinated (Asynchronous) Checkpointing**: Each process decides independently. High parallelism, but vulnerable to the **Domino Effect**. - **Communication-Induced Checkpointing (CIC)**: Processes take forced (induced) checkpoints based on piggybacked information from received messages, ensuring a consistent cut is always reachable. **Dimension 2 — State Granularity:** - **Full Checkpoint**: Entire memory image is saved. Simple but **storage-intensive**. - **Incremental Checkpoint**: Only modified pages since the last checkpoint are saved. Used by systems like BLCR and DMTCP. **Dimension 3 — Storage Location:** - **Local Checkpointing**: State saved on the node's local disk. - **Centralized / Shared Checkpointing**: State saved on a parallel file system (e.g., Lustre, GPFS). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description / Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Failure Rate | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{M}$ | Failures per unit time; `M` = MTBF | | 2 | Optimal Checkpoint Interval (Young's Formula) | $T_{opt} = \sqrt{2 \cdot \delta \cdot M}$ | Minimizes total expected execution time | | 3 | Effective Execution Time per Cycle | $T_{eff} = T + \delta$ | Where `T` is work interval, `δ` is checkpoint cost | | 4 | Expected Overhead per Cycle | $E[O] = \delta + \dfrac{T_r \cdot T}{M}$ | Includes probabilistic recovery cost | | 5 | System Availability | $A = \dfrac{M}{M + T_r}$ | Fraction of uptime; KTU 2024 expects this form | | 6 | Waste Fraction (Wasted Computation) | $W = \dfrac{\delta}{T_{opt}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\delta}{2M}}$ | Optimal wasted fraction of compute time | | 7 | Number of Checkpoints in Computation | $N_c = \left\lceil \dfrac{T_{total}}{T_{opt}} \right\rceil$ | For a total wall-clock budget `T_total` | | 8 | Storage per Checkpoint (Full) | $S_c = \vert \text{AddrSpace} \vert$ | In bytes; volatile memory size | | 9 | Storage per Checkpoint (Incremental) | $S_i = \vert \Delta \text{DirtyPages} \vert$ | Smaller than `S_c`, but needs page tracking | | 10 | Recovery Point Objective | $RPO \approx T_{opt}$ | Maximum data loss tolerated | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Markdown Table Integrity Rule:** Notice the use of `\vert` (LaTeX vertical bar) instead of the raw `|` character in the storage formulas. This preserves the KTU-compliant markdown table structure. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Exascale HPC (Frontier, Aurora, Fugaku)**: The "fault wall" is the #1 research problem. Checkpointing is mandatory every 30–60 minutes. 2. **Cloud Microservices (Kubernetes)**: Container state checkpoints via **CRIU (Checkpoint/Restore In Userspace)** enable live migration and faster pod rescheduling. 3. **Database Systems (PostgreSQL, Oracle)**: **Write-Ahead Logging (WAL)** combined with periodic checkpoints implements ARIES recovery. 4. **Long-Running ML Training**: Frameworks like PyTorch DDP and TensorFlow save model snapshots — a form of application-level checkpointing. 5. **Aerospace & Embedded Systems**: RTEMS and VxWorks use checkpointing to survive radiation-induced Single Event Upsets (SEUs). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Young's Optimal Checkpoint Interval Young (1974) formulated the problem of finding the *checkpoint interval* `T` that minimizes the total expected execution time of a long-running computation, given: - A constant checkpoint overhead `δ` per checkpoint, - A Mean Time Between Failures `M`, - A mean recovery time `T_r` (includes reloading the saved state plus deterministic re-execution). ### Step 1 — Define the Cycle The system alternates between a *compute phase* of duration `T` and a *checkpoint phase* of duration `δ`. Each such pair is one **cycle**. If a failure occurs during the compute phase, the system must roll back to the start of that phase and replay the lost work, then re-take the checkpoint (additional cost `T_r`). ### Step 2 — Compute the Probability of Failure The probability that at least one failure occurs in an interval of length `T`, assuming an exponential failure distribution with rate `λ = 1/M`, is given by: $$P(\text{fail in } T) = 1 - e^{-T/M}$$ For the regime where `T ≪ M` (which is the realistic case — we checkpoint far more often than the MTBF), we use the first-order Taylor approximation: $$1 - e^{-T/M} \approx \frac{T}{M}$$ ### Step 3 — Expected Time of One Cycle The expected time consumed by one cycle, accounting for the *probabilistic* recovery cost, is the sum of the deterministic time plus the conditional recovery time: $$E[T_{cycle}] = \underbrace{T}_{\text{work}} + \underbrace{\delta}_{\text{checkpoint}} + \underbrace{\frac{T}{M} \cdot T_r}_{\text{expected recovery}}$$ This is a weighted sum: a failure (probability `T/M`) costs an additional `T_r` seconds on average. ### Step 4 — Effective Throughput (Work per Unit Time) The throughput — the *useful work* `T` delivered per unit *expected* time — is therefore: $$\text{Throughput} = \frac{T}{E[T_{cycle}]} = \frac{T}{T + \delta + \dfrac{T \cdot T_r}{M}}$$ ### Step 5 — Minimize Total Expected Time (Equivalently Maximize Throughput) To find the optimal `T`, we minimize the **denominator** (total expected time per cycle) by taking the partial derivative with respect to `T` and equating to zero: $$\frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left[T + \delta + \frac{T \cdot T_r}{M}\right] = 0$$ Differentiating term-by-term: $$\frac{\partial T}{\partial T} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial \delta}{\partial T} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial}{\partial T}\left(\frac{T \cdot T_r}{M}\right) = \frac{T_r}{M}$$ Therefore: $$1 + \frac{T_r}{M} = 0$$ This yields a *negative* solution, indicating we should *never* checkpoint — which contradicts reality. The reason is that **Young's original model ignored the recovery cost `T_r`** in the simple form. The cleaner derivation (also attributed to Gelenbe, 1979 and Daly, 2006) uses the **expected total time over `n` cycles** including inter-failure re-work. ### Step 6 — Rigorous Daly Variant (Used in Modern HPC Literature) Let the system perform `n` cycles before natural termination. The total expected time is: $$E[T_{total}] = nT + n\delta + \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot T_r$$ where `P_i` is the probability of failure in cycle `i`. Assuming each cycle has the same failure probability `P = T/M`, and that on average half the interval is lost on failure (worst case), `P_i = T/M` and the average lost work is `T/2`. The total recovery overhead then sums to: $$E[T_{total}] = nT + n\delta + n \cdot \frac{T}{M} \cdot \left(T_r + \frac{T}{2}\right)$$ ### Step 7 — Final Optimization To minimize the **per-cycle expected time** `E[T_{cycle}] = T + \delta + \dfrac{T(T_r + T/2)}{M}`: $$\frac{d E[T_{cycle}]}{dT} = 1 + \frac{T_r}{M} + \frac{T}{M} = 0 \quad \text{(still linear in T)}$$ Daly's full derivation uses a different cost model (cost per *checkpoints stored*, not per cycle) yielding the canonical **Young–Daly formula**: $$\boxed{T_{opt} = \sqrt{2 \cdot \delta \cdot M}}$$ This is the equation universally tested in KTU 2024 Module 2 questions. ### Step 3.1.1 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A parallel application has `M = 1000` hours MTBF and `δ = 1 second` per checkpoint. Compute the optimal checkpoint interval and the wasted fraction of computation. **Solution:** Step A — Unit Conversion: `δ = 1` second `= 1/3600` hours `≈ 2.778 × 10⁻⁴` hours. Step B — Apply formula: $$T_{opt} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 2.778 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 1000} = \sqrt{0.5556} \approx 0.7453 \text{ hours}$$ Step C — Convert to minutes: `T_opt ≈ 44.72 minutes`. Step D — Wasted fraction: $$W = \frac{\delta}{T_{opt}} = \frac{2.778 \times 10^{-4}}{0.7453} \approx 3.73 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0373\%$$ This means **less than 0.04%** of the total execution time is wasted on checkpointing — an excellent engineering result. ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Favorite) | Property | Coordinated | Uncoordinated | Comm.-Induced (CIC) | Message Logging | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Coordination Overhead** | High | None | Low | Low–Medium | | **Storage Overhead** | Low (one set) | High (multiple sets) | Medium | Medium | | **Domino Effect Risk** | None | High | None | None | | **Recovery Complexity** | Low | High | Medium | Medium | | **Scalability** | Poor | Excellent | Good | Good | | **Blocking** | Yes (synchronous) | No | No | No | | **Failure-Free Cost** | High | Low | Medium | Medium | | **Used In** | Chandy-Lamport impl. | BLCR | Manetho | MPI-VC, FTI | ## 3.3 Recovery Line Computation (Chandy–Lamport Perspective) The **Recovery Line** `L` of a distributed computation is the set of most recent local checkpoints, one per process, that form a **consistent cut**. Mathematically: $$L = \{ c_{i,k_i} \mid \text{for all } i \in P, \text{ and } \forall m: receive(m) \in L \Rightarrow send(m) \in L \}$$ For `n` processes each having taken `k` checkpoints, the search space for a recovery line is `O(kⁿ)`. The KTU examiner may ask you to **trace** a recovery line on a space-time diagram of message-passing events. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Checkpoint–Recovery State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Running Running --> Checkpointing : Timer fires / Event trigger Checkpointing --> CoordinationWait : Send CHECKPOINT_REQ CoordinationWait --> Checkpointing : All ACKs received Checkpointing --> StableWrite : Serialize state StableWrite --> Running : fsync complete Running --> FailureDetected : Watchdog / Heartbeat timeout FailureDetected --> Recovery : Trigger RECOVERY Recovery --> Rollback : Load last checkpoint Rollback --> Replay : Re-execute deterministic prefix Replay --> Running : Resume normal Recovery --> [*] : Unrecoverable ``` ## 4.2 Coordinated vs Uncoordinated Checkpointing (Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph COORDINATED["COORDINATED CHECKPOINTING"] C1[Coordinator Process] -->|CHECKPOINT_REQ| P1C[Process 1] C1 -->|CHECKPOINT_REQ| P2C[Process 2] C1 -->|CHECKPOINT_REQ| P3C[Process 3] P1C -->|ACK| C1 P2C -->|ACK| C1 P3C -->|ACK| C1 C1 -->|CHECKPOINT_DONE| P1C C1 -->|CHECKPOINT_DONE| P2C C1 -->|CHECKPOINT_DONE| P3C end subgraph UNCOORDINATED["UNCOORDINATED CHECKPOINTING"] P1U[Process 1] -->|Local decision| S1[Local Stable Storage] P2U[Process 2] -->|Local decision| S2[Local Stable Storage] P3U[Process 3] -->|Local decision| S3[Local Stable Storage] S1 -.->|Garbage collected| GC1[Discard old] S2 -.->|Garbage collected| GC2[Discard old] S3 -.->|Garbage collected| GC3[Discard old] end ``` ## 4.3 Domino Effect Visualization (Space-Time Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Process P1
CP 1,0] --> A2[CP 1,1] --> A3[CP 1,2] --> A4[CP 1,3] B1[Process P2
CP 2,0] --> B2[CP 2,1] --> B3[CP 2,2] --> B4[CP 2,3] C1[Process P3
CP 3,0] --> C2[CP 3,1] --> C3[CP 3,2] --> C4[CP 3,3] A2 -.send m1.-> B2 B3 -.send m2.-> C2 A4 -.send m3.-> C4 B4 -.send m4.-> A3 F[FAILURE at C4]:::fail A4 --> F B4 --> F C4 --> F classDef fail fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Each row is a process's timeline. Vertical dotted lines represent checkpoints. Arrows are messages. The failure forces rollback, and *in the worst case* cascades back to the initial state — the classic **Domino Effect**. ## 4.4 Checkpoint-Recovery Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD APP[Application Layer] --> LIB[Checkpointing Library
e.g., DMTCP, FTI, BLCR] LIB --> COORD[Coordinator Module] LIB --> STAB[Stable Storage Interface] LIB --> COMM[Communication Layer
MPI / Sockets] COORD --> STAB COORD --> COMM STAB --> LFS[Local File System
ext4, XFS] STAB --> PFS[Parallel File System
Lustre, GPFS] STAB --> NVRAM[Non-Volatile RAM
Intel Optane] COMM --> NET[Interconnect
InfiniBand, Ethernet] MON[Failure Monitor
ULM, Heartbeat] --> COORD MON --> APP classDef layer fill:#e7f5ff,stroke:#1971c2,color:#000 classDef storage fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#f08c00,color:#000 classDef fault fill:#ffe3e3,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#000 class APP,LIB,COORD,STAB,COMM layer class LFS,PFS,NVRAM storage class MON fault ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Checkpointing and Recovery. List the various types of checkpointing techniques. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Checkpointing** is the process of saving the state of a running process (or a set of processes) to stable storage at periodic intervals, so that execution can be resumed from that point after a failure. **Recovery** is the mechanism by which, after a failure, the system rolls back to the most recent valid checkpoint and resumes normal execution. **Types of Checkpointing:** 1. **Coordinated (Synchronous) Checkpointing** — All processes coordinate to take a globally consistent checkpoint. 2. **Uncoordinated (Asynchronous) Checkpointing** — Each process checkpoints independently. 3. **Communication-Induced Checkpointing (CIC)** — Forced checkpoints triggered by message-receive events. 4. **Incremental Checkpointing** — Only modified pages are saved. 5. **Full Checkpointing** — Entire memory image is saved. ### Question 2: What is the Domino Effect? How can it be prevented? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The **Domino Effect** is a cascading rollback phenomenon in uncoordinated checkpointing where, upon failure of a process, its rollback may invalidate checkpoints of other processes (because of in-flight messages), forcing *those* processes to roll back as well, which in turn invalidates *more* checkpoints, propagating all the way back to the initial state — just like a chain of falling dominoes. **Prevention Strategies:** 1. Use **Coordinated Checkpointing** to maintain a single consistent global state. 2. Use **Communication-Induced Checkpointing (CIC)** to enforce consistency via forced checkpoints. 3. Use **Message Logging** (pessimistic or optimistic) to replay lost messages without rolling back. 4. Avoid the problem entirely by **always taking a checkpoint after every message send** (impractical, but illustrates the cure). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module 2 Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` #### (a) Explain the different types of checkpointing techniques in distributed systems with suitable diagrams. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** Distributed systems employ several checkpointing strategies, each balancing **consistency**, **overhead**, and **scalability**. **1. Coordinated (Synchronous) Checkpointing:** A coordinator process initiates the checkpoint by broadcasting a `CHECKPOINT_REQUEST` to all processes. Each process flushes its in-flight messages, saves its state to stable storage, and sends an `ACK`. After collecting all ACKs, the coordinator broadcasts a `CHECKPOINT_DONE`. The result is a single **consistent global state** with no need to maintain multiple checkpoint sets. - ✅ Simple recovery, no Domino Effect. - ❌ High synchronization overhead; *stop-the-world* blocking; poor scalability. **2. Uncoordinated (Asynchronous) Checkpointing:** Each process takes checkpoints independently at its own discretion. No coordination messages are exchanged, maximizing parallelism. - ✅ No coordination overhead; highly scalable. - ❌ Susceptible to the **Domino Effect**; requires garbage collection of obsolete checkpoints; recovery is complex (must find a consistent cut from `O(kⁿ)` combinations). **3. Communication-Induced Checkpointing (CIC):** Processes piggyback protocol-level information (e.g., *Z-cycle* paths) on every message. A receiving process takes an *induced (forced)* checkpoint whenever the piggybacked info could lead to an inconsistent cut. - ✅ Avoids Domino Effect without full coordination. - ❌ Induced checkpoints may be unnecessary, increasing overhead. **4. Message Logging Based Recovery:** Instead of (or in addition to) checkpointing state, the system logs all messages. On failure, the failed process rolls back to its last checkpoint and **replays** the logged messages deterministically. - **Pessimistic Logging**: Log every message to stable storage *before* the receive completes. Survives any single failure. - **Optimistic Logging**: Log in volatile memory; flush periodically. Faster but vulnerable to multi-failure scenarios. **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | Coordinated | Uncoordinated | CIC | Message Logging | |---|---|---|---|---| | Overhead | High | Low | Medium | Medium | | Domino Effect | No | Yes | No | No | | Recovery Speed | Fast | Slow | Fast | Medium | | Scalability | Poor | Excellent | Good | Good | #### (b) Derive the optimal checkpoint interval formula. A system has an MTBF of 500 hours and a checkpoint overhead of 2 seconds. Compute the optimal checkpoint interval and the wasted fraction of computation. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — State the assumptions:** - Failure arrivals follow a Poisson process (exponential inter-failure times). - Recovery time `T_r` is absorbed into the per-interval overhead. - The system runs a long computation divided into identical cycles of work `T` + checkpoint overhead `δ`. **Step 2 — Expected time per cycle:** $$E[T_{cycle}] = T + \delta + \frac{T^2}{2M}$$ The term `T² / (2M)` arises because the expected amount of work lost when a failure occurs is approximately `T/2` (uniformly distributed within the interval), and the failure probability is `T/M`. **Step 3 — Minimize with respect to T:** $$\frac{dE[T_{cycle}]}{dT} = 1 + \frac{T}{M} = 0 \implies T = -M \quad \text{(incorrect sign)}$$ The correct form (using the standard Young–Daly model, which models the *wall-clock* cost rather than throughput) gives: $$\frac{d}{dT}\left[\frac{T + \delta}{1 - T/M}\right] = 0$$ After differentiation and solving, the canonical result is: $$T_{opt} = \sqrt{2 \cdot \delta \cdot M}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical Computation:** Given: `M = 500` hours, `δ = 2` seconds. Convert: `δ = 2 / 3600` hours `≈ 5.556 × 10⁻⁴` hours. $$T_{opt} = \sqrt{2 \times 5.556 \times 10^{-4} \times 500} = \sqrt{0.5556} \approx 0.7454 \text{ hours}$$ Converting to minutes: `T_opt ≈ 44.72 minutes`. **Step 5 — Wasted Fraction:** $$W = \frac{\delta}{T_{opt}} = \frac{5.556 \times 10^{-4}}{0.7454} \approx 7.45 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0745\%$$ **Conclusion:** The system should checkpoint approximately every **44.7 minutes**, wasting only **0.075%** of total execution time on checkpoint overhead. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### (a) With a neat diagram, explain the checkpoint-recovery mechanism in a distributed system. Discuss stable storage and the role of the initiator process. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Architecture Overview:** The mechanism involves three key actors: 1. **The Initiator/Coordinator Process** — A designated process (elected via a leader-election algorithm like Bully or Ring) that triggers and orchestrates the checkpoint. 2. **The Participating Processes** — All processes `P₁, P₂, …, Pₙ` in the distributed computation. 3. **The Stable Storage** — A fault-tolerant, non-volatile repository (typically a mirrored disk, RAID, or a parallel file system like Lustre). **Operational Sequence (Numbered):** 1. **Trigger:** The initiator fires a checkpoint either periodically or on reaching a defined event count. 2. **Broadcast:** Sends a `CHECKPOINT_REQUEST` to all participants. 3. **Local Snapshot:** Each participant: - Suspends its application-level execution. - Flushes all messages currently in transit in the network buffer (in coordinated checkpointing) — this is the *channel-state* capture. - Serializes its address space, register file, program counter, and PCB. 4. **Stable Write:** Writes the serialized state to its assigned slot in stable storage. The `fsync` call ensures the data has reached the disk platters. 5. **Acknowledgment:** Sends `CHECKPOINT_ACK` back to the initiator. 6. **Commit:** After receiving all `ACK`s, the initiator broadcasts `CHECKPOINT_DONE`, signaling participants to resume. 7. **Recovery (on failure):** The initiator detects the failure (via heartbeats), broadcasts `RECOVERY_INIT`, and each process loads its checkpoint and re-executes. **Role of Stable Storage:** Stable storage must survive the failure of any *single* node. It is implemented via: - **Hardware Mirroring** (e.g., RAID-1, RAID-6) - **Geographically Replicated** file systems (e.g., HDFS, Ceph) - **NVRAM** (Intel Optane, battery-backed RAM) The storage layer is *abstracted* via a **Stable Storage Interface** that exposes `stable_write(addr, data)` and `stable_read(addr)` primitives, both of which block until the operation is durably committed. **Diagram Reference:** (See SECTION 4.4 — Checkpoint-Recovery Block Architecture) #### (b) Explain message-logging based recovery in detail. Compare pessimistic and optimistic message logging techniques. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Concept:** In message-logging protocols, the system treats the *set of in-flight messages* as a first-class recoverable resource, in addition to (or instead of) process checkpoints. The idea is that if a process fails and rolls back to its last checkpoint, the messages it had received *since* that checkpoint can be **replayed** from the log, allowing the process to deterministically reach the same state it had before the failure. **Key Invariant (Piecewise Determinism Assumption, PDA):** The execution of a process is *piecewise deterministic* — given the same initial state and the same sequence of message deliveries, the process reaches the same final state. This makes message logging a viable recovery strategy. **Two Variants:** **1. Pessimistic (Synchronous) Logging:** - Every received message `m` is logged to **stable storage BEFORE** the application is allowed to process it. - The receive operation is delayed until `stable_write(m)` returns. - **Advantage:** Survives any single failure with no rollbacks of *other* processes. Strong guarantee. - **Disadvantage:** High latency on every message receive; severe performance penalty. Unsuited for high-throughput systems. **2. Optimistic (Asynchronous) Logging:** - Messages are logged in **volatile memory** (RAM) and flushed to stable storage periodically. - The application continues without waiting. - **Advantage:** Low runtime overhead; near-zero blocking. - **Disadvantage:** On failure, *all* logged-but-not-flushed messages are lost, forcing **other processes to roll back** to a state prior to sending those messages. A *transitive* rollback problem. **3. Causal Logging (Hybrid):** - Each message is logged in volatile memory, and the *dependency information* (which other messages this one causally depends on) is piggybacked onto outgoing acknowledgments. - On failure, only the causally-affected processes roll back, but the failure recovery is more complex. **Comparison Table:** | Property | Pessimistic | Optimistic | Causal | |---|---|---|---| | Synchronous log write? | Yes | No | No | | Stable storage dependency | Every message | Periodic flush | Periodic flush | | Output commit problem | Solved | Unsolved | Solved | | Rollback on failure | Only failed proc. | Many procs. | Few procs. | | Performance | Poor | Excellent | Good | | Used in | Manetho-P, MPICH-V | — | Manetho-C | **Use Case Mapping:** - Pessimistic → **Banking/Transaction systems** (correctness > speed) - Optimistic → **Scientific HPC** (speed > simplicity) - Causal → **Real-time distributed control** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit Mismatch in Young's Formula:** Students often plug `M` in hours and `δ` in seconds *without conversion*. The formula requires *consistent* units. The numerical question above is the most common trap. > 2. **Confusing "Domino Effect" with "Cascading Failure":** A cascading failure is a *hardware* chain (one node fails, overloads others). The Domino Effect is a *rollback* chain caused by inconsistent checkpoints. They are not the same. > 3. **Drawing Chandy–Lamport Without Labels:** The space-time diagram must label *processes* (P1, P2, P3), *checkpoints* (filled circles), and *messages* (arrows with `m1, m2…`). Bare lines lose 2 marks. > 4. **Mixing Pessimistic and Optimistic Definitions:** Pessimistic = *log before processing*. Optimistic = *log asynchronously*. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > 5. **Forgetting the "Recovery Line" Definition:** It is the *most recent* consistent cut, not just *any* consistent cut. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 1 hour before the exam.)** - [ ] **Checkpoint** = state snapshot saved to stable storage; **Recovery** = rollback to last valid state. - [ ] **Three main types**: Coordinated, Uncoordinated, Communication-Induced. - [ ] **Domino Effect** = cascading rollback to initial state; occurs in uncoordinated schemes. - [ ] **Young's Formula**: `T_opt = √(2·δ·M)` — *always* use consistent units. - [ ] **Wasted Fraction**: `W = δ / T_opt = √(δ / 2M)`. - [ ] **Availability**: `A = M / (M + T_r)`. - [ ] **Stable Storage**: Non-volatile, replicated, fault-tolerant (RAID/NVRAM/HDFS). - [ ] **Piecewise Determinism Assumption (PDA)**: Foundation of message-logging recovery. - [ ] **Pessimistic logging** = synchronous log, slow but safe. **Optimistic logging** = async log, fast but vulnerable to multi-failure. - [ ] **Recovery Line** = most recent consistent global cut. - [ ] **Channel State** = in-flight messages; must be captured for consistency. - [ ] **Chandy–Lamport Algorithm** = canonical global snapshot algorithm (uses marker messages). - [ ] **Incremental vs Full Checkpoint**: Incremental saves only dirty pages; needs page-tracking hardware/software. - [ ] **Real systems**: BLCR (Linux), DMTCP (user-space), FTI (HPC), CRIU (containers), ARIES (databases). - [ ] **In KTU 2024 Numerical Problems**: Always show (a) unit conversion, (b) formula substitution, (c) final answer in proper units. - [ ] **HPC Context**: Exascale systems have MTBF measured in *minutes* — checkpointing is no longer optional. - [ ] **Related Module 2 Topics to Revise Next**: Fault Tolerance Models (Byzantine, Fail-Stop), Replication Strategies, Consensus Protocols (Paxos, Raft). --- **End of Module 2 — Checkpoint and Recovery Techniques**

topics.title: Cluster Job and Resource Management: Job Scheduling Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cluster Job and Resource Management: Job Scheduling Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: CLUSTER JOB AND RESOURCE MANAGEMENT
## Topic: Job Scheduling Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024 – PCCST602):** This topic falls under *Cluster Resource Management* and focuses on the methodologies by which a centralized job scheduler dispatches computational jobs onto a pool of distributed compute nodes.

---

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a High-Performance Computing (HPC) cluster, a **Job** is a self-contained unit of work submitted by a user — typically a request to execute a parallel application (MPI/OpenMP program, parameter sweep, or batch script) for a specified duration, requesting a specific number of CPUs, memory, and optional accelerators (GPUs).

**Job Scheduling** is the set of policies, algorithms, and mechanisms used by the *Resource Manager / Scheduler* (e.g., **SLURM**, **Torque/Moab**, **PBS Pro**, **LSF**, **Grid Engine**) to decide:

1. **When** a pending job should be promoted from the queue to a running state.
2. **Where** (on which compute node(s)) it should be launched.
3. **How** its allocated resources (cores, memory, GPUs) should be protected, accounted for, and reclaimed upon completion or preemption.

The scheduler is a *resource-arbitration layer* sitting between user submissions and the underlying compute fabric, whose goal is to **maximize cluster utilization** while **minimizing user-perceived wait time**, subject to site-specific policies (fairness, priority, quotas).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction for KTU:** A **Resource Manager** (e.g., Slurmctld, pbs_server) handles *state tracking and policy enforcement*; a **Scheduler** is the *decision-making module* that ranks and dispatches jobs. In practice, the term "Resource Manager" is used to mean the combined system.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The Airport Air-Traffic Control Tower

Think of a cluster as a busy **international airport**:

| Airport Element | Cluster Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Runways & gates | Compute nodes, cores, memory |
| Aircraft (with passenger counts, fuel needs) | Jobs (with core count, walltime, memory requests) |
| Pilots submitting flight plans | Users submitting batch scripts |
| **Air Traffic Control Tower** | **Cluster Scheduler (Slurm/PBS)** |
| Ground-control "slots" open between two large jets | **Backfill windows** |

- A jumbo jet requesting 4 gates for 10 hours is *not* very flexible — it blocks many resources for a long time. The control tower holds it on the taxiway until all four gates are simultaneously free.
- Meanwhile, a small private jet asking for 1 gate for 30 minutes can be slotted into the *gap* between two jumbo jets without delaying either. This is **backfilling**.
- A medical-emergency aircraft gets priority over all others — this is **priority scheduling**.
- Airports divide slots between airlines based on historical slot ownership — this is **fair-share scheduling**.

The tower's job is to keep the runways busy, ensure no plane waits longer than necessary, and respect ownership/priority rules. That is *exactly* what a cluster scheduler does for compute jobs.

---

### 1.3 Why Job Scheduling is Non-Trivial

A naive **First-Come-First-Serve (FCFS)** strategy is *deadlock-prone* and *starvation-prone*:

- **Fragmentation problem:** Job J1 (needing 64 cores) sits at the head of the queue because it arrived first. Behind it are ten 1-core jobs. Until 64 cores become free simultaneously, all 10 small jobs are blocked — even though 10 cores are sitting idle. This is **resource fragmentation**.
- **Starvation problem:** A long, low-priority job at the head of the queue can be perpetually overtaken by newer high-priority jobs, delaying it indefinitely.
- **Utilization loss:** Idle nodes cost money and electricity without producing useful work.

> [!NOTE]
> Scheduling theory draws from three classical sub-fields: **Queueing Theory** (M/M/1, M/G/k models), **Operations Research** (linear programming for packing), and **Real-Time Scheduling Theory** (EDF, Rate Monotonic). The KTU examiner expects students to recognize the *queueing-theoretic* and *heuristic* flavors of these algorithms.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Scheduling Methods (Taxonomy)

```
Job Scheduling Methods
├── Non-Preemptive (Batch) Scheduling
│   ├── FCFS / FIFO
│   ├── Shortest Job First (SJF)
│   ├── Priority-Based
│   └── Backfilling
│       ├── Conservative Backfill
│       └── Aggressive (EASY) Backfill
├── Preemptive Scheduling
│   ├── Gang Scheduling (co-scheduled)
│   ├── Time-Sliced / Time-Shared
│   └── Preemption + Resume
└── Policy-Driven / Multi-Objective
    ├── Fair-Share Scheduling
    ├── Deadline / Advance Reservation
    └── Multi-Resource (CPU + GPU + Memory aware)
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 First-Come, First-Served (FCFS / FIFO)

**Operational Logic:**
1. Jobs are inserted into a strict queue in order of submission timestamp.
2. The scheduler scans the queue head-to-tail.
3. The first job whose requested resources are *fully* available is launched.
4. Subsequent jobs wait until earlier jobs complete and free enough resources.

**Why and How it works:**
- *Why simple?* No state beyond the queue; no estimation of job length is required.
- *Why flawed?* Causes **head-of-line blocking** — a single large job at the head can fragment resources indefinitely. A 128-core job in front of ten 1-core jobs blocks all ten.

**Theoretical Properties:**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Optimal for minimizing average wait time | **No** (counter-example: SPT is provably optimal) |
| Starvation | **None** (FIFO guarantees eventual service) |
| Overhead | **Lowest** (O(1) per job submission) |
| Utilization (typical) | 50–65 % on heterogeneous workloads |

---

## 2.2 Backfilling — The Workhorse of Modern HPC Schedulers

**Core Idea (operational steps):**
1. A large job J* sits at the *head* of the queue. The scheduler computes the **earliest start time** for J* (i.e., when enough resources will be free for J*).
2. The scheduler designates a **shadow time** — the earliest moment a sufficient resource block is reserved for J*.
3. Jobs *behind* J* in the queue are examined. If a job can run *entirely within the shadow window* (i.e., finish before J* is scheduled to start) without delaying J* and without violating its resource request, it is *backfilled* and launched immediately.
4. If a job cannot fit, it remains queued; the scheduler re-evaluates continuously.

**Two canonical variants:**

| Variant | Shadow Reservation Policy | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Conservative Backfill** | Reserve resources for *all* subsequent jobs that could fit in the shadow gap before backfilling them. | Higher fairness, fewer jobs backfilled, slightly lower utilization. |
| **Aggressive (EASY) Backfill** | Reserve resources *only* for the head-of-queue job J*. Backfill *any* job that fits in J*'s shadow. | Higher utilization, risk of delaying J* if backfilled jobs over-run their estimated time. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Aggressive backfill is also called **EASY** (Extensible Argonne Scheduling sYstem) backfill, after the system where it was first deployed. It is the default in **SLURM** today.

**The Why:** Backfill exploits the *gap* between a small number of long jobs and a long tail of short jobs. By dispatching short jobs into gaps, it transforms idle resources into useful work without affecting the user-perceived wait time of the head-of-queue job.

---

## 2.3 Fair-Share Scheduling

**Operational Logic:**
1. Each user (or user-group/account) is assigned an **historical share target** — e.g., a target fraction of the cluster's compute-hours over a rolling window (typically 7 or 14 days).
2. The scheduler tracks *usage* per account and *target* per account.
3. **Priority decay / boost formula:**
   $$\text{Priority}_\text{acct} = \text{BasePriority} + f\!\left(\text{Usage},\ \text{Target},\ \text{Age}\right)$$
4. Accounts that have *used less* than their target over the recent window get a **priority boost**; accounts that have *exceeded* their target get a **priority penalty**.

**The Why:** Prevents a single heavy user or a single Pl-lab from monopolizing the cluster. Allows a campus to be a *good citizen* in shared cyberinfrastructure.

**Decay Model (typical SLURM fair-share):**
- Usage is an exponentially weighted moving average:
$$U_t = \alpha \cdot \text{Usage}_{t-\Delta} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \text{NewUsage}$$

---

## 2.4 Gang Scheduling (Co-Scheduling)

**Operational Logic:**
1. A parallel MPI job is decomposed into N processes, one per requested core.
2. The scheduler ensures that *all* N processes of a job are launched **simultaneously** on N cores, often with **time-slicing**: the job's processes occupy those cores for a quantum (e.g., 100 ms), then are descheduled for another job's quantum, then resumed.
3. This produces a **time × space grid**: rows are jobs, columns are cores, and each cell is filled for a quantum. The schedule repeats cyclically.

**The Why:** Parallel jobs that perform frequent inter-process communication suffer a **2–10× slowdown** if their peers are descheduled mid-communication (a phenomenon called **MPI noise** or **co-scheduling degradation**). Gang scheduling eliminates this by ensuring all peers are context-switched together.

**Trade-off:** Reduces effective memory per job (no swapping of idle pages) and increases context-switch overhead.

---

## 2.5 Priority-Based Scheduling

**Operational Logic:** Each job is tagged with a priority (multi-factor: account, queue, job-size, job-age, QoS, partition). At every scheduling tick, the scheduler picks the highest-priority runnable job.

**The Why:** Reflects organizational value. A faculty job may be in a higher queue; a paying industry partner's job may be in a premium QoS.

**Risk:** A long-running low-priority job can suffer **starvation** unless the policy includes an **aging mechanism** (priority increases linearly with queue-time).

---

## 2.6 Advance Reservation & Deadline Scheduling

- **Advance Reservation:** User *reserves* a specific time-window in the future on a specific set of nodes. The scheduler treats the reservation as a *committed contract* and refuses to schedule other jobs that would overlap it. Used for production workloads, demos, and large-scale campaigns.
- **Deadline Scheduling:** User specifies a *latest acceptable completion time*. Scheduler either guarantees completion or rejects the job. Implemented in Maui/Moab and (in part) Slurm.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table for Part A 3-mark questions and 7-mark sub-parts.** It contains the *exact* metrics and formulas examiners expect students to reproduce.

| # | Metric / Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Response Time** | $T_r = T_\text{completion} - T_\text{arrival}$ | All in seconds | Measures scheduling latency |
| 2 | **Turnaround Time** | $T_\text{ta} = T_\text{completion} - T_\text{submission}$ | Seconds | Includes queue wait |
| 3 | **Wait Time** | $T_w = T_\text{ta} - T_\text{execution}$ | Seconds | Pure queueing time |
| 4 | **Bounded Slowdown** | $\text{BSD} = \max\!\left(\dfrac{T_w}{T_\text{exec}}, 10\right)$ | Dimensionless | Penalizes short jobs that wait |
| 5 | **Throughput** | $\Theta = \dfrac{N_\text{completed}}{\Delta t}$ | Jobs / hour | Cluster productivity |
| 6 | **Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{T_\text{busy}}{T_\text{available}} \times 100\%$ | Percent | Resource efficiency |
| 7 | **Little's Law** | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | $L$ = avg jobs in system, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $W$ = avg time in system | Queueing analysis |
| 8 | **Fair-Share Ratio** | $r = \dfrac{\text{Usage}}{\text{Target}}$ | Dimensionless | $r \ll 1$ ⇒ boost; $r \gg 1$ ⇒ penalty |
| 9 | **EASY Shadow** | $t_\text{shadow} = t_\text{now} + \text{EarliestStart}(\text{HeadJob})$ | Seconds | Backfill upper bound |
| 10 | **Gang Quantum** | $Q_\text{slice} = \dfrac{T_\text{context\_switch}}{N_\text{context\_switches}}$ | Seconds | Per-job time per cycle |
| 11 | **Aging Boost** | $P_\text{age} = P_0 + k \cdot T_w$ | Dimensionless | Anti-starvation mechanism |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are not academic. Real cluster operators (NCSA, TACC, ORNL) publish **monthly utilization reports** using equations (5), (6), and (7). The "Top 500" Linpack machine list is essentially a measure of aggregate throughput. Production schedulers log all of these per-job for billing and capacity planning.

---

## 2.8 Comparative Analysis of Methods

| Method | Avg. Wait Time | Utilization | Fairness | Starvation Risk | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FCFS** | High | Low–Med | Excellent (FIFO) | None | **O(1)** |
| **SJF (Shortest Job First)** | **Provably optimal** | High | Poor (large jobs suffer) | High for large jobs | Requires runtime estimate |
| **Priority (no aging)** | Low for high-pri | Med | Poor | **Yes** for low-pri | O(log N) with heap |
| **EASY Backfill** | Low | **High (75–90 %)** | Good | Low | O(N) per tick |
| **Conservative Backfill** | Med | High (70–85 %) | **Very Good** | Very Low | O(N²) per tick |
| **Fair-Share** | Med | Med | Excellent | **None** | O(N·A) per tick |
| **Gang Scheduling** | Low for parallel | Med (context-switch cost) | Good | None | **O(N·M)** |

Here $N$ = queued jobs, $A$ = active accounts, $M$ = cores.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict Prohibition Notice:** No step is skipped. Every algebraic transition is followed by a textual explanation of the *logic* behind the transformation.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Little's Law Applied to the Cluster Queue

**Claim:** For a stable cluster job queue (steady-state arrival rate $\lambda$ and steady-state mean response time $W$), the *mean number of jobs in the system* $L$ obeys
$$L = \lambda \cdot W$$

**Proof (renewal-theoretic / measure-theoretic sketch — KTU depth):**

**Step 1 — Define cumulative arrival and departure functions.**
Let $A(t)$ be the total number of jobs that have arrived in $[0, t]$.
Let $D(t)$ be the total number of jobs that have departed in $[0, t]$.
Define the *number in system* at time $t$ as
$$N(t) = A(t) - D(t)$$

**Step 2 — Define the time-average and arrival-rate limits.**
The *arrival rate* (jobs per unit time) in steady state is
$$\lambda = \lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{A(t)}{t}$$

The *time-average number in system* is
$$L = \lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{1}{t}\int_0^t N(\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 3 — Define the per-job time in system and the customer-average.**
For the $i$-th job, let $W_i$ be its time spent in the system. The *customer-average response time* is
$$W = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i$$

**Step 4 — Express the integral of $N(t)$ as a sum of $W_i$ intervals.**
Consider the function $N(t)$ in the interval $[0, T]$ where $A(T) = n$ jobs have arrived. Each of the $n$ jobs contributes a "rectangle" of height 1 and width $W_i$ to the area under $N(t)$ — namely, the rectangle from its arrival instant to its departure instant. So
$$\int_0^T N(\tau)\,d\tau = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $T$ and take limits.**
$$L = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T N(\tau)\,d\tau = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T}\sum_{i=1}^{n(T)} W_i$$

**Step 6 — Multiply and divide by $n(T)$.**
$$L = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{n(T)}{T} \cdot \frac{1}{n(T)}\sum_{i=1}^{n(T)} W_i = \lambda \cdot W \quad \blacksquare$$

**Practical KTU Application Example:**

> A cluster receives jobs at a sustained rate of **8 jobs/min**. The scheduler maintains a mean response time of **6 minutes**. Compute the mean number of jobs in the system (running + queued).

*Step-by-step:*
- $\lambda = 8 \text{ jobs/min}$
- $W = 6 \text{ min}$
- By Little's Law:
$$L = \lambda \cdot W = 8 \times 6 = 48 \text{ jobs}$$

Therefore, on average, **48 jobs** are simultaneously either running on the cluster or sitting in the queue. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bounded Slowdown Metric

**Why it exists:** Plain *wait time* $T_w$ unfairly penalizes short jobs (a 30-second job waiting 5 minutes is "killed"), while plain *response time* $T_r$ over-rewards long jobs. The HPC community adopted **Bounded Slowdown**:
$$\text{BS} = \max\!\left(\frac{T_w}{\max(T_\text{exec},\, \text{RunThreshold})},\, 1\right)$$

**Step-by-step derivation of the standard form:**

**Step 1** — Start from the *slowdown* definition (response time divided by service time):
$$S = \frac{T_r}{T_\text{exec}} = \frac{T_w + T_\text{exec}}{T_\text{exec}} = 1 + \frac{T_w}{T_\text{exec}}$$

**Step 2** — Identify the *singularity problem*: when $T_\text{exec} \to 0$ (an instant job), $S \to \infty$ even for a 1-second wait. To avoid this, the *bounded* version caps the denominator:
$$T_\text{exec}^\text{bounded} = \max(T_\text{exec},\, T_\text{min})$$

The standard *KTU/SLURM convention* is $T_\text{min} = 10$ seconds (some sources use 60 s).

**Step 3** — Substitute:
$$\text{BS} = 1 + \frac{T_w}{\max(T_\text{exec},\, 10)}$$

or equivalently
$$\text{BS} = \frac{T_w + \max(T_\text{exec},\, 10)}{\max(T_\text{exec},\, 10)}$$

**Step 4** — Some authors subtract 1 and take the max with 0:
$$\text{BS}_\text{alt} = \max\!\left(\frac{T_w}{\max(T_\text{exec},\, 10)},\ 0\right)$$

**Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style):**
- A job requests $T_\text{exec} = 5$ s, actually waits $T_w = 90$ s, runs for 5 s.
- Plain slowdown: $S = 1 + 90/5 = 19$
- Bounded slowdown (using $T_\text{min}=10$):
$$\text{BS} = 1 + \frac{90}{\max(5,\,10)} = 1 + 9 = 10$$

A 90-second wait on a 5-second job is capped at $\text{BS}=10$, reflecting "moderately annoying but not catastrophic." $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Conservative vs Aggressive Backfill — Shadow Time Computation

**Scenario (small, exam-style):** Cluster has 100 cores. Currently running:
- Job A: 30 cores, finishes at $t = 30$ min
- Job B: 40 cores, finishes at $t = 60$ min
- Job C: 20 cores, finishes at $t = 90$ min

Pending jobs (in FCFS order):
- **J1 (head-of-queue):** needs 60 cores, runtime = 40 min
- **J2:** needs 10 cores, runtime = 15 min
- **J3:** needs 25 cores, runtime = 20 min
- **J4:** needs 30 cores, runtime = 35 min

**Step 1 — Compute free cores at each future time.**

- At $t = 0$ (now): total busy = $30+40+20 = 90$, free = **10 cores**.
- At $t = 30$ (after A finishes): busy = $40+20 = 60$, free = **40 cores**.
- At $t = 60$ (after B finishes): busy = $20$, free = **80 cores**.
- At $t = 90$ (after C finishes): busy = $0$, free = **100 cores**.

**Step 2 — Compute earliest start time for J1 (head-of-queue).**
J1 needs 60 cores. We need 60 cores free at some $t$. From the table above:
- 60 cores are free starting at $t = 60$ (when B finishes), and remain free thereafter.
- So $T_\text{earliest}^\text{J1} = 60$ min.
- Earliest completion: $T_\text{earliest}^\text{J1} + 40 = 100$ min.

**Step 3 — Compute the EASY shadow time.**
$$\text{Shadow} = T_\text{earliest}^\text{J1} = 60 \text{ min}$$
The system reserves 60 cores continuously from $t=0$ to $t=60$ for J1 (in aggressive EASY), so no other job may use those 60 cores for that window.

**Step 4 — Examine J2 (10 cores, 15 min) for backfill.**

*Constraint check (EASY aggressive):* J2 must fit in the shadow window.
- J2 needs 10 cores, 15 min.
- Is there a 10-core, 15-min gap before $t=60$ that does not intersect the 60-core reservation for J1?

Available windows:
- $[0, 30]$: 10 cores free, window length = 30 min. J2 needs 15 min. **Fits!** ✓

So J2 is **backfilled** to run from $t = 0$ to $t = 15$.

**Step 5 — Examine J3 (25 cores, 20 min) for backfill.**

*Aggressive EASY check:* J3 needs 25 cores for 20 min before $t=60$, without delaying J1.
- Available 25-core windows before $t=60$?
- $[0, 30]$: 10 cores only. ✗
- $[30, 60]$: 40 cores free, length 30 min. 25 cores × 20 min **fits!** ✓

So J3 is **backfilled** to run from $t = 30$ to $t = 50$.

**Step 6 — Examine J4 (30 cores, 35 min) for backfill.**

*Aggressive EASY check:* J4 needs 30 cores for 35 min before $t=60$.
- $[0, 30]$: 10 cores only. ✗
- $[30, 60]$: 40 cores, length 30 min. 30 cores × 35 min — **does not fit** because the window is only 30 min long. ✗

J4 remains queued. (In **conservative** backfill, we would have additionally reserved resources for J2 and J3, narrowing the window — so J4 would *also* fail there, same outcome in this example.)

**Final EASY Backfill Schedule:**

| Time (min) | Running jobs | Cores used | Free |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0–15 | A(30) + B(40) + C(20) + J2(10) | 100 | 0 |
| 15–30 | A(30) + B(40) + C(20) | 90 | 10 |
| 30–50 | B(40) + C(20) + J3(25) | 85 | 15 |
| 50–60 | B(40) + C(20) | 60 | **40 (reserved for J1)** |
| 60–100 | J1(60) | 60 | 40 |
| 60–90 | (whatever fills 40 cores from J4 etc.) | — | — |

**Key Insight:** Without backfill, the 10 free cores at $t=0$ would be **idle for 30 minutes**, wasting 300 core-minutes. With EASY backfill, J2 (150 core-minutes) and J3 (500 core-minutes) execute in the gaps. **Total recovered work = 650 core-minutes.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Fair-Share Priority Decay

**Setup:** Account "ResearchLabA" has a target share of 25 % of cluster compute-time over a 14-day window. In the last 7 days it consumed 35 % of compute-time. Compute the *usage ratio* and the resulting priority adjustment.

**Step 1 — Compute raw usage ratio.**
$$r = \frac{\text{Usage}}{\text{Target}} = \frac{35\%}{25\%} = 1.4$$

**Step 2 — Apply the standard fair-share penalty function (SLURM-style).**
The priority adjustment is often:
$$\Delta P = -K \cdot \log_2(r)$$

with $K = 1000$ (typical SLURM configuration).

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**
$$\Delta P = -1000 \cdot \log_2(1.4) = -1000 \cdot 0.4854 \approx -485 \text{ priority units}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** ResearchLabA loses 485 priority points; its jobs will sit behind those of under-served accounts until its usage ratio drops below 1.0 (i.e., until its usage falls back to 25 % or below over the rolling window). $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Inverse case (under-served account):** If Usage = 15 % and Target = 25 %, then $r = 0.6$, and $\log_2(0.6) = -0.737$, giving $\Delta P = +737$. The account's jobs are *boosted* ahead of the queue, accelerating their start.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem: Utilization on a 4-Node Cluster

**Problem:** A 4-node cluster, each node 8 cores, is observed for 24 hours. The total *busy-core-seconds* reported by the scheduler is $5.2 \times 10^5$ core-seconds. Compute the *utilization*.

**Step 1 — Compute total available core-seconds.**
$$T_\text{available} = 4 \text{ nodes} \times 8 \text{ cores/node} \times 24 \times 3600 \text{ s} = 32 \times 86400 = 2{,}764{,}800 \text{ core-s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute utilization.**
$$U = \frac{T_\text{busy}}{T_\text{available}} \times 100\% = \frac{5.2 \times 10^5}{2.7648 \times 10^6} \times 100\%$$

$$U = 0.1881 \times 100\% \approx 18.81\%$$

The cluster is severely under-utilized (typical well-tuned HPC clusters run at 75–90 % utilization). $\blacksquare$

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Cluster Resource Manager Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UserA[User A submits job] -->|sbatch script| RM[Resource Manager<br>Slurmctld or pbs_server]
    UserB[User B submits job] -->|sbatch script| RM
    UserC[User C submits job] -->|sbatch script| RM
    RM --> Q[Priority Queue + Job Database]
    Q --> Sched[Scheduling Engine]
    Sched -->|FCFS / Backfill / Fair-Share| Disp[Dispatcher]
    Sched -.reads.-> Pol[Cluster Policy Module]
    Sched -.reads.-> FS[Fair-Share State DB]
    Pol --> Sched
    FS --> Sched
    Disp -->|SSH / PMIx| Node1[Compute Node 1]
    Disp -->|SSH / PMIx| Node2[Compute Node 2]
    Disp -->|SSH / PMIx| Node3[Compute Node N]
    Node1 -->|Munge / job completion| RM
    Node2 -->|Munge / job completion| RM
    Node3 -->|Munge / job completion| RM
    RM --> Acct[Accounting Logger]
    Acct --> DB[(Job Accounting DB)]
```

---

## 4.2 Flowchart: EASY Aggressive Backfill Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Scheduling Tick]) --> H[Identify Head-of-Queue Job Jstar]
    H --> Calc[Compute Earliest Start of Jstar<br>using reservation table]
    Calc --> Shadow[Define Shadow Window<br>from now to Tstar]
    Shadow --> Loop{For each queued job J after Jstar}
    Loop -->|yes| Fit{Does J fit in Shadow<br>without exceeding free cores<br>and finishing before Tstar?}
    Fit -->|yes| Launch[Backfill: Launch J immediately]
    Fit -->|no| Skip[Keep J in queue]
    Launch --> Next[Advance to next job]
    Skip --> Next
    Next --> Loop
    Loop -->|no more jobs| End([Wait for next tick])
```

---

## 4.3 Timeline Diagram: Gang Scheduling Time-Space Grid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TG[Gang Scheduling Time x Core Grid]
        direction LR
        Q1["Quantum 1: 0-100ms"] --> Q2["Quantum 2: 100-200ms"] --> Q3["Quantum 3: 200-300ms"]
    end
    subgraph JA["Job A: needs 4 cores"]
        A1["Core1-A<br>Core2-A<br>Core3-A<br>Core4-A"] -.-> A2["Core1-A<br>Core2-A<br>Core3-A<br>Core4-A"] -.-> A3["Core1-A<br>Core2-A<br>Core3-A<br>Core4-A"]
    end
    subgraph JB["Job B: needs 2 cores"]
        B1["Core1-B<br>Core2-B"] -.-> B2["Idle<br>Idle"] -.-> B3["Core1-B<br>Core2-B"]
    end
    subgraph JC["Job C: needs 3 cores"]
        C1["Idle"] -.-> C2["Core1-C<br>Core2-C<br>Core3-C"] -.-> C3["Idle"]
    end
    TG --> JA
    TG --> JB
    TG --> JC
```

> **Reading the Grid:** In Quantum 1, Job A and Job B are co-scheduled; in Quantum 2, A and C are co-scheduled; in Quantum 3, A and B are again co-scheduled. Job A's 4 processes are *always* running together — eliminating MPI noise. Jobs B and C share the remaining cores via time-slicing.

---

## 4.4 Architecture: Policy-Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN[Job Arrives]
        JQ[Job submitted to queue]
    end
    JQ --> P1{Is QoS = premium?}
    P1 -->|yes| High[High priority lane]
    P1 -->|no| P2{Account over-served?}
    P2 -->|yes| Pen[Penalty applied]
    P2 -->|no| P3{Age over threshold?}
    P3 -->|yes| Boost[Aging boost]
    P3 -->|no| Base[Base priority]
    Pen --> Score[Final Priority Score]
    Boost --> Score
    Base --> Score
    High --> Score
    Score --> Wait[Insert into heap]
    Wait --> Tick[Next Scheduling Tick]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`
**Define job scheduling in a cluster. List any four job scheduling policies used in HPC resource managers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):** Job scheduling is the process by which a cluster resource manager decides *when* and *where* (on which compute nodes) to launch user-submitted jobs from a pending queue, subject to resource requests, priority rules, and fairness policies.

**Four policies (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **First-Come, First-Served (FCFS / FIFO)** — strict submission-order queue.
2. **Backfilling (EASY / Conservative)** — runs smaller jobs in resource gaps left by the head-of-queue job.
3. **Fair-Share Scheduling** — distributes compute resources proportionally to accounts based on historical usage vs target.
4. **Priority Scheduling** — ranks jobs by a composite priority score; high-priority jobs run first (often with an aging anti-starvation mechanism).

*(Other acceptable policies: Gang Scheduling, Deadline / Advance Reservation, Shortest Job First.)*

---

### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Series 1]`
**What is the difference between conservative and aggressive (EASY) backfilling? Which is used in SLURM by default?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Conservative backfill (1.5 marks):** Reserves resources for *all* queued jobs that could fit in the shadow window, ensuring *no* job's estimated start is delayed. It is *safer* and *more fair*, but yields slightly lower utilization.
- **Aggressive (EASY) backfill (1 mark):** Reserves resources *only* for the head-of-queue job J*. Any other job that can run in the remaining gap without delaying J* is launched immediately. Higher utilization, but if a backfilled job over-runs its requested time, it may delay J*.
- **SLURM default (0.5 mark):** Aggressive (EASY) backfill.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**Compare and contrast the major cluster job scheduling methods. Discuss FCFS, Backfilling, Fair-Share, and Gang Scheduling with their operational logic, advantages, limitations, and typical use-cases.**

**OR**

### **Question B (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Module 2]`
**(a)** Explain the architecture of a cluster resource manager. Describe the role of the scheduler, dispatcher, and accounting modules with the help of a block diagram.
**(b)** A cluster has 64 cores. Currently running: Job X (16 cores, 30 min remaining) and Job Y (24 cores, 60 min remaining). Pending FCFS queue: J1 (40 cores, 50 min), J2 (12 cores, 10 min), J3 (20 cores, 15 min). Apply EASY backfill and produce the final schedule.

---

### **Solution to Question A**

#### (a) FCFS / FIFO Scheduling — 3.5 marks

- **Operational logic:** Jobs are dispatched in strict submission order. The first job whose requested resources are available is launched; others wait.
- **Advantages:** Simple, no estimation needed, no starvation, predictable.
- **Limitations:** Head-of-line blocking causes *resource fragmentation*; one large job at the head can idle many cores; poor average wait time.
- **Use case:** Small clusters with low contention or jobs of similar size.

#### (b) Backfilling (EASY and Conservative) — 3.5 marks

- **Operational logic:** Head-of-queue job J* is reserved a shadow window. Smaller jobs that can run *entirely within* the shadow without delaying J* are dispatched immediately.
- **EASY / Aggressive:** Reserves only for J*. **Conservative:** Reserves for all jobs in queue.
- **Advantages:** Dramatically improves utilization (75–90 % vs 50–65 % for FCFS); preserves fairness for J*; does not require accurate job-size estimates for J*.
- **Limitations:** Requires *user-provided* runtime estimates (bad estimates can cause delays); slightly more complex implementation.
- **Use case:** Mainstream HPC clusters; default in SLURM, PBS Pro, LSF.

#### (c) Fair-Share Scheduling — 3.5 marks

- **Operational logic:** Each account has a target share. The scheduler penalizes over-served accounts (priority decrement via $\Delta P = -K \log_2(r)$) and boosts under-served accounts. Priority is recomputed at every scheduling tick.
- **Advantages:** Long-term fairness; prevents single-account monopolization; aligns with funding-agency policies.
- **Limitations:** Short-term user-perceived wait can be longer for heavy users; requires careful tuning of $K$ and decay window.
- **Use case:** Shared national / campus clusters; multi-tenant clouds.

#### (d) Gang Scheduling — 3.5 marks

- **Operational logic:** All processes of a parallel job are co-scheduled onto cores in lock-step time-slices. The cluster maintains a time × space grid; each row of the grid is a job, each cell is filled for a quantum.
- **Advantages:** Eliminates *co-scheduling degradation* / MPI noise; predictable performance for tightly coupled parallel codes.
- **Limitations:** Higher context-switch overhead; memory pressure (idle processes still occupy memory); complex implementation.
- **Use case:** Clusters running MPI/OpenMP jobs with frequent inter-process communication; HPC centers serving scientific simulation workloads.

---

### **Solution to Question B**

#### (a) Cluster Resource Manager Architecture — 7 marks

A modern resource manager (e.g., **SLURM**) consists of three logical modules working in concert:

1. **Controller / Daemon (`slurmctld`)** — A centralized fault-tolerant process that:
   - Maintains the global state of the cluster (node status, job queue, reservations).
   - Receives job submissions via the `sbatch`/`squeue` API.
   - Invokes the **scheduler** to make placement decisions.
   - Pushes launch instructions to the **slurmd** daemons on compute nodes.

2. **Scheduler Module** — A pluggable algorithm implementing the chosen policy (FCFS, EASY backfill, fair-share, etc.). It runs in the same process as the controller and produces a *schedule plan* every scheduling interval (typically 1–60 s).

3. **Dispatcher / Compute Daemon (`slurmd`)** — One process per compute node. It:
   - Receives the launch plan from the controller.
   - Uses **PMIx / srun** to fork MPI/thread processes on its cores.
   - Tracks local resource consumption and forwards to the controller.
   - On job completion, returns exit status and accounting records.

4. **Accounting / Database Module** — Persists per-job records (submit/start/end times, cores used, user, account, energy consumed) to a database (typically MySQL or SQLite). Used for billing, fairness recalculation, and reporting.

```
[User]  --(sbatch)-->  [slurmctld Controller]
                            |
                            v
                       [Scheduler: EASY/FCFS/Fair-Share]
                            |
                            v
                       [slurmctld Dispatcher]
                       /        |        \
                      v         v         v
                [slurmd@N1] [slurmd@N2] [slurmd@N3]
                (job runs locally; results returned to controller)
                            |
                            v
                    [Accounting DB]
```

*(Block-diagram description: 4 marks; Module explanations: 3 marks.)*

#### (b) EASY Backfill Problem — 7 marks

**Given:**
- 64-core cluster.
- Running: **Job X** (16 cores, 30 min remaining), **Job Y** (24 cores, 60 min remaining).
- Pending FCFS: **J1** (40 cores, 50 min), **J2** (12 cores, 10 min), **J3** (20 cores, 15 min).

**Step 1 — Compute free cores at each future time instant.**

- $t=0$ (now): busy $= 16+24 = 40$, free $= 64-40 = \mathbf{24}$ cores.
- $t=30$ (after X ends): busy $= 24$, free $= \mathbf{40}$ cores.
- $t=60$ (after Y ends): busy $= 0$, free $= \mathbf{64}$ cores.

**Step 2 — Compute earliest start for J1 (head-of-queue).**
J1 needs 40 cores. Looking at the free-core timeline, 40 cores become free at $t=30$ and stay free thereafter.
$$T_\text{earliest}^\text{J1} = 30 \text{ min}, \quad T_\text{end}^\text{J1} = 80 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Define the EASY shadow window.**
$$\text{Shadow} = [0,\ 30] \text{ min}$$
The system reserves 40 cores continuously over $[0, 30]$ for J1. Effective available slots:

| Sub-window | Free cores (after reservation) | Length |
|---|---|---|
| $[0, 30]$ | $24 - 0 = 24$ | 30 min |
| $[30, 60]$ | $40 - 0 = 40$ (Y still running) | 30 min |

**Step 4 — Examine J2 (12 cores, 10 min) for backfill.**
- J2 needs 12 cores for 10 min within the shadow $[0, 30]$.
- $[0, 30]$ window has 24 cores free, 30 min long. 12 cores × 10 min **fits** ✓
- **Backfill J2** to run $t=0 \to 10$. *(1.5 marks)*

**Step 5 — Examine J3 (20 cores, 15 min) for backfill.**
- J3 needs 20 cores for 15 min within $[0, 30]$.
- After dispatching J2 at $t=0$–$10$, free cores in $[0, 10]$ drop to $24 - 12 = 12$ cores — insufficient for J3's 20 cores.
- Check $[10, 30]$: free cores = 24 (J2 has finished). 20 cores × 15 min **fits** ✓
- **Backfill J3** to run $t=10 \to 25$. *(1.5 marks)*

**Step 6 — Final Schedule (2 marks).**

| Time (min) | Running jobs | Cores used | Free |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0–10 | X(16) + Y(24) + J2(12) | 52 | 12 (reserved 0) |
| 10–25 | X(16) + Y(24) + J3(20) | 60 | 4 (reserved 0) |
| 25–30 | X(16) + Y(24) | 40 | 24 (reserved 0) |
| 30–60 | Y(24) + **J1(40)** | 64 | 0 |
| 60–80 | **J1(40)** | 40 | 24 |
| 80+ | idle (or new jobs) | — | 64 |

**Result:** J2 and J3 were executed in gaps that would otherwise have been idle, recovering $(12 \times 10) + (20 \times 15) = 120 + 300 = \mathbf{420}$ core-minutes of useful work. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones**
>
> 1. **Confusing "scheduler" with "resource manager".** Marks are awarded for *separately* identifying the *controller*, the *scheduler*, and the *dispatcher*. A vague answer that says "Slurm does scheduling" loses 2 of 7 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute the shadow time** when applying backfill. Many students begin backfilling immediately and forget the 40-core reservation for J1, leading to incorrect placement of J2/J3.
> 3. **Ignoring that J2 finishes at t=10** and freeing cores for J3's later dispatch. Step-by-step re-evaluation is mandatory after every backfill.
