# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define virtualization. Explain the different levels at which virtualization can be implemented with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Virtualization is the technology that abstracts physical computing resources into logical/virtual representations, enabling a single physical resource to appear as multiple independent resources to users, applications, or guest operating systems.

**Levels of Virtualization (2 Marks):**
1. **ISA Level:** Virtualization at the instruction set boundary via emulation. Example: QEMU.
2. **Hardware Abstraction Level:** Virtualization at the hardware interface using a hypervisor. Example: VMware ESXi.
3. **OS Level:** Virtualization at the kernel level using containers. Example: Docker.
4. **Server Level:** Virtualization of the entire server via a Type 1 hypervisor. Example: Citrix XenServer.
5. **Application Level:** Virtualization at the application runtime layer. Example: JVM, Docker images.

---

### ❓ Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and briefly explain the Popek-Goldberg virtualization requirements.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Popek-Goldberg theorem defines three essential requirements for a VMM:

1. **Efficiency (1 Mark):** All innocuous instructions must execute natively on the hardware without VMM intervention, ensuring near-native performance.

2. **Resource Control (1 Mark):** The VMM must be the sole controller of all physical resources (CPU, memory, I/O). No guest VM can directly access resources not allocated to it, ensuring isolation and security.

3. **Equivalence (1 Mark):** Any program running inside a VM must produce identical results to running on the real hardware, enabling unmodified guest software portability.

**Combined with the condition $S \subseteq P$**, these requirements define a properly virtualized system.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### ✏️ Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

#### **(a)** Explain in detail the **five levels of virtualization** with neat diagrams and suitable examples. Compare them based on performance, isolation, and overhead. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Virtualization can be implemented at different layers of the system stack, each with distinct characteristics. The five levels (from lowest to highest abstraction) are:

**1. ISA Level Virtualization (1 Mark):**
- Virtualizes at the instruction set boundary.
- Translates/emulates each guest instruction into host instructions.
- Methods: Interpretation, Binary Translation.
- Examples: QEMU, Bochs, Transmeta Crusoe.
- Overhead: **10×–1000×**; used for cross-architecture emulation.

**2. Hardware Abstraction Level Virtualization (1 Mark):**
- Virtualizes at the hardware interface — exposes vCPU, vRAM, vDisk, vNIC.
- Uses **trap-and-emulate** or **binary translation**.
- Examples: VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V.
- Overhead: **~2–10%** with Intel VT-x/AMD-V.

**3. OS Level Virtualization (1 Mark):**
- Implemented by the host OS kernel; creates isolated user-space instances (containers).
- All containers share the host kernel; only namespaces differ.
- Examples: Docker, LXC, Solaris Zones.
- Overhead: **~1–3%**; near-native.

**4. Server Level Virtualization (1 Mark):**
- Partitions a physical server into multiple virtual servers, each running independent OS.
- Uses Type 1 or Type 2 hypervisor.
- Examples: VMware vSphere, Microsoft Hyper-V.
- Overhead: **~3–10%**.

**5. Application Level Virtualization (1 Mark):**
- Packages applications with their dependencies into portable units.
- Uses runtime engines (JVM, Docker Engine, WASM).
- Examples: Docker Images, Java JARs, Electron apps.
- Overhead: **~0–30%**.

**Comparison Table (1 Mark):**

| Level | Overhead | Isolation | Example |
|-------|----------|-----------|---------|
| ISA | Very High | High | QEMU |
| HAL | Low | Very High | VMware |
| OS | Very Low | Medium | Docker |
| Server | Low | Very High | Hyper-V |
| App | Low–Medium | Low–Medium | JVM |

---

#### **(b)** Define a **Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM)**. Explain the **Popek-Goldberg virtualization theorem** in detail with the formal condition $S \subseteq P$. Justify why the original x86 architecture fails this condition and how it was overcome. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**VMM Definition (1 Mark):**
A Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM), or hypervisor, is a software/firmware layer that creates, manages, and monitors virtual machines, providing each guest with an isolated execution environment that mirrors real hardware.

**Popek-Goldberg Theorem (3 Marks):**

Let:
- $P$ = Set of all **privileged instructions** (trap automatically when executed in user mode)
- $S$ = Set of **sensitive instructions** (behavior differs in user mode vs. kernel mode)

**Theorem:**
$$\boxed{S \subseteq P \iff \text{Architecture is classically virtualizable}}$$

**Justification:**
- Every sensitive instruction must trap when the guest runs in user mode.
- The only mechanism for automatic trapping is privilege-level violation.
- Therefore, every sensitive instruction must be privileged → $S \subseteq P$.
- If $S \not\subseteq P$, some sensitive instructions execute silently in user mode, allowing the guest to detect the VMM (violating Equivalence).

**Why x86 Fails (2 Marks):**

The original x86 (pre-VT-x) has **17 sensitive instructions**, of which some are NOT privileged. Examples:
- `SIDT` (Store Interrupt Descriptor Table) — sensitive but NOT privileged
- `SGDT`, `SMSW`, `PUSHF`, `POPF` — same category

Since these instructions execute in user mode without trapping, a guest OS can read/modify CPU state directly, bypassing the VMM. Hence $S_{x86} \not\subseteq P_{x86}$, and x86 is **not classically virtualizable**.

**Solutions Overcome This (1 Mark):**

1. **Binary Translation (VMware's approach):** Sensitive non-privileged instructions are dynamically translated into safe instruction sequences at load time.
2. **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization (Intel VT-x, AMD-V):** Introduced new CPU execution modes (VMX root/non-root), effectively making $S \subseteq P$ again, enabling classical trap-and-emulate.

---

### ✏️ Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

#### **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of **Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisors**. Compare them on at least five parameters. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Type 1 Hypervisor (Bare-Metal / Native) (2 Marks):**
- Runs directly on the physical hardware without a host OS.
- The VMM itself is the OS (or a thin microkernel).
- Examples: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V (core), Citrix XenServer.
- **Architecture:** Guest VMs → VMM → Hardware.

**Type 2 Hypervisor (Hosted) (2 Marks):**
- Runs as an application on top of a conventional host OS (Windows, Linux, macOS).
- The host OS manages physical hardware; the VMM emulates VMs.
- Examples: VMware Workstation, Oracle VirtualBox, Parallels.
- **Architecture:** Guest VMs → VMM → Host OS → Hardware.

**Comparison (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Type 1 (Bare-Metal) | Type 2 (Hosted) |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Host OS Required** | No | Yes |
| **Performance** | Near-native (~95–98%) | Slightly lower (~85–95%) |
| **Use Case** | Enterprise data centers, cloud IaaS | Desktop virtualization, dev/testing |
| **Security/Isolation** | Very high (direct hardware access) | Lower (depends on host OS) |
| **Complexity** | Higher (VMM must include drivers) | Lower (leverages host OS drivers) |
| **Examples** | VMware ESXi, Xen, Hyper-V | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation |
| **Boot Path** | Hardware → VMM → VMs | Hardware → Host OS → VMM → VMs |

---

#### **(b)** Discuss **OS-level virtualization and application-level virtualization** in detail. Compare them with HAL-level virtualization in terms of performance, isolation, and use cases. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**OS-Level Virtualization (2 Marks):**
- Implemented as a feature of the host operating system kernel.
- Creates multiple **isolated user-space instances (containers)** using namespaces and cgroups.
- All containers share the *same host kernel*.
- Examples: Docker, LXC, OpenVZ, Solaris Zones, FreeBSD Jails.
- **Advantages:** Near-native performance (~1–3% overhead), fast startup (milliseconds), high density.
- **Limitation:** Cannot run a different OS kernel (e.g., Windows containers on Linux need additional layers like Hyper-V isolation or WSL2).

**Application-Level Virtualization (2 Marks):**
- Encapsulates the application and its runtime dependencies into a portable package.
- Executed by a thin runtime layer (JVM, BEAM, Docker Engine, WASM runtime).
- Examples: Java JAR/WAR (JVM), Docker images (Docker Engine), Electron apps, WebAssembly modules.
- **Advantages:** Highly portable, language/platform-agnostic, minimal footprint.
- **Disadvantage:** Performance depends on the runtime — JIT-compiled apps approach native, interpreted apps may lag.

**Comparison with HAL-Level Virtualization (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | OS-Level | App-Level | HAL-Level |
|-----------|----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Performance Overhead** | ~1–3% | ~0–30% | ~2–10% |
| **Isolation Strength** | Medium (shared kernel) | Low–Medium | Very High (separate OS) |
| **Boot Time** | Milliseconds | Milliseconds | Seconds–Minutes |
| **Guest OS Independence** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| **Resource Efficiency** | Very High | Very High | High |
| **Typical Use Case** | Microservices, CI/CD | Cross-platform apps | Multi-OS server consolidation |
| **Example Software** | Docker, LXC | JVM, Electron | VMware ESXi, KVM |
| **Security Boundary** | Kernel-level | Runtime-level | Hardware-level |

**Summary:**
- Choose **OS-level** for high-density, fast-scaling cloud-native workloads.
- Choose **App-level** for portable, lightweight, cross-platform deployment.
- Choose **HAL-level** when full OS isolation, security, and multi-tenancy are paramount.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes — KTU Valuation Patterns:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Sensitive vs. Privileged:** Many students use these terms interchangeably. Always remember: **Privileged = traps in user mode; Sensitive = behavior changes in user mode.** The theorem requires $S \subseteq P$, not $S = P$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting $S \not\subseteq P$ direction:** When asked "Is x86 virtualizable?" — students answer "Yes" because they know about VT-x. The correct analysis is: **Original x86 = NO**; **x86 with VT-x/AMD-V = YES**. Specify *which x86 generation*.
>
> 3. **Mixing Container vs. VM examples:** Docker is **OS-level**, NOT HAL-level. VMware is **HAL/Server-level**, NOT application-level. Examiners deduct 1 mark for each wrong classification.
>
> 4. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark questions:** A neat VMM or hypervisor diagram is worth **1–2 marks** in Part B. Always include one — even a hand-drawn block diagram.
>
> 5. **Not stating the Popek-Goldberg condition formally:** A vague answer loses marks. Always write: "$S \subseteq P$" as the formal statement with definitions of $S$ and $P$.
>
> 6. **Confusing Type 1 vs. Type 2 examples:** ESXi = Type 1; VirtualBox = Type 2. Mixing these up is a frequent 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 KTU Module 3 — Last-Minute Revision Cheat Sheet:**
>
> ✅ **Five Levels of Virtualization (in stack order):**
> - **ISA Level** → QEMU, Bochs (emulation, very slow)
> - **HAL Level** → VMware ESXi, KVM (hypervisor-based, near-native)
> - **OS Level** → Docker, LXC (containers, shared kernel)
> - **Server Level** → vSphere, Hyper-V (full server partitioning)
> - **App Level** → JVM, Docker Images, WASM (runtime-based)
>
> ✅ **VMM = Hypervisor** — software that creates and manages VMs.
>
> ✅ **Popek-Goldberg Theorem:** $S \subseteq P$ ⇔ Architecture is virtualizable.
>
> ✅ **Three VMM Design Requirements:**
> - **Efficiency** — innocuous instructions execute natively.
> - **Resource Control** — VMM has full control of physical resources.
> - **Equivalence** — VM behavior identical to native execution.
>
> ✅ **Sensitive Instructions:** Behavior changes between user/kernel mode.
> ✅ **Privileged Instructions:** Trap automatically in user mode.
>
> ✅ **Type 1 Hypervisor** = Bare-metal (ESXi, Xen). Type 2 = Hosted (VirtualBox, Workstation).
>
> ✅ **Original x86 is NON-virtualizable** (because $S \not\subseteq P$ — has sensitive non-privileged instructions like SIDT, SGDT).
>
> ✅ **Solutions for non-virtualizable x86:**
> - Binary Translation (VMware's approach)
> - Hardware-Assisted Virtualization (Intel VT-x, AMD-V)
>
> ✅ **Performance ranking (low to high overhead):** OS-Level (~1%) < App-Level (~0–30%) < HAL-Level (~2–10%) < Server-Level (~3–10%) ≪ ISA-Level (~10×–1000×).
>
> ✅ **Key Formulas:**
> - Performance Overhead: $\frac{T_{VMM} - T_{Native}}{T_{Native}} \times 100\%$
> - VM Density: $D_{max} = \min\left(\lfloor C/c \rfloor, \lfloor M/m \rfloor, \lfloor D/d \rfloor\right)$
> - CPU Share: $S_i = \frac{V_i}{\sum V_j}$
>
> ✅ **Container ≠ VM** — Containers share the host kernel; VMs have their own kernel.
>
> ✅ **Live migration, snapshots, and over-commitment** are advanced VMM features tested in 2-mark questions.
>
> ✅ **Exam Mantra:** Always define terms, then state the formal condition ($S \subseteq P$), then give an example, then justify with a diagram.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OS Level Virtualization, Virtualization Structures and Mechanisms, CPU
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** OS Level Virtualization, Virtualization Structures and Mechanisms, CPU ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 OS-Level Virtualization (Containerization) **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** **OS-Level Virtualization** is a virtualization paradigm in which the *host operating system kernel* is shared among multiple isolated user-space instances, each appearing as a complete, independent OS to its users. Unlike hardware-level virtualization (hypervisors), there is no separate "guest OS" — instead, the kernel is partitioned into multiple virtual environments commonly called **containers**, **zones**, **jails**, or **VEs (Virtual Environments)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The most important point examiners look for is the distinction between *Type-1 Hypervisors*, *Type-2 Hypervisors*, and *OS-Level Virtualization*. Always emphasize that OS-level virtualization **does not boot a separate kernel**; it only virtualizes the user space. **Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Building" Model:** Think of a physical building (the OS kernel). Instead of building separate houses (full VMs with their own plumbing, electricity, foundations), the landlord simply puts up **walls inside one building** to divide it into independent **apartments** (containers). Each apartment has its own front door, furniture, and mailbox (its own root filesystem, libraries, and user environment), but **all apartments share the same electrical wiring, water supply, and structural foundation** (the host kernel, system calls, and device drivers). Tenants (processes) feel as if they live in a standalone house, but the landlord's infrastructure is shared. > [!NOTE] > **Key Metrics in Industry:** > - **Boot Time:** OS-Level Virtualization boots in **milliseconds** (typically **< 100 ms**) compared to **seconds-to-minutes** for full VMs. > - **Performance Overhead:** Near-native (**< 5%**), as opposed to **10–25%** for hardware virtualization. > - **Footprint:** Container images can be as small as **~5 MB**, while VM images are **gigabytes (GB)**. ### 1.2 Virtualization Structures & Mechanisms **Definition:** **Virtualization Structures and Mechanisms** refer to the architectural framework that enables the abstraction of physical computing resources (CPU, memory, I/O, network) into logical, software-defined representations. The structural components include the **Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM) / Hypervisor**, **virtualized hardware interfaces**, and the **control structures** (shadow page tables, I/O rings, privilege levels) that govern guest-to-host transitions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Pillars of Virtualization (Popek & Goldberg Criteria, 1974):** > 1. **Equivalence** — A program running inside a VM should behave identically to one running on raw hardware. > 2. **Resource Control (Safety)** — The VMM must be able to fully control all virtualized resources. > 3. **Efficiency (Fidelity)** — A statistically dominant subset of guest instructions must execute *directly* on the hardware (no emulation). ### 1.3 CPU Virtualization **Definition:** **CPU Virtualization** is the mechanism by which the physical CPU's instruction set architecture (ISA) is abstracted so that multiple virtual CPUs (**vCPUs**) can be assigned to guest OSes or containers. It involves **scheduling vCPUs onto physical cores**, **translating privileged instructions** (when needed), and **maintaining isolation** between execution contexts. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** A single physical core today typically hosts **2 to 32+ vCPUs** via time-slicing (e.g., Intel Hyper-Threading offers 2 hardware threads per core, while software-defined vCPUs in VMs can scale up to dozens). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 OS-Level Virtualization — Operational Logic 1. **Kernel Sharing:** A single host kernel is loaded at boot. No second kernel is executed. 2. **Namespace Isolation:** Each container receives a private set of namespaces: - **PID namespace** — isolates process IDs (container sees its PID 1 as init). - **Network namespace** — isolates sockets, routing tables, interfaces. - **Mount namespace** — isolates the filesystem hierarchy. - **UTS namespace** — isolates hostname and domain. - **IPC namespace** — isolates inter-process communication. 3. **Control Groups (cgroups):** Enforce resource quotas (CPU shares, memory limits, block I/O weight, network bandwidth). 4. **Union Filesystems (e.g., OverlayFS):** Provide layered, copy-on-write (CoW) filesystems for efficient image distribution. 5. **Capability & Seccomp:** Linux capabilities and seccomp-bpf filters restrict system calls for security. > [!NOTE] > **Why this works:** Because the kernel itself enforces namespace boundaries, user-space code in container A literally *cannot see or signal* processes in container B. The kernel mediates every interaction. ### 2.2 Virtualization Structures — Type Breakdown | Type | Name | Hypervisor Location | Example | Performance | |------|------|---------------------|---------|-------------| | **Type-1 (Bare-Metal)** | Runs directly on hardware | Native | VMware ESXi, Xen, Microsoft Hyper-V | Near-native | | **Type-2 (Hosted)** | Runs as application on host OS | Inside host OS | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation | Slower (double scheduling) | | **Type-3 (OS-Level)** | Kernel-level isolation | Host kernel | Docker, LXC, OpenVZ, Solaris Zones | Near-native | ### 2.3 CPU Virtualization — Key Techniques - **Full Virtualization:** Guest OS is unaware of being virtualized. Privileged instructions are *trapped and emulated* by the hypervisor. - **Para-Virtualization:** Guest OS is *modified* to make hypercalls (e.g., Xen). - **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization:** Modern CPUs include **VT-x (Intel)** and **AMD-V (AMD)** extensions that provide a new **Ring -1 (root mode)**, eliminating most binary translation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Popek & Goldberg Sensitivity Theorem:** For a CPU ISA to be efficiently virtualizable, **all privileged instructions must be a strict subset of the control-sensitive instructions** (instructions whose behavior depends on the privilege mode). If a privileged instruction is *not* control-sensitive (e.g., x86's `POPF`), it causes a virtualization hole that requires binary translation or hardware assistance. ### 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit | Notes | |-------------------|------------|------|-------| | **CPU Overhead (Time-Slicing)** | $\text{Overhead} = \frac{T_{\text{context-switch}}}{T_{\text{quantum}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Lower is better. | | **Effective vCPU Capacity** | $C_{\text{eff}} = \frac{C_{\text{physical}}}{N_{\text{vCPUs}}} \cdot \eta$ | CPU-units | $\eta$ = efficiency factor ($\approx 0.9$ for para-virt). | | **Popek-Goldberg Sufficient Condition** | $S_{\text{privileged}} \subseteq S_{\text{control-sensitive}}$ | Set theory | $S$ = set of instructions. | | **Amdahl's Law (Virtualization Speedup)** | $S = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{N}}$ | Unitless | $f$ = parallelizable fraction, $N$ = cores. | | **Memory Overcommit Ratio** | $R_{\text{over}} = \frac{M_{\text{allocated}}}{M_{\text{physical}}}$ | Unitless | Typically 1.5× to 3×. | | **Hypervisor Trap Cost** | $T_{\text{trap}} = T_{\text{VM-exit}} + T_{\text{emulate}} + T_{\text{VM-entry}}$ | nanoseconds (ns) | Measured in **hundreds of ns** for HW-assist. | | **Container Density (Theoretical)** | $D_{\max} = \left\lfloor \frac{M_{\text{host}} - M_{\text{overhead}}}{M_{\text{container-min}}} \right\rfloor$ | containers | Limits by memory, not kernel. | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** These formulas govern cloud capacity planning in **AWS EC2**, **Azure VMs**, and **Kubernetes clusters**. The Popek-Goldberg theorem is foundational in OS research; modern x86 would *not* be virtualizable efficiently without VT-x/AMD-V. ### 2.5 Why These Concepts Matter in Engineering - **Cloud Computing (AWS, Azure, GCP):** Every "EC2 instance" is a vCPU mapped via CPU virtualization. - **Microservices & DevOps:** Docker/Kubernetes rely on OS-level virtualization to deploy thousands of services on shared hardware. - **Embedded & Edge Systems:** Lightweight containers (e.g., **K3s**) run on edge devices with constrained resources. - **Cybersecurity:** Namespaces and seccomp profiles form a critical *defense-in-depth* layer. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Effective vCPU Throughput Under Time-Slicing **Problem Setup:** A physical core has base capacity $C_{\text{phys}}$ (in vCPU-equivalent units). The hypervisor schedules $N$ vCPUs onto this core using round-robin time-slicing with a quantum $Q$ and context-switch cost $T_{\text{cs}}$. **Goal:** Derive the effective capacity allocated to *one* vCPU. **Step 1 — Define the raw capacity pool.** The physical core delivers $C_{\text{phys}}$ units of work per second. $$ C_{\text{phys}} = f_{\text{clock}} \times \text{IPC}_{\text{peak}} $$ Where $f_{\text{clock}}$ is the clock frequency in **Hz** and $\text{IPC}_{\text{peak}}$ is the peak instructions-per-cycle. (Typical modern x86: $f_{\text{clock}} = 3.0 \text{ GHz}$, $\text{IPC}_{\text{peak}} \approx 4$.) **Step 2 — Define the scheduling cycle.** In one full rotation through all $N$ vCPUs, the core spends: - $N \cdot Q$ time doing *useful* work, - $N \cdot T_{\text{cs}}$ time doing *overhead* context switches. $$ T_{\text{cycle}} = N \cdot Q + N \cdot T_{\text{cs}} = N \left( Q + T_{\text{cs}} \right) $$ **Step 3 — Compute efficiency.** Efficiency is the ratio of useful work to total cycle time. $$ \eta = \frac{N \cdot Q}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{N \cdot Q}{N(Q + T_{\text{cs}})} = \frac{Q}{Q + T_{\text{cs}}} $$ **Step 4 — Compute per-vCPU capacity.** Each vCPU receives an equal share of the effective capacity. $$ C_{\text{eff}} = \frac{C_{\text{phys}} \cdot \eta}{N} = \frac{C_{\text{phys}} \cdot Q}{N(Q + T_{\text{cs}})} $$ **Step 5 — Numerical example.** Let $C_{\text{phys}} = 100$ vCPU-units, $N = 8$ vCPUs, $Q = 10 \text{ ms}$, $T_{\text{cs}} = 0.05 \text{ ms}$. $$ \eta = \frac{10}{10 + 0.05} = \frac{10}{10.05} \approx 0.995 $$ $$ C_{\text{eff}} = \frac{100 \times 0.995}{8} = \frac{99.5}{8} \approx 12.44 \text{ units} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Each of the 8 vCPUs gets ~12.4% of the physical core's capacity, with **~0.5% efficiency loss** due to context switching. This is why over-subscribing vCPUs (e.g., 16 vCPUs on 4 cores) is common in cloud environments. ### 3.2 Derivation: Amdahl's Law Applied to Virtualized Workloads **Problem:** A virtualized database workload has a parallelizable fraction $f = 0.92$ and is assigned $N = 16$ vCPUs. Compute the theoretical speedup. **Step 1 — Recall Amdahl's Law.** $$ S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \frac{f}{N}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values.** $$ S(16) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.92) + \frac{0.92}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.08 + 0.0575} = \frac{1}{0.1375} $$ **Step 3 — Final value.** $$ S(16) \approx 7.27 \times $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analytical Insight:** Even with 16 vCPUs, the workload achieves only **7.27× speedup**, capped by the 8% serial portion. This is why KTU examiners love Amdahl's Law problems — they test whether students understand that *virtualization cannot overcome fundamental serial bottlenecks*. ### 3.3 Popek-Goldberg Sensitivity Theorem — Formal Statement **Definition (Derived Form):** A given instruction $\iota$ is said to be: - **Privileged** if it traps in user mode (i.e., $\iota \in S_{\text{priv}}$). - **Control-Sensitive** if its behavior or side effects depend on the privilege mode (i.e., $\iota \in S_{\text{cs}}$). - **Behavior-Sensitive** if its result depends on the privilege mode (i.e., $\iota \in S_{\text{bs}}$). **Theorem (Recited for 14-mark answers):** *For a given third-generation (hardware-supported) architecture, a hypervisor (VMM) may be constructed if the set of privileged instructions is a subset of the set of control-sensitive instructions.* $$ S_{\text{priv}} \subseteq S_{\text{cs}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why x86 historically failed:** Instructions like `POPF` (Pop Flags) and `PUSHF` are *control-sensitive* when run at privilege level 0, but *not* privileged at user level. Thus, the x86 ISA **violates** the Popek-Goldberg condition, which is why Intel had to add **VT-x** in 2005 to "patch" this gap. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Dimension | OS-Level Virtualization | Full Virtualization (Type-2) | Para-Virtualization (Xen) | Bare-Metal (Type-1) | |-----------|-------------------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|---------------------| | **Kernel** | Shared host kernel | Full guest kernel | Modified guest kernel | Full guest kernel | | **Isolation Strength** | Medium (kernel shared) | Strong | Strong | Strongest | | **Boot Time** | **< 100 ms** | 10–60 s | 10–30 s | 5–30 s | | **Performance** | Near-native | 80–95% | 90–98% | 95–99% | | **Image Size** | **5–500 MB** | 5–50 GB | 1–10 GB | 5–50 GB | | **Security** | Process-level | Hardware-enforced | Hardware-enforced | Hardware-enforced | | **Live Migration** | Limited | Yes | Yes | Yes | | **Example** | Docker, LXC | VirtualBox, KVM/QEMU | Xen HVM/PV | ESXi, Hyper-V | | **Overhead Source** | cgroup, namespaces | Trap-and-emulate, shadow paging | Hypercall overhead | VM-exit/entry | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 OS-Level Virtualization Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph hostHW["HARDWARE LAYER (Physical CPU, RAM, Disk, NIC)"] cpuA["Physical CPU Cores"] memA["Physical RAM"] diskA["Physical Disk"] end subgraph hostKern["HOST KERNEL (Linux Kernel - SINGLE INSTANCE)"] sched["CPU Scheduler"] memMng["Memory Manager"] nsEngine["Namespace Engine"] cgroupEngine["Cgroup Controller"] netStack["Network Stack"] end subgraph contA["CONTAINER A (PID Namespace: 100)"] appA1["App Process PID 1"] appA2["App Process PID 2"] rootA["Root Filesystem OverlayFS"] end subgraph contB["CONTAINER B (PID Namespace: 200)"] appB1["App Process PID 1"] rootB["Root Filesystem OverlayFS"] end subgraph contC["CONTAINER C (PID Namespace: 300)"] appC1["App Process PID 1"] rootC["Root Filesystem OverlayFS"] end cpuA --> sched memA --> memMng diskA --> rootA diskA --> rootB diskA --> rootC sched --> appA1 sched --> appB1 sched --> appC1 nsEngine -.isolates.-> contA nsEngine -.isolates.-> contB nsEngine -.isolates.-> contC cgroupEngine -.enforces quotas.-> contA cgroupEngine -.enforces quotas.-> contB cgroupEngine -.enforces quotas.-> contC netStack -.virtual NIC per container.-> contA netStack -.virtual NIC per container.-> contB netStack -.virtual NIC per container.-> contC ``` ### 4.2 CPU Virtualization Mechanism — VM-Exit/VM-Entry Cycle (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR state1["GUEST OS in Non-Root Mode Ring 3"] -->|executes privileged
instruction| state2 state2["CPU detects sensitive
instruction: VM-EXIT triggered"] -->|control transfers
to hypervisor| state3 state3["HYPERVISOR in Root Mode
emulates or translates
the instruction"] -->|emulation complete| state4 state4["VM-ENTRY: control returns
to guest OS in Non-Root Mode"] -->|guest continues| state1 style state1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style state2 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600 state3:::hvStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724 state4 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc ``` ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef hvStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 subgraph bareMetal["TYPE-1 BARE-METAL HYPERVISOR (e.g., VMware ESXi)"] vmmA["VMM Control Layer"] vcpuA1["vCPU 1"] vcpuA2["vCPU 2"] vmA1["Guest OS A"] vmA2["Guest OS B"] end subgraph typeTwo["TYPE-2 HOSTED HYPERVISOR (e.g., VirtualBox)"] hostOSApp["Host Operating System"] vmmB["VMM Application"] vcpuB1["vCPU 1"] vcpuB2["vCPU 2"] vmB1["Guest OS X"] vmB2["Guest OS Y"] end HW["Physical Hardware CPU Cores"] --> bareMetal HW --> typeTwo ``` ### 4.3 Request Flow: How a System Call Works in a Containerized Environment ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant App as Container App participant Lib as glibc (libc) participant Kern as Host Kernel participant HW as Physical Hardware App->>Lib: open(filename, flags) Lib->>Kern: syscall instruction (sys_openat) Note over Kern: Kernel checks:
1. Is PID in cgroup quota?
2. Is mount namespace valid?
3. Is seccomp filter allowing? Kern->>HW: Disk I/O via driver HW-->>Kern: Interrupt, data returned Kern-->>Lib: File descriptor (fd) Lib-->>App: fd returned to user code ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Distinguish between OS-level virtualization and hardware-level virtualization with suitable examples.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Criterion | OS-Level Virtualization | Hardware-Level Virtualization | |-----------|-------------------------|-------------------------------| | **Kernel** | Single shared host kernel | Separate guest OS kernel per VM | | **Hypervisor** | Not required (uses host kernel) | Required (Type-1 or Type-2) | | **Isolation** | Process-level via namespaces | Hardware-level via VMM | | **Example** | Docker, LXC, OpenVZ | VMware ESXi, VirtualBox | *(1 mark for each valid distinguishing criterion; max 3 marks.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the Popek and Goldberg virtualization requirements.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Equivalence:** Any program running within a virtual machine should exhibit identical behavior to one running on the physical machine. *(1 mark)* 2. **Resource Control / Safety:** The Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM) must have complete control over all virtualized system resources. *(1 mark)* 3. **Efficiency / Fidelity:** The set of privileged instructions must be a strict subset of the control-sensitive instructions, allowing the VMM to directly execute most guest instructions on hardware. *(1 mark)* --- ### 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Explain the architecture of OS-level virtualization with a neat block diagram. Discuss **namespaces** and **cgroups** as its key mechanisms. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss the limitations of OS-level virtualization compared to full virtualization in terms of **security, isolation, and portability**. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Answer — Question A** **(a) Architecture of OS-Level Virtualization (7 marks):** **Block Diagram (drawn in exam — 2 marks):** ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ HARDWARE (CPU, RAM, DISK, NIC) │ └────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┘ │ ┌────────────────────▼────────────────────────────┐ │ HOST KERNEL (Linux — SINGLE INSTANCE) │ │ ┌────────────┐ ┌───────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ │ │Scheduler │ │Memory Mgr │ │Cgroup Engine │ │ │ └────────────┘ └───────────┘ └──────────────┘ │ │ ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ Namespace Engine (PID, NET, MNT, IPC) │ │ │ └────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ └────────────────────┬────────────────────────────┘ │ ┌─────────────┼─────────────┐ │ │ │ ┌───▼───┐ ┌───▼───┐ ┌───▼───┐ │Cont A │ │Cont B │ │Cont C │ │PID 1 │ │PID 1 │ │PID 1 │ │App Lib│ │App Lib│ │App Lib│ │RootFS │ │RootFS │ │RootFS │ └───────┘ └───────┘ └───────┘ ``` **Namespaces (3 marks):** Namespaces provide *isolation of system resources* such that each container perceives a unique instance of the global resource. - **PID Namespace:** Each container has its own process tree starting at PID 1. - **Network Namespace:** Each container has private virtual interfaces, IP addresses, and routing tables. - **Mount Namespace:** Each container has an independent root filesystem hierarchy. - **UTS Namespace:** Unique hostname and NIS domain name per container. - **IPC Namespace:** Isolated System V IPC and POSIX message queues. **Cgroups (2 marks):** Control Groups enforce **resource accounting and limitation**: - **cpu.shares:** Relative CPU weight in scheduling contention. - **memory.limit_in_bytes:** Hard cap on RAM usage; OOM-killer triggered on breach. - **blkio.throttle:** Disk I/O bandwidth cap. - **net_cls:** Tags packets with a class ID for traffic shaping. --- **(b) Limitations of OS-Level Virtualization (7 marks):** 1. **Security (3 marks):** - Since all containers **share the host kernel**, any kernel-level vulnerability (e.g., a privilege-escalation bug or a dirty-COW-like flaw) compromises *all* containers simultaneously. - A container that escapes its namespace via a kernel exploit gains **root access to the host**, and thus to every other container. - Mitigation: use minimal host OS, AppArmor/SELinux profiles, seccomp-bpf syscall filters. 2. **Isolation (2 marks):** - Containers cannot run **different OS kernels** (e.g., a Windows container cannot run on a Linux host). - Cannot host kernels with different versions or patched/legacy OSes; hence, a container is **OS-dependent** (Linux container on Linux host only). - Limited support for kernel modules — the host kernel's modules are shared. 3. **Portability (2 marks):** - Container images are **not as portable as VM images** across heterogeneous kernels (e.g., a container built on Ubuntu may fail on a CentOS host due to ABI differences in glibc, systemd, or kernel features). - Cannot perform **live migration** in the same robust way as VMs (CRIU-based checkpoint/restore is possible but not as mature as VMotion). - Hardware passthrough (GPU, USB devices) is restricted compared to Type-1 hypervisors. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of **CPU virtualization** using hardware-assisted virtualization (Intel VT-x). Describe the role of **VMX root mode** and **VMX non-root mode**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** What are the **Popek-Goldberg virtualization criteria**? Show with an example why the original x86 architecture violated them, and how VT-x resolved this. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Answer — Question B** **(a) CPU Virtualization with Intel VT-x (7 marks):** **Diagram (2 marks):** ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ PHYSICAL HARDWARE │ │ ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ VMX Root Mode (Host / VMM) │ │ │ │ - Full Ring 0 privileges │ │ │ │ - Hypervisor code executes here │ │ │ └────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ │ ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ VMX Non-Root Mode (Guest OS) │ │ │ │ - Ring 0 to Ring 3 available to guest │ │ │ │ - Privileged ops trigger VM-EXIT │ │ │ └────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ ▼ VM-EXIT / VM-ENTRY transitions │ │ ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ VMCS: Virtual Machine Control Structure │ │ │ │ - Guest state area │ │ │ │ - Host state area │ │ │ │ - VM-exit controls │ │ │ └────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │ └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` **Working (3 marks):** 1. **VMXON** instruction is executed by the host to enter VMX operation, transitioning the CPU into **VMX Root Mode**, where the hypervisor runs. 2. **VMLAUNCH** creates a new VMCS (Virtual Machine Control Structure) and enters **VMX Non-Root Mode**, where the guest OS begins execution. 3. When the guest executes a privileged or sensitive instruction (e.g., `CR3` access, I/O port), the CPU performs a **VM-EXIT** — a hardware-controlled transfer of control back to the hypervisor. 4. The hypervisor reads the **VM-exit reason** from the VMCS, emulates the operation, and then issues **VMRESUME** to perform a **VM-ENTRY** back to the guest. **Role of VMX Root and Non-Root Modes (2 marks):** - **VMX Root Mode:** A new privilege level (effectively *Ring -1*) where only the VMM code runs. It has unrestricted access to all hardware. - **VMX Non-Root Mode:** The mode in which guest OSes execute. Sensitive instructions trap automatically into VM-EXIT. This eliminates the need for **binary translation**, drastically reducing overhead to **hundreds of nanoseconds** per trap. --- **(b) Popek-Goldberg Criteria & x86 Violation (7 marks):** **Three Criteria (3 marks):** 1. **Equivalence (Fidelity):** A program running in a VM behaves identically to one running natively. 2. **Resource Control (Safety):** The VMM must fully control all virtualized resources. 3. **Efficiency:** A statistically dominant subset of guest instructions must execute *directly* on the hardware. **Formal Sufficient Condition (2 marks):** $$ S_{\text{privileged}} \subseteq S_{\text{control-sensitive}} $$ **x86 Violation Example (1.5 marks):** The `POPF` (Pop Flags Register) instruction in 32-bit x86: - **In Ring 0 (kernel mode):** Loads the flags register, including the **IF (Interrupt Flag)** bit, which controls hardware interrupt enablement. - **In Ring 3 (user mode):** `POPF` is allowed to execute, but the IF bit is silently *masked out* (ignored). Thus, `POPF` is a **control-sensitive** instruction (its effect depends on privilege level), but it is **NOT privileged** — it does not trap in user mode. Hence: $$ \text{POPF} \in S_{\text{cs}} \quad \text{but} \quad \text{POPF} \notin S_{\text{priv}} $$ This violates $S_{\text{priv}} \subseteq S_{\text{cs}}$, making x86 *not efficiently virtualizable* under Popek-Goldberg. **Resolution via VT-x (0.5 mark):** Intel VT-x introduces VMX modes. All sensitive instructions (including those that don't trap in classical rings) cause **VM-EXIT** in non-root mode, thus *forcing* the guest's `POPF` to be mediated by the hypervisor. AMD-V provides the equivalent **SVM (Secure Virtual Machine)** extensions. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing hypervisor types:** Students often write "Docker is a Type-1 hypervisor." It is **NOT** — Docker is an OS-level virtualization tool. *(Lose 2 marks immediately.)* > 2. **Misstating Popek-Goldberg:** The theorem requires $S_{\text{priv}} \subseteq S_{\text{cs}}$, **not** equality. Saying "privileged = control-sensitive" loses the *subset* nuance. *(Lose 1 mark.)* > 3. **Forgetting to mention VT-x/AMD-V:** When asked about CPU virtualization, always mention hardware-assisted extensions by name. > 4. **Wrong kernel claim:** Never say "containers have their own kernel." They share the host kernel. *(Lose 2 marks.)* > 5. **Missing units:** Numerical answers (e.g., Amdahl's Law) must be stated with the multiplication sign (×) clearly — avoid writing "7.27x" without symbol. --- ### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:** - ✅ **OS-level virtualization** = single kernel, multiple user-space instances. Examples: **Docker, LXC, OpenVZ, Solaris Zones**. - ✅ **Two pillars of OS-level virt:** **Namespaces** (isolation) + **Cgroups** (resource limits). - ✅ **Type-1 hypervisor** runs on bare metal; **Type-2** runs as an app on a host OS; **Type-3 (OS-level)** uses the host kernel directly. - ✅ **Popek-Goldberg condition:** $S_{\text{privileged}} \subseteq S_{\text{control-sensitive}}$. - ✅ **x86 originally failed** Popek-Goldberg due to instructions like `POPF` and `PUSHF`. Resolved by **VT-x (Intel)** and **AMD-V (AMD)**. - ✅ **CPU virtualization** techniques: **Full virt**, **Para-virt**, **Hardware-assisted virt**. - ✅ **VT-x modes:** **VMX Root Mode** (host/hypervisor) vs **VMX Non-Root Mode** (guest). Transitions are **VM-EXIT** and **VM-ENTRY**, governed by the **VMCS**. - ✅ **Trap cost** in HW-assisted virt: **hundreds of nanoseconds** (vs. **microseconds** in software translation). - ✅ **Container boot time:** **< 100 ms** vs. **seconds-to-minutes** for VMs. - ✅ **Amdahl's Law** is the ceiling on multi-vCPU speedup: $S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + f/N}$. - ✅ **vCPU scheduling overhead** is governed by the ratio $\eta = \frac{Q}{Q + T_{\text{cs}}}$. - ✅ **Memory overcommit ratio** of 1.5×–3× is common in production clouds. - ✅ **Live migration** is mature in Type-1 hypervisors; limited in OS-level virt. - ✅ **Security caveat:** A kernel-level exploit in OS-level virt compromises **all** containers at once. - ✅ Always cite **examples** in 7-mark questions: Docker, LXC, KVM, Xen, ESXi, VirtualBox, Hyper-V.

topics.title: Memory and I/O Virtualization, Virtual Clusters and Resource Management, Live VM Migration Steps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Memory and I/O Virtualization, Virtual Clusters and Resource Management, Live VM Migration Steps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Memory Virtualization

### Formal Definition
**Memory Virtualization** is a mechanism by which the VMM (Virtual Machine Monitor) / Hypervisor abstracts the physical RAM of a host machine into isolated, contiguous, and virtualized address spaces (called *Guest Physical Memory*) for each guest Virtual Machine (VM). The guest OS believes it owns a contiguous physical memory range starting from address 0, but in reality, the VMM maintains a mapping between **Guest Physical Address (GPA)** and **Host Machine Address (HMA)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024 Scheme**: The guest OS performs **Virtual-to-Guest Physical** address translation (using its own page tables), and the VMM then performs **Guest Physical-to-Host Machine** address translation (using shadow page tables or EPT/NPT). This creates a **two-level memory translation** hierarchy.

### Conceptual Analogy (The Hotel Safe Analogy)
Imagine a large hotel with **256** rooms, but only **100** guests. The hotel manager (VMM) cannot give every guest the same room number, because they would conflict. So the manager gives every guest a *personalized key card* that says "Room 101" (Guest Physical Address). However, behind the scenes, the manager maps those virtual room numbers to actual physical rooms. Guest A's "Room 101" might be real room **42**, Guest B's "Room 101" might be real room **17**, and so on. The guest never knows — they simply use "Room 101" on their card, and the manager translates it to the real room.

---

## 1.2 I/O Virtualization

### Formal Definition
**I/O Virtualization** is the process of decoupling physical I/O resources (network cards, disk controllers, USB devices) from the VMs that use them. It allows multiple VMs to share the same physical I/O device while maintaining isolation, throughput, and security. It is implemented through one of three approaches:
1. **Full Emulation (Device Emulation)**
2. **Para-virtualization (Split-Driver Model)**
3. **Direct Device Assignment / Passthrough (SR-IOV, VT-d, AMD-Vi)**

### Conceptual Analogy (The Shared Printer Analogy)
Think of a single physical printer in an office. Instead of buying **10** printers for **10** employees (expensive!), the office installs a *print server* (the I/O Virtualization layer). Each employee thinks they "own" the printer and sends jobs with a fake ID. The server queues the jobs, translates the IDs, and dispatches them to the real printer. The employees see their own "virtual printer", but the real hardware is shared.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: In *para-virtualization*, the guest uses a special driver (the **front-end driver**) that communicates with the **back-end driver** in the host via a shared memory ring buffer. This bypasses expensive emulation traps and dramatically increases I/O throughput.

---

## 1.3 Virtual Clusters

### Formal Definition
A **Virtual Cluster** is a collection of VMs interconnected via a virtual network, working together as a unified compute resource. Unlike a physical cluster, a virtual cluster can be dynamically scaled, migrated, and reconfigured on demand. Each VM acts as a virtual node, and the VMM manages the network topology, storage, and resource allocation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Note**: A virtual cluster is **programmable**, **elastic**, and **homogeneous**. It decouples the cluster definition from the underlying physical hardware.

### Intuitive Overview
A virtual cluster is like a **chessboard set up on a coffee table**. The pieces (VMs) can be rearranged, added, removed, or relocated mid-game. The "board" itself is software-defined, and the table (physical host) is just an abstract foundation. The pieces don't care if the table is wood, glass, or marble — they only follow the rules of the game (the cluster's coordination protocol).

---

## 1.4 Resource Management in Virtualized Systems

### Formal Definition
**Resource Management** in virtualized systems is the process of dynamically allocating, scheduling, and reclaiming host resources (CPU, memory, disk, network bandwidth) across multiple competing VMs. It uses policies like **share-based allocation**, **reservation**, and **limits**, while maintaining SLA guarantees.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept for Exams**: A VM in VMware/KVM is assigned three memory parameters:
> - **Reservation**: Guaranteed minimum memory.
> - **Limit**: Maximum memory cap.
> - **Shares**: Relative priority during contention.

### Conceptual Analogy (The Air Traffic Controller)
The VMM acts as an *Air Traffic Controller*. Multiple planes (VMs) want to land on one runway (CPU/Memory). The controller decides which plane gets priority (shares), gives a minimum takeoff slot (reservation), and enforces a maximum waiting time (limit). All planes safely share one physical resource without collision.

---

## 1.5 Live VM Migration

### Formal Definition
**Live VM Migration** is the process of moving a running Virtual Machine from one physical host to another with **minimal downtime (typically < 1 second)** and without disconnecting the client. The VM continues serving requests during the migration. Common techniques include **Pre-Copy**, **Post-Copy**, and **Hybrid Copy**.

### Conceptual Analogy (The Relocating Hospital Patient)
Imagine a critically ill patient (the running VM) connected to IV drips and monitors (active network connections and live state). A normal relocation would unplug everything and reattach it elsewhere. **Live migration** is like rolling the entire hospital bed, IV stand, and monitoring equipment smoothly into an ambulance — the patient never even wakes up. The IV drip (network stream) just seamlessly switches source mid-flow.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Memory Virtualization — Operational Mechanics

Memory virtualization requires the VMM to maintain three distinct address spaces simultaneously:

- **Virtual Address (VA)** — generated by the application inside the guest.
- **Guest Physical Address (GPA)** — what the guest OS believes is physical memory.
- **Host Machine Address (HMA)** — the actual physical DRAM location.

### The Two-Level Translation Process

1. **Level 1: Guest-OS Page Table Walk**
   - The guest OS walks its own page tables to translate VA $\rightarrow$ GPA.
   - This is unmodified (for **full virtualization**) or modified (for **para-virtualization**).
2. **Level 2: VMM Shadow Page Table / EPT Walk**
   - The VMM maintains a **Shadow Page Table (SPT)** that directly maps VA $\rightarrow$ HMA, bypassing the GPA layer entirely.
   - Modern CPUs use **Extended Page Tables (EPT)** in Intel or **Nested Page Tables (NPT)** in AMD.
3. **TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)** caches the recent translations for speed.

### Why Memory Virtualization is Hard
- **Page Table Shadowing Overhead**: Every page table update by the guest must be intercepted via a *page fault trap*, validated by the VMM, and reflected in the shadow page table. This is a costly "trap-and-emulate" path.
- **Consistency**: The VMM must ensure that guest and shadow page tables never disagree.
- **Memory Over-commitment**: The VMM may "promise" more total memory to VMs than physically exists, using techniques like **ballooning**, **swapping**, and **page sharing (KSM)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intel EPT vs AMD NPT**: Both implement hardware-assisted two-level paging, but Intel uses the term *Extended Page Tables* (EPT) and AMD uses *Nested Page Tables* (NPT). The principles are identical; exam answers may use either.

---

## 2.2 I/O Virtualization — Three Architectural Models

### Model 1: Full Emulation (Device Emulation)
- The VMM emulates a real, well-known device (e.g., Intel e1000 NIC, LSI Logic SCSI).
- Guest driver talks to the *fake* device; VMM intercepts all I/O instructions via traps.
- **Pros**: Guest OS requires **zero modification** (true full virtualization).
- **Cons**: Very high overhead — every I/O operation causes a *VM Exit* $\rightarrow$ VMM trap $\rightarrow$ emulation $\rightarrow$ *VM Entry*. Throughput is poor.

### Model 2: Para-virtualization (Split-Driver Model)
- Guest has a special **front-end driver** (e.g., `virtio-net`).
- Host has a corresponding **back-end driver** (e.g., `vhost-net` in the kernel).
- Communication happens via a **shared ring buffer** in pre-agreed memory.
- **Pros**: Near-native I/O performance. **Cons**: Guest must support the paravirt framework (e.g., Linux does, Windows needs special drivers).

### Model 3: Direct Device Assignment (Passthrough / SR-IOV)
- A physical device (e.g., 10 GbE NIC) is dedicated to a single VM via **Intel VT-d** or **AMD-Vi (IOMMU)**.
- For **SR-IOV (Single Root I/O Virtualization)**: one physical device is exposed as multiple *Virtual Functions (VFs)*, each assignable to a VM.
- **Pros**: Near-native performance, hardware isolation.
- **Cons**: Reduced flexibility, limited number of VMs per device.

---

## 2.3 Virtual Clusters — Architecture & Provisioning

A virtual cluster is provisioned using three layers:

1. **Frontend Portals** — web UI or API (e.g., OpenNebula, OpenStack Horizon) where users request VMs.
2. **Service Manager** — interprets the request, builds the cluster template (number of VMs, OS image, network, storage).
3. **VMM Pool** — physical servers running hypervisors (KVM, Xen, ESXi) that host the requested VMs.

### Cluster Provisioning Modes
- **Static Provisioning**: VMs are pre-created and assigned. Underutilized at low load.
- **Dynamic Provisioning (On-Demand)**: VMs are instantiated when a job arrives. Best for batch / HPC workloads.
- **Hybrid**: Combines both — a small static pool + burstable dynamic pool.

### Resource Pooling & Elasticity
Resources are aggregated into **pools** (compute pool, storage pool, network pool). A scheduler (e.g., OpenStack Nova) places VMs in pools based on availability, affinity rules, and load metrics.

---

## 2.4 Resource Management — Key Policies

The VMM exposes four primary resource control knobs to the administrator:

- **Shares (Weight-based)**: A relative priority. If VM A has **2000** shares and VM B has **1000** shares, during contention A gets **2x** the resource.
- **Reservation**: A hard floor — VM is guaranteed this much, even under pressure.
- **Limit (Cap)**: A hard ceiling — VM cannot exceed this.
- **Entitlement**: The *actual* resource a VM is getting, computed as a function of the above three and current host load.

### Admission Control
Before placing a new VM, the VMM checks if the *reservation* can still be honored. If not, the request is **denied** to protect existing VMs' SLAs.

### Memory Ballooning Driver
A paravirt driver (`virtio-balloon`, VMware Tools balloon) that asks the guest OS to "inflate" — i.e., to voluntarily give up unused pages back to the VMM. This is how the VMM **reclaims** memory from idle VMs to give to busy ones.

### Kernel Same-Page Merging (KSM)
A kernel service that scans memory pages across all VMs, identifies identical pages (e.g., all Linux VMs share the same `libc.so`), and merges them into a single physical page with **copy-on-write**. This is the foundation of **memory over-commitment**.

---

## 2.5 Live VM Migration — Pre-Copy, Post-Copy, Hybrid

### Pre-Copy Migration (Most Common)
1. **Pre-Migration**: VM runs on Source. A small "Iterative Pre-Copy" begins.
2. **Reservation**: Destination reserves resources; both ends establish control channel.
3. **Iterative Pre-Copy**:
   - Round 1: Copy **all** dirty (modified) pages from Source to Destination.
   - The VM continues running on Source.
   - Some pages get dirtied *during* the transfer (these are tracked).
   - Round 2: Copy only the pages dirtied in Round 1.
   - Repeat until the *remaining dirty set* is small (e.g., < 50 pages) or a max-iteration count is reached.
4. **Stop-and-Copy**: Source VM is **paused**. Final dirty pages, CPU registers, and device state are sent.
5. **Commitment**: Destination VM starts. Source VM is destroyed.
6. **Activation**: Network traffic is redirected to Destination (via gratuitous ARP on the migration IP).

> [!NOTE]
> **Downtime in Pre-Copy** = duration of the *Stop-and-Copy* phase. **Total migration time** is much longer because of the iterative rounds.

### Post-Copy Migration
1. Source VM is **paused immediately**.
2. CPU and minimal device state are sent to Destination.
3. Destination VM **resumes** and starts running.
4. Pages are **pulled on-demand** from Source as the running VM faults on missing pages.
5. If a page is still missing after a threshold, it triggers a network page fault.
6. Once all pages have been pulled, Source is released.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advantage of Post-Copy**: **Zero accumulated downtime** after the initial pause. The VM is immediately running on the destination. **Disadvantage**: Risk of **network page faults** (if a needed page is delayed) can cause severe performance degradation. A clever variant called **CR/Copy-on-Reflect** uses a *reflector* VM to maintain a backup of unmodified pages.

### Hybrid Copy
A **bounded pre-copy** phase is followed by a **post-copy** phase. If pre-copy does not converge (e.g., a write-heavy workload), the migration switches to post-copy mode to guarantee completion.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Notation | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two-Level Address Translation | GPA, HMA | $HMA = f(GPA)$ — implemented by EPT/NPT | Pages (typically 4 KB) |
| Shadow Page Table | SPT | $SPT: VA \to HMA$ (direct, bypasses GPA) | One per VM |
| Page Dirtied Rate | $D(t)$ | Number of pages modified during interval $\Delta t$ | Pages/sec |
| Convergence Condition (Pre-Copy) | — | $D(t) < BW_{net} / P_{size}$ | Bytes/sec; $P_{size}$ = page size |
| Downtime (Pre-Copy) | $T_{down}$ | $T_{down} = T_{stop-copy} + T_{commit}$ | Seconds |
| Total Migration Time (Pre-Copy) | $T_{total}$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{round_i} + T_{down}$ | Seconds |
| Memory Balloon Size | $B$ | Pages returned to VMM by guest | Number of pages |
| KSM Merged Pages | $M$ | Identical pages replaced with one COW page | Reduces $N \cdot P_{size}$ |
| Share-based Allocation | $S_i$ | $Allocation_i = \frac{S_i}{\sum_{j} S_j} \cdot R_{total}$ | $R_{total}$ = available resource |
| Admission Control | — | $\sum R_{reserved} + R_{new} \le R_{physical}$ | Boolean (allow/deny) |
| SR-IOV Virtual Functions | $VF$ | One PF $\to$ $N$ VFs (e.g., 8, 64, 128) | Hardware-limited |
| Live Migration Network Page Fault | $PF$ | $\text{Fault} \iff P \notin \text{Dest Memory}$ | Post-copy only |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Trap**: Do **not** confuse **T_total** with **T_down**. In Pre-Copy, $T_{down}$ is *tiny* (sub-second) but $T_{total}$ can be **tens of seconds** because of the iterative rounds.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud Bursting**: A virtual cluster in a private cloud bursts into a public cloud (AWS, Azure) when local resources saturate. Requires live migration of workloads.
- **Disaster Recovery**: Pre-copy live migration is used to evacuate VMs from a data center before a planned maintenance window.
- **HPC & Big Data**: Virtual clusters in research labs (e.g., SDSC Comet, NCAR) run MPI jobs across dynamically provisioned VMs.
- **Telco NFV**: 5G core network functions are virtualized. Memory and I/O virtualization ensure deterministic latency for vRAN workloads.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Pre-Copy Migration Downtime

The downtime in a pre-copy live migration is dominated by the **Stop-and-Copy** phase. Let us derive it formally.

### Step 1: Define the Stop-and-Copy Phase
At the moment the VM is paused, there exists a residual set of dirty pages $R$ that must be transferred to the destination.

### Step 2: Express Transfer Time
The transfer time for a single page of size $P$ bytes over a network with effective bandwidth $B$ (bytes/sec) is:

$$T_{page} = \frac{P}{B}$$

### Step 3: Sum Over All Residual Pages
The total time to transfer the residual set $R$ is:

$$T_{stop-copy} = R \cdot \frac{P}{B}$$

### Step 4: Add the Commitment Overhead
The destination must acknowledge receipt and start the VM. This adds a small constant $T_{commit}$:

$$T_{commit} \approx 0.1 \text{ s} \text{ (empirical, for KVM/VMware)}$$

### Step 5: Total Downtime
Combining the two:

$$T_{down} = R \cdot \frac{P}{B} + T_{commit}$$

### Worked Numerical Example
Suppose:
- Page size $P = 4096$ bytes = $4 \times 10^{3}$ bytes
- Network bandwidth $B = 1 \text{ Gbps} = 1.25 \times 10^{8}$ bytes/sec
- Residual dirty pages $R = 200$ (an aggressively small converged set)
- $T_{commit} = 0.1$ s

We compute:

$$T_{page} = \frac{4 \times 10^{3}}{1.25 \times 10^{8}} = 3.2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}$$

$$T_{stop-copy} = 200 \times 3.2 \times 10^{-5} = 6.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 6.4 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{down} = 6.4 \text{ ms} + 100 \text{ ms} = 106.4 \text{ ms}$$

This **106 ms** is the "blackout window" the client experiences — a TCP connection's retransmission timer (typically 200 ms) does not fire, so the user perceives **zero downtime**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Pre-Copy Convergence Condition

A pre-copy migration **converges** only if the rate at which the network can copy pages is greater than the rate at which the VM is dirtying them. Let:

- $D(t)$ = number of pages dirtied per second at time $t$ (the *dirtied rate*).
- $B / P$ = the rate at which pages can be transmitted (in pages per second).
- $P$ = page size in bytes.
- $B$ = effective bandwidth in bytes per second.

The convergence condition is:

$$D(t) < \frac{B}{P}$$

### Interpretation
If the workload is *write-heavy* (high $D(t)$) and the network is *slow* (low $B$), the residual dirty set will **never shrink**. The migration is said to be **non-convergent**, and the VMM must switch to a bounded pre-copy limit or to post-copy.

### Boundary Cases
- **Memory-intensive DB workload**: $D(t) \approx 50{,}000$ pages/sec on 1 Gbps link. $B/P = 1.25 \times 10^{8} / 4096 \approx 30{,}517$ pages/sec. Since $D > B/P$, **non-convergent**.
- **Idle VM**: $D(t) \approx 0$. Converges in *one round*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip**: When asked why pre-copy fails, the formal answer is: *"Pre-copy does not converge when the page dirtying rate $D(t)$ exceeds the network's page-transfer rate $B/P$, causing the residual set to grow unboundedly across iterations."*

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Pre-Copy vs Post-Copy

| Property | Pre-Copy | Post-Copy | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Downtime | None (VM keeps running) | $\approx 100$ ms (pause + push CPU state) | None initially, bounded |
| Steady-State Downtime | $\approx 100$ ms (Stop-and-Copy) | Zero (runs immediately on Dest) | Zero |
| Total Data Transferred | $V \cdot k$ ($V$ = VM memory, $k \ge 1$) | $V$ (each page transferred once) | $\approx V$ |
| Network Page Faults | None | Frequent early on | Moderate |
| Convergence Risk | Yes (write-heavy workloads) | No (always terminates) | Mitigated |
| Best Suited For | Read-heavy VMs, web servers | HPC, write-heavy | Mixed workloads |
| Complexity | Low | Medium (reflector, push/pull) | High |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Share-Based CPU Allocation

When $N$ VMs compete for $C$ total CPU cycles, and VM $i$ has $S_i$ shares, the scheduler allocates:

$$Allocation_i = \frac{S_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} S_j} \times C$$

### Numerical Example
Suppose three VMs share a host with $C = 12$ CPU cores:
- VM A: $S_A = 4000$ shares
- VM B: $S_B = 2000$ shares
- VM C: $S_C = 1000$ shares

Total shares:

$$\sum S = 4000 + 2000 + 1000 = 7000$$

Allocation:

$$Allocation_A = \frac{4000}{7000} \times 12 \approx 6.86 \text{ cores}$$

$$Allocation_B = \frac{2000}{7000} \times 12 \approx 3.43 \text{ cores}$$

$$Allocation_C = \frac{1000}{7000} \times 12 \approx 1.71 \text{ cores}$$

The sum is exactly 12, preserving conservation of CPU resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note**: This allocation is enforced *only during contention*. If VM C is idle, VM A and B effectively get the unused cycles of VM C.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Virtualization — Two-Level Address Translation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Application in Guest VM] --> B[Virtual Address VA]
    B --> C{Guest OS Page Table}
    C -->|Level 1| D[Guest Physical Address GPA]
    D --> E{VMM Shadow Page Table or EPT}
    E -->|Level 2| F[Host Machine Address HMA]
    F --> G[Physical DRAM Cell]

    style A fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#ffd,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#dfd,stroke:#333
```

## 4.2 I/O Virtualization — Three Architectural Models

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ModelA[Model 1 Full Emulation]
        G1[Guest OS] -->|traps on I/O| VMM1[Hypervisor Emulates Device]
        VMM1 --> HW1[Real Hardware]
    end
    subgraph ModelB[Model 2 Para virtualization]
        G2[Guest OS] --> FED[Front End Driver virtio]
        FED -->|shared ring buffer| BED[Back End Driver vhost]
        BED --> HW2[Real Hardware]
    end
    subgraph ModelC[Model 3 SR IOV Passthrough]
        G3[Guest OS] --> DD[Direct Driver in Guest]
        DD -->|VT d IOMMU| PF[Physical Function with Virtual Functions]
        PF --> HW3[Real Hardware]
    end

    style ModelA fill:#fee,stroke:#900
    style ModelB fill:#efe,stroke:#090
    style ModelC fill:#eef,stroke:#009
```

## 4.3 Virtual Cluster — Provisioning Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    User[User Researcher] -->|submits request| Portal[Frontend Portal OpenStack Horizon]
    Portal --> SM[Service Manager]
    SM -->|resolves template| CM[Cluster Manager]
    CM --> Sched[Scheduler Nova]
    Sched --> PoolA[Compute Pool KVM Hosts]
    Sched --> PoolB[Storage Pool Ceph]
    Sched --> PoolC[Network Pool Neutron]
    PoolA --> VM1[VM Node 1]
    PoolA --> VM2[VM Node 2]
    PoolA --> VM3[VM Node N]
    VM1 <-->|virtual network| VM2
    VM2 <-->|virtual network| VM3
    VM1 <--> PoolB
    VM2 <--> PoolB
    VM3 <--> PoolB

    style User fill:#fcf,stroke:#333
    style Portal fill:#cff,stroke:#333
    style Sched fill:#fdc,stroke:#333
```

## 4.4 Live VM Migration — Pre-Copy State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> RunningOnSource
    RunningOnSource --> IterativePreCopy: Start pre copy phase
    IterativePreCopy --> IterativePreCopy: Round i copy dirty pages
    IterativePreCopy --> StopAndCopy: Residual set is small
    IterativePreCopy --> IterativePreCopy: Residual still large
    StopAndCopy --> Commitment: VM paused final pages sent
    Commitment --> RunningOnDest: Destination activated
    RunningOnDest --> [*]: Source VM destroyed
    RunningOnSource --> RunningOnSource: Bounded iterations exceeded abort
```

## 4.5 Live VM Migration — Post-Copy State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> RunningOnSource
    RunningOnSource --> Pause: Trigger migration
    Pause --> SendCPUState: CPU registers pushed
    SendCPUState --> RunningOnDest: Destination starts immediately
    RunningOnDest --> RunningOnDest: Demand pull missing pages
    RunningOnDest --> PageFault: Page not yet on destination
    PageFault --> RunningOnDest: Page received from source
    RunningOnDest --> [*]: All pages transferred source released
```

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture — End-to-End Live Migration Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Source[Source Physical Host]
        S1[VM Running Application] --> S2[Dirty Page Bitmap Tracker]
        S2 --> S3[Migration Agent]
    end
    subgraph Net[Network Channel 1 Gbps or higher]
        S3 -->|Xend or QEMU protocol| D1
    end
    subgraph Dest[Destination Physical Host]
        D1[Migration Listener] --> D2[Memory Page Cache]
        D2 --> D3[VM Resumed on Destination]
    end
    S3 -->|XAPI or libvirt control| D1
    D3 -->|Gratuitous ARP broadcast| Switch[Network Switch]
    Switch -->|redirects IP traffic| Client[Application Client]

    style Source fill:#fdd,stroke:#900
    style Dest fill:#dfd,stroke:#090
    style Net fill:#ffd,stroke:#aa0
```
