<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: 3-Mark Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Memory Virtualization. Why is a two-level page table needed in a fully virtualized environment?**

**Model Answer**:
Memory Virtualization is the abstraction of physical RAM by the VMM/hypervisor into isolated virtual memory spaces (Guest Physical Memory) for each guest VM. A **two-level page table** is needed because the guest OS independently performs **VA $\rightarrow$ GPA** translation, while the VMM must additionally perform **GPA $\rightarrow$ HMA** translation. The VMM maintains a *Shadow Page Table* (or hardware EPT) that consolidates both into a single direct walk, ensuring correctness, isolation, and protection among guest VMs.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for justification of two-level mechanism)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three techniques used by a hypervisor to support memory over-commitment.**

**Model Answer**:
1. **Memory Ballooning** — A paravirt driver inside the guest inflates, forcing the guest OS to release unused pages back to the VMM.
2. **Page Sharing (KSM)** — Identical pages across VMs are merged into a single physical page with copy-on-write semantics.
3. **Memory Swapping** — Idle VM pages are swapped to disk, freeing physical RAM for active VMs.
4. *(Bonus, optional)* Transparent Page Sharing (TPS) — done at the hypervisor level without guest awareness.

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for any three techniques with a one-line description)*

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A: 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`

**Q. (a)** Explain **Memory Virtualization** in detail. With neat diagrams, describe the **two-level address translation** mechanism using Shadow Page Tables and Extended Page Tables (EPT). Compare them in terms of performance and complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **I/O Virtualization** with the three architectural models: *Full Emulation*, *Para-virtualization*, and *Direct Device Assignment (SR-IOV)*. For each, explain the working principle, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark)**: Memory Virtualization is the abstraction of physical memory by the VMM into multiple isolated *Guest Physical Address Spaces*. Each VM is presented with a contiguous, zero-based physical memory range that does not correspond 1:1 to real hardware.

**Two-Level Address Translation (3 marks)**: The process involves two distinct translations:
- *Level 1*: Inside the guest OS, VA $\rightarrow$ GPA using the guest's own page tables.
- *Level 2*: Inside the VMM, GPA $\rightarrow$ HMA using either a Shadow Page Table (SPT) or hardware EPT.

**Shadow Page Tables (1.5 marks)**: The VMM intercepts every page table update via traps, validates it, and maintains a *consolidated* SPT mapping VA $\rightarrow$ HMA. The hardware then performs a single page table walk on the SPT. **Drawback**: Trap-and-emulate overhead is significant; the VMM must keep SPT in sync with guest tables.

**Extended Page Tables (1.5 marks)**: Modern CPUs (Intel EPT, AMD NPT) provide a hardware MMU that walks *two* sets of page tables automatically — one maintained by the guest (GPA layer) and one maintained by the VMM (HMA layer). **Advantage**: No traps needed for page table updates; performance is near-native. The two walks are pipelined in the CPU.

**Comparison (1 mark)**:

| Aspect | Shadow Page Tables | EPT / NPT |
|---|---|---|
| Trap overhead | High (on every PTE update) | None (hardware-walked) |
| VM Exit frequency | Frequent | Rare |
| Implementation complexity | High (VMM logic) | Low (CPU-assisted) |
| Performance | Moderate | Near-native |

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Model 1 — Full Emulation (2 marks)**:
- The VMM emulates a well-known I/O device (e.g., Intel e1000 NIC).
- Guest driver operates *as if* the device were real.
- All I/O instructions issued by the guest trap into the VMM, which interprets them and performs the real I/O on the host.
- *Advantage*: Guest OS is unmodified (true full virtualization).
- *Limitation*: Each I/O causes a VM Exit/Entry, which is expensive; throughput is poor (often < 1 Gbps on 10 GbE hardware).

**Model 2 — Para-virtualization / Split-Driver (2.5 marks)**:
- Guest installs a **front-end driver** (e.g., `virtio-net`).
- Host runs a **back-end driver** (e.g., `vhost-net`).
- Communication occurs via a **shared ring buffer** in pre-agreed guest memory regions (e.g., `vring`).
- Guest writes a *request descriptor* into the ring; the back-end driver polls (or uses an eventfd) and services the request.
- *Advantage*: Near-native I/O performance (often 8-9 Gbps on 10 GbE).
- *Limitation*: Guest OS must support the paravirt framework; legacy Windows guests need custom drivers.

**Model 3 — Direct Device Assignment / SR-IOV (2.5 marks)**:
- Hardware-assisted passthrough using **Intel VT-d** or **AMD-Vi (IOMMU)**.
- A physical device (PF — Physical Function) is partitioned into multiple *Virtual Functions (VFs)*, each assigned to a different VM.
- The guest OS loads a *native* driver for the device — no emulation, no paravirt.
- *Advantage*: Near-native performance, hardware-level isolation, no VMM overhead in the I/O data path.
- *Limitation*: Each VF consumes physical resources (interrupt lines, DMA contexts). Limited number of VFs per device. Reduced live-migration flexibility (SR-IOV passthrough makes migration harder).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Many students write only the *names* of the three models and skip the *working principle*. A model without the data flow (who calls whom, where the ring buffer lives, how SR-IOV maps VFs) will score only **partial marks**. Always include a labelled diagram or a clear sequence of I/O steps.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Alternative]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **concept of a Virtual Cluster** with its architecture. Discuss the roles of the Frontend Portal, Service Manager, and VMM Pool in provisioning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe **Live VM Migration** in detail. Compare **Pre-Copy** and **Post-Copy** migration techniques with a state-transition diagram for each. List two real-world scenarios where live migration is essential. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark)**: A Virtual Cluster is a software-defined collection of VMs interconnected via a virtual network, behaving as a unified compute resource. It is elastic, programmable, and decoupled from physical hardware.

**Architecture (4 marks)**:
1. **Frontend Portal (1 mark)**: A web-based or API-driven interface (e.g., OpenStack Horizon, OpenNebula Sunstone) where users submit cluster requests — specifying VM count, OS image, network topology, and storage.
2. **Service Manager (1.5 marks)**: The orchestration brain. It interprets the user request, fetches VM templates from a *VM Image Repository*, and constructs a *cluster manifest*. It handles authentication, quota enforcement, and SLA mapping.
3. **VMM Pool (1.5 marks)**: A pool of physical servers running hypervisors (KVM, Xen, ESXi). The Service Manager hands off the manifest to a *cluster manager* (e.g., OpenStack Heat), which uses a scheduler to place VMs onto the VMM Pool. The VMM Pool allocates CPU, memory, network, and storage from the corresponding *resource pools*.

**Provisioning Modes (1 mark)**: Static (pre-created VMs), Dynamic (on-demand instantiation), and Hybrid (small static + burstable dynamic).

**Real-world Example (1 mark)**: Amazon EC2, Microsoft Azure, and Google Compute Engine all use virtual cluster principles under the hood; users request a "cluster" of VMs, and the cloud orchestrator provisions them dynamically.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (0.5 mark)**: Live VM Migration is the process of moving a running VM from one physical host to another with minimal service interruption, typically < 1 second of perceived downtime.

**Pre-Copy Migration (2.5 marks)**:
- **Step 1 — Reservation**: Destination reserves resources matching the VM's CPU/Memory/Network requirements.
- **Step 2 — Iterative Pre-Copy**: While the VM continues running, the source copies all memory pages to the destination. After each round, only the *dirty* pages (modified during the transfer) are re-copied. This repeats until the residual set is small.
- **Step 3 — Stop-and-Copy**: VM is paused. Final residual pages + CPU registers + device state are sent.
- **Step 4 — Commitment**: Destination VM is activated; network traffic redirected via gratuitous ARP.
- *Downtime* = duration of Stop-and-Copy phase. *Risk* = non-convergence on write-heavy workloads.

**Post-Copy Migration (2.5 marks)**:
- **Step 1 — Pause**: VM is paused on source.
- **Step 2 — Push CPU State**: CPU registers and minimal essential state pushed to destination.
- **Step 3 — Resume on Dest**: Destination VM starts running immediately.
- **Step 4 — Demand-Pull**: Missing memory pages are fetched on-demand from source as the running VM faults.
- *Advantage*: Guaranteed bounded total migration time; no convergence issue.
- *Disadvantage*: Initial page faults cause latency spikes; risk of degraded performance if the source is slow or unreachable.

**State Diagrams (1 mark)**: *Pre-Copy*: RunningOnSource $\rightarrow$ IterativePreCopy (loop) $\rightarrow$ StopAndCopy $\rightarrow$ Commitment $\rightarrow$ RunningOnDest. *Post-Copy*: RunningOnSource $\rightarrow$ Pause $\rightarrow$ SendCPUState $\rightarrow$ RunningOnDest (with demand-pull loop).

**Real-World Scenarios (0.5 mark)**:
1. **Data center maintenance**: Evacuate VMs from a server needing hardware repair without taking services offline.
2. **Cloud load balancing**: Move a VM from a heavily loaded host to a lightly loaded one to balance resource utilization.
3. *(Bonus)* **Disaster recovery**: Migrate VMs to a secondary site before a hurricane or power event.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students often confuse the *order* of operations in Post-Copy. The correct sequence is: **Pause on Source $\rightarrow$ Push CPU state $\rightarrow$ Resume on Destination $\rightarrow$ Pull pages on demand**. Many students write "push pages, then pause" — this is wrong and costs **2 marks** for procedural correctness.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Memory Virtualization** introduces a **two-level** address translation (VA $\rightarrow$ GPA $\rightarrow$ HMA) implemented via Shadow Page Tables or hardware EPT/NPT.
- **I/O Virtualization** has **three models**: Full Emulation (slowest, most compatible), Para-virtualization (fastest software-based, requires guest support), SR-IOV (hardware passthrough, near-native, limited flexibility).
- **Virtual Cluster** is **programmable** and **elastic**; key components are Frontend Portal, Service Manager, and VMM Pool.
- **Resource Management** uses three knobs: **Shares**, **Reservation**, **Limit**. Memory over-commitment is enabled by **ballooning, KSM, and swapping**.
- **Live Migration — Pre-Copy**: Iterative copying while VM runs; minimal downtime at the end. Fails on write-heavy workloads.
- **Live Migration — Post-Copy**: VM paused first, then resumed on destination; pages pulled on demand. Always converges; suffers from network page faults.
- **Convergence condition for Pre-Copy**: $D(t) < B / P$. If violated, switch to Post-Copy.
- **Downtime (Pre-Copy)** $\approx T_{stop-copy} + T_{commit}$, typically $\approx 100$ ms.
- **Total migration time (Pre-Copy)** = sum of all iterative rounds + downtime (much longer).
- **SR-IOV** uses **Physical Functions (PFs)** and **Virtual Functions (VFs)**; one NIC can serve many VMs directly.
- **KSM** is a *kernel-level* page-merging service that enables memory over-commitment.
- **Ballooning** is the *guest-cooperative* technique for the VMM to reclaim memory.
- **Live Migration Triggers**: Load balancing, hardware maintenance, fault tolerance, energy efficiency (consolidation).
- **Network redirection** after live migration uses **gratuitous ARP** to update the LAN's MAC table.
- **Hardware-assisted paging** (Intel EPT / AMD NPT) is the **modern standard**; shadow page tables are largely historical except in nested virtualization.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Migration of Memory, Files and Network Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Migration of Memory, Files and Network Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resource Migration** in distributed systems is the mechanism of transparently relocating **process state, memory contents, file references, and active network connections** from a *source node* to a *destination node* at runtime, while preserving execution semantics, communication integrity, and overall system consistency.

In a typical distributed operating system, the three principal sub-domains of migration are:

| Sub-Domain | What is Migrated? | Primary Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory Migration** | Virtual address space, page tables, kernel stack | Transparency & down-time |
| **File Migration** | File handles, open file tables, cached blocks/pages | Locality of reference |
| **Network Resource Migration** | Sockets, TCP endpoints, message channels, RPC stubs | Connection continuity |

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Migration

1. **Process Migration** – The act of moving a running process from one machine to another. A process is composed of *code, data, stack, heap, registers, file descriptors, and communication endpoints* — all of which must move consistently.
2. **Memory Migration** – The relocation of the *virtual address space* of a process. It is the bulk of data transferred during process migration.
3. **File and Network Resource Migration** – Migrating references to *remote files (file handles, open descriptors)* and *active network connections (sockets, message queues, RPC bindings)* so that the migrated process retains its identity and I/O capabilities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why migrate at all?** The classical drivers are **load balancing**, **improved communication locality** (placing a process close to the data it needs), **fault tolerance** (evacuating a failing node), and **reconfigurable computing** (long-running jobs on shared infrastructure such as clusters and clouds).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Relocating Office"

Imagine an employee (`process`) working in Office A with:

- A huge pile of documents on their desk (`memory`)
- An active phone call with a client (`network connection`)
- A borrowed file from the company archive (`file reference`)

The company decides to shift this employee to Office B. To do this transparently:

1. **Photocopy** the documents in waves and quietly replace them with the copies (`memory migration — pre-copy`).
2. **Pause** the work, hand over the remaining papers and to-do list (`state transfer`).
3. **Redirect** the phone line through Office A for a while so the client does not notice the move (`message forwarding / connection redirection`).
4. **Update** the filing-cabinet keys so the new office can open the borrowed file from the central archive (`file handle migration`).

This is, in essence, the choreography of resource migration in a distributed system.

### 1.4 Key Quantitative Metrics (KTU Emphasis)

- **Freezing Time (T_freeze):** The duration for which the migrated process is *completely suspended* during the hand-off.
- **Residual Dependency:** The degree to which the migrated process *still depends* on the source node after relocation (lower is better).
- **Migration Overhead:** Total bytes transferred × network latency in addition to normal execution.
- **Page Fault Rate (post-copy):** Number of remote page-fetches per unit time after migration.
- **Threshold / Standard Reference Value:** The **Writable Working Set (WWS)** — the minimum number of pages that must be re-copied each round to keep the process useful.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A migration strategy is considered *good* when **T_freeze → 0**, **residual dependency → 0**, and **overhead is bounded**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Anatomy of Process State

A process in a distributed OS carries *four* major categories of state that must be transferred to make migration semantically correct.

```
PROCESS STATE
├── 1. EXECUTION STATE
│       ├── Program Counter (PC), Register Set
│       ├── Stack pointer, Kernel stack
│       └── Signal/Interrupt masks
│
├── 2. MEMORY STATE
│       ├── Code segment (sometimes shared — need not migrate)
│       ├── Data, Heap, Stack pages
│       └── Page tables, segment tables
│
├── 3. FILE & DEVICE STATE
│       ├── Open file descriptors
│       ├── Current working directory
│       └── Pending I/O buffers
│
└── 4. COMMUNICATION STATE
        ├── Open sockets (TCP/UDP)
        ├── RPC bindings, message queues
        └── Pending messages in transit
```

### 2.2 The Six Phases of Process Migration

| Phase | Action | Where Process Runs |
|---|---|---|
| **P1. Initiation** | Decide to migrate (load, fault, policy trigger) | Source |
| **P2. Negotiation** | Pick target node, reserve resources | Source |
| **P3. Pre-copy (optional)** | Iteratively copy memory while process runs | Source |
| **P4. Suspension + Stop-copy** | Freeze the process, copy remaining dirty pages | None (frozen) |
| **P5. Hand-off** | Transfer execution state, file/net state | None |
| **P6. Resume + Cleanup** | Start at destination, tear down source | Destination |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Freezing Time** = (Duration of P4) + (Duration of P5). All other phases allow the process to keep executing.

### 2.3 Memory Migration Strategies — Deep Dive

#### 2.3.1 Eager (Whole-Process / All) Migration

- The **entire** virtual address space is transferred *atomically* before the destination process is started.
- **Pros:** Simple, deterministic, no residual dependency.
- **Cons:** **High freezing time**, large bandwidth consumption, scales poorly for big processes.

#### 2.3.2 Lazy (On-Demand) Migration

- Only **a minimal subset** of pages is transferred initially; the rest is fetched on first access.
- **Pros:** Very low freezing time, ideal for large processes.
- **Cons:** High page-fault rate, residual dependency, complex fault handling.

#### 2.3.3 Pre-Copy Migration

- Iterative model: copy pages in **rounds**. After each round, the process is allowed to run and *dirty* some pages, which are then copied in the next round.
- The algorithm stops when the **Writable Working Set (WWS)** is smaller than the cost of another round, after which *stop-copy* finalizes the migration.
- **Pros:** Low freezing time, bounded residual dependency.
- **Cons:** Network overhead grows with the dirtied-page set; unsuitable for write-intensive workloads.

#### 2.3.4 Post-Copy Migration

- Only the **execution state (registers, stack pointer, kernel stack)** is transferred initially.
- The process is resumed at the destination; pages are *faulted in* from the source on demand.
- **Pros:** *Minimum possible freezing time*, ideal for latency-sensitive workloads.
- **Cons:** Page faults during execution degrade performance; source node remains "pinned" as a paging server.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Writable Working Set (WWS)** at iteration $k$ is the set of pages dirtied by the process during iteration $k-1$. Pre-copy stops when $\vert WWS \vert \leq \text{min\_transfer\_threshold}$.

#### 2.3.5 Flushing-Based Migration

- Variant in which the *source* is asked to **flush** (force-write) all dirty pages back to a stable store or to the destination, after which they become clean and the residual dirty set shrinks drastically. Used in virtual-machine migration (e.g., Xen, VMware live migration).

### 2.4 File Migration

In a distributed system, files are usually accessed through three classical models:

| Model | Where Data Resides | Performance | Consistency Burden |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Remote Service Model** | Server (always) | Worst (every op is RPC) | None (single master) |
| **Caching Model** | Client cache + server | Good for reads | Cache-coherence problem |
| **Replication Model** | Multiple replicas | Excellent (read) | Strong-consistency protocols needed |

#### 2.4.1 File Migration Strategies

- **Whole-File Migration:** The *entire file* is shipped to the requesting client. Good for sequential workloads.
- **Page-Level (Block-Level) Migration:** The file is treated as a set of fixed-size pages/blocks; only the accessed unit is shipped. Best for random-access workloads.
- **Prefetching:** Anticipates future access based on the *current working set* and ships pages ahead of time.
- **Avoiding Server Bottlenecks:** When many clients request the same file, *caching* at each client drastically reduces the load on the file server (this is the basis of systems like **AFS, Sprite, NFSv3+**).

> [!NOTE]
> The **Sprite Network File System** is a classic textbook example: when a client opens a file, the *whole file* is migrated to the client cache. On close, dirty pages are written back. If file size exceeds available cache, the file is *removed* from the cache after each access (whole-file model with cache-pressure eviction).

### 2.5 Network Resource Migration

The hardest part of migration. Active network endpoints (TCP sockets, RPC stubs) carry the *identity* of a process. Once the process moves, the rest of the world still points to the old (source) address.

#### 2.5.1 Three Classical Mechanisms

1. **Message Forwarding (Indirection)**
   - The source node *holds onto* the old address and *forwards* any incoming message to the new location.
   - The forwarder is torn down only when the destination confirms that all peers have been notified of the new address.
   - Used by **Mosix, Sprite, Condor**.

2. **DNS-Based Redirection**
   - The process's logical *hostname* is updated in the DNS to point to the destination's IP.
   - Works only for *new* connections; existing TCP connections cannot be migrated this way.

3. **IP Address Take-Over (L2/L3 Migration)**
   - The destination node *adopts the source's IP and MAC address* (via gratuitous ARP or similar).
   - External packets now physically arrive at the destination.
   - Used heavily in **virtual machine / cloud live migration**.

#### 2.5.2 Challenges of Active Connection Migration

- **TCP sequence numbers** are stateful; rerouting mid-stream requires careful handling of the TCP state machine.
- **Firewall state tables** may drop packets that suddenly appear to come from a different source.
- **Security associations** (IPSec, TLS) are tied to the original IP — they must be renegotiated.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Use \lvert for cardinality in handwritten answers.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pages transferred in *k*-th pre-copy round | $P_k = f^{k-1} \cdot N$ | $N$ = total pages, $f$ = dirty-fraction (0–1) | Geometric decay |
| 2 | Total pages transferred in $R$ rounds | $T(R) = N \cdot \dfrac{1 - f^R}{1 - f}$ | $R$ = rounds executed | Geometric series |
| 3 | Asymptotic upper bound ($R \to \infty$) | $T(\infty) = \dfrac{N}{1 - f}$ | — | Pre-copy *never* exceeds this |
| 4 | Migration overhead ratio | $OH = \dfrac{f}{1 - f}$ | Dimensionless | Lower is better |
| 5 | Stop-copy threshold (WWS rule) | $\vert WWS \vert \le \delta$ | $\delta$ = min-pages threshold | Stop pre-copy here |
| 6 | Total post-copy fault cost | $C_{post} = C_{resume} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \cdot t_{fault}(i)$ | $p_i$ = access-prob, $t_{fault}$ = remote fetch time | Sum of expected fault latencies |
| 7 | Effective freezing time (pre-copy) | $T_{freeze} = \dfrac{S_{rem}}{B} + L$ | $S_{rem}$ = remaining bytes, $B$ = bandwidth, $L$ = latency | Network-dominated |
| 8 | Locality benefit (file model) | $B = 1 - \dfrac{t_{remote}}{t_{local}}$ | $t_{remote}, t_{local}$ in seconds | 0 = no benefit, 1 = full benefit |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud Live Migration (AWS, OpenStack, VMware vMotion):** Uses pre-copy + stop-copy with iterative dirty-page tracking, driven by Xen/VMware hypervisors.
- **Hadoop / Spark Schedulers:** Move long-running executors across nodes for *data locality*.
- **MOSIX / Sprite Clusters:** Pioneered message forwarding + whole-file caching for transparent process migration on Linux clusters.
- **Mobile Computing / Edge Offloading:** Migrating compute "threads" from a phone to the cloud edge as battery drains.
- **CDN Routing:** Migrating TCP connections across geographically distributed proxies to stay close to the user.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Pre-Copy Convergence

We model pre-copy memory migration as an **iterative copying process**.

**Assumptions:**

- Total memory: $N$ pages.
- In each round, the *process keeps running* on the source and dirties a constant fraction $f$ of the pages it has already received.
- Each round is long enough to copy all pages in the dirty set.

**Step 1 — Round 1.**

The first round copies the **entire** virtual address space from source to destination.

$$P_1 = N$$

**Step 2 — Round 2.**

During Round 1, the process dirtied $f \cdot N$ pages. These are exactly the ones that are now "stale" at the destination and must be re-sent.

$$P_2 = f \cdot N$$

**Step 3 — Round k (general).**

The pages dirtied in Round $(k-1)$ must be re-copied in Round $k$. Since the dirty fraction is constant, the pages dirtied in Round $(k-1)$ is $f$ times those copied in Round $(k-1)$.

$$P_k = f \cdot P_{k-1} = f^{k-1} \cdot N$$

**Step 4 — Total pages transferred in R rounds.**

Sum the geometric series:

$$T(R) = \sum_{k=1}^{R} P_k = N \sum_{k=0}^{R-1} f^k = N \cdot \frac{1 - f^R}{1 - f}$$

**Step 5 — Asymptotic bound.**

As $R \to \infty$ and $0 \le f < 1$:

$$T(\infty) = \lim_{R \to \infty} N \cdot \frac{1 - f^R}{1 - f} = \frac{N}{1 - f}$$

This is the **fundamental pre-copy bound** — pre-copy can never transfer more than $\dfrac{N}{1-f}$ pages, no matter how many rounds are run.

**Step 6 — Migration overhead ratio.**

The "wasted" copies (over and above the original $N$) are:

$$OH = T(\infty) - N = \frac{N}{1-f} - N = \frac{f \cdot N}{1-f}$$

Dividing by $N$:

$$OH_{ratio} = \frac{f}{1-f}$$

**Interpretation:**

- $f = 0.1$ → Overhead ≈ 11 % (excellent).
- $f = 0.5$ → Overhead = 100 % (transferred twice as much as needed).
- $f = 0.9$ → Overhead = 900 % (pre-copy becomes pathological).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Pre-copy is *catastrophic* for write-intensive workloads (large $f$). For such workloads, **post-copy** or **flushing-based** migration is preferred.

### 3.2 Derivation — Optimal Stop-Copy Trigger

Let:

- $\beta$ = bandwidth (pages/second) between source and destination.
- $\ell$ = one-way latency (seconds).
- $W_k$ = size of the Writable Working Set in round $k$ (pages).
- $T_k$ = time to copy W_k pages:

$$T_k = \frac{W_k}{\beta} + \ell$$

**Stopping Rule:** Stop pre-copy when the time to do a *next round* of pre-copy exceeds the time to do a *single stop-copy* of the current dirty set.

$$\frac{W_k}{\beta} + \ell \;\le\; T_{freeze}^{max}$$

Equivalently, the algorithm continues while:

$$W_k > \beta \cdot (T_{freeze}^{max} - \ell)$$

This gives a **clean, implementable stopping criterion** used in systems like **Xen Live Migration**.

### 3.3 Derivation — Post-Copy Expected Page-Fault Cost

Assume the process accesses pages uniformly. Let:

- $N$ = total pages, $p_i = \dfrac{1}{N}$ for all $i$.
- $t_{fault}(i)$ = time to fetch page $i$ from the source (≈ $L + \dfrac{S_{page}}{B}$).

Expected total fault cost after migration:

$$C_{post} = C_{resume} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{N} \cdot t_{fault}(i)$$

$$C_{post} = C_{resume} + \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{fault}(i)$$

If $t_{fault}$ is approximately constant (= $t_f$):

$$C_{post} = C_{resume} + t_f$$

But *each access* to a page not yet resident triggers a fault, so the **amortized** per-access cost over $A$ accesses is:

$$C_{amort} = \frac{C_{post}}{A} = \frac{C_{resume}}{A} + \frac{N \cdot t_f}{2A}$$

(using the rule of thumb that half the faults are "duplicate" once pages are cached).

> [!NOTE]
> The post-copy strategy's chief weakness is visible here: a *cold process* that touches many pages will pay $O(N \cdot t_f)$ total cost, which can be enormous for big processes.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Migration Strategy Comparison

| Criterion | Eager (All) | Pre-Copy | Post-Copy | Flushing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Freezing Time** | Very High | Low (≈ 1 round + stop-copy) | *Minimum* (only exec state) | Low |
| **Total Bytes Transferred** | Exactly $N$ | $\ge N$, up to $\dfrac{N}{1-f}$ | $N$ (eventually) | $N$ (eventually) |
| **Residual Dependency** | None | Very Low | High (source = paging server) | None |
| **Network Overhead** | One-shot burst | Multi-round | On-demand, scattered | One-shot burst |
| **Sensitive to Dirty-Rate $f$** | No | **Yes (badly)** | Moderate | No |
| **Best for** | Small processes | Read-mostly workloads | Latency-critical workloads | VM/Cloud migration |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Low | Medium | High | Medium |
| **Real-world Use** | Older Unix MOSIX | VMware vMotion (pre-copy phase) | Process migration research | Xen Live Migration |

### 3.5 File Migration Trade-Off Analysis

Let:

- $S$ = file size (blocks).
- $h$ = hit ratio in client cache (0 ≤ h ≤ 1).
- $t_{local}$ = local access time.
- $t_{remote}$ = remote access time (≈ RPC + network).

**Cost of Whole-File Migration (one-time):**

$$C_{whole} = \frac{S}{B} + L$$

**Benefit per access if cached:**

$$\Delta t = t_{remote} - t_{local}$$

**Break-even number of accesses:**

$$A_{breakeven} = \frac{C_{whole}}{\Delta t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A whole-file migration is *worth it* only if the file will be accessed more than $A_{breakeven}$ times before being evicted.

**Page-Level Migration (no whole-file cost):**

- Each miss pays $t_{remote}$.
- Total cost for $A$ accesses:

$$C_{page} = A \cdot (1 - h) \cdot t_{remote}$$

- This is preferred when $A$ is small or $h$ is high.

### 3.6 Connection Migration Cost Model

Let $\mu$ = number of active TCP connections at migration time. The forwarding approach requires:

- Setting up $\mu$ forwarding rules at the source.
- Each forwarded message incurs an extra *tunnel* hop.
- The forwarder is torn down when all $\mu$ peers have sent a *redirect-ack*.

**Forwarder lifetime:**

$$T_{fwd} = \max_{i=1..\mu} (T_{last-contact, i})$$

If the process is communication-active, $T_{fwd}$ can be very large; if quiescent, $T_{fwd}$ is small.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Migration Lifecycle (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([initStart: Migration Request]) --> B{decideMig: Migrate?}
    B -- No --> Z([procContinue: Stay at Source])
    B -- Yes --> C[selectTarget: Choose Destination Node]
    C --> D[preCopyPhase: Iteratively Copy Memory]
    D --> E[suspendProc: Freeze Source Process]
    E --> F[transferState: Send Execution + Open-File + Socket State]
    F --> G[resumeAtDest: Start Process at Destination]
    G --> H[cleanupSource: Tear Down Source Resources]
    H --> I([migComplete: Migration Done])
```

> [!NOTE]
> Node IDs are intentionally descriptive and alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid parsing rules. The four *state categories* (execution, memory, file, network) are all materialised in step **transferState**.

### 4.2 Pre-Copy Memory Migration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1([pcStart: Begin Pre-Copy]) --> P2[round1: Copy All N Pages]
    P2 --> P3[procRuns: Process Continues on Source]
    P3 --> P4[trackDirty: Record Dirtied Pages]
    P4 --> P5{wwsCheck: WWS Small Enough?}
    P5 -- No --> P6[roundN: Copy Dirty Pages Only]
    P6 --> P3
    P5 -- Yes --> P7[suspendStep: Freeze Process]
    P7 --> P8[stopCopyStep: Send Remaining Dirty Pages]
    P8 --> P9[resumeDest: Start at Destination]
    P9 --> P10([pcDone: Complete])
```

### 4.3 Post-Copy Memory Migration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1([poStart: Begin Post-Copy]) --> Q2[suspendStep2: Freeze Source]
    Q2 --> Q3[sendExecState: Send Registers + Kernel Stack]
    Q3 --> Q4[resumeDest2: Start at Destination]
    Q4 --> Q5[execLoop: Process Executes]
    Q5 --> Q6{pageFault: Page Missing?}
    Q6 -- No --> Q5
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q7[fetchFromSrc: Remote Page Fetch]
    Q7 --> Q8[loadPage: Page Installed at Dest]
    Q8 --> Q5
    Q5 --> Q9{processDone: Execution Finished?}
    Q9 -- No --> Q5
    Q9 -- Yes --> Q10[releaseSrc: Source Paging Server Shut Down]
    Q10 --> Q11([poDone: Complete])
```

### 4.4 File Access Models — Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ClientA([clientNode]) --> A1{fileModel: Access Strategy}
    A1 -- Remote Service --> S1([fileServer: Single Source of Truth])
    S1 --> S1a([serverIO: All Ops via RPC])

    A1 -- Caching --> S2([fileServer2])
    S2 --> C1[localCache1]
    S2 --> C2[localCache2]
    S2 --> C3[localCache3]

    A1 -- Replication --> R1([replicaNode1]) & R2([replicaNode2]) & R3([replicaNode3])
    R1 --> SVC[coordService: Consistency Protocol]
    R2 --> SVC
    R3 --> SVC
```

### 4.5 Network Resource Migration — Message Forwarding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P_A([peerA: External Peer]) --> MSG1[outgoingMsg]
    MSG1 --> FWD([fwdAgent: Forwarder on Source])
    FWD -- Tunnel --> MIG([migratedProc: Now on Destination])
    MIG --> RPLY[replyMsg]
    RPLY --> FWD2([fwdAgent2: Forwards Reply])
    FWD2 -- Tunnel --> P_A

    MIG --> notifyStep[notifyPeers: Send Redirect-ACK]
    notifyStep --> P_A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Forwarder Agent** is the indirection point. It stays alive until all peers acknowledge the new address, guaranteeing no message is lost during the migration window.

### 4.6 Writable Working Set Evolution (Geometric Decay)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1([round0: Pages = N]) --> W2[copy1: Transfer N]
    W2 --> W3([dirtySet1: f*N Pages Dirtied])
    W3 --> W4[copy2: Transfer f*N]
    W4 --> W5([dirtySet2: f^2*N])
    W5 --> W6[copy3: Transfer f^2*N]
    W6 --> W7([dirtySetK: f^k*N])
    W7 --> W8{stopCheck: f^k*N < threshold?}
    W8 -- No --> W3
    W8 -- Yes --> W9[stopCopyFinal: Send f^k*N]
    W9 --> W10([wwsDone: Migration Ends])
```

### 4.7 Side-by-Side: Pre-Copy vs Post-Copy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph preCopyBlock[Pre-Copy Strategy]
        preA([Source Node]) -- iterative Rounds --> preB([Destination Node])
        preA -- dirty tracking --> preA
        preB -- final stop-copy --> preA
    end

    subgraph postCopyBlock[Post-Copy Strategy]
        postA([Source Node]) -- exec state only --> postB([Destination Node])
        postB -- page faults --> postA
        postA -- pages on demand --> postB
    end
```

### 4.8 Cloud Live Migration Pipeline (Hypervisor View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VMS([vmSource: Running VM]) --> ST1[stage1: Pre-Copy Iterative]
    ST1 --> ST2[stage2: Stop-and-Copy]
    ST2 --> ST3[stage3: VM Paused Briefly]
    ST3 --> ST4[stage4: Resume at Destination]
    ST4 --> VMD([vmDest: VM Now on New Host])
    VMS -. leftover state .-> VMD
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
