## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **Define process migration. List the four major components of a process state that must be transferred during migration.**

**Model Answer:**

*Process migration* is the act of relocating a running process from one node (source) to another node (destination) in a distributed system, *transparently*, without restarting it. The four major components of process state are:

1. **Execution state** – program counter, register set, kernel stack, signal masks.
2. **Memory state** – code, data, stack, heap pages, and page/segment tables.
3. **File and device state** – open file descriptors, current working directory, pending I/O buffers.
4. **Communication state** – open sockets, RPC bindings, pending messages in transit.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the four components.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Distinguish between eager (whole-process) and lazy (on-demand) memory migration. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Eager (All) Migration | Lazy (On-Demand) Migration |
|---|---|---|
| *When memory moves* | All at once, *before* process resumes | Incrementally, on first access |
| **Advantage** | Simple; no residual dependency | Minimum freezing time; scalable to large processes |
| **Disadvantage** | High freezing time; huge bandwidth burst | High page-fault rate; source remains pinned |

*(3 marks: 1 for contrast, 1 for each advantage/disadvantage pair.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU pattern):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

#### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q.A(a). [7 Marks]**
> **Explain the complete process migration mechanism in a distributed system. Describe the six phases with a neat diagram.**

**Model Solution:**

*Definition and motivation (2 marks):*
Process migration is the runtime relocation of a process from one machine to another, performed to achieve *load balancing*, *improved communication locality*, *fault tolerance*, and *reconfigurable resource use*. The system must preserve the *transparent* execution of the process: the process, its peers, and the user should ideally not notice the move.

*Six Phases (5 marks):*

1. **Initiation Phase** – A migration request is raised either by a system-level policy (e.g., load index exceeds threshold) or by the process itself. Decision metrics: CPU load, memory pressure, communication locality.

2. **Negotiation / Selection Phase** – A suitable *target node* is selected. Resources (CPU, memory, network) are *reserved* at the destination. The target must be alive, have capacity, and ideally be topologically close.

3. **Pre-copy Phase (optional but common)** – Iterative copying of *memory pages* from source to destination while the process continues to run. Each round copies pages dirtied in the previous round. (See 3.1 derivation.)

4. **Suspension + Stop-copy Phase** – The source process is *frozen*. Remaining dirty pages (the Writable Working Set) and the *execution state* are copied. This is the only phase that contributes to *freezing time*.

5. **Hand-off / Transfer Phase** – File descriptors, sockets, RPC bindings, signal masks, and other kernel state are transferred and reconstructed at the destination. Communication state is updated (forwarding agents installed, peers notified).

6. **Resumption + Cleanup Phase** – The process is *resumed* at the destination. Source-side residual state is released. A *redirect* is sent to all known peers so future messages arrive at the new address.

*Diagram (refer to Section 4.1).*

---

> **Q.A(b). [7 Marks]**
> **With a clear diagram and analytical model, compare Pre-copy and Post-copy memory migration strategies. Derive the geometric bound for pre-copy.**

**Model Solution:**

*Pre-Copy (3 marks):*
In pre-copy, memory is copied in *iterative rounds* before the process is suspended.

- Round 1 copies all $N$ pages.
- Subsequent rounds re-copy pages dirtied in the previous round.

Let $f$ = fraction of pages dirtied per round.

$$T(R) = N \cdot \frac{1 - f^R}{1 - f} \quad ; \quad T(\infty) = \frac{N}{1-f}$$

Overhead ratio: $\dfrac{f}{1-f}$. Pre-copy is unsuitable for write-intensive workloads.

*Post-Copy (3 marks):*
In post-copy, *only* the execution state is transferred initially. The destination process is resumed immediately; pages are *demand-paged* from the source on page faults.

$$C_{post} = C_{resume} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \cdot t_{fault}(i)$$

- Freezing time ≈ zero.
- Residual dependency: source acts as a paging server.
- Sensitive to access pattern; cold processes suffer.

*Comparison Table (1 mark):*

| Criterion | Pre-copy | Post-copy |
|---|---|---|
| Freezing Time | Low (1 round + stop) | *Minimum* |
| Residual Dependency | Very Low | High |
| Sensitivity to $f$ | High | Moderate |
| Best for | Read-mostly | Latency-critical |

*Diagrams: see Section 4.2 and 4.3.*

---

#### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks)

> **Q.B(a). [7 Marks]**
> **Explain the file migration mechanism in distributed systems. Discuss the three classical file access models and the strategies for file migration with examples.**

**Model Solution:**

*Introduction (1 mark):*
File migration is the dynamic relocation of file data (or its references) closer to the process that is using it, to minimise *remote access latency* and *server load*.

*Three File Access Models (4 marks):*

1. **Remote Service Model** – The file server *owns* the file; every read/write is a remote procedure call. Simple, consistent, but slow.

2. **Caching Model** – Files (or blocks/pages) are *cached* at the client. The server is contacted only on cache miss or for write-back. Improves performance dramatically; introduces *cache coherence* issues. Example: **AFS, NFS with client-side cache**.

3. **Replication Model** – The file is *replicated* across multiple servers. Reads can be served from any replica; writes require a *consistency protocol* (e.g., primary-backup, quorum). Example: **Google File System (GFS), HDFS**.

*File Migration Strategies (2 marks):*

- **Whole-File Migration** – The entire file is shipped to the client cache (e.g., **Sprite Network File System**).
- **Page/Block-Level Migration** – Only accessed units are shipped (e.g., modern NFS).
- **Prefetching** – Anticipated pages are shipped in advance based on access pattern.

---

> **Q.B(b). [7 Marks]**
> **Describe network resource migration. Explain the three classical mechanisms for redirecting communication to a migrated process, with their advantages and limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

*Introduction (1 mark):*
Network resource migration is the redirection of *active network endpoints* (TCP sockets, RPC bindings, message channels) belonging to a process that has been migrated. It is the *hardest* part of process migration because peers may be in the middle of an open connection.

*Three Mechanisms (5 marks):*

1. **Message Forwarding (Indirection):**
   - The source node retains the *old address* and runs a *forwarder agent*.
   - Incoming messages for the migrated process are *tunneled* to the destination.
   - *Advantage:* Transparent to peers; no protocol change.
   - *Limitation:* Source becomes a bottleneck; forwarder must remain alive until all peers acknowledge.

2. **DNS-Based Redirection:**
   - The *hostname-to-IP* mapping in DNS is updated to point to the destination.
   - *Advantage:* Simple, works for new connections.
   - *Limitation:* DNS caching delays propagation; **existing TCP connections cannot be migrated**.

3. **IP Address Take-Over:**
   - The destination node *adopts* the source's IP and MAC (via gratuitous ARP, Mobile IP, or similar L2/L3 mechanism).
   - *Advantage:* External packets physically arrive at the new location.
   - *Limitation:* Breaks firewalls, IPSec, TLS; complex with IPv4/IPv6 mobility.

*Conclusion (1 mark):*
The choice depends on the *workload*: forwarding suits long-running processes with many peers, IP take-over suits VMs and cloud workloads, DNS suits stateless/short-lived services.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing pre-copy and post-copy freezing time.** Many students write that "pre-copy has zero freezing time" — this is wrong. Pre-copy has *very low but non-zero* freezing time (the stop-copy phase). Post-copy has the *true minimum* freezing time.
> 2. **Forgetting the communication state.** When listing process-state components, students often leave out the *communication state* (sockets, RPC). This is a 2-mark deduction in KTU valuations.
> 3. **Mixing up file *access models* with file *migration strategies*.** These are two different questions. Access models describe *where data lives*; migration strategies describe *how data is moved*.
> 4. **Ignoring residual dependency.** When recommending a strategy, you must mention *residual dependency*, not just freezing time. Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for this trade-off discussion.
> 5. **Forgetting the WWS (Writable Working Set).** The pre-copy stopping criterion is *always* asked in KTU exams. Memorise the geometric series derivation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Migration of Memory, Files & Network Resources**

- ✅ **Process migration** = relocation of execution + memory + file + communication state across nodes.
- ✅ **Six phases:** Initiation → Negotiation → Pre-copy → Suspension+Stop-copy → Hand-off → Resume+Cleanup.
- ✅ **Freezing Time** = duration of *suspension + stop-copy* phases only.
- ✅ **Eager migration** = all memory at once, high freezing time, no residual dependency.
- ✅ **Lazy migration** = on-demand pages, low freezing time, high page-fault cost.
- ✅ **Pre-copy** uses iterative rounds; total transfer bounded by $\dfrac{N}{1-f}$; fails for high $f$.
- ✅ **Writable Working Set (WWS)** is the *stopping criterion* for pre-copy: stop when $\lvert WWS \rvert \le \delta$.
- ✅ **Post-copy** transfers only execution state; pages demand-paged from source; source becomes a paging server.
- ✅ **Flushing-based migration** is the technique used in **Xen / VMware live VM migration**.
- ✅ **File access models:** Remote Service, Caching, Replication.
- ✅ **File migration strategies:** Whole-File, Page-Level, Block-Level, Prefetching.
- ✅ **Sprite NFS** is the canonical whole-file migration example.
- ✅ **Network resource migration mechanisms:** (1) Message Forwarding, (2) DNS Update, (3) IP Address Take-Over.
- ✅ **Message forwarding** keeps the source alive as a forwarder until peers acknowledge; used in MOSIX, Sprite, Condor.
- ✅ **DNS redirection** works only for *new* connections — never for active TCP.
- ✅ **Key metrics:** Freezing Time, Residual Dependency, Migration Overhead, Page Fault Rate, WWS.
- ✅ **Pre-copy overhead ratio:** $\dfrac{f}{1-f}$ (explodes for write-intensive workloads).
- ✅ **Real-world cloud migration** (vMotion, Xen) = pre-copy rounds + stop-copy + brief pause.
- ✅ **Trade-off matrix:** Eager ↔ simple but slow; Pre-copy ↔ balanced; Post-copy ↔ fast resume but residual dep.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Mnemonics for the Exam Hall:**
> *"**E**ager is **A**ll, **L**azy is **L**ittle, **P**re-copy is **P**rolonged, **P**ost-copy is **P**unctual."*
> *"File access — **R**emote, **C**ache, **R**eplicate."*
> *"Network migration — **F**orward, **D**NS, **T**ake-over."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Cloud Computing # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (NIST SP 800-145) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cloud Computing** is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with **minimal management effort or service provider interaction**. This **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology)** definition is the global academic benchmark and is the standard expected in KTU board examinations. > [!NOTE] > **The cloud metaphor** refers to two things: > 1. The **abstraction** of underlying infrastructure (the user doesn't see the hardware). > 2. The **Internet** (depicted as a "cloud" in network diagrams since the 1990s). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Electricity Grid Imagine a city before and after the electricity grid was built: | Pre-Cloud Era (Owning a Generator) | Cloud Era (Paying for Electricity) | |---|---| | You buy a diesel generator for your house | You plug into a socket and pay only for the units consumed | | Maintenance, fuel, breakdowns — your headache | Provider handles generation, maintenance, and delivery | | Underutilized capacity sits idle | Capacity scales up/down dynamically with demand | | High upfront **Capital Expenditure (CapEx)** | Pure **Operational Expenditure (OpEx)** — pay-per-use | **Cloud Computing applies this same "utility" model to compute, storage, and software.** Just as you don't generate your own electricity, you don't need to physically own servers or run data centers to deploy applications. --- ## 1.3 The Five Essential Characteristics of Cloud Computing (NIST) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU frequently asks for these **5 characteristics** as a 3-mark or 7-mark question. Memorize this list verbatim. 1. **On-Demand Self-Service** — A consumer can provision compute/storage automatically without human interaction with the provider. 2. **Broad Network Access** — Services are reachable over the network through standard mechanisms (HTTP, REST, etc.) used by diverse client platforms (mobile, laptop, IoT). 3. **Resource Pooling** — Provider's resources are pooled to serve multiple tenants (multi-tenant model) with physical/virtual resources dynamically reassigned according to demand. 4. **Rapid Elasticity** — Capabilities can be **elastically** provisioned and released, scaling outward or inward commensurate with demand. To the consumer, the capability often appears **unlimited**. 5. **Measured Service** — Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging metering at a level appropriate to the service (storage, processing, bandwidth, active user accounts). Resource usage is **monitored, controlled, and reported** providing transparency for both provider and consumer. --- ## 1.4 Key Terminology Checklist - **Tenant:** A single organization or consumer using cloud resources. - **Multi-tenancy:** A single software instance serves multiple tenants, with logical isolation between them. - **Hypervisor:** A piece of system software (firmware) that creates and runs **Virtual Machines (VMs)**. Examples: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM, Xen. - **SLA (Service Level Agreement):** A formal contract between provider and consumer defining quality metrics (uptime, latency, support response). - **Workload:** Any application, service, or job executing on cloud resources. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Service Models (SPI Stack) The SPI model stands for **Software, Platform, Infrastructure** — the three fundamental layers of cloud service abstraction. ### A. Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) - Provides **fundamental computing resources**: processing, storage, networks, and raw VMs. - The consumer can deploy arbitrary software (OS, middleware, applications). - **Provider's responsibility:** Hardware, networking, storage, hypervisor, host OS. - **Consumer's responsibility:** Guest OS, application, data, runtime. - **Examples:** Amazon EC2, Google Compute Engine (GCE), Microsoft Azure VMs, DigitalOcean Droplets, Linode. ### B. Platform as a Service (PaaS) - Provides a **deployment platform** where the consumer can deploy their own applications using supported programming languages, libraries, services, and tools. - The consumer does **not** manage the underlying cloud infrastructure (network, servers, OS, storage). - **Examples:** Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku, Microsoft Azure App Service, Red Hat OpenShift. ### C. Software as a Service (SaaS) - The consumer uses the **provider's applications running on a cloud infrastructure**. - Applications are accessible from various client devices via a thin client (e.g., web browser) or a program interface. - The consumer does not manage or control the underlying infrastructure **or even individual application capabilities** (except limited user-specific configuration). - **Examples:** Gmail, Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, Salesforce, Dropbox, Zoom, Slack. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Memory Trick — "I-P-S = Infrastructure, Platform, Software"** > Think of the responsibility moving UP the stack: In IaaS, you manage the most; in SaaS, you manage almost nothing. The provider's responsibility grows in the opposite direction. ### Comparison Table | Aspect | On-Premises | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Applications** | User | User | User | Provider | | **Data** | User | User | User | User | | **Runtime** | User | User | Provider | Provider | | **Middleware** | User | User | Provider | Provider | | **OS** | User | User | Provider | Provider | | **Virtualization** | User | Provider | Provider | Provider | | **Servers** | User | Provider | Provider | Provider | | **Storage** | User | Provider | Provider | Provider | | **Networking** | User | Provider | Provider | Provider | --- ## 2.2 The Four Deployment Models > [!IMPORTANT] > These four are commonly tested as a 3-mark short-answer or as a sub-part of a 14-mark question. 1. **Private Cloud** — Provisioned for **exclusive use by a single organization** comprising multiple business units. May be owned/managed/operated by the organization, a third party, or a combination. Hosted on-premises or off-premises. 2. **Community Cloud** — Provisioned for **exclusive use by a specific community of consumers** with shared concerns (e.g., mission, security requirements, policy, compliance). Examples: Government cloud, healthcare cloud. 3. **Public Cloud** — Provisioned for **open use by the general public**. May be owned/managed/operated by a business, academic, or government organization (or a combination). Exists on the premises of the cloud provider. 4. **Hybrid Cloud** — A composition of **two or more distinct cloud infrastructures** (private, community, or public) that remain unique entities, but are bound together by standardized or proprietary technology enabling **data and application portability** (e.g., cloud bursting for load balancing between clouds). --- ## 2.3 Virtualization — The Engine of the Cloud Virtualization is the **core enabling technology** of cloud computing. It allows a single physical machine (host) to be partitioned into multiple isolated virtual machines (guests), each with its own OS. ### Type-1 (Bare-Metal) Hypervisor Runs **directly on the host's hardware** to control the hardware and manage guest OSes. No host OS is required. - **Examples:** VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V (Server Core), Citrix XenServer, KVM. ### Type-2 (Hosted) Hypervisor Runs **as a software layer on top of a conventional host OS**. Useful for development and testing. - **Examples:** Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels Desktop. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Metrics Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All metrics below are part of the formal KTU syllabus assessment for cloud computing fundamentals. | # | Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Availability** $A$ | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Dimensionless ratio or % | Quantifies uptime of a service. Critical for SLA design. | | 2 | **Annual Downtime** | $T_{down} = (1 - A) \times 8760 \text{ hours}$ | Hours/year | Used to compare SLA tiers (e.g., "three nines" vs "four nines"). | | 3 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = C_{capex} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{C_{opex,t}}{(1+r)^t}$ | Currency | Compares owning infrastructure vs paying for cloud services. | | 4 | **Elasticity (Scale Factor)** | $S = \dfrac{R_{max}}{R_{avg}}$ where $R$ = resource usage | Dimensionless | Measures peak-to-average resource ratio. Cloud aims for $S \to 1$. | | 5 | **Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Tasks Completed}}{\text{Time Unit}}$ | Tasks/sec or req/sec | Performance benchmark for cloud services. | | 6 | **Latency** | $\text{Latency} = T_{transmit} + T_{propagate} + T_{queue} + T_{process}$ | Milliseconds (ms) | Critical for real-time cloud applications. | | 7 | **Storage Cost per GB-Month** | $C_{storage} = \dfrac{\text{Monthly Storage Bill}}{\text{Provisioned GB}}$ | \$/GB/month | Tier selection in object storage. | | 8 | **Energy Efficiency (PUE)** | $PUE = \dfrac{\text{Total Facility Power}}{\text{IT Equipment Power}}$ | Dimensionless $\geq 1$ | Green data-center design metric. Google achieved **1.10 PUE**. | | 9 | **Scalability Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Performance at Peak}}{\text{Cost at Peak}}$ | Per \$ | Measures how cost-effectively a system scales. | | 10 | **Multi-tenancy Isolation Index** | $I = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Shared Resource Contention Events}}{\text{Total Requests}}$ | $0 \le I \le 1$ | Security/performance isolation between tenants. | > [!TIP] > **SLA Availability Tiers (commonly tested)** > - **"Two Nines" (99%):** ~7.2 hours downtime/month > - **"Three Nines" (99.9%):** ~43.2 minutes/month > - **"Four Nines" (99.99%):** ~4.32 minutes/month > - **"Five Nines" (99.999%):** ~26.3 seconds/month # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: SLA Availability vs. Annual Downtime This derivation is the **single most frequently asked calculation** in KTU 2019–2024 board papers for cloud and distributed systems. ### Given - **Availability** $A$ is defined as the probability that a system is operational at any random time. - A year contains $365 \times 24 = 8760$ hours. ### Step 1 — Define the Downtime Fraction The fraction of time the system is **unavailable** is simply the complement of availability: $$\text{Downtime Fraction} = 1 - A$$ ### Step 2 — Convert Fraction to Hours per Year Multiply the downtime fraction by the total number of hours in a year: $$T_{down, \text{annual}} = (1 - A) \times 8760 \text{ hours}$$ ### Step 3 — Convert to Minutes and Seconds To make the number physically interpretable for SLA tiers, we convert hours to minutes (×60) and seconds (×60 again): $$T_{down, \text{min}} = (1 - A) \times 8760 \times 60 \text{ minutes}$$ $$T_{down, \text{sec}} = (1 - A) \times 8760 \times 3600 \text{ seconds}$$ ### Step 4 — Worked Example: "Three Nines" (99.9%) Let $A = 0.999$. Substitute into the formula: $$T_{down} = (1 - 0.999) \times 8760 = 0.001 \times 8760 = 8.76 \text{ hours/year}$$ Convert to minutes: $$T_{down, \text{min}} = 8.76 \times 60 = 525.6 \text{ minutes/year}$$ Convert to per-month average (divide by 12): $$T_{down, \text{monthly}} = \dfrac{525.6}{12} = 43.8 \text{ minutes/month}$$ > [!NOTE] > This **43.8 minutes/month** is the canonical "three nines" downtime figure cited in industry SLAs (AWS, Azure, GCP all advertise this tier for compute services). ### Step 5 — Verification with the MTBF/MTTR Identity The MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) and MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) define availability. Rearranging: $$A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$ For $A = 0.999$, if we set $\text{MTBF} = 999$ hours, then $\text{MTTR} = 1$ hour, giving a ratio of $999 / 1000 = 0.999$. This is internally consistent. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) — On-Prem vs Cloud ### Given - $C_{capex}$ = one-time capital cost (servers, networking, data center). - $C_{opex,t}$ = annual operational cost (power, cooling, admin, licenses) in year $t$. - $r$ = annual discount rate. - $N$ = evaluation period in years. ### Step 1 — Present Value of All Future OpEx A dollar in year $t$ is worth $\dfrac{1}{(1+r)^t}$ dollars today. Sum the discounted OpEx over $N$ years: $$PV_{opex} = \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{C_{opex,t}}{(1+r)^t}$$ ### Step 2 — Total TCO Add the upfront capital expenditure: $$\boxed{TCO = C_{capex} + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{C_{opex,t}}{(1+r)^t}}$$ ### Step 3 — Cloud OpEx (Pay-Per-Use) For cloud, there is no $C_{capex}$. The user pays a **per-unit consumption price** $p$ (e.g., \$/hour for a VM). If the average active usage is $U$ hours per year: $$TCO_{cloud} = \sum_{t=1}^{N} \dfrac{p \times U_t}{(1+r)^t}$$ ### Step 4 — Break-Even Analysis The break-even point occurs when $TCO_{on-prem} = TCO_{cloud}$. For uniform usage and $r=0$: $$C_{capex} + N \times C_{opex} = N \times p \times U$$ Solve for the critical number of years $N^*$: $$N^* = \dfrac{C_{capex}}{p \times U - C_{opex}}$$ If $p \times U > C_{opex}$, the cloud becomes cheaper after $N^*$ years. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: PUE and Data-Center Energy ### Given - $P_{IT}$ = power consumed by IT equipment (servers, storage, network gear). - $P_{cooling}$ = power for cooling (chillers, CRAC units). - $P_{lighting}$ + $P_{misc}$ = overhead loads. ### Step 1 — Total Facility Power The **Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE)** is the ratio of the total power entering the data center to the power used by IT equipment: $$PUE = \dfrac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}} = \dfrac{P_{IT} + P_{cooling} + P_{lighting} + P_{misc}}{P_{IT}}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Overhead Fraction A PUE of 1.0 is the **theoretical ideal** (no overhead). The overhead fraction is: $$f_{overhead} = PUE - 1 = \dfrac{P_{cooling} + P_{lighting} + P_{misc}}{P_{IT}}$$ ### Step 3 — Worked Example Suppose a data center uses $P_{IT} = 1000$ kW, and total facility draw is $P_{total} = 1500$ kW. Compute the PUE: $$PUE = \dfrac{1500}{1000} = 1.5$$ This means **50% of the energy is overhead** (cooling, lighting, etc.). Modern hyperscale cloud providers target $PUE \approx 1.1$, meaning only 10% overhead — a **5x efficiency gain** over the 1.5 example. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Service Model Decision Tree | Decision Question | If YES → Choose | If NO → Choose | |---|---|---| | Do you want to avoid all software installation? | **SaaS** | Continue to next | | Do you want to focus only on your code/data? | **PaaS** | Continue to next | | Do you need full control over OS, middleware, runtime? | **IaaS** | **On-Premises** | | Do you have strict data-residency/regulatory requirements? | **Private / Community** | **Public / Hybrid** | | Is your workload highly variable / spiky? | **Public (elastic)** | **Reserved / Private** | | Is your workload steady-state and predictable? | **Reserved IaaS / Private** | Spot / burst to public | > [!TIP] > **Mark Allocation Tip (KTU Valuation):** When asked to "compare SaaS, PaaS, IaaS," examiners award 2 marks for a clear table, 1 mark for at least one example per layer, and 1 mark for the responsibility assignment (who manages what). Do not forget examples. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cloud Computing Reference Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Client Devices
Mobile, Laptop, IoT, Thin Client] --> B[Internet Network Edge
CDN, Load Balancer, Firewall] B --> C[Cloud Service Layer
SaaS Apps, PaaS Runtimes, IaaS VMs] C --> D[Virtualization & Orchestration Layer
Hypervisor, Kubernetes, OpenStack] D --> E[Physical Resource Layer
Servers, Storage Arrays, Network Fabric] E --> F[Data Center Facility Layer
Power, Cooling, Physical Security] F --> E E --> D D --> C C --> B B --> A G[Management & Monitoring Plane
SLA, Billing, IAM, Metering] -.-> C G -.-> D H[Tenants and Users] --> A ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **inner loop** (E ↔ D ↔ C) represents the data-plane hierarchy from physical hardware up to delivered services. - The **dotted lines** from `Management & Monitoring Plane` represent the **control plane** that meters usage, enforces SLAs, and authenticates users. - The client entry point flows **downward** through edge services (CDN, firewall) before reaching the service layer. --- ## 4.2 Service Model Stack (SPI Layered Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SaaS_Layer [SaaS Layer - Provider Manages Everything] S1[Gmail] S2[Microsoft 365] S3[Salesforce CRM] end subgraph PaaS_Layer [PaaS Layer - Provider Manages Platform] P1[AWS Elastic Beanstalk] P2[Google App Engine] P3[Heroku] P4[Azure App Service] end subgraph IaaS_Layer [IaaS Layer - Provider Manages Infrastructure] I1[Amazon EC2] I2[Google Compute Engine] I3[Azure Virtual Machines] I4[DigitalOcean Droplets] end subgraph HW_Layer [Physical Hardware Layer - Provider Managed] H1[Servers] H2[Storage Arrays] H3[Network Switches] H4[Data Center Facility] end SaaS_Layer --> PaaS_Layer PaaS_Layer --> IaaS_Layer IaaS_Layer --> HW_Layer ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Each layer is **built on top of** the layer below it. - As you move **upward** (SaaS direction), the **consumer's management burden decreases** while the **provider's burden increases**. - As you move **downward** (IaaS direction), the **consumer gains flexibility** but must manage more components themselves. --- ## 4.3 Deployment Model Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Organization Needs Cloud]) --> Q1{Data Sensitivity
or Compliance?} Q1 -->|High| Q2{Single Organization
Exclusive Use?} Q1 -->|Low| Q3{Shared Mission
with Partner Orgs?} Q2 -->|Yes| Private[Private Cloud
On-Prem or Hosted] Q2 -->|No| Community[Community Cloud
Industry or Government] Q3 -->|Yes| Community Q3 -->|No| Q4{Burst Capacity
Needed?} Q4 -->|Yes| Hybrid[Hybrid Cloud
Private + Public] Q4 -->|No| Public[Public Cloud
AWS, Azure, GCP] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Start at the top with the organizational need. - Follow the decision branches based on **data sensitivity**, **community/shared mission**, and **capacity-bursting needs**. - The four terminal nodes are the four canonical NIST deployment models. --- ## 4.4 Virtualization Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph GuestVMs [Guest Virtual Machines] VM1[VM1: Linux Ubuntu
App: Web Server] VM2[VM2: Windows Server
App: Database] VM3[VM3: Linux Alpine
App: Microservice] end subgraph Hypervisor [Hypervisor Layer Type 1 Bare Metal] HVis[VMware ESXi / KVM / Hyper-V] end subgraph Hardware [Physical Host Hardware] CPU[CPU and Memory] Disk[Storage Disks] Net[Network Interface Cards] end GuestVMs --> Hypervisor Hypervisor --> Hardware HVis -. Manages and Allocates .-> CPU HVis -. Manages and Allocates .-> Disk HVis -. Manages and Allocates .-> Net ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **hypervisor** sits between the guest VMs and physical hardware. - The hypervisor **abstracts** the physical resources and **partitions** them into isolated virtual resources for each guest. - Each guest VM runs an **independent OS** and is unaware of the other guests sharing the hardware. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define Cloud Computing. List any FOUR essential characteristics of cloud computing as per the NIST definition.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Cloud computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (networks, servers, storage, applications, services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction (NIST SP 800-145). **Four Essential Characteristics (2 marks — ½ mark each):** 1. **On-demand self-service** — Consumers provision compute resources automatically without human interaction. 2. **Broad network access** — Capabilities are available over the network and accessed through standard mechanisms by various client platforms. 3. **Resource pooling** — Provider resources are pooled in a multi-tenant model to serve multiple consumers dynamically. 4. **Rapid elasticity** — Capabilities can be elastically scaled out or in, appearing unlimited to the consumer. *(Alternatively, the 5th is "Measured Service" — metering at appropriate granularity.)* --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS with one example for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks — table format preferred):** | Parameter | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS | |---|---|---|---| | **What is provided** | Raw compute, storage, network resources | Deployment platform with runtime, middleware, OS | Ready-to-use software applications | | **Consumer manages** | OS, middleware, runtime, apps, data | Apps and data only | Only user configuration | | **Example** | Amazon EC2, Google Compute Engine | Google App Engine, Heroku | Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce | --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, Part B questions from Module 4 carry 14 marks and provide **internal choice**. Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, adapted]** **(a)** Explain the **NIST reference architecture of cloud computing** with a neat block diagram. Clearly identify the roles of **cloud consumer, cloud provider, cloud broker, cloud auditor, and cloud carrier**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **four cloud deployment models** in detail with appropriate real-world examples. Compare their advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **Block Diagram (3 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR Consumer[Cloud Consumer
Uses cloud services] -->|Service request| Broker[Cloud Broker
Intermediary] Broker -->|Forwards optimized request| Provider[Cloud Provider
AWS, Azure, GCP] Provider -->|Provisions resources| Carrier[Cloud Carrier
Network connectivity] Consumer <-->|Secured access via| Carrier Auditor[Cloud Auditor
Independent evaluator] -.->|Assesses| Consumer Auditor -.->|Assesses| Provider Auditor -.->|Assesses| Broker ``` **Roles (4 marks — 1 mark each approximately):** 1. **Cloud Consumer** — The individual or organization that acquires and uses cloud services. The consumer browses a service catalog, provisions services, monitors usage, and manages SLAs. 2. **Cloud Provider** — The entity responsible for making the service available to consumers. They manage the underlying infrastructure, deploy and configure software, and implement security and monitoring. 3. **Cloud Broker** — An intermediary that manages the use, performance, and delivery of cloud services. Brokers address issues like data integration, service customization, and negotiating SLAs across multiple providers. 4. **Cloud Auditor** — A third-party that conducts independent assessment of cloud services regarding security controls, privacy impact, and performance. Audits provide assurance to consumers. 5. **Cloud Carrier** — The intermediary that provides connectivity and transport of cloud services between providers and consumers (e.g., telecom operators, ISPs). #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **1. Private Cloud (1.5 marks):** - Provisioned for **exclusive use by a single organization** (multiple business units). - Owned/managed by the organization, a third party, or both. Hosted on- or off-premises. - **Examples:** Bank of America's internal private cloud, US Department of Defense cloud. - **Advantages:** Full control, high security and customization, regulatory compliance. - **Disadvantages:** High CapEx, underutilization, limited elasticity. **2. Community Cloud (1.5 marks):** - Shared by **several organizations with common concerns** (mission, security, policy, compliance). - **Examples:** Google "Gov Cloud" for government, healthcare consortium clouds. - **Advantages:** Cost sharing among community members, meets common compliance. - **Disadvantages:** Still requires significant investment, governance complexity. **3. Public Cloud (1.5 marks):** - Open to **general public**, owned by cloud service providers. - **Examples:** AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform, IBM Cloud. - **Advantages:** Massive elasticity, pay-per-use, no CapEx, global reach. - **Disadvantages:** Less control, data residency concerns, vendor lock-in. **4. Hybrid Cloud (2.5 marks):** - A composition of **two or more clouds** (private, community, public) bound by standardized technology. - Enables **data and application portability** (e.g., cloud bursting for load spikes). - **Examples:** An enterprise runs sensitive data in a private cloud and bursts overflow into AWS. - **Advantages:** Best of both worlds — privacy of private + elasticity of public. - **Disadvantages:** Complex integration, network latency between clouds, dual management overhead. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **three service models: IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS**. Discuss who manages what in each layer. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cloud provider offers an SLA guaranteeing **99.95% availability**. - **(i)** Calculate the **maximum allowed annual downtime in minutes**. **(3 marks)** - **(ii)** If the average **MTBF is 800 hours**, compute the **MTTR** the system must achieve to meet this SLA. **(4 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks **IaaS, PaaS, SaaS Diagram (3 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OnPrem[On Premises] O1[Applications] O2[Data] O3[Runtime] O4[Middleware] O5[Operating System] O6[Virtualization] O7[Servers and Storage] O8[Networking] end subgraph IaaS[IaaS Layer] I1[Applications - User] I2[Data - User] I3[Runtime - User] I4[Middleware - User] I5[Operating System - User] I6[Virtualization - Provider] I7[Servers and Storage - Provider] I8[Networking - Provider] end subgraph PaaS[PaaS Layer] P1[Applications - User] P2[Data - User] P3[Runtime - Provider] P4[Middleware - Provider] P5[Operating System - Provider] P6[Virtualization - Provider] P7[Servers and Storage - Provider] P8[Networking - Provider] end subgraph SaaS[SaaS Layer] SA1[Applications - Provider] SA2[Data - User] SA3[Runtime - Provider] SA4[Middleware - Provider] SA5[Operating System - Provider] SA6[Virtualization - Provider] SA7[Servers and Storage - Provider] SA8[Networking - Provider] end OnPrem --> IaaS IaaS --> PaaS PaaS --> SaaS ``` **Responsibility Explanation (4 marks):** - **IaaS:** Consumer manages OS, middleware, runtime, applications, and data. Provider manages virtualization, servers, storage, and networking. Example: Amazon EC2. - **PaaS:** Consumer manages applications and data only. Provider handles everything else. Example: Google App Engine. - **SaaS:** Consumer uses the application and manages only their own data. Provider manages the application, runtime, OS, and infrastructure. Example: Gmail. - **Key Insight:** As you move from IaaS to SaaS, the **provider's responsibility increases** and the **consumer's burden decreases**, leading to higher abstraction and faster time-to-market. #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks **(i) Maximum annual downtime in minutes (3 marks):** $$T_{down} = (1 - A) \times 8760 \times 60 \text{ minutes}$$ $$T_{down} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 8760 \times 60$$ $$T_{down} = 0.0005 \times 525{,}600 = 262.8 \text{ minutes/year}$$ **Answer: 262.8 minutes per year (≈ 4.38 hours/year)** **(ii) Compute the required MTTR given MTBF = 800 hours (4 marks):** Step 1 — Start with the availability formula: $$A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$ Step 2 — Substitute the known values $A = 0.9995$ and $\text{MTBF} = 800$: $$0.9995 = \dfrac{800}{800 + \text{MTTR}}$$ Step 3 — Cross-multiply: $$0.9995 \times (800 + \text{MTTR}) = 800$$ $$799.6 + 0.9995 \cdot \text{MTTR} = 800$$ Step 4 — Solve for MTTR: $$0.9995 \cdot \text{MTTR} = 800 - 799.6 = 0.4$$ $$\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{0.4}{0.9995} \approx 0.4002 \text{ hours}$$ Step 5 — Convert to minutes: $$\text{MTTR} \approx 0.4002 \times 60 \approx 24.01 \text{ minutes}$$ **Answer: MTTR ≈ 24 minutes** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 60 or 3600** when converting hours to minutes/seconds. Marks lost for unit errors. > 2. **Mixing up MTBF and MTTR:** Some students invert the formula. The correct form is $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$. > 3. **Not including all 5 NIST characteristics** in definition questions — listing only 3 or 4 costs marks. > 4. **Forgetting to provide examples** for service models — at least one example per model is expected. > 5. **Confusing "Deployment Models" with "Service Models"** — these are two distinct categorizations. Mixed-up answers fetch partial credit at best. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Cloud Computing (Module 4)** - **Definition Source:** NIST SP 800-145 is the authoritative definition. Memorize the phrasing. - **Five Essential Characteristics:** On-Demand Self-Service, Broad Network Access, Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, Measured Service. - **Three Service Models (SPI):** - **SaaS** — Provider does everything; user only uses the app. - **PaaS** — Provider manages the platform; user manages app + data. - **IaaS** — Provider manages hardware + virtualization; user manages OS upward. - **Four Deployment Models:** Private (one org), Community (shared mission), Public (anyone), Hybrid (combination of two or more). - **Key Actors:** Cloud Consumer, Cloud Provider, Cloud Broker, Cloud Auditor, Cloud Carrier. - **Virtualization:** Type-1 hypervisor runs on bare metal (faster); Type-2 runs on host OS (for dev/test). - **Availability Formula:** $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$. Memorize. - **SLA Tiers — Quick Reference:** - 99% → 7.2 hours/month - 99.9% → 43.8 minutes/month - 99.95% → 21.9 minutes/month - 99.99% → 4.38 minutes/month - 99.999% → 26.3 seconds/month - **PUE Formula:** $PUE = P_{total} / P_{IT}$, ideal value is 1.0. Industry target ≈ 1.1. - **TCO Formula:** $TCO = C_{capex} + \sum C_{opex,t} / (1+r)^t$. Compare on-prem vs cloud. - **Cloud vs. Grid vs. Cluster — Distinction:** - **Grid** → Loosely coupled, distributed, often for scientific workloads. - **Cluster** → Tightly coupled, homogeneous, single administrative domain. - **Cloud** → Elastic, multi-tenant, pay-per-use, virtualized resources. - **Real-World Mapping (Examples to Remember):** - IaaS → EC2, GCE, Azure VMs - PaaS → App Engine, Heroku, Beanstalk - SaaS → Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce, Dropbox - Public Cloud → AWS, Azure, GCP - Private Cloud → On-prem OpenStack, VMware vSphere - Hybrid Cloud → AWS Outposts, Azure Arc, Google Anthos - **Mnemonic for Service Model Order (bottom-up):** **"I Provide Services"** → IaaS, PaaS, SaaS. - **Mnemonic for NIST Actors:** **"A B C P C"** — Auditor, Broker, Consumer, Provider, Carrier. - **Elasticity vs. Scalability:** - **Scalability** = Ability to handle increased load by adding resources. - **Elasticity** = Ability to **automatically** scale in both directions (in and out) based on demand. - **Important Trade-offs:** Higher abstraction (SaaS) ⇒ less control but faster deployment. Lower abstraction (IaaS) ⇒ more control but more management overhead. - **Cost Models:** - **CapEx** → Capital Expenditure (upfront, on-prem). - **OpEx** → Operational Expenditure (ongoing, cloud pay-per-use). - **TCO** → Total Cost of Ownership (the apples-to-apples comparison).

topics.title: Microservices and Containers: Cloud Computing and Service Models - Private
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Microservices and Containers: Cloud Computing and Service Models - Private

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Cloud Computing

According to the **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) Special Publication 800-145**, Cloud Computing is defined as:

> A model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction.

The five **Essential Characteristics** of Cloud Computing (NIST SP 800-145) are:
1. **On-demand self-service**
2. **Broad network access**
3. **Resource pooling**
4. **Rapid elasticity**
5. **Measured service**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The three **Service Models** are classified as:
> - **SaaS** — Software as a Service
> - **PaaS** — Platform as a Service
> - **IaaS** — Infrastructure as a Service
>
> The four **Deployment Models** are: **Public, Private, Hybrid, and Community**.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition: Private Cloud

A **Private Cloud** (also known as an *Internal Cloud* or *Corporate Cloud*) is a cloud infrastructure provisioned for **exclusive use by a single organization** comprising multiple business units. It may be owned, managed, and operated by the organization, a third party, or a combination thereof, and it may exist on or off premises.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Unlike a Public Cloud, a Private Cloud is dedicated to a single tenant. The physical infrastructure may be located **on-premises (in-house)** or in a **dedicated off-site data center** managed by a third party (commonly called a *Hosted Private Cloud*).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of cloud computing like **utilities in your house**:

- **Public Cloud** → Like the **public electricity grid**. You share the same power plant infrastructure with thousands of other homes. You pay only for what you consume. Cheap, but you don't control the plant.
- **Private Cloud** → Like installing your **own private generator** in your house. You have full control, dedicated power, and it's not shared with neighbors. More expensive upfront, but offers better security, customization, and predictability.
- **Hybrid Cloud** → Like having **both a generator AND a connection to the city grid**. You use your private generator during peak hours or sensitive workloads, and switch to the public grid for overflow or non-sensitive work.
- **Community Cloud** → Like a **shared generator in a gated community of similar houses** (e.g., several government agencies or hospitals sharing infrastructure).

| Term | Plain-English Analogy |
|---|---|
| **IaaS** | Renting a plot of land to build your own house |
| **PaaS** | Renting a fully built house with utilities, you just bring furniture |
| **SaaS** | Renting a fully furnished hotel room — everything is ready |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid (KTU favorite question):**
> **S**aaS = You eat the **S**andwich (caller/user controls nothing below data)
> **P**aaS = You bring the **P**late (user controls app & data)
> **I**aaS = You build the **I**ngredients stack (user controls OS, storage, apps, data)

---

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

The following are the most commonly referenced metrics in Private Cloud design and examination:

- **Uptime SLA (Service Level Agreement)**: Typically **99.9% (Three Nines)** to **99.99% (Four Nines)** for enterprise private clouds.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**: Measured in hours; higher is better.
- **Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR)**: Measured in minutes; lower is better.
- **VM Consolidation Ratio**: Typically **10:1 to 20:1** in production private clouds.
- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)**: Maximum tolerable downtime.
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)**: Maximum tolerable data loss measured in time.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Private Cloud — The Operational Stack

A Private Cloud is not a single product; it is a **layered architectural pattern**. The following is the canonical operational stack examined at KTU:

### Layer 1: Physical Infrastructure Layer
- **Compute**: x86/x64 blade servers, GPU servers, ARM-based servers
- **Storage**: SAN (Storage Area Network), NAS (Network Attached Storage), Object Stores (S3-compatible)
- **Network**: 10/25/40/100 GbE fabric, SDN (Software-Defined Networking) controllers

### Layer 2: Virtualization & Hypervisor Layer
- **Type-1 Hypervisors (Bare Metal)**: VMware vSphere/ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM, Citrix XenServer
- **Type-2 Hypervisors (Hosted)**: Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation (rarely used in production private clouds)
- **Container Runtimes**: Docker Engine, containerd, CRI-O

### Layer 3: Cloud Management Platform (CMP) / Orchestration Layer
- **OpenStack** (most popular open-source private cloud platform)
- **VMware vCloud Suite**
- **Microsoft Azure Stack Hub**
- **Apache CloudStack**

### Layer 4: Service Delivery Layer
- Self-service portal
- Service catalog
- Chargeback / Showback engine
- Identity & Access Management (IAM)

### Layer 5: Service Models Delivered
- **IaaS** → VMs, raw storage, virtual networks
- **PaaS** → Application runtimes, middleware, databases (e.g., OpenShift, Cloud Foundry)
- **SaaS** → Hosted applications built on top

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A Private Cloud *must* deliver all 5 NIST essential characteristics. Just having a virtualized data center does NOT make it a private cloud — it only becomes a private cloud when it has **self-service, elasticity, and measured service**.

---

## 2.2 Private Cloud vs. Traditional Virtualization (Critical Distinction)

| Feature | Traditional Virtualization | Private Cloud |
|---|---|---|
| **Self-service provisioning** | Manual via admin | Automated via portal/API |
| **Multi-tenancy** | Single business unit | Multiple BUs / departments |
| **Elasticity** | Static capacity | Dynamic, on-demand scaling |
| **Metering / Chargeback** | Usually absent | Built-in metering |
| **Service Catalog** | Absent | Centralized catalog |
| **API-driven** | Rare | First-class RESTful APIs |
| **Resource Pooling** | Limited | Pooled across organization |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear frequently in KTU numericals and derivations.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **VM Consolidation Ratio** | $R_{consol} = \dfrac{N_{physical\_cores}}{N_{vCPUs\_allocated}}$ | Dimensionless; typical 10:1 to 20:1 |
| 2 | **Server Utilization Rate** | $U = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i \cdot W_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i} \times 100\%$ | $U_i$ = utilization of server $i$; $W_i$ = weight |
| 3 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = C_{capex} + \sum_{t=1}^{T} \dfrac{C_{opex,t}}{(1+r)^{t}}$ | $r$ = discount rate; $T$ = lifecycle years |
| 4 | **Availability (Uptime)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | All in hours |
| 5 | **Downtime per Year** | $D_{year} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ minutes | 525,600 = minutes in a year |
| 6 | **RTO / RPO** | Disaster recovery boundaries | Measured in minutes/hours |
| 7 | **Chargeback Rate** | $R_{charge} = \dfrac{C_{infra}}{U_{total}} \times U_{consumed}$ | $C_{infra}$ = infrastructure cost |
| 8 | **Capacity Headroom** | $H = C_{total} - C_{used}$ | Always $H \geq 15\%$ recommended |
| 9 | **Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE)** | $PUE = \dfrac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}}$ | Ideal $\approx 1.0$; typical 1.5–2.0 |
| 10 | **Storage IOPS Required** | $IOPS_{req} = IOPS_{per\_disk} \times N_{disks\,(RAID\,adjusted)}$ | RAID-10 ≈ $N \times IOPS$; RAID-5 ≈ $(N-1) \times IOPS$ |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Private clouds are deployed by:
- **Banking & Financial Services** (regulatory compliance: RBI, PCI-DSS)
- **Defense & Government** (data sovereignty requirements)
- **Healthcare** (HIPAA compliance for patient data)
- **Research Labs** (predictable HPC workloads)
- **Manufacturing** (sensitive IP and process data)

The primary engineering trade-off: **Control & Security vs. Cost & Agility**. Private clouds favor the former; public clouds favor the latter. This is why **Hybrid Cloud** (the dominant enterprise pattern in 2024–2026) emerged.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Private Cloud Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)

The **TCO** model is the most common KTU numerical for cloud deployment decisions. The goal is to compare the cost of running workloads on a Private Cloud vs. a Public Cloud over a period $T$ years.

### Given:
- Capital expenditure (CapEx) for private cloud setup: $C_{capex}$
- Annual operational expenditure (OpEx): $C_{opex}$ per year
- Discount rate: $r$ (typically 8–12%)
- Lifecycle: $T$ years

### Step-by-step derivation:

The Present Value (PV) of OpEx over $T$ years using the **annuity present value formula**:

$$
PV_{opex} = C_{opex} \times \left[ \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-T}}{r} \right]
$$

Total Cost of Ownership for the private cloud is:

$$
TCO_{private} = C_{capex} + C_{opex} \times \left[ \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-T}}{r} \right]
$$

The total cost of running the same workload on a Public Cloud is pure OpEx, paid annually. Its present value is:

$$
TCO_{public} = C_{opex,public} \times \left[ \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-T}}{r} \right]
$$

The break-even point occurs when $TCO_{private} = TCO_{public}$:

$$
C_{capex} + C_{opex,private} \times \left[ \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-T}}{r} \right] = C_{opex,public} \times \left[ \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-T}}{r} \right]
$$

Solving for the annuity factor:

$$
C_{capex} = \left[ C_{opex,public} - C_{opex,private} \right] \times \left[ \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-T}}{r} \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The private cloud is financially justified when the *savings in annual OpEx* (public minus private) over the lifecycle exceed the upfront CapEx investment.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Annual Downtime Calculation from Availability SLA

A Private Cloud provider promises an **SLA of 99.95%**. Calculate the maximum allowable annual downtime.

### Given:
- Availability: $A = 99.95\% = 0.9995$
- Minutes in a year: $525{,}600$

### Step-by-step calculation:

Unavailability fraction:
$$
U_{frac} = 1 - A = 1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005
$$

Annual downtime in minutes:
$$
D_{year} = U_{frac} \times 525{,}600
$$
$$
D_{year} = 0.0005 \times 525{,}600 = 262.8 \text{ minutes}
$$

Converting to hours:
$$
D_{hours} = \dfrac{262.8}{60} \approx 4.38 \text{ hours/year}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Reference Table** — Memorize these SLA-to-downtime conversions:
>
> | SLA | Downtime/Year |
> |---|---|
> | 99% (Two Nines) | 3.65 days |
> | 99.9% (Three Nines) | 8.76 hours |
> | 99.95% | 4.38 hours |
> | 99.99% (Four Nines) | 52.56 minutes |
> | 99.999% (Five Nines) | 5.26 minutes |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: VM Consolidation Ratio and Capacity Planning

A private cloud administrator must consolidate 200 physical servers (each 16-core) onto new hardware. The desired VM consolidation ratio is **12:1** (12 vCPUs per physical core). Calculate:
- (a) Total vCPUs required
- (b) Number of new physical servers needed (assuming 32-core servers)
- (c) Required headroom for peak loads (20% spare)

### Given:
- $N_{servers,old} = 200$
- $Cores_{old} = 16$ per server
- $R_{consol} = 12:1$
- $Cores_{new} = 32$ per server
- Headroom $H = 20\%$

### Step-by-step:

**(a)** Total existing physical cores:
$$
C_{total} = N_{servers,old} \times Cores_{old} = 200 \times 16 = 3{,}200 \text{ cores}
$$

Total vCPUs to be allocated:
$$
vCPUs_{total} = C_{total} \times R_{consol} = 3{,}200 \times 12 = 38{,}400 \text{ vCPUs}
$$

**(b)** New physical cores required (without headroom):
$$
Cores_{new,required} = \dfrac{vCPUs_{total}}{R_{consol}} = \dfrac{38{,}400}{12} = 3{,}200 \text{ cores}
$$

Wait — that is identical because the consolidation ratio converts cores to vCPUs and back. The correct method is to apply the consolidation ratio to determine **per-physical-core vCPU capacity**:

Required physical cores (raw):
$$
Cores_{raw} = \dfrac{vCPUs_{total}}{R_{consol}} = 3{,}200 \text{ cores}
$$

With 20% headroom:
$$
Cores_{final} = Cores_{raw} \times (1 + H) = 3{,}200 \times 1.20 = 3{,}840 \text{ cores}
$$

Number of new 32-core servers:
$$
N_{servers,new} = \left\lceil \dfrac{Cores_{final}}{Cores_{new}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{3{,}840}{32} \right\rceil = 120 \text{ servers}
$$

**(c)** Verification of headroom:
$$
U_{actual} = \dfrac{3{,}200}{3{,}840} \times 100\% \approx 83.3\%
$$

This satisfies the engineering best practice of keeping utilization below ~85%.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Private Cloud Service Models (KTU Comparison Table)

| Layer | IaaS (Private) | PaaS (Private) | SaaS (Private) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **User manages** | OS, Runtime, App, Data | App & Data only | Data only (or nothing) |
| **Provider manages** | Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Network | OS, Runtime, Middleware | All layers except possibly data |
| **Example Platform** | OpenStack, vSphere | OpenShift, Cloud Foundry | Hosted Exchange, Hosted ERP |
| **Use Case** | Lift-and-shift of legacy apps | Microservices deployment | End-user productivity |
| **Time to deploy** | Hours–Days | Minutes–Hours | Instant (sign-up) |
| **Skill required** | Sysadmin / DevOps | App developer | End user |
| **Control level** | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| **Abstraction** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Define Private Cloud. List any FOUR characteristics that differentiate it from a Public Cloud.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Private Cloud** is a cloud infrastructure provisioned for the exclusive use of a single organization. It may be owned, managed, and operated by the organization itself, a third party, or a combination, and may exist on or off premises.

**Four distinguishing characteristics (½ mark each):**

1. **Single-Tenancy**: Dedicated to one organization; resources are not shared with external tenants.
2. **Enhanced Security & Compliance**: Suitable for regulated industries (BFSI, Healthcare, Defense); provides data sovereignty.
3. **Greater Control**: The organization controls the physical infrastructure, network policies, and hypervisor configuration.
4. **Predictable Performance**: No "noisy neighbor" effect; resources are reserved for organizational workloads.
5. **Higher CapEx**: Requires upfront investment in hardware, virtualization, and management software.
6. **Customization**: Hardware, OS, and middleware can be tailored to specific organizational needs.

> [!TIP]
> **Allocation:** 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for four characteristics (½ mark each).

---
