## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Part B questions carry 14 marks. Each question below contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Series 1]**

**(a)** Explain the NIST reference model of Cloud Computing in detail. Discuss the **five essential characteristics**, **three service models**, and **four deployment models** with appropriate examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company is evaluating a Private Cloud deployment. The CapEx is **₹50,00,000** and the annual OpEx is **₹8,00,000**. The equivalent public cloud cost would be **₹18,00,000 per year** (OpEx only). Assume a discount rate of **10%** and a lifecycle of **5 years**. Calculate the TCO for both options using the annuity formula. Recommend which option is financially better. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(a) (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Cloud Computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort.

**Five Essential Characteristics (2 marks):**
1. **On-demand self-service** – Users provision resources automatically without human interaction.
2. **Broad network access** – Available over the network via standard mechanisms used by heterogeneous client platforms.
3. **Resource pooling** – Provider's resources are pooled to serve multiple consumers using a multi-tenant model.
4. **Rapid elasticity** – Capabilities can be elastically provisioned and released to scale with demand.
5. **Measured service** – Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging metering.

**Three Service Models (2 marks):**
- **SaaS**: Consumer uses provider's applications (e.g., Gmail).
- **PaaS**: Consumer deploys their own applications onto the cloud infrastructure (e.g., Google App Engine).
- **IaaS**: Consumer provisions processing, storage, networks, and other fundamental computing resources (e.g., AWS EC2).

**Four Deployment Models (2 marks):**
- **Private Cloud**: Exclusively for one organization.
- **Public Cloud**: Open for general public use.
- **Hybrid Cloud**: Composition of two or more clouds (private, public, community).
- **Community Cloud**: Shared by organizations with common concerns (e.g., mission, security, compliance).

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(b) (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $C_{capex} = ₹50,00,000$
- $C_{opex,private} = ₹8,00,000$ per year
- $C_{opex,public} = ₹18,00,000$ per year
- $r = 10\% = 0.10$
- $T = 5$ years

**Step 1: Compute the annuity present value factor (1 mark)**

$$
APV = \dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-T}}{r} = \dfrac{1 - (1.10)^{-5}}{0.10}
$$

$$
(1.10)^{-5} = \dfrac{1}{1.61051} \approx 0.6209
$$

$$
APV = \dfrac{1 - 0.6209}{0.10} = \dfrac{0.3791}{0.10} = 3.7908
$$

**Step 2: TCO for Private Cloud (2 marks)**

$$
TCO_{private} = C_{capex} + C_{opex,private} \times APV
$$

$$
TCO_{private} = 50{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 \times 3.7908
$$

$$
TCO_{private} = 50{,}00{,}000 + 30{,}32{,}640 = ₹80{,}32{,}640
$$

**Step 3: TCO for Public Cloud (2 marks)**

$$
TCO_{public} = C_{opex,public} \times APV
$$

$$
TCO_{public} = 18{,}00{,}000 \times 3.7908 = ₹68{,}23{,}440
$$

**Step 4: Comparison and Recommendation (2 marks)**

$$
\Delta TCO = TCO_{private} - TCO_{public} = 80{,}32{,}640 - 68{,}23{,}440 = ₹12{,}09{,}200
$$

**Conclusion:** The **Public Cloud** is financially cheaper by approximately **₹12.09 Lakhs** over the 5-year lifecycle. However, the recommendation must also consider **non-financial factors** (data sovereignty, compliance, performance). If the workload handles regulated data (e.g., PII, financial records), the Private Cloud's incremental cost may be justified.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> Students often forget the **annuity formula** and simply multiply annual cost by 5. This is **wrong** because it ignores the **time value of money**. Always discount future OpEx to present value.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Regular]**

**(a)** Discuss the **architecture of a Private Cloud** with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of the **Cloud Management Platform (CMP)** and **Hypervisor** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A private cloud promises an **SLA of 99.99%**. Calculate the **maximum allowable annual downtime** in minutes and hours. If the administrator improves the system to achieve an **MTBF of 8,000 hours** and **MTTR of 30 minutes**, verify whether the SLA target is met. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(a) (7 marks):**

**Architecture (Block Diagram — 3 marks):**

A Private Cloud architecture consists of the following five layers (bottom-up):
1. **Physical Infrastructure**: Servers, storage arrays, network switches.
2. **Virtualization Layer**: Hypervisors (KVM, ESXi) that abstract physical resources.
3. **Cloud Management Platform (CMP)**: Software like OpenStack that orchestrates and provisions resources via APIs.
4. **Service Delivery Layer**: Self-service portal, service catalog, IAM.
5. **Consumer Layer**: End users, applications, and tenant workloads.

**Role of Hypervisor (2 marks):**
The **Hypervisor** (also called Virtual Machine Monitor, VMM) is the software layer that creates, runs, and manages **Virtual Machines (VMs)**. It allows multiple operating systems to share a single physical host by partitioning hardware resources (CPU, memory, I/O). Type-1 hypervisors (ESXi, KVM) run directly on hardware, providing near-native performance suitable for private cloud workloads.

**Role of CMP (2 marks):**
The **Cloud Management Platform** (e.g., OpenStack, VMware vCenter) is the brain of the private cloud. It performs:
- **Resource orchestration** (Nova, vSphere)
- **Service catalog management**
- **Identity and tenant management** (Keystone, Active Directory integration)
- **Metering and chargeback**
- **API gateway** for programmatic access (REST APIs)

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Calculate SLA-based annual downtime (2 marks)**

Availability $A = 99.99\% = 0.9999$

Unavailability fraction:
$$
U = 1 - 0.9999 = 0.0001
$$

Annual downtime in minutes:
$$
D_{minutes} = 0.0001 \times 525{,}600 = 52.56 \text{ minutes}
$$

Converting to hours:
$$
D_{hours} = \dfrac{52.56}{60} = 0.876 \text{ hours} = 52 \text{ minutes } 33 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate actual achieved availability (3 marks)**

Convert MTTR to hours for unit consistency:
$$
MTTR = 30 \text{ minutes} = 0.5 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
A_{actual} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{8{,}000}{8{,}000 + 0.5}
$$

$$
A_{actual} = \dfrac{8{,}000}{8{,}000.5} \approx 0.9999375 = 99.99375\%
$$

**Step 3: Verify SLA compliance (2 marks)**

SLA target: $A_{SLA} = 99.99\%$

Achieved: $A_{actual} = 99.99375\%$

Comparison:
$$
A_{actual} > A_{SLA} \quad \text{since} \quad 99.99375\% > 99.99\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Yes, the system **meets** the SLA target. In fact, the actual availability (99.99375%) is **better** than the contracted SLA (99.99%) by 0.00375 percentage points, providing a safety margin equivalent to about **19.7 minutes of additional allowable downtime per year**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> 1. Students frequently forget to convert **MTTR units** to match **MTBF units** (hours vs. minutes). This yields wildly incorrect availability percentages.
> 2. Many students confuse **MTBF** (a reliability metric, measuring time *between* failures) with **MTTR** (a maintainability metric, measuring time *to repair*). They are NOT the same.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Comprehensive Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Cloud Computing Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Service Models with Deployment Models** — IaaS/PaaS/SaaS are *service* models; Public/Private/Hybrid are *deployment* models. They are orthogonal.
> 2. **Equating Virtualization with Cloud** — A virtualized data center is NOT automatically a private cloud. The 5 NIST essential characteristics must be present.
> 3. **Ignoring Non-Functional Requirements** — When comparing deployment models, always consider security, compliance, and performance — not just cost.
> 4. **Mixing Public and Private Cloud Properties** — "Public" and "Private" refer to the *access boundary*, not the *ownership*. A private cloud can be hosted by a third party.
> 5. **Forgetting Units in Numericals** — MTBF in hours, MTTR in minutes/hours, Downtime in minutes. Always declare units explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- ✅ **NIST definition** of Cloud Computing is the textbook-standard definition; quote it verbatim if possible.
- ✅ **5 Essential Characteristics**: On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, Measured service.
- ✅ **3 Service Models**: SaaS, PaaS, IaaS (in order of increasing user control and decreasing abstraction).
- ✅ **4 Deployment Models**: Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.
- ✅ **Private Cloud** = Single-tenant, dedicated to one org, can be on-premise or hosted.
- ✅ **Public Cloud** = Multi-tenant, open to general public, owned by provider.
- ✅ **Hybrid Cloud** = Private + Public with orchestration between them; enables **cloud bursting**.
- ✅ **Community Cloud** = Shared by organizations with common mission/compliance (e.g., government agencies).
- ✅ **TCO formula** uses **annuity present value** — never multiply annual cost by years directly.
- ✅ **SLA Nine Table**: 99% = 3.65 days, 99.9% = 8.76 hours, 99.99% = 52.56 minutes.
- ✅ **Availability formula**: $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- ✅ **VM Consolidation Ratio**: typically **10:1 to 20:1** in production environments.
- ✅ **Headroom**: Maintain **at least 15–20%** spare capacity for peak loads.
- ✅ **OpenStack** is the most widely-used open-source **Cloud Management Platform** for private clouds.
- ✅ **Type-1 Hypervisors** (bare-metal) are used in production; Type-2 (hosted) are for desktops.
- ✅ **Cloud bursting** is a key hybrid cloud pattern — peak load spills from private to public.
- ✅ **Chargeback vs. Showback**: Chargeback = actual billing; Showback = cost visibility without billing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Public and Hybrid Clouds, Cloud Design Objectives and Cost Model, Infrastructure-as-a-Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Public and Hybrid Clouds, Cloud Design Objectives and Cost Model, Infrastructure-as-a-Service

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Public Cloud

**Formal KTU Definition:**
A **Public Cloud** is a cloud computing model in which IT infrastructure (servers, storage, networking) and computing resources (platforms, software) are owned, managed, and operated by a third-party cloud service provider and delivered to the general public over the public internet. Resources are shared across multiple tenants (multi-tenancy) using a pay-per-use utility pricing model.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Public cloud is the *default cloud deployment model* referenced in NCC and IaaS modules. Key examples: **AWS**, **Microsoft Azure**, **Google Cloud Platform (GCP)**, **IBM Cloud**, **Oracle Cloud**.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Public Bus System":**
Think of the public cloud exactly like a **KSRTC city bus** in Kerala. You don't buy the bus; you simply pay a fare per kilometer traveled. The bus (infrastructure) is owned and maintained by the corporation (cloud provider). Hundreds of unrelated passengers (tenants) ride it every day. You only pay for the distance you cover, and the bus scales — more buses are added during rush hour (auto-scaling) and fewer during off-peak. You never worry about engine maintenance, fuel cost, or insurance — that is the provider's responsibility (undifferentiated heavy lifting).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Public Cloud Character Traits (High-Yield for KTU):**
> - **Multi-tenancy** — Resources are pooled and shared
> - **Pay-as-you-go (PAYG)** — Utility-style billing
> - **Elasticity** — Rapid scale-out and scale-in
> - **Internet-facing** — Accessed over the public internet
> - **No capital expenditure (CAPEX)** — Pure operational expenditure (OPEX)
> - **High availability SLA** — Providers offer **99.9% to 99.99%** uptime guarantees

---

### 1.2 Hybrid Cloud

**Formal KTU Definition:**
A **Hybrid Cloud** is a composite cloud deployment model that combines an **on-premises private cloud** (or legacy on-prem infrastructure) with a **public cloud**, enabling orchestrated data and application portability between the two through standardized or proprietary technology that allows orchestration, portability, and management across both environments.

> [!NOTE]
> **NIST SP 800-145 (the global standard KTU examiners love to reference):** Hybrid = "two or more distinct cloud infrastructures (private, community, or public) bound together by standardized or proprietary technology enabling data and application portability."

**Conceptual Analogy — "Your Home Kitchen + Swiggy/Zomato":**
You cook everyday meals at home (private cloud — sensitive, predictable workload) but when guests arrive unexpectedly, you order biriyani from Swiggy (public cloud — burst capacity). The **Hybrid Cloud** is exactly this orchestration: keep your confidential, regulated, or steady workloads at home, but seamlessly overflow to a public cloud for spikes, big-data analytics, or disaster recovery. The "Cloud Bursting" pattern is the hallmark of hybrid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Hook — Hybrid Cloud Connection Requirements:**
> - **VPN (Virtual Private Network)** — Site-to-site encrypted tunnels
> - **Direct Connect / ExpressRoute** — Dedicated private fiber lines
> - **API-based integration** — Programmatic orchestration
> - **Common data formats** — For seamless workload portability
> - **Unified management plane** — Single pane of glass (e.g., AWS Outposts, Azure Arc)

---

### 1.3 Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
**Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** is a category of cloud computing services in which the provider delivers fundamental computing, network, and storage resources on demand, on a pay-as-you-go basis. The consumer can provision processing power, storage, networks, and other fundamental computing resources and is allowed to deploy arbitrary software (OS, middleware, applications) while the provider manages the underlying physical infrastructure.

**Conceptual Analogy — "Renting an Empty Flat vs. Buying a House":**
Imagine you are shifting to Bangalore. Instead of buying a flat (owning physical servers — massive CAPEX), you **rent a fully-built but empty flat** from a landlord (the IaaS provider). The walls, plumbing, electricity, water (CPU, RAM, storage, network) are already wired. You bring your own furniture, paint, and appliances (OS, runtime, application, data). The landlord handles structural maintenance, security guard, and generator backup (physical security, power, cooling). If you need a bigger flat, you just upgrade your rent agreement — no renovation needed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IaaS = The "Bottom Layer" of the Cloud Stack — KTU Layering Mnemonic "SPI":**
> - **S** — Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) — Top
> - **P** — Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS) — Middle
> - **I** — Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) — Bottom
>
> In IaaS, the **user manages**: OS, Middleware, Runtime, Applications, Data.
> The **provider manages**: Servers, Storage, Networking, Virtualization, Physical Security.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Comparative Theoretical Analysis: Public vs. Hybrid Cloud

| Parameter | Public Cloud | Hybrid Cloud |
|---|---|---|
| **Ownership** | Third-party provider | Split between provider and on-prem |
| **Tenancy** | Multi-tenant | Mixed (private + public tenants) |
| **Cost Model** | Pure OPEX (PAYG) | Hybrid CAPEX + OPEX |
| **Control Level** | Low (provider-controlled) | Medium (workload-tiered control) |
| **Scalability** | Virtually unlimited | Effectively unlimited (via bursting) |
| **Security** | Provider-secured; shared responsibility | Customizable per workload tier |
| **Latency** | Higher (internet backbone) | Optimized (on-prem for sensitive paths) |
| **Compliance** | Limited to provider certifications | Customizable for regulated workloads |
| **Best For** | Startups, web apps, unpredictable load | Enterprises with regulated data |
| **Risk Profile** | Vendor lock-in, internet dependency | Integration complexity, dual skill sets |

---

### 2.2 Cloud Design Objectives — The Seven Pillars (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These seven objectives are **direct KTU module-level outcomes**. Expect 3-mark short notes and 7-mark descriptive questions.

1. **Scalability** — Ability to handle increasing load by adding resources. Two flavors:
   - *Vertical Scaling (Scale-Up)*: Add more CPU/RAM to a single VM
   - *Horizontal Scaling (Scale-Out)*: Add more VM instances behind a load balancer

2. **Elasticity** — Automatic, on-demand provisioning and de-provisioning. Distinguished from scalability by its **automated, dynamic, and time-bound** nature (scales up AND down).

3. **Availability** — Percentage of time the system is operational. Expressed as a Service Level Agreement (SLA):
$$\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}} \times 100\%$$

4. **Reliability** — Probability the system performs its intended function without failure over a specified period (often measured in **MTBF** — Mean Time Between Failures).

5. **Performance** — Throughput, latency, and response time under load. Often tied to **QoS** metrics.

6. **Security & Compliance** — CIA triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) plus regulatory adherence (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, India's DPDP Act 2023).

7. **Cost Optimization** — Achieving desired business outcomes at the lowest possible cost. The FinOps framework operationalizes this.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Cloud Cost Model

| Formula | Expression | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Total Cost of Ownership** | $TCO = CAPEX + OPEX + \text{Indirect Costs}$ | INR/USD | Compare cloud vs on-prem |
| **Pay-As-You-Go** | $C_{payg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times U_i)$ | Currency | Compute variable usage cost |
| **Reserved Instance ROI** | $ROI_{RI} = \frac{C_{payg} - C_{RI}}{C_{payg}} \times 100\%$ | Percent | Justify commitment discount |
| **Break-Even Utilization** | $U_{BE} = \frac{C_{reserved}}{P_{payg} \times T}$ | Hours/Year | Decide on-demand vs reserved |
| **Cloud Bursting Cost** | $C_{burst} = C_{base} + (P_{pub} \times \Delta t_{burst})$ | Currency | Hybrid overflow cost |
| **Availability SLA Downtime** | $D = (1 - A) \times T_{period}$ | Minutes/Year | Compute max allowable downtime |
| **Cost per Request** | $C_{req} = \frac{C_{infra}}{N_{requests}}$ | Currency/Request | Unit economics |

**Symbol Legend (Strict):**
- $P_i$ = Per-unit price of resource $i$ (e.g., INR/hour per vCPU)
- $U_i$ = Units consumed of resource $i$ (e.g., hours)
- $T$ = Time horizon (typically 8760 hours = 1 year)
- $A$ = Availability fraction (e.g., 0.9999)
- $C_{base}$ = Fixed cost of on-prem capacity
- $\Delta t_{burst}$ = Duration of burst into public cloud

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility in KTU Context:** A startup running a video streaming app on AWS EC2 must compute its monthly OPEX by summing compute hours, storage GB-months, and data transfer GB-out. The break-even utilization formula helps decide whether to book a 1-year Reserved Instance (40-60% discount) or stay on On-Demand PAYG.

---

### 2.4 Public Cloud Cost Model — Detailed Engineering Analysis

**Step 1: Variable Cost Quantification**
Every public cloud resource is metered at a per-unit rate. The total monthly bill is the **sum of metered usage across services**.

$$C_{monthly} = C_{compute} + C_{storage} + C_{network} + C_{misc}$$

**Step 2: Cost Drivers in Public Cloud**
- **Compute**: vCPU-hours, GB-RAM-hours, GPU-hours
- **Storage**: GB-month (charged even when idle)
- **Network Egress**: GB transferred out (most expensive cost vector)
- **API Calls**: Per-1000-requests pricing for serverless / managed services
- **Licensing**: BYOL (Bring Your Own License) vs. PAID (Provider-Administered License Included)

**Step 3: Pricing Models Available**
1. **On-Demand (PAYG)** — Highest unit cost, maximum flexibility, no commitment
2. **Reserved Instances (RI)** — 1- or 3-year commitment, **40% to 60%** discount
3. **Savings Plans** — Flexible commitment across regions/services, up to **72%** discount (AWS)
4. **Spot Instances** — Bid-based, up to **90%** discount, can be reclaimed
5. **Dedicated Hosts** — Physical server isolation for compliance

---

### 2.5 Hybrid Cloud Cost Model — The Engineering Reality

A hybrid model incurs **dual costs**:

$$C_{hybrid} = C_{private} + C_{public} + C_{interconnect} + C_{orchestration}$$

Where:
- $C_{private}$ = On-prem CAPEX amortized + power + cooling + admin
- $C_{public}$ = PAYG for burst workloads
- $C_{interconnect}$ = VPN bandwidth / Direct Connect port fees
- $C_{orchestration}$ = License for hybrid management tools (Azure Arc, Anthos, AWS Outposts)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Student Mistake:** Treating hybrid cloud as *cheaper* than pure public. In reality, hybrid incurs **20-35% additional operational overhead** due to dual skills, dual monitoring, and integration complexity. It is chosen for **compliance and latency**, not cost savings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked-Out Problem 1 — TCO Comparison (Cloud vs. On-Prem)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A startup in Kochi needs 10 servers (64 vCPU, 256 GB RAM each) for 3 years.
- **On-Prem Cost:** Each server = INR 8,00,000; Power + Cooling/year = INR 15,00,000; Admin staff = INR 12,00,000/year.
- **Cloud Cost (AWS equivalent):** INR 45/hour per server, 24x7 usage.
- **Discount Rate (WACC):** 8% per annum.

Determine the 3-year TCO for both options.

---

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate On-Prem CAPEX (Year 0)**

$$CAPEX_{onprem} = 10 \times 8,00,000 = 80,00,000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 2 — Annual OPEX (On-Prem)**

$$OPEX_{annual} = 15,00,000 + 12,00,000 = 27,00,000 \text{ INR/year}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Cloud Annual Cost**

Hours in a year $= 365 \times 24 = 8760$ hours

$$C_{cloud\_annual} = 45 \times 8760 \times 10 = 39,42,000 \text{ INR/year}$$

**Step 4 — Convert On-Prem to Present Value (PV) using annuity formula**

$$PV_{annuity} = PMT \times \frac{1 - (1 + r)^{-n}}{r}$$

Where $PMT = 27,00,000$, $r = 0.08$, $n = 3$

$$PV_{annuity} = 27,00,000 \times \frac{1 - (1.08)^{-3}}{0.08}$$

$$PV_{annuity} = 27,00,000 \times \frac{1 - 0.7938}{0.08}$$

$$PV_{annuity} = 27,00,000 \times \frac{0.2062}{0.08} = 27,00,000 \times 2.5771 = 69,58,170 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 5 — Total TCO On-Prem**

$$TCO_{onprem} = CAPEX + PV_{annuity} = 80,00,000 + 69,58,170 = 1,49,58,170 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 6 — Total TCO Cloud (3 years, all OPEX)**

$$TCO_{cloud} = 39,42,000 \times 3 = 1,18,26,000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Savings**

$$\text{Savings} = 1,49,58,170 - 1,18,26,000 = 31,32,170 \text{ INR}$$

$$\text{Savings \%} = \frac{31,32,170}{1,49,58,170} \times 100 \approx 20.94\%$$

**Conclusion:** Cloud saves approximately **20.94%** in 3-year TCO. The startup should migrate.

---

### 3.2 Worked-Out Problem 2 — Reserved Instance Break-Even Analysis

**Problem:**
An EC2 instance costs INR 45/hour on-demand. A 1-year Reserved Instance (No Upfront) costs INR 28/hour.
What is the **break-even utilization percentage** at which the Reserved Instance becomes more economical?

---

**Step 1 — Define Cost Functions**

$$C_{ondemand} = 45 \times H$$

$$C_{reserved} = 28 \times H$$

where $H$ = total hours used in 1 year ($H \le 8760$).

**Step 2 — Set Equal for Break-Even**

$$45 \times H = 28 \times H$$

This appears tautological because both are linear. The break-even here is at the *commitment* level. The real break-even is the **commitment threshold** below which on-demand is cheaper.

**Step 3 — Compute Discount Achieved**

$$\text{Discount \%} = \frac{45 - 28}{45} \times 100 = 37.78\%$$

**Step 4 — True Break-Even (when RI is fully utilized)**

RI is profitable if and only if:

$$H_{actual} \ge 0$$

Since RI has a fixed hourly commitment, the break-even is met **as long as the instance is used at all**. The instance becomes unprofitable only if utilization drops below:

$$U_{min} = \frac{45 - 28}{45} = 0.3778 = 37.78\%$$

**Interpretation:** A 1-year No-Upfront RI in this scenario is always cheaper than on-demand, but you must commit to pay for 8760 hours regardless. If the instance is shut down for more than ~62% of the year, the savings evaporate.

---

### 3.3 Worked-Out Problem 3 — Cloud Bursting Cost Model

**Problem:**
A college portal is hosted on a private cluster with capacity for 1000 concurrent users. During exam result day, it needs to handle 4000 concurrent users. The private cluster costs INR 50,000/month fixed. Public cloud costs INR 15 per user-hour beyond 1000.

Estimate the cost of bursting during a 6-hour exam result window.

---

**Step 1 — Determine Overflow**

$$N_{overflow} = 4000 - 1000 = 3000 \text{ users}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Burst Cost**

$$C_{burst} = 15 \times 3000 \times 6 = 2,70,000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with Provisioning Public for All 4000**

$$C_{full\_public} = 15 \times 4000 \times 6 = 3,60,000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 4 — Savings from Burst Strategy**

$$\text{Savings} = 3,60,000 - 2,70,000 = 90,000 \text{ INR (25% saved)}$$

**Step 5 — Plus Private Base Cost (already incurred)**

$$C_{total\_hybrid} = 50,000 + 2,70,000 = 3,20,000 \text{ INR}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Cloud bursting is the **canonical use case** of hybrid cloud. It lets the college retain a small, cost-predictable private footprint and pay only for public cloud during predictable event-driven spikes.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Best Fit | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Static, predictable workload, 24/7 | Reserved Instance / On-Prem | Steady utilization favors commitment discounts |
| Highly variable, unpredictable spikes | Pure Public + Auto-Scaling | Elasticity maximizes value |
| Regulated data (PHI, PII, financial) | Hybrid / Private | Compliance and data residency |
| Disaster Recovery | Public (cold storage) | Pay only for retention |
| Dev/Test environment | Spot Instances | 70-90% discount, fault-tolerant |
| AI/ML training | GPU Spot + Reserved base | Burstable compute for transient jobs |
| Legacy monolithic ERP | Private / Dedicated Host | Predictable license costs |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Cloud Deployment Models Taxonomy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Cloud Deployment Models] --> B[Public Cloud]
    A --> C[Private Cloud]
    A --> D[Hybrid Cloud]
    A --> E[Community Cloud]

    B --> B1[AWS, Azure, GCP]
    B --> B2[Multi-Tenant]
    B --> B3[Internet Accessible]
    B --> B4[PAYG Billing]

    C --> C1[On-Premises]
    C --> C2[Single Tenant]
    C --> C3[Full Control]
    C --> C4[CAPEX Heavy]

    D --> D1[Public + Private]
    D --> D2[Cloud Bursting]
    D --> D3[VPN / Direct Connect]
    D --> D4[Orchestrated Workloads]

    E --> E1[Shared by Organizations]
    E --> E2[Common Mission]
    E --> E3[Government / Defense]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — IaaS Service Stack and Responsibility Boundary

```mermaid
graph TD
    L1[Applications - User Managed] --> L2[Data - User Managed]
    L2 --> L3[Runtime / Middleware - User Managed]
    L3 --> L4[Operating System - User Managed]
    L4 --> L5[Virtualization - Provider Managed]
    L5 --> L6[Servers - Provider Managed]
    L6 --> L7[Storage - Provider Managed]
    L7 --> L8[Networking - Provider Managed]
    L8 --> L9[Physical Datacenter - Provider Managed]

    IaaS[IaaS Responsibility Cutoff] --> L4
    IaaS --> L5
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Hybrid Cloud Cloud-Bursting Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    User[End User] --> LB[Global Load Balancer]

    LB -->|Normal Load| Private[Private Cloud - On-Prem]
    LB -->|Spike Detected| Public[Public Cloud - Burst Pool]

    Private --> DB1[(On-Prem Database)]
    Public --> DB2[(Cloud Database - Read Replica)]

    DB1 <-->|Secure VPN Tunnel| DB2

    Monitor[Cloud Monitoring] --> LB
    Monitor -->|Auto-Scale Trigger| Public

    style Public fill:#FFE4B5
    style Private fill:#90EE90
    style LB fill:#ADD8E6
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Public Cloud Cost Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Workload Arrives] --> Q1{Predictable Steady Usage?}

    Q1 -->|Yes, 1-3 yr| Q2{Compliance Critical?}
    Q1 -->|No, Spiky| Q3{Fault Tolerant?}

    Q2 -->|Yes| Reserved[Reserved Instance 40-60 percent off]
    Q2 -->|No| Savings[Savings Plan 30-72 percent off]

    Q3 -->|Yes| Spot[Spot Instance up to 90 percent off]
    Q3 -->|No| OnDemand[On-Demand PAYG]

    Reserved --> Run[Provision VM]
    Savings --> Run
    Spot --> Run
    OnDemand --> Run
```

---

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Cloud Design Objectives Interrelationships

```mermaid
graph TD
    Scal[Scalability] --> Perf[Performance]
    Elas[Elasticity] --> Scal
    Avail[Availability] --> Reli[Reliability]
    Reli --> Avail
    Sec[Security and Compliance] --> Avail
    Cost[Cost Optimization] --> Elas
    Cost --> Scal
    Perf --> Sec

    style Scal fill:#FFD700
    style Elas fill:#FFD700
    style Avail fill:#87CEEB
    style Reli fill:#87CEEB
    style Sec fill:#FFB6C1
    style Cost fill:#90EE90
    style Perf fill:#DDA0DD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The yellow nodes (Scalability, Elasticity) are the *capacity* objectives; blue nodes (Availability, Reliability) are the *resilience* objectives; pink (Security) and green (Cost) are *cross-cutting* constraints that influence every other objective.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]**
*List any three characteristics of Public Cloud and explain the pay-per-use pricing model in brief.*

**Model Answer:**

**Three Characteristics of Public Cloud:**
1. **Multi-tenancy** — Infrastructure is shared by multiple organizations (tenants) using logical isolation. A single physical server can host VMs belonging to hundreds of customers.
2. **Internet Accessibility** — Services are delivered over the public internet using standard protocols (HTTP, REST, SSH).
3. **Pay-per-use / Utility Billing** — Customers are billed only for the resources consumed, measured in metered units (vCPU-hour, GB-month, GB-out).

**Pay-Per-Use Model Explained:**
The pay-per-use model is analogous to utility services such as electricity. The customer is billed based on **metered consumption** — compute hours, storage capacity used, and data transferred. There is **no upfront capital expenditure**; all expenditure is operational (OPEX). Billing is typically monthly, and customers can scale consumption up or down elastically. This model shifts IT spending from a **fixed-cost** paradigm to a **variable-cost** paradigm, improving cash flow and aligning cost with business value.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**
*What is a Hybrid Cloud? Mention two real-world use cases.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Hybrid Cloud** is a cloud deployment model that combines a private cloud (or on-premises infrastructure) with a public cloud, allowing workloads and data to be orchestrated between the two environments via standardized or proprietary connectivity (e.g., VPN, Direct Connect).

**Two Real-World Use Cases:**
1. **Cloud Bursting During Peak Load** — An e-commerce website hosted on a private cloud bursts into AWS during festive sales (e.g., Onam, Diwali) when traffic spikes to 5-10x normal levels. The private base handles 70% of traffic; the public overflow handles the rest.
2. **Regulatory Data with Analytics** — A bank stores customer PII in its on-premise private cloud (mandated by RBI), but pushes anonymized data to AWS for big-data analytics and ML model training — combining compliance with innovation.

---

### Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare Public Cloud and Hybrid Cloud deployment models across any **five** parameters. Explain Cloud Bursting with a suitable diagram.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A retail company is evaluating whether to migrate its on-premises 3-tier e-commerce application to AWS. The on-prem setup costs **INR 30,00,000 CAPEX** and **INR 8,00,000/month OPEX**. The AWS equivalent costs **INR 11,00,000/month** with **zero CAPEX**. Compute the **2-year TCO** for both and recommend the better option.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Parameter | Public Cloud | Hybrid Cloud |
|---|---|---|
| **Tenancy** | Multi-tenant shared infrastructure | Mixed — private (single-tenant) + public (multi-tenant) |
| **Cost Model** | Pure OPEX (PAYG) | Hybrid CAPEX + OPEX |
| **Scalability** | Virtually unlimited; auto-scaling built-in | Bounded by on-prem capacity; bursts to public |
| **Control & Security** | Lower (provider governs physical) | Higher (sensitive workloads stay on-prem) |
| **Connectivity** | Internet-based | Requires VPN, Direct Connect, or ExpressRoute |
| **Compliance** | Limited to provider certifications | Custom-fit for regulated data (PHI, PII) |
| **Vendor Lock-in** | Higher | Moderate (split workload reduces) |
| **Operational Overhead** | Low (provider handles infra) | High (dual ops skill set) |

**Cloud Bursting Explanation:**
Cloud bursting is the architectural pattern where an application primarily runs in a private cloud or on-premises data center, and *bursts* into a public cloud when demand exceeds the private capacity threshold. A **load monitor / orchestrator** triggers the elastic provisioning of additional VMs in the public cloud, with traffic distributed via a **Global Load Balancer**. The burst pool is decommissioned when the load subsides, and the customer is billed only for the burst duration.

*See the Mermaid Diagram 3 (Section 4.3) for the architectural visualization.*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — 2-Year TCO On-Premises**

$$CAPEX_{onprem} = 30,00,000 \text{ INR}$$

$$OPEX_{onprem\_24m} = 8,00,000 \times 24 = 1,92,00,000 \text{ INR}$$

$$TCO_{onprem} = 30,00,000 + 1,92,00,000 = 2,22,00,000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 2 — 2-Year TCO Cloud**

$$CAPEX_{cloud} = 0 \text{ INR}$$

$$OPEX_{cloud\_24m} = 11,00,000 \times 24 = 2,64,00,000 \text{ INR}$$

$$TCO_{cloud} = 0 + 2,64,00,000 = 2,64,00,000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison**

| Option | 2-Year TCO | Cost Difference |
|---|---|---|
| On-Prem | INR 2,22,00,000 | Baseline (cheaper) |
| Cloud (AWS) | INR 2,64,00,000 | +19.82% more expensive |

**Step 4 — Recommendation**

The **on-premises deployment is cheaper by INR 42,00,000** (approximately **18.92% savings**) over the 2-year horizon. However, the recommendation must consider **non-monetary factors**:
- Cloud offers **higher availability** (99.99% SLA vs. self-managed).
- Cloud offers **elasticity** for festive-season spikes.
- Cloud eliminates **CAPEX burden** and improves **cash flow**.
- Cloud migration is justified if **monthly OPEX growth** is expected to drop (e.g., Reserved Instances can reduce AWS cost by 40%).

**Final Recommendation:** If the workload is **stable and predictable**, retain on-prem. If future growth, scalability, or DR is anticipated, **migrate to AWS with 1-year Reserved Instances** — the TCO equation flips at approximately **Year 3**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** model with a neat architecture diagram. List **three** key characteristics and any **two** IaaS providers.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A startup deployed an EC2 On-Demand instance at INR 50/hour. The instance runs 24x7 for 1 year. A 1-year Reserved Instance (No Upfront) is offered at INR 30/hour. Calculate the annual savings in INR and the **percentage discount** achieved. Also explain the conditions under which the Reserved Instance becomes a poor financial decision.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**IaaS Definition:**
Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) is the foundational cloud service model in which a third-party provider delivers on-demand, scalable compute, storage, and networking resources over the internet. The consumer provisions virtualized resources via APIs or consoles, installs their own OS, middleware, runtime, and applications, and is billed on a metered basis.

**Architecture Diagram (refer Section 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2):**
The IaaS stack has a clear **responsibility cut-off line**. The user is responsible for: *Application, Data, Runtime, Middleware, OS, and Virtualization configuration*. The provider is responsible for: *Physical servers, storage hardware, network fabric, and datacenter facilities*.

**Three Key Characteristics of IaaS:**
1. **Resource Pooling & Multi-tenancy** — Provider pools compute, storage, and network resources and dynamically assigns them to multiple consumers on demand, using virtualization (hypervisors like KVM, VMware ESXi, Hyper-V).
2. **Broad Network Access** — Resources are accessible over the network via standard protocols (HTTP, SSH, RDP) and heterogeneous client platforms (laptops, mobiles, tablets).
3. **Measured Service (Pay-per-use)** — Cloud systems automatically meter and optimize resource use. Billing is transparent, with usage reports available via dashboards.

**Two IaaS Providers:**
1. **Amazon Web Services (AWS) — EC2, S3, EBS, VPC**
2. **Microsoft Azure — Virtual Machines, Blob Storage, Virtual Network**
3. *(Bonus)* Google Compute Engine (GCE), IBM Cloud, Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute Annual Cost (On-Demand)**

$$C_{ondemand} = 50 \times 8760 = 4,38,000 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Annual Cost (Reserved, No Upfront)**

$$C_{reserved} = 30 \times 8760 = 2,62,800 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 3 — Annual Savings**

$$\text{Savings} = C_{ondemand} - C_{reserved} = 4,38,000 - 2,62,800 = 1,75,200 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 4 — Discount Percentage**

$$\text{Discount \%} = \frac{4,38,000 - 2,62,800}{4,38,000} \times 100 = \frac{1,75,200}{4,38,000} \times 100 = 40\%$$

**Step 5 — Conditions When RI Becomes a Poor Decision:**

1. **Low Utilization (< 37.78% in this case)** — If the instance is shut down for more than ~62% of the year, the commitment cost exceeds actual usage. The break-even utilization for this case is:
$$U_{BE} = \frac{30}{50} = 0.60 = 60\% \text{ of the year}$$
If usage drops below ~5256 hours/year, on-demand would have been cheaper.

2. **Rapid Technology Obsolescence** — If a newer, cheaper instance type (e.g., Graviton, AMD EPYC) is launched mid-commitment, the RI locks the customer into older, less efficient hardware.

3. **Workload Migration or Project Cancellation** — A 1-year or 3-year RI cannot be cancelled; the customer pays for the entire term regardless of usage.

4. **Uncertain Demand Forecast** — Startups with shifting product-market fit may over-commit and waste the reserved capacity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Don't confuse "Reserved Instance" with "Reserved Hardware"** — RI is a *billing discount* applied to any instance of the chosen family in a region, not a physical hardware reservation (unless you buy a *Dedicated Host*).
> - **Always show units** in cost calculations. Marks are explicitly awarded for units like "INR/year" and "INR/hour."
> - **For TCO problems, show the formula before plugging in numbers** — the examiner looks for the *method* as much as the answer.
> - **Hybrid cloud is NOT cheaper than public** — losing 1-2 marks is common when students answer "hybrid saves money." The correct answer is "hybrid offers control and compliance."

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read This 1 Hour Before Exam**

- [x] **Public Cloud** = Third-party-owned, multi-tenant, internet-accessible, PAYG, OPEX-only.
- [x] **Hybrid Cloud** = Private + Public orchestrated; **NOT cheaper**, chosen for **compliance** and **bursting**.
- [x] **Cloud Bursting** = Private-first, public-on-spike; orchestrated via VPN or Direct Connect.
- [x] **Seven Design Objectives** = Scalability, Elasticity, Availability, Reliability, Performance, Security, Cost Optimization.
- [x] **Vertical vs. Horizontal Scaling** = Scale-Up (add RAM/CPU) vs. Scale-Out (add instances).
- [x] **IaaS** = User manages OS up; Provider manages hardware down. Mnemonic: **SPI** → SaaS / PaaS / IaaS.
- [x] **Top IaaS Providers** = AWS, Azure, GCP, IBM, Oracle. Mention at least two in any answer.
- [x] **Cost Models** = On-Demand (PAYG) > Savings Plan > Reserved Instance (40-60% off) > Spot (up to 90% off).
- [x] **TCO Formula** = CAPEX + OPEX (PV-adjusted) + Indirect Costs.
- [x] **PV Annuity Formula** must be remembered: $PV = PMT \times \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$.
- [x] **Availability SLA** = 99.9% (3-nines) = 8.77 hrs/year downtime; 99.99% (4-nines) = 52.6 min/year.
- [x] **Reserved Instance Break-Even** is the utilization threshold below which the discount is wasted.
- [x] **Hybrid Cloud Costs** = $C_{private} + C_{public} + C_{interconnect} + C_{orchestration}$ — never forget the last two terms.
- [x] **NIST SP 800-145** is the gold-standard definition source — cite it in definitional questions.
- [x] **Cloud Egress (outbound data)** is the *most expensive* line item in public cloud bills — remember this for FinOps questions.
- [x] **Three Deployment Models** + **Three Service Models (SPI)** = 9-cell matrix, frequently asked.
- [x] **Examples to memorize for KTU exams:** AWS EC2/S3, Azure VM/Blob, GCP Compute Engine/Cloud Storage.
- [x] **DPDP Act 2023** (India) is the modern compliance angle — mention for full marks in security questions.
- [x] **Multi-tenancy + Isolation** = the architectural hallmark of public cloud.
- [x] **Spot Instances** are reclaimable — never use for stateful, critical workloads.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Platform-as-a-Service
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Platform-as-a-Service

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)** is a cloud computing service model that delivers a **pre-configured, ready-to-use development and deployment platform** — including hardware, operating systems, storage, network capacity, middleware, databases, development tools, and runtime environments — *as a service over the Internet* on a pay-per-use basis. It enables developers to **build, test, deploy, run, and scale applications** without the burden of managing the underlying infrastructure (servers, storage, networking) or provisioning the development stack.

According to the **NIST SP 800-145** definition (which KTU references), PaaS is defined as:

> *"The capability provided to the consumer is to deploy onto the cloud infrastructure consumer-created or acquired applications created using programming languages, libraries, services, and tools supported by the provider. The consumer does not manage or control the underlying cloud infrastructure including network, servers, operating systems, or storage, but has control over the deployed applications and possibly configuration settings for the application-hosting environment."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** PaaS = Platform + Middleware + Development Tools + Runtime, delivered as a **fully managed, consumption-based cloud service**, abstracting infrastructure management away from developers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Furnished Cloud Kitchen" 🍳

Imagine three ways to start a restaurant business:

| Model | Analogy | Who Manages What? |
|---|---|---|
| **On-Premise (Traditional)** | You buy land, build a kitchen, buy utensils, hire electricians, plumbers, and chefs. | **You** manage *everything*. |
| **IaaS** | You rent an empty kitchen with gas and electricity — but you still buy ovens, mixers, and hire your own chefs. | You manage **platform + apps**; provider manages raw hardware. |
| **PaaS** | You walk into a **fully equipped, ready-to-cook commercial kitchen** — appliances pre-installed, pantry stocked, chef stations ready, billing per hour of kitchen usage. | You only **bring your recipes (apps)** and cook. |
| **SaaS** | You simply **order food from a restaurant** — you don't cook at all. | Provider manages *everything*; you only consume. |

**PaaS** is the "furnished kitchen" — Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Microsoft Azure App Service, Red Hat OpenShift, and Heroku are prime examples.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Physical/Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Bolded for Quick Recall:**
> - **SLA (Service Level Agreement)** — typically **99.9% ("three nines")** to **99.99% ("four nines")** for enterprise PaaS offerings.
> - **Pay-per-use billing** — billed per **second, minute, or hour** of resource consumption.
> - **Auto-scaling elasticity** — provisioning latency typically measured in **seconds to minutes**.
> - **Multi-tenancy** — a single platform instance serves multiple customers (tenants) with logical isolation.
> - **Statelessness** — PaaS runtimes favor stateless application components for horizontal scaling.

---

## 1.4 PaaS vs. IaaS vs. SaaS — Boundary Mapping

PaaS sits **between IaaS and SaaS** in the cloud stack. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes understanding the **responsibility boundary** — *what the cloud provider manages vs. what the customer manages*.

| Layer Managed | On-Premise | IaaS | **PaaS** | SaaS |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Application | You | You | You | Provider |
| Data | You | You | You | Provider |
| Runtime / Middleware | You | You | **Provider** | Provider |
| Operating System | You | You | **Provider** | Provider |
| Virtualization | You | Provider | **Provider** | Provider |
| Servers / Storage / Network | You | Provider | **Provider** | Provider |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The PaaS Stack

A PaaS environment is structured as a **layered stack**, with each layer offering a distinct abstraction. KTU expects students to be able to *draw and label* this stack from memory.

**Layered Stack (bottom → top):**

1. **Infrastructure Layer** — Physical servers, storage arrays, network fabric, data-center facilities. *Abstracted away from user.* (Often built on top of an underlying IaaS layer like AWS EC2, OpenStack, or VMware vSphere.)
2. **Virtualization & Container Layer** — Hypervisors (KVM, Xen, VMware ESXi) or **container runtimes** (Docker Engine, containerd, CRI-O). Provides workload isolation and density.
3. **Operating System & Kernel Layer** — Linux distributions (Ubuntu, RHEL, Alpine) optimized for the platform. Mostly transparent to the user.
4. **Middleware Layer** — The *heart* of PaaS. Includes:
   - **Application servers** (Tomcat, JBoss/WildFly, WebLogic, Node.js runtime, Python WSGI servers).
   - **Message brokers** (RabbitMQ, Apache Kafka, ActiveMQ).
   - **Caching systems** (Redis, Memcached).
   - **Service buses** and **API gateways**.
5. **Data Layer (PaaS Database Services)** — Managed databases: Amazon RDS, Google Cloud SQL, Azure Cosmos DB, MongoDB Atlas, etc.
6. **Development Tooling Layer** — SDKs, CLIs, IDE plugins, build tools, CI/CD pipelines, version-control integrations (GitHub, GitLab).
7. **Application Layer** — *User-controlled.* Your code lives here.

---

## 2.2 Core Characteristics of PaaS (KTU High-Yield Points)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are **exam-favorite characteristics** — KTU frequently asks for a list with short explanations:

- **a) High-Level Abstraction** — Developer is shielded from infrastructure management.
- **b) Pre-built Services & APIs** — Out-of-the-box functionality for messaging, queuing, authentication, push notifications, ML inference, etc.
- **c) Built-in Scalability & Load Balancing** — Horizontal and vertical scaling happen automatically.
- **d) Multi-tenancy** — Multiple developers/organizations share the same logical platform with strict isolation.
- **e) Integrated Development Lifecycle** — Code → Build → Test → Deploy → Monitor all happen within the platform.
- **f) Built-in Security & Compliance** — Patches, vulnerability scans, and compliance certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2, HIPAA) handled by the provider.
- **g) Pay-per-use Metering** — Granular billing based on compute-seconds, GB-hours, API calls, etc.
- **h) Polyglot Programming** — Supports multiple languages (Java, Python, Node.js, Go, Ruby, PHP, .NET, etc.).
- **i) Service Composition & Orchestration** — Ability to glue microservices together declaratively.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Markdown Convention:** Vertical bars in formulas below are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Resource Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{R_{used}}{R_{allocated}} \times 100\%$ | **%** | Measures PaaS container/pod efficiency. |
| 2 | **Cost per User / Request** | $C_{user} = \dfrac{T_{cost}}{N_{users}}$ | **₹ / user** | TCO analysis of platform usage. |
| 3 | **Availability (SLA)** | $A = \dfrac{T_{up}}{T_{up} + T_{down}} \times 100\%$ | **%** | Service uptime guarantee. |
| 4 | **Annual Downtime from SLA** | $D_{yr} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ | **minutes/year** | 525,600 = minutes in a year. |
| 5 | **Horizontal Scalability Factor** | $S_f = \dfrac{L_{peak}}{L_{avg}}$ | **dimensionless** | Auto-scaling trigger ratio. |
| 6 | **ROI of PaaS Migration** | $ROI = \dfrac{G - I}{I} \times 100\%$ | **%** | Migration cost-benefit justification. |
| 7 | **Throughput** | $Th = \dfrac{N_{req}}{T_{window}}$ | **req/s** | Platform load handling capacity. |
| 8 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{total\_runtime}}{N_{failures}}$ | **hours** | Reliability engineering. |
| 9 | **Mean Time to Recovery** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum T_{recovery}}{N_{incidents}}$ | **minutes** | Recovery efficiency metric. |
| 10 | **Tenant Density** | $TD = \dfrac{N_{tenants}}{N_{host\_machines}}$ | **tenants/host** | Multi-tenancy efficiency. |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PaaS is the **backbone of modern digital engineering**:

- **Startups** use Heroku / Vercel to ship MVPs in days without DevOps hiring.
- **Enterprises** use Red Hat OpenShift / Pivotal Tanzu for **private PaaS** behind their firewall.
- **AI/ML teams** use AWS SageMaker / Google Vertex AI as **specialized ML-PaaS** for model training and deployment.
- **IoT platforms** (AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub) are essentially *vertical PaaS* stacks for connected devices.
- **Serverless FaaS** (AWS Lambda, Azure Functions) is often considered an *evolution* of PaaS — sometimes called **PaaS 2.0**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** PaaS enforces a **12-factor app methodology** (Heroku's seminal guideline) — codebase, dependencies, config, backing services, build/release/run stages, processes, port binding, concurrency, disposability, dev/prod parity, logs, and admin processes. KTU expects familiarity with this.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Annual Downtime from an SLA Percentage

Many KTU numerical problems ask: *"If a PaaS provider guarantees 99.95% availability, what is the maximum allowed annual downtime?"*

**Given:**
- SLA Availability: $A = 99.95\% = 0.9995$
- Total minutes in a (non-leap) year: $T_{yr} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600$ minutes.

**Step 1 — Express the relationship between availability, uptime, and downtime.**

$$
A = \dfrac{T_{up}}{T_{up} + T_{down}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $T_{up} = T_{yr} - T_{down}$.**

$$
A = \dfrac{T_{yr} - T_{down}}{T_{yr}}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for $T_{down}$.**

$$
A \cdot T_{yr} = T_{yr} - T_{down}
$$

$$
T_{down} = T_{yr} - A \cdot T_{yr} = T_{yr} (1 - A)
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in the numerical values.**

$$
T_{down} = 525{,}600 \times (1 - 0.9995)
$$

$$
T_{down} = 525{,}600 \times 0.0005
$$

$$
T_{down} = 262.8 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 4 \text{ hours } 22 \text{ minutes } 48 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 99.95% SLA allows ~262.8 minutes of downtime per year.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PaaS Cost-Per-User (TCO Analysis)

**Scenario (KTU-style problem):** A startup migrates its e-commerce platform to a PaaS provider. The platform serves **N = 50,000** monthly active users. The total monthly PaaS bill (compute + storage + database + bandwidth) is **₹2,50,000**. Additionally, the team spends **₹75,000/month** on PaaS-managed services (monitoring, security, backups). Calculate the **cost per user per month** and the **annual TCO**.

**Step 1 — Identify the cost components.**

- Platform cost: $C_{plat} = \text{₹}2{,}50{,}000$
- Managed services cost: $C_{svc} = \text{₹}75{,}000$
- Number of users: $N = 50{,}000$

**Step 2 — Compute the total monthly cost.**

$$
C_{total} = C_{plat} + C_{svc}
$$

$$
C_{total} = 2{,}50{,}000 + 75{,}000 = 3{,}25{,}000 \text{ INR/month}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute cost per user per month.**

$$
C_{user} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{N}
$$

$$
C_{user} = \dfrac{3{,}25{,}000}{50{,}000} = 6.50 \text{ INR/user/month}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute annual TCO.**

$$
TCO_{annual} = C_{total} \times 12
$$

$$
TCO_{annual} = 3{,}25{,}000 \times 12 = 39{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR/year}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each user costs the company **₹6.50/month**, and the total annual platform TCO is **₹39 lakh**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Auto-Scaling Decision Threshold

**Scenario:** A PaaS application has a baseline of **B = 4 instances**. The auto-scaling policy is set to trigger a new instance when CPU utilization exceeds **U_{thr} = 70%**, and to scale down when it falls below **U_{down} = 30%**. The scaling step is **k = 2 instances**. Given the current load produces an average CPU utilization of **U_{cur} = 85%** across all instances, determine the new desired instance count.

**Step 1 — Check if scale-up is triggered.**

$$
U_{cur} > U_{thr} \implies 85\% > 70\% \quad \checkmark \text{ (Scale-Up Required)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of scale-up steps.**

$$
n_{steps} = \left\lceil \dfrac{U_{cur} - U_{thr}}{100 - U_{thr}} \right\rceil
$$

$$
n_{steps} = \left\lceil \dfrac{85 - 70}{100 - 70} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{15}{30} \right\rceil = \lceil 0.5 \rceil = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the new instance count.**

$$
N_{new} = B + (n_{steps} \times k)
$$

$$
N_{new} = 4 + (1 \times 2) = 6 \text{ instances}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the new per-instance load.**

$$
U_{new} = \dfrac{U_{cur} \times B}{N_{new}}
$$

$$
U_{new} = \dfrac{85 \times 4}{6} = 56.67\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The auto-scaler provisions **2 additional instances (total = 6)**, bringing per-instance CPU down to ~57%, safely below the 70% threshold.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — PaaS Provider Comparison

| Feature | **AWS Elastic Beanstalk** | **Google App Engine** | **Azure App Service** | **Red Hat OpenShift** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Deployment Type** | Public PaaS | Public PaaS | Public PaaS | Hybrid / Private PaaS |
| **Supported Languages** | Java, .NET, Python, Node, Go, PHP, Ruby | Java, Python, Node, Go, PHP, Ruby | .NET, Java, Node, Python, PHP | Any (Docker/Kubernetes) |
| **Underlying Orchestrator** | Amazon EC2 + Docker | Borg / Kubernetes | Azure Kubernetes Service | Kubernetes |
| **Pricing Model** | Pay-per-instance hour | Pay-per-instance hour + requests | Pay-per-plan tier | Subscription (node-based) |
| **Best Use Case** | AWS-centric web apps | Google Cloud-native apps | Microsoft-stack enterprises | On-prem / regulated industries |
| **Auto-scaling** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (very granular) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (via K8s HPA) |
| **Lock-in Risk** | Medium-High | Medium-High | Medium-High | Low (Kubernetes portable) |

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Categorization of PaaS Types

PaaS is broadly classified into **three sub-categories** by KTU:

1. **Public PaaS** — Delivered over public Internet (AWS, GCP, Azure). Lowest cost, multi-tenant, public-cloud shared infrastructure.
2. **Private PaaS** — Deployed inside an organization's firewall (e.g., OpenShift on-premises, Cloud Foundry). Higher control, compliance-friendly.
3. **Hybrid PaaS** — Combines public and private (e.g., Pivotal / Tanzu, IBM Cloud Pak). Sensitive workloads stay private; elastic bursts go public.

Specialized PaaS variants (commonly asked):

- **iPaaS (Integration PaaS)** — MuleSoft, Dell Boomi — for integrating SaaS/enterprise apps.
- **mPaaS (Mobile PaaS)** — Firebase, AWS Amplify, Azure Mobile Apps.
- **cPaaS (Communications PaaS)** — Twilio, Vonage — for embedding voice/video/SMS.
- **aPaaS (Application PaaS)** — OutSystems, Mendix — low-code application development.
- **FaaS (Function-as-a-Service)** — AWS Lambda — serverless evolution of PaaS.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The PaaS Layered Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A1["USER LAYER"]
        A["DEVELOPER"]
        B["END USER"]
    end

    subgraph A2["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        C["CUSTOM APPLICATION CODE"]
        D["DEPLOYED MICROSERVICES"]
    end

    subgraph A3["DEVELOPMENT TOOLING LAYER"]
        E["IDE and SDK"]
        F["CI CD PIPELINE"]
        G["VERSION CONTROL GIT"]
    end

    subgraph A4["MIDDLEWARE LAYER PAAS CORE"]
        H["APP SERVER TOMCAT JBOSS"]
        I["MESSAGE BROKER KAFKA RABBITMQ"]
        J["CACHE REDIS MEMCACHED"]
        K["API GATEWAY"]
    end

    subgraph A5["DATA LAYER PAAS DB SERVICES"]
        L["MANAGED RELATIONAL DB"]
        M["MANAGED NOSQL DB"]
        N["OBJECT STORAGE BLOB"]
    end

    subgraph A6["PLATFORM FOUNDATION LAYER"]
        O["OPERATING SYSTEM LINUX"]
        P["CONTAINER RUNTIME DOCKER"]
        Q["ORCHESTRATOR KUBERNETES"]
    end

    subgraph A7["INFRASTRUCTURE LAYER ABSTRACTED"]
        R["PHYSICAL SERVERS"]
        S["NETWORK FABRIC"]
        T["DATA CENTER FACILITY"]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A7

    style A4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    style A7 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style A1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
```

> **Reading Guide:** The **Middleware Layer (A4)** is highlighted in gold because it is the *defining heart* of PaaS — the layer that distinguishes PaaS from raw IaaS. The **Infrastructure Layer (A7)** is shown in green because, in PaaS, it is *abstracted away* from the user.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — PaaS Request Lifecycle (End-to-End Workflow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as Developer
    participant Git as Git Repository
    participant CI as CI CD Pipeline
    participant Reg as Container Registry
    participant Orch as PaaS Orchestrator
    participant Run as Runtime Instances
    participant DB as Managed Database
    participant User as End User

    Dev->>Git: 1. Push code commit
    Git->>CI: 2. Trigger build webhook
    CI->>CI: 3. Compile and run tests
    CI->>Reg: 4. Push container image
    Dev->>Orch: 5. Trigger deployment
    Orch->>Reg: 6. Pull latest image
    Orch->>Run: 7. Schedule pods and containers
    User->>Run: 8. Send HTTP request
    Run->>DB: 9. Query managed database
    DB-->>Run: 10. Return result set
    Run-->>User: 11. Return HTTP response
    Orch->>Run: 12. Auto scale based on load
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — PaaS vs. IaaS vs. SaaS Responsibility Matrix (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stack["RESPONSIBILITY STACK"]
        direction TB
        L1["APPLICATION"]
        L2["DATA"]
        L3["RUNTIME MIDDLEWARE"]
        L4["OPERATING SYSTEM"]
        L5["VIRTUALIZATION"]
        L6["HARDWARE NETWORK STORAGE"]
    end

    subgraph OnPrem["ON PREMISE"]
        OP1["USER"]
        OP2["USER"]
        OP3["USER"]
        OP4["USER"]
        OP5["USER"]
        OP6["USER"]
    end

    subgraph IaaS["IAAS"]
        IA1["USER"]
        IA2["USER"]
        IA3["USER"]
        IA4["USER"]
        IA5["PROVIDER"]
        IA6["PROVIDER"]
    end

    subgraph PaaS["PAAS"]
        PA1["USER"]
        PA2["USER"]
        PA3["PROVIDER"]
        PA4["PROVIDER"]
        PA5["PROVIDER"]
        PA6["PROVIDER"]
    end

    subgraph SaaS["SAAS"]
        SA1["PROVIDER"]
        SA2["PROVIDER"]
        SA3["PROVIDER"]
        SA4["PROVIDER"]
        SA5["PROVIDER"]
        SA6["PROVIDER"]
    end

    Stack ~~~ OnPrem
    Stack ~~~ IaaS
    Stack ~~~ PaaS
    Stack ~~~ SaaS

    style PaaS fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:4px
```

> **Reading Guide:** The **gold-highlighted PaaS column** shows the **responsibility boundary** — the user controls only the *Application* and *Data* layers, while the provider manages *everything from the runtime down*.

---
