topics.title: Discovery Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Discovery Service # 1. Discovery Service — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Discovery Service** is a foundational architectural pattern in distributed computing systems (especially **microservices**, **cloud-native**, and **IoT** ecosystems) that provides a **dynamic, runtime mechanism** for service instances to **register**, **locate**, and **communicate** with one another without hard-coded network locations (IP addresses, ports, or hostnames). In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** and the **Advanced Computing Systems** syllabus, a Discovery Service is a **highly available, fault-tolerant, and horizontally scalable registry** that maintains a real-time, eventually-consistent view of all active service instances and their network endpoints, enabling **loose coupling**, **elastic scalability**, and **resilient inter-service communication** in modern distributed architectures. The Discovery Service typically comprises three logical sub-components: 1. **Service Registry** — A replicated, distributed key-value store of service → instance mappings. 2. **Registration/Deregistration Mechanism** — Handles the lifecycle of service instances. 3. **Discovery/Query API** — Exposes interfaces (REST, DNS, gRPC) for clients to look up healthy instances. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation > [!NOTE] > **Imagine a Hospital Directory Desk** 🏥 > > You walk into a large hospital and need to find **Dr. Smith's** office. You don't know her room number, and worse — she might be moving between buildings today. What do you do? > > You approach the **Reception Desk** (the *Service Registry*). The receptionist has a **live, continuously updated list** of every doctor, their current building, floor, and room number. She tells you, "Dr. Smith is currently in **Block B, 3rd Floor, Room 312**." > > When Dr. Smith moves, she **pings the desk** to update her location (*self-registration*). If she goes on leave, her name is **removed** from the active list (*deregistration/heartbeat timeout*). The desk also **periodically calls** each doctor to confirm they're still available (*health check*). > > **You (the service consumer) never memorise static room numbers** — you just trust the desk. This is exactly what a Discovery Service does in a distributed system. ## 1.3 Why Discovery Service is a Non-Negotiable in Modern Systems | Problem in Static Configuration | How Discovery Service Solves It | |--------------------------------|--------------------------------| | Hard-coded IPs fail when containers restart | Dynamic registration on startup | | Load balancers become single point of failure | Client-side load balancing over registry | | Manual scaling requires code changes | Auto-scaling instances auto-register | | Service health unknown until failure | Continuous health checks | | Tight coupling between teams | Decoupled, contract-based discovery | ## 1.4 Key Metrics & Standards (Highlighted) - **Consistency Model:** Typically **AP** (Availability + Partition tolerance) under **CAP Theorem**, often using **eventual consistency**. - **Heartbeat Interval:** Typically **5–30 seconds** (industry default for Eureka, Consul). - **Service TTL (Time-To-Live):** **30–90 seconds** before an unresponsive instance is evicted. - **Replication Factor:** **3 or 5 nodes** (Quorum-based, e.g., Raft consensus in etcd/Consul). - **Standard Protocols:** **DNS (RFC 1035)**, **HTTP/REST**, **gRPC**, **mDNS (RFC 6762)**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Pillars of Discovery Service The Discovery Service is built on **five theoretical pillars** that every KTU student must internalize: ### Pillar 1: Service Registration - **Definition:** The process by which a service instance publishes its network coordinates (IP, port, metadata) to the registry upon startup. - **Mechanism:** The service sends a `POST /register` request containing: - **service_id** (unique UUID/hostname) - **address** (IP) - **port** - **health_check_url** - **tags/metadata** (version, region, zone) - **Why?** Decouples service lifecycle from client configuration. ### Pillar 2: Heartbeats & Health Checks - **Heartbeat:** Periodic `PUT /heartbeat` from service → registry. - **Health Check:** Registry actively probes the service (HTTP `/health`, TCP port check, gRPC `Health.Check`). - **Eviction Policy:** If **N consecutive heartbeats** are missed (typically 3), the instance is **evicted**. - **Formula:** $\text{Eviction Time} = \text{Heartbeat Interval} \times \text{Missed Threshold}$ ### Pillar 3: Service Discovery (Lookup) Two flavours: - **Pull-based (Client-Side Discovery):** Client queries registry, then chooses instance. - **Push-based (Server-Side Discovery):** A load balancer / sidecar queries registry and routes the request. ### Pillar 4: Service Deregistration - Triggered by **graceful shutdown** (SIGTERM) → `DELETE /deregister`. - Triggered by **hard failure** → registry evicts after heartbeat timeout. ### Pillar 5: Consistency & Replication - Registries use **distributed consensus** (Raft, Paxos) for replication. - Trade-off: Strong consistency vs. availability (CAP Theorem). ## 2.2 Core Discovery Patterns (KTU High-Yield) ### Pattern A: Client-Side Discovery Pattern ``` Client → Service Registry (query) → Returns list of instances Client → Selects instance (load balance) → Directly calls instance ``` - **Pros:** Fewer network hops, client controls load-balancing algorithm. - **Cons:** Tight coupling between client and registry; language-specific logic per client. - **Example:** Netflix **Eureka** + Ribbon. ### Pattern B: Server-Side Discovery Pattern ``` Client → Load Balancer (queries registry internally) → Forwards to instance ``` - **Pros:** Client is simple; logic centralised in LB. - **Cons:** Load balancer becomes a potential bottleneck; extra network hop. - **Example:** **AWS ELB**, **Kubernetes Service**, **NGINX Plus**. ### Pattern C: Service Registry Pattern - **Self-Registration:** Service registers itself. - **Third-Party Registration:** A separate process (e.g., Registrator, Kubernetes Controller) handles registration. ## 2.3 Real-World Implementations (Industry Reference) | Tool | Algorithm | Use Case | |------|-----------|----------| | **Netflix Eureka** | AP, eventual consistency | Spring Cloud microservices | | **HashiCorp Consul** | Raft consensus, CP/AP toggle | Multi-datacenter discovery | | **etcd** | Raft consensus, strong consistency | Kubernetes control plane | | **Apache ZooKeeper** | ZAB protocol | Hadoop, Kafka | | **Kubernetes DNS (CoreDNS)** | DNS-based, watches API server | K8s-native | | **AWS Cloud Map** | Proprietary | AWS cloud workloads | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **This is the single most important table to memorize for the KTU University Exam.** | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Value | |-----------|--------|----------------------|----------------| | Heartbeat Interval | $T_h$ | Time between successive heartbeats | $5\text{ s} \leq T_h \leq 30\text{ s}$ | | Missed Threshold | $N_{miss}$ | Number of failed heartbeats before eviction | $N_{miss} = 3$ (default) | | Eviction Time | $T_{evict}$ | $T_{evict} = T_h \times N_{miss}$ | $15\text{ s} \text{ to } 90\text{ s}$ | | Registry Availability | $A_r$ | $A_r = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ for $n$ replicas | $\geq 99.99\%$ | | Quorum Size | $Q$ | $Q = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$ for $n$ nodes | $Q=2$ for $n=3$ | | Discovery Latency | $L_d$ | $L_d = T_{query} + T_{network} + T_{LB}$ | $< 50\text{ ms}$ | | CAP Trade-off | — | AP (Eureka) vs. CP (etcd) | — | | DNS TTL | $TTL_{DNS}$ | Caching duration of DNS records | $30\text{ s} \text{ to } 300\text{ s}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL LATEX NOTE:** All absolute value expressions use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax. Example: $\vert x \vert$ is correctly written as `$\vert x \vert$`. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility In modern **cloud-native** engineering, Discovery Service is the **backbone** that enables: - **Blue-Green Deployments** (zero-downtime releases). - **Canary Releases** (rolling out to 1% of instances). - **Auto-Scaling** under variable load. - **Multi-Region Failover** in global systems. - **Service Mesh** integration (Istio, Linkerd use discovery as foundation). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Eviction Time Calculation This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU exams. Let us derive the **Eviction Time** of a service instance from the registry. ### Given: - A service instance sends heartbeats every $T_h$ seconds. - The registry evicts an instance after $N_{miss}$ consecutive missed heartbeats. - We assume heartbeats follow a **Poisson arrival process** for the purpose of failure modelling. ### Step 1: Define the Event Let $X_i$ be the indicator random variable for the $i$-th heartbeat: $$X_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if the } i\text{-th heartbeat arrives} \\ 0 & \text{if it is missed} \end{cases}$$ ### Step 2: Expected Time Between Heartbeats Since the service sends heartbeats at fixed intervals $T_h$, the expected inter-arrival time is: $$\mathbb{E}[T] = T_h$$ ### Step 3: Total Time Until Eviction If $N_{miss}$ heartbeats are missed consecutively, the total elapsed time is: $$T_{evict} = N_{miss} \times T_h$$ ### Step 4: With Probabilistic Network Delays If the network has packet loss probability $p$, then the probability of a missed heartbeat is $p$, and the probability of $N_{miss}$ consecutive misses (assuming independence) is: $$P(\text{eviction in window}) = p^{N_{miss}}$$ ### Step 5: Substituting Combining, the **expected time to eviction** is: $$T_{evict} = N_{miss} \times T_h + \epsilon_{clock}$$ where $\epsilon_{clock}$ accounts for clock drift (typically $\pm 1\text{ s}$ in NTP-synchronized systems). ### Step 6: Numerical Example (KTU-style) If $T_h = 10\text{ s}$ and $N_{miss} = 3$: $$T_{evict} = 3 \times 10 = 30\text{ seconds}$$ **This means a crashed service instance will be removed from the registry within 30 seconds of failure — a critical SLO (Service Level Objective) for fault tolerance.** ## 3.2 Derivation: Registry Availability Under Replication ### Given: - $n$ identical registry nodes, each with availability $A_i = 0.99$. - The registry is considered available if **at least one node** responds (AP mode) or **a quorum** responds (CP mode). ### Step 1: Probability of Single Node Failure $$P(\text{node } i \text{ fails}) = 1 - A_i = 1 - 0.99 = 0.01$$ ### Step 2: Probability of All Nodes Failing (AP mode) $$P(\text{all fail}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i) = (0.01)^n$$ ### Step 3: Registry Availability (AP mode) $$A_r = 1 - (0.01)^n$$ ### Step 4: For $n = 3$ (Eureka default): $$A_r = 1 - (0.01)^3 = 1 - 0.000001 = 0.999999 = 99.9999\%$$ This is the **"five nines"** availability — only **31.5 seconds of downtime per year**. ### Step 5: For CP mode (Quorum = 2 of 3) $$A_{r,CP} = 1 - \left[ (0.01)^3 + \binom{3}{2}(0.01)^2(0.99) \right]$$ $$A_{r,CP} = 1 - [0.000001 + 3 \times 0.0001 \times 0.99]$$ $$A_{r,CP} = 1 - 0.0002971 = 99.97\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Insight:** AP mode trades strict consistency for higher availability. CP mode is preferred for systems where stale data causes catastrophic failures (e.g., leader election in Kubernetes). ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing a Discovery Pattern | Decision Criterion | Client-Side Discovery | Server-Side Discovery | Service Registry (Self) | Service Registry (3rd Party) | |-------------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------------------|------------------------------| | **Coupling** | High (client knows registry) | Low | Medium | Low | | **Network Hops** | 2 | 3 (LB adds one) | 2 | 2 | | **Load Balancing** | Client-side (Ribbon) | LB-side (NGINX) | Client-side | LB-side | | **Language Lock-in** | Yes (per-language SDK) | No | Yes | No | | **Best For** | Internal microservices | Public APIs, polyglot | Small deployments | Large, heterogeneous systems | | **Examples** | Eureka + Ribbon | AWS ALB, K8s Service | Eureka | Consul + Registrator | ## 3.4 Lifecycle State Diagram (Derivation of Transitions) A service instance moves through these states: $$\text{START} \rightarrow \text{REGISTERING} \rightarrow \text{HEALTHY} \rightleftarrows \text{UNHEALTHY} \rightarrow \text{EVICTED}$$ Transition probabilities: $$P(\text{HEALTHY} \to \text{UNHEALTHY}) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$ $$P(\text{UNHEALTHY} \to \text{HEALTHY}) = p_{recovery}$$ $$P(\text{UNHEALTHY} \to \text{EVICTED}) = 1 \text{ after } T_{evict}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Discovery Service Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB client1[Service Consumer A] client2[Service Consumer B] client3[Service Consumer C] reg[(Service Registry)] node1[Service Instance 1] node2[Service Instance 2] node3[Service Instance 3] hc[Health Checker] lb[Load Balancer] api[Discovery API] client1 -->|1 Query| api client2 -->|1 Query| api client3 -->|1 Query| api api -->|2 Lookup| reg reg -->|3 Return List| api api -->|4 Healthy Instances| client1 api -->|4 Healthy Instances| client2 api -->|4 Healthy Instances| client3 client1 -.->|5 Direct Call| node1 client2 -.->|5 Direct Call| node2 client3 -.->|5 Direct Call| node3 node1 -->|A Register| reg node2 -->|A Register| reg node3 -->|A Register| reg node1 -->|B Heartbeat| reg node2 -->|B Heartbeat| reg node3 -->|B Heartbeat| reg hc -->|C Probe Health| node1 hc -->|C Probe Health| node2 hc -->|C Probe Health| node3 lb -.->|D Route Traffic| reg ``` ## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Discovery Service in Action ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant S as Service Instance participant R as Service Registry participant C as Service Consumer participant LB as Load Balancer Note over S,R: Phase 1: Registration S->>R: POST /register (id=svc-1, ip=10.0.0.5, port=8080) R-->>S: 200 OK (registered) Note over S,R: Phase 2: Heartbeats loop Every T_h seconds S->>R: PUT /heartbeat (id=svc-1) R-->>S: 200 OK end Note over C,R: Phase 3: Discovery C->>R: GET /services/payment-service R-->>C: [svc-1: 10.0.0.5:8080, svc-2: 10.0.0.6:8080] Note over C,LB: Phase 4: Invocation C->>LB: Route to healthy instance LB->>S: Forward request S-->>LB: Response LB-->>C: Response Note over S,R: Phase 5: Deregistration (Graceful Shutdown) S->>R: DELETE /deregister (id=svc-1) R-->>S: 200 OK ``` ## 4.3 Discovery Pattern Comparison Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart LR start[Service Discovery Required] --> q1{Need Polyglot Clients?} q1 -->|Yes| server[Server-Side Discovery] q1 -->|No| q2{Need Custom Load Balancing?} q2 -->|Yes| client[Client-Side Discovery] q2 -->|No| q3{Large Scale, Many Services?} q3 -->|Yes| mesh[Service Mesh
Istio Linkerd] q3 -->|No| simple[Self-Registration
Eureka] server --> aws[AWS ELB / K8s Service] client --> ribbon[Eureka + Ribbon] mesh --> sidecar[Sidecar Proxy Discovery] simple --> reg[(Embedded Registry)] ``` ## 4.4 CAP Theorem Trade-off in Discovery ```mermaid flowchart TB cap{CAP Trade-off} cap -->|Choose AP| ap[Eureka Consul AP mode] cap -->|Choose CP| cp[etcd ZooKeeper Consul CP mode] ap --> apro[High Availability
Eventual Consistency
Stale Reads Possible] cp --> cpro[Strong Consistency
Lower Availability
Blocking on Partition] apro --> use1[Social Media Feeds
Gaming Leaderboards] cpro --> use2[Distributed Locks
Leader Election
Config Management] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `client1`, `reg`) and labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text — fully compliant with Mermaid v10 syntax. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define Discovery Service. List any two challenges in implementing it in a distributed microservices architecture.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (2 Marks):** A Discovery Service is a mechanism that enables dynamically located service instances to register themselves with a central registry and allows other services to discover and communicate with them at runtime, eliminating hard-coded network locations. - **Challenges (1 Mark):** 1. **Service instance churn** — high turnover makes registry consistency difficult. 2. **Network partitions** — may split the registry (CAP trade-off). 3. (Any other valid point: security, latency, scalability.) ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Differentiate between Client-Side and Server-Side Service Discovery with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Client-Side | Server-Side | |--------|-------------|-------------| | Lookup responsibility | Client | Load Balancer | | Example | Eureka + Ribbon | AWS ELB | | Coupling | High | Low | | Extra hop | No | Yes (via LB) | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** With a neat architecture diagram, explain the working of a **Service Registry-based Discovery Service**. Discuss the **registration**, **heartbeat**, and **eviction** mechanisms in detail. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Eviction Time** of a service instance given heartbeat interval $T_h = 15\text{ s}$ and missed threshold $N_{miss} = 4$. What is its impact on system availability? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Architecture & Working (7 Marks):** 1. **Service Registry** — A logically centralised, replicated database storing `service_id → (IP, port, metadata)` mappings. *(1 mark)* 2. **Registration Phase** *(2 marks)*: - On startup, the service sends a `POST /register` API call to the registry with its unique ID, IP, port, and health check URL. - The registry stores the entry in an in-memory map (e.g., Eureka) or persistent store (e.g., etcd). 3. **Heartbeat Phase** *(2 marks)*: - The service sends periodic heartbeats (default every 30 s in Eureka) via `PUT /heartbeat`. - The registry refreshes the **lease timestamp**. 4. **Eviction Phase** *(2 marks)*: - A background scheduler in the registry scans for expired leases. - If a service misses $N_{miss}$ heartbeats, it is evicted and removed from the registry. - Deregistration also occurs on graceful shutdown via `DELETE /deregister`. **(b) Derivation (7 Marks):** **Step 1:** Formula statement *(1 mark)* $$T_{evict} = N_{miss} \times T_h$$ **Step 2:** Substitution *(1 mark)* $$T_{evict} = 4 \times 15 = 60\text{ seconds}$$ **Step 3:** Availability impact *(3 marks)*: - During the 60-second window, the registry still routes traffic to the dead instance, causing **failed requests**. - To minimise this, $T_{evict}$ should be **< average failure detection time SLO**. - Trade-off: Lower $T_{evict}$ → more false evictions during transient network blips. - Higher $T_{evict}$ → longer recovery time. **Step 4:** Mitigation *(2 marks)*: - Use **active health checks** alongside heartbeats to reduce detection time. - Implement **circuit breakers** (Hystrix, Resilience4j) to fail fast during the 60 s window. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** Compare **Self-Registration** and **Third-Party Registration** patterns of service discovery. Which is more suitable for containerised environments like Kubernetes, and why? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A Discovery Service runs on **3 registry nodes**, each with availability $0.99$. Calculate: **(i)** overall availability in **AP mode** and **(ii)** availability in **CP mode** (quorum = 2). Comment on the trade-off. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Comparison (7 Marks):** | Feature | Self-Registration | Third-Party Registration | |---------|-------------------|--------------------------| | Who registers? | Service itself | External process (e.g., sidecar) | | Coupling | High (service knows registry) | Low | | Language support | Limited (per-SDK) | Polyglot | | Failure handling | Service handles retry | Registrar handles retry | | Example | Eureka client | Registrator + Consul, K8s Controller | | **Best for containers?** | No (containers are ephemeral; need external registrar) | **Yes** (Kubernetes has a built-in controller that auto-registers pods) *(2 marks)* | Kubernetes uses **Third-Party Registration** via the **Endpoints API** — a controller watches pods and updates the Service object automatically, making it the most natural fit for ephemeral container workloads. **(b) Availability Calculation (7 Marks):** **Given:** $n = 3$, $A_i = 0.99$ for each node. **(i) AP Mode (any one node responds):** *(3 marks)* $$P(\text{all fail}) = (1 - 0.99)^3 = (0.01)^3 = 10^{-6}$$ $$A_{r,AP} = 1 - 10^{-6} = 0.999999 = 99.9999\%$$ **(ii) CP Mode (quorum = 2 of 3 must respond):** *(3 marks)* The system fails if **2 or more** nodes are down: $$P(\text{fail}) = \binom{3}{2}(0.01)^2(0.99)^1 + \binom{3}{3}(0.01)^3$$ $$P(\text{fail}) = 3 \times 0.0001 \times 0.99 + 0.000001$$ $$P(\text{fail}) = 0.000297 + 0.000001 = 0.000298$$ $$A_{r,CP} = 1 - 0.000298 = 0.999702 = 99.97\%$$ **Comment on trade-off (1 mark):** - AP mode gives higher availability ($99.9999\%$) but tolerates stale reads. - CP mode sacrifices $0.03\%$ availability for strong consistency. - **AP is preferred** for read-heavy service discovery; **CP** for leader election and configuration. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️ > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing Registration with Discovery** — Registration is *publishing*; Discovery is *querying*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these are interchanged. > 2. **Forgetting the Heartbeat Mechanism** — A common 3-mark question is "How does the registry know a service is dead?" Missing the answer "heartbeat timeout / lease expiry" is a guaranteed mark loss. > 3. **Skipping the Diagram** — Part B (a) questions explicitly ask for a "neat diagram." A text-only answer loses 2–3 marks even if the explanation is perfect. > 4. **Wrong CAP Interpretation** — Eureka is **AP**, not CP. etcd is **CP**, not AP. Mixing these up is a top mistake. > 5. **Unit Confusion in Derivations** — Always specify units (seconds, milliseconds) in $T_h$, $T_{evict}$. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark for ambiguous units. > 6. **Not Mentioning Real-world Tools** — Vague answers lose marks. Always cite at least one tool (Eureka, Consul, etcd, Kubernetes DNS). ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌 > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam** - ✅ **Definition:** Discovery Service = dynamic registry + lookup mechanism for service instances. - ✅ **Three Core Components:** Service Registry, Registration Mechanism, Discovery API. - ✅ **Two Main Patterns:** Client-Side (Eureka + Ribbon) vs. Server-Side (AWS ELB, K8s Service). - ✅ **Two Registration Modes:** Self-Registration vs. Third-Party Registration (K8s uses latter). - ✅ **Eviction Formula:** $T_{evict} = T_h \times N_{miss}$. - ✅ **Default Values:** Eureka $T_h = 30\text{ s}$, $N_{miss} = 3$ → $T_{evict} = 90\text{ s}$. - ✅ **CAP Theorem:** Eureka = AP, etcd/ZooKeeper = CP, Consul = both (configurable). - ✅ **Health Checks:** Two types — passive (heartbeat) and active (HTTP/TCP/gRPC probe). - ✅ **Key Tools:** Eureka, Consul, etcd, ZooKeeper, CoreDNS, AWS Cloud Map. - ✅ **Availability Formula (AP):** $A_r = 1 - (1 - A_i)^n$. - ✅ **Quorum Formula:** $Q = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$. - ✅ **Service Mesh:** Istio and Linkerd build on discovery services using sidecar proxies. - ✅ **Kubernetes Context:** Pods register via Endpoints controller; Services provide stable virtual IPs. - ✅ **DNS-based Discovery:** CoreDNS watches K8s API and exposes `service.namespace.svc.cluster.local`. - ✅ **Real-world Analogy:** Hospital reception desk that tracks every doctor's current location.

topics.title: API Gateway
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** API Gateway

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **API Gateway** is a single, unified entry-point pattern in a distributed microservices architecture that acts as an **intermediary layer** between external clients (mobile apps, web browsers, third-party systems, IoT devices) and the internal collection of backend microservices. It is responsible for **request routing, protocol translation, composition/aggregation, authentication, rate limiting, caching, monitoring, and load balancing** — encapsulating all cross-cutting concerns so that individual microservices remain focused purely on domain logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Phrasing):**
> *“An API Gateway is a server-side architectural pattern that sits at the edge of a distributed system, exposing a single, consolidated, and protocol-uniform interface to clients, while internally dispatching, transforming, and orchestrating calls to multiple backend services.”*

Formally, if we denote the client request set as $C = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_n\}$ and the backend microservice set as $S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_m\}$, the API Gateway $G$ implements a mapping function:

$$G: C \rightarrow \{s_i \mid s_i \in S\}$$

such that for a composite request $c_k$ requiring multiple services, the gateway returns a *fused response*:

$$R_{c_k} = f\big(s_1(c_k), s_2(c_k), \dots, s_j(c_k)\big)$$

where $f(\cdot)$ is the aggregation/joining function (parallel, sequential, or fan-out).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hotel Receptionist

Imagine a large international hotel with **50 service counters** inside:
- A **Receptionist** stands at the single front door.
- A guest (client) says, *"I need a taxi, a wake-up call at 6 AM, and a table for two at 7 PM."*
- The receptionist **does not hand the guest a map** of 50 counters. Instead, the receptionist:
  1. **Authenticates** the guest (checks ID/booking).
  2. **Routes** each sub-request to the right internal counter (taxi desk, housekeeping, restaurant).
  3. **Aggregates** all confirmations and hands back **one consolidated answer** to the guest.
  4. **Logs** the request, **rate-limits** (no booking? sorry, 3 rooms max per guest), and **monitors** the stay.

| Hotel Receptionist | API Gateway |
|---|---|
| Single front door | Single public URL endpoint |
| ID/Booking check | Authentication & Authorization (OAuth2, JWT) |
| Internal counter routing | Service discovery & request routing |
| Combining confirmations | Response aggregation / composition |
| Limiting rooms per guest | Rate limiting & throttling |
| Guest log book | Logging, monitoring, analytics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The microservices *inside* a system may change, scale, or even be rewritten in a different language — the client never knows, because the **API Gateway is the only contract** the client sees. This is the essence of **encapsulation at the system boundary**.

## 1.3 Why the API Gateway Exists — The Problem It Solves

In a naive microservices deployment, every client app (mobile, web, desktop, partner-API) would need to:
- Know the **network address** of every microservice.
- Handle **multiple protocols** (REST, gRPC, WebSocket, GraphQL).
- Implement **auth, retry, caching, rate-limiting** logic N times.
- Deal with **cross-cutting concerns** like SSL termination.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Direct Client-to-Microservice Anti-Pattern:**
> A common student error is to draw a "client → 12 microservices" diagram with 12×12 = 144 connections. This is the **“distributed monolith chatty mess”** that the API Gateway pattern explicitly prevents. The correct architectural stance is **“one client → one gateway → many services.”**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Responsibilities of an API Gateway (Layered View)

An API Gateway is not a "thing" but a **composite of concerns**. KTU examiners expect the student to be able to enumerate these layers from top (client-facing) to bottom (service-facing):

### Layer 1 — Edge / Cross-Cutting Concerns
1. **TLS/SSL Termination** — Decrypts HTTPS at the gateway; backend traffic can be plain HTTP on a private VPC network (faster, cheaper).
2. **Authentication & Authorization** — Validates JWT tokens, OAuth2 bearer tokens, API keys. Forwards user identity as headers (e.g., `X-User-ID`) to backends.
3. **Rate Limiting & Throttling** — Caps requests per client (e.g., 100 req/min).
4. **Caching** — Stores idempotent GET responses in Redis/Memcached.
5. **Logging, Monitoring & Tracing** — Emits metrics to Prometheus, traces to Jaeger/Zipkin.

### Layer 2 — Request Transformation
6. **Protocol Translation** — REST → gRPC, HTTP → WebSocket, SOAP → REST.
7. **Request/Response Transformation** — Renames fields, converts XML ↔ JSON, strips internal headers.
8. **Payload Validation** — JSON-Schema enforcement before forwarding.

### Layer 3 — Routing & Composition
9. **Path/Host/Header-based Routing** — `/users/*` → User Service, `/orders/*` → Order Service.
10. **Service Discovery Integration** — Queries Consul, Eureka, or Kubernetes DNS.
11. **Load Balancing** — Round-robin, least-connections, weighted, or consistent-hashing.
12. **Response Aggregation (Backend-for-Frontend, BFF)** — Fans out to multiple services in parallel and merges the JSON.

### Layer 4 — Resilience
13. **Circuit Breaking** — Opens circuit if downstream error rate > threshold (Hystrix/Resilience4j).
14. **Retries with Exponential Backoff** — Bounded retry budget to avoid thundering herd.
15. **Timeouts & Bulkheading** — Per-route timeout; isolate thread pools so one slow service cannot starve others.

## 2.2 Architectural Variants (KTU High-Yield)

| Variant | Description | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Gateway** | One gateway for all clients | Small/medium systems |
| **Backend-for-Frontend (BFF)** | One gateway **per client type** (Mobile-BFF, Web-BFF, Partner-BFF) | When mobile needs different payload shape than web |
| **Hierarchical Gateway** | Outer (edge) → Inner (aggregation) | Large enterprises, multi-region |
| **Sidecar / Service Mesh Gateway** | Gateway as a sidecar proxy (Envoy, Istio) | Cloud-native Kubernetes deployments |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Throughput** | $\lambda = \dfrac{N_{req}}{T_{window}}$ | requests / second |
| 2 | **Mean Latency** | $L = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{resp,i} - t_{req,i})$ | milliseconds (ms) |
| 3 | **End-to-End Latency (composite call)** | $L_{total} = L_G + \max(L_{s_i}) + L_{agg}$ | Gateway + slowest parallel backend + aggregation |
| 4 | **Sequential Aggregation Latency** | $L_{seq} = L_G + \sum_{i=1}^{k} L_{s_i} + L_{agg}$ | Sum of all backend latencies (no parallelism) |
| 5 | **Parallel Aggregation Latency** | $L_{par} = L_G + \max_{i=1..k}(L_{s_i}) + L_{agg}$ | Max — gateways exploit this via async fan-out |
| 6 | **Rate-Limit Token Bucket Refill Rate** | $r = \dfrac{\text{burst capacity}}{\text{time window}}$ | tokens / second |
| 7 | **Connection Pool Sizing (Little's Law)** | $C = \lambda \cdot W$ | $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $W$ = avg wait time, $C$ = concurrent connections |
| 8 | **P99 Latency** | $L_{99} : P(L \le L_{99}) = 0.99$ | Tail latency; KTU 2024 expects this metric, not just average |
| 9 | **Availability (Gateway HA)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | MTBF = mean time between failures, MTTR = mean time to repair |
| 10 | **Cache Hit Ratio** | $H = \dfrac{N_{hit}}{N_{hit} + N_{miss}}$ | Dimensionless, 0 ≤ H ≤ 1 |
| 11 | **Effective Throughput with Cache** | $\lambda_{eff} = \lambda \cdot H \cdot C_{cache} + \lambda \cdot (1-H) \cdot C_{origin}$ | Accounts for cache vs origin cost |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Mark Hint:** Whenever a question says *"design an API gateway for a system handling 10,000 concurrent users"*, you MUST quote **Little's Law** ($C = \lambda \cdot W$) to size connection pools. Examiners award full marks only when this is shown explicitly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-commerce (Amazon/Flipkart):** Aggregates Product, Pricing, Inventory, Reviews, Recommendations into a single `GET /product-page` call. Reduces mobile-app round-trips from ~15 to 1.
- **Banking (FinTech):** Enforces OAuth2, PCI-DSS scope reduction, fraud-detection header injection.
- **IoT:** Translates lightweight MQTT from devices into REST/gRPC for analytics backends.
- **Serverless (AWS API Gateway):** Native managed gateway; routes to Lambda, DynamoDB, S3.
- **Kubernetes (Istio Ingress Gateway / Kong / NGINX):** Industry-standard gateways.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Example 1 — Latency Analysis of a Composite API Call

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A mobile app makes a single request to an API Gateway. The gateway fans out **3 parallel calls** to backend microservices and aggregates the responses before returning to the client. The gateway itself adds **5 ms** of processing, and the aggregation step takes **3 ms**. The three backend services have individual latencies (in ms) as follows:

$$
L_{s_1} = 40, \quad L_{s_2} = 65, \quad L_{s_3} = 25
$$

Compute the **end-to-end latency** seen by the mobile client.

### Step 1 — Identify the Pattern
Since the gateway issues calls to all three services *in parallel* and waits for the slowest one (the classic "barrier synchronization" of fan-out/fan-in), the relevant formula is the **parallel aggregation latency**:

$$
L_{par} = L_G + \max_{i=1..k}(L_{s_i}) + L_{agg}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Given Values
Plug in:

$$
L_G = 5\ \text{ms}, \quad \max(L_{s_1}, L_{s_2}, L_{s_3}) = \max(40, 65, 25) = 65\ \text{ms}, \quad L_{agg} = 3\ \text{ms}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Total

$$
L_{par} = 5 + 65 + 3
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{par} = 73\ \text{ms}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Contrast — Sequential Case:** Had the gateway called them sequentially (slower service first or last), the latency would have been $L_{seq} = 5 + (40 + 65 + 25) + 3 = 138\ \text{ms}$. The parallel approach gives a **speedup of 1.89×** — a standard KTU "design-decision" mark-earning justification.

### Step 4 — P99 Tail Latency Discussion (Extra Credit Answer)
If the P99 latencies of the three services are $P_{99,s_1} = 120$, $P_{99,s_2} = 200$, $P_{99,s_3} = 90$ ms, then the **P99 of the parallel fan-out** is approximately (by independence assumption):

$$
L_{99,par} = L_G + \max(P_{99,s_1}, P_{99,s_2}, P_{99,s_3}) + L_{agg} = 5 + 200 + 3 = 208\ \text{ms}
$$

This is the metric KTU 2024 examiners check when asking *"why is the slowest service the bottleneck?"*

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — Sizing the Connection Pool via Little's Law

**Problem:**
An API Gateway must support a peak load of **$\lambda = 5{,}000$ requests/second**. The average time each request occupies a backend connection (network + service processing) is **$W = 80$ ms = 0.08 s**. How many concurrent backend connections must the gateway's pool hold to avoid queuing delays?

### Step 1 — State Little's Law
Little's Law for a stable system in steady state:

$$
C = \lambda \cdot W
$$

where $C$ is the average number of customers (here, connections) in the system.

### Step 2 — Convert Units
$\lambda = 5{,}000\ \text{req/s}$ and $W = 0.08\ \text{s}$. Units are consistent: $(\text{req/s}) \times (\text{s}) = \text{req}$, which is the count of in-flight requests.

### Step 3 — Compute

$$
C = 5{,}000 \times 0.08
$$

$$
C = 400
$$

### Step 4 — Add a Safety Margin (KTU 2024 Practical Reasoning)

A real gateway is provisioned for **peak + 30% headroom** to absorb traffic bursts:

$$
C_{pool} = \lceil 1.3 \times 400 \rceil = \lceil 520 \rceil
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{pool} = 520\ \text{concurrent connections}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not infinite?** Pool exhaustion causes head-of-line blocking → queueing delay → timeout cascade. KTU 2024 wants students to mention **bulkheading** (separate pools per service) as the resilience strategy.

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Throughput Improvement via Caching

**Problem:**
The Product Service receives **$\lambda = 8{,}000$ req/s** through the gateway. **70 %** of the requests are idempotent `GET /product/{id}` and can be cached. The cache hit ratio is $H = 0.85$. The cache responds in **$L_{cache} = 2$ ms** and the origin in **$L_{origin} = 90$ ms**. Compute the **effective average latency** seen by clients.

### Step 1 — Split the Traffic
Cache hits: $\lambda \cdot H = 8{,}000 \times 0.85 = 6{,}800\ \text{req/s}$
Cache misses: $\lambda \cdot (1-H) = 8{,}000 \times 0.15 = 1{,}200\ \text{req/s}$

### Step 2 — Weighted Average Latency

$$
L_{eff} = H \cdot L_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot L_{origin}
$$

$$
L_{eff} = 0.85 \times 2 + 0.15 \times 90
$$

$$
L_{eff} = 1.7 + 13.5
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{eff} = 15.2\ \text{ms}}
$$

### Step 3 — Compare with No-Cache Baseline
Without caching: $L_{no\_cache} = 90$ ms. The gateway + cache has reduced latency by a factor of:

$$
\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{90}{15.2} \approx 5.92\times
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (KTU 2024 Board Tip)

| Decision Question | Choose This Option | Justification (Killer Answer) |
|---|---|---|
| Mobile vs Web want different payloads? | **BFF pattern** | One gateway per client type |
| 50+ microservices? | **Hierarchical gateway** (Edge + Internal) | Avoids single-point routing explosion |
| Serverless workloads? | **Managed gateway** (AWS API GW / Azure APIM) | No infra overhead, auto-scaling |
| Need mTLS between services? | **Service mesh sidecar (Istio)** | Gateway is a sidecar, not a hop |
| Latency-critical (< 10 ms)? | **Single-hop gateway + cache + co-location** | Every hop adds ≥ 1 ms |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level API Gateway Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Clients["CLIENT ZONE"]
        c1["Mobile App"]
        c2["Web Browser"]
        c3["Partner API Client"]
        c4["IoT Device"]
    end

    subgraph Edge["API GATEWAY ZONE"]
        gw1["TLS Termination"]
        gw2["Auth & Rate Limit"]
        gw3["Router & Aggregator"]
        gw4["Cache Layer Redis"]
        gw5["Logger Metrics Trace"]
    end

    subgraph Services["MICROSERVICE ZONE"]
        s1["User Service"]
        s2["Order Service"]
        s3["Product Service"]
        s4["Payment Service"]
        s5["Inventory Service"]
    end

    c1 --> gw1
    c2 --> gw1
    c3 --> gw1
    c4 --> gw1
    gw1 --> gw2 --> gw3
    gw3 -.cache lookup.-> gw4
    gw3 --> s1
    gw3 --> s2
    gw3 --> s3
    gw3 --> s4
    gw3 --> s5
    gw3 -.emits.-> gw5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Clients exist in a *public* zone; services exist in a *private VPC*. The gateway is the **only** network entity visible from the internet. This single image answers a 7-mark KTU question: *"Explain how API Gateway ensures encapsulation."*

---

## 4.2 Request Flow — Fan-Out and Fan-In (Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Cli as Mobile Client
    participant Gw as API Gateway
    participant Us as User Service
    participant Os as Order Service
    participant Ps as Product Service

    Cli->>Gw: GET /dashboard (JWT)
    activate Gw
    Gw->>Gw: Validate JWT and rate limit
    Gw->>Us: GET /user/profile
    Gw->>Os: GET /orders/recent
    Gw->>Ps: GET /products/recommended
    par Parallel Fan-Out
        Us-->>Gw: 200 user JSON
    and
        Os-->>Gw: 200 order list
    and
        Ps-->>Gw: 200 product list
    end
    Gw->>Gw: Aggregate JSON
    Gw-->>Cli: 200 consolidated dashboard
    deactivate Gw
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Point:** Notice the `par` (parallel) block — this is what makes the gateway *faster* than direct calls. The `Cli` sees **one** network round-trip, even though **three** backend calls happened. This is the **response-aggregation pattern** in action.

---

## 4.3 Comparison — With vs Without API Gateway

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Without["WITHOUT API GATEWAY ANTI-PATTERN"]
        w1["Mobile"] --- s11["User"]
        w1 --- s12["Order"]
        w1 --- s13["Product"]
        w2["Web"] --- s11
        w2 --- s12
        w2 --- s13
        w3["Partner"] --- s11
        w3 --- s12
        w3 --- s13
    end

    subgraph With["WITH API GATEWAY PATTERN"]
        c1["Mobile"] --> g1["Gateway"]
        c2["Web"] --> g1
        c3["Partner"] --> g1
        g1 --- ss1["User"]
        g1 --- ss2["Order"]
        g1 --- ss3["Product"]
    end
```

| Aspect | Without Gateway | With Gateway |
|---|---|---|
| Client connections | $N_{clients} \times N_{services}$ | $N_{clients} \times 1$ |
| Auth code | Replicated in every client | Centralized in gateway |
| New service added | Update every client | Just register in gateway |
| Protocol change | Breaks clients | Gateway translates |

---

## 4.4 Backend-for-Frontend (BFF) Variant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mob["Mobile Client"] --> mbff["Mobile BFF"]
    web["Web Client"] --> wbff["Web BFF"]
    partner["Partner API"] --> pbff["Partner BFF"]

    mbff --> s1["User Service"]
    mbff --> s2["Order Service"]
    wbff --> s1
    wbff --> s2
    wbff --> s3["Analytics Service"]
    pbff --> s4["Bulk Export Service"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Answer Bonus:** Mentioning **BFF** as the answer to *"mobile needs a smaller payload than web"* earns 2 extra marks. The Mobile BFF strips heavy fields; the Web BFF keeps full nested objects.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the API Gateway pattern. List any FOUR responsibilities handled by an API Gateway in a microservices architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
An **API Gateway** is a server-side architectural pattern that acts as a single, unified entry point for all client requests in a distributed system, internally routing, transforming, and aggregating calls to multiple backend microservices while encapsulating cross-cutting concerns.

**Any FOUR responsibilities (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Request Routing & Load Balancing** — Directs incoming requests to appropriate backend services.
2. **Authentication & Authorization** — Validates JWT/OAuth2 tokens before forwarding.
3. **Rate Limiting & Throttling** — Enforces per-client request quotas to protect backends.
4. **Response Aggregation** — Combines multiple backend responses into a single payload (BFF).
5. **Protocol Translation** — Converts REST ↔ gRPC, JSON ↔ XML.
6. **Caching** — Stores idempotent responses in Redis to reduce backend load.
7. **Logging, Monitoring & Distributed Tracing** — Emits metrics to Prometheus, traces to Jaeger.
8. **Circuit Breaking & Resilience** — Opens circuit on downstream failure to prevent cascade.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (3-mark Q):** Students often give 1-line answers like *"It routes requests."* The 2024 KTU scheme awards full 3 marks ONLY when the student gives a **precise definition + 4 distinct responsibilities** with at least one technical keyword (e.g., JWT, BFF, circuit breaker). Memorize the *keywords*.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between an API Gateway and a Load Balancer. When would you choose one over the other?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | API Gateway | Load Balancer (L4/L7) |
|---|---|---|
| **Layer** | Application (L7) — protocol-aware | L4 (transport) or L7 (HTTP) |
| **Primary role** | Routing + cross-cutting concerns | Pure traffic distribution |
| **Aggregation** | ✅ Combines multiple service responses | ❌ Forwards single requests |
| **Auth** | ✅ JWT/OAuth2 validation | ❌ Not its job (L4 LBs) |
| **Transformation** | ✅ JSON/XML rewrite | ❌ None |
| **Use when** | Microservices, BFF, composition | Stateless service replicas, high TPS |

**Choice (1 mark):** Choose **API Gateway** when multiple backend services exist, payload composition is needed, or protocol translation is required. Choose **Load Balancer** when scaling identical stateless service replicas (e.g., 50 NGINX pods behind one VIP). **In practice, both are used together** — Load Balancer distributes traffic across gateway instances, and the gateway routes to services.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Aggregation & Latency

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **request-aggregation** pattern implemented by an API Gateway. With a neat diagram, describe how a single client call is decomposed into multiple parallel backend calls and the responses are merged. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile e-commerce app invokes `GET /homepage` on an API Gateway. The gateway fans out to **4 services in parallel**: Authentication (15 ms), User Profile (45 ms), Recommended Products (120 ms), and Cart Summary (60 ms). The gateway adds 4 ms of internal processing, and aggregation takes 2 ms. Compute the **end-to-end latency**. If the design was changed to **sequential** calls in the same order, what would the latency be? Comment on the design decision. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Aggregation Pattern (7 marks)**

The **request aggregation** pattern (a.k.a. **composition pattern**) is a flagship responsibility of the API Gateway. Instead of forcing the client to make N separate calls to N services (which is slow, chatty, and insecure), the gateway:

1. **Receives** a single high-level client request (e.g., `GET /homepage`).
2. **Decomposes** it into multiple backend calls based on routing rules.
3. **Fans out** the calls — ideally in **parallel** using async I/O.
4. **Waits** for all responses (barrier synchronization).
5. **Aggregates** the responses into a single consolidated JSON/XML.
6. **Returns** the fused response to the client.

**Diagram (already drawn in §4.2) — Mobile Client → Gateway → 3 services in parallel → Aggregated Response.**

**Benefits (mention 3 for full marks):**
- **Reduced round-trips** — 1 call instead of N.
- **Hides internal topology** — services can be added/removed without client updates.
- **Smaller client payload control** — BFF tailors aggregation per device type.
- **Centralized error handling** — gateway can use partial responses or fallbacks.

**(b) — Latency Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify the pattern.** Parallel fan-out.

**Step 2 — Apply the parallel aggregation formula:**

$$
L_{par} = L_G + \max_{i=1..k}(L_{s_i}) + L_{agg}
$$

**Step 3 — Find the maximum of the four latencies:**

$$
\max(15, 45, 120, 60) = 120\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$
L_{par} = 4 + 120 + 2 = 126\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Sequential case:**

$$
L_{seq} = L_G + \sum_{i=1}^{4} L_{s_i} + L_{agg}
$$

$$
L_{seq} = 4 + (15 + 45 + 120 + 60) + 2
$$

$$
L_{seq} = 4 + 240 + 2 = 246\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 6 — Design commentary (2 marks):**
- Parallel design is **2× faster** (246 / 126 ≈ 1.95).
- The **bottleneck is the Recommended Products service** (120 ms) — optimization should target this service (caching, precomputation, or async push).
- Sequential would be acceptable only if the calls have **data dependencies** (e.g., cart depends on user profile).

**Speedup achieved:**

$$
S = \dfrac{L_{seq}}{L_{par}} = \dfrac{246}{126} \approx 1.95\times
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Throughput, Caching, and Connection Pool Sizing

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **role of an API Gateway** in a microservices-based e-commerce platform. Clearly mark the **client zone, gateway zone, and microservice zone**, and label at least **five** responsibilities handled by the gateway. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An e-commerce API Gateway is expected to handle a peak arrival rate of **$\lambda = 12{,}000$ requests/second**. The average time a request holds a backend connection is **$W = 50$ ms**. Using **Little's Law**, calculate the **required concurrent connection pool size**. If a Redis cache is introduced that serves **80 %** of requests in **1 ms** and the remaining **20 %** hit the origin in **80 ms**, compute the **new effective latency** and the **percentage reduction** in average latency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — Block Diagram and Roles (7 marks)**

The diagram is the same as **§4.1** of these notes — mark the three zones:

- **Client Zone:** Mobile App, Web Browser, Partner API.
- **Gateway Zone:** TLS termination, Auth, Router, Cache, Logger.
- **Microservice Zone:** User, Product, Order, Payment, Inventory.

**Five responsibilities (5 × 1 mark each, pick any 5):**
1. **TLS/SSL termination** — Decrypts HTTPS at the edge.
2. **Authentication** — Validates JWT bearer tokens.
3. **Routing & Service Discovery** — Directs `/orders/*` to Order Service via Consul/K8s.
4. **Response Aggregation** — Combines Product + Pricing + Inventory into one call.
5. **Caching** — Redis-backed GET caching.
6. **Rate Limiting** — Token-bucket per user.
7. **Circuit Breaking** — Resilience4j opens circuit on Payment Service errors.
8. **Logging / Metrics / Tracing** — Prometheus + Jaeger.

**Closing statement (1 mark):** *"The API Gateway thus becomes the single, secure, observable entry-point that decouples clients from the internal microservice topology."*

**(b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Little's Law application.**

$$
C = \lambda \cdot W = 12{,}000 \times 0.050\ \text{s} = 600
$$

**Step 2 — Add 30 % headroom** (practical engineering):

$$
C_{pool} = \lceil 1.3 \times 600 \rceil = 780\ \text{concurrent connections}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective latency with cache.**

$$
L_{eff} = H \cdot L_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot L_{origin}
$$

$$
L_{eff} = 0.80 \times 1 + 0.20 \times 80
$$

$$
L_{eff} = 0.8 + 16 = 16.8\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with origin-only baseline (no cache).**

$$
L_{no\_cache} = 80\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage reduction:**

$$
\Delta L\,\% = \dfrac{L_{no\_cache} - L_{eff}}{L_{no\_cache}} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta L\,\% = \dfrac{80 - 16.8}{80} \times 100 = \dfrac{63.2}{80} \times 100 = 79\,\%
$$

**Final boxed answers:**

$$
\boxed{C_{pool} = 780} \qquad \boxed{L_{eff} = 16.8\ \text{ms}} \qquad \boxed{\text{Latency reduction} = 79\,\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on This Question Type:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in Little's Law — write $W = 0.050$ **seconds**, not 50 ms. Mixing units is a 1-mark penalty.
> 2. **Not adding headroom** — pure Little's Law gives 600, but the *practical* answer is 780. Examiners expect both: theoretical + practical.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert 80 % to 0.80** in the cache formula — a common decimal-percentage error.
> 4. **Not stating the formula before substituting** — KTU 2024 marking scheme deducts 0.5 marks if formulas are skipped.
> 5. **Citing Kafka / RabbitMQ as "API Gateways"** — they are message brokers, NOT API gateways. Mixing concepts is a 1–2 mark penalty.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print-and-revise bullet list — 60-second pre-exam revision.**

- ✅ **API Gateway = Single Entry Point** between clients and microservices; encapsulates cross-cutting concerns.
- ✅ **Core Responsibilities (mnemonic: "AAR-RT-CB")** — Auth, Aggregation, Routing, Rate-limiting, Transformation, Caching, Bulkheading (resilience).
- ✅ **Pattern Variants** — Single Gateway, **BFF (Backend-for-Frontend)**, Hierarchical, Service-Mesh Sidecar (Istio).
- ✅ **Latency — Parallel Fan-Out** is the **default**; latency equals `L_G + max(L_si) + L_agg`, **not** the sum.
- ✅ **Latency — Sequential** is used when there are data dependencies; `L_seq = L_G + ΣL_si + L_agg`.
- ✅ **Little's Law** — `C = λ × W`; always convert W to **seconds**; add **30 % headroom** in production.
- ✅ **Cache Hit Ratio** — `H = N_hit / (N_hit + N_miss)`; effective latency = `H·L_cache + (1−H)·L_origin`.
- ✅ **P99 tail latency** is the bottleneck metric, not the mean; **slowest service dominates** in parallel fan-out.
- ✅ **Anti-pattern to AVOID** — client connected directly to many microservices (chatty mess).
- ✅ **API Gateway ≠ Load Balancer** — Gateway = L7 logic; LB = traffic distribution. They are **complementary**, not substitutes.
- ✅ **API Gateway ≠ Message Broker** — Kafka/RabbitMQ are async messaging; Gateways are synchronous request/response.
- ✅ **Popular Implementations** — Kong, NGINX, AWS API Gateway, Azure APIM, Google Cloud Endpoints, Istio Ingress, Apigee.
- ✅ **BFF Key Idea** — One gateway **per client type**; mobile-BFF trims payload, web-BFF keeps full structure.
- ✅ **High-Availability formula** — `A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)`; design for **A = 0.99999** (five-9s) in production.
- ✅ **Most Important KTU Buzzwords** — JWT, OAuth2, TLS termination, BFF, circuit breaker, service discovery, fan-out/fan-in, Little's Law, P99 latency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Service Registry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Service Registry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Service Registry** is a critical architectural pattern in distributed systems and microservices-based Advanced Computing environments that maintains a centralized, dynamically updated database of available service instances, their network locations (host:port), metadata, and health status. It acts as the **single source of truth** for service discovery, enabling loose coupling between service providers and consumers.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCST602 – Advanced Computing Systems)**, the Service Registry is studied under the broader umbrella of *Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)* and *microservices design patterns*, where it functions as the nervous system that allows ephemeral, auto-scaling service instances to be located by other services without hard-coded network addresses.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Service Registry falls under the "Service Discovery and Registration" unit, which is a high-weightage topic in Part B questions (14-mark category).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Service Registry** as the **Yellow Pages + GPS Directory** of a modern city:

- **The City (Your System):** Imagine a large city with thousands of restaurants (microservices).
- **Yellow Pages (Registry):** Instead of memorizing every restaurant's address, customers (service consumers) look up the directory.
- **Dynamic Updates:** When a restaurant opens, closes, or relocates, the directory updates in real-time.
- **GPS Coordinates (Network Locations):** Each entry has the current IP address and port.
- **Health Inspector (Heartbeat):** Restaurants that fail inspection are temporarily delisted.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Lost & Found" Analogy:** Without a Service Registry, if a service instance crashes and restarts on a new container with a different IP, clients have no way to find it. The Service Registry is the **lost-and-found desk** that tracks all running instances.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Metric | Standard Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Heartbeat Interval** | **30 seconds** | Default health-check ping frequency (Eureka default) |
| **Lease Renewal Duration** | **90 seconds** | Time after which an instance is marked stale |
| **Registry Replication Factor** | **≥ 3 nodes** | Quorum-based fault tolerance (Raft/Paxos) |
| **Service TTL (Time-to-Live)** | **15–60 seconds** | Validity window of a cached registration |
| **Eventual Consistency Window** | **< 2 seconds** | Max propagation delay across replica nodes |
| **CAP Trade-off Default** | **AP (Availability + Partition tolerance)** | Prioritized by Eureka, Consul (default mode) |

## 1.4 Architectural Position

The Service Registry sits at the **infrastructure layer** of the distributed system stack:

$$
\text{Application Layer} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Service Layer (Microservices)} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{\text{Service Registry Layer}} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Network Layer}
$$

It is **decoupled** from the services themselves, operating as an independent, highly available tier.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Core Operational Principles

The Service Registry operates on three fundamental phases:

### Phase 1: Service Registration (Publishing)
- A service instance **announces** itself to the registry upon startup.
- Registration payload typically contains: `serviceID`, `host`, `port`, `metadata`, `healthCheckURL`, and `tags`.
- Registration modes:
  - **Self-Registration:** The service itself pushes its info to the registry.
  - **Third-Party Registration:** A sidecar (e.g., Registrator, Kubernetes pod lifecycle) registers the service on behalf of the application.

### Phase 2: Service Discovery (Lookup)
- Client applications query the registry to find available service instances.
- Discovery modes:
  - **Client-Side Discovery:** Client queries registry, applies load-balancing logic locally.
  - **Server-Side Discovery:** A load balancer/router (e.g., AWS ALB, Envoy) queries the registry and routes traffic.

### Phase 3: Health Monitoring & Deregistration
- Registry periodically pings each instance's **health endpoint**.
- Unhealthy instances are **de-listed** automatically.
- Self-healing: when a service is killed, the registry removes it after lease expiry.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Service Registry — Distributed Systems Pain Points

| Problem | Without Registry | With Registry |
|---|---|---|
| **Hard-coded IPs** | Brittle, breaks on scaling | Dynamic resolution |
| **Auto-scaling** | New instances unreachable | Auto-registered & discoverable |
| **Failure handling** | Stuck connections | Self-healing via health checks |
| **Multi-region deploy** | Manual config files | Federated registries |
| **Dev/Test/Prod parity** | Environment drift | Consistent topology |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following mathematical models are essential for KTU Numerical/Analytical questions on Service Registry availability and consistency.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Registry Availability (Parallel Replicas)** | $A_{sys} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ | $A_i$ = availability of $i^{th}$ replica, dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 2 | **Mean Time Between Failures (System)** | $MTBF_{sys} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}$ | $\lambda_i$ = failure rate (failures/hour) |
| 3 | **Heartbeat-Based Staleness** | $T_{stale} = T_{heartbeat} \times N_{missed}$ | $N_{missed}$ = missed heartbeats threshold |
| 4 | **Eventual Consistency Lag** | $T_{converge} \le T_{replication} \times \log_2(N)$ | $N$ = number of registry nodes |
| 5 | **Service Discovery Latency (Cache Hit)** | $L_{cached} = L_{cache} + L_{net}$ | $L_{cache}$ in ms, $L_{net}$ in ms |
| 6 | **Discovery Latency (Cache Miss)** | $L_{miss} = L_{net} + L_{registry} + L_{cache\_write}$ | All in milliseconds |
| 7 | **Quorum Size (Raft Consensus)** | $Q = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$ | $N$ = cluster size, $Q$ = quorum nodes |
| 8 | **CAP Theorem Constraint** | $C + A + P \le 2$ (only 2 of 3 simultaneously) | Categorical (Boolean-style) |
| 9 | **Registry Throughput** | $\Theta = \frac{R_{queries}}{T_{window}}$ | Queries per second (QPS) |
| 10 | **Effective Service Availability** | $A_{eff} = A_{registry} \times A_{service}$ | Product of registry & service uptime |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Question 2.1 (availability formula) is a **favourite 14-mark Part B question**. Memorize the parallel-availability formula — it's used every KTU exam cycle.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Netflix Eureka:** Manages thousands of microservice instances across AWS regions.
- **HashiCorp Consul:** Multi-datacenter service mesh with KV store.
- **Apache Zookeeper:** Used by Kafka, Hadoop for distributed coordination.
- **etcd (CoreOS):** Backbone of Kubernetes service discovery.
- **AWS Cloud Map:** Managed service registry integrated with ECS/EKS.

In production engineering, Service Registries are foundational to:
- **Blue-Green deployments**
- **Canary releases**
- **Chaos engineering** (failure injection testing)
- **Multi-cloud failover**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: System Availability with N Replicated Registry Nodes

This is the **most frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU ESE for this topic.

**Problem Statement:** A Service Registry is deployed with $n$ parallel replica nodes. If each node has an individual availability of $A_i$, derive the overall system availability.

### Step 1: Define Failure Probability
The probability that a single node $i$ is **unavailable** (has failed) is:

$$
P_{fail,i} = 1 - A_i
$$

### Step 2: Define Total System Failure
For the entire system to fail, **all** $n$ replicas must fail simultaneously. Since failures are independent:

$$
P_{sys\_fail} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_{fail,i} = \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)
$$

### Step 3: Compute System Availability
System availability is the complement of total failure:

$$
A_{sys} = 1 - P_{sys\_fail}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{A_{sys} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)}
$$

### Step 4: Special Case — Identical Replicas
If all $n$ replicas have equal availability $A$ (homogeneous cluster):

$$
A_{sys} = 1 - (1 - A)^n
$$

### Step 5: Numerical Example
Given: $n = 3$ replicas, $A_i = 0.99$ for each.

$$
A_{sys} = 1 - (1 - 0.99)^3
$$

$$
A_{sys} = 1 - (0.01)^3
$$

$$
A_{sys} = 1 - 0.000001
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{sys} = 0.999999 = 99.9999\%}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why Netflix runs **3 Eureka replicas per region** — you get "five nines" availability, which is the SLA target for production-grade registries.

## 3.2 Derivation: Heartbeat-Based Staleness Threshold

**Problem:** A service sends a heartbeat every $T_{heartbeat}$ seconds. The registry marks a service as stale after $N_{missed}$ consecutive missed heartbeats. Derive the maximum staleness time.

### Step 1: Express Missed Heartbeats
If a service crashes, the registry will not receive heartbeats. The detection time depends on how many heartbeats it waits before acting.

### Step 2: Total Staleness Window
The time elapsed between the last successful heartbeat and the deregistration action is:

$$
T_{stale} = T_{heartbeat} \times N_{missed}
$$

### Step 3: Numerical Example
Eureka defaults: $T_{heartbeat} = 30$ s, $N_{missed} = 3$.

$$
T_{stale} = 30 \times 3 = 90 \text{ seconds}
$$

This matches Eureka's default lease expiration of **90 seconds** (matches the constant in §1.3).

## 3.3 Derivation: Eventual Consistency Convergence Bound

**Problem:** In a Raft-based registry cluster of $N$ nodes, derive the upper bound on time for all nodes to agree on a service registration.

### Step 1: Leader Election
Raft uses a leader-based replication model. The leader replicates log entries to followers.

### Step 2: Replication Rounds
In the worst case, a leader may need $\log_2(N)$ rounds to commit a value to a majority quorum of size $Q$.

### Step 3: Convergence Bound
If $T_{replication}$ is the time for one round-trip replication:

$$
\boxed{T_{converge} \le T_{replication} \times \log_2(N)}
$$

### Step 4: Practical Bound
For $N = 5$ nodes and $T_{replication} = 100$ ms:

$$
T_{converge} \le 100 \times \log_2(5) \approx 100 \times 2.32 = 232 \text{ ms}
$$

This is the theoretical **upper bound**; in practice, etcd and Consul achieve sub-100 ms convergence.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Service Registry Implementations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **high-yield** for KTU Part A (3-mark) comparison questions.

| Feature | **Eureka (Netflix)** | **Consul (HashiCorp)** | **Zookeeper (Apache)** | **etcd (CoreOS)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CAP Priority** | AP | CP (default) | CP | CP |
| **Consensus Algorithm** | None (peer-to-peer) | Raft | ZAB (Zookeeper Atomic Broadcast) | Raft |
| **Health Checks** | Built-in | Built-in (multi-type) | External | External |
| **Multi-Datacenter** | Limited | Native | Manual | Limited |
| **KV Store** | No | Yes | Yes (znodes) | Yes |
| **Used By** | Netflix | HashiCorp stack | Kafka, Hadoop | Kubernetes |
| **Language** | Java | Go | Java | Go |
| **Service Mesh** | No | Yes (via Connect) | No | No |
| **Spring Boot Integration** | Native | Good | Manual | Manual |

## 3.5 Trade-Off Analysis: Client-Side vs Server-Side Discovery

| Criterion | Client-Side Discovery | Server-Side Discovery |
|---|---|---|
| **Load Balancing Logic** | In client (smart client) | In router/LB |
| **Coupling** | Client knows registry API | Client is dumb |
| **Language Dependency** | Tied to client's language | Language-agnostic |
| **Latency** | One extra hop to registry | Direct routing |
| **Failure Domain** | Registry outage = total discovery fail | LB can cache |
| **KTU Example** | Eureka + Ribbon | AWS ALB + ECS |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Service Registry Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS1[Service Provider A1]
    nodeS2[Service Provider A2]
    nodeS3[Service Provider B1]
    nodeC1[Service Consumer X]
    nodeC2[Service Consumer Y]
    nodeReg[Service Registry Cluster]
    nodeR1[Registry Node 1]
    nodeR2[Registry Node 2]
    nodeR3[Registry Node 3]
    nodeH[Health Check Service]
    nodeLB[Load Balancer]

    nodeS1 -- Register Instance --> nodeReg
    nodeS2 -- Register Instance --> nodeReg
    nodeS3 -- Register Instance --> nodeReg
    nodeH -- Ping Health Endpoint --> nodeS1
    nodeH -- Ping Health Endpoint --> nodeS2
    nodeH -- Ping Health Endpoint --> nodeS3
    nodeH -- Update Health Status --> nodeReg

    nodeC1 -- Query for Service A --> nodeLB
    nodeC2 -- Query for Service B --> nodeLB
    nodeLB -- Lookup Endpoint --> nodeReg
    nodeReg -- Return Instance List --> nodeLB
    nodeLB -- Route to Healthy Instance --> nodeS1
    nodeLB -- Route to Healthy Instance --> nodeS2

    nodeReg --- nodeR1
    nodeReg --- nodeR2
    nodeReg --- nodeR3
    nodeR1 <-- Replicate --> nodeR2
    nodeR2 <-- Replicate --> nodeR3
```

## 4.2 Service Registration & Discovery Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SP as Service Provider
    participant SR as Service Registry
    participant HC as Health Checker
    participant SC as Service Consumer
    participant LB as Load Balancer

    Note over SP,SR: PHASE 1 REGISTRATION
    SP->>SR: POST register host port metadata
    SR-->>SP: 200 OK registration confirmed
    SR->>SR: Store entry in registry table

    Note over SR,HC: PHASE 2 HEALTH MONITORING
    loop Every 30 seconds
        HC->>SP: GET health endpoint
        SP-->>HC: 200 OK healthy
        HC->>SR: PUT health status UP
    end

    Note over SC,LB: PHASE 3 DISCOVERY AND ROUTING
    SC->>LB: Request service A
    LB->>SR: GET instances of service A
    SR-->>LB: Return list with health filter
    LB->>SP: Forward request to healthy instance
    SP-->>SC: Response

    Note over SP,SR: PHASE 4 DEREGISTRATION
    SP->>SR: DELETE register on graceful shutdown
    SR-->>SP: 200 OK removed from registry
```

## 4.3 CAP Theorem Visualization for Registries

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeCAP[CAP Theorem]
    nodeAP[AP Registries Eureka]
    nodeCP[CP Registries Consul etcd]
    nodeCA[CA Registries RDBMS based]

    nodeCAP --- nodeAP
    nodeCAP --- nodeCP
    nodeCAP --- nodeCA

    nodeAP -- Trades Consistency --> nodeAPD[Always responds partition tolerant eventually consistent]
    nodeCP -- Trades Availability --> nodeCPD[Strongly consistent may reject writes during partition]
    nodeCA -- No Partition Tolerance --> nodeCAD[Single datacenter only]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Self-Registration Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeApp[Application Code]
    nodeSD[Service Discovery Client Library]
    nodeReg2[Service Registry Server]
    nodeCons[Service Consumer Code]
    nodeConsSD[Consumer Discovery Client]
    nodeCache[Local Cache]

    nodeApp -- Instantiate on startup --> nodeSD
    nodeSD -- Register host port --> nodeReg2
    nodeReg2 -- Heartbeat renewal --> nodeSD
    nodeSD -- Deregister on shutdown --> nodeReg2
    nodeCons -- Needs service --> nodeConsSD
    nodeConsSD -- Query registry --> nodeReg2
    nodeConsSD -- Cache results --> nodeCache
    nodeConsSD -- Select instance from cache --> nodeCons
```

## 4.5 Component Dependency Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INFRA[Infrastructure Layer]
        nodeN1[Network DNS]
        nodeN2[Load Balancer]
        nodeN3[Container Orchestrator Kubernetes]
    end

    subgraph REG[Registry Layer]
        nodeRE1[Registry API Server]
        nodeRE2[Registry Data Store KV]
        nodeRE3[Health Monitor Daemon]
        nodeRE4[Replication Engine Raft]
    end

    subgraph SVC[Service Layer]
        nodeSV1[Microservice A]
        nodeSV2[Microservice B]
        nodeSV3[Microservice C]
    end

    nodeN3 --> nodeRE1
    nodeRE1 --> nodeRE2
    nodeRE3 --> nodeSV1
    nodeRE3 --> nodeSV2
    nodeRE3 --> nodeSV3
    nodeRE4 --> nodeRE2
    nodeSV1 --> nodeRE1
    nodeSV2 --> nodeRE1
    nodeSV3 --> nodeRE1
    nodeN2 --> nodeRE1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set A]**

**Q: Define Service Registry. List any two popular implementations.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Service Registry** is a centralized database that maintains a dynamically updated list of active service instances along with their network locations and health status. It enables service discovery in distributed systems and microservices architectures by acting as the single source of truth for service endpoints.

**Two popular implementations:**
1. **Netflix Eureka** — AP-oriented, REST-based, deeply integrated with Spring Cloud.
2. **HashiCorp Consul** — CP-oriented, supports multi-datacenter, includes KV store and health checks.

*(Other valid answers: Apache Zookeeper, etcd, AWS Cloud Map, Kubernetes Service + DNS.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

**Q: Differentiate between Client-Side and Server-Side Service Discovery.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Client-Side Discovery | Server-Side Discovery |
|---|---|---|
| Who queries registry? | The client application | The load balancer / router |
| Load balancing | Done by client (e.g., Ribbon) | Done by LB (e.g., AWS ALB) |
| Client smartness | Smart client (needs SDK) | Dumb client |
| Example | Eureka + Netflix Ribbon | AWS ALB + ECS Service |

In **client-side discovery**, the client queries the registry, receives a list of instances, and uses a load-balancing algorithm (round-robin, random, least-connections) to pick one. In **server-side discovery**, the client sends the request to a router/LB, which queries the registry and forwards the request transparently.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Main Slot, Q4(b) Pattern]**

**Q: Explain the Service Registry pattern in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the working of client-side service discovery. A registry cluster has 4 replica nodes each with availability 0.98. Compute the system availability.**

#### (a) Service Registry Pattern Explanation (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **Service Registry pattern** is a key microservices design pattern that addresses the dynamic nature of service instances in a distributed system. Unlike monolithic deployments with fixed IPs, microservices run in containers/VMs that may scale, fail, or migrate frequently.

**Key Components:**

1. **Service Registry Server:** A centralized server (or replicated cluster) that stores service instance metadata. Each entry contains:
   - Service name / ID
   - Hostname and IP address
   - Port number
   - Health check URL
   - Metadata tags (version, region, etc.)

2. **Service Provider / Registrant:** The microservice instance that registers itself (or is registered by a third party) with the registry on startup.

3. **Service Consumer / Discoverer:** A client that queries the registry to find available instances of a service it needs to call.

4. **Health Check Mechanism:** A periodic ping to the service's health endpoint to ensure liveness.

**Working Principle:**
1. On startup, a service instance sends a **registration request** (HTTP REST, gRPC, or DNS) to the registry.
2. The registry stores the entry and assigns a lease.
3. The service sends periodic **heartbeats** to renew the lease.
4. A health-checker daemon pings each registered instance.
5. Failed/missed heartbeats result in **automatic deregistration**.
6. Clients fetch the list of instances when they need to make a call.

#### (b) Client-Side Discovery Working with Diagram (7 Marks)

In **client-side service discovery**, the client application is responsible for:
1. Querying the Service Registry to get all instances of the desired service.
2. Applying a load-balancing algorithm locally to select one instance.
3. Caching the result to reduce registry load.

**Flow Diagram (Textual):**

```
Client App → Service Registry → Get List [A1, A2, A3]
                                    ↓
                              Local LB Algorithm
                                    ↓
                          Select A2 (round-robin)
                                    ↓
                         Direct HTTP call to A2
```

**Example Stack:** Netflix Eureka (Registry) + Netflix Ribbon (Client LB) + Spring Cloud.

**System Availability Calculation:**

Given:
- Number of replicas $n = 4$
- Individual availability $A_i = 0.98$ for each

Using the parallel-availability formula:

$$
A_{sys} = 1 - (1 - A)^n
$$

Substituting values:

$$
A_{sys} = 1 - (1 - 0.98)^4
$$

$$
A_{sys} = 1 - (0.02)^4
$$

$$
A_{sys} = 1 - 0.00000016
$$

$$
\boxed{A_{sys} = 0.99999984 \approx 99.999984\%}
$$

This demonstrates the **"replication for resilience"** principle — 4 replicas each at 98% give 8 nines of availability.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary, Q4(a) Pattern]**

**Q: Compare Self-Registration and Third-Party Registration patterns. Explain the CAP theorem implications for Service Registry design with examples.**

#### (a) Self-Registration vs Third-Party Registration (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Self-Registration | Third-Party Registration |
|---|---|---|
| Who registers? | The service itself | An external process (sidecar/operator) |
| Coupling | Service knows registry API | Service is unaware of registry |
| Language dependency | Tied to service's language stack | Language-agnostic |
| Failure handling | Service crash may leave stale entries | Registrar handles deregistration cleanly |
| Complexity | Low (simple) | Higher (additional component) |
| Example | Eureka client SDK | Registrator + Consul, Kubernetes Operator |

**Self-Registration Example:**
A Spring Boot microservice uses `spring-cloud-starter-netflix-eureka-client`. On `@PostConstruct`, it auto-registers with the Eureka server at `http://eureka-server:8761/eureka/apps/MYSERVICE`.

**Third-Party Registration Example:**
In Kubernetes, a **pod's lifecycle** is managed by the kubelet. When a pod with a specific label comes up, a **service mesh sidecar** (like Istio) or a **custom operator** registers the pod's endpoint with Consul, decoupled from the application code.

**Trade-offs:**
- **Self-Registration** is simpler but couples business code to infrastructure concerns.
- **Third-Party Registration** is cleaner but introduces an extra moving part that can fail.

#### (b) CAP Theorem & Service Registry Design (7 Marks)

The **CAP Theorem** (Eric Brewer, 2000) states that a distributed data store can simultaneously provide only **two out of three** guarantees:
- **C** — Consistency
- **A** — Availability
- **P** — Partition Tolerance

Since **network partitions are inevitable** in distributed systems, the real choice is between **CP** and **AP**.

**CP Registries (Consistency + Partition Tolerance):**
- Trade off availability during a partition.
- Writes are rejected if the cluster cannot reach quorum.
- Example: **HashiCorp Consul** (default mode), **etcd**, **Zookeeper**.
- Used when **strong consistency** is critical (e.g., distributed locks, leader election).

**AP Registries (Availability + Partition Tolerance):**
- Trade off strict consistency; use **eventual consistency**.
- Always respond to reads/writes, even during partitions.
- Example: **Netflix Eureka**.
- Used when **service availability** matters more than perfect consistency (e.g., a slightly stale service list is acceptable).

**CA Registries (theoretical):**
- Cannot tolerate partitions; only viable in single-datacenter setups.
- Example: Traditional RDBMS used as a registry (not recommended for cloud).

**Design Implication for KTU:**
For a **microservices application deployed across multiple cloud regions**, an **AP registry like Eureka** is preferred because:
- During an inter-region network partition, services must still be discoverable.
- A 1-second stale list is acceptable; a 5-minute outage is not.

For a **distributed lock service or coordination layer** (e.g., Kafka's controller election), a **CP registry like Zookeeper** is mandatory because stale data could cause double-leader scenarios.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Service Registry Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Registry with Load Balancer:** The registry stores metadata; it does NOT load-balance traffic. A load balancer queries the registry.
> 2. **Forgetting the availability formula derivation:** Students often write only the formula, skipping the **derivation steps**. KTU awards method marks (up to 3 marks) for showing the logic.
> 3. **Mixing up AP and CP examples:** Eureka = AP, Consul/Zookeeper/etcd = CP. This is a **1-mark item** in Part A — easy to lose.
> 4. **Not labeling diagram nodes:** In 7-mark diagram questions, KTU examiners deduct marks for unlabeled arrows or missing component names. Always label: `Register`, `Heartbeat`, `Query`, `Response`.
> 5. **Ignoring the "Why":** Merely defining terms without explaining engineering motivation loses marks. Always state the **problem solved** (e.g., "avoids hard-coded IPs in a dynamic scaling environment").
> 6. **Units & numerical context:** When computing availability, **always state the result in percentage** (e.g., 99.9999%), not just the decimal. Examiners explicitly check this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Service Registry (Module 4)**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Service Registry = **centralized directory** of service instances
- ☐ Three phases: **Registration → Discovery → Health Monitoring**
