- ☐ Two registration modes: **Self** vs **Third-Party**
- ☐ Two discovery modes: **Client-Side** vs **Server-Side**

### Must-Know Formulas
- ☐ **System availability:** $A_{sys} = 1 - (1-A)^n$
- ☐ **Heartbeat staleness:** $T_{stale} = T_{heartbeat} \times N_{missed}$
- ☐ **Convergence bound:** $T_{converge} \le T_{replication} \times \log_2(N)$
- ☐ **Quorum size:** $Q = \lfloor N/2 \rfloor + 1$

### Must-Know Examples
- ☐ **Eureka** = AP, Netflix, REST-based
- ☐ **Consul** = CP default, multi-DC, KV store
- ☐ **Zookeeper** = CP, ZAB protocol
- ☐ **etcd** = CP, Raft, used by Kubernetes

### Must-Know Numbers
- ☐ Eureka heartbeat = **30 s**, lease = **90 s**
- ☐ 3 replicas at 99% = **99.9999% availability** (5 nines)
- ☐ Quorum for 5 nodes = **3 nodes**

### Must-Know Trade-offs
- ☐ CP → **strong consistency**, may reject during partition
- ☐ AP → **always available**, may be eventually consistent
- ☐ Self-registration → **simple, coupled**
- ☐ Third-party → **decoupled, complex**

### High-Yield KTU Phrases to Use
- ☐ "Single source of truth"
- ☐ "Loose coupling between provider and consumer"
- ☐ "Eventual consistency window"
- ☐ "Heartbeat-based lease renewal mechanism"
- ☐ "Quorum-based fault tolerance"

---

**End of Notes — Service Registry (Module 4, PCCST602)**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Aligned, Outcome-Based Education.*

topics.title: Containers: Comparison of Virtual Machines and Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Containers: Comparison of Virtual Machines and Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Virtual Machines (VMs) — Formal Definition

A **Virtual Machine (VM)** is a software-based emulation of a physical computing environment. It executes an entire operating system (OS) along with its kernel, drivers, and applications on top of a **hypervisor** (also called Virtual Machine Monitor or VMM), which partitions the underlying physical hardware (CPU, RAM, storage, network) into multiple isolated logical servers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCST602 — Module 4):**
> "A Virtual Machine is an isolated compute environment created by abstracting the physical hardware of a single server into multiple execution environments through a Type-1 (bare-metal) or Type-2 (hosted) hypervisor."

**Key Characteristics of VMs:**

- **Full OS Stack:** Each VM runs a complete guest OS (Linux, Windows, etc.), independent of the host OS.
- **Hardware Virtualization:** A hypervisor maps virtual hardware resources to physical hardware.
- **Strong Isolation:** Hardware-level isolation via separate kernels.
- **Boot Time:** Typically **30 to 60 seconds** for a full guest OS to boot.
- **Image Size:** VM images are typically **10 GB to 100 GB** (the size of the entire OS + applications).
- **Hypervisor Overhead:** A bare-metal hypervisor (e.g., VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V) consumes roughly **5% to 10%** of physical resources.

## 1.2 Containers — Formal Definition

A **Container** is a lightweight, standalone, executable software package that bundles an application together with its dependencies (libraries, binaries, configuration files) using **OS-level virtualization**. All containers on a host share the **same host OS kernel** but run in isolated user-space environments defined by **namespaces** and **cgroups** (control groups) in Linux.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCST602 — Module 4):**
> "A Container is an OS-level virtualization mechanism that packages an application and its dependencies into a single portable artifact, sharing the host OS kernel and providing process-level isolation through Linux namespaces and cgroups."

**Key Characteristics of Containers:**

- **Shared OS Kernel:** All containers use the host OS kernel (no guest OS).
- **Lightweight Footprint:** No hypervisor, no separate OS — just the application and its user-space libraries.
- **Process-Level Isolation:** Isolation achieved via Linux **namespaces** (PID, Network, Mount, UTS, IPC, User) and **cgroups** (resource limits).
- **Boot Time:** Typically **milliseconds** (often under 1 second).
- **Image Size:** Container images are typically **10 MB to 500 MB** (no OS, only app + libs).
- **Engine Examples:** Docker, containerd, CRI-O, runC.

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — Housing Model

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Housing Model):**

Imagine you are a software application that needs a "home" to run in:

- **Virtual Machine = A Standalone House:**
  You buy an entire plot of land, build your own house from the foundation up, install your own plumbing, electrical wiring, walls, and roof. Each house is completely independent. Building one takes days (boot time), costs a lot of space (GB of disk and GB of RAM), and you cannot share walls with neighbors. You get complete isolation, but at great cost in space and time.

- **Container = An Apartment in a Shared Building:**
  You move into a large apartment building (the **host OS kernel**). The building provides shared infrastructure — foundation, plumbing, electrical mains, elevator, parking (the **OS kernel, system calls, device drivers**). Your apartment is a self-contained unit (the **container**) with its own walls, doors, and address (the **namespace isolation**). You get privacy and isolation, but you share the building's core resources. Building your apartment takes hours (milliseconds), takes minimal space (MB of disk), and you can stack many apartments in one building (high density on one host).

- **The Building Manager = The Container Engine (e.g., Docker daemon):**
  The manager enforces the rules — which apartment gets how much electricity (CPU quota via cgroups), how much water (memory limit via cgroups), and ensures one tenant's mess doesn't leak into another's apartment (namespace isolation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Analogy Matters for KTU Exams:**
> Examiners love to award marks for the housing/apartment analogy when defining VMs vs Containers. Use it during your descriptive answers to demonstrate conceptual clarity.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Boundaries

| Metric | Virtual Machine | Container |
|---|---|---|
| **Boot Time** | 30 to 60 s | < 1 s |
| **Image Size** | 10 GB to 100 GB | 10 MB to 500 MB |
| **Memory Overhead** | 500 MB to 4 GB per VM | 10 MB to 100 MB per container |
| **Density per Host** | 10 to 100 VMs | 1000+ containers |
| **Isolation Level** | Hardware-level (strong) | Process-level (moderate) |
| **OS Dependency** | Independent guest OS | Shared host kernel |
| **Hypervisor Required** | Yes | No (engine required) |
| **Performance Loss vs Native** | 5% to 15% | 1% to 5% |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Virtual Machine Architecture — Layered Decomposition

The VM stack is a **two-tier virtualization model**:

**Step 1 — Hardware Layer (Physical Server):**
The actual physical CPU, RAM, disks, and NICs. Modern CPUs support hardware-assisted virtualization via **Intel VT-x** or **AMD-V** instruction extensions.

**Step 2 — Hypervisor Layer (VMM):**
The hypervisor is the critical abstraction layer. It has two flavors:
- **Type-1 (Bare-Metal):** Runs directly on hardware (e.g., VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen, KVM). Used in production data centers.
- **Type-2 (Hosted):** Runs as an application on a host OS (e.g., Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation). Used for development and testing.

**Step 3 — Guest OS Layer:**
Each VM contains an entire OS image, including its own kernel, drivers, system libraries, and services. This is the heaviest part of the stack — typically 4 to 8 GB of disk just for a minimal Linux distribution.

**Step 4 — Application Layer:**
The user workload runs inside the guest OS as a normal process. The app is unaware it is virtualized.

**Why VMs Use More Resources — The "Why":**
Every VM must allocate dedicated memory for its kernel, page tables, I/O buffers, and drivers — even if the application inside is idle. This is the **fixed overhead** of VMs.

## 2.2 Container Architecture — Layered Decomposition

The container stack is an **OS-level virtualization model**:

**Step 1 — Hardware Layer:**
Same as VMs — the physical host hardware.

**Step 2 — Host Operating System (Linux Kernel):**
A single Linux kernel runs directly on hardware. The kernel exposes features for isolation:
- **Namespaces:** Provide isolation. The **PID namespace** makes a process believe it is PID 1. The **Network namespace** gives it its own network stack. The **Mount namespace** gives it its own filesystem view.
- **cgroups (Control Groups):** Enforce resource limits — CPU shares, memory caps, I/O bandwidth, PIDs limit.

**Step 3 — Container Engine (e.g., Docker Engine):**
The user-facing layer (CLI, REST API, daemon). The engine pulls images, creates containers, and manages lifecycle. Under the hood, it uses **runC** (a CLI tool conforming to the OCI specification) to spawn containers.

**Step 4 — Application + User-Space Libraries:**
The container image contains only the app binaries and required libraries (e.g., libc, libssl) — no kernel, no drivers.

**Why Containers Are Lightweight — The "How":**
Because all containers share the **same kernel** (the kernel is loaded once in RAM and shared across all containers), there is no duplication of OS-level memory pages. Linux uses **copy-on-write (COW)** filesystems (OverlayFS, AUFS) to share identical file layers across containers.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Must-Memorize Comparison Table**

| Parameter | Virtual Machine | Container |
|---|---|---|
| **Virtualization Level** | Hardware-level | OS-level |
| **Hypervisor/Engine** | Hypervisor (VMM) | Container Engine (Docker) |
| **Guest OS** | Required, full OS | Not required |
| **Kernel** | Separate kernel per VM | Shared host kernel |
| **Isolation Mechanism** | Separate kernel + hardware VT | Namespaces + cgroups |
| **Boot Time** | 30 to 60 s | < 1 s (milliseconds) |
| **Image Size** | 10 GB to 100 GB | 10 MB to 500 MB |
| **Memory Overhead per Instance** | ~500 MB to 4 GB | ~10 MB to 100 MB |
| **Performance Overhead** | 5% to 15% | 1% to 5% |
| **Density on 1 Host** | 10 to 100 VMs | 1000+ containers |
| **Security Isolation** | Strong (hardware boundaries) | Moderate (shared kernel) |
| **Startup Mechanism** | BIOS to bootloader to kernel | runC invokes container process |
| **Portability** | Less portable (hypervisor-specific) | Highly portable (OCI compliant) |
| **Typical Use Case** | Multi-OS workloads, legacy apps | Microservices, cloud-native apps |
| **Examples** | VMware vSphere, KVM, Hyper-V | Docker, Podman, containerd |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cloud Data Centers (AWS EC2, Azure VMs):** Use VMs as the unit of compute allocation because customers need different OSes (Windows Server, multiple Linux distros).
- **Cloud-Native Microservices (Kubernetes Pods):** Use containers because microservices need fast scaling (hundreds of pods per minute), small footprint, and identical environments from dev to production.
- **Hybrid Approach:** Modern stacks run **containers inside VMs**. The VM provides the strong security boundary; containers inside provide the application density. This is what **AWS EKS, Azure AKS, and Google GKE** do.
- **Edge Computing & IoT:** Containers dominate edge devices because of their tiny footprint and ARM portability.
- **DevOps / CI-CD Pipelines:** Containers enable reproducible build environments ("works on my machine" problem solved).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Density Calculation on a Single Host

A common KTU numerical or analytical question asks: *"How many instances can I run on a host with X GB of RAM?"*

**Given Parameters:**

- Total physical RAM on host: $R_{\text{host}}$ (in GB).
- RAM consumed by host OS + container engine / hypervisor: $R_{\text{mgmt}}$ (in GB).
- RAM per instance: $R_{\text{inst}}$ (in GB).
- Usable RAM for instances: $R_{\text{usable}} = R_{\text{host}} - R_{\text{mgmt}}$.
- Maximum number of instances: $N_{\max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{R_{\text{usable}}}{R_{\text{inst}}} \right\rfloor$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & R_{\text{usable}} = R_{\text{host}} - R_{\text{mgmt}} \quad \text{(subtract management overhead)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & N_{\max} = \left\lfloor \frac{R_{\text{usable}}}{R_{\text{inst}}} \right\rfloor \quad \text{(integer floor division)} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Substitute the values and solve.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example:**

A server has **128 GB** of physical RAM. The host OS and Docker engine consume **4 GB**. Each VM requires **4 GB** RAM; each container requires **128 MB** RAM.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{usable}} &= 128 - 4 = 124 \text{ GB} \\
N_{\max}^{\text{VM}} &= \left\lfloor \frac{124}{4} \right\rfloor = 31 \text{ VMs} \\
N_{\max}^{\text{Container}} &= \left\lfloor \frac{124 \times 1024}{128} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{126976}{128} \right\rfloor = 992 \text{ containers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The same hardware supports **31 VMs** but **992 containers** — a **32x density improvement**. This is the core engineering reason containers won the cloud-native era.

## 3.2 Derivation: Storage Footprint Comparison

A common KTU conceptual-cum-numerical question: *"Compare storage overhead of VMs vs Containers for the same application workload."*

**Model Assumption:**
- Minimal Linux guest OS size: $S_{\text{OS}} = 4 \text{ GB}$ (for a bare VM).
- Application binary + dependencies: $S_{\text{app}} = 500 \text{ MB}$.
- Container base image (e.g., Alpine Linux): $S_{\text{base}} = 5 \text{ MB}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{VM (single)}} &= S_{\text{OS}} + S_{\text{app}} = 4 \text{ GB} + 500 \text{ MB} = 4.5 \text{ GB} \\
S_{\text{Container (single)}} &= S_{\text{base}} + S_{\text{app}} = 5 \text{ MB} + 500 \text{ MB} = 505 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ratio of VM to Container Footprint:**

$$
R_{\text{size}} = \frac{S_{\text{VM}}}{S_{\text{Container}}} = \frac{4.5 \text{ GB}}{505 \text{ MB}} = \frac{4500 \text{ MB}}{505 \text{ MB}} \approx 8.91
\end{aligned}
$$

A single VM image is **~9x larger** than a single container image. This compounds across deployments — for 100 instances, the difference is **~400 GB of wasted storage**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Boot Time Overhead

The boot time of a VM is dominated by hardware initialization, BIOS/UEFI, bootloader, kernel load, and init system. Container "boot" is just process forking.

**Empirical KTU-Standard Values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{VM-boot}} &\approx 30 \text{ s to } 60 \text{ s} \\
T_{\text{Container-start}} &\approx 50 \text{ ms to } 500 \text{ ms} \\
R_{\text{boot}} &= \frac{T_{\text{VM-boot}}}{T_{\text{Container-start}}} = \frac{30000 \text{ ms}}{100 \text{ ms}} = 300
\end{aligned}
$$

Containers are **~300x faster to start** than VMs. This is why **Kubernetes** can scale a pod in seconds, while auto-scaling a VM in cloud takes minutes.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Decision Framework (High-Yield for ESE Answers):**

| Use Virtual Machines When… | Use Containers When… |
|---|---|
| You need to run a **different OS** than the host (e.g., Windows on Linux host) | You need **fast scaling** (hundreds of instances per minute) |
| You need **strong security isolation** (multi-tenant cloud, banking workloads) | You run **microservices** that are stateless and lightweight |
| The workload is a **legacy monolithic app** that expects a full OS environment | You want **portability** across dev, test, and production |
| You need to run **kernel-level code** or custom drivers | You want **CI/CD reproducibility** (build once, run anywhere) |
| The workload is **long-lived** and rarely restarted | The workload is **ephemeral** (serverless, batch jobs, scheduled tasks) |
| You need **GPU passthrough** with full hardware access | You need to run on **edge devices** with limited resources |

## 3.5 Hybrid Stack — Containers Inside VMs

Modern production deployments use **VM + Container nesting**:

$$
\text{Physical Hardware} \rightarrow \text{Type-1 Hypervisor (e.g., KVM)} \rightarrow \text{VM (with Linux guest OS)} \rightarrow \text{Container Engine (Docker)} \rightarrow \text{Containers}
$$

**Why This Works (The "Why"):**
- The **VM** provides the **security boundary** — different customers/tenants get different VMs.
- The **containers inside** provide **application density** — many containers share one VM's resources efficiently.
- This is the architecture used by **AWS Fargate, Google Cloud Run, Azure Container Instances**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Virtual Machine Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    hwLayer1[PHYSICAL HARDWARE LAYER]
    cpu1[CPU with VT-x or AMD-V]
    ram1[RAM Modules]
    disk1[NVMe Storage]
    nic1[Network Interface Card]

    hvLayer[TYRE 1 HYPERVISOR LAYER]
    hvName[VMware ESXi or KVM or Hyper-V]
    hvRole[Schedules CPU, Memory, IO across VMs]

    vmA[VM 1 INSTANCE]
    vmB[VM 2 INSTANCE]
    vmC[VM 3 INSTANCE]

    guestA[Guest OS Linux Kernel A]
    guestB[Guest OS Windows Kernel B]
    guestC[Guest OS Linux Kernel C]

    appA[Application A with Libraries]
    appB[Application B with Libraries]
    appC[Application C with Libraries]

    hwLayer1 --> cpu1
    hwLayer1 --> ram1
    hwLayer1 --> disk1
    hwLayer1 --> nic1

    cpu1 --> hvLayer
    ram1 --> hvLayer
    disk1 --> hvLayer
    nic1 --> hvLayer

    hvLayer --> vmA
    hvLayer --> vmB
    hvLayer --> vmC

    vmA --> guestA
    vmB --> guestB
    vmC --> guestC

    guestA --> appA
    guestB --> appB
    guestC --> appC
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Container Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    hwLayer2[PHYSICAL HARDWARE LAYER]
    cpu2[CPU x86 or ARM]
    ram2[RAM Modules]
    disk2[SSD Storage]
    nic2[Network Interface Card]

    hostOS[HOST OPERATING SYSTEM LAYER]
    kernel[Linux Kernel with Namespaces and Cgroups]
    ns[Namespaces: PID, NET, MNT, UTS, IPC, USER]
    cg[Cgroups: CPU shares, Memory limits, IO bandwidth]

    engineLayer[CONTAINER ENGINE LAYER]
    eng[Docker Engine or containerd]
    runc[runC: OCI runtime]
    api[REST API and CLI]

    cA[CONTAINER 1]
    cB[CONTAINER 2]
    cC[CONTAINER 3]
    cD[CONTAINER 4]

    appD[App1 plus User Libs]
    appE[App2 plus User Libs]
    appF[App3 plus User Libs]
    appG[App4 plus User Libs]

    hwLayer2 --> cpu2
    hwLayer2 --> ram2
    hwLayer2 --> disk2
    hwLayer2 --> nic2

    cpu2 --> hostOS
    ram2 --> hostOS
    disk2 --> hostOS
    nic2 --> hostOS

    hostOS --> kernel
    kernel --> ns
    kernel --> cg

    hostOS --> engineLayer
    engineLayer --> eng
    eng --> runc
    eng --> api

    engineLayer --> cA
    engineLayer --> cB
    engineLayer --> cC
    engineLayer --> cD

    cA --> appD
    cB --> appE
    cC --> appF
    cD --> appG
```

## 4.3 Side-by-Side Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vmSide[VM ARCHITECTURE]
    ctrSide[CONTAINER ARCHITECTURE]

    vmSide --> vm1[App]
    vmSide --> vm2[Bins and Libs]
    vmSide --> vm3[Guest OS]
    vmSide --> vm4[Hypervisor]
    vmSide --> vm5[Host Hardware]

    ctrSide --> c1[App]
    ctrSide --> c2[Bins and Libs]
    ctrSide --> c3[Container Engine]
    ctrSide --> c4[Host OS Kernel]
    ctrSide --> c5[Host Hardware]
```

## 4.4 Resource Isolation Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    req[Container or VM Request for Resource]
    nsCheck{Linux Namespace Check}
    cgCheck{cgroup Resource Limit Check}

    pidNs[PID Namespace: Isolated Process Tree]
    netNs[Network Namespace: Isolated Network Stack]
    mntNs[Mount Namespace: Isolated Filesystem View]
    cpuLimit[CPU Quota Enforcement]
    memLimit[Memory Cap Enforcement]
    ioLimit[Block IO Bandwidth Throttling]

    allow[Resource Allocated to Container]
    deny[Request Throttled or Rejected]

    req --> nsCheck
    req --> cgCheck

    nsCheck --> pidNs
    nsCheck --> netNs
    nsCheck --> mntNs

    cgCheck --> cpuLimit
    cgCheck --> memLimit
    cgCheck --> ioLimit

    pidNs --> allow
    netNs --> allow
    mntNs --> allow

    cpuLimit --> allow
    cpuLimit --> deny
    memLimit --> allow
    memLimit --> deny
    ioLimit --> allow
    ioLimit --> deny
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Module 4, Q1]**

**Q1. Define a Virtual Machine. List any three differences between a Virtual Machine and a Container. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Virtual Machine (VM)** is a software emulation of a physical computer system, created by a hypervisor that partitions the underlying hardware into multiple isolated execution environments, each running its own complete guest operating system.

**Three Key Differences:**

1. **Virtualization Level:** VMs use hardware-level virtualization via a hypervisor; containers use OS-level virtualization sharing the host kernel.
2. **Guest OS Requirement:** Each VM requires a full guest OS (~4 GB+); containers share the host OS kernel and have no guest OS.
3. **Boot Time and Size:** VMs take 30-60 seconds to boot and are 10-100 GB in size; containers start in milliseconds and are 10-500 MB.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Module 4, Q2]**

**Q2. What is a Linux namespace? How does it enable container isolation? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Linux namespace** is a kernel feature that wraps a global system resource (process IDs, network stack, mount points, etc.) in an abstraction layer, so that processes inside a namespace see their own isolated instance of the resource and remain unaware of resources outside.

**Container Isolation via Namespaces:** When a container is created, the Docker engine invokes the `clone()` system call with flags like `CLONE_NEWPID`, `CLONE_NEWNET`, `CLONE_NEWNS`, `CLONE_NEWUTS`, `CLONE_NEWIPC`, and `CLONE_NEWUSER`. This gives the container:

- Its own **PID tree** (process inside sees itself as PID 1).
- Its own **network interfaces** (private IP, isolated sockets).
- Its own **filesystem mount table**.
- Its own **hostname and domain name**.
- Its own **inter-process communication** channels.
- Its own **user ID mappings**.

This makes each container appear to be an independent Linux system to the application, even though it shares the host kernel with other containers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam — December 2024, Module 4, Q11 — Part A Choice]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q11(a). Explain the architecture of a Virtual Machine in detail. Describe Type-1 and Type-2 hypervisors with examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Virtual Machine (VM)** is built on a layered architecture that abstracts physical hardware into multiple isolated compute environments.

**Architecture Layers (Top to Bottom):**

1. **Application Layer:** The user workload (web server, database, etc.) runs as a normal process inside the guest OS.
2. **Guest OS Layer:** Each VM contains a complete OS — kernel, drivers, system libraries, init system, package manager. Common examples: Ubuntu, CentOS, Windows Server.
3. **Hypervisor Layer (VMM):** The most critical layer. It creates, schedules, and manages VMs by mapping virtual hardware (vCPU, vRAM, vDisk, vNIC) to physical hardware. It enforces isolation between VMs.
4. **Physical Hardware Layer:** The actual server with CPU, RAM, storage, and network.

**Type-1 Hypervisor (Bare-Metal):**
- Runs **directly on the physical hardware** without a host OS.
- Higher performance, lower overhead, used in enterprise data centers.
- Examples: **VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Citrix XenServer, Red Hat KVM**.
- KVM (Kernel-based VM) converts the Linux kernel itself into a hypervisor.

**Type-2 Hypervisor (Hosted):**
- Runs as a **software application on top of a host OS**.
- Easier to install and use, suitable for development and testing.
- Lower performance due to the extra host OS layer.
- Examples: **Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels Desktop**.

**Diagrammatic View (Mental Picture):**

- Type-1: `Hardware → Hypervisor → VMs (Guest OS → App)`
- Type-2: `Hardware → Host OS → Hypervisor (App) → VMs (Guest OS → App)`

**Q11(b). With neat diagrams, explain the architecture of a Container system. Discuss the role of namespaces and cgroups. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Container** is an OS-level virtualization unit that packages an application with its dependencies into a single portable artifact. The architecture is:

1. **Application Layer:** The user workload plus its required user-space libraries (libc, libssl, etc.).
2. **Container Engine Layer:** Docker Engine (or containerd, Podman) — the user-facing daemon that pulls images, creates containers, and manages lifecycle.
3. **Host OS Kernel Layer:** A single Linux kernel providing isolation primitives and resource control.
4. **Physical Hardware Layer:** The underlying server.

**Role of Namespaces:**
Namespaces provide **isolation**. Each container gets its own view of global resources. There are 6 main namespace types in Linux:

- **PID namespace:** Container sees its processes as PIDs 1 to N. Host sees them as PIDs 1000 to 1000+N.
- **Network namespace:** Container has its own virtual interface (e.g., `eth0`), IP address, port space, and routing table.
- **Mount namespace:** Container has its own root filesystem and mount points.
- **UTS namespace:** Container can have its own hostname and domain name.
- **IPC namespace:** Container has its own inter-process communication (System V IPC, POSIX message queues).
- **User namespace:** Container has its own UID/GID mapping to host users.

**Role of cgroups (Control Groups):**
cgroups provide **resource limits**. They enforce:

- **CPU limits:** `cpu.shares`, `cpu.cfs_quota_us`, `cpu.cfs_period_us`.
- **Memory limits:** `memory.limit_in_bytes` — kills the container if exceeded.
- **Block I/O limits:** `blkio.throttle.read_bps_device`, `blkio.throttle.write_bps_device`.
- **PIDs limit:** `pids.max` — prevents fork bombs.
- **Network bandwidth limits** (via net_cls and net_prio cgroups).

**Diagrammatic View (Mental Picture):**
`Hardware → Host OS Kernel (Namespaces + cgroups) → Docker Engine → [Container1, Container2, Container3, ...]`

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing the engine with the kernel:** Many students write "Docker is a kernel" — Docker is the **engine**, the Linux kernel is below it. Lose 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Skipping the 6 namespace names:** Examiners award 1 mark each for listing PID, Network, Mount, UTS, IPC, User namespaces. Writing only "namespace provides isolation" without the 6 types loses 3-4 marks.
> 3. **Writing "cgroups provide isolation":** cgroups provide **resource limits**, NOT isolation. Isolation = namespaces, Limits = cgroups. Conflating them is a common 2-mark deduction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam — December 2024, Module 4, Q11 — Part B Choice]**

**Q11(a). Compare Virtual Machines and Containers across 10 key parameters. Justify why containers have become the preferred choice for cloud-native microservices. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | Virtual Machine | Container |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Virtualization Level | Hardware-level | OS-level |
| 2 | Hypervisor/Engine | Required (VMM) | Required (Container engine) |
| 3 | Guest OS | Full OS per VM | None — shares host kernel |
| 4 | Image Size | 10-100 GB | 10-500 MB |
| 5 | Boot Time | 30-60 s | < 1 s |
| 6 | Memory Overhead | 500 MB-4 GB per VM | 10-100 MB per container |
| 7 | Density per Host | 10-100 VMs | 1000+ containers |
| 8 | Isolation | Hardware-level (strong) | Process-level (moderate) |
| 9 | Performance Overhead | 5-15% | 1-5% |
| 10 | Portability | Hypervisor-specific | OCI-compliant, highly portable |

**Why Containers Won the Cloud-Native Era:**

1. **Microservices architecture** demands hundreds of small stateless services. Containers boot in milliseconds, allowing auto-scaling in seconds.
2. **Kubernetes (K8s)** orchestrates containers natively. Pods, Deployments, Services, and Ingress are all designed around container primitives.
3. **Resource efficiency** — a single host runs 10-30x more containers than VMs, reducing cloud bills.
4. **DevOps & CI/CD** — `docker build` produces an immutable artifact that runs identically from dev to test to prod ("build once, run anywhere").
5. **Portability** — the OCI (Open Container Initiative) standard ensures a container image runs on any compliant engine (Docker, Podman, containerd).

**Q11(b). A cloud server has 256 GB RAM. The host OS consumes 8 GB. Each VM needs 8 GB; each container needs 256 MB. Calculate the maximum number of VMs and containers. Also discuss the hybrid stack model. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Compute Usable RAM:**

$$
R_{\text{usable}} = R_{\text{host}} - R_{\text{mgmt}} = 256 \text{ GB} - 8 \text{ GB} = 248 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 2: Convert Usable RAM to MB:**

$$
R_{\text{usable}} = 248 \times 1024 = 253952 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Maximum VMs:**

$$
N_{\max}^{\text{VM}} = \left\lfloor \frac{248 \text{ GB}}{8 \text{ GB}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 31 \right\rfloor = 31 \text{ VMs}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Maximum Containers:**

$$
N_{\max}^{\text{Container}} = \left\lfloor \frac{253952 \text{ MB}}{256 \text{ MB}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 992 \right\rfloor = 992 \text{ containers}
$$

**Step 5: Density Ratio:**

$$
R_{\text{density}} = \frac{992}{31} = 32
\end{aligned}
$$

The same 256 GB host supports **31 VMs** but **992 containers** — a **32x density improvement**.

**Hybrid Stack Model — Discussion:**

In modern production, neither VMs nor containers are used in isolation. The dominant pattern is **"containers inside VMs"**:

- **Public cloud providers** (AWS, Azure, GCP) provision **VMs** as the unit of compute (because customers need different OSes and strong isolation).
- Inside each VM, customers run a **container engine** (Docker/containerd) and orchestrate **containers** (managed by Kubernetes).
- The **VM** provides the **multi-tenant security boundary**; the **container** provides the **application density**.

**Example: AWS EKS Architecture:**
`Bare Metal → Xen Hypervisor → EC2 VM (Linux) → kubelet → containerd → Pods (containers)`

This combines the **security of VMs** with the **efficiency of containers** — the best of both worlds.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE**

### Core Concepts
- **VM = Hardware-level virtualization** using a **hypervisor**, with a **full guest OS** per instance.
- **Container = OS-level virtualization** using a **container engine** (Docker), sharing the **host kernel** with no guest OS.
- **Isolation in containers = Linux namespaces** (PID, Network, Mount, UTS, IPC, User).
- **Resource limits in containers = cgroups** (CPU, memory, I/O, PIDs).
- **Type-1 hypervisor** runs on bare metal (VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V).
- **Type-2 hypervisor** runs on a host OS (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation).

### Key Numbers to Remember
- VM boot time: **30 to 60 s**
- Container start time: **< 1 s** (milliseconds)
- VM image size: **10 to 100 GB**
- Container image size: **10 to 500 MB**
- VM memory overhead: **500 MB to 4 GB**
- Container memory overhead: **10 to 100 MB**
- VM density per host: **10 to 100**
- Container density per host: **1000+**
- Density improvement factor: **~30x**

### Mnemonics
- **"VIM"** → **V**Ms need a full OS, **I**solation is hardware, **M**emory-heavy.
- **"CAP"** → **C**ontainers share the **A**pplication kernel, **P**rocess-level isolation, lightweight.
- **"NC-RL"** → **N**amespaces = isolation, **C**groups = **R**esource **L**imits.

### Common Exam Traps
- ❌ Writing "Docker is a kernel" → Docker is an **engine**, the **Linux kernel** is below it.
- ❌ Writing "cgroups provide isolation" → cgroups provide **resource limits**; namespaces provide **isolation**.
- ❌ Saying "containers have multiple OS" → containers **share one** host kernel.
- ❌ Saying "VMs are always faster" → VMs are **slower to boot and heavier**; containers are faster.
- ❌ Confusing Type-1 and Type-2 hypervisors — **Type-1 = bare metal**, **Type-2 = hosted on OS**.

### One-Line Exam Definitions
- **Virtual Machine:** "A software emulation of a physical computer, created by a hypervisor, running a full guest OS."
- **Container:** "An OS-level virtualization unit that packages an application with its dependencies, sharing the host kernel via namespaces and cgroups."
- **Namespace:** "A Linux kernel feature that isolates a global resource so a process sees its own private instance."
- **Cgroup:** "A Linux kernel feature that limits and accounts for resource usage (CPU, memory, I/O) of a process group."
- **Hypervisor:** "Software that creates, runs, and manages virtual machines by abstracting physical hardware."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Docker
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Docker

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Docker** is an open-source **containerization platform** that enables developers to package, distribute, and run applications inside lightweight, portable, self-sufficient **containers** that include everything needed to run the application: code, runtime, system tools, system libraries, and settings. It follows the **Build, Ship, Run** paradigm and is built on top of operating-system-level virtualization (containerization) using Linux kernel features such as **Namespaces** and **Control Groups (cgroups)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Docker is a tool designed to make it easier to create, deploy, and run applications by using containers. Containers allow a developer to package an application with all of its required parts, such as libraries and other dependencies, and ship it all out as one package.

The three pillars of the Docker ecosystem are:

1. **Build** – Create a Docker Image from a `Dockerfile`.
2. **Ship** – Push the image to a Registry (Docker Hub, AWS ECR, Azure ACR, etc.).
3. **Run** – Pull the image and instantiate it as a running Container on any Docker host.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Container** is a runtime instance of a **Docker Image**. An image is a *read-only template*; a container is a *writable, executable* process spawned from that template.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Shipping Container Revolution

Imagine the world of logistics **before standardized shipping containers** (pre-1956). Goods — coal, wheat, electronics — were transported in all shapes and sizes: wooden crates, sacks, barrels, loose piles. Loading a single ship with mixed cargo took days of manual labor, and damage rates were catastrophically high.

Then came the **standardized intermodal shipping container** (a fixed steel box). Suddenly, **any** container could be loaded onto **any** ship, train, or truck. The goods inside could be anything — but the *outer* interface became universal. The world economy boomed.

**Docker is to software what the shipping container was to global trade.**

- The **Container** = the standardized steel box
- The **App + Dependencies** = the goods inside
- The **Ship/Train/Truck** = the OS or Cloud (any host)
- The **Crane (Crane Operator)** = the Docker Engine (Daemon)

Before Docker, developers packaged software in **Virtual Machines (VMs)** — heavy, OS-laden "boxes" that bundled an entire guest OS for every app. This is like shipping each item in its own custom-built truck. Docker, by contrast, shares the host OS kernel and ships only the application and its unique libraries — light, fast, and uniform.

## 1.3 Why Docker? Core Value Propositions

| Value | Description |
|---|---|
| **Portability** | "Build once, run anywhere" — on laptop, on-prem server, or any cloud |
| **Lightweight** | Containers share the host kernel, so no full OS per app |
| **Speed** | Boot time in **milliseconds** (vs minutes for VMs) |
| **Isolation** | Each container has its own filesystem, network, and process space |
| **Scalability** | Easily replicated horizontally via orchestrators (Kubernetes, Swarm) |
| **Versioning** | Images are layered; `git`-like rollbacks and tagging |
| **Reproducibility** | `Dockerfile` = declarative, version-controlled environment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metrics You Must Memorize for KTU:**
> - **Container Boot Time:** ~ **50–500 ms**
> - **Image Size (typical):** **5 MB – 500 MB** (vs **1 GB – 50 GB** for a VM)
> - **Containers per Host:** **100s to 1000s** (vs **10s** for VMs)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

## 2.1 Docker Architecture: The Client–Server Model

The Docker platform follows a classic **Client–Daemon** architecture. Every interaction you make with the `docker` CLI is translated into a REST API call that the **Docker Daemon** (`dockerd`) processes.

### 2.1.1 The Three High-Level Components

1. **Docker Client (`docker`)** — The user-facing CLI. It sends commands via **REST API** over a UNIX socket or TCP port.
2. **Docker Daemon (`dockerd`)** — The persistent background service. It manages images, containers, networks, and volumes.
3. **Docker Registry** — A stateless, scalable server that stores and distributes Docker Images. Examples: **Docker Hub** (public), **AWS ECR**, **Azure Container Registry**, **Google GCR**.

### 2.1.2 Detailed Component Breakdown

- **Docker Image:** A read-only, layered, *union-mounted* filesystem snapshot defined by a `Dockerfile`. It is the **blueprint**.
- **Docker Container:** A runnable instance of an image. It is the **building** constructed from the blueprint.
- **Dockerfile:** A text manifest of instructions (`FROM`, `RUN`, `COPY`, `CMD`, `EXPOSE`, `ENTRYPOINT`) used to build an image.
- **Docker Volume:** A persistent storage mechanism decoupled from the container's ephemeral filesystem, stored on the host.
- **Docker Network:** A virtual network interface that allows containers to communicate. Drivers include `bridge`, `host`, `overlay`, `macvlan`, and `none`.
- **Docker Compose:** A YAML-based tool to define and run multi-container applications using a single `docker-compose.yml` file.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Underlying Linux Kernel Features (must know for KTU viva):**
> - **Namespaces** (`pid`, `net`, `mnt`, `uts`, `ipc`, `user`) — provide *isolation*. Each container gets its own view of processes, networking, filesystems, hostnames, and users.
> - **Control Groups (cgroups)** — provide *resource limiting* (CPU, RAM, block I/O, network bandwidth).
> - **Union File Systems (UnionFS / OverlayFS)** — enable the **layered image architecture**.

## 2.2 The Layered Image Architecture

Docker images are built as a **stack of read-only layers**. Each instruction in a `Dockerfile` creates a new layer. Layers are cached, shared, and re-used across images, which makes builds extremely fast and storage efficient.

When a container is launched, Docker adds a thin **read-write layer** on top of the image stack. All runtime changes (file creation, modification, deletion) go into this ephemeral writable layer.

## 2.3 Container vs Virtual Machine — The Definitive Comparison

This is the **single most-asked Docker question in KTU exams**. Memorize the table below.

| Feature | Virtual Machine (VM) | Docker Container |
|---|---|---|
| **Virtualization Level** | Hardware-level | OS-level |
| **Boot Time** | **1–5 minutes** | **50–500 ms** |
| **Size** | **GBs** (full guest OS) | **MBs** (app + libs only) |
| **OS Support** | Any OS on any hypervisor | Shares host kernel (Linux host required for Linux containers) |
| **Performance** | Near-native (with overhead) | Native (negligible overhead) |
| **Isolation** | Strong (separate kernel) | Process-level (weaker but sufficient) |
| **Density per Host** | **10–20** VMs | **100s–1000s** containers |
| **Hypervisor Required** | **Yes** (KVM, VMware, Hyper-V) | **No** (uses `containerd`/`runc`) |
| **Security Boundary** | Strong VM boundary | Relies on kernel hardening |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Containers are the **de-facto deployment unit** of modern **microservices**, **CI/CD pipelines**, **DevOps workflows**, and **cloud-native applications** (Kubernetes, OpenShift, ECS). VMs remain the choice for *strong multi-tenancy* and *heterogeneous OS workloads*.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Conceptual Cheat Sheet

| Term | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|
| Image | Read-only template with all dependencies |
| Container | Running instance of an image |
| Dockerfile | Build instructions (declarative text file) |
| Registry | Storage and distribution system for images |
| Volume | Persistent storage decoupled from container lifecycle |
| Network | Virtual network for inter-container communication |
| Compose | Tool to run multi-container apps from YAML |
| Daemon | Background service (`dockerd`) managing everything |
| Namespace | Linux kernel feature providing isolation |
| cgroup | Linux kernel feature providing resource limits |
| OverlayFS | Union filesystem enabling image layers |
| Multi-stage build | Dockerfile pattern to shrink final image size |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Workflow Analysis & The Build–Ship–Run Pipeline

Since Docker is a workflow-driven technology rather than a math-heavy derivation, the equivalent of a "derivation" is the **end-to-end Build–Ship–Run lifecycle** of a containerized application. We trace it step-by-step.

## 3.1 The Build–Ship–Run Pipeline: Exhaustive Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU answer skeleton** for any "Explain the Docker workflow" question worth 7+ marks.

### **Step 1: WRITE — Author the Application Source Code**

The developer writes application code in any language (Python, Java, Node.js, Go, etc.). For this derivation, assume a simple **Python Flask** web application with a single file `app.py`.

The application needs a specific Python interpreter version and a `flask` library. This *dependency declaration* is what makes the build reproducible.

### **Step 2: DECLARE — Write the `Dockerfile`**

A `Dockerfile` is a sequential, top-to-bottom recipe. Each line becomes a **layer** in the final image.

```dockerfile
# Step 2a: Choose a base image with the required runtime
FROM python:3.11-slim

# Step 2b: Set the working directory inside the container
WORKDIR /app

# Step 2c: Copy dependency manifest first (for layer caching)
COPY requirements.txt .

# Step 2d: Install dependencies — this creates a layer
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Step 2e: Copy the actual application source code
COPY app.py .

# Step 2f: Document the port the container listens on
EXPOSE 5000

# Step 2g: Define the default executable command
CMD ["python", "app.py"]
```

**Logic behind each step (the "Why"):**
- `FROM` initializes the base layer — choosing `slim` minimizes size.
- `WORKDIR` avoids hardcoded `cd` commands and creates a clean directory.
- Copying `requirements.txt` *before* `app.py` exploits layer caching: if the source code changes but dependencies don't, Docker reuses the dependency layer.
- `--no-cache-dir` keeps the image small by skipping pip's download cache.
- `EXPOSE` is *documentation*; it does **not** actually publish the port (that requires `-p` at runtime).
- `CMD` can be overridden at runtime, unlike `ENTRYPOINT`.

### **Step 3: BUILD — Compile the Dockerfile into an Image**

The user issues the `docker build` command:

```bash
docker build -t my-flask-app:v1.0 .
```

**What happens internally (the derivation):**

1. The Docker CLI sends the build context (the `.` directory) to the daemon via REST API.
2. The daemon reads the `Dockerfile` line by line.
3. For each instruction, a **temporary container** is launched, the instruction is executed inside it, the resulting filesystem changes are committed as a new **image layer**, and the temporary container is discarded.
4. The final image is tagged `my-flask-app:v1.0` and stored in the local image cache.

The image is identified by a **SHA-256 digest** for cryptographic immutability:

$$
\text{imageID} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{image layers concatenated})
$$

### **Step 4: SHIP — Push the Image to a Registry**

```bash
docker login                 # authenticate with Docker Hub
docker tag my-flask-app:v1.0 username/my-flask-app:v1.0
docker push username/my-flask-app:v1.0
```

**Logic:** Tagging creates an alias pointing to the same image digest. The push uploads each layer to the registry, but layers already present on the registry (from prior pushes) are **deduplicated and skipped** — this is why image pushes are usually fast.

### **Step 5: RUN — Pull and Instantiate a Container**

On any other machine (CI server, cloud VM, Kubernetes node):

```bash
docker pull username/my-flask-app:v1.0   # download layers
docker run -d -p 8080:5000 --name webapp username/my-flask-app:v1.0
```

**What `-d -p 8080:5000` means:**
- `-d` → **detached mode** (runs in the background).
- `-p 8080:5000` → **port mapping**: host port `8080` is forwarded to container port `5000`.
- `--name webapp` → assigns a human-readable container name.

**Internally, the daemon does the following:**
1. Verifies the image (and pulls missing layers).
2. Creates a new **container directory** on the host.
3. Adds a **thin read-write layer** on top of the image.
4. Allocates a **network namespace** (with a virtual interface on a bridge network).
5. Sets **cgroup limits** (defaults: unlimited unless specified with `--memory`, `--cpus`).
6. Executes the `CMD` (or `ENTRYPOINT`) as **PID 1** inside the new namespace.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The container is now a *process* (or a small group of processes) on the host — not a separate OS. This is why containers boot in milliseconds.

## 3.2 The Layer-Caching Derivation: Why Build Times Vary

Consider a 3-instruction Dockerfile:

$$
\text{Layer}_{1} = \text{FS}(\text{FROM base})
$$
$$
\text{Layer}_{2} = \Delta\text{FS}(\text{RUN apt-get install...})
$$
$$
\text{Layer}_{3} = \Delta\text{FS}(\text{COPY ./app /app})
$$

When you rebuild, Docker computes a **cache hash** for each instruction (the instruction text + the SHA-256 of the parent layer + the SHA-256 of any files copied). If a hash matches the cache, the layer is **reused**; otherwise, that layer *and all subsequent layers* are rebuilt.

$$
\text{BuildTime} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Big[ \mathbb{1}(\text{cache hit}_i) \cdot T_{\text{cache}} + \mathbb{1}(\text{cache miss}_i) \cdot T_{\text{exec}} \Big]
$$

where $\mathbb{1}(\cdot)$ is the **indicator function** (1 if true, 0 if false), and $T_{\text{exec}} \gg T_{\text{cache}}$.

**Engineering implication:** Always place *least-frequently-changed* instructions at the top of the `Dockerfile` to maximize cache reuse — this is the **layer-ordering optimization principle**.

## 3.3 The Multi-Stage Build: A Size-Derivation Example

A "fat" image containing compilers, build tools, and intermediate artifacts can be **> 1 GB**. A multi-stage build derives the final image size as follows:

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{final}} = \text{Size}(\text{assets copied from builder stage})
$$

**Worked Example:**

```dockerfile
# Stage 1: builder (large image with compilers)
FROM golang:1.22 AS builder
WORKDIR /src
COPY . .
RUN go build -o myapp

# Stage 2: runtime (tiny image)
FROM gcr.io/distroless/base
COPY --from=builder /src/myapp /myapp
CMD ["/myapp"]
```

- **Builder stage size:** $\approx$ **900 MB**
- **Final stage size:** $\approx$ **20 MB**
- **Reduction factor:** $\frac{900 - 20}{900} \times 100\% \approx$ **97.8%**

This is a quantitative derivation the KTU examiner loves to see in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Docker High-Level Architecture (Client–Daemon–Registry)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    user[Developer User]
    cli[Docker Client CLI]
    api[REST API over UNIX Socket or TCP]
    daemon[Docker Daemon dockerd]
    imgStore[Local Image Cache]
    ctrMgr[Container Manager]
    netMgr[Network Manager]
    volMgr[Volume Manager]
    imgBuild[Image Builder]
    hub[Docker Registry Hub ECR ACR]
    c1[Container A Web App]
    c2[Container B DB]
    c3[Container C Cache]
    host[Host OS Kernel Namespaces cgroups OverlayFS]

    user -->|types commands| cli
    cli -->|HTTPS REST calls| api
    api --> daemon
    daemon --> imgStore
    daemon --> ctrMgr
    daemon --> netMgr
    daemon --> volMgr
    daemon --> imgBuild
    imgBuild -->|pulls or pushes| hub
    imgStore -->|instantiates| c1
    imgStore -->|instantiates| c2
    imgStore -->|instantiates| c3
    ctrMgr --> host
    netMgr --> host
    c1 --> host
    c2 --> host
    c3 --> host
```

## 4.2 Container vs Virtual Machine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VMStack[Virtual Machine Stack]
        app1vm[App A]
        app2vm[App B]
        binvm[Bins Libs]
        guestosvm[Guest OS Ubuntu]
        hypervisorvm[Hypervisor VMware KVM]
        hostvm[Host OS]
        hwvm[Hardware]
        app1vm --> binvm
        app2vm --> binvm
        binvm --> guestosvm
        guestosvm --> hypervisorvm
        hypervisorvm --> hostvm
        hostvm --> hwvm
    end

    subgraph ContainerStack[Container Stack]
        app1c[App A]
        app2c[App B]
        binc[Bins Libs]
        dockerengc[Container Engine Docker]
        hostc[Host OS]
        hwc[Hardware]
        app1c --> binc
        app2c --> binc
        binc --> dockerengc
        dockerengc --> hostc
        hostc --> hwc
    end
```

## 4.3 The Build Ship Run Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    code[Source Code app.py]
    dockerfile[Dockerfile]
    build[docker build]
    image[Docker Image Tagged Read Only Layers]
    push[docker push]
    registry[Docker Registry Docker Hub]
    pull[docker pull]
    run[docker run]
    container[Running Container Writable Layer]
    request[HTTP Request on Port 8080]

    code --> dockerfile
    dockerfile --> build
    build --> image
    image --> push
    push --> registry
    registry --> pull
    pull --> image
    image --> run
    run --> container
    container --> request
```

## 4.4 Image Layer Stack with Container Writable Layer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer0[Layer 0 Base Image ubuntu 22.04 ~77 MB]
    layer1[Layer 1 apt install python3 ~50 MB]
    layer2[Layer 2 pip install flask ~5 MB]
    layer3[Layer 3 COPY app.py ~2 KB]
    rw[Container Writable Layer Runtime Changes ~0 MB at start]
    layer0 --> layer1
    layer1 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer3
    layer3 --> rw
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the layer diagram in an exam:** The total image size is the **sum of all read-only layers** (cumulative). The writable layer is **per-container** and is lost when the container is removed (unless changes are committed or volumes are used).

## 4.5 Docker Network and Volume Abstraction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph bridgeNet[Bridge Network mynet]
        cA[Container A IP 172.17.0.2]
        cB[Container B IP 172.17.0.3]
        cC[Container C IP 172.17.0.4]
    end
    vol1[Named Volume data_persist Stored on Host /var/lib/docker/volumes]
    cA --- vol1
    cB --- vol1
    hostPort[Host Port 8080]
    cA --> hostPort
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme** assessment pattern: Part A (2-mark and 3-mark short answers) and Part B (14-mark descriptive with internal choice). Bloom's levels tagged in `[...]`.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] [Remember / Understand]**
**Define a Docker container. How is it different from a Docker image? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Docker image** is a read-only, layered filesystem snapshot that serves as a *blueprint* containing the application code, runtime, libraries, and configuration. It is built from a `Dockerfile` and stored in a registry.

A **Docker container** is a *runnable instance* of a Docker image. It adds a thin **read-write layer** on top of the image and runs as an isolated process (or group of processes) on the host OS using Linux **namespaces** and **cgroups**.

**Key distinction:** Image = static template (class). Container = running instance (object). One image can spawn unlimited containers. **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] [Understand]**
**List and briefly explain any three advantages of Docker containers over Virtual Machines. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Lightweight Footprint:** Containers share the host OS kernel and ship only the application + dependencies (typically **MBs**), while VMs bundle a full guest OS (**GBs**). This yields **higher density** (100s of containers vs 10s of VMs per host).
2. **Faster Startup:** Containers boot in **milliseconds** (≈ 50–500 ms) because no OS initialization is required, whereas VMs take **1–5 minutes** to boot the guest OS.
3. **Better Resource Utilization:** Because there is no hypervisor overhead and no duplicate OS, CPU and memory utilization is more efficient, lowering cloud compute costs. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Students frequently confuse **Image** with **Container** in their answers — losing 2 marks immediately. Always define both explicitly in the first paragraph. Also, students describe *what* Docker does but forget the *underlying kernel mechanism* (namespaces + cgroups) — this costs another 2 marks in 14-mark answers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] [Understand / Apply]**

**(a)** Explain the Docker architecture with a neat block diagram. Describe the roles of the **Docker Client**, **Docker Daemon**, and **Docker Registry**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Virtual Machines** and **Docker Containers** based on architecture, boot time, size, isolation mechanism, performance, and OS support. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The Docker platform follows a **client–server architecture** built on a REST API.

**1. Docker Client:**
- It is the primary user interface, invoked through the CLI command `docker`.
- It can communicate with one or more daemons using REST API over UNIX sockets (`/var/run/docker.sock`) or TCP.
- It only sends commands; all heavy lifting is performed by the daemon.

**2. Docker Daemon (`dockerd`):**
- A persistent background service running on the host machine.
- Responsible for building images, running containers, managing networks and volumes.
- Exposes a **REST API** that the client talks to.
- Internally uses **`containerd`** (high-level container runtime) and **`runc`** (low-level runtime that interfaces with Linux kernel features like namespaces and cgroups).

**3. Docker Registry:**
- A stateless, distributed server for storing and distributing images.
- **Docker Hub** is the default public registry; private registries include AWS ECR, Azure ACR, and JFrog Artifactory.
- Supports `docker pull` (download) and `docker push` (upload) operations.
- Images are content-addressed by their **SHA-256 digest**, ensuring immutability.

**4. Supporting components:**
- **Docker Images** – read-only layered templates.
- **Docker Containers** – runnable instances.
- **Docker Networks** – virtual networks (`bridge`, `overlay`).
- **Docker Volumes** – persistent storage.
- **Dockerfile** – declarative build script.
- **Docker Compose** – multi-container YAML orchestrator.

Refer to the **block diagram in Section 4.1** for the visual architecture. **(7 Marks — full marks with diagram)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

| Parameter | Virtual Machine | Docker Container |
|---|---|---|
| **Virtualization Level** | Hardware-level (full machine emulation) | OS-level (process isolation) |
| **Architecture** | App → Bins/Libs → **Guest OS** → Hypervisor → Host OS → Hardware | App → Bins/Libs → **Container Engine** → Host OS → Hardware |
| **Boot Time** | **1–5 minutes** (requires BIOS, bootloader, kernel init, services) | **50–500 ms** (just spawns a process under existing kernel) |
| **Size** | **GBs** (entire guest OS + app) | **MBs** (only app + unique libs) |
| **Isolation Mechanism** | **Hypervisor** enforces strong isolation via separate kernel | **Linux namespaces + cgroups** provide process-level isolation |
| **OS Support** | Can run **any OS** (Linux VM on Windows host, etc.) | Container must share host kernel (Linux containers need Linux host) |
| **Performance** | Near-native, but with hypervisor overhead (5–10%) | Essentially **native** (≤ 1% overhead) |
| **Density** | 10–20 VMs per host | 100s–1000s of containers per host |
| **Security** | Strong boundary (separate kernel) | Weaker; depends on kernel hardening and seccomp/AppArmor |
| **Use Case** | Multi-OS, strong multi-tenancy | Microservices, cloud-native, CI/CD |

**Conclusion:** VMs provide **stronger isolation and OS heterogeneity**; containers provide **superior speed, density, and DevOps agility**. Modern cloud architectures often combine both — running containers **inside** VMs for security + agility. **(7 Marks — full marks with comparison table and conclusion)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] [Apply / Analyze]**

**(a)** Explain the **Docker Image layered architecture** with a suitable diagram. Describe how **layer caching** improves build performance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a sample **`Dockerfile`** for a Node.js application. Explain each instruction. Discuss **multi-stage builds** and derive the size-reduction benefit. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Docker Image Layered Architecture:**

A Docker image is **not a single monolithic filesystem** — it is a **stack of read-only layers**, each representing a set of filesystem changes from a `Dockerfile` instruction. Layers are merged at runtime using **Union File Systems** (e.g., OverlayFS).

Refer to **Section 4.4 diagram** for the layer stack.

**Properties of layers:**

1. **Immutability:** Once created, a layer never changes. This makes images tamper-evident via SHA-256 digests.
2. **Caching:** Each layer is identified by a hash. If an instruction and its inputs are unchanged, the cached layer is reused.
3. **Sharing:** Multiple images and running containers can share the same base layers, drastically reducing disk usage.
4. **Order matters:** The order of `Dockerfile` instructions determines the layer order. A change in *any* layer invalidates **all subsequent layers** in the cache.

**Layer-Caching Derivation:**

When a build runs, Docker computes for each instruction $i$:

$$
\text{hash}_i = \text{SHA-256}\big(\text{instruction text}_i \;\|\; \text{hash}_{i-1} \;\|\; \text{file hashes for COPY/ADD}\big)
$$

If $\text{hash}_i$ matches a cached layer, the layer is reused in **≈ 1 ms**; otherwise, it must be rebuilt by launching a temporary container, executing the instruction, and committing the result (takes **seconds to minutes**).

**Engineering Best Practice:** Place the **least frequently changing** instructions (e.g., OS package installs, language runtime installs) at the **top** of the `Dockerfile`, and the **most frequently changing** code at the **bottom**. This way, daily code changes do not invalidate the expensive dependency-install layer. **(7 Marks — full marks with diagram and derivation)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Sample `Dockerfile` for a Node.js Application:**

```dockerfile
# Step 1: Base image with Node.js runtime
FROM node:20-alpine

# Step 2: Set working directory
WORKDIR /usr/src/app

# Step 3: Copy package files (for layer caching)
COPY package*.json ./

# Step 4: Install dependencies
RUN npm ci --only=production

# Step 5: Copy application source
COPY . .

# Step 6: Document the listening port
EXPOSE 3000

# Step 7: Default startup command
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

**Explanation of each instruction:**
- `FROM node:20-alpine` — Alpine-based image keeps base size at ~**180 MB** vs ~**1 GB** for full `node:20`.
- `WORKDIR` — sets the in-container working directory; cleaner than `RUN cd ...`.
- `COPY package*.json ./` — copying *only* manifest files first ensures `npm install` is cached as long as dependencies don't change.
- `RUN npm ci` — clean, reproducible install (faster than `npm install` for CI).
- `COPY . .` — copy source code last; this layer changes most often.
- `EXPOSE 3000` — documentation; actual port mapping requires `-p` at run time.
- `CMD [...]` — uses exec form (preferred over shell form for proper signal handling).

**Multi-Stage Build — Size Derivation:**

In a production Go/Node binary, you don't need the compiler, build tools, or `node_modules` cache in the final image. A multi-stage build separates the **build environment** from the **runtime environment**.

```dockerfile
# Stage 1: Build (large)
FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build       # produces dist/ folder

# Stage 2: Runtime (small)
FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]
```

**Size derivation:**

$$
\text{Size}_{\text{builder}} = \text{Size}_{\text{base}} + \text{Size}_{\text{node\_modules}} + \text{Size}_{\text{source}} + \text{Size}_{\text{build artifacts}}
$$
$$
\text{Size}_{\text{final}} = \text{Size}_{\text{base}} + \text{Size}_{\text{production assets only}}
$$
$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{\text{Size}_{\text{builder}} - \text{Size}_{\text{final}}}{\text{Size}_{\text{builder}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Realistic numbers:** Builder ≈ **450 MB** → Final ≈ **180 MB** → **≈ 60% size reduction**. For compiled languages (Go, Rust), the reduction is even more dramatic: **> 90%**. **(7 Marks — full marks with explanation, code, and derivation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** In `Dockerfile` questions, students often write `apt-get update` and `apt-get install` in *separate* `RUN` instructions. This defeats the cache because Docker may reuse the outdated `apt-get update` layer and try to install a non-existent package version. **Always combine them in a single `RUN`** with `&&` and use `rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*` at the end. This single mistake can cost you **2–3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Docker** is an open-source **containerization platform** that packages apps + dependencies into portable containers.
- **Image** = immutable, layered, read-only blueprint. **Container** = runnable instance with an added read-write layer.
- **Architecture:** Client (`docker` CLI) → REST API → Daemon (`dockerd`) → Registry / Images / Containers.
- **Underlying Linux kernel features:** **Namespaces** (isolation) + **cgroups** (resource limits) + **OverlayFS** (layered FS).
- **Container vs VM:** Containers are **OS-level**, boot in **ms**, sized in **MBs**, share the host kernel; VMs are **hardware-level**, boot in **minutes**, sized in **GBs**, run a separate guest OS.
- **`Dockerfile` keywords:** `FROM`, `WORKDIR`, `COPY`, `RUN`, `EXPOSE`, `CMD`, `ENTRYPOINT`, `ENV`, `ARG`, `VOLUME`, `HEALTHCHECK`.
- **Layer caching rule:** Reused if instruction text + parent hash + input file hashes match. Place *stable* instructions first.
- **Multi-stage builds** drastically reduce final image size — often by **60–95%**.
- **Three pillars:** **Build** (Dockerfile → Image), **Ship** (Image → Registry), **Run** (Image → Container on any host).
- **Key flags of `docker run`:** `-d` (detached), `-p` (port map), `-v` (volume), `--name`, `--restart`, `--memory`, `--cpus`, `--network`.
- **Memorize these numbers for numerical derivations:** Boot time **50–500 ms**, container size **MBs**, VM size **GBs**, image layers = **1 per Dockerfile instruction**.
- **Registries to mention in answers:** Docker Hub, AWS ECR, Azure ACR, Google GCR, JFrog Artifactory, Harbor.
- **Related tools to know (for higher marks):** Docker Compose, Docker Swarm, Kubernetes, Podman, containerd, runc.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: Docker Containers Architecture, Components and Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST602 - ADVANCED COMPUTING SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Study: Docker Containers Architecture, Components and Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Docker** is an open-source **containerization platform** that enables developers to package, ship, and run applications inside lightweight, portable, isolated execution environments called **containers**. These containers share the host operating system's kernel but run in isolated user-space instances, encapsulating the application code, runtime, system libraries, dependencies, and configuration files into a single immutable artifact called a **Docker Image**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Docker is studied as a representative **Operating-System-Level Virtualization** technology, sitting architecturally between traditional hypervisor-based Virtual Machines (VMs) and bare-metal deployments. The platform is built around a **client-server architecture** governed by the **Open Container Initiative (OCI)** standards.

### The Core Triad of Docker
| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Image** | A read-only, layered, immutable template used to create containers. |
| **Container** | A runnable instance of an image — a lightweight, isolated process on the host. |
| **Registry** | A repository service (e.g., Docker Hub) for storing and distributing images. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course PCCST602 expects students to understand Docker as a **case study in advanced computing systems** — focusing on its architecture (client-server, daemon, registry), components (images, containers, volumes, networks), and hands-on examples (`docker run`, `docker build`, `docker-compose`).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of Docker containers like **standardized shipping containers** in global logistics.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Shipping Container Analogy**
>
> Before 1956, cargo was loaded manually — boxes of different shapes, sizes, and materials. Loading a ship was chaotic, slow, and dependent on the dock worker's skill. Then Malcolm McLean introduced the **standardized metal shipping container**. Suddenly, the same container could move from a truck to a ship to a train anywhere in the world — without anyone caring what was inside.
>
> **Docker does the same thing for software:**
> - **The Container** = the metal box (isolated, standardized, portable)
> - **The Image** = the blueprint that defines what goes inside the box
> - **The Docker Engine** = the crane and port infrastructure (the runtime that loads, unloads, and moves containers)
> - **Docker Hub / Registry** = the global warehouse where empty and pre-packed containers are stored
> - **The Dockerfile** = the packing list / recipe that tells workers exactly what to load

A "works on my machine" problem disappears — because the container carries its **entire software environment** with it, regardless of the host OS, libraries, or kernel configuration.

---

## 1.3 Docker vs. Virtual Machines — The Critical Distinction

| Feature | Virtual Machine (VM) | Docker Container |
|---|---|---|
| **Virtualization Level** | Hardware-level | OS-level |
| **Boot Time** | Minutes (full OS boot) | Milliseconds to seconds |
| **Size** | Gigabytes (GB) | Megabytes (MB) |
| **OS Overhead** | Runs a full guest OS | Shares host kernel |
| **Hypervisor Required** | Yes (Type 1 or Type 2) | No (uses `containerd`/`runc`) |
| **Isolation** | Strong (separate kernel) | Process-level (namespaces + cgroups) |
| **Performance** | Near-native (5-10% loss) | Native (near-zero loss) |
| **Density per Host** | 10-20 VMs | Hundreds to thousands of containers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question:** *"Differentiate between containers and virtual machines. Why are containers considered lightweight?"* — Always mention **kernel sharing** as the central reason. Containers do not boot a separate OS; they only spin up an isolated process tree, governed by Linux **namespaces** and **cgroups**.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary

- **Daemon (`dockerd`)** — The long-running background service that manages Docker objects (images, containers, networks, volumes).
- **CLI (`docker`)** — The command-line interface used by users to interact with the daemon.
- **`containerd`** — An industry-standard container runtime that manages the complete container lifecycle.
- **`runc`** — The low-level reference implementation of the OCI container-runtime spec, actually creating containers.
- **Union File System (UnionFS)** — A layered filesystem that allows multiple directories to be mounted simultaneously, enabling Docker's signature image layering.
- **Layer** — A read-only modification to a Docker image; each instruction in a Dockerfile typically creates one layer.
- **Dockerfile** — A declarative, line-by-line build script describing how to assemble an image.
- **Docker Compose** — A tool for defining and running multi-container applications using a `docker-compose.yml` file.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Docker Architecture

Docker follows a strict **Client → REST API → Daemon → Registries** architecture. Understanding this flow is the single most important KTU board topic under "Architecture."

> [!NOTE]
> **Tier 1 — The Client (`docker` CLI)**
> The client is the user's primary interface. It does NOT create containers or build images directly. Instead, it constructs **Docker API requests** (typically over a Unix socket `/var/run/docker.sock` or TCP port `2375`/`2376`) and dispatches them to the daemon. Common commands: `docker build`, `docker pull`, `docker run`, `docker ps`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tier 2 — The Docker Host (Daemon + Runtimes + Images + Containers + Networks + Volumes)**
> The daemon `dockerd` is the persistent service. It exposes a REST API, manages Docker objects, and orchestrates the lower-level runtimes:
> - `containerd` (high-level lifecycle: pull, push, start, stop)
> - `runc` (low-level OCI runtime: namespace setup, cgroup limits, exec)
>
> It also manages the local storage areas: `/var/lib/docker/` (images, layer data, volumes, network configs).

> [!NOTE]
> **Tier 3 — The Registry**
> A stateless, scalable server-side application that stores and serves Docker images. Public registry: **Docker Hub**. Private registries: **AWS ECR**, **Azure ACR**, **Google GCR**, **Harbor** (open-source). Images are pulled on demand and cached locally.

### The Request Flow
When a user types `docker run nginx`:

1. **CLI parses** the command and converts it into a JSON-serialized REST request.
2. **HTTP/Socket transport** delivers the request to `dockerd`.
3. **Daemon checks** the local image cache (`/var/lib/docker/image/`). If absent, it pulls from the configured registry.
4. **`containerd`** receives the run task and prepares the bundle.
5. **`runc`** is invoked to set up namespaces, cgroups, rootfs, and the container init process.
6. **The container starts** as a child of the Docker daemon process tree.

---

## 2.2 Core Docker Components — Detailed Walkthrough

### 2.2.1 The Docker Image (Build Artifact)
An image is a **stack of read-only layers** plus metadata. Each layer corresponds to one instruction in a `Dockerfile`. Layers are content-addressable via **SHA-256 hashes**.

**Example Dockerfile Layer Breakdown:**

| Line | Instruction | Resulting Layer |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `FROM ubuntu:22.04` | Base layer (Ubuntu rootfs) |
| 2 | `RUN apt-get update` | Layer with updated apt cache |
| 3 | `RUN apt-get install -y python3` | Layer with Python installed |
| 4 | `COPY app.py /app/` | Layer with application code |
| 5 | `CMD ["python3", "/app/app.py"]` | Metadata only (no new layer) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Layer Caching is critical for build performance.** If lines 1-3 are unchanged, Docker reuses their layers from the cache. Only line 4 onwards is rebuilt. This is why **Dockerfile ordering matters** — place least-frequently-changing instructions at the top.

### 2.2.2 The Docker Container (Runtime Instance)
A container is an **image + a thin writable layer** (the "container layer") at the top. All filesystem changes (writes, deletes, file creations) are stored in this layer. When the container is destroyed, the writable layer is deleted **unless** it was committed to a new image (`docker commit`) or persisted via a **volume**.

### 2.2.3 Docker Networking
Docker creates a virtual network bridge (`docker0` on Linux) and assigns each container a virtual ethernet interface. The primary drivers are:

| Driver | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **bridge** | Default; containers on same host communicate via `docker0` |
| **host** | Container shares the host's network namespace (no isolation) |
| **none** | No networking; container is fully isolated |
| **overlay** | Multi-host networking (Swarm / Kubernetes) |
| **macvlan** | Container appears as a physical device on the LAN |

### 2.2.4 Docker Volumes (Persistent Storage)
Volumes are the **preferred mechanism** for persisting data generated by containers. They are stored in `/var/lib/docker/volumes/` and managed by the Docker daemon. They decouple data from the container lifecycle.

| Type | Mount Mechanism | Lifetime |
|---|---|---|
| **Named Volume** | Managed by Docker, stored in `/var/lib/docker/volumes/` | Independent of container |
| **Bind Mount** | Maps a host directory into the container | Tied to host path |
| **tmpfs Mount** | Stored in host memory (RAM) | Lost on container stop |

### 2.2.5 The Dockerfile — Build Specification
A `Dockerfile` is a **declarative, ordered set of instructions** that the Docker daemon processes to assemble an image.

**Common Instructions (KTU High-Yield List):**

| Instruction | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `FROM` | Specifies the base image (must be the first instruction) |
| `RUN` | Executes a command during build; creates a new layer |
| `COPY` | Copies files/directories from build context into image |
| `ADD` | Like `COPY` but also supports URL fetch and tar auto-extract |
| `CMD` | Default command when container starts (one per Dockerfile) |
| `ENTRYPOINT` | Fixed executable; arguments are appended |
| `ENV` | Sets environment variables |
| `EXPOSE` | Documents which ports the container listens on |
| `WORKDIR` | Sets the working directory for subsequent instructions |
| `VOLUME` | Declares a mount point for external volumes |
| `USER` | Sets the user (UID) for subsequent instructions |
| `ARG` | Defines a build-time variable |
| `HEALTHCHECK` | Defines a health probe for the container |

---

## 2.3 The Linux Kernel Mechanisms Docker Relies On

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** *"Explain the underlying Linux kernel features that make container isolation possible."*

### A. Namespaces (Isolation)
A namespace **wraps a global system resource** in an abstraction that makes it appear to the processes within the namespace that they have their own isolated instance. There are 8 namespace types in Linux; Docker uses 6:

| Namespace | Variable | Isolates |
|---|---|---|
| PID | `CLONE_NEWPID` | Process IDs (each container sees itself as PID 1) |
| NET | `CLONE_NEWNET` | Network interfaces, IP addresses, ports |
| MNT | `CLONE_NEWNS` | Mount points / filesystem hierarchy |
| UTS | `CLONE_NEWUTS` | Hostname and domain name |
| IPC | `CLONE_NEWIPC` | Inter-process communication (System V IPC, POSIX queues) |
| USER | `CLONE_NEWUSER` | User and group IDs (UID mapping) |

### B. Control Groups — cgroups (Resource Limitation)
Cgroups **limit, account for, and isolate** the resource usage (CPU, memory, disk I/O, network) of a collection of processes. Without cgroups, a runaway container could exhaust the host's resources.

### C. Union File Systems (Layered Storage)
**UnionFS** allows multiple directories (branches) to be transparently overlaid, forming a single coherent filesystem. Docker's primary driver is **Overlay2** on modern Linux, which uses a **lower dir (image layers) + upper dir (container writes) + merged view (combined)** architecture.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> While Docker is a systems / DevOps topic and not heavily formula-based, the following **definitional equations, ratios, and rules** are routinely tested in KTU board exams.

| Concept | Definition / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Container vs VM Memory** | `Memory_container ≪ Memory_VM` | Containers do not reserve guest OS memory (typically **~1-50 MB** overhead) |
| **Boot Time Ratio** | `T_boot(container) / T_boot(VM) ≈ 1:100` | Containers start in **< 1 s**; VMs take **10-60 s** |
| **Image Layer Count** | `n_layers ≤ n_instructions(Dockerfile)` | `CMD`, `ENTRYPOINT`, `LABEL`, `ENV` (JSON form) do not create layers |
| **Copy-on-Write** | All layers are read-only; writes go to the container's writable layer | No duplication; efficient disk usage |
| **UID Mapping (USER NS)** | `UID_host = f(UID_container)` | Root inside container (UID 0) can map to a non-root UID on host |
| **Cgroup CPU Quota** | `CPU_quota / CPU_period = # of CPU cores` | E.g., `quota=50000, period=100000` ⇒ 0.5 CPU cores |
| **REST API Endpoint** | `unix:///var/run/docker.sock` (default) | TCP fallback: `tcp://host:2375` (insecure) or `:2376` (TLS) |
| **OCI Compliance** | All Docker images comply with **OCI Image Spec v1.x** | Image manifests are JSON, addressed by SHA-256 |
| **Port Mapping** | `host_port:container_port` | E.g., `-p 8080:80` maps host 8080 → container 80 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Docker is the foundational building block of modern **microservices**, **CI/CD pipelines**, **cloud-native computing**, and **Kubernetes orchestration**. Every major cloud provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) offers managed container services (ECS, AKS, GKE) built on OCI-compliant runtimes derived directly from Docker's architecture.
